lorna DOONE , 

 a romance of exmoor 


 by R. D. Blackmore 






 preface 

 this work be call a ' romance , ' because the incident , character , time , and scenery , be alike romantic . and in shape this old tale , the Writer neither dare , nor desire , to claim for it the dignity or cumber it with the difficulty of an historic novel . 
 and yet he think that the outline be fill in more carefully , and the situation   however simple   more warmly coloured and quicken , than a reader would expect to find in what be call a ' legend . ' 
 and he know that any son of Exmoor , chance on this volume , can not fail to bring to mind the nurse - tale of his childhood the savage deed of the outlaw Doones in the depth of Bagworthy Forest , the beauty of the hapless maid bring up in the midst of they , the plain John Ridd 's Herculean power , and   memory 's too congenial food   the exploit of Tom Faggus . 
 March , 1869 .

 CHAPTER LXXV 




 CHAPTER I 

 element of education 

 if anybody care to read a simple tale tell simply , I , John Ridd , of the parish of Oare , in the county of Somerset , yeoman and churchwarden , have see and have a share in some doing of this neighborhood , which I will try to set down in order , God spar my life and memory . and they who light upon this book should bear in mind not only that I write for the clearing of our parish from ill fame and calumny , but also a thing which will , I trow , appear too often in it , to wit that I be nothing more than a plain unlettered man , not read in foreign language , as a gentleman might be , nor gift with long word   even in mine own tongue , save what I may have win from the Bible or Master William Shakespeare , whom , in the face of common opinion , I do value highly . in short , I be an ignoramus , but pretty well for a yeoman . 
 my father be of good substance , at least as we reckon in Exmoor , and seize in his own right , from many generation , of one , and that the good and large , of the three farm into which our parish be divide   or rather the cultured part thereof , he John Ridd , the elder , churchwarden , and overseer , be a great admirer of learn , and well able to write his name , send I his only son to be school at Tiverton , in the county of Devon . for the chief boast of that ancient town   next to its woollen staple   be a worthy grammar - school , the large in the west of England , found and handsomely endow in the year 1604 by Master Peter Blundell , of that same place , clothier . 
 here , by the time I be twelve year old , I have rise into the upper school , and could make bold with Eutropius and Caesar by aid of an english version and as much as six line of Ovid . some even say that I might , before manhood , rise almost to the third form , be of a perservering nature ; albeit , by full consent of all   except my mother , thick - headed . but that would have be , as I now perceive , an ambition beyond a farmer 's son ; for there be but one form above it , and that make of masterful scholar , entitle rightly ' monitor ' . so it come to pass , by the grace of God , that I be call away from learning , whilst sit at the desk of the junior first in the upper school , and begin the greek verb   greek word . 
 my eld grandson make bold to say that I never could have learn   greek word , ten page far on , be all he himself could manage , with plenty of stripe to help he . I know that he hath more head than I though never will he have such body ; and be thankful to have stop betime , with a meek and wholesome head - piece . 
 but if you doubt of my have be there , because now I know so little , go and see my name , ' John Ridd , ' graven on that very form . Forsooth , from the time I be strong enough to open a knife and to spell my name , I begin to grave it in the oak , first of the block whereon I sate , and then of the desk in front of it , accord as I be promote from one to other of they : and there my grandson read it now , at this present time of writing , and hath fight a boy for scoff at it ' John Ridd his name ' and do again in ' winkey , ' a mischievous but cheerful device , in which we take great pleasure . 
 this be the manner of a ' winkey , ' which I here set down , lest child of mine , or grandchild , dare to make one on my premise ; if he do , I shall know the mark at once , and score it well upon he . the scholar obtain , by prayer or price , a handful of saltpetre , and then with the knife wherewith he should rather be try to mend his pen , what do he do but scoop a hole where the desk be some three inch thick . this hole should be leave with the middle exalt , and the circumfere dig more deeply . then let he fill it with saltpetre , all save a little space in the midst , where the boss of the wood be . upon that boss   and it will be the well if a splinter of timber rise upward   he stick the end of his candle of tallow , or ' rat 's tail , ' as we call it , kindle and burn smoothly . Anon , as he read by that light his lesson , lift his eye now and then it may be , the fire of candle lay hold of the petre with a splutter noise and a leaping . then should the pupil seize his pen , and , regardless of the nib , stir bravely , and he will see a glow as of burn mountain , and a rich smoke , and spark go merrily ; nor will it cease , if he stir wisely , and there be a good store of petre , until the wood be devour through , like the sinking of a well - shaft . now well may it go with the head of a boy intent upon his primer , who betide to sit thereunder ! but , above all thing , have good care to exercise this art before the master stride up to his desk , in the early gray of the morning . 
 other custom , no less worthy , abide in the school of Blundell , such as the singeing of nightcap ; but though they have a pleasant savour , and refreshing to think of , I may not stop to note they , unless it be that goodly one at the incoming of a flood . the school - house stand beside a stream , not very large , call Lowman , which flow into the broad river of Exe , about a mile below . this Lowman stream , although it be not fond of brawl and violence   in the manner of our Lynn , yet be will not to flood into a mighty head of water when the storm of rain provoke it ; and most of all when its little co - mate , call the Taunton Brook where I have pluck the very good cress that ever man put salt on come foam down like a great roan horse , and rear at the leap of the hedgerow . then be the gray stone wall of Blundell on every side encompass , the vale be spread over with loop water , and it be a hard thing for the day - boy to get home to their supper . 
 and in that time , old Cop , the porter   so call because he hath copper boot to keep the wet from his stomach , and a nose of copper also , in right of other water , his place be to stand at the gate , attend to the flood - board groove into one another , and so to watch the torrent rise , and not be wash away , if it please God he may help it . but long ere the flood hath attain this height , and while it be only wax , certain boy of deputy will watch at the stoop of the drain - hole , and be apt to look outside the wall when Cop be take a cordial . and in the very front of the gate , just without the archway , where the ground be pave most handsomely , you may see in copy - letter do a great P.B. of white pebble . now , it be the custom and the law that when the invading water , either flux along the wall from below the road - bridge , or pour sharply across the meadow from a cut call Owen 's Ditch and I myself have see it come both way upon the very instant when the waxing element lip though it be but a single pebble of the founder 's letter , it be in the license of any boy , soever small and undoctrined , to rush into the great school - room , where a score of master sit heavily , and scream at the top of his voice , ' P.B. ' 
 then , with a yell , the boy leap up , or break away from their standing ; they toss their cap to the black - beamed roof , and haply the very book after they ; and the great boy vex no more the small one , and the small boy stick up to the great one . one with another , hard they go , to see the gain of the water , and the tribulation of Cop , and be prone to kick the day - boy out , with word of scanty compliment . then the master look at one another , have no class to look to , and   boy be no more left to watch   in a manner they put their mouth up . with a spirited bang they close their book , and make invitation the one to the other for pipe and foreign cordial , recommend the chance of the time , and the comfort away from cold water . 
 but , lo ! I be dwell on little thing and the pigeon ' egg of the infancy , forget the bitter and heavy life go over I since then . if I be neither a hard man nor a very close one , God know I have have no lack of rub and pound to make stone of I . yet can I not somehow believe that we ought to hate one another , to live far asunder , and block the mouth each of his little den ; as do the wild beast of the wood , and the hairy outrang now bring over , each with a chain upon he . let that matter be as it will . it be beyond I to unfold , and mayhap of my grandson 's grandson . all I know be that wheat be well than when I begin to sow it . 




 CHAPTER II 

 AN important ITEM 

 now the cause of my leave Tiverton school , and the way of it , be as follow . on the 29th day of November , in the year of our Lord 1673 , the very day when I be twelve year old , and have spend all my substance in sweetmeat , with which I make treat to the little boy , till the large boy run in and take they , we come out of school at five o'clock , as the rule be upon Tuesdays . accord to custom we drive the day - boy in brave rout down the causeway from the school - porch even to the gate where Cop have his dwelling and duty . little it recke we and help they less , that they be our founder 's citizen , and haply his own grand - nephews   for he leave no direct descendant , neither do we much inquire what their lineage be . for it have long be fix among we , who be of the house and chamber , that these same day - boy be all ' cadde , ' as we have discover to call it , because they pay no groat for their schooling , and bring their own common with they . in consumption of these we would help they , for our fare in hall fed appetite ; and while we eat their victual , we allow they freely to talk to we . nevertheless , we could not feel , when all the victual be go , but that these boy require kick from the premise of Blundell . and some of they be shopkeeper ' son , young grocer , fellmonger , and poulterer , and these to their credit seem to know how righteous it be to kick they . but other be of high family , as any need be , in Devon Carews , and bouchier , and Bastards , and some of these would turn sometimes , and strike the boy that kick they . but to do they justice , even these know that they must be kick for not pay . 
 after these ' charity - boy ' be go , as in contumely we call they ' if you break my bag on my head , ' say one , ' how will feed thence to - morrow ? ' and after old Cop with clang of iron have jam the double gate in under the scruff - stone archway , whereupon be latin verse , do in brass of small quality , some of we who be not hungry , and care not for the supper - bell , have suck much parliament and dump at my only charge not that I ever bear much wealth , but because I have be thrifte it for this time of my birth we be lean quite at dusk against the iron bar of the gate some six , or it may be seven of we , small boy all , and not conspicuous in the closing of the daylight and the fog that come at eventide , else Cop would have rat we up the green , for he be churly to little boy when his wife have take their money . there be plenty of room for all of we , for the gate will hold nine boy close - pack , unless they be feed rankly , whereof be little danger ; and now we be look out on the road and wish we could get there ; hop , moreover , to see a good string of pack - horse come by , with trooper to protect they . for the day - boy have bring we word that some intend their way to the town have lie that morning at Sampford Peveril , and must be in ere nightfall , because Mr. Faggus be after they . now Mr. Faggus be my first cousin and an honour to the family , be a Northmolton man of great renown on the highway from Barum town even to London . therefore of course , I hop that he would catch the packman , and the boy be ask my opinion as of an oracle , about it . 
 a certain boy lean up against I would not allow my elbow room , and strike I very sadly in the stomach part , though his own be full of my parliament . and this I feel so unkindly , that I smite he straightway in the face without tarrying to consider it , or weigh the question duly . upon this he put his head down , and present it so vehemently at the middle of my waistcoat , that for a minute or more my breath seem drop , as it be , from my pocket , and my life seem to stop from great want of ease . before I come to myself again , it have be settle for we that we should move to the ' Ironing - box , ' as the triangle of turf be call where the two causeway come from the school - porch and the hall - porch meet , and our fight be mainly celebrate ; only we must wait until the convoy of horse have pass , and then make a ring by candlelight , and the other boy would like it . but suddenly there come round the post where the letter of our founder be , not from the way of Taunton but from the side of Lowman bridge , a very small string of horse , only two indeed   counting for one the pony , and a red - faced man on the big nag . 
 ' plaise ye , worshipful master , ' he say , be fear of the gateway , ' carn ' e tull whur our Jan Ridd be ? ' 
 ' hyur a be , ee fai , Jan Ridd , ' answer a sharp little chap , make game of John Fry 's language . 
 ' zhow un up , then , ' say John Fry poke his whip through the bar at we ; ' zhow un up , and putt un aowt . ' 
 the other little chap point at I , and some begin to hallo ; but I know what I be about . 
 ' oh , John , John , ' I cry , ' what be the use of your come now , and Peggy over the moor , too , and it so cruel cold for she ? the holiday do not   begin till Wednesday fortnight , John . to think of your not know that ! ' 
 John Fry lean forward in the saddle , and turn his eye away from I ; and then there be a noise in his throat like a snail crawl on a window - pane . 
 ' oh , us knaw that wull enough , Maister Jan ; reckon every Oare - man knaw that , without go to skoo - ull , like you doth . your moother have keep arl the apple up , and old Betty toorne the black pudden , and none dare set trap for a blagbird . arl for thee , lad ; every bit of it now for thee ! ' 
 he check himself suddenly , and frighten I . I know that John Fry 's way so well . 
 ' and father , and father oh , how be father ? ' I push the boy right and leave as I say it . ' John , be father up in town ! he always use to come for I , and leave nobody else to do it . ' 
 ' vayther'll be at the crooked post , tother zide o ' telling - house .   her coodn't   lave ' ouze by raison of the Chirstmas bakkon comin ' on , and zome o ' the cider welt . ' 
    the ' telling - house ' on the moor be rude cot where the 
  shepherd meet to ' tell ' their sheep at the end of the 
  pasture season . 
 he look at the nag 's ear as he say it ; and , be up to John Fry 's way , I know that it be a lie . and my heart fall like a lump of lead , and I lean back on the stay of the gate , and long no more to fight anybody . a sort of dull power hang over I , like the cloud of a brood tempest , and I fear to be tell anything . I do not even care to stroke the nose of my pony Peggy , although she push it in through the rail , where a square of broad lattice be , and sniff at I , and begin to crop gently after my finger . but whatever live or dy , business must be attend to ; and the principal business of good Christians be , beyond all controversy , to fight with one another . 
 ' come up , Jack , ' say one of the boy , lift I under the chin ; ' he hit you , and you hit he , you know . ' 
 ' pay your debt before you go , ' say a monitor , stride up to I , after hear how the honour lay ; ' Ridd , you must go through with it . ' 
 ' fight , for the sake of the junior first , ' cry the little fellow in my ear , the clever one , the head of our class , who have mock John Fry , and know all about the aorist , and try to make I know it ; but I never go more than three place up , and then it be an accident , and I come down after dinner . the boy be urgent round I to fight , though my stomach be not up for it ; and be very slow of wit   which be not chargeable on I , I look from one to other of they , seek any cure for it . not that I be afraid of fight , for now I have be three year at Blundell 's , and foughten , all that time , a fight at least once every week , till the boy begin to know I ; only that the load on my heart be not sprightly as of the hay - field . it be a very sad thing to dwell on ; but even now , in my time of wisdom , I doubt it be a fond thing to imagine , and a motherly to insist upon , that boy can do without fight . unless they be very good boy , and afraid of one another . 
 ' Nay , ' I say , with my back against the wrought - iron stay of the gate , which be sockete into Cop 's house - front : ' I will not fight thee now , Robin Snell , but wait till I come back again . ' 
 ' take coward 's blow , Jack Ridd , then , ' cry half a dozen little boy , shove Bob Snell forward to do it ; because they all know well enough , have strive with I ere now , and prove I to be their master they know , I say , that without great change , I would never accept that contumely . but I take little heed of they , look in dull wonderment at John Fry , and Smiler , and the blunderbuss , and Peggy . John Fry be scratch his head , I could see , and get blue in the face , by the light from Cop 's parlour - window , and go to and fro upon Smiler , as if he be hard set with it . and all the time he be look briskly from my eye to the fist I be clench , and methought he try to wink at I in a covert manner ; and then Peggy whisk her tail . 
 ' shall I fight , John ? ' I say at last ; ' I would an you have not come , John . ' 
 ' Chraist 's will be do ; I zim thee have well faight , Jan , ' he answer , in a whisper , through the gridiron of the gate ; ' there be a dale of faighting avore thee . good wai to begin gude taime laike . wull the geatman latt I in , to zee as thee hast vair plai , lad ? ' 
 he look doubtfully down at the colour of his cowskin boot , and the mire upon the horse , for the slough be exceedingly mucky . Peggy , indeed , my sorrel pony , be light of weight , be not crust much over the shoulder ; but Smiler   our young sledder   have be well in over his wither , and none would have deem he a piebald , save of red mire and black mire . the great blunderbuss , moreover , be choke with a dollop of slough - cake ; and John Fry 's sad - coloured Sunday hat be indue with a plume of marish - weed . all this I see while he be dismount , heavily and wearily , lift his leg from the saddle - cloth as if with a sore crick in his back . 
 by this time the question of fighting be go quite out of our discretion ; for sundry of the eld boy , grave and reverend signor , who have take no small pleasure in teach our hand to fight , to ward , to parry , to feign and counter , to lunge in the manner of sword - play , and the weak child to drop on one knee when no cunning of fence might baffle the onset these great master of the art , who would far liefer see we little one practise it than themselves engage , six or seven of they come run down the rounded causeway , have hear that there have arise ' a snug little mill ' at the gate . now whether that word hath origin in a greek term mean a conflict , as the well - read boy asseverate , or whether it be nothing more than a figure of similitude , from the beat arm of a mill , such as I have see in county where be no waterbrook , but folk make bread with wind it be not for a man devoid of scholarship to determine . enough that they who make the ring intitule the scene a ' mill , ' while we who must be thump inside it try to rejoice in their pleasantry , till it turn upon the stomach . 
 moreover , I feel upon I now a certain responsibility , a dutiful need to maintain , in the presence of John Fry , the manliness of the Ridd family , and the honour of Exmoor . Hitherto none have worst I , although in the three year of my schooling , I have fight more than threescore battle , and bedew with blood every plant of grass towards the middle of the Ironing - box . and this success I owe at first to no skill of my own ; until I come to know well ; for up to twenty or thirty fight , I strike as nature guide I , no wise than a father - long - leg in the heat of a lanthorn ; but I have conquer , partly through my native strength , and the Exmoor toughness in I , and still more that I could not see when I have get my bellyful . but now I be like to have that and more ; for my heart be down , to begin with ; and then Robert Snell be a big boy than I have ever encounter , and as thick in the skull and hard in the brain as even I could claim to be . 
 I have never tell my mother a word about these frequent striving , because she be soft - hearted ; neither have I tell by father , because he have not see it . therefore , behold I still an innocent - look child , with fair curl on my forehead , and no store of bad language , John Fry think this be the very first fight that ever have befall I ; and so when they let he at the gate , ' with a message to the headmaster , ' as one of the monitor tell Cop , and Peggy and Smiler be tie to the railing , till I should be through my business , John come up to I with the tear in his eye , and say , ' Doon't   thee goo for to do it , Jan ; doon't   thee do it , for gude now . ' but I tell he that now it be much too late to cry off ; so he say , ' the Lord be with thee , Jan , and turn thy thumb - knuckle inward . ' 
 it be not a very large piece of ground in the angle of the causeway , but quite big enough to fight upon , especially for Christians , who love to be cheek by jowl at it . the great boy stand in a circle around , be gift with strong privilege , and the little boy have leave to lie flat and look through the leg of the great boy . but while we be yet prepare , and the candle hiss in the fog - cloud , old Phoebe , of more than fourscore year , whose room be over the hall - porch , come hobble out , as she always do , to mar the joy of the conflict . no one ever heed she , neither do she expect it ; but the evil be that two senior boy must always lose the first round of the fight , by have to lead her home again . 
 I marvel how Robin Snell feel . very likely he think nothing of it , always have be a boy of a hectoring and unruly sort . but I feel my heart go up and down as the boy come round to strip I ; and greatly fear to be beat , I blow hot upon my knuckle . then pull I off my little cut jerkin , and lay it down on my head cap , and over that my waistcoat , and a boy be proud to take care of they . Thomas Hooper be his name , and I remember how he look at I . my mother have make that little cut jerkin , in the quiet winter evening . and take pride to loop it up in a fashionable way , and I be loth to soil it with blood , and good filberd be in the pocket . then up to I come Robin Snell   mayor of Exeter thrice since that , and he stand very square , and look at I , and I lack not long to look at he . round his waist he have a kerchief busk up his small - clothe , and on his foot light pumpkin shoe , and all his upper raiment off . and he dance about in a way that make my head swim on my shoulder , and he stand some inch over I . but I , be muddle with much doubt about John Fry and his errand , be only strip of my jerkin and waistcoat , and not comfortable to begin . 
 ' come now , shake hand , ' cry a big boy , jump in joy of the spectacle , a third - former nearly six foot high ; ' shake hand , you little devil . keep your pluck up , and show good sport , and Lord love the well man of you . ' 
 Robin take I by the hand , and gaze at I disdainfully , and then smote I painfully in the face , ere I could get my fence up . 
 ' Whutt be 'bout , lad ? ' cry John Fry ; ' hutt un again , Jan , wull ' e ? well do then , our Jan boy . ' 
 for I have reply to Robin now , with all the weight and cadence of penthemimeral caesura   a thing , the name of which I know , but could never make head nor tail of it , and the strife begin in a serious style , and the boy look on be not cheat . although I could not collect their shout when the blow be ring upon I , it be no great loss ; for John Fry tell I afterwards that their oath go up like a furnace fire . but to these we pay no heed or hap , be in the thick of swing , and devoid of judgment . all I know be , I come to my corner , when the round be over , with very hard pump in my chest , and a great desire to fall away . 
 ' time be up , ' cry head - monitor , ere ever I get my breath again ; and when I fain would have linger awhile on the knee of the boy that hold I . John Fry have come up , and the boy be laugh because he want a stable lanthorn , and threaten to tell my mother . 
 ' time be up , ' cry another boy , more headlong than head - monitor . ' if we count three before the come of thee , thwack thou art , and must go to the woman . ' I feel it hard upon I . he begin to count , one , too , three but before the ' three ' be out of his mouth , I be face my foe , with both hand up , and my breath go rough and hot , and resolve to wait the turn of it . for I have find seat on the knee of a boy sage and skilled to tutor I , who know how much the end very often differ from the beginning . a rare ripe scholar he be ; and now he hath rout up the Germans in the matter of criticism . sure the clever boy and man have most love towards the stupid one . 
 ' finish he off , Bob , ' cry a big boy , and that I notice especially , because I think it unkind of he , after eat of my toffee as he have that afternoon ; ' finish he off , neck and crop ; he deserve it for stick up to a man like you . ' 
 but I be not so to be finish off , though feel in my knuckle now as if it be a blueness and a sense of chilblain . nothing hold except my leg , and they be good to help I . so this bout , or round , if you please , be foughten warily by I , with gentle recollection of what my tutor , the clever boy , have tell I , and some resolve to earn his praise before I come back to his knee again . and never , I think , in all my life , sound sweet word in my ear   except when my love love I   than when my second and backer , who have make himself part of my doing now , and would have weep to see I beat , say , 
 ' famously do , Jack , famously ! only keep your wind up , Jack , and you will go right through he ! ' 
 meanwhile John Fry be prowl about , ask the boy what they think of it , and whether I be like to be kill , because of my mother 's trouble . but find now that I have foughten three - score fight already , he come up to I woefully , in the quickness of my breathing , while I sit on the knee of my second , with a piece of spongious coralline to ease I of my bloodshed , and he say in my ear , as if he be clap spur into a horse , 
 ' never thee knack under , Jan , or never coom naigh Hexmoor no more . ' 
 with that it be all up with I . a simmering buzz in my heavy brain , and a light come through my eyeplace . at once I set both fist again , and my heart stick to I like cobbler 's wax . either Robin Snell should kill I , or I would conquer Robin Snell . so I go in again with my courage up , and Bob come smile for victory , and I hat he for smile . he let at I with his left hand , and I give he my right between his eye , and he blink , and be not pleased with it . I fear he not , and spar he not , neither spar myself . my breath come again , and my heart stand cool , and my eye strike fire no long . only I know that I would die soon than shame my birthplace . how the rest of it be I know not ; only that I have the end of it , and help to put Robin in bed . 




 CHAPTER III 

 the WAR - path of the doones 

 from Tiverton town to the town of Oare be a very long and painful road , and in good truth the traveller must make his way , as the saying be ; for the way be still unmade , at least , on this side of Dulverton , although there be less danger now than in the time of my schooling ; for now a good horse may go there without much cost of leaping , but when I be a boy the spur would fail , when need most , by reason of the slough - cake . it be to the credit of this age , and our advance upon fatherly way , that now we have lay down rod and fagot , and even stump - oak here and there , so that a man in good daylight need not sink , if he be quite sober . there be nothing I have strive at more than do my duty , way - warden over Exmoor . 
 but in those day , when I come from school   and good time they be , too , full of a warmth and fine hearth - comfort , which now be die out , it be a sad and sorry business to find where lay the highway . we be take now to mark it off with a fence on either side , at least , when a town be handy ; but to I his seem of a high pretence , and a sort of landmark , and channel for robber , though well enough near London , where they have earn a race - course . 
 we leave the town of the two ford , which they say be the meaning of it , very early in the morning , after lie one day to rest , as be demand by the nag , sore of foot and founder . for my part , too , I be glad to rest , have ache all over I , and very heavy bruise ; and we lodge at the sign of the White Horse Inn , in the street call Gold Street , opposite where the soul be of John and Joan Greenway , set up in gold letter , because we must take the homeward way at cockcrow of the morning . though still John Fry be dry with I of the reason of his come , and only tell lie about father , and could not keep they agreeable , I hop for the good , as all boy will , especially after a victory . and I think , perhaps father have send for I because he have a good harvest , and the rat be bad in the corn - chamber . 
 it be high noon before we be get to Dulverton that day , near to which town the river Exe and its big brother Barle have union . my mother have an uncle live there , but we be not to visit his house this time , at which I be somewhat astonish , since we need must stop for at least two hour , to bait our horse thorough well , before come to the black bogway . the bog be very good in frost , except where the hot - spring rise ; but as yet there have be no frost this year , save just enough to make the blackbird look big in the morning . in a hearty black - frost they look small , until the snow fall over they . 
 the road from Bampton to Dulverton have not be very delicate , yet nothing to complain of much no deep , indeed , than the hock of a horse , except in the rotten place . the day be incline to be mild and foggy , and both nag sweat freely ; but Peggy carry little weight   for my wardrobe be upon Smiler , and John Fry grumble always , we could easily keep in front , as far as you may hear a laugh . 
 John have be rather bitter with I , which methought be a mark of ill taste at come home for the holiday ; and yet I make allowance for John , because he have never be at school , and never would have chance to eat fry upon condition of spell it ; therefore I ride on , think that he be hard - set , like a saw , for his dinner , and would soften after tooth - work . and yet at his most hungry time , when his mind be far go upon bacon , certe he seem to check himself and look at I as if he be sorry for little thing come over great . 
 but now , at Dulverton , we din upon the rare and choicest victual that ever I do taste . even now , at my time of life , to think of it give I appetite , as once and awhile to think of my first love make I love all goodness . hot mutton pasty be a thing I have often hear of from very wealthy boy and man , who make a dessert of dinner ; and to hear they talk of it make my lip smack , and my rib come inward . 
 and now John Fry stride into the hostel , with the air and grace of a short - legged man , and shout as loud as if he be call sheep upon Exmoor , 
 ' hot mooton pasty for twoo trarv'lers , at number vaive , in vaive minnit ! dish un up in the tin with the grahvy , zame as I hardere last Tuesday . ' 
 of course it do not come in five minute , nor yet in ten or twenty ; but that make it all the well when it come to the real presence ; and the smell of it be enough to make an empty man thank God for the room there be inside he . fifty year have pass I quick than the taste of that gravy . 
 it be the manner of all good boy to be careless of apparel , and take no pride in adornment . good lack , if I see a boy make to do about the fit of his crumpler , and the creasing of his breech , and desire to be shod for comeliness rather than for use , I can not ' scape the mark that God take thought to make a girl of he . not so when they grow old , and court the regard of the maiden ; then may the bravery pass from the inside to the outside of they ; and no big fool be they , even then , than their father be before they . but God forbid any man to be a fool to love , and be love , as I have be . else would he have prevent it . 
 when the mutton pasty be do , and Peggy and Smiler have din well also , out I go to wash at the pump , be a lover of soap and water , at all risk , except of my dinner . and John Fry , who care very little to wash , save Sabbath day in his own soap , and who have keep I from the pump by threaten loss of the dish , out he come in a satisfied manner , with a piece of quill in his hand , to lean against a door - post , and listen to the horse feed , and have his tooth ready for supper . 
 then a lady's - maid come out , and the sun be on her face , and she turn round to go back again ; but put a well face upon it , and give a trip and hitch her dress , and look at the sun full body , lest the hostler should laugh that she be lose her complexion . with a long italian glass in her finger very daintily , she come up to the pump in the middle of the yard , where I be run the water off all my head and shoulder , and arm , and some of my breast even , and though I have glimpse she through the sprinkle , it give I quite a turn to see she , child as I be , in my open aspect . but she look at I , no whit abash , make a baby of I , no doubt , as a woman of thirty will do , even with a very big boy when they catch he on a hayrick , and she say to I in a brazen manner , as if I have be nobody , while I be shrink behind the pump , and crave to get my shirt on , ' good leetle boy , come hither to I . fine heaven ! how blue your eye be , and your skin like snow ; but some naughty man have beat it black . oh , leetle boy , let I feel it . ah , how then it must have hurt you ! there now , and you shall love I . ' 
 all this time she be touch my breast , here and there , very lightly , with her delicate brown finger , and I understand from her voice and manner that she be not of this country , but a foreigner by extraction . and then I be not so shy of she , because I could talk well English than she ; and yet I long for my jerkin , but like not to be rude to she . 
 ' if you please , madam , I must go . John Fry be wait by the tapster 's door , and Peggy neigh to I . if you please , we must get home to - night ; and father will be wait for I this side of the telling - house . ' 
 ' there , there , you shall go , leetle dear , and perhaps I will go after you . I have take much love of you . but the baroness be hard to I . how far you call it now to the bank of the sea at Wash Wash ' 
 ' at Watchett , likely you mean , madam . oh , a very long way , and the road as soft as the road to Oare . ' 
 ' oh - ah , oh - ah I shall remember ; that be the place where my leetle boy live , and some day I will come seek for he . now make the pump to flow , my dear , and give I the good water . the baroness will not touch unless a nebule be form outside the glass . ' 
 I do not know what she mean by that ; yet I pump for her very heartily , and marvel to see she for fifty time throw the water away in the trough , as if it be not good enough . at last the water suit she , with a likeness of fog outside the glass , and the gleam of a crystal under it , and then she make a curtsey to I , in a sort of mocking manner , hold the long glass by the foot , not to take the cloud off ; and then she want to kiss I ; but I be out of breath , and have always be shy of that work , except when I come to offer it ; and so I duck under the pump - handle , and she knock she chin on the knob of it ; and the hostler come out , and ask whether they would do as well . 
 upon this , she retreat up the yard , with a certain dark dignity , and a foreign way of walking , which stop they at once from go far , because it be so different from the fashion of their sweetheart . one with another they hang back , where half a cart - load of hay be , and they look to be sure that she would not turn round ; and then each one laugh at the rest of they . 
 now , up to the end of Dulverton town , on the northward side of it , where the two new pig - sty be , the Oare folk and the Watchett folk must trudge on together , until we come to a broken cross , where a murder man lie bury . Peggy and Smiler go up the hill , as if nothing could be too much for they , after the bean they have eat , and suddenly turn a corner of tree , we happen upon a great coach and six horse labour very heavily . John Fry ride on with his hat in his hand , as become he towards the quality ; but I be amazed to that degree , that I leave my cap on my head , and draw bridle without know it . 
 for in the front seat of the coach , which be half - way open , be of the city - make , and the day in want of air , sate the foreign lady , who have meet I at the pump and offer to salute I . by her side be a little girl , dark - haired and very wonderful , with a wealthy softness on she , as if she must have her own way . I could not look at she for two glance , and she do not look at I for one , be such a little child , and busy with the hedge . but in the honourable place sate a handsome lady , very warmly dress , and sweetly delicate of colour . and close to she be a lively child , two or it may be three year old , bear a white cockade in his hat , and star at all and everybody . now , he see Peggy , and take such a liking to she , that the lady his mother if so she be be force to look at my pony and I . and , to tell the truth , although I be not of those who adore the high folk , she look at we very kindly , and with a sweetness rarely find in the woman who milk the cow for we . 
 then I take off my cap to the beautiful lady , without ask wherefore ; and she put up her hand and kiss it to I , think , perhaps , that I look like a gentle and good little boy ; for folk always call I innocent , though God know I never be that . but now the foreign lady , or lady 's maid , as it might be , who have be busy with little dark eye , turn upon all this go - on , and look I straight in the face . I be about to salute she , at a distance , indeed , and not with the nicety she have offer to I , but , strange to say , she star at my eye as if she have never see I before , neither wish to see I again . at this I be so startled , such thing being out of my knowledge , that I startle Peggy also with the muscle of my leg , and she be fresh from stable , and the mire scrap off with cask - hoop , break away so suddenly that I could do no more than turn round and lower my cap , now five month old , to the beautiful lady . soon I overtake John Fry , and ask he all about they , and how it be that we have miss their starting from the hostel . but John would never talk much till after a gallon of cider ; and all that I could win out of he be that they be ' murder Papishers , ' and little he care to do with they , or the devil , as they come from . and a good thing for I , and a providence , that I be go down Dulverton town to buy sweetstuff for Annie , else my stupid head would have go astray with their great out - come . 
 we see no more of they after that , but turn into the sideway ; and soon have the fill of our hand and eye to look to our own going . for the road get bad and bad , until there be none at all , and perhaps the pure thing it could do be to be ashamed to show itself . but we push on as good we might , with doubt of reach home any time , except by special grace of God . 
 the fog come down upon the moor as thick as ever I see it ; and there be no sound of any sort , nor a breath of wind to guide we . the little stubby tree that stand here and there , like bush with a wooden leg to they , be drizzle with a mess of wet , and hang their point with dropping . wherever the butt - end of a hedgerow come up from the hollow ground , like the wither of a horse , hole of splash be pock and pimple in the yellow sand of coney , or under the dwarf tree 's oven . but soon it be too dark to see that , or anything else , I may say , except the crease in the dusk , where prisone light creep up the valley . 
 after awhile even that be go , and no other comfort leave we except to see our horse ' head jog to their footstep , and the dark ground pass below we , lighter where the wet be ; and then the splash , foot after foot , more clever than we can do it , and the orderly jerk of the tail , and the smell of what a horse be . 
 John Fry be bow forward with sleep upon his saddle , and now I could no long see the frizzle of wet upon his beard for he have a very brave one , of a bright red colour , and trim into a whale - oil knot , because he be newly married although that comb of hair have be a subject of some wonder to I , whether I , in God 's good time , should have the like of that , handsomely set with shin bead , small above and large below , from the weeping of the heaven . but still I could see the jog of his hat a Sunday hat with a top to it and some of his shoulder bow out in the mist , so that one could say ' hold up , John , ' when Smiler put his foot in . ' mercy of God ! where be we now ? ' say John Fry , wake suddenly ; ' we ought to have pass hold hash , Jan. Zeen it on the road , have ' ee ? ' 
 ' no indeed , John ; no old ash . nor nothing else to my knowing ; nor hear nothing , save thee snore . ' 
 ' watt a vule thee must be then , Jan ; and I myzell no well . harken , lad , harken ! ' 
 we draw our horse up and listen , through the thickness of the air , and with our hand lay to our ear . at first there be nothing to hear , except the panting of the horse and the trickle of the eave drop from our head - cover and clothing , and the soft sound of the lonely night , that make we feel , and try not to think . then there come a mellow noise , very low and mournsome , not a sound to be afraid of , but to long to know the meaning , with a soft rise of the hair . three time it come and go again , as the shaking of a thread might pass away into the distance ; and then I touch John Fry to know that there be something near I . 
 ' Doon't   ' e be a vule , Jan ! vaine moozick as iver I ' eer . God bless the man as make un doo it . ' 
 ' have they hang one of the Doones then , John ? ' 
 ' hush , lad ; niver talk laike o ' thiccy . hang a Doone ! God knoweth , the King would hang pretty quick if she do . ' 
 ' then who be it in the chain , John ? ' 
 I feel my spirit rise as I ask ; for now I have cross Exmoor so often as to hope that the people sometimes deserve it , and think that it might be a lesson to the rogue who unjustly love the mutton they be never bear to . but , of course , they be bear to hang , when they set themselves so high . 
 ' it be nawbody , ' say John , ' vor we to make a fush about . belong to t'other zide o ' the moor , and come staling shape to our zide . Red Jem Hannaford his name . thank God for he to be hang , lad ; and good cess to his soul for craikin ' zo . ' 
 so the sound of the quiet swinging lead we very modestly , as it come and go on the wind , loud and low pretty regularly , even as far as the foot of the gibbet where the four cros - way be . 
 ' vamous job this here , ' cry John , look up to be sure of it , because there be so many ; ' here be my own nick on the post . Red Jem , too , and no doubt of he ; he do hang so handsome like , and his rib up laike a horse a'most . God bless they as discoovere the way to make a rogue so useful . good - naight to thee , Jem , my lad ; and not break thy drame with the craikin ' . ' 
 John Fry shake his bridle - arm , and smote upon Smiler merrily , as he jog into the homeward track from the guiding of the body . but I be sorry for Red Jem , and want to know more about he , and whether he might not have avoid this miserable end , and what his wife and child think of it , if , indeed , he have any . 
 but John would talk no more about it ; and perhaps he be move with a lonesome feeling , as the creak sound come after we . 
 ' Hould thee tongue , lad , ' he say sharply ; ' we be naigh the Doone - track now , two maile from Dunkery Beacon hill , the haighest place of Hexmoor . so happen they be abroad to - naight , we must crawl on our belly - place , boy . ' 
 I know at once what he mean those bloody Doones of Bagworthy , the awe of all Devon and Somerset , outlaws , traitor , murderer . my little leg begin to tremble to and fro upon Peggy 's side , as I hear the dead robber in chain behind we , and think of the live one still in front . 
 ' but , John , ' I whisper warily , sidle close to his saddle - bow ; ' dear John , you do not   think they will see we in such a fog as this ? ' 
 ' never God make vog as could stop their eyesen , ' he whisper in answer , fearfully ; ' here we be by the hollow ground . zober , lad , goo zober now , if thee wish to see thy moother . ' 
 for I be incline , in the manner of boy , to make a run of the danger , and cross the Doone - track at full speed ; to rush for it , and be do with it . but even then I wonder why he talk of my mother so , and say not a word of father . 
 we be come to a long deep ' goyal , ' as they call it on Exmoor , a word whose fountain and origin I have nothing to do with . only I know that when little boy laugh at I at Tiverton , for talk about a ' goyal , ' a big boy clout they on the head , and say that it be in Homer , and mean the hollow of the hand . and another time a Welshman tell I that it must be something like the thing they call a ' pant ' in those part . still I know what it mean well enough to wit , a long trough among wild hill , fall towards the plain country , round at the bottom , perhaps , and stiff , more than steep , at the side of it . whether it be straight or crooked , make no difference to it . 
 we ride very carefully down our side , and through the soft grass at the bottom , and all the while we listen as if the air be a speaking - trumpet . then gladly we breast our nag to the rise , and be come to the comb of it , when I hear something , and catch John 's arm , and he bend his hand to the shape of his ear . it be the sound of horse ' foot knock up through splashy ground , as if the bottom suck they . then a grunting of weary man , and the lift noise of stirrup , and sometimes the clank of iron mix with the wheezy croning of leather and the blowing of hairy nostril . 
 ' God 's sake , Jack , slip round her belly , and let she go where she wull . ' 
 as John Fry whisper , so I do , for he be off Smiler by this time ; but our two pad be too fagged to go far , and begin to nose about and crop , sniff more than they need have do . I creep to John 's side very softly , with the bridle on my arm . 
 ' let goo braidle ; let goo , lad . plaise God they take they for forest - pony , or they will zend a bullet through we . ' 
 I see what he mean , and let go the bridle ; for now the mist be roll off , and we be against the sky - line to the dark cavalcade below we . John lie on the ground by a barrow of heather , where a little gullet be , and I creep to he , afraid of the noise I make in drag my leg along , and the creak of my cord breech . John bleat like a sheep to cover it a sheep very cold and tremble . 
 then just as the foremost horseman pass , scarce twenty yard below we , a puff of wind come up the glen , and the fog roll off before it . and suddenly a strong red light , cast by the cloud - weight downward , spread like finger over the moorland , open the alley of darkness , and hang on the steel of the rider . 
 ' Dunkery Beacon , ' whisper John , so close into my ear , that I feel his lip and tooth ashake ; ' dursn't   fire it now except to show the Doones way home again , since the naight as they go up and throw the watchman atop of it . why , wutt be 'bout , lad ? God 's sake ' 
 for I could keep still no long , but wriggle away from his arm , and along the little gullet , still go flat on my breast and thigh , until I be under a grey patch of stone , with a fringe of dry fern round it ; there I lay , scarce twenty foot above the head of the rider , and I fear to draw my breath , though prone to do it with wonder . 
 for now the beacon be rush up , in a fiery storm to heaven , and the form of its flame come and go in the fold , and the heavy sky be hover . all around it be hang with red , deep in twisted column , and then a giant beard of fire stream throughout the darkness . the sullen hill be flank with light , and the valley chin with shadow , and all the sombrous moor between awoke in furrowed anger . 
 but most of all the flinging fire leap into the rocky mouth of the glen below I , where the horseman pass in silence , scarcely deign to look round . heavy man and large of stature , reckless how they bear their gun , or how they sate their horse , with leathern jerkin , and long boot , and iron plate on breast and head , plunder heap behind their saddle , and flagon sling in front of they ; I count more than thirty pass , like cloud upon red sunset . some have carcass of sheep swing with their skin on , other have deer , and one have a child fling across his saddle - bow . whether the child be dead , or alive , be more than I could tell , only it hang head downwards there , and must take the chance of it . they have get the child , a very young one , for the sake of the dress , no doubt , which they could not stop to pull off from it ; for the dress shone bright , where the fire strike it , as if with gold and jewel . I long in my heart to know most sadly what they would do with the little thing , and whether they would eat it . 
 it touch I so to see that child , a prey among those vulture , that in my foolish rage and burn I stand up and shout to they leap on a rock , and rave out of all possession . two of they turn round , and one set his carbine at I , but the other say it be but a pixie , and bid he keep his powder . little they know , and less think I , that the pixie then before they would dance their castle down one day . 
 John Fry , who in the spring of fright have bring himself down from Smiler 's side , as if he be dip in oil , now come up to I , all risk be over , cross , and stiff , and ache sorely from his wet couch of heather . 
 ' small thank to thee , Jan , as my new waife bain't   a widder . and who be you to zupport of she , and her son , if she have one ? zarve thee right if I be to chuck thee down into the Doone - track . Zim thee'll come to un , zooner or later , if this be the zample of thee . ' 
 and that be all he have to say , instead of thank God ! for if ever bear man be in a fright , and ready to thank God for anything , the name of that man be John Fry not more than five minute agone . 
 however , I answer nothing at all , except to be ashamed of myself ; and soon we find Peggy and Smiler in company , well embark on the homeward road , and victual where the grass be good . right glad they be to see we again not for the pleasure of carrying , but because a horse   like a woman   lack , and be well without , self - reliance . 
 my father never come to meet we , at either side of the telling - house , neither at the crooked post , nor even at home - linhay although the dog keep such a noise that he must have hear we . home - side of the linhay , and under the ashen hedge - row , where father teach I to catch blackbird , all at once my heart go down , and all my breast be hollow . there be not even the lanthorn light on the peg against the cow 's house , and nobody say ' hold your noise ! ' to the dog , or shout ' here our Jack be ! ' 
 I look at the post of the gate , in the dark , because they be tall , like father , and then at the door of the harness - room , where he use to smoke his pipe and sing . then I think he have guest perhaps people lose upon the moor whom he could not leave unkindly , even for his son 's sake . and yet about that I be jealous , and ready to be vex with he , when he should begin to make much of I . and I feel in my pocket for the new pipe which I have bring he from Tiverton , and say to myself , ' he shall not have it until to - morrow morning . ' 
 Woe be I ! I can not tell . how I know I know not now only that I slunk away , without a tear , or thought of weeping , and hide I in a saw - pit . there the timber , over - head , come like streak across I ; and all I want be to lack , and none to tell I anything . 
 by - and - by , a noise come down , as of woman 's weeping ; and there my mother and sister be , choke and hold together . although they be my dearest love , I could not bear to look at they , until they seem to want my help , and put their hand before their eye . 




 CHAPTER IV 

 a very rash visit 

 my dear father have be kill by the Doones of Bagworthy , while rid home from Porlock market , on the Saturday evening . with he be six brother - farmer , all of they very sober ; for father would have no company with any man who go beyond half a gallon of beer , or a single gallon of cider . the robber have no grudge against he ; for he have never flout they , neither make overmuch of outcry , because they rob other people . for he be a man of such strict honesty , and due parish feeling , that he know it to be every man 's own business to defend himself and his good ; unless he belong to our parish , and then we must look after he . 
 these seven good farmer be jog along , help one another in the trouble of the road , and sing goodly hymn and song to keep their courage move , when suddenly a horseman stop in the starlight full across they . 
 by dress and arm they know he well , and by his size and stature , show against the glimmer of the evening star ; and though he seem one man to seven , it be in truth one man to one . of the six who have be sing song and psalm about the power of God , and their own regeneration such psalm as go the round , in those day , of the public - house there be not one but pull out his money , and sing small beer to a Doone . 
 but father have be use to think that any man who be comfortable inside his own coat and waistcoat deserve to have no other set , unless he would strike a blow for they . and so , while his gossip doff their hat , and shake with what be leave of they , he set his staff above his head , and ride at the Doone robber . with a trick of his horse , the wild man escape the sudden onset , although it must have amaze he sadly that any durst resist he . then when Smiler be carry away with the dash and the weight of my father   not be bring up to battle , nor use to turn , save in plough harness , the outlaw whistle upon his thumb , and plunder the rest of the yeoman . but father , draw at Smiler 's head , to try to come back and help they , be in the midst of a dozen man , who seem to come out of a turf - rick , some on horse , and some a - foot . nevertheless , he smite lustily , so far as he could see ; and be of great size and strength , and his blood well up , they have no easy job with he . with the play of his wrist , he crack three or four crown , be always famous at single - stick ; until the rest draw their horse away , and he think that he be master , and would tell his wife about it . 
 but a man beyond the range of staff be crouch by the peat - stack , with a long gun set to his shoulder , and he get poor father against the sky , and I can not tell the rest of it . only they know that Smiler come home , with blood upon his wither , and father be find in the morning dead on the moor , with his ivy - twisted cudgel lie break under he . now , whether this be an honest fight , God judge betwixt the Doones and I . 
 it be more of woe than wonder , be such day of violence , that mother know herself a widow , and her child fatherless . of child there be only three , none of we fit to be useful yet , only to comfort mother , by make she to work for we . I , John Ridd , be the eld , and feel it a heavy thing on I ; next come sister Annie , with about two year between we ; and then the little Eliza . 
 now , before I get home and find my sad loss and no boy ever love his father more than I love mine mother have do a most wondrous thing , which make all the neighbour say that she must be mad , at least . upon the Monday morning , while her husband lie unburie , she cast a white hood over her hair , and gather a black cloak round she , and , take counsel of no one , set off on foot for the Doone - gate . 
 in the early afternoon she come to the hollow and barren entrance , where in truth there be no gate , only darkness to go through . if I get on with this story , I shall have to tell of it by - and - by , as I see it afterwards ; and will not dwell there now . enough that no gun be fire at she , only her eye be cover over , and somebody lead she by the hand , without any wish to hurt she . 
 a very rough and headstrong road be all that she remember , for she could not think as she wish to do , with the cold iron push against she . at the end of this road they deliver her eye , and she could scarce believe they . 
 for she stand at the head of a deep green valley , carve from out the mountain in a perfect oval , with a fence of sheer rock standing round it , eighty foot or a hundred high ; from whose brink black woode hill sweep up to the sky - line . by her side a little river glide out from underground with a soft dark babble , unawares of daylight ; then grow bright , lapse away , and fall into the valley . then , as it run down the meadow , alder stand on either marge , and grass be blade out upon it , and yellow tuft of rush gather , look at the hurry . but far down , on either bank , be cover house build of stone , square and roughly cornered , set as if the brook be mean to be the street between they . only one room high they be , and not place opposite each other , but in and out as skittle be ; only that the first of all , which prove to be the captain 's , be a sort of double house , or rather two house join together by a plank - bridge , over the river . 
 fourteen cot my mother count , all very much of a pattern , and nothing to choose between they , unless it be the captain 's . deep in the quiet valley there , away from noise , and violence , and brawl , save that of the rivulet , any man would have deem they home of simple mind and innocence . yet not a single house stand there but be the home of murder . 
 two man lead my mother down a steep and gliddery stair - way , like the ladder of a hay - mow ; and thence from the break of the fall water as far as the house of the captain . and there at the door they leave her trembling , string as she be , to speak her mind . 
 now , after all , what right have she , a common farmer 's widow , to take it amiss that man of birth think fit to kill her husband . and the Doones be of very high birth , as all we clod of Exmoor know ; and we have enough of good teaching now let any man say the contrary to feel that all we have belong of right to those above we . therefore my mother be half - ashamed that she could not help complain . 
 but after a little while , as she say , remembrance of her husband come , and the way he use to stand by her side and put his strong arm round she , and how he like his bacon fry , and praise she kindly for it and so the tear be in her eye , and nothing should gainsay they . 
 a tall old man , Sir Ensor Doone , come out with a bill - hook in his hand , hedger 's glove go up his arm , as if he be no well than a labourer at ditch - work . only in his mouth and eye , his gait , and most of all his voice , even a child could know and feel that here be no ditch - labourer . good cause he have find since then , perhaps , to wish that he have be one . 
 with his white lock move upon his coat , he stop and look down at my mother , and she could not help herself but curtsey under the fix black gazing . 
 ' good woman , you be none of we . who have bring you hither ? young man must be young but I have have too much of this work . ' 
 and he scowl at my mother , for her comeliness ; and yet look under his eyelid as if he like she for it . but as for she , in her depth of love - grief , it strike scorn upon her womanhood ; and in the flash she speak . 
 ' what you mean I know not . traitor ! cut - throat ! coward ! I be here to ask for my husband . ' she could not say any more , because her heart be now too much for she , come hard in her throat and mouth ; but she open up her eye at he . 
 ' Madam , ' say Sir Ensor Doone be bear a gentleman , although a very bad one ' I crave pardon of you . my eye be old , or I might have know . now , if we have your husband prisoner , he shall go free without ransom , because I have insult you . ' 
 ' Sir , ' say my mother , be suddenly take away with sorrow , because of his gracious manner , ' please to let I cry a bit . ' 
 he stand away , and seem to know that woman want no help for that . and by the way she cry he know that they have kill her husband . then , have feel of grief himself , he be not angry with she , but leave she to begin again . 
 ' Loth would I be , ' say mother , sob with her new red handkerchief , and look at the pattern of it , ' loth indeed , Sir Ensor Doone , to accuse any one unfairly . but I have lose the very good husband God ever give to a woman ; and I know he when he be to your belt , and I not up to your knee , sir ; and never an unkind word he speak , nor stop I short in speak . all the herb he leave to I , and all the bacon - curing , and when it be good to kill a pig , and how to treat the maiden . not that I would ever wish oh , John , it seem so strange to I , and last week you be everything . ' 
 here mother burst out cry again , not loudly , but turn quietly , because she know that no one now would ever care to wipe the tear . and fifty or a hundred thing , of weekly and daily happen , come across my mother , so that her spirit fall like slacken lime . 
 ' this matter must be see to ; it shall be see to at once , ' the old man answer , move a little in spite of all his knowledge . ' Madam , if any wrong have be do , trust the honour of a Doone ; I will redress it to my utmost . come inside and rest yourself , while I ask about it . what be your good husband 's name , and when and where fall this mishap ? ' 
 ' deary I , ' say mother , as he set a chair for her very polite , but she would not sit upon it ; ' Saturday morning I be a wife , sir ; and Saturday night I be a widow , and my child fatherless . my husband 's name be John Ridd , sir , as everybody know ; and there be not a fine or well man in Somerset or Devon . he be come home from Porlock market , and a new gown for I on the crupper , and a shell to put my hair up oh , John , how good you be to I ! ' 
 of that she begin to think again , and not to believe her sorrow , except as a dream from the evil one , because it be too bad upon she , and perhaps she would awake in a minute , and her husband would have the laugh of she . and so she wipe her eye and smile , and look for something . 
 ' Madam , this be a serious thing , ' Sir Ensor Doone say graciously , and show grave concern : ' my boy be a little wild , I know . and yet I can not think that they would willingly harm any one . and yet and yet , you do look wrong . send Counsellor to I , ' he shout , from the door of his house ; and down the valley go the call , ' send Counsellor to Captain . ' 
 Counsellor Doone come in ere yet my mother be herself again ; and if any sight could astonish she when all her sense of right and wrong be go astray with the force of thing , it be the sight of the Counsellor . a square - build man of enormous strength , but a foot below the Doone stature   which I shall describe hereafter , he carry a long grey beard descend to the leather of his belt . great eyebrow overhang his face , like ivy on a pollard oak , and under they two large brown eye , as of an owl when mute . and he have a power of hide his eye , or show they bright , like a blaze fire . he stand there with his beaver off , and mother try to look at he , but he seem not to descry she . 
 ' Counsellor , ' say Sir Ensor Doone , stand back in his height from he , ' here be a lady of good repute ' 
 ' oh , no , sir ; only a woman . ' 
 ' allow I , madam , by your good leave . here be a lady , Counsellor , of great repute in this part of the country , who charge the Doones with have unjustly slay her husband ' 
 ' murder he ! murder he ! ' cry my mother , ' if ever there be a murder . oh , sir ! oh , sir ! you know it . ' 
 ' the perfect right and truth of the case be all I wish to know , ' say the old man , very loftily : ' and justice shall be do , madam . ' 
 ' oh , I pray you pray you , sir , make no matter of business of it . God from Heaven , look on I ! ' 
 ' put the case , ' say the Counsellor . 
 ' the case be this , ' reply Sir Ensor , hold one hand up to mother : ' this lady 's worthy husband be slain , it seem , upon his return from the market at Porlock , no long ago than last Saturday night . Madam , amend I if I be wrong . ' 
 ' no long , indeed , indeed , sir . sometimes it seem a twelvemonth , and sometimes it seem an hour . ' 
 ' cite his name , ' say the Counsellor , with his eye still roll inward . 
 ' Master John Ridd , as I understand . counsellor , we have hear of he often ; a worthy man and a peaceful one , who meddle not with our duty . now , if any of our boy have be rough , they shall answer it dearly . and yet I can scarce believe it . for the folk about these part be apt to misconceive of our suffering , and to have no feeling for we . Counsellor , you be our record , and very stern against we ; tell we how this matter be . ' 
 ' oh , Counsellor ! ' my mother cry ; ' Sir Counsellor , you will be fair : I see it in your countenance . only tell I who it be , and set I face to face with he , and I will bless you , sir , and God shall bless you , and my child . ' 
 the square man with the long grey beard , quite unmove by anything , draw back to the door and speak , and his voice be like a fall of stone in the bottom of a mine . 
 ' few word will be enow for this . four or five of our well - behave and most peaceful gentleman go to the little market at Porlock with a lump of money . they buy some household store and comfort at a very high price , and prick upon the homeward road , away from vulgar reveller . when they draw bridle to rest their horse , in the shelter of a peat - rick , the night be dark and sudden , a robber of great size and strength ride into the midst of they , think to kill or terrify . his arrogance and hardihood at the first amazed they , but they would not give up without a blow good which be on trust with they . he have smite three of they senseless , for the power of his arm be terrible ; whereupon the last man try to ward his blow with a pistol . Carver , sir , it be , our brave and noble Carver , who save the life of his brother and his own ; and glad enow they be to escape . notwithstanding , we hop it might be only a flesh - wound , and not to speed he in his sin . ' 
 as this atrocious tale of lie turn up joint by joint before she , like a ' devil 's coach - horse , '   mother be too much amazed to do any more than look at he , as if the earth must open . but the only thing that open be the great brown eye of the Counsellor , which rest on my mother 's face with a dew of sorrow , as he speak of sin . 
    the cock - tail beetle have earn this name in the West of 
  England . 
 she , unable to bear they , turn suddenly on Sir Ensor , and catch   as she fancy   a smile on his lip , and a sense of quiet enjoyment . 
 ' all the Doones be gentleman , ' answer the old man gravely , and look as if he have never smile since he be a baby . ' we be always glad to explain , madam , any mistake which the rustic people may fall upon about we ; and we wish you clearly to conceive that we do not charge your poor husband with any set purpose of robbery , neither will we bring suit for any attainder of his property . be it not so , Counsellor ? ' 
 ' without doubt his land be attaint ; unless be mercy you forbear , sir . ' 
 ' Counsellor , we will forbear . Madam , we will forgive he . like enough he know not right from wrong , at that time of night . the water be strong at Porlock , and even an honest man may use his staff unjustly in this unchartered age of violence and rapine . ' 
 the Doones to talk of rapine ! Mother 's head go round so that she curtsey to they both , scarcely know where she be , but call to mind her manner . all the time she feel a warmth , as if the right be with she , and yet she could not see the way to spread it out before they . with that , she dry her tear in haste and go into the cold air , for fear of speak mischief . 
 but when she be on the homeward road , and the sentinel have charge of she , blind her eye , as if she be not blind enough with weeping , some one come in haste behind she , and thrust a heavy leathern bag into the limp weight of her hand . 
 ' captain send you this , ' he whisper ; ' take it to the little one . ' 
 but mother let it fall in a heap , as if it have be a blind worm ; and then for the first time crouch before God , that even the Doones should pity she . 




 CHAPTER V 

 AN ILLEGAL SETTLEMENT 

 good folk who dwell in a lawful land , if any such there be , may for want of exploration , judge our neighbourhood harshly , unless the whole truth be set before they . in bar of such prejudice , many of we ask leave to explain how and why it be the robber come to that head in the midst of we . we would rather not have have it so , God know as well as anybody ; but it grow upon we gently , in the follow manner . only let all who read observe that here I enter many thing which come to my knowledge in later year . 
 in or about the year of our Lord 1640 , when all the trouble of England be swell to an outburst , great estate in the North country be suddenly confiscate , through some feud of family and strong influence at Court , and the owner be turn upon the world , and might think themselves lucky to save their neck . these estate be in co - heirship , joint tenancy I think they call it , although I know not the meaning , only so that if either tenant die , the other living , all would come to the live one in spite of any testament . 
 one of the joint owner be Sir Ensor Doone , a gentleman of brisk intellect ; and the other owner be his cousin , the Earl of Lorne and Dykemont . 
 Lord Lorne be some year the elder of his cousin , Ensor Doone , and be make suit to gain severance of the cumbersome joint tenancy by any fair apportionment , when suddenly this blow fall on they by wile and woman 's meddling ; and instead of divide the land , they be divide from it . 
 the nobleman be still well - to - do , though cripple in his expenditure ; but as for the cousin , he be leave a beggar , with many to beg from he . he think that the other have wrong he , and that all the trouble of law befell through his unjust petition . many friend advise he to make interest at Court ; for have do no harm whatever , and be a good Catholic , which Lord Lorne be not , he would be sure to find hearing there , and probably some favour . but he , like a very hot - brain man , although he have long be married to the daughter of his cousin   whom he like none the more for that , would have nothing to say to any attempt at make a patch of it , but drive away with his wife and son , and the relic of his money , swear hard at everybody . in this he may have be quite wrong ; probably , perhaps , he be so ; but I be not convince at all but what most of we would have do the same . 
 some say that , in the bitterness of that wrong and outrage , he slay a gentleman of the Court , whom he suppose to have bear a hand in the plundering of his fortune . other say that he beard King Charles the First himself , in a manner beyond forgiveness . one thing , at any rate , be sure Sir Ensor be attaint , and make a felon outlaw , through some violent deed ensue upon his dispossession . 
 he have search in many quarter for somebody to help he , and with good warrant for hop it , inasmuch as he , in lucky day , have be open - handed and cousinly to all who beg advice of he . but now all these provide he with plenty of good advice indeed , and great assurance of feeling , but not a movement of leg , or lip , or purse - string in his favour . all good people of either persuasion , royalty or commonalty , know his kitchen - range to be cold , no long would play turnspit . and this , it may be , sear his heart more than loss of land and fame . 
 in great despair at last , he resolve to settle in some outlandish part , where none could be find to know he ; and so , in an evil day for we , he come to the West of England . not that our part of the world be at all outlandish , accord to my view of it   for I never find a well one , but that it be know to be rugge , and large , and desolate . and here , when he have discover a place which seem almost to be make for he , so withdraw , so self - defend , and uneasy of access , some of the country - folk around bring he little offering a side of bacon , a keg of cider , hung mutton , or a brisket of venison ; so that for a little while he be very honest . but when the newness of his coming begin to wear away , and our good folk be apt to think that even a gentleman ought to work or pay other man for do it , and many farmer be grow weary of manner without discourse to they , and all cry out to one another how unfair it be that own such a fertile valley young man would not spade or plough by reason of noble lineage then the young Doones grow up take thing they would not ask for . 
 and here let I , as a solid man , owner of five hundred acre   whether fenced or otherwise , and that be my own business , churchwarden also of this parish   until I go to the churchyard , and proud to be call the parson 's friend for a well man I never know with tobacco and strong water , nor one who could read the lesson so well and he have be at Blundell 's too once for all let I declare , that I be a thorough - go Church - and - state man , and Royalist , without any mistake about it . and this I lie down , because some people judge a sausage by the skin , may take in evil part my little gloss of style and glibness , and the mottled nature of my remark and crack now and then on the frying - pan . I assure they I be good inside , and not a bit of rue in I ; only queer knot , as of marjoram , and a stupid manner of burst . 
 there be not more than a dozen of they , count a few retainer who still hold by Sir Ensor ; but soon they grow and multiply in a manner surprising to think of . whether it be the venison , which we call a strengthen victual , or whether it be the Exmoor mutton , or the keen soft air of the moorland , anyhow the Doones increase much fast than their honesty . at first they have bring some lady with they , of good repute with charity ; and then , as time go on , they add to their stock by carry . they carry off many good farmer ' daughter , who be sadly displeased at first ; but take to they kindly after awhile , and make a new home in their baby . for woman , as it seem to I , like strong man more than weak one , feel that they need some staunchness , something to hold fast by . 
 and of all the man in our country , although we be of a thick - set breed , you scarce could find one in three - score fit to be place among the Doones , without look no more than a tailor . like enough , we could meet they man for man   if we choose all around the crown and the skirt of Exmoor , and show they what a cross - buttock mean , because we be so stuggy ; but in regard of stature , comeliness , and bearing , no woman would look twice at we . not but what I myself , John Ridd , and one or two I know of but it become I good not to talk of that , although my hair be gray . 
 perhaps their den might well have be storm , and themselves drive out of the forest , if honest people have only agree to begin with they at once when first they take to plunder . but have respect for their good birth , and pity for their misfortune , and perhaps a little admiration at the justice of God , that rob man now be robber , the squire , and farmer , and shepherd , at first do nothing more than grumble gently , or even make a laugh of it , each in the case of other . after awhile they find the matter go too far for laughter , as violence and deadly outrage stain the hand of robbery , until every woman clutch her child , and every man turn pale at the very name of Doone . for the son and grandson of Sir Ensor grow up in foul liberty , and haughtiness , and hatre , to utter scorn of God and man , and brutality towards dumb animal . there be only one good thing about they , if indeed it be good , to wit , their faith to one another , and truth to their wild eyry . but this only make they fear the more , so certain be the revenge they wreak upon any who dare to strike a Doone . one night , some ten year ere I be bear , when they be sack a rich man 's house not very far from Minehead , a shot be fire at they in the dark , of which they take little notice , and only one of they know that any harm be do . but when they be well on the homeward road , not have slay either man or woman , or even burn a house down , one of their number fall from his saddle , and die without so much as a groan . the youth have be strike , but would not complain , and perhaps take little heed of the wound , while he be bleed inwardly . his brother and cousin lay he softly on a bank of whortle - berry , and just ride back to the lonely hamlet where he have take his death - wound . no man nor woman be leave in the morning , nor house for any to dwell in , only a child with its reason go . 
   this vile deed be do , beyond all doubt . 
 this affair make prudent people find more reason to let they alone than to meddle with they ; and now they have so entrench themselves , and wax so strong in number , that nothing less than a troop of soldier could wisely enter their premise ; and even so it might turn out ill , as perchance we shall see by - and - by .
