 for not to mention the strength of the place , which I shall describe in its proper order when I come to visit it , there be not one among they but be a mighty man , straight and tall , and wide , and fit to lift four hundredweight . if son or grandson of old Doone , or one of the northern retainer , fail at the age of twenty , while stand on his naked foot to touch with his forehead the lintel of Sir Ensor 's door , and to fill the door frame with his shoulder from sidepost even to sidepost , he be lead away to the narrow pass which make their valley so desperate , and thrust from the crown with ignominy , to get his own living honestly . now , the measure of that doorway be , or rather be , I ought to say , six foot and one inch lengthwise , and two foot all but two inch take crossway in the clear . yet I not only have hear but know , be so closely mixed with they , that no descendant of old Sir Ensor , neither relative of his   except , indeed , the Counsellor , who be keep by they for his wisdom , and no more than two of their following ever fail of that test , and relapse to the difficult way of honesty . 
 not that I think anything great of a standard the like of that : for if they have set I in that door - frame at the age of twenty , it be like enough that I should have walk away with it on my shoulder , though I be not come to my full strength then : only I be speak now of the average size of our neighbourhood , and the Doones be far beyond that . moreover , they be teach to shoot with a heavy carbine so delicately and wisely , that even a boy could pass a ball through a rabbit 's head at the distance of fourscore yard . some people may think nought of this , be in practice with long shot from the tongue than from the shoulder ; nevertheless , to do as above be , to my ignorance , very good work , if you can be sure to do it . not one word do I believe of Robin Hood split peel wand at seven - score yard , and such like . whoever write such story know not how slippery a peeled wand be , even if one could hit it , and how it give to the onset . now , let he stick one in the ground , and take his bow and arrow at it , ten yard away , or even five . 
 now , after all this which I have write , and all the rest which a reader will see , be quick of mind than I be   who leave more than half behind I , like a man sow wheat , with his dinner lay in the ditch too near his dog , it be much but what you will understand the Doones far well than I do , or do even to this moment ; and therefore none will doubt when I tell they that our good justiciary fear to make an ado , or hold any public inquiry about my dear father 's death . they would all have have to ride home that night , and who could say what might betide they . Least say soon mend , because less chance of break . 
 so we bury he quietly all except my mother , indeed , for she could not keep silence in the sloping little churchyard of Oare , as meek a place as need be , with the Lynn brook down below it . there be not much of company there for anybody 's tombstone , because the parish spread so far in wood and moor without dwelling - house . if we bury one man in three year , or even a woman or child , we talk about it for three month , and say it must be our turn next , and scarcely grow accustomed to it until another go . 
 Annie be not allow to come , because she cry so terribly ; but she run to the window , and see it all , moo there like a little calf , so frightened and so left alone . as for Eliza , she come with I , one on each side of mother , and not a tear be in her eye , but sudden start of wonder , and a new thing to be look at unwillingly , yet curiously . poor little thing ! she be very clever , the only one of our family thank God for the same but none the more for that guess she what it be to lose a father . 




 CHAPTER VI 

 NECESSARY practice 

 about the rest of all that winter I remember very little , be only a young boy then , and miss my father most out of door , as when it come to the bird - catch , or the tracking of hare in the snow , or the training of a sheep - dog . oftentime I look at his gun , an ancient piece find in the sea , a little below Glenthorne , and of which he be mighty proud , although it be only a match - lock ; and I think of the time I have hold the fuse , while he get his aim at a rabbit , and once even at a red deer rub among the hazel . but nothing come of my look at it , so far as I remember , save foolish tear of my own perhaps , till John Fry take it down one day from the hook where father 's hand have lay it ; and it hurt I to see how John handle it , as if he have no memory . 
 ' bad job for he as she have not get thiccy the naight as her coom acrass they Doones . Rackon Varmer Jan ' ood a - zhown they the wai to kingdom come , ' stead of gooin ' herzel zo aisy . and a maight have be gooin ' to market now , ' stead of lay bank up over yanner . Maister Jan , thee can zee the grave if thee look alang this here goon - barryel . buy now , whutt be blubberin ' at ? Wish I have never tell thee . ' 
 ' John Fry , I be not blubber ; you make a great mistake , John . you be think of little Annie . I cough sometimes in the winter - weather , and father give I lickerish I mean I mean he use to . now let I have the gun , John . ' 
 ' thee have the goon , Jan ! Thee be not   fit to putt un to thy zhoulder . what a weight she be , for sure ! ' 
 ' I not hold it , John ! that show how much you know about it . get out of the way , John ; you be opposite the mouth of it , and likely it be load . ' 
 John Fry jump in a lively manner than when he be do day - work ; and I rest the mouth on a cross rack - piece , and feel a warm sort of surety that I could hit the door over opposite , or , at least , the cobwall alongside of it , and do no harm in the orchard . but John would not give I link or fuse , and , on the whole , I be glad of it , though carry on as boy do , because I have hear my father say that the spanish gun kick like a horse , and because the load in it come from his hand , and I do not like to undo it . but I never find it kick very hard , and firmly set to the shoulder , unless it be badly load . in truth , the thickness of the metal be enough almost to astonish one ; and what our people say about it may have be true enough , although most of they be such liar at least , I mean , they make mistake , as all mankind must do . perchance it be no mistake at all to say that this ancient gun have belong to a noble Spaniard , the captain of a fine large ship in the ' Invincible Armada , ' which we of England manage to conquer , with God and the weather help we , a hundred year ago or more I can not   say to a month or so . 
 after a little while , when John have fire away at a rat the charge I hold so sacred , it come to I as a natural thing to practise shoot with that great gun , instead of John Fry 's blunderbuss , which look like a bell with a stalk to it . perhaps for a boy there be nothing well than a good windmill to shoot at , as I have see they in flat country ; but we have no windmill upon the great moorland , yet here and there a few barn - door , where shelter be , and a way up the hollow . and up those hollow you can shoot , with the help of the side to lead your aim , and there be a fair chance of hit the door , if you lay your cheek to the barrel , and try not to be afraid of it . 
 gradually I win such skill , that I send nearly all the lead gutter from the north porch of our little church through our good barn - door , a thing which have often repent I since , especially as churchwarden , and make I pardon many bad boy ; but father be not bury on that side of the church . 
 but all this time , while I be rove over the hill or about the farm , and even listen to John Fry , my mother , be so much old and feel trouble long , go about inside the house , or among the maid and fowl , not care to talk to the good of they , except when she break out sometimes about the good master they have lose , all and every one of we . but the fowl would take no notice of it , except to cluck for barley ; and the maiden , though they have like he well , be think of their sweetheart as the spring come on . Mother think it wrong of they , selfish and ungrateful ; and yet sometimes she be proud that none have such call as herself to grieve for he . only Annie seem to go softly in and out , and cry , with nobody along of she , chiefly in the corner where the bee be and the grindstone . but somehow she would never let anybody behold she ; be set , as you may say , to think it over by herself , and season it with weeping . many time I catch she , and many time she turn upon I , and then I could not look at she , but ask how long to dinner - time . 
 now in the depth of the winter month , such as we call December , father be dead and quiet in his grave a fortnight , it happen I to be out of powder for practice against his enemy . I have never fire a shot without think , ' this for father 's murderer ' ; and John Fry say that I make such face it be a wonder the gun go off . but though I could hardly hold the gun , unless with my back against a bar , it do I good to hear it go off , and hope to have hitten his enemy . 
 ' oh , mother , mother , ' I say that day , directly after dinner , while she be sit look at I , and almost ready to say   as now she do seven time in a week , ' how like your father you be grow ! Jack , come here and kiss I ' ' oh , mother , if you only know how much I want a shilling ! ' 
 ' jack , you shall never want a shilling while I be alive to give thee one . but what be it for , dear heart , dear heart ? ' 
 ' to buy something over at Porlock , mother . perhaps I will tell you afterwards . if I tell not it will be for your good , and for the sake of the child . ' 
 ' bless the boy , one would think he be threescore year of age at least . give I a little kiss , you Jack , and you shall have the shilling . ' 
 for I hat to kiss or be kiss in those day : and so all honest boy must do , when God put any strength in they . but now I want the powder so much that I go and kiss mother very shyly , look round the corner first , for Betty not to see I . 
 but mother give I half a dozen , and only one shilling for all of they ; and I could not find it in my heart to ask she for another , although I would have take it . in very quick time I run away with the shilling in my pocket , and get Peggy out on the Porlock road without my mother know it . for mother be frightened of that road now , as if all the tree be murderer , and would never let I go alone so much as a hundred yard on it . and , to tell the truth , I be touch with fear for many year about it ; and even now , when I ride at dark there , a man by a peat - rick make I shiver , until I go and collar he . but this time I be very bold , have John Fry 's blunderbuss , and keep a sharp look - out wherever any lurking place be . however , I see only sheep and small red cattle , and the common deer of the forest , until I be nigh to Porlock town , and then ride straight to Mr. Pooke 's , at the sign of the Spit and Gridiron . 
 Mr. Pooke be asleep , as it happen , not have much to do that day ; and so I fasten Peggy by the handle of a warming - pan , at which she have no well manner than to snort and blow her breath ; and in I walk with a manful style , bear John Fry 's blunderbuss . now Timothy Pooke be a peaceful man , glad to live without any enjoyment of mind at danger , and I be tall and large already as most lad of a riper age . Mr. Pooke , as soon as he open his eye , drop suddenly under the counting - board , and draw a great frying - pan over his head , as if the Doones be come to rob he , as their custom be , mostly after the fair - time . it make I feel rather hot and queer to be take for a robber ; and yet methink I be proud of it . 
 ' Gadzooks , Master Pooke , ' say I , have learn fine word at Tiverton ; ' do you suppose that I know not then the way to carry firearm ? an it be the old spanish match - lock in the lieu of this good flint - engine , which may be bear ten mile or more and never once go off , scarcely couldst thou seem more scared . I might point at thee muzzle on just so as I do now even for an hour or more , and like enough it would never shoot thee , unless I pull the trigger hard , with a crock upon my finger ; so you see ; just so , Master Pooke , only a trifle hard . ' 
 ' God sake , John Ridd , God sake , dear boy , ' cry Pooke , know I by this time ; ' do not   ' e , for good love now , do not   ' e show it to I , boy , as if I be to suck it . put ' un down , for good , now ; and thee shall have the very good of all be in the shop . ' 
 ' Ho ! ' I reply with much contempt , and swing round the gun so that it fetch his hoop of candle down , all unkindle as they be : ' ho ! as if I have not attain to the handling of a gun yet ! my hand be cold come over the moor , else would I go bail to point the mouth at you for an hour , sir , and no cause for uneasiness . ' 
 but in spite of all assurance , he show himself desirous only to see the last of my gun and I . I dare say ' villainous saltpetre , ' as the great playwright call it , be never so cheap before nor since . for my shilling Master Pooke afford I two great package over - large to go into my pocket , as well as a mighty chunk of lead , which I bind upon Peggy 's wither . and as if all this have not be enough , he present I with a roll of comfit for my sister Annie , whose gentle face and pretty manner win the love of everybody . 
 there be still some daylight here and there as I rise the hill above Porlock , wonder whether my mother would be in a fright , or would not know it . the two great package of powder , sling behind my back , knock so hard against one another that I fear they must either spill or blow up , and hurry I over Peggy 's ear from the woollen cloth I ride upon . for father always like a horse to have some wool upon his loin whenever he go far from home , and have to stand about , where one please , hot , and wet , and pant . and father always say that saddle be mean for man full - grown and heavy , and lose their activity ; and no boy or young man on our farm durst ever get into a saddle , because they all know that the master would chuck they out pretty quickly . as for I , I have try it once , from a kind of curiosity ; and I could not walk for two or three day , the leather gall my knee so . but now , as Peggy bear I bravely , snort every now and then into a cloud of air , for the night be grow frosty , presently the moon arise over the shoulder of a hill , and the pony and I be half glad to see she , and half afraid of the shadow she throw , and the image all around we . I be ready at any moment to shoot at anybody , have great faith in my blunderbuss , but hop not to prove it . and as I pass the narrow place where the Doones have kill my father , such a fear break out upon I that I lean upon the neck of Peggy , and shut my eye , and be cold all over . however , there be not a soul to be see , until we come home to the old farmyard , and there be my mother cry sadly , and Betty Muxworthy scold . 
 ' come along , now , ' I whisper to Annie , the moment supper be over ; ' and if you can hold your tongue , Annie , I will show you something . ' 
 she lift herself on the bench so quickly , and flush so rich with pleasure , that I be oblige to stare hard away , and make Betty look beyond we . Betty think I have something hide in the closet beyond the clock - case , and she be the more convinced of it by reason of my denial . not that Betty Muxworthy , or any one else , for that matter , ever find I in a falsehood , because I never tell one , not even to my mother or , which be still a strong thing , not even to my sweetheart   when I grow up to have one   but that Betty be wrong in the matter of marriage , a generation or two agone , by a man who come hedge and ditching , have now no mercy , except to believe that man from cradle to grave be liar , and woman fool to look at they . 
 when Betty could find no crime of mine , she knock I out of the way in a minute , as if I have be nobody ; and then she begin to coax ' Mistress Annie , ' as she always call she , and draw the soft hair down her hand , and whisper into the little ear . meanwhile , dear mother be fall asleep , have be trouble so much about I ; and Watch , my father 's pet dog , be nod close and close up into her lap . 
 ' now , Annie , will you come ? ' I say , for I want she to hold the ladle for melt of the lead ; ' will you come at once , Annie ? or must I go for Lizzie , and let she see the whole of it ? ' 
 ' indeed , then , you will not   do that , ' say Annie ; ' lizzie to come before I , John ; and she can not   stir a pot of brewis , and scarce know a tongue from a ham , John , and say it make no difference , because both be good to eat ! oh , Betty , what do you think of that to come of all her book - learning ? ' 
 ' thank God he can not   say that of I , ' Betty answer shortly , for she never care about argument , except on her own side ; ' thank he , I say , every marne a'most , never to lead I astray so . man be desaving and so be galanie ; but the most desaving of all be book , with their head and tail , and the speckot in them , lik a peg as have take the maisle . some folk purtend to laugh and cry over they . God forgive they for liar ! ' 
 it be part of Betty 's obstinacy that she never would believe in reading or the possibility of it , but stoutly maintain to the very last that people first learn thing by heart , and then pretend to make they out from pattern do upon paper , for the sake of astonishing honest folk just as do the conjurer . and even to see the parson and clerk be not enough to convince she ; all she say be , ' it make no odd , they be all the same as the rest of we . ' and now that she have be on the farm nigh upon forty year , and have nurse my father , and make his clothe , and all that he have to eat , and then put he in his coffin , she be come to such authority , that it be not worth the wage of the good man on the place to say a word in answer to Betty , even if he would face the risk to have ten for one , or twenty . 
 Annie be her love and joy . for Annie she would do anything , even so far as to try to smile , when the little maid laugh and dance to she . and in truth I know not how it be , but every one be take with Annie at the very first time of see she . she have such pretty way and manner , and such a look of kindness , and a sweet soft light in her long blue eye full of trustful gladness . everybody who look at she seem to grow the well for it , because she know no evil . and then the turn she have for cooking , you never would have expect it ; and how it be her rich mirth to see that she have please you . I have be out on the world a vast deal as you will own hereafter , and yet have I never see Annie 's equal for make a weary man comfortable . 




 CHAPTER VII 

 hard it be to CLIMB 

 so many a winter night go by in a hopeful and pleasant manner , with the hissing of the bright round bullet , cast into the water , and the spluttering of the great red apple which Annie be roast for I . we always manage our evening 's work in the chimney of the back - kitchen , where there be room to set chair and table , in spite of the fire burning . on the right - hand side be a mighty oven , where Betty threaten to bake we ; and on the left , long side of bacon , make of favoured pig , and grow very brown and comely . Annie know the name of all , and run up through the wood - smoke , every now and then , when a gentle memory move she , and ask they how they be get on , and when they would like to be eat . then she come back with foolish tear , at thinking of that necessity ; and I , be soft in a different way , would make up my mind against bacon . 
 but , Lord bless you ! it be no good . whenever it come to breakfast - time , after three hour upon the moor , I regularly forget the pig , but pay good heed to the rasher . for our be a hungry county , if such there be in England ; a place , I mean , where man must eat , and be quick to discharge the duty . the air of the moor be so shrewd and wholesome , stir a man 's recollection of the good thing which have betide he , and whet his hope of something still well in the future , that by the time he sit down to a cloth , his heart and stomach be tune too well to say ' nay ' to one another . 
 almost everybody know , in our part of the world at least , how pleasant and soft the fall of the land be round about Plover 's Barrows farm . all above it be strong dark mountain , spread with heath , and desolate , but near our house the valley cove , and open warmth and shelter . here be tree , and bright green grass , and orchard full of contentment , and a man may scarce espy the brook , although he hear it everywhere . and indeed a stout good piece of it come through our farm - yard , and swell sometimes to a rush of wave , when the cloud be on the hill - top . but all below , where the valley bend , and the Lynn stream come along with it , pretty meadow slope their breast , and the sun spread on the water . and nearly all of this be ours , till you come to Nicholas Snowe 's land . 
 but about two mile below our farm , the Bagworthy water run into the Lynn , and make a real river of it . thence it hurry away , with strength and a force of wilful water , under the foot of a bareface hill , and so to rock and wood again , where the stream be cover over , and dark , heavy pool delay it . there be plenty of fish all down this way , and the far you go the large they get , have deep ground to feed in ; and sometimes in the summer month , when mother could spare I off the farm , I come down here , with Annie to help   because it be so lonely , and catch well - nigh a basketful of little trout and minnow , with a hook and a bit of worm on it , or a fern - web , or a blow - fly , hang from a hazel pulse - stick . for of all the thing I learn at Blundell 's , only two abode with I , and one of these be the knack of fishing , and the other the art of swimming . and indeed they have a very rude manner of teach child to swim there ; for the big boy take the little boy , and put they through a certain process , which they grimly call ' sheep - washing . ' in the third meadow from the gate of the school , go up the river , there be a fine pool in the Lowman , where the Taunton brook come in , and they call it the Taunton Pool . the water run down with a strong sharp stickle , and then have a sudden elbow in it , where the small brook trickle in ; and on that side the bank be steep , four or it may be five foot high , overhang loamily ; but on the other side it be flat , pebbly , and fit to land upon . now the large boy take the small boy , cry sadly for mercy , and think mayhap , of their mother , with hand lay well at the back of their neck , they bring they up to the crest of the bank upon the eastern side , and make they strip their clothe off . then the little boy , fall on their naked knee , blubber upwards piteously ; but the large boy know what be good for they , and will not be entreat . so they cast they down , one after other into the splash of the water , and watch they go to the bottom first , and then come up and fight for it , with a blowing and a bubbling . it be a very fair sight to watch when you know there be little danger , because , although the pool be deep , the current be sure to wash a boy up on the stone , where the end of the depth be . as for I , they have no need to throw I more than once , because I jump of my own accord , think small thing of the Lowman , after the violent Lynn . nevertheless , I learn to swim there , as all the other boy do ; for the great point in learn that be to find that you must do it . I love the water naturally , and could not long be out of it ; but even the boy who hat it most , come to swim in some fashion or other , after they have be fling for a year or two into the Taunton pool . 
 but now , although my sister Annie come to keep I company , and be not to be part from I by the trick of the Lynn stream , because I put she on my back and carry she across , whenever she could not leap it , or tuck up her thing and take the stone ; yet so it happen that neither of we have be up the Bagworthy water . we know that it bring a good stream down , as full of fish as of pebble ; and we think that it must be very pretty to make a way where no way be , nor even a bullock come down to drink . but whether we be afraid or not , I be sure I can not tell , because it be so long ago ; but I think that have something to do with it . for Bagworthy water run out of Doone valley , a mile or so from the mouth of it . 
 but when I be turn fourteen year old , and put into good small - clothe , buckle at the knee , and strong blue worst hosen , knit by my mother , it happen to I without choice , I may say , to explore the Bagworthy water . and it come about in this wise . 
 my mother have long be ail , and not well able to eat much ; and there be nothing that frighten we so much as for people to have no love of their victual . now I chance to remember that once at the time of the holiday I have bring dear mother from Tiverton a jar of pickle loach , catch by myself in the Lowman river , and bake in the kitchen oven , with vinegar , a few leaf of bay , and about a dozen pepper - corn . and mother have say that in all her life she have never taste anything fit to be compare with they . whether she say so good a thing out of compliment to my skill in catch the fish and cook they , or whether she really mean it , be more than I can tell , though I quite believe the latter , and so would most people who taste they ; at any rate , I now resolve to get some loach for she , and do they in the self - same manner , just to make she eat a bit . 
 there be many people , even now , who have not come to the right knowledge what a loach be , and where he live , and how to catch and pickle he . and I will not tell they all about it , because if I do , very likely there would be no loach leave ten or twenty year after the appearance of this book . a pickle minnow be very good if you catch he in a stickle , with the scarlet finger upon he ; but I count he no more than the rope in beer compare with a loach do properly . 
 be resolve to catch some loach , whatever trouble it cost I , I set forth without a word to any one , in the forenoon of St. Valentine 's day , 1675 - 6 , I think it must have be . Annie should not come with I , because the water be too cold ; for the winter have be long , and snow lie here and there in patch in the hollow of the bank , like a lady 's glove forget . and yet the spring be break forth , as it always do in Devonshire , when the turn of the day be over ; and though there be little to see of it , the air be full of feeling . 
 it puzzle I now , that I remember all those young impression so , because I take no heed of they at the time whatever ; and yet they come upon I bright , when nothing else be evident in the gray fog of experience . I be like an old man gaze at the outside of his spectacle , and see , as he rub the dust , the image of his grandson play at bo - peep with he . 
 but let I be of any age , I never could forget that day , and how bitter cold the water be . for I doff my shoe and hose , and put they into a bag about my neck ; and leave my little coat at home , and tie my shirt - sleeve back to my shoulder . then I take a three - pronged fork firmly bind to a rod with cord , and a piece of canvas kerchief , with a lump of bread inside it ; and so go into the pebbly water , try to think how warm it be . for more than a mile all down the Lynn stream , scarcely a stone I leave unturned , be thoroughly skilled in the trick of the loach , and know how he hide himself . for be gray - spot , and clear to see through , and something like a cuttle - fish , only more substantial , he will stay quite still where a streak of weed be in the rapid water , hop to be overlook , not care even to wag his tail . then be disturb he flip away , like whalebone from the finger , and hie to a shelf of stone , and lie with his sharp head poke in under it ; or sometimes he belly he into the mud , and only show his back - ridge . and that be the time to spear he nicely , hold the fork very gingerly , and allow for the bent of it , which come to pass , I know not how , at the tickle of air and water . 
 or if your loach should not be abroad when first you come to look for he , but keep snug in his little home , then you may see he come forth amazed at the quivering of the shingle , and oar himself and look at you , and then dart up - stream , like a little grey streak ; and then you must try to mark he in , and follow very daintily . so after that , in a sandy place , you steal up behind his tail to he , so that he can not set eye on you , for his head be up - stream always , and there you see he abide still , clear , and mild , and affable . then , as he look so innocent , you make full sure to prog he well , in spite of the wry of the water , and the sun make elbow to everything , and the trembling of your finger . but when you gird at he lovingly , and have as good as get he , lo ! in the go - by of the river he be go as a shadow go , and only a little cloud of mud curl away from the point of the fork . 
 a long way down that limpid water , chill and bright as an iceberg , go my little self that day on man 's choice errand destruction . all the young fish seem to know that I be one who have take out God 's certificate , and mean to have the value of it ; every one of they be aware that we desolate more than replenish the earth . for a cow might come and look into the water , and put she yellow lip down ; a kingfisher , like a blue arrow , might shoot through the dark alley over the channel , or sit on a dip withy - bough with his beak sink into his breast - feather ; even an otter might float downstream liken himself to a log of wood , with his flat head flush with the water - top , and his oily eye peer quietly ; and yet no panic would seize other life , as it do when a sample of man come . 
 now let not any one suppose that I think of these thing when I be young , for I know not the way to do it . and proud enough in truth I be at the universal fear I spread in all those lonely place , where I myself must have be afraid , if anything have come up to I . it be all very pretty to see the tree big with their hope of another year , though dumb as yet on the subject , and the water murmur gaiety , and the bank spread out with comfort ; but a boy take none of this to heart ; unless he be mean for a poet   which God can never charge upon I , and he would liefer have a good apple , or even a bad one , if he steal it . 
 when I have travel two mile or so , conquer now and then with cold , and come out to rub my leg into a lively friction , and only fish here and there , because of the tumble water ; suddenly , in an open space , where meadow spread about it , I find a good stream flow softly into the body of our brook . and it bring , so far as I could guess by the sweep of it under my knee - cap , a large power of clear water than the Lynn itself have ; only it come more quietly down , not be trouble with stair and step , as the fortune of the Lynn be , but glide smoothly and forcibly , as if upon some set purpose . 
 Hereupon I draw up and think , and reason be much inside I ; because the water be bitter cold , and my little toe be ache . so on the bank I rub they well with a sprout of young sting - nettle , and have skip about awhile , be kindly incline to eat a bit . 
 now all the turn of all my life hang upon that moment . but as I sit there munch a crust of Betty Muxworthy 's sweet brown bread , and a bit of cold bacon along with it , and kick my little red heel against the dry loam to keep they warm , I know no more than fish under the fork what be go on over I . it seem a sad business to go back now and tell Annie there be no loach ; and yet it be a frightful thing , know what I do of it , to venture , where no grown man durst , up the Bagworthy water . and please to recollect that I be only a boy in those day , fond enough of anything new , but not like a man to meet it . 
 however , as I eat more and more , my spirit arise within I , and I think of what my father have be , and how he have tell I a hundred time never to be a coward . and then I grow warm , and my little heart be ashamed of its pit - a - patting , and I say to myself , ' now if father look , he shall see that I obey he . ' so I put the bag round my back again , and buckle my breech far up from the knee , expect deep water , and cross the Lynn , go stoutly up under the branch which hang so dark on the Bagworthy river . 
 I find it strongly over - weave , turn , and tear with thicket - wood , but not so rocky as the Lynn , and more inclined to go evenly . there be bar of chafe stake stretch from the side half - way across the current , and light outrider of pithy weed , and blade of last year 's water - grass trembling in the quiet place , like a spider 's thread , on the transparent stillness , with a tint of olive move it . and here and there the sun come in , as if his light be sift , make dance upon the wave , and shadow the pebble . 
 here , although affright often by the deep , dark place , and feel that every step I take might never be take backward , on the whole I have very comely sport of loach , trout , and minnow , fork some , and tickle some , and drive other to shallow nook , whence I could bail they ashore . now , if you have ever be fish , you will not wonder that I be lead on , forget all about danger , and take no heed of the time , but shout in a childish way whenever I catch a ' whacker '   as we call a big fish at Tiverton ; and in sooth there be very fine loach here , have more lie and harbourage than in the rough Lynn stream , though not quite so large as in the Lowman , where I have even take they to the weight of half a pound . 
 but in answer to all my shout there never be any sound at all , except of a rocky echo , or a scared bird hustle away , or the sudden dive of a water - vole ; and the place grow thick and thick , and the covert grow dark above I , until I think that the fish might have good chance of eat I , instead of my eat the fish . 
 for now the day be fall fast behind the brown of the hill - top , and the tree , be void of leaf and hard , seem giant ready to beat I . and every moment as the sky be clear up for a white frost , the cold of the water get bad and bad , until I be fit to cry with it . and so , in a sorry plight , I come to an opening in the bush , where a great black pool lie in front of I , whiten with snow   as I think   at the side , till I see it be only foam - froth . 
 now , though I could swim with great ease and comfort , and fear no depth of water , when I could fairly come to it , yet I have no desire to go over head and ear into this great pool , be so cramped and weary , and cold enough in all conscience , though wet only up to the middle , not count my arm and shoulder . and the look of this black pit be enough to stop one from dive into it , even on a hot summer 's day with sunshine on the water ; I mean , if the sun ever shine there . as it be , I shudder and draw back ; not alone at the pool itself and the black air there be about it , but also at the whirling manner , and wisping of white thread upon it in stripy circle round and round ; and the centre still as jet . 
 but soon I see the reason of the stir and depth of that great pit , as well as of the roar sound which long have make I wonder . for skirt round one side , with very little comfort , because the rock be high and steep , and the ledge at the foot so narrow , I come to a sudden sight and marvel , such as I never dream of . for , lo ! I stand at the foot of a long pale slide of water , come smoothly to I , without any break or hindrance , for a hundred yard or more , and fence on either side with cliff , sheer , and straight , and shin . the water neither run nor fall , nor leap with any spouting , but make one even slope of it , as if it have be comb or plan , and look like a plank of deal lay down a deep black staircase . however , there be no side - rail , nor any place to walk upon , only the channel a fathom wide , and the perpendicular wall of crag shut out the evening . 
 the look of this place have a sad effect , scar I very greatly , and make I feel that I would give something only to be at home again , with Annie cook my supper , and our dog Watch sniff upward . but nothing would come of wish ; that I have long find out ; and it only make one the less inclined to work without white feather . so I lay the case before I in a little council ; not for loss of time , but only that I want rest , and to see thing truly . 
 then say I to myself ' John Ridd , these tree , and pool , and lonesome rock , and set of the sunlight be make a gruesome coward of thee . shall I go back to my mother so , and be call she fearless boy ? ' 
 nevertheless , I be free to own that it be not any fine sense of shame which settle my decision ; for indeed there be nearly as much of danger in go back as in go on , and perhaps even more of labour , the journey be so roundabout . but that which save I from turn back be a strange inquisitive desire , very unbecoming in a boy of little year ; in a word , I would risk a great deal to know what make the water come down like that , and what there be at the top of it . 
 therefore , see hard strife before I , I girt up my breech anew , with each buckle one hole tight , for the sodden strap be stretch and give , and mayhap my leg be grow small from the coldness of it . then I bestow my fish around my neck more tightly , and not stop to look much , for fear of fear , crawl along over the fork of rock , where the water have scoop the stone out , and shun thus the ledge from whence it rise like the mane of a white horse into the broad black pool , softly I let my foot into the dip and rush of the torrent . 
 and here I have reckon without my host , although   as I think   so clever ; and it be much but that I go down into the great black pool , and have never be hear of more ; and this must have be the end of I , except for my trusty loach - fork . for the green wave come down like great bottle upon I , and my leg be go off in a moment , and I have not time to cry out with wonder , only to think of my mother and Annie , and knock my head very sadly , which make it go round so that brain be no good , even if I have any . but all in a moment , before I know aught , except that I must die out of the way , with a roar of water upon I , my fork , praise God stick fast in the rock , and I be bear up upon it . I feel nothing except that here be another matter to begin upon ; and it might be worth while , or again it might not , to have another fight for it . but presently the dash of the water upon my face revive I , and my mind grow use to the roar of it , and meseeme I have be bad off than this , when first fling into the Lowman . 
 therefore I gather my leg back slowly , as if they be fish to be land , stop whenever the water fly too strongly off my shin - bone , and come along without stick out to let the wave get hold of I . and in this manner I win a footing , lean well forward like a draught - horse , and balance on my strength as it be , with the ashen stake set behind I . then I say to my self , ' John Ridd , the soon you get yourself out by the way you come , the well it will be for you . ' but to my great dismay and affright , I see that no choice be leave I now , except that I must climb somehow up that hill of water , or else be wash down into the pool and whirl around it till it drown I . for there be no chance of fetch back by the way I have go down into it , and far up be a hedge of rock on either side of the waterway , rise a hundred yard in height , and for all I could tell five hundred , and no place to set a foot in . 
 have say the Lord 's Prayer   which be all I know , and make a very bad job of it , I grasp the good loach - stick under a knot , and steady I with my left hand , and so with a sigh of despair begin my course up the fearful torrent - way . to I it seem half a mile at least of slide water above I , but in truth it be little more than a furlong , as I come to know afterwards . it would have be a hard ascent even without the slippery slime and the force of the river over it , and I have scanty hope indeed of ever win the summit . nevertheless , my terror leave I , now I be face to face with it , and have to meet the bad ; and I set myself to do my good with a vigour and sort of hardness which do not then surprise I , but have do so ever since . 
 the water be only six inch deep , or from that to nine at the utmost , and all the way up I could see my foot look white in the gloom of the hollow , and here and there I find resting - place , to hold on by the cliff and pant awhile . and gradually as I go on , a warmth of courage breathe in I , to think that perhaps no other have dare to try that pass before I , and to wonder what mother would say to it . and then come think of my father also , and the pain of my foot abate . 
 how I go carefully , step by step , keep my arm in front of I , and never dare to straighten my knee be more than I can tell clearly , or even like now to think of , because it make I dream of it . only I must acknowledge that the great danger of all be just where I see no jeopardy , but run up a patch of black ooze - weed in a very boastful manner , be now not far from the summit . 
 here I fall very piteously , and be like to have break my knee - cap , and the torrent get hold of my other leg while I be indulge the bruised one . and then a vile knotting of cramp disable I , and for awhile I could only roar , till my mouth be full of water , and all of my body be slide . but the fright of that bring I to again , and my elbow catch in a rock - hole ; and so I manage to start again , with the help of more humility . 
 now be in the most dreadful fright , because I be so near the top , and hope be beat within I , I labour hard with both leg and arm , go like a mill and grunting . at last the rush of fork water , where first it come over the lip of the fall , drive I into the middle , and I stick awhile with my toe - ball on the slippery link of the pop - weed , and the world be green and gliddery , and I durst not look behind I . then I make up my mind to die at last ; for so my leg would ache no more , and my breath not pain my heart so ; only it do seem such a pity after fight so long to give in , and the light be come upon I , and again I fight towards it ; then suddenly I feel fresh air , and fall into it headlong . 




 CHAPTER VIII 

 a boy and a girl when I come to myself again , my hand be full of young grass and mould , and a little girl kneeling at my side be rub my forehead tenderly with a dock - leaf and a handkerchief . 
 ' oh , I be so glad , ' she whisper softly , as I open my eye and look at she ; ' now you will try to be well , will not   you ? ' 
 I have never hear so sweet a sound as come from between her bright red lip , while there she kneel and gaze at I ; neither have I ever see anything so beautiful as the large dark eye intent upon I , full of pity and wonder . and then , my nature be slow , and perhaps , for that matter , heavy , I wander with my hazy eye down the black shower of her hair , as to my jade gaze it seem ; and where it fall on the turf , among it   like an early star   be the first primrose of the season . and since that day I think of she , through all the rough storm of my life , when I see an early primrose . perhaps she like my countenance , and indeed I know she do , because she say so afterwards ; although at the time she be too young to know what make she take to I . not that I have any beauty , or ever pretend to have any , only a solid healthy face , which many girl have laugh at . 
 thereupon I sate upright , with my little trident still in one hand , and be much afraid to speak to she , be conscious of my country - brogue , lest she should cease to like I . but she clap her hand , and make a trifling dance around my back , and come to I on the other side , as if I be a great plaything . 
 ' what be your name ? ' she say , as if she have every right to ask I ; ' and how do you come here , and what be these wet thing in this great bag ? ' 
 ' you have well let they alone , ' I say ; ' they be loach for my mother . but I will give you some , if you like . ' 
 ' dear I , how much you think of they ! why , they be only fish . but how your foot be bleed ! oh , I must tie they up for you . and no shoe nor stocking ! be your mother very poor , poor boy ? ' 
 ' no , ' I say , be vex at this ; ' we be rich enough to buy all this great meadow , if we choose ; and here my shoe and stocking be . ' 
 ' why , they be quite as wet as your foot ; and I can not bear to see your foot . oh , please to let I manage they ; I will do it very softly . ' 
 ' oh , I do not   think much of that , ' I reply ; ' I shall put some goose - grease to they . but how you be look at I ! I never see any one like you before . my name be John Ridd . what be your name ? ' 
 ' Lorna Doone , ' she answer , in a low voice , as if afraid of it , and hang her head so that I could see only her forehead and eyelash ; ' if you please , my name be Lorna Doone ; and I think you must have know it . ' 
 then I stand up and touch her hand , and try to make she look at I ; but she only turn away the more . young and harmless as she be , her name alone make guilt of she . nevertheless I could not help look at her tenderly , and the more when her blush turn into tear , and her tear to long , low sob . 
 ' do not   cry , ' I say , ' whatever you do . I be sure you have never do any harm . I will give you all my fish Lorna , and catch some more for mother ; only do not   be angry with I . ' 
 she fling her little soft arm up in the passion of her tear , and look at I so piteously , that what do I do but kiss she . it seem to be a very odd thing , when I come to think of it , because I hat kiss so , as all honest boy must do . but she touch my heart with a sudden delight , like a cowslip - blossom   although there be none to be see yet , and the sweet flower of spring . 
 she give I no encouragement , as my mother in her place would have do ; nay , she even wipe her lip   which methought be rather rude of she , and draw away , and smooth her dress , as if I have use a freedom . then I feel my cheek grow burn red , and I gaze at my leg and be sorry . for although she be not at all a proud child   at any rate in her countenance , yet I know that she be by birth a thousand year in front of I . they might have take and frame I , or   which would be more to the purpose   my sister , until it be time for we to die , and then have train our child after we , for many generation ; yet never could we have get that look upon our face which Lorna Doone have naturally , as if she have be bear to it . 
 here be I , a yeoman 's boy , a yeoman every inch of I , even where I be naked ; and there be she , a lady bear , and thoroughly aware of it , and dress by people of rank and taste , who take pride in her beauty and set it to advantage . for though her hair be fall down by reason of her wildness , and some of her frock be touch with wet where she have tend I so , behold her dress be pretty enough for the queen of all the angel . the colour be bright and rich indeed , and the substance very sumptuous , yet simple and free from tinsel stuff , and match most harmoniously . all from her waist to her neck be white , plait in close like a curtain , and the dark soft weeping of her hair , and the shadowy light of her eye   like a wood ray through with sunset , make it seem yet whiter , as if it be do on purpose . as for the rest , she know what it be a great deal well than I do , for I never could look far away from her eye when they be open upon I . 
 now , see how I heed she , and feel that I have kiss she , although she be such a little girl , eight year old or thereabout , she turn to the stream in a bashful manner , and begin to watch the water , and rub one leg against the other . 
 I , for my part , be vex at her behaviour to I , take up all my thing to go , and make a fuss about it ; to let she know I be go . but she do not call I back at all , as I have make sure she would do ; moreover , I know that to try the descent be almost certain death to I , and it look as dark as pitch ; and so at the mouth I turn round again , and come back to she , and say , ' Lorna . ' 
 ' oh , I think you be go , ' she answer ; ' why do you ever come here ? do you know what they would do to we , if they find you here with I ? ' 
 ' beat we , I dare say , very hard ; or I , at least . they could never beat you . ' 
 ' no . they would kill we both outright , and bury we here by the water ; and the water often tell I that I must come to that . ' 
 ' but what should they kill I for ? ' 
 ' because you have find the way up here , and they never could believe it . now , please to go ; oh , please to go . they will kill we both in a moment . yes , I like you very much ' for I be tease she to say it ' ve ry much indeed , and I will call you John Ridd , if you like ; only please to go , John . and when your foot be well , you know , you can come and tell I how they be . ' 
 ' but I tell you , Lorna , I like you very much indeed nearly as much as Annie , and a great deal more than Lizzie . and I never see any one like you , and I must come back again to - morrow , and so must you , to see I ; and I will bring you such lot of thing there be apple still , and a thrush I catch with only one leg break , and our dog have just have puppy ' 
 ' oh , dear , they will not   let I have a dog . there be not a dog in the valley . they say they be such noisy thing ' 
 ' only put your hand in mine what little thing they be , Lorna ! and I will bring you the lovely dog ; I will show you just how long he be . ' 
 ' hush ! ' a shout come down the valley , and all my heart be tremble , like water after sunset , and Lorna 's face be alter from pleasant play to terror . she shrink to I , and look up at I , with such a power of weakness , that I at once make up my mind to save she or to die with she . a tingle go through all my bone , and I only long for my carbine . the little girl take courage from I , and put she cheek quite close to mine . 
 ' come with I down the waterfall . I can carry you easily ; and mother will take care of you . ' 
 ' no , no , ' she cry , as I take she up : ' I will tell you what to do . they be only look for I . you see that hole , that hole there ? ' 
 she point to a little niche in the rock which verge the meadow , about fifty yard away from we . in the fading of the twilight I could just descry it . 
 ' yes , I see it ; but they will see I cross the grass to get there . ' 
 ' look ! look ! ' she could hardly speak . ' there be a way out from the top of it ; they would kill I if I tell it . oh , here they come , I can see they . ' 
 the little maid turn as white as the snow which hang on the rock above she , and she look at the water and then at I , and she cry , ' oh dear ! oh dear ! ' and then she begin to sob aloud , be so young and unready . but I draw she behind the withy - bush , and close down to the water , where it be quiet and shelving deep , ere it come to the lip of the chasm . here they could not see either of we from the upper valley , and might have seek a long time for we , even when they come quite near , if the tree have be clothe with their summer clothe . luckily I have pick up my fish and take my three - pronged fork away . 
 crouch in that hollow nest , as child get together in ever so little compass , I see a dozen fierce man come down , on the other side of the water , not bear any fire - arm , but look lax and jovial , as if they be come from rid and a dinner take hungrily . ' queen , queen ! ' they be shout , here and there , and now and then : ' where the pest be our little queen go ? ' 
 ' they always call I " queen , " and I be to be queen by - and - by , ' Lorna whisper to I , with her soft cheek on my rough one , and her little heart beat against I : ' oh , they be cross by the timber there , and then they be sure to see we . ' 
 ' stop , ' say I ; ' now I see what to do . I must get into the water , and you must go to sleep . ' 
 ' to be sure , yes , away in the meadow there . but how bitter cold it will be for you ! ' 
 she see in a moment the way to do it , soon than I could tell she ; and there be no time to lose . 
 ' now mind you never come again , ' she whisper over her shoulder , as she creep away with a childish twist hide her white front from I ; ' only I shall come sometimes oh , here they be , Madonna ! ' 
 dare scarce to peep , I creep into the water , and lie down bodily in it , with my head between two block of stone , and some flood - drift combing over I . the dusk be deepen between the hill , and a white mist lie on the river ; but I , be in the channel of it , could see every ripple , and twig , and rush , and glazing of twilight above it , as bright as in a picture ; so that to my ignorance there seem no chance at all but what the man must find I . for all this time they be shout and swear , and keep such a hullabaloo , that the rock all round the valley rang , and my heart quake , so   what with this and the cold   that the water begin to gurgle round I , and to lap upon the pebble . 
 neither in truth do I try to stop it , be now so desperate , between the fear and the wretchedness ; till I catch a glimpse of the little maid , whose beauty and whose kindliness have make I yearn to be with she . and then I know that for her sake I be bind to be brave and hide myself . she be lie beneath a rock , thirty or forty yard from I , feign to be fast asleep , with her dress spread beautifully , and her hair draw over she . 
 presently one of the great rough man come round a corner upon she ; and there he stop and gaze awhile at her fairness and her innocence . then he catch she up in his arm , and kiss she so that I hear he ; and if I have only bring my gun , I would have try to shoot he . 
 ' here our queen be ! here be the queen , here be the captain 's daughter ! ' he shout to his comrade ; ' fast asleep , by God , and hearty ! now I have first claim to she ; and no one else shall touch the child . back to the bottle , all of you ! ' 
 he set she dainty little form upon his great square shoulder , and her narrow foot in one broad hand ; and so in triumph march away , with the purple velvet of her skirt ruffle in his long black beard , and the silken length of her hair fetch out , like a cloud by the wind behind she . this way of she go vex I so , that I leap upright in the water , and must have be spy by some of they , but for their haste to the wine - bottle . of their little queen they take small notice , be in this urgency ; although they have think to find she drown ; but troop away after one another with kindly challenge to gambling , so far as I could make they out ; and I keep sharp watch , I assure you . 
 go up that darken glen , little Lorna , rid still the large and most fierce of they , turn and put up a hand to I , and I put up a hand to she , in the thick of the mist and the willow . 
 she be go , my little dear   though tall of her age and healthy ; and when I get over my thriftless fright , I long to have more to say to she . her voice to I be so different from all I have ever hear before , as might be a sweet silver bell intone to the small chord of a harp . but I have no time to think about this , if I hop to have any supper . 
 I creep into a bush for warmth , and rub my shivering leg on bark , and long for mother 's fagot . then as daylight sink below the forget - I - not of star , with a sorrow to be quit , I know that now must be my time to get away , if there be any . 
 therefore , wring my sodden breach , I manage to crawl from the bank to the niche in the cliff which Lorna have show I . 
 through the dusk I have trouble to see the mouth , at even the five land - yard of distance ; nevertheless , I enter well , and hold on by some dead fern - stem , and do hope that no one would shoot I . 
 but while I be hug myself like this , with a boyish manner of reasoning , my joy be like to have end in sad grief both to myself and my mother , and haply to all honest folk who shall love to read this history . for hear a noise in front of I , and like a coward not know where , but afraid to turn round or think of it , I feel myself go down some deep passage into a pit of darkness . it be no good to catch the side , the whole thing seem to go with I . then , without know how , I be lean over a night of water . 
 this water be of black radiance , as be certain diamond , span across with vault of rock , and carry no image , neither show marge nor end , but centre   at it might be   with a bottomless indrawal . 
 with that chill and dread upon I , and the sheer rock all around , and the faint light heave wavily on the silence of this gulf , I must have lose my wit and go to the bottom , if there be any . 
 but suddenly a robin sing   as they will do after dark , towards spring   in the brown fern and ivy behind I . I take it for our little Annie 's voice   for she could call any robin , and gather quick warm comfort , spring up the steep way towards the starlight . climb back , as the stone glid down , I hear the cold greedy wave go jappe , like a blind black dog , into the distance of arch and hollow depth of darkness . 




 CHAPTER IX 

 there be no PLACE like HOME 

 I can assure you , and tell no lie   as John Fry always use to say , when tell his very large , that I scramble back to the mouth of that pit as if the evil one have be after I . and sorely I repent now of all my boyish folly , or madness it might well be term , in venturing , with none to help , and nothing to compel I , into that accurse valley . once let I get out , think I , and if ever I get in again , without be cast in by neck and by crop , I will give our new - bear donkey leave to set up for my schoolmaster . 
 how I keep that resolution we shall see hereafter . it be enough for I now to tell how I escape from the den that night . first I sit down in the little opening which Lorna have point out to I , and wonder whether she have mean , as bitterly occur to I , that I should run down into the pit , and be drown , and give no more trouble . but in less than half a minute I be ashamed of that idea , and remember how she be vex to think that even a loach should lose his life . and then I say to myself , ' now surely she would value I more than a thousand loach ; and what she say must be quite true about the way out of this horrible place . ' 
 therefore I begin to search with the utmost care and diligence , although my tooth be chatter , and all my bone begin to ache with the chilliness and the wetness . before very long the moon appear , over the edge of the mountain , and among the tree at the top of it ; and then I espy rough step , and rocky , make as if with a sledge - hammer , narrow , steep , and far asunder , scoop here and there in the side of the entrance , and then round a bulge of the cliff , like the mark upon a great brown loaf , where a hungry child have pick at it . and high up , where the light of the moon shine broader upon the precipice , there seem to be a rude break track , like the shadow of a crooked stick throw upon a house - wall . 
 Herein be small encouragement ; and at first I be minded to lie down and die ; but it seem to come amiss to I . God have his time for all of we ; but he seem to advertise we when he do not mean to do it . moreover , I see a movement of light at the head of the valley , as if lanthorn be come after I , and the nimbleness give thereon to my heel be in front of all meditation . 
 Straightway I set foot in the low stirrup   as I might almost call it , and clung to the rock with my nail , and work to make a jump into the second stirrup . and I compass that too , with the aid of my stick ; although , to tell you the truth , I be not at that time of life so agile as boy of small frame be , for my size be grow beyond my year , and the muscle not keep time with it , and the joint of my bone not closely hinge , with star at one another . but the third step - hole be the hard of all , and the rock swell out on I over my breast , and there seem to be no attempt it , until I espy a good stout rope hang in a groove of shadow , and just manage to reach the end of it . 
 how I clomb up , and across the clearing , and find my way home through the Bagworthy forest , be more than I can remember now , for I take all the rest of it then as a dream , by reason of perfect weariness . and indeed it be quite beyond my hope to tell so much as I have tell , for at first begin to set it down , it be all like a mist before I . nevertheless , some part grow clear , as one by one I remember they , have take a little soft cordial , because the memory frighten I . 
 for the toil of the water , and danger of labour up the long cascade or rapid , and then the surprise of the fair young maid , and terror of the murderer , and desperation of get away all these be much to I even now , when I be a stout churchwarden , and sit by the side of my fire , after go through many far bad adventure , which I will tell , God willing . only the labour of writing be such   especially so as to construe , and challenge a reader on part of speech , and hope to be even with he ; that by this pipe which I hold in my hand I ever expect to be beat , as in the day when old Doctor Twiggs , if I make a bad stroke in my exercise , shout aloud with a sour joy , ' John Ridd , sirrah , down with your small - clothe ! ' 
 let that be as it may , I deserve a good beating that night , after make such a fool of myself , and grind good fustian to piece . but when I get home , all the supper be in , and the man sit at the white table , and mother and Annie and Lizzie near by , all eager , and offer to begin   except , indeed , my mother , who be look out at the doorway , and by the fire be Betty Muxworthy , scolding , and cooking , and taste her work , all in a breath , as a man would say . I look through the door from the dark by the wood - stack , and be half of a mind to stay out like a dog , for fear of the rating and reckoning ; but the way my dear mother be look about and the brown of the sausage get the well of I . 
 but nobody could get out of I where I have be all the day and evening ; although they worry I never so much , and long to shake I to piece , especially Betty Muxworthy , who never could learn to let well alone . not that they make I tell any lie , although it would have serve they right almost for intrude on other people 's business ; but that I just hold my tongue , and eat my supper rarely , and let they try their taunt and jibe , and drive they almost wild after supper , by smile exceed knowingly . and indeed I could have tell they thing , as I hint once or twice ; and then poor Betty and our little Lizzie be so mad with eagerness , that between they I go into the fire , be thoroughly overcome with laughter and my own importance . 
 now what the working of my mind be   if , indeed it work at all , and do not rather follow suit of body   it be not in my power to say ; only that the result of my adventure in the Doone Glen be to make I dream a good deal of night , which I have never do much before , and to drive I , with tenfold zeal and purpose , to the practice of bullet - shooting . not that I ever expect to shoot the Doone family , one by one , or even desire to do so , for my nature be not revengeful ; but that it seem to be somehow my business to understand the gun , as a thing I must be at home with . 
 I could hit the barn - door now capitally well with the spanish match - lock , and even with John Fry 's blunderbuss , at ten good land - yard distance , without any rest for my fusil . and what be very wrong of I , though I do not see it then , I keep John Fry there , to praise my shot , from dinner - time often until the grey dusk , while he all the time should have be at work spring - ploughing upon the farm . and for that matter so should I have be , or at any rate drive the horse ; but John be by no mean loath to be there , instead of hold the plough - tail . and indeed , one of our old saying be , 
 for pleasure 's sake I would liefer wet , than ha ' ten lump of gold for each one of my sweat . 
 and again , which be not a bad proverb , though unthrifty and unlike a Scotsman 's , 
 God make the wheat grow greener , while farmer be at his dinner . 
 and no Devonshire man , or Somerset either   and I belong to both of they , ever think of work hard than God like to see he . 
 nevertheless , I work hard at the gun , and by the time that I have send all the church - roof gutter , so far as I honestly could cut they , through the red pine - door , I begin to long for a well tool that would make less noise and throw straighter . but the sheep - shearing come and the hay - season next , and then the harvest of small corn , and the digging of the root call ' batata '   a new but good thing in our neighbourhood , which our folk have make into ' tatie ' , and then the sweating of the apple , and the turning of the cider - press , and the stacking of the firewood , and netting of the woodcock , and the springle to be minded in the garden and by the hedgerow , where blackbird hop to the molehill in the white October morning , and grey bird come to look for snail at the time when the sun be rise . 
 it be wonderful how time run away , when all these thing and a great many other come in to load he down the hill and prevent he from stop to look about . and I for my part can never conceive how people who live in town and city , where neither lamb nor bird be   except in some shop window , nor grow corn , nor meadow - grass , nor even so much as a stick to cut or a stile to climb and sit down upon how these poor folk get through their life without be utterly weary of they , and die from pure indolence , be a thing God only know , if his mercy allow he to think of it . 
 how the year go by I know not , only that I be abroad all day , shooting , or fishing , or mind the farm , or rid after some stray beast , or away by the seaside below Glenthorne , wonder at the great water , and resolve to go for a sailor . for in those day I have a firm belief , as many other strong boy have , of be bear for a seaman . and indeed I have be in a boat nearly twice ; but the second time mother find it out , and come and draw I back again ; and after that she cry so badly , that I be force to give my word to she to go no more without tell she . 
 but Betty Muxworthy speak her mind quite in a different way about it , the while she be wring my hosen , and clatter to the dry - horse . 
 ' Zailor , ee fai ! ay and zarve un raight . she can not   kape out o ' the watter here , whur a ' must goo vor to vaind un , zame as a gurt to - ad squalloping , and mux up till I be wear out , I be , wi ' the very saight of 's braiche . how wil un ever baide aboard zhip , wi ' the watter zinge out under un , and comin ' up splash when the wind blow . Latt un goo , missus , latt un goo , zay I for wan , and old Davy wash his clout for un . ' 
 and this discourse of Betty 's tend more than my mother 's prayer , I fear , to keep I from go . for I hat Betty in those day , as child always hate a cross servant , and often get fond of a false one . but Betty , like many active woman , be false by her crossness only ; think it just for the moment perhaps , and rush away with a bucket ; ready to stick to it , like a clenched nail , if beat the wrong way with argument ; but melt over it , if you leave she , as stinging soap , leave along in a basin , spread all abroad without bubble . 
 but all this be beyond the child , and beyond I too for that matter , even now in ripe experience ; for I never do know what woman mean , and never shall except when they tell I , if that be in their power . now let that question pass . for although I be now in a place of some authority , I have observe that no one ever listen to I , when I attempt to lay down the law ; but all be wait with open ear until I do enforce it . and so methink he who read a history care not much for the wisdom or folly of the writer   know well that the former be far less than his own , and the latter vastly great , but hurry to know what the people do , and how they get on about it . and this I can tell , if any one can , have be myself in the thick of it . 
 the fright I have take that night in Glen Doone satisfy I for a long time thereafter ; and I take good care not to venture even in the field and wood of the outer farm , without John Fry for company . John be greatly surprised and please at the value I now set upon he ; until , what betwixt the desire to vaunt and the longing to talk thing over , I gradually lay bare to he nearly all that have befall I ; except , indeed , about Lorna , whom a sort of shame keep I from mention . not that I do not think of she , and wish very often to see she again ; but of course I be only a boy as yet , and therefore inclined to despise young girl , as be unable to do anything , and only mean to listen to order . and when I get along with the other boy , that be how we always speak of they , if we deign to speak at all , as being of a low order , only good enough to run errand for we , and to nurse boy - baby . 
 and yet my sister Annie be in truth a great deal more to I than all the boy of the parish , and of Brendon , and Countisbury , put together ; although at the time I never dream it , and would have laugh if tell so . Annie be of a pleasing face , and very gentle manner , almost like a lady some people say ; but without any air whatever , only try to give satisfaction . and if she fail , she would go and weep , without let any one know it , believe the fault to be all her own , when mostly it be of other . but if she succeed in please you , it be beautiful to see her smile , and stroke her soft chin in a way of her own , which she always use when take note how to do the right thing again for you . and then her cheek have a bright clear pink , and her eye be as blue as the sky in spring , and she stand as upright as a young apple - tree , and no one could help but smile at she , and pat her brown curl approvingly ; whereupon she always curtsey . for she never try to look away when honest people gaze at she ; and even in the court - yard she would come and help to take your saddle , and tell   without your ask she   what there be for dinner . 
 and afterwards she grow up to be a very comely maiden , tall , and with a well - build neck , and very fair white shoulder , under a bright cloud of curl hair . alas ! poor Annie , like most of the gentle maiden but tush , I be not come to that yet ; and for the present she seem to I little to look at , after the beauty of Lorna Doone . 




 CHAPTER X 

 a brave RESCUE and a ROUGH RIDE 

 it happen upon a November evening   when I be about fifteen year old , and out - grow my strength very rapidly , my sister Annie be turn thirteen , and a deal of rain have fall , and all the trough in the yard be flood , and the bark from the wood - rick wash down the gutter , and even our water - shoot go brown   that the duck in the court make a terrible quacking , instead of march off to their pen , one behind another . Thereupon Annie and I run out to see what might be the sense of it . there be thirteen duck , and ten lily - white   as the fashion then of duck be , not I mean twenty - three in all , but ten white and three brown - strip one ; and without be nice about their colour , they all quack very movingly . they push their gold - colour bill here and there   yet dirty , as gold be apt to be , and they jump on the triangle of their foot , and sound out of their nostril ; and some of the over - excite one run along low on the ground , quack grievously with their bill snap and bending , and the roof of their mouth exhibit . 
 Annie begin to cry ' dilly , dilly , einy , einy , ducksey , ' accord to the burden of a tune they seem to have accept as the national duck 's anthem ; but instead of be soothe by it , they only quack three time as hard , and run round till we be giddy . and then they shake their tail together , and look grave , and go round and round again . now I be uncommonly fond of duck , both roasted and roast and roystering ; and it be a fine sight to behold they walk , poddle one after other , with their toe out , like soldier drill , and their little eye cock all way at once , and the way that they dib with their bill , and dabble , and throw up their head and enjoy something , and then tell the other about it . therefore I know at once , by the way they be carry on , that there must be something or other go wholly amiss in the duck - world . Sister Annie perceive it too , but with a great quickness ; for she count they like a good duck - wife , and could only tell thirteen of they , when she know there ought to be fourteen . 
 and so we begin to search about , and the duck run to lead we aright , have come that far to fetch we ; and when we get down to the foot of the court - yard where the two great ash - tree stand by the side of the little water , we find good reason for the urgence and melancholy of the duck - bird . Lo ! the old white drake , the father of all , a bird of high manner and chivalry , always the last to help himself from the pan of barley - meal , and the first to show fight to a dog or cock intrude upon his family , this fine fellow , and pillar of the state , be now in a sad predicament , yet quack very stoutly . for the brook , wherewith he have be familiar from his callow childhood , and wherein he be will not to quest for water - newts , and tadpole , and caddi - worm , and other game , this brook , which afford he very often scanty space to dabble in , and sometimes starve the cress , be now come down in a great brown flood , as if the bank never belong to it . the foaming of it , and the noise , and the cresting of the corner , and the up and down , like a wave of the sea , be enough to frighten any duck , though breed upon stormy water , which our duck never have be . 
 there be always a hurdle six foot long and four and a half in depth , swing by a chain at either end from an oak lay across the channel . and the use of this hurdle be to keep our kine at milk time from stray away there drink   for in truth they be very dainty   and to fence strange cattle , or Farmer Snowe 's horse , from come along the bed of the brook unknown , to steal our substance . but now this hurdle , which hang in the summer a foot above the trickle , would have be dip more than two foot deep but for the power against it . for the torrent come down so vehemently that the chain at full stretch be creak , and the hurdle buffet almost flat , and thatch   so to say   with the drift - stuff , be go see - saw , with a sulky splash on the dirty red comb of the water . but saddest to see be between two bar , where a fog be of rush , and flood - wood , and wild - celery haulm , and dead crowsfoot , who but our venerable mallard jammed in by the joint of his shoulder , speak aloud as he rise and fall , with his top - knot full of water , unable to comprehend it , with his tail wash far away from he , but often compel to be silent , be duck very harshly against his will by the choke fall - to of the hurdle . 
 for a moment I could not help laugh , because , be bear up high and dry by a tumult of the torrent , he give I a look from his one little eye   have lose one in fight with the turkey - cock , a gaze of appeal sorrow , and then a loud quack to second it . but the quack come out of time , I suppose , for his throat get fill with water , as the hurdle carry he back again . and then there be scarcely the screw of his tail to be see until he swing up again , and leave small doubt by the way he sputter , and fail to quack , and hang down his poor crest , but what he must drown in another minute , and frog triumph over his body . 
 Annie be cry , and wring her hand , and I be about to rush into the water , although I like not the look of it , but hop to hold on by the hurdle , when a man on horseback come suddenly round the corner of the great ash - hedge on the other side of the stream , and his horse 's foot be in the water . 
 ' Ho , there , ' he cry ; ' get thee back , boy . the flood will carry thee down like a straw . I will do it for thee , and no trouble . ' 
 with that he lean forward , and speak to his mare she be just of the tint of a strawberry , a young thing , very beautiful and she arch up her neck , as mislike the job ; yet , trust he , would attempt it . she enter the flood , with her dainty fore - leg slop far and far in front of she , and her delicate ear prick forward , and the size of her great eye increase , but he keep she straight in the turbid rush , by the pressure of his knee on she . then she look back , and wonder at he , as the force of the torrent grow strong , but he bid she go on ; and on she go , and it foam up over her shoulder ; and she toss up her lip and scorn it , for now her courage be wake . then as the rush of it sweep she away , and she strike with her forefeet down the stream , he lean from his saddle in a manner which I never could have think possible , and catch up old Tom with his left hand , and set he between his holster , and smile at his faint quack of gratitude . in a moment all these be carry downstream , and the rider lie flat on his horse , and toss the hurdle clear from he , and make for the bend of smooth water . 
 they land some thirty or forty yard low , in the midst of our kitchen - garden , where the winter - cabbage be ; but though Annie and I creep in through the hedge , and be full of our thank and admire he , he would answer we never a word , until he have speak in full to the mare , as if explain the whole to she . 
 ' Sweetheart , I know thou couldst have leap it , ' he say , as he pat her cheek , be on the ground by this time , and she be nudge up to he , with the water patter off she ; ' but I have good reason , Winnie dear , for make thee go through it . ' 
 she answer he kindly with her soft eye , and smile at he very lovingly , and they understand one another . then he take from his waistcoat two peppercorn , and make the old drake swallow they , and try he softly upon his leg , where the lead gap in the hedge be . Old Tom stand up quite bravely , and clap his wing , and shake off the wet from his tail - feather ; and then away into the court - yard , and his family gather around he , and they all make a noise in their throat , and stand up , and put their bill together , to thank God for this great deliverance .
