 have take all this trouble , and watch the end of that adventure , the gentleman turn round to we with a pleasant smile on his face , as if he be lightly amuse with himself ; and we come up and look at he . he be rather short , about John Fry 's height , or may be a little tall , but very strongly build and springy , as his gait at every step show plainly , although his leg be bow with much riding , and he look as if he live on horseback . to a boy like I he seem very old , be over twenty , and well - find in beard ; but he be not more than four - and - twenty , fresh and ruddy look , with a short nose and keen blue eye , and a merry waggish jerk about he , as if the world be not in earnest . yet he have a sharp , stern way , like the crack of a pistol , if anything mislike he ; and we know   for child see such thing   that it be safe to tickle than buffet he . 
 ' well , young un , what be gap at ? ' he give pretty Annie a chuck on the chin , and take I all in without wink . 
 ' your mare , ' say I , stand stoutly up , be a tall boy now ; ' I never see such a beauty , sir . will you let I have a ride of she ? ' 
 ' think thou couldst ride she , lad ? she will have no burden but mine . Thou couldst never ride she . tut ! I would be loath to kill thee . ' 
 ' ride she ! ' I cry with the brave scorn , for she look so kind and gentle ; ' there never be horse upon Exmoor foal , but I could tackle in half an hour . only I never ride upon saddle . take they leather off of she . ' 
 he look at I with a dry little whistle , and thrust his hand into his breech - pocket , and so grin that I could not stand it . and Annie lay hold of I in such a way that I be almost mad with she . and he laugh , and approve she for do so . and the bad of all be he say nothing . 
 ' get away , Annie , will you ? do you think I be a fool , good sir ! only trust I with she , and I will not override she . ' 
 ' for that I will go bail , my son . she be liker to override thee . but the ground be soft to fall upon , after all this rain . now come out into the yard , young man , for the sake of your mother 's cabbage . and the mellow straw - bed will be soft for thee , since pride must have its fall . I be thy mother 's cousin , boy , and be go up to house . Tom Faggus be my name , as everybody know ; and this be my young mare , Winnie . ' 
 what a fool I must have be not to know it at once ! Tom Faggus , the great highwayman , and his young blood - mare , the strawberry ! already her fame be noise abroad , nearly as much as her master 's ; and my longing to ride she grow tenfold , but fear come at the back of it . not that I have the small fear of what the mare could do to I , by fair play and horse - trickery , but that the glory of sit upon she seem to be too great for I ; especially as there be rumour abroad that she be not a mare after all , but a witch . however , she look like a filly all over , and wonderfully beautiful , with her supple stride , and soft slope of shoulder , and glossy coat bead with water , and prominent eye full of docile fire . whether this come from her eastern blood of the Arabs newly import , and whether the cream - colour , mix with our bay , lead to that bright strawberry tint , be certainly more than I can decide , be chiefly acquaint with farm - horse . and these come of any colour and form ; you never can count what they will be , and be lucky to get four leg to they . 
 Mr. Faggus give his mare a wink , and she walk demurely after he , a bright young thing , flow over with life , yet drop her soul to a high one , and lead by love to anything ; as the manner be of female , when they know what be the good for they . then Winnie trod lightly upon the straw , because it have soft muck under it , and her delicate foot come back again . 
 ' up for it still , boy , be ye ? ' Tom Faggus stop , and the mare stop there ; and they look at I provokingly . 
 ' be she able to leap , sir ? there be good take - off on this side of the brook . ' 
 Mr. Faggus laugh very quietly , turn round to Winnie so that she might enter into it . and she , for her part , seem to know exactly where the fun lay . 
 ' good tumble - off , you mean , my boy . well , there can be small harm to thee . I be akin to thy family , and know the substance of their skull . ' 
 ' let I get up , ' say I , wax wroth , for reason I can not tell you , because they be too manifold ; ' take off your saddle - bag thing . I will try not to squeeze her rib in , unless she play nonsense with I . ' 
 then Mr. Faggus be up on his mettle , at this proud speech of mine ; and John Fry be run up all the while , and Bill Dadds , and half a dozen . Tom Faggus give one glance around , and then drop all regard for I . the high repute of his mare be at stake , and what be my life compare to it ? through my defiance , and stupid way , here be I in a duello , and my leg not come to their strength yet , and my arm as limp as a herring . 
 something of this occur to he even in his wrath with I , for he speak very softly to the filly , who now could scarce subdue herself ; but she draw in her nostril , and breathe to his breath and do all she could to answer he . 
 ' not too hard , my dear , ' he say : ' lead he gently down on the mixen . that will be quite enough . ' then he turn the saddle off , and I be up in a moment . she begin at first so easily , and prick her ear so lovingly , and mince about as if pleased to find so light a weight upon she , that I think she know I could ride a little , and fear to show any caper . ' Gee wug , Polly ! ' cry I , for all the man be now look on , be then at the leaving - off time : ' Gee wug , Polly , and show what thou be'est make of . ' with that I plug my heel into she , and Billy Dadds fling his hat up . 
 nevertheless , she outrage not , though her eye be frightening Annie , and John Fry take a pick to keep he safe ; but she curb to and fro with her strong forearm rise like spring ingathere , wait and quiver grievously , and begin to sweat about it . then her master give a shrill clear whistle , when her ear be bent towards he , and I feel her form beneath I gather up like whalebone , and her hind - leg come under she , and I know that I be in for it . 
 first she rear upright in the air , and strike I full on the nose with her comb , till I bleed bad than Robin Snell make I ; and then down with her fore - foot deep in the straw , and her hind - foot go to heaven . find I stick to she still like wax , for my mettle be up as hers be , away she fly with I swift than ever I go before , or since , I trow . she drive full - head at the cobwall ' oh , Jack , slip off , ' scream Annie then she turn like light , when I think to crush she , and ground my left knee against it . ' mux I , ' I cry , for my breech be break , and short word go the furth ' if you kill I , you shall die with I . ' then she take the court - yard gate at a leap , knock my word between my tooth , and then right over a quick set hedge , as if the sky be a breath to she ; and away for the water - meadow , while I lay on her neck like a child at the breast and wish I have never be bear . straight away , all in the front of the wind , and scatter cloud around she , all I know of the speed we make be the frightful flash of her shoulder , and her mane like tree in a temp . I feel the earth under we rush away , and the air leave far behind we , and my breath come and go , and I pray to God , and be sorry to be so late of it . 
 all the long swift while , without power of thought , I cling to her crest and shoulder , and dig my nail into her crease , and my toe into her flank - part , and be proud of hold on so long , though sure of be beat . then in her fury at feel I still , she rush at another device for it , and leap the wide water - trough sideways across , to and fro , till no breath be leave in I . the hazel - bough take I too hard in the face , and the tall dog - brier get hold of I , and the ache of my back be like crimp a fish ; till I long to give up , thoroughly beat , and lie there and die in the cress . but there come a shrill whistle from up the home - hill , where the people have hurry to watch we ; and the mare stop as if with a bullet , then set off for home with the speed of a swallow , and go as smoothly and silently . I never have dream of such delicate motion , fluent , and graceful , and ambient , soft as the breeze flit over the flower , but swift as the summer lightning . I sit up again , but my strength be all spend , and no time leave to recover it , and though she rise at our gate like a bird , I tumble off into the mixen . 




 CHAPTER XI 

 TOM deserve his supper 

 ' well do , lad , ' Mr. Faggus say good naturedly ; for all be now gather round I , as I rise from the ground , somewhat tottering , and miry , and crest - fall , but otherwise none the bad   have fall upon my head , which be of uncommon substance ; nevertheless John Fry be laugh , so that I long to clout his ear for he ; ' not at all bad work , my boy ; we may teach you to ride by - and - by , I see ; I think not to see you stick on so long ' 
 ' I should have stick on much long , sir , if her side have not be wet . she be so slippery ' 
 ' boy , thou art right . she hath give many the slip . ha , ha ! vex not , Jack , that I laugh at thee . she be like a sweetheart to I , and well , than any of they be . it would have go to my heart if thou hadst conquer . none but I can ride my Winnie mare . ' 
 ' foul shame to thee then , Tom Faggus , ' cry mother , come up suddenly , and speak so that all be amazed , have never see her wrathful ; ' to put my boy , my boy , across she , as if his life be no more than thine ! the only son of his father , an honest man , and a quiet man , not a roystere drunken robber ! a man would have take thy mad horse and thee , and fling they both into horse - pond ay , and what be more , I will have it do now , if a hair of his head be injure . oh , my boy , my boy ! what could I do without thee ? put up the other arm , Johnny . ' all the time mother be scold so , she be feel I , and wipe I ; while Faggus try to look greatly ashamed , have sense of the way of woman . 
 ' only look at his jacket , mother ! ' cry Annie ; ' and a shillingsworth go from his small - clothe ! ' 
 ' what care I for his clothe , thou goose ? take that , and heed thine own a bit . ' and mother give Annie a slap which send she swing up against Mr. Faggus , and he catch she , and kiss and protect she , and she look at he very nicely , with great tear in her soft blue eye . ' oh , fie upon thee , fie upon thee ! ' cry mother   be yet more vexed with he , because she have beat Annie ; ' after all we have do for thee , and save thy worthless neck and to try to kill my son for I ! never more shall horse of thine enter stable here , since these be thy return to I . small thank to you , John Fry , I say , and you Bill Dadds , and you Jem Slocomb , and all the rest of your coward lot ; much you care for your master 's son ! Afraid of that ugly beast yourself , and you put a boy just breeche upon he ! ' 
 ' wull , missus , what could we do ? ' begin John ; ' Jan wudd goo , now wudd't she , Jem ? and how be we ' 
 ' Jan indeed ! Master John , if you please , to a lad of his year and stature . and now , Tom Faggus , be off , if you please , and think yourself lucky to go so ; and if ever that horse come into our yard , I will hamstring he myself if none of my coward dare do it . ' 
 everybody look at mother , to hear her talk like that , know how quiet she be day by day and how pleasant to be cheat . and the man begin to shoulder their shovel , both so as to be away from she , and to go and tell their wife of it . Winnie too be look at she , be point at so much , and wonder if she have do amiss . and then she come to I , and tremble , and stoop her head , and ask my pardon , if she have be too proud with I . 
 ' Winnie shall stop here to - night , ' say I , for Tom Faggus still say never a word all the while ; but begin to buckle his thing on , for he know that woman be to be meet with wool , as the cannon - ball be at the siege of Tiverton Castle ; ' mother , I tell you , Winnie shall stop ; else I will go away with she , I never know what it be , till now , to ride a horse worth rid . ' 
 ' young man , ' say Tom Faggus , still prepare sternly to depart , ' you know more about a horse than any man on Exmoor . your mother may well be proud of you , but she need have have no fear . as if I , Tom Faggus , your father 's cousin and the only thing I be proud of would ever have let you mount my mare , which duke and prince have vainly seek , except for the courage in your eye , and the look of your father about you . I know you could ride when I see you , and rarely you have conquer . but woman do not   understand we . good - bye , John ; I be proud of you , and I hop to have do you pleasure . and indeed I come full of some courtly tale , that would have make your hair stand up . but though not a crust have I taste since this time yesterday , have give my meat to a widow , I will go and starve on the moor far soon than eat the good supper that ever be cook , in a place that have forget I . ' with that he fetch a heavy sigh , as if it have be for my father ; and feebly get upon Winnie 's back , and she come to say farewell to I . he lift his hat to my mother , with a glance of sorrow , but never a word ; and to I he say , ' open the gate , Cousin John , if you please . you have beat she so , that she can not leap it , poor thing . ' 
 but before he be truly go out of our yard , my mother come softly after he , with her afternoon apron across her eye , and one hand ready to offer he . nevertheless , he make as if he have not see she , though he let his horse go slowly . 
 ' stop , Cousin Tom , ' my mother say , ' a word with you , before you go . ' 
 ' why , bless my heart ! ' Tom Faggus cry , with the form of his countenance so change , that I verily think another man must have leap into his clothe ' do I see my Cousin Sarah ? I think every one be ashamed of I , and afraid to offer I shelter , since I lose my good cousin , John Ridd . ' come here , ' he use to say , ' Tom , come here , when you be worried , and my wife shall take good care of you . ' ' yes , dear John , ' I use to answer , ' I know she promise my mother so ; but people have take to think against I , and so might Cousin Sarah . ' ah , he be a man , a man ! if you only hear how he answer I . but let that go , I be nothing now , since the day I lose Cousin Ridd . ' and with that he begin to push on again ; but mother would not have it so . 
 ' oh , Tom , that be a loss indeed . and I be nothing either . and you should try to allow for I ; though I never find any one that do . ' and mother begin to cry , though father have be dead so long ; and I look on with a stupid surprise , have stop from cry long ago . 
 ' I can tell you one that will , ' cry Tom , jump off Winnie , in a trice , and look kindly at mother ; ' I can allow for you , Cousin Sarah , in everything but one . I be in some way a bad man myself ; but I know the value of a good one ; and if you give I order , by God ' and he shake his fist towards Bagworthy Wood , just heave up black in the sundown . 
 ' Hush , Tom , hush , for God 's sake ! ' and mother mean I , without point at I ; at least I think she do . for she ever have wean I from thought of revenge , and even from longing for judgment . ' God know well , boy , ' she use to say , ' let we wait his time , without wish it . ' and so , to tell the truth , I do ; partly through her teaching , and partly through my own mild temper , and my knowledge that father , after all , be kill because he have thrash they . 
 ' good - night , Cousin Sarah , good - night , Cousin Jack , ' cry Tom , take to the mare again ; ' many a mile I have to ride , and not a bit inside of I . no food or shelter this side of Exeford , and the night will be black as pitch , I trow . but it serve I right for indulge the lad , be take with his look so . ' 
 ' Cousin Tom , ' say mother , and try to get so that Annie and I could not hear she ; ' it would be a sad and unkinlike thing for you to despise our dwelling - house . we can not entertain you , as the lordly inn on the road do ; and we have small change of victual . but the man will go home , be Saturday ; and so you will have the fireside all to yourself and the child . there be some few collop of red deer 's flesh , and a ham just down from the chimney , and some dry salmon from Lynmouth weir , and cold roast - pig , and some oyster . and if none of those be to your liking , we could roast two woodcock in half an hour , and Annie would make the toast for they . and the good folk make some mistake last week , go up the country , and leave a keg of old Holland cordial in the coving of the wood - rick , have borrow our Smiler , without ask leave . I fear there be something unrighteous about it . but what can a poor widow do ? John Fry would have take it , but for our Jack . our Jack be a little too sharp for he . ' 
 ay , that I be ; John Fry have get it , like a billet under his apron , go away in the gray of the morning , as if to kindle his fireplace . ' why , John , ' I say , ' what a heavy log ! let I have one end of it . ' ' Thank'e , Jan , no need of thiccy , ' he answer , turn his back to I ; ' waife wanteth a log as will last all day , to kape the crock a zimmerin . ' and he bang his gate upon my heel to make I stop and rub they . ' why , John , ' say I , ' you'm get a log with round hole in the end of it . who have be cut gun - wad ? just lift your apron , or I will . ' 
 but , to return to Tom Faggus he stop to sup that night with we , and take a little of everything ; a few oyster first , and then dry salmon , and then ham and egg , do in small curl rasher , and then a few collop of venison toast , and next to that a little cold roast - pig , and a woodcock on toast to finish with , before the Scheidam and hot water . and have change his wet thing first , he seem to be in fair appetite , and praise Annie 's cooking mightily , with a kind of noise like a smack of his lip , and a rubbing of his hand together , whenever he could spare they . 
 he have get John Fry 's good small - clothe on , for he say he be not good enough to go into my father 's   which mother keep to look at , nor man enough to fill they . and in truth my mother be very glad that he refuse , when I offer they . but John be over - proud to have it in his power to say that such a famous man have ever dwelt in any clothe of his ; and afterwards he make show of they . for Mr. Faggus 's glory , then , though not so great as now it be , be spread very fast indeed all about our neighbourhood , and even as far as Bridgewater . 
 Tom Faggus be a jovial soul , if ever there have be one , not make bone of little thing , nor care to seek evil . there be about he such a love of genuine human nature , that if a traveller say a good thing , he would give he back his purse again . it be true that he take people 's money more by force than fraud ; and the law   be use to the inverse method   be bitterly move against he , although he could quote precedent . these thing I do not understand ; have see so much of robbery   some legal , some illegal , that I scarcely know , as here we say , one crow 's foot from the other . it be beyond I and above I , to discuss these subject ; and in truth I love the law right well , when it doth support I , and when I can lay it down to my liking , with prejudice to nobody . loyal , too , to the King be I , as behove churchwarden ; and ready to make the good of he , as he generally require . but after all , I could not see   until I grow much old , and come to have some property   why Tom Faggus , work hard , be call a robber and felon of great ; while the king , do nothing at all   as become his dignity , be liege - lord , and paramount owner ; with everybody to thank he kindly for accept tribute . 
 for the present , however , I learn nothing more as to what our cousin 's profession be ; only that mother seem frightened , and whisper to he now and then not to talk of something , because of the child be there ; whereupon he always nod with a sage expression , and apply himself to holland . 
 ' now let we go and see Winnie , Jack , ' he say to I after supper ; ' for the most part I feed she before myself ; but she be so hot from the way you drive she . now she must be grieve for I , and I never let she grieve long . ' 
 I be too glad to go with he , and Annie come slyly after we . the filly be walk to and fro on the naked floor of the stable   for he would not let she have any straw , until he should make a bed for she , and without so much as a headstall on , for he would not have she fasten . ' do you take my mare for a dog ? ' he have say when John Fry bring he a halter . and now she run to he like a child , and her great eye shine at the lanthorn . 
 ' hit I , Jack , and see what she will do . I will not let she hurt thee . ' he be rub her ear all the time he speak , and she be lean against he . then I make believe to strike he , and in a moment she catch I by the waistband , and lift I clean from the ground , and be cast I down to trample upon I , when he stop she suddenly . 
 ' what think you of that , boy ? have you horse or dog that would do that for you ? ay , and more than that she will do . if I be to whistle , by - and - by , in the tone that tell my danger , she would break this stable - door down , and rush into the room to I . nothing will keep she from I then , stone - wall or church - tower . ah , Winnie , Winnie , you little witch , we shall die together . ' 
 then he turn away with a joke , and begin to feed she nicely , for she be very dainty . not a husk of oat would she touch that have be under the breath of another horse , however hungry she might be . and with her oats he mix some powder , fetch it from his saddle - bag . what this be I could not guess , neither would he tell I , but laugh and call it ' star - shaving . ' he watch she eat every morsel of it , with two or three drink of pure water , minister between while ; and then he make her bed in a form I have never see before , and so we say ' good - night ' to she . 
 afterwards by the fireside he keep we very merry , sit in the great chimney - corner , and make we play game with he . and all the while he be smoke tobacco in a manner I never have see before , not use any pipe for it , but have it roll in little stick about as long as my finger , blunt at one end and sharp at the other . the sharp end he would put in his mouth , and lie a brand of wood to the other , and then draw a white cloud of curl smoke , and we never tired of watch he . I want he to let I do it , but he say , ' no , my son ; it be not mean for boy . ' then Annie put up her lip and ask , with both hand on his knee   for she have take to he wonderfully , ' be it mean for girl then cousin Tom ? ' but she have well not have ask , for he give it she to try , and she shut both eye , and suck at it . one breath , however , be quite enough , for it make her cough so violently that Lizzie and I must thump she back until she be almost cry . to atone for that , cousin Tom set to , and tell we whole page of story , not about his own doing at all , but strangely enough they seem to concern almost every one else we have ever hear of . without halt once for a word or a deed , his tale flow onward as freely and brightly as the flame of the wood up the chimney , and with no small variety . for he speak with the voice of twenty people , give each person the proper manner , and the proper place to speak from ; so that Annie and Lizzie run all about , and search the clock and the linen - press . and he change his face every moment so , and with such power of mimicry that without so much as a smile of his own , he make even mother laugh so that she break her new tenpenny waistband ; and as for we child , we roll on the floor , and Betty Muxworthy roar in the wash - up . 




 CHAPTER XII 

 a MAN JUSTLY popular 

 now although Mr. Faggus be so clever , and generous , and celebrate , I know not whether , upon the whole , we be rather proud of he as a member of our family , or incline to be ashamed of he . and indeed I think that the sway of the balance hang upon the company we be in . for instance , with the boy at Brendon for there be no village at Oare I be exceed proud to talk of he , and would freely brag of my Cousin Tom . but with the rich parson of the neighbourhood , or the justice   who come round now and then , and be glad to ride up to a warm farm - house , or even the well - to - do tradesman of Porlock in a word , any settle power , which be afraid of lose thing with all of they we be very shy of claim our kinship to that great outlaw . 
 and sure , I should pity , as well as condemn he though our way in the world be so different , know as I do his story ; which knowledge , methink , would often lead we to let alone God 's prerogative judgment , and hold by man 's privilege pity . not that I would find excuse for Tom 's downright dishonesty , which be beyond doubt a disgrace to he , and no credit to his kinsfolk ; only that it come about without his meaning any harm or see how he take to wrong ; yet gradually know it . and now , to save any further trouble , and to meet those who disparage he   without allowance for the time or the cross lay upon he , I will tell the history of he , just as if he be not my cousin , and hop to be heed . and I defy any man to say that a word of this be either false , or in any way colour by family . much cause he have to be harsh with the world ; and yet all acknowledge he very pleasant , when a man give up his money . and often and often he pay the toll for the carriage come after he , because he have empty their pocket , and would not add inconvenience . by trade he have be a blacksmith , in the town of Northmolton , in Devonshire , a rough rude place at the end of Exmoor , so that many people marvel if such a man be breed there . not only could he read and write , but he have solid substance ; a piece of land worth a hundred pound , and right of common for two hundred sheep , and a score and a half of beast , lift up or lie down . and be leave an orphan   with all these care upon he   he begin to work right early , and make such a fame at the shoeing of horse , that the farrier of Barum be like to lose their custom . and indeed he win a golden Jacobus for the good - shod nag in the north of Devon , and some say that he never be forgive . 
 as to that , I know no more , except that man be jealous . but whether it be that , or not , he fall into bitter trouble within a month of his victory ; when his trade be grow upon he , and his sweetheart ready to marry he . for he love a maid of Southmolton   a currier 's daughter I think she be , and her name be Betsy Paramore , and her father have give consent ; and Tom Faggus , wish to look his good , and be clean of course , have a tailor at work upstairs for he , who have come all the way from Exeter . and Betsy 's thing be ready too for which they accuse he afterwards , as if he could help that when suddenly , like a thunderbolt , a lawyer 's writ fall upon he . 
 this be the beginning of a law - suit with Sir Robert Bampfylde , a gentleman of the neighbourhood , who try to oust he from his common , and drive his cattle and harass they . and by that suit of law poor Tom be ruin altogether , for Sir Robert could pay for much swearing ; and then all his good and his farm be sell up , and even his smithery take . but he saddle his horse , before they could catch he , and ride away to Southmolton , look more like a madman than a good farrier , as the people say who see he . but when he arrive there , instead of comfort , they show he the face of the door alone ; for the news of his loss be before he , and Master Paramore be a sound , prudent man , and a high member of the town council . it be say that they even give he notice to pay for Betsy 's wedding - clothe , now that he be too poor to marry she . this may be false , and indeed I doubt it ; in the first place , because Southmolton be a busy place for talk ; and in the next , that I do not think the action would have lain at law , especially as the maid lose nothing , but use it all for her wedding next month with Dick Vellacott , of Mockham . 
 all this be very sore upon Tom ; and he take it to heart so grievously , that he say , as a well man might have say , be loose of mind and property , ' the world hath preyed on I like a wolf . God help I now to prey on the world . ' 
 and in sooth it do seem , for a while , as if Providence be with he ; for he take rare toll on the highway , and his name be soon as good as gold anywhere this side of Bristowe . he study his business by night and by day , with three horse all in hard work , until he have make a fine reputation ; and then it be competent to he to rest , and he have plenty leave for charity . and I ought to say for society too , for he truly love high society , treat squire and nobleman   who much affect his company   to the very good fare of the hostel . and they say that once the King 's justitiarie , be upon circuit , accept his invitation , declare merrily that if never true bill have be find against he , mine host should now be qualified to draw one . and so the landlord do ; and he always pay they handsomely , so that all of they be kind to he , and contend for his visit . let it be know in any township that Mr. Faggus be take his leisure at the inn , and straightway all the man flock thither to drink his health without outlay , and all the woman to admire he ; while the child be set at the cross - road to give warning of any officer . one of his early meeting be with Sir Robert Bampfylde himself , who be rid along the Barum road with only one serve - man after he . Tom Faggus put a pistol to his head , be then oblige to be violent , through want of reputation ; while the serve - man pretend to be along way round the corner . then the baronet pull out his purse , quite tremble in the hurry of his politeness . Tom take the purse , and his ring , and time - piece , and then hand they back with a very low bow , say that it be against all usage for he to rob a robber . then he turn to the unfaithful knave , and trounce he right well for his cowardice , and strip he of all his property . 
 but now Mr. Faggus keep only one horse , lest the Government should steal they ; and that one be the young mare Winnie . how he come by she he never would tell , but I think that she be present to he by a certain Colonel , a lover of sport , and very clever in horseflesh , whose life Tom have save from some gambler . when I have add that Faggus as yet have never be guilty of bloodshed   for his eye , and the click of his pistol at first , and now his high reputation make all his wish respect , and that he never rob a poor man , neither insult a woman , but be very good to the Church , and of hot patriotic opinion , and full of jest and jollity , I have say as much as be fair for he , and show why he be so popular . everybody curse the Doones , who live apart disdainfully . but all good people like Mr. Faggus when he have not rob they and many a poor sick man or woman bless he for other people 's money ; and all the hostler , stable - boy , and tapster entirely worship he . 
 I have be rather long , and perhaps tedious , in my account of he , lest at any time hereafter his character should be misunderstood , and his good name disparage ; whereas he be my second cousin , and the lover of my but let that bide . ' tis a melancholy story . 
 he come again about three month afterwards , in the beginning of the spring - time , and bring I a beautiful new carbine , have learn my love of such thing , and my great desire to shoot straight . but mother would not let I have the gun , until he aver upon his honour that he have buy it honestly . and so he have , no doubt , so far as it be honest to buy with money acquire rampantly . Scarce could I stop to make my bullet in the mould which come along with it , but must be off to the Quarry Hill , and new target I have make there . and he teach I then how to ride bright Winnie , who be grow since I have see she , but remember I most kindly . after make much of Annie , who have a wondrous liking for he and he say he be her godfather , but God know how he could have be , unless they confirm he precociously away he go , and young Winnie 's side shine like a cherry by candlelight . 
 now I feel that of those boyish day I have little more to tell , because everything go quietly , as the world for the most part do with we . I begin to work at the farm in earnest , and try to help my mother , and when I remember Lorna Doone , it seem no more than the thought of a dream , which I could hardly call to mind . now who care to know how many bushel of wheat we grow to the acre , or how the cattle milche till we eat they , or what the turn of the season be ? but my stupid self seem like to be the big of all the cattle ; for have much to look after the sheep , and be always in kind appetite , I grow four inch long in every year of my farming , and a matter of two inch wide ; until there be no man of my size to be see elsewhere upon Exmoor . let that pass : what odd to any how tall or wide I be ? there be no Doone 's door at Plover 's Barrows and if there be I could never go through it . they vex I so much about my size , long before I have complete it , gird at I with paltry joke whose wit be good only to stay at home , that I grow shame - face about the matter , and fear to encounter a look - glass . but mother be very proud , and say she never could have too much of I . 
 the bad of all to make I ashamed of bear my head so high a thing I see no way to help , for I never could hang my chin down , and my back be like a gatepost whenever I try to bend it the bad of all be our little Eliza , who never could come to a size herself , though she have the wine from the Sacrament at Easter and Allhallowmas , only to be small and skinny , sharp , and clever crookedly . not that her body be out of the straight   be too small for that perhaps , but that her wit be full of corner , jagged , and strange , and uncomfortable . you never could tell what she might say next ; and I like not that kind of woman . now God forgive I for talk so of my own father 's daughter , and so much the more by reason that my father could not help it . the right way be to face the matter , and then be sorry for every one . my mother fall grievously on a slide , which John Fry have make nigh the apple - room door , and hide with straw from the stable , to cover his own great idleness . my father lay John 's nose on the ice , and keep he warm in spite of it ; but it be too late for Eliza . she be bear next day with more mind than body the bad thing that can befall a man . 
 but Annie , my other sister , be now a fine fair girl , beautiful to behold . I could look at she by the fireside , for an hour together , when I be not too sleepy , and think of my dear father . and she would do the same thing by I , only wait the between of the blaze . her hair be do up in a knot behind , but some would fall over her shoulder ; and the dancing of the light be sweet to see through a man 's eyelash . there never be a face that show the light or the shadow of feeling , as if the heart be sun to it , more than our dear Annie 's do . to look at she carefully , you might think that she be not dwell on anything ; and then she would know you be look at she , and those eye would tell all about it . God know that I try to be simple enough , to keep to his meaning in I , and not make the bad of his child . yet often have I be put to shame , and ready to bite my tongue off , after speak amiss of anybody , and let out my littleness , when suddenly mine eye have meet the pure soft gaze of Annie . 
 as for the Doones , they be thrive still , and no one to come against they ; except indeed by word of mouth , to which they lend no heed whatever . complaint be make from time to time , both in high and low quarter   as the rank might be of the people rob , and once or twice in the high of all , to wit , the King himself . but his Majesty make a good joke about it   not mean any harm , I doubt , and be so much pleased with himself thereupon , that he quite forgave the mischief . moreover , the main authority be a long way off ; and the Chancellor have no cattle on Exmoor ; and as for my lord the Chief Justice , some rogue have take his silver spoon ; whereupon his lordship swear that never another man would he hang until he have that one by the neck . therefore the Doones go on as they list , and none see fit to meddle with they . for the only man who would have dare to come to close quarter with they , that be to say Tom Faggus , himself be a quarry for the law , if ever it should be unhooded . moreover , he have transfer his business to the neighbourhood of Wantage , in the county of Berks , where he find the climate drier , also good down and common excellent for galloping , and rich yeoman than ours be , and well road to rob they on . 
 some folk , who have wiser attend to their own affair , say that I   be sizeable now , and able to shoot not badly   ought to do something against those Doones , and show what I be make of . but for a time I be very bashful , shake when call upon suddenly , and blush as deep as a maiden ; for my strength be not come upon I , and mayhap I have grow in front of it . and again , though I love my father still , and would fire at a word about he , I see not how it would do he good for I to harm his injurer . some race be of revengeful kind , and will for year pursue their wrong , and sacrifice this world and the next for a moment 's foul satisfaction , but methink this come of some black blood , pervert and never purify . and I doubt but man of true english birth be stouter than so to be twist , though some of the woman may take that turn , if their own life run unkindly . 
 let that pass I be never good at talk of thing beyond I . all I know be , that if I have meet the Doone who have kill my father , I would gladly have thrash he black and blue , suppose I be able ; but would never have fire a gun at he , unless he begin that game with I , or fall upon more of my family , or be violent among woman . and to do they justice , my mother and Annie be equally kind and gentle , but Eliza would flame and grow white with contempt , and not trust herself to speak to we . 
 now a strange thing come to pass that winter , when I be twenty - one year old , a very strange thing , which affright the rest , and make I feel uncomfortable . not that there be anything in it , to do harm to any one , only that none could explain it , except by attribute it to the devil . the weather be very mild and open , and scarcely any snow fall ; at any rate , none lie on the ground , even for an hour , in the high part of Exmoor ; a thing which I know not before nor since , as long as I can remember . but the night be wonderfully dark , as though with no star in the heaven ; and all day long the mist be roll upon the hill and down they , as if the whole land be a wash - house . the moorland be full of snipe and teal , and curlew fly and cry , and lapwing flap heavily , and raven hover round dead sheep ; yet no redshank nor dottrell , and scarce any golden plover   of which we have great store generally   but vast lonely bird , that cry at night , and move the whole air with their pinion ; yet no man ever see they . it be dismal as well as dangerous now for any man to go fowling   which of late I love much in the winter   because the fog would come down so thick that the pan of the gun be reek , and the fowl out of sight ere the powder kindle , and then the sound of the piece be so dead , that the shooter fear harm , and glance over his shoulder . but the danger of course be far less in this than in lose of the track , and fall into the mire , or over the brim of a precipice . 
 nevertheless , I must need go out , be young and very stupid , and fear of be afraid ; a fear which a wise man have long cast by , have learn of the manifold danger which ever and ever encompass we . and beside this folly and wildness of youth , perchance there be something , I know not what , of the joy we have in uncertainty . mother , in fear of my miss home though for that matter , I could smell supper , when hungry , through a hundred land - yard of fog my dear mother , who think of I ten time for one thought about herself , give order to ring the great sheep - bell , which hang above the pigeon - cote , every ten minute of the day , and the sound come through the plait of fog , and I be vex about it , like the letter of a copy - book . it remind I , too , of Blundell 's bell , and the grief to go into school again . 
 but during those two month of fog   for we have it all the winter , the sad and the heavy thing be to stand beside the sea . to be upon the beach yourself , and see the long wave come in ; to know that they be long wave , but only see a piece of they ; and to hear they lift roundly , swell over smooth green rock , plash down in the hollow corner , but bear on all the same as ever , soft and sleek and sorrowful , till their little noise be over . 
 one old man who live at Lynmouth , seek to be bury there , have be more than half over the world , though shy to speak about it , and fain to come home to his birthplace , this old Will Watcombe   who dwelt by the water   say that our strange winter arise from a thing he call the ' Gulf - stream ' , rush up Channel suddenly . he say it be hot water , almost fit for a man to shave with , and it throw all our cold water out , and ruin the fish and the spawning - time , and a cold spring would come after it . I be fond of go to Lynmouth on Sunday to hear this old man talk , for sometimes he would discourse with I , when nobody else could move he . he tell I that this powerful flood set in upon our west so hard sometimes once in ten year , and sometimes not for fifty , and the Lord only know the sense of it ; but that when it come , therewith come warmth and cloud , and fog , and moisture , and nut , and fruit , and even shell ; and all the tide be throw abroad . as for nut he wink awhile , and chew a piece of tobacco ; yet do I not comprehend he . only afterwards I hear that nut with liquid kernel come , travel on the Gulf stream ; for never before be know so much foreign cordial land upon our coast , float ashore by mistake in the fog , and   what with the tossing and the mist   too much astray to learn its duty . 
 Folk , who be ever too prone to talk , say that will watcombe himself know well than anybody else about this drift of the Gulf - stream , and the place where it would come ashore , and the cave that take the in - draught . but De Whichehalse , our great magistrate , certify that there be no proof of unlawful importation ; neither good cause to suspect it , at a time of christian charity . and we know that it be a foul thing for some quarryman to say that night after night they have be dig a new cellar at Ley Manor to hold the little mark of respect find in the cavern at high - water weed . let that be , it be none of my business to speak evil of dignity ; duly we common people joke of the ' Gulp - stream , ' as we call it . 
 but the thing which astonish and frighten we so , be not , I do assure you , the landing of foreign spirit , nor the loom of a lugger at twilight in the gloom of the winter moonrise . that which make as crouch in by the fire , or draw the bed - clothe over we , and try to think of something else , be a strange mysterious sound . 
 at grey of night , when the sun be go , and no red in the west remain , neither be star forthcoming , suddenly a wail voice rise along the valley , and a sound in the air , as of people run . it matter not whether you stand on the moor , or crouch behind rock away from it , or down among reedy place ; all as one the sound would come , now from the heart of the earth beneath , now overhead bear down on you . and then there be rush of something by , and melancholy laughter , and the hair of a man would stand on end before he could reason properly . 
 God , in his mercy , know that I be stupid enough for any man , and very slow of impression , nor ever could bring myself to believe that our Father would let the evil one get the upper hand of we . but when I have hear that sound three time , in the lonely gloom of the evening fog , and the cold that follow the line of air , I be loath to go abroad by night , even so far as the stable , and love the light of a candle more , and the glow of a fire with company . 
 there be many story about it , of course , all over the breadth of the moorland . but those who have hear it most often declare that it must be the wail of a woman 's voice , and the rustle of robe flee horribly , and fiend in the fog go after she . to that , however , I pay no heed , when anybody be with I ; only we draw more close together , and bar the door at sunset . 




 CHAPTER XIII 

 MASTER HUCKABACK come in 

 Mr. Reuben Huckaback , whom many good folk in Dulverton will remember long after my time , be my mother 's uncle , be indeed her mother 's brother . he own the very good shop in the town , and do a fine trade in soft ware , especially when the pack - horse come safely in at Christmas - time . and we be now his only kindre   except indeed his granddaughter , little Ruth Huckaback , of whom no one take any heed , mother beheld it a christian duty to keep as well as could be with he , both for love of a nice old man , and for the sake of her child . and truly , the Dulverton people say that he be the rich man in their town , and could buy up half the county armiger ; ' ay , and if it come to that , they would like to see any man , at Bampton , or at Wivelscombe , and you might say almost Taunton , who could put down golden Jacobus and Carolus against he . 
 now this old gentleman so they call he , accord to his money ; and I have see many bad one , more violent and less wealthy he must need come away that time to spend the New Year - tide with we ; not that he want to do it   for he hat country - life , but because my mother press , as mother will do to a good bag of gold , have wring a promise from he ; and the only boast of his life be that never yet have he break his word , at least since he open business . 
 now it please God that Christmas - time   in spite of all the fog   to send safe home to Dulverton , and what be more , with their load quite safe , a goodly string of packhorse . nearly half of their charge be for Uncle Reuben , and he know how to make the most of it . then have balance his debit and credit , and set the writ run against defaulter , as behove a good Christian at Christmas - tide , he saddle his horse , and ride off towards Oare , with a good stout coat upon he , and leave Ruth and his head man plenty to do , and little to eat , until they should see he again . 
 it have be settle between we that we should expect he soon after noon on the last day of December . for the Doones be lazy and fond of bed , as the manner be of dishonest folk , the sure way to escape they be to travel before they be up and about , to - wit , in the forenoon of the day . but herein we reckon without our host : for be in high festivity , as become good Papists , the robber be too lazy , it seem , to take the trouble of go to bed ; and forth they ride on the Old Year - morning , not with any view of business , but purely in search of mischief . 
 we have put off our dinner till one o'clock   which to I be a sad foregoing , and there be to be a brave supper at six of the clock , upon New year's - eve ; and the singer to come with their lanthorn , and do it outside the parlour - window , and then have hot cup till their head should go round , after make away with the victual . for although there be nobody now in our family to be churchwarden of Oare , it be well admit that we be the people entitle alone to that dignity ; and though Nicholas Snowe be in office by name , he manage it only by mother 's advice ; and a pretty mess he make of it , so that every one long for a Ridd again , soon as ever I should be old enough . this Nicholas Snowe be to come in the evening , with his three tall comely daughter , strap girl , and well skilled in the dairy ; and the story be all over the parish , on a stupid conceit of John Fry 's , that I should have be in love with all three , if there have be but one of they . these snowe be to come , and come they do , partly because Mr. Huckaback like to see fine young maiden , and partly because none but Nicholas Snowe could smoke a pipe now all around our part , except of the very high people , whom we durst never invite . and Uncle Ben , as we all know well , be a great hand at his pipe , and would sit for hour over it , in our warm chimney - corner , and never want to say a word , unless it be inside he ; only he like to have somebody there over against he smoking . 
 now when I come in , before one o'clock , after see to the cattle for the day be thick than ever , and we must keep the cattle close at home , if we wish to see any more of they I fully expect to find Uncle Ben sit in the fireplace , lift one cover and then another , as his favourite manner be , and make sweet mouth over they ; for he love our bacon rarely , and they have no good leek at Dulverton ; and he be a man who always would see his business do himself . but there instead of my find he with his quaint dry face pull out at I , and then shut up sharp not to be cheat who should run out but Betty Muxworthy , and poke I with a saucepan lid . 
 ' get out of that now , Betty , ' I say in my politest manner , for really Betty be now become a great domestic evil . she would have her own way so , and of all thing the most distressful be for a man to try to reason . 
 ' Zider - press , ' cry Betty again , for she think it a fine joke to call I that , because of my size , and my hatred of it ; ' here be a rare get up , anyhow . ' 
 ' a rare good dinner , you mean , Betty . well , and I have a rare good appetite . ' with that I want to go and smell it , and not to stop for Betty . 
 ' troost thee for thiccy , Jan Ridd . but thee must keep it bit langer , I reckon . her baint coom , Maister Ziderpress . Whatt'e mak of that now ? ' 
 ' do you mean to say that Uncle Ben have not arrive yet , Betty ? ' 
 ' raive ! I knaw nout about that , whuther a hath of noo . only I tell ' e , her baint coom . rackon they doonese hath gat ' un . ' 
 and Betty , who hat Uncle Ben , because he never give she a groat , and she be not allow to dine with he , I be sorry to say that Betty Muxworthy grin all across , and poke I again with the greasy saucepan cover . but I mislike so to be treat , stride through the kitchen indignantly , for Betty behave to I even now , as if I be only Eliza . 
 ' oh , Johnny , Johnny , ' my mother cry , run out of the grand show - parlour , where the case of stuff bird be , and peacock - feather , and the white hare kill by grandfather ; ' I be so glad you be come at last . there be something sadly amiss , Johnny . ' 
 Mother have upon her wrist something very wonderful , of the nature of fal - lal as we say , and for which she have an inborn turn , be of good draper family , and polish above the yeomanry . nevertheless I could never bear it , partly because I feel it to be out of place in our good farm - house , partly because I hate frippery , partly because it seem to I to have nothing to do with father , and partly because I never could tell the reason of my hat it . and yet the poor soul have put they on , not to show her hand off   which be above her station   but simply for her child 's sake , because Uncle Ben have give they . but another thing , I never could bear for man or woman to call I , ' johnny , ' ' jack , ' or ' John , ' I care not which ; and that be honest enough , and no smallness of I there , I say . 
 ' well , mother , what be the matter , then ? ' 
 ' I be sure you need not be angry , Johnny . I only hope it be nothing to grieve about , instead of be angry . you be very sweet - temper , I know , John Ridd , and perhaps a little too sweet at time ' here she mean the Snowe girl , and I hang my head ' but what would you say if the people there ' she never would call they ' Doones ' ' have get your poor Uncle Reuben , horse , and Sunday coat , and all ? ' 
 ' why , mother , I should be sorry for they . he would set up a shop by the river - side , and come away with all their money . ' 
 ' that all you have to say , John ! and my dinner do to a very turn , and the supper all fit to go down , and no worry , only to eat and be do with it ! and all the new plate come from Watchett , with the Watchett blue upon they , at the risk of the life of everybody , and the capia from good Aunt Jane for stuff a curlew with onion before he begin to get cold , and make a woodcock of he , and the way to turn the flap over in the inside of a roast pig ' 
 ' well , mother dear , I be very sorry . but let we have our dinner . you know we promise not to wait for he after one o'clock ; and you only make we hungry . everything will be spoil , mother , and what a pity to think of ! after that I will go to seek for he in the thick of the fog , like a needle in a hay - band . that be to say , unless you think ' for she look very grave about it ' unless you really think , mother , that I ought to go without dinner . ' 
 ' oh no , John , I never think that , thank God ! bless he for my child 's appetite ; and what be Uncle Ben to they ? ' 
 so we make a very good dinner indeed , though wish that he could have some of it , and wonder how much to leave for he ; and then , as no sound of his horse have be hear , I set out with my gun to look for he . 
 I follow the track on the side of the hill , from the farm - yard , where the sledd - mark be for we have no wheel upon Exmoor yet , nor ever shall , I suppose ; though a dunder - head man try it last winter , and break his axle piteously , and be nigh to break his neck and after that I go all along on the ridge of the rabbit - cleve , with the brook run thin in the bottom ; and then down to the Lynn stream and leap it , and so up the hill and the moor beyond . the fog hang close all around I then , when I turn the crest of the highland , and the gorse both before and behind I look like a man crouch down in ambush . but still there be a good cloud of daylight , be scarce three of the clock yet , and when a lead of red deer come across , I could tell they from sheep even now . I be half inclined to shoot at they , for the child do love venison ; but they droop their head so , and look so faithful , that it seem hard measure to do it . if one of they have bolt away , no doubt I have let go at he . 
 after that I keep on the track , trudge very stoutly , for nigh upon three mile , and my beard   now begin to grow at some length   be full of great drop and prickly , whereat I be very proud . I have not so much as a dog with I , and the place be unkind and lonesome , and the roll cloud very desolate ; and now if a wild sheep run across he be scared at I as an enemy ; and I for my part could not tell the meaning of the mark on he . we call all this part Gibbet - moor , not be in our parish ; but though there be gibbet enough upon it , most part of the body be go for the value of the chain , they say , and the teaching of young chirurgeon . but of all this I have little fear , be no more a schoolboy now , but a youth well - acquaint with Exmoor , and the wise art of the sign - post , whereby a man , who bar the road , now open it up both way with his finger - bone , so far as rogue allow he . my carbine be load and freshly prim , and I know myself to be even now a match in strength for any two man of the size around our neighbourhood , except in the Glen Doone . ' Girt Jan Ridd , ' I be call already , and folk grow fear to wrestle with I ; though I be tired of hear about it , and often long to be small . and most of all upon Sundays , when I have to make way up our little church , and the maiden titter at I . 
 the soft white mist come thick around I , as the evening fall ; and the peat rick here and there , and the furze - huck of the summer - time , be all out of shape in the twist of it . by - and - by , I begin to doubt where I be , or how come there , not have see a gibbet lately ; and then I hear the draught of the wind up a hollow place with rock to it ; and for the first time fear break out   like cold sweat   upon I . and yet I know what a fool I be , to fear nothing but a sound ! but when I stop to listen , there be no sound , more than a beat noise , and that be all inside I . therefore I go on again , make company of myself , and keep my gun quite ready . 
 now when I come to an unknown place , where a stone be set up endwise , with a faint red cross upon it , and a polish from some conflict , I gather my courage to stop and think , have speed on the way too hotly . against that stone I set my gun , try my spirit to leave it so , but keep with half a hand for it ; and then what to do next be the wonder . as for find Uncle Ben that be his own business , or at any rate his executor 's ; first I have to find myself , and plentifully would thank God to find myself at home again , for the sake of all our family . 
 the volume of the mist come roll at I   like great log of wood , pillow out with sleepiness , and between they there be nothing more than wait for the next one . then everything go out of sight , and glad be I of the stone behind I , and view of mine own shoe . then a distant noise go by I , as of many horse gallop , and in my fright I set my gun and say , ' God send something to shoot at . ' yet nothing come , and my gun fall back , without my will to lower it . 
 but presently , while I be think ' what a fool I be ! ' arise as if from below my foot , so that the great stone tremble , that long , lament lonesome sound , as of an evil spirit not know what to do with it . for the moment I stand like a root , without either hand or foot to help I , and the hair of my head begin to crawl , lift my hat , as a snail lift his house ; and my heart like a shuttle go to and fro . but find no harm to come of it , neither visible form approach , I wipe my forehead , and hop for the good , and resolve to run every step of the way , till I draw our own latch behind I . 
 yet here again I be disappoint , for no soon be I come to the cross - way by the black pool in the hole , but I hear through the patter of my own foot a rough low sound very close in the fog , as of a hobbled sheep a - coughing . I listen , and fear , and yet listen again , though I want not to hear it . for be in haste of the homeward road , and all my heart have heel to it , loath I be to stop in the dusk for the sake of an aged wether . yet partly my love of all animal , and partly my fear of the farmer 's disgrace , compel I to go to the succour , and the noise be come near . a dry short wheezing sound it be , bar with cough and want of breath ; but thus I make the meaning of it . 
 ' Lord have mercy upon I ! o Lord , upon my soul have mercy ! an if I cheat Sam Hicks last week , Lord know how well he deserve it , and lie in every stocking 's mouth oh Lord , where be I a - go ? ' 
 these word , with many jog between they , come to I through the darkness , and then a long groan and a choking . I make towards the sound , as nigh as ever I could guess , and presently be meet , point - blank , by the head of a mountain - pony . upon its back lie a man bind down , with his foot on the neck and his head to the tail , and his arm fall down like stirrup . the wild little nag be scared of its life by the unaccustomed burden , and have be toss and roll hard , in desire to get ease of it . 
 before the little horse could turn , I catch he , jade as he be , by his wet and grizzle forelock , and he see that it be vain to struggle , but strove to bite I none the less , until I smite he upon the nose . 
 ' good and worthy sir , ' I say to the man who be rid so roughly ; ' fear nothing ; no harm shall come to thee . ' 
 ' help , good friend , whoever thou art , ' he gasp , but could not look at I , because his neck be jerk so ; ' God hath send thee , and not to rob I , because it be do already . ' 
 ' what , Uncle Ben ! ' I cry , let go the horse in amazement , that the rich man in Dulverton ' Uncle Ben here in this plight ! what , Mr. Reuben Huckaback ! ' 
 ' an honest hosier and draper , serge and longcloth warehouseman ' he groan from rib to rib ' at the sign of the Gartered Kitten in the loyal town of Dulverton . for God 's sake , let I down , good fellow , from this accursed marrow - bone ; and a groat of good money will I pay thee , safe in my house to Dulverton ; but take notice that the horse be mine , no less than the nag they rob from I . ' 
 ' what , Uncle Ben , dost thou not know I , thy dutiful nephew John Ridd ? ' 
 not to make a long story of it , I cut the thong that bind he , and set he astride on the little horse ; but he be too weak to stay so . therefore I mount he on my back , turn the horse into horse - step , and lead the pony by the cord which I fasten around his nose , set out for Plover 's barrow . 
 Uncle Ben go fast asleep on my back , be jade and shake beyond his strength , for a man of three - score and five ; and as soon he feel assure of safety he would talk no more . and to tell the truth he snore so loudly , that I could almost believe that fearful noise in the fog every night come all the way from Dulverton . 
 now as soon as ever I bring he in , we set he up in the chimney - corner , comfortable and handsome ; and it be no little delight to I to get he off my back ; for , like his own fortune , Uncle Ben be of a good round figure . he give his long coat a shake or two , and he stamp about in the kitchen , until he be sure of his whereabouts , and then he fall asleep again until supper should be ready . 
 ' he shall marry Ruth , ' he say by - and - by to himself , and not to I ; ' he shall marry Ruth for this , and have my little saving , soon as they be worth the having . very little as yet , very little indeed ; and ever so much go to - day along of they rascal robber . ' 
 my mother make a dreadful stir , of course , about Uncle Ben be in such a plight as this ; so I leave he to her care and Annie 's , and soon they feed he rarely , while I go out to see to the comfort of the capture pony . and in truth he be worth the catching , and serve we very well afterwards , though Uncle Ben be inclined to claim he for his business at Dulverton , where they have cart and that like . ' but , ' I say , ' you shall have he , sir , and welcome , if you will only ride he home as first I find you rid he . ' and with that he drop it . 
 a very strange old man he be , short in his manner , though long of body , glad to do the contrary thing to what any one expect of he , and always look sharp at people , as if he fear to be cheat . this surprise I much at first , because it show his ignorance of what we farmer be an upright race , as you may find , scarcely ever cheat indeed , except upon market - day , and even then no more than may be help by reason of buyer expect it . now our simple way be a puzzle to he , as I tell he very often ; but he only laugh , and rub his mouth with the back of his dry shin hand , and I think he shortly begin to languish for want of some one to higgle with . I have a great mind to give he the pony , because he think himself cheat in that case ; only he would conclude that I do it with some view to a legacy . 
 of course , the Doones , and nobody else , have rob good Uncle Reuben ; and then they grow sportive , and take his horse , an especially sober nag , and bind the master upon the wild one , for a little change as they tell he . for two or three hour they have fine enjoyment chase he through the fog , and make much sport of his groaning ; and then wax hungry , they go their way , and leave he to opportunity . now Mr. Huckaback grow able to walk in a few day ' time , become thereupon impatient , and could not be bring to understand why he should have be rob at all . 
 ' I have never deserve it , ' he say to himself , not know much of Providence , except with a small p to it ; ' I have never deserve it , and will not stand it in the name of our lord the King , not I ! ' at other time he would burst forth thus : ' three - score year and five have I live an honest and laborious life , yet never be I rob before . and now to be rob in my old age , to be rob for the first time now ! ' 
 thereupon of course we would tell he how truly thankful he ought to be for never have be rob before , in spite of live so long in this world , and that he be take a very ungrateful , not to say ungracious , view , in thus repine , and feel aggrieve ; when anyone else would have kneel and thank God for enjoy so long an immunity . but say what we would , it be all as one . Uncle Ben stick fast to it , that he have nothing to thank God for . 




 CHAPTER XIV 

 a motion which end in a mull 

 instead of mind his New - Year pudding , Master Huckaback carry on so about his mighty grievance , that at last we begin to think there must be something in it , after all ; especially as he assure we that choice and costly present for the young people of our household be among the good divest . but mother tell he her child have plenty , and want no gold and silver , and little Eliza speak up and say , ' you can give we the pretty thing , Uncle Ben , when we come in the summer to see you . ' 
 our mother reprove Eliza for this , although it be the heel of her own foot ; and then to satisfy our uncle , she promise to call Farmer Nicholas Snowe , to be of our council that evening , ' and if the young maiden would kindly come , without take thought to smoothe themselves , why it would be all the merry , and who know but what Uncle Huckaback might bless the day of his robbery , etc . , etc . and thorough good honest girl they be , fit helpmate either for shop or farm . ' all of which be mean for I ; but I stick to my platter and answer not . 
 in the evening Farmer Snowe come up , lead his daughter after he , like filly trim for a fair ; and Uncle Ben , who have not see they on the night of his mishap   because word have be send to stop they , be mightily pleased and very pleasant , accord to his town breed way . the damsel have see good company , and soon get over their fear of his wealth , and play he a number of merry prank , which make our mother quite jealous for Annie , who be always shy and diffident . however , when the hot cup be do , and before the mulled wine be ready , we pack all the maiden in the parlour and turn the key upon they ; and then we draw near to the kitchen fire to hear Uncle Ben 's proposal . Farmer Snowe sit up in the corner , care little to bear about anything , but smoke slowly , and nod backward like a sheep - dog dreaming . mother be in the settle , of course , knit hard , as usual ; and Uncle Ben take to a three - legged stool , as if all but that have be thieve from he . Howsoever , he keep his breath from speech , give privilege , as be due , to mother . 
 ' Master Snowe , you be well assured , ' say mother , colour like the furze as it take the flame and fall over , ' that our kinsman here hath receive rough harm on his peaceful journey from Dulverton . the time be bad , as we all know well , and there be no sign of better they , and if I could see our Lord the King I might say thing to move he ! nevertheless , I have have so much of my own account to vex for ' 
 ' you be fly out of the subject , Sarah , ' say Uncle Ben , see tear in her eye , and tired of that matter . 
 ' Zettle the pralimbinarie , ' speak Farmer Snowe , on appeal from we , ' virst zettle the pralimbinarie ; and then we know what be drivin ' at . ' 
 ' preliminary be damned , sir , ' cry Uncle Ben , lose his temper . ' what preliminary be there when I be rob ; I should like to know ? rob in this parish as I can prove , to the eternal disgrace of Oare and the scandal of all England . and I hold this parish to answer for it , sir ; this parish shall make it good , be a nest of foul thief as it be ; ay , farmer , and yeoman , and all of you . I will beggar every man in this parish , if they be not beggar already , ay , and sell your old church up before your eye , but what I will have back my tarlatan , time - piece , saddle , and dove - tail nag . ' 
 Mother look at I , and I look at Farmer Snowe , and we all be sorry for Master Huckaback , put our hand up one to another , that nobody should browbeat he ; because we all know what our parish be , and none the bad for strong language , however rich the man might be . but Uncle Ben take it in a different way . he think that we all be afraid of he , and that Oare parish be but as Moab or Edom , for he to cast his shoe over . 
 ' Nephew Jack , ' he cry , look at I when I be think what to say , and find only emptiness , ' you be a heavy lout , sir ; a bumpkin , a clodhopper ; and I shall leave you nothing , unless it be my boot to grease . ' 
 ' well , uncle , ' I make answer , ' I will grease your boot all the same for that , so long as you be our guest , sir . ' 
 now , that answer , make without a thought , stand I for two thousand pound , as you shall see , by - and - by , perhaps . 
 ' as for the parish , ' my mother cry , be too hard set to contain herself , ' the parish can defend itself , and we may leave it to do so . but our Jack be not like that , sir ; and I will not have he speak of . leave he indeed ! who want you to do more than to leave he alone , sir ; as he might have do you the other night ; and as no one else would have dare to do . and after that , to think so meanly of I , and of my child ! ' 
 ' Hoity , toity , Sarah ! your child , I suppose , be the same as other people 's . ' 
 ' that they be not ; and never will be ; and you ought to know it , Uncle Reuben , if any one in the world ought . other people 's child ! ' 
 ' well , well ! ' Uncle Reuben answer , ' I know very little of child ; except my little Ruth , and she be nothing wonderful . ' 
 ' I never say that my child be wonderful Uncle Ben ; nor do I ever think it . but as for be good ' 
 here mother fetch out her handkerchief , be overcome by our goodness ; and I tell she , with my hand to my mouth , not to notice he ; though he might be worth ten thousand time ten thousand pound . 
 but Farmer Snowe come forward now , for he have some sense sometimes ; and he think it be high time for he to say a word for the parish . 
 ' Maister Huckaback , ' he begin , point with his pipe at he , the end that be do in sealing - wax , ' tooching of what you be plaize to zay 'bout this here parish , and no oother , mind I no oother parish but thee , I use the vreedom , zur , for to tell ' e , that thee be a laiar . ' 
 then Farmer Nicholas Snowe fold his arm across with the bowl of his pipe on the upper one , and give I a nod , and then one to mother , to testify how he have do his duty , and recke not what might come of it . however , he get little thank from we ; for the parish be nothing at all to my mother , compare with her child 's interest ; and I think it hard that an uncle of mine , and an old man too , should be call a liar , by a visitor at our fireplace . for we , in our rude part of the world , count it one of the bad disgrace that could befall a man , to receive the lie from any one . but Uncle Ben , as it seem be use to it , in the way of trade , just as people of fashion be , by a style of courtesy . 
 therefore the old man only look with pity at Farmer Nicholas ; and with a sort of sorrow too , reflect how much he might have make in a bargain with such a customer , so ignorant and hot - headed . 
 ' now let we bandy word no more , ' say mother , very sweetly ; ' nothing be easy than sharp word , except to wish they unspoken ; as I do many and many be the time , when I think of my good husband . but now let we hear from Uncle Reuben what he would have we do to remove this disgrace from amongst we , and to satisfy he of his good . ' 
 ' I care not for my good , woman , ' Master Huckaback answer grandly ; ' although they be of large value , about they I say nothing . but what I demand be this , the punishment of those scoundrel . ' 
 ' Zober , man , zober ! ' cry Farmer Nicholas ; ' we be too naigh Badgery ' ood , to spake like that of they doonese . ' 
 ' pack of coward ! ' say Uncle Reuben , look first at the door , however ; ' much chance I see of get redress from the valour of this Exmoor ! and you , Master Snowe , the very man whom I look to to raise the country , and take the lead as churchwarden why , my young shopman would match his ell against you . Pack of coward , ' cry Uncle Ben , rise and shake his lappet at we ; ' do not   pretend to answer I . shake you all off , that I do nothing more to do with you ! ' 
 we know it useless to answer he , and convey our knowledge to one another , without anything to vex he . however , when the mulled wine be come , and a good deal of it go   the season be Epiphany , Uncle Reuben begin to think that he might have be too hard with we . moreover , he be begin now to respect Farmer Nicholas bravely , because of the way he have smoke his pipe , and the little noise make over they . and Lizzie and Annie be do their good for now we have let the girl out to wake more lightsome uproar ; also young Faith Snowe be toward to keep the old man 's cup aflow , and hansel they to their liking . 
 so at the close of our entertainment , when the girl be go away to fetch and light their lanthorn   over which they make rare noise , blow each the other be out for counting of the spark to come , Master Huckaback stand up , without much aid from the crock - saw , and look at mother and all of we . 
 ' let no one leave this place , ' say he , ' until I have say what I want to say ; for save of ill - will among we ; and growth of cheer and comfort . may be I have carry thing too far , even to the bound of churlishness , and beyond the bound of good manner . I will not unsay one word I have say , have never yet do so in my life ; but I would alter the manner of it , and set it forth in this light . if you folk upon Exmoor here be loath and wary at fighting , yet you be brave at well stuff ; the good and kind I ever know , in the matter of feed . ' 
 here he sit down with tear in his eye , and call for a little mulled bastard . all the maid , who be now come back , race to get it for he , but Annie of course be foremost . and herein end the expedition , a perilous and a great one , against the Doones of Bagworthy ; an enterprise over which we have all talk plainly more than be good for we . for my part , I sleep well that night , feel myself at home again , now that the fighting be put aside , and the fear of it turn to the comfort of talk what we would have do . 




 CHAPTER XV 

 MASTER HUCKABACK fails of warrant 

 on the following day Master Huckaback , with some show of mystery , demand from my mother an escort into a dangerous part of the world , to which his business compel he . my mother make answer to this that he be kindly welcome to take our John Fry with he ; at which the good clothier laugh , and say that John be nothing like big enough , but another John must serve his turn , not only for his size , but because if he be carry away , no stone would be leave unturned upon Exmoor , until he should be bring back again . Hereupon my mother grow very pale , and find fifty reason against my going , each of they weighty than the true one , as Eliza   who be jealous of I   manage to whisper to Annie . on the other hand , I be quite resolve   directly the thing be mention   to see Uncle Reuben through with it ; and it add much to my self - esteem to be the guard of so rich a man . therefore I soon persuade mother , with her head upon my breast , to let I go and trust in God ; and after that I be greatly vex to find that this dangerous enterprise be nothing more than a visit to the Baron de Whichehalse , to lay an information , and sue a warrant against the Doones , and a posse to execute it . 
 Stupid as I always have be , and must ever be no doubt , I could well have tell Uncle Reuben that his journey be no wise than that of the man of Gotham ; that he never would get from Hugh de Whichehalse a warrant against the Doones ; moreover , that if he do get one , his own wig would be sing with it . but for diver reason I hold my peace , partly from youth and modesty , partly from desire to see whatever please God I should see , and partly from other cause . 
 we ride by way of Brendon town , Illford Bridge , and Babbrook , to avoid the great hill above Lynmouth ; and the day be fine and clear again , I laugh in my sleeve at Uncle Reuben for all his fine precaution . when we arrive at Ley Manor , we be show very civilly into the hall , and refresh with good ale and collared head , and the back of a Christmas pudding . I have never be under so fine a roof   unless it be of a church   before ; and it please I greatly to be so kindly entreat by high - bear folk . but Uncle Reuben be vex a little at be set down side by side with a man in a very small way of trade , who be come upon some business there , and who make bold to drink his health after finish their horn of ale . 
 ' Sir , ' say Uncle Ben , look at he , ' my health would fare much well , if you would pay I three pound and twelve shilling , which you have owe I these five year back ; and now we be meet at the Justice 's , the opportunity be good , sir . ' 
 after that , we be call to the Justice - room , where the Baron himself be sit with Colonel Harding , another Justiciary of the King 's peace , to help he . I have see the Baron de Whichehalse before , and be not at all afraid of he , have be at school with his son as he know , and it make he very kind to I . and indeed he be kind to everybody , and all our people speak well of he ; and so much the more because we know that the house be in decadence . for the first De Whichehalse have come from Holland , where he have be a great nobleman , some hundred and fifty year agone . be persecute for his religion , when the spanish power be everything , he flee to England with all he could save , and buy large estate in Devonshire . since then his descendant have intermarry with ancient county family , cottwell , and marwood , and Walronds , and welse of Pylton , and Chichesters of Hall ; and several of the lady bring they large increase of property . and so about fifty year before the time of which I be write , there be few name in the West of England think more of than De Whichehalse . but now they have lose a great deal of land , and therefore of that which go with land , as surely as fame belong to earth I mean big reputation . how they have lose it , none could tell ; except that as the first descendant have a manner of amass , so the later one be gift with a power of scatter . whether this come of good Devonshire blood open the sluice of Low Country vein , be beyond both my province and my power to inquire . anyhow , all people love this last strain of De Whichehalse far more than the name have be like a hundred year agone .
