 Hugh de Whichehalse , a white - haired man , of very noble presence , with friendly blue eye and a sweet smooth forehead , and aquiline nose quite beautiful   as you might expect in a lady of birth , and thin lip curve delicately , this gentleman rise as we enter the room ; while Colonel Harding turn on his chair , and strike one spur against the other . I be sure that , without know aught of either , we must have reverence more of the two the one who show respect to we . and yet nine gentleman out of ten make this dull mistake when deal with the class below they ! 
 Uncle Reuben make his very good scrape , and then walk up to the table , try to look as if he do not know himself to be wealthy than both the gentleman put together . of course he be no stranger to they , any more than I be ; and , as it prove afterwards , Colonel Harding owe he a lump of money , upon very good security . of he Uncle Reuben take no notice , but address himself to De Whichehalse . 
 the Baron smile very gently , so soon as he learn the cause of this visit , and then he reply quite reasonably . 
 ' a warrant against the Doones , Master Huckaback . which of the Doones , so please you ; and the christian name , what be they ? ' 
 ' my lord , I be not their godfather ; and most like they never have any . but we all know old Sir Ensor 's name , so that may be no obstacle . ' 
 ' Sir Ensor Doone and his son so be it . how many son , Master Huckaback , and what be the name of each one ? ' 
 ' how can I tell you , my lord , even if I have know they all as well as my own shop - boy ? nevertheless there be seven of they , and that should be no obstacle . ' 
 ' a warrant against Sir Ensor Doone , and seven son of Sir Ensor Doone , christian name unknown , and doubt if they have any . so far so good Master Huckaback . I have it all down in writing . Sir Ensor himself be there , of course , as you have give in evidence ' 
 ' no , no , my lord , I never say that : I never say ' 
 ' if he can prove that he be not there , you may be indict for perjury . but as for those seven son of his , of course you can swear that they be his son and not his nephew , or grandchild , or even no Doones at all ? ' 
 ' my lord , I can swear that they be Doones . moreover , I can pay for any mistake I make . therein need be no obstacle . ' 
 ' oh , yes , he can pay ; he can pay well enough , ' say Colonel Harding shortly . 
 ' I be heartily glad to hear it , ' reply the Baron pleasantly ; ' for it prove after all that this robbery   if robbery there have be   be not so very ruinous . sometimes people think they be rob , and then it be very sweet afterwards to find that they have not be so ; for it add to their joy in their property . now , be you quite convinced , good sir , that these people   if there be any   stole , or take , or even borrow anything at all from you ? ' 
 ' my lord , do you think that I be drunk ? ' 
 ' not for a moment , Master Huckaback . although excuse might be make for you at this time of the year . but how do you know that your visitor be of this particular family ? ' 
 ' because it could be nobody else . because , in spite of the fog ' 
 ' Fog ! ' cry Colonel Harding sharply . 
 ' Fog ! ' say the Baron , with emphasis . ' ah , that explain the whole affair . to be sure , now I remember , the weather have be too thick for a man to see the head of his own horse . the Doones   if still there be any Doones   could never have come abroad ; that be as sure as simony . Master Huckaback , for your good sake , I be heartily glad that this charge have miscarry . I thoroughly understand it now . the fog explain the whole of it . ' 
 ' go back , my good fellow , ' say Colonel Harding ; ' and if the day be clear enough , you will find all your thing where you leave they . I know , from my own experience , what it be to be catch in an Exmoor fog . ' 
 Uncle Reuben , by this time , be so put out , that he hardly know what he be say . 
 ' my lord , Sir Colonel , be this your justice ! if I go to London myself for it , the King shall know how his commission how a man may be rob , and the justice prove that he ought to be hang at back of it ; that in his good shire of Somerset ' 
 ' your pardon a moment , good sir , ' De Whichehalse interrupt he ; ' but I be about   have hear your case   to mention what need be an obstacle , and , I fear , would prove a fatal one , even if satisfactory proof be afford of a felony . the mal - feasance   if any   be lay in Somerset ; but we , two humble servant of his Majesty , be in commission of his peace for the county of Devon only , and therefore could never deal with it . ' 
 ' and why , in the name of God , ' cry Uncle Reuben now carry at last fairly beyond himself , ' why could you not say as much at first , and save I all this waste of time and worry of my temper ? gentleman , you be all in league ; all of you stick together . you think it fair sport for an honest trader , who make no sham as you do , to be rob and wellnigh murder , so long as they who do it win the high birthright of felony . if a poor sheep stealer , to save his child from die of starvation , have dare to look at a two - month lamb , he would swing on the Manor gallow , and all of you cry " good riddance ! " but now , because good birth and bad manner ' here poor Uncle Ben , not be so strong as before the Doones have play with he , begin to foam at the mouth a little , and his tongue go into the hollow where his short grey whisker be . 
 I forget how we come out of it , only I be greatly shock at beard of the gentry so , and mother scarce could see her way , when I tell she all about it . ' depend upon it you be wrong , John , ' be all I could get out of she ; though what have I do but listen , and touch my forelock , when call upon . ' John , you may take my word for it , you have not do as you should have do . your father would have be shock to think of go to Baron de Whichehalse , and in his own house insult he ! and yet it be very brave of you John . just like you , all over . and   as none of the man be here , dear John   I be proud of you for do it . ' 
 all throughout the homeward road , Uncle Ben have be very silent , feel much displeased with himself and still more so with other people . but before he go to bed that night , he just say to I , ' Nephew Jack , you have not behave so badly as the rest to I . and because you have no gift of talk , I think that I may trust you . now , mark my word , this villain job shall not have end here . I have another card to play . ' 
 ' you mean , sir , I suppose , that you will go to the justice of this shire , Squire Maunder , or Sir Richard Blewitt , or ' 
 ' Oaf , I mean nothing of the sort ; they would only make a laughing - stock , as those Devonshire people do , of I . no , I will go to the King himself , or a man who be big than the king , and to whom I have ready access . I will not tell thee his name at present , only if thou art bring before he , never wilt thou forget it . ' that be true enough , by the bye , as I discover afterwards , for the man he mean be Judge Jeffreys . 
 ' and when be you likely to see he , sir ? ' 
 ' maybe in the spring , maybe not until summer , for I can not go to London on purpose , but when my business take I there . only remember my word , Jack , and when you see the man I mean , look straight at he , and tell no lie . he will make some of your zany squire shake in their shoe , I reckon . now , I have be in this lonely hole far long than I intend , by reason of this outrage ; yet I will stay here one day more upon a certain condition . ' 
 ' upon what condition , Uncle Ben ? I grieve that you find it so lonely . we will have Farmer Nicholas up again , and the singer , and ' 
 ' the fashionable milkmaid . I thank you , let I be . the wench be too loud for I . your nanny be enough . nanny be a good child , and she shall come and visit I . ' Uncle Reuben would always call she ' nanny ' ; he say that ' Annie ' be too fine and frenchify for we . ' but my condition be this , Jack that you shall guide I to - morrow , without a word to any one , to a place where I may well descry the dwelling of these scoundrel Doones , and learn the good way to get at they , when the time shall come . can you do this for I ? I will pay you well , boy . ' 
 I promise very readily to do my good to serve he , but , of course , would take no money for it , not be so poor as that come to . accordingly , on the day follow , I manage to set the man at work on the other side of the farm , especially that inquisitive and busybody John Fry , who would pry out almost anything for the pleasure of tell his wife ; and then , with Uncle Reuben mount on my ancient Peggy , I make foot for the westward , directly after breakfast . Uncle Ben refuse to go unless I would take a loaded gun , and indeed it be always wise to do so in those day of turbulence ; and none the less because of late more than usual of our sheep have leave their skin behind they . this , as I need hardly say , be not to be charge to the appetite of the Doones , for they always say that they be not butcher   although upon that subject might well be two opinion ; and their practice be to make the shepherd kill and skin , and quarter for they , and sometimes carry to the Doone - gate the prime among the fatling , for fear of any bruising , which spoil the look at table . but the bad of it be that ignorant folk , unaware of their fastidiousness , score to they the sheep they lose by lower - bear marauder , and so be afraid to speak of it : and the issue of this error be that a farmer , with five or six hundred sheep , could never command , on his wedding - day , a prime saddle of mutton for dinner . 
 to return now to my Uncle Ben and indeed he would not let I go more than three land - yard from he there be very little say between we along the lane and across the hill , although the day be pleasant . I could see that he be half amiss with his mind about the business , and not so full of security as an elderly man should keep himself . therefore , out I spake , and say , 
 ' Uncle Reuben , have no fear . I know every inch of the ground , sir ; and there be no danger nigh we . ' 
 ' fear , boy ! who ever think of fear ? ' tis the last thing would come across I . pretty thing those primrose . ' 
 at once I think of Lorna Doone , the little maid of six year back , and how my fancy go with she . could Lorna ever think of I ? be I not a lout go by , only fit for loach - sticking ? have I ever see a face fit to think of near she ? the sudden flash , the quickness , the bright desire to know one 's heart , and not withhold her own from it , the soft withdrawal of rich eye , the longing to love somebody , anybody , anything , not imbrue with wickedness 
 my uncle interrupt I , mislike so much silence now , with the naked wood fall over we . for we be come to Bagworthy forest , the black and the lonely place of all that keep the sun out . even now , in winter - time , with most of the wood unriddled , and the rest of it pinch brown , it hang around we like a cloak contain little comfort . I keep quite close to Peggy 's head , and Peggy keep quite close to I , and prick her ear at everything . however , we see nothing there , except a few old owl and hawk , and a magpie sit all alone , until we come to the bank of the hill , where the pony could not climb it . Uncle Ben be very loath to get off , because the pony seem company , and he think he could gallop away on she , if the bad come to the bad , but I persuade he that now he must go to the end of it . therefore he make Peggy fast , in a place where we could find she , and speak cheerfully as if there be nothing to be afraid of , he take his staff , and I my gun , to climb the thick ascent . 
 there be now no path of any kind ; which add to our courage all it lessen of our comfort , because it prove that the robber be not in the habit of pass there . and we know that we could not go astray , so long as we breast the hill before we ; inasmuch as it form the rampart , or side - fence of Glen Doone . but in truth I use the right word there for the manner of our ascent , for the ground come forth so steep against we , and withal so woody , that to make any way we must throw ourselves forward , and labour as at a breast - plough . rough and loamy rung of oak - root bulge here and there above our head ; brier need must speak with we , use more of tooth than tongue ; and sometimes bulk of rugged stone , like great sheep , stand across we . at last , though very loath to do it , I be force to leave my gun behind , because I require one hand to drag myself up the difficulty , and one to help Uncle Reuben . and so at last we gain the top , and look forth the edge of the forest , where the ground be very stony and like the crest of a quarry ; and no more tree between we and the brink of cliff below , three hundred yard below it might be , all strong slope and gliddery . and now for the first time I be amazed at the appearance of the Doones 's stronghold , and understand its nature . for when I have be even in the valley , and climb the cliff to escape from it , about seven year agone , I be no more than a stripling boy , note little , as boy do , except for their present purpose , and even that soon do with . but now , what with the fame of the Doones , and my own recollection , and Uncle Ben 's insistence , all my attention be call forth , and the end be simple astonishment . 
 the chine of highland , whereon we stand , curve to the right and leave of we , keep about the same elevation , and crown with tree and brushwood . at about half a mile in front of we , but look as if we could throw a stone to strike any man upon it , another crest just like our own bow around to meet it ; but fail by reason of two narrow cleft of which we could only see the brink . one of these cleft be the Doone - gate , with a portculli of rock above it , and the other be the chasm by which I have once make entrance . betwixt they , where the hill fall back , as in a perfect oval , traverse by the wind water , lie a bright green valley , rim with sheer black rock , and seem to have sink bodily from the bleak rough height above . it look as if no frost could enter neither wind go ruffle ; only spring , and hope , and comfort , breathe to one another . even now the ray of sunshine dwelt and fall back on one another , whenever the cloud lift ; and the pale blue glimpse of the grow day seem to find young encouragement . 
 but for all that , Uncle Reuben be none the bad nor well . he look down into Glen Doone first , and sniff as if he be smell it , like a sample of good from a wholesale house ; and then he look at the hill over yonder , and then he star at I . 
 ' see what a pack of fool they be ? ' 
 ' of course I do , Uncle Ben . " all rogue be fool , " be my first copy , begin of the alphabet . ' 
 ' pack of stuff lad . though true enough , and very good for young people . but see you not how this great Doone valley may be take in half an hour ? ' 
 ' yes , to be sure I do , uncle ; if they like to give it up , I mean . ' 
 ' three culverin on yonder hill , and three on the top of this one , and we have they under a pestle . ah , I have see the war , my lad , from Keinton up to Naseby ; and I might have be a general now , if they have take my advice ' 
 but I be not attend to he , be draw away on a sudden by a sight which never strike the sharp eye of our General . for I have long ago descry that little opening in the cliff through which I make my exit , as before relate , on the other side of the valley . no big than a rabbit - hole it seem from where we stand ; and yet of all the scene before I , that   from my remembrance perhaps   have the most attraction . now gaze at it with full thought of all that it have cost I , I see a little figure come , and pause , and pass into it . something very light and white , nimble , smooth , and elegant , go almost before I know that any one have be there . and yet my heart come to my rib , and all my blood be in my face , and pride within I fight with shame , and vanity with self - contempt ; for though seven year be go , and I from my boyhood come to manhood , and all must have forget I , and I have half - forget ; at that moment , once for all , I feel that I be face to face with fate   however poor it might be , weal or woe , in Lorna Doone . 




 CHAPTER XVI 

 lorna grow FORMIDABLE 

 have reconnoitre thus the position of the enemy , Master Huckaback , on the homeward road , cross - examine I in a manner not at all desirable . for he have note my confusion and eager gaze at something unseen by he in the valley , and thereupon he make up his mind to know everything about it . in this , however , he partly fail ; for although I be no hand at fence , and would not tell he a falsehood , I manage so to hold my peace that he put himself upon the wrong track , and continue thereon with many vaunt of his shrewdness and experience , and some chuckle at my simplicity . thus much however , he learn aright , that I have be in the Doone valley several year before , and might be bring upon strong inducement to venture there again . but as to the mode of my get in , the thing I see , and my thought upon they , he not only fail to learn the truth , but certify himself into an obstinacy of error , from which no after - knowledge be able to deliver he . and this he do , not only because I happen to say very little , but forasmuch as he disbelieve half of the truth I tell he , through his own too great sagacity . 
 upon one point , however , he succeed more easily than he expect , viz . in make I promise to visit the place again , as soon as occasion offer , and to hold my own counsel about it . but I could not help smile at one thing , that accord to his point of view my own counsel mean my own and Master Reuben Huckaback 's . 
 now he be go , as he go next day , to his favourite town of Dulverton , and leave behind he shadowy promise of the mountain he would do for I , my spirit begin to burn and pant for something to go on with ; and nothing show a braver hope of movement and adventure than a lonely visit to Glen Doone , by way of the perilous passage discover in my boyhood . therefore I wait for nothing more than the slow arrival of new small - clothe make by a good tailor at Porlock , for I be wishful to look my good ; and when they be come and approve , I start , regardless of the expense , and forget   like a fool   how badly they would take the water . 
 what with urging of the tailor , and my own misgiving , the time be now come round again to the high - day of St. Valentine , when all our maid be full of lover , and all the lad look foolish . and none of they more sheepish or innocent than I myself , albeit twenty - one year old , and not afraid of man much , but terrify of woman , at least , if they be comely . and what of all thing scar I most be the thought of my own size , and knowledge of my strength , which come like knot upon I daily . in honest truth I tell this thing ,   which often since hath puzzle I , when I come to mix with man more , I be to that degree ashamed of my thickness and my stature , in the presence of a woman , that I would not put a trunk of wood on the fire in the kitchen , but let Annie scold I well , with a smile to follow , and with her own plump hand lift up a little log , and fuel it . many a time I long to be no big than John Fry be ; whom now   when insolent   I take with my left hand by the waist - stuff , and set he on my hat , and give he little chance to tread it ; until he speak of his family , and request to come down again . 
 now take for good oman this , that I be a seven - year Valentine , though much too big for a Cupidon , I choose a seven - foot staff of ash , and fix a loach - fork in it , to look as I have look before ; and leave word upon matter of business , out of the back door I go , and so through the little orchard , and down the brawl Lynn - brook . not be now so much afraid , I strike across the thicket land between the meeting water , and come upon the Bagworthy stream near the great black whirlpool . nothing amaze I so much as to find how shallow the stream now look to I , although the pool be still as black and greedy as it use to be . and still the great rocky slide be dark and difficult to climb ; though the water , which once have take my knee , be satisfied now with my ankle . after some labour , I reach the top ; and halt to look about I well , before trust to broad daylight . 
 the winter   as I say before   have be a very mild one ; and now the spring be toward so that bank and bush be touch with it . the valley into which I gaze be fair with early promise , have shelter from the wind and take all the sunshine . the willow - bush over the stream hang as if they be angle with tasseled float of gold and silver , burst like a bean - pod . between they come the water laughing , like a maid at her own dancing , and spread with that young blue which never live beyond the April . and on either bank , the meadow ruffle as the breeze come by , open   through new tuft , of green   daisy - bud or celandine , or a shy glimpse now and then of the love - lorn primrose . 
 though I be so blank of wit , or perhaps for that same reason , these little thing come and dwell with I , and I be happy about they , and long for nothing well . I feel with every blade of grass , as if it have a history ; and make a child of every bud as though it know and love I . and be so , they seem to tell I of my own delusion , how I be no more than they , except in self - importance . 
 while I be forget much of many thing that harm one , and letting of my thought go wild to sound and sight of nature , a sweet note than thrush or ouzel ever woo a mate in , float on the valley breeze at the quiet turn of sundown . the word be of an ancient song , fit to laugh or cry at . 
 Love , an if there be one , come my love to be , my love be for the one love unto I . 
 not for I the show , love , of a gild bliss ; only thou must know , love , what my value be . 
 if in all the earth , love , Thou hast none but I , this shall be my worth , love : to be cheap to thee . 
 but , if so thou ever Strivest to be free , ' twill be my endeavour to be dear to thee . 
 so shall I have plea , love , be thy heart andbreath cling still to thee , love , in the doom of death . 
 all this I take in with great eagerness , not for the sake of the meaning   which be no doubt an allegory , but for the power and richness , and softness of the singing , which seem to I well than we ever have even in Oare church . but all the time I keep myself in a black niche of the rock , where the fall of the water begin , lest the sweet singer   espy I   should be alarm , and flee away . but presently I venture to look forth where a bush be ; and then I behold the lovely sight one glimpse of which be enough to make I kneel in the cold water . 
 by the side of the stream she be come to I , even among the primrose , as if she love they all ; and every flower look the brighter , as her eye be on they , I could not see what her face be , my heart so awake and tremble ; only that her hair be flow from a wreath of white violet , and the grace of her come be like the appearance of the first wind - flower . the pale gleam over the western cliff throw a shadow of light behind she , as if the sun be linger . never do I see that light from the closing of the west , even in these my aged day , without think of she . ah I , if it come to that , what do I see of earth or heaven , without think of she ? 
 the tremulous thrill of her song be hang on her open lip ; and she glance around , as if the bird be accustom to make answer . to I it be a thing of terror to behold such beauty , and feel myself the while to be so very low and common . but scarcely know what I do , as if a rope be draw I , I come from the dark mouth of the chasm ; and stand , afraid to look at she . 
 she be turn to fly , not know I , and frighten , perhaps , at my stature , when I fall on the grass   as I fall before her seven year agone that day , and I just say , ' Lorna Doone ! ' 
 she know I at once , from my manner and way , and a smile break through her trembling , as sunshine come through aspen - leaf ; and be so clever , she see , of course , that she need not to fear I . 
 ' oh , indeed , ' she cry , with a feint of anger   because she have show she cowardice , and yet in her heart she be laugh ; ' oh , if you please , who be you , sir , and how do you know my name ? ' 
 ' I be John Ridd , ' I answer ; ' the boy who give you those beautiful fish , when you be only a little thing , seven year ago to - day . ' 
 ' yes , the poor boy who be frightened so , and oblige to hide here in the water . ' 
 ' and do you remember how kind you be , and save my life by your quickness , and go away rid upon a great man 's shoulder , as if you have never see I , and yet look back through the willow - tree ? ' 
 ' oh , yes , I remember everything ; because it be so rare to see any except I mean because I happen to remember . but you seem not to remember , sir , how perilous this place be . ' 
 for she have keep her eye upon I ; large eye of a softness , a brightness , and a dignity which make I feel as if I must for ever love and yet for ever know myself unworthy . unless themselves should fill with love , which be the spring of all thing . and so I could not answer she , but be overcome with thinking and feeling and confusion . neither could I look again ; only wait for the melody which make every word like a poem to I , the melody of her voice . but she have not the least idea of what be go on with I , any more than I myself have . 
 ' I think , Master Ridd , you can not know , ' she say , with her eye take from I , ' what the danger of this place be , and the nature of the people . ' 
 ' yes , I know enough of that ; and I be frighten greatly , all the time , when I do not look at you . ' 
 she be too young to answer I in the style some maiden would have use ; the manner , I mean , which now we call from a foreign word ' coquettish . ' and more than that , she be tremble from real fear of violence , lest strong hand might be lay on I , and a miserable end of it . and to tell the truth , I grow afraid ; perhaps from a kind of sympathy , and because I know that evil come more readily than good to we . 
 therefore , without more ado , or take any advantage although I would have be glad at heart , if need have be , to kiss she   without any thought of rudeness   it strike I that I have well go , and have no more to say to she until next time of come . so would she look the more for I and think the more about I , and not grow weary of my word and the want of change there be in I . for , of course , I know what a churl I be compare to her birth and appearance ; but meanwhile I might improve myself and learn a musical instrument . ' the wind hath a draw after fly straw ' be a saying we have in Devonshire , make , peradventure , by somebody who have see the way of woman . 
 ' Mistress Lorna , I will depart ' mark you , I think that a powerful word ' in fear of cause disquiet . if any rogue shoot I it would grieve you ; I make bold to say it , and it would be the death of mother . few mother have such a son as I . try to think of I now and then , and I will bring you some new - lay egg , for our young blue hen be begin . ' 
 ' I thank you heartily , ' say Lorna ; ' but you need not come to see I . you can put they in my little bower , where I be almost always I mean whither daily I repair to read and to be away from they . ' 
 ' only show I where it be . Thrice a day I will come and stop ' 
 ' Nay , Master Ridd , I would never show thee never , because of peril only that so happen it thou hast find the way already . ' 
 and she smile with a light that make I care to cry out for no other way , except to her dear heart . but only to myself I cry for anything at all , have enough of man in I to be bashful with young maiden . so I touch her white hand softly when she give it to I , and   fancy that she have sigh   be touch at heart about it , and resolve to yield she all my good , although my mother be live ; and then grow angry with myself   for a mile or more of walk   to think she would condescend so ; and then , for the rest of the homeward road , be mad with every man in the world who would dare to think of have she . 




 CHAPTER XVII 

 JOHN be CLEARLY bewitch 

 to forget one 's luck of life , to forget the cark of care and wither of young finger ; not to feel , or not be move by , all the change of thought and heart , from large young heat to the sinewy line and dry bone of old age this be what I have to do ere ever I can make you know   even as a dream be know   how I love my Lorna . I myself can never know ; never can conceive , or treat it as a thing of reason , never can behold myself dwell in the midst of it , and think that this be I ; neither can I wander far from perpetual thought of it . perhaps I have two farrow of pig ready for the chapman ; perhaps I have ten stone of wool wait for the factor . it be all the same . I look at both , and what I say to myself be this : ' which would Lorna choose of they ? ' of course , I be a fool for this ; any man may call I so , and I will not quarrel with he , unless he guess my secret . of course , I fetch my wit , if it be worth the fetching , back again to business . but there my heart be and must be ; and all who like to try can cheat I , except upon parish matter . 
 that week I could do little more than dream and dream and rove about , seek by perpetual change to find the way back to myself . I care not for the people round I , neither take delight in victual ; but make believe to eat and drink and blush at any question . and be call the master now , head - farmer , and chief yeoman , it irk I much that any one should take advantage of I ; yet everybody do so as soon as ever it be know that my wit be go moon - rake . for that be the way they look at it , not be able to comprehend the greatness and the loftiness . neither do I blame they much ; for the wise thing be to laugh at people when we can not understand they . I , for my part , take no notice ; but in my heart despise they as being of a less nature , who never have see Lorna . yet I be vex , and rub myself , when John Fry spread all over the farm , and even at the shoeing forge , that a mad dog have come and bitten I , from the other side of Mallond . 
 this seem little to I now ; and so it might to any one ; but , at the time , it work I up to a fever of indignity . to make a mad dog of Lorna , to compare all my imagining   which be strange , I do assure you the faculty not be apt to work , to count the raising of my soul no more than hydrophobia ! all this act on I so , that I give John Fry the sound thresh that ever a sheaf of good corn deserve , or a bundle of tare be bless with . afterwards he go home , too tired to tell his wife the meaning of it ; but it prove of service to both of they , and an example for their child . 
 now the climate of this country be so far as I can make of it to throw no man into extreme ; and if he throw himself so far , to pluck he back by change of weather and the need of look after thing . lest we should be like the Southerns , for whom the sky do everything , and man sit under a wall and watch both food and fruit come beckon . their sky be a mother to they ; but our a good stepmother to we fear to hurt by indulgence , and know that severity and change of mood be wholesome . 
 the spring be now too forward , a check to it be needful ; and in the early part of March there come a change of weather . all the young growth be arrest by a dry wind from the east , which make both face and finger burn when a man be do ditching . the lilac and the woodbine , just crowd forth in little tuft , close kernel their blossom , be ruffle back , like a sleeve turn up , and nick with brown at the corner . in the hedge any man , unless his eye be very dull , could see the mischief do . the russet of the young elm - bloom be fain to be in its scale again ; but have push forth , there must be , and turn to a tawny colour . the hanger of the hazel , too , have shed their dust to make the nut , do not spread their little comb and dry they , as they ought to do ; but shrivel at the base and fall , as if a knife have cut they . and more than all to notice be   at least about the hedge   the shuddering of everything and the shiver sound among they toward the feeble sun ; such as we make to a poor fireplace when several door be open . sometimes I put my face to warm against the soft , rough maple - stem , which feel like the foot of a red deer ; but the pitiless east wind come through all , and take and shake the caved hedge aback till its knee be knock together , and nothing could be shelter . then would any one have blood , and try to keep at home with it , run to a sturdy tree and hope to eat his food behind it , and look for a little sun to come and warm his foot in the shelter . and if it do he might strike his breast , and try to think he be warmer . 
 but when a man come home at night , after long day 's labour , know that the day increase , and so his care should multiply ; still he find enough of light to show he what the day have do against he in his garden . every ridge of new - turn earth look like an old man 's muscle , honeycomb , and stand out void of spring , and powdery . every plant that have rejoice in pass such a winter now be cower , turn away , unfit to meet the consequence . flow sap have stop its course ; fluted line show want of food , and if you pinch the topmost spray , there be no rebound or firmness . 
 we think a good deal , in a quiet way , when people ask we about they of some fine , upstande pear - tree , graft by my grandfather , who have be very greatly respect . and he get those graft by shelter a poor italian soldier , in the time of James the First , a man who never could do enough to show his grateful memory . how he come to our place be a very difficult story , which I never understand rightly , have hear it from my mother . at any rate , there the pear - tree be , and there they be to this very day ; and I wish every one could taste their fruit , old as they be , and rugge . 
 now these fine tree have take advantage of the west wind , and the moisture , and the promise of the spring time , so as to fill the tip of the spray - wood and the rowel all up the branch with a crowd of eager blossom . not that they be yet in bloom , nor even show whiteness , only that some of the cone be open at the side of the cap which pinch they ; and there you might count perhaps , a dozen nob , like very little button , but groove , and line , and huddle close , to make room for one another . and among these bud be gray - green blade , scarce big than a hair almost , yet curve so as if their purpose be to shield the blossom . 
 other of the spur - point , stand on the old wood where the sap be not so eager , have not burst their tunic yet , but be flay and flake with light , cast off the husk of brown in three - cornered patch , as I have see a Scotchman 's plaid , or as his leg show through it . these bud , at a distance , look as if the sky have be rain cream upon they . 
 now all this fair delight to the eye , and good promise to the palate , be mar and baffle by the wind and cutting of the night - frost . the opening cone be strike with brown , in between the button bud , and on the scape that shield they ; while the foot part of the cover hang like rag , peel back , and quivering . and there the little stalk of each , which might have be a pear , God willing , have a ring around its base , and seek a chance to drop and die . the other which have not open comb , but only prepare to do it , be a little well off , but still very brown and unkid , and shrivel in doubt of health , and neither peart nor lusty . 
 now this I have not tell because I know the way to do it , for that I do not , neither yet have see a man who do know . it be wonderful how we look at thing , and never think to notice they ; and I be as bad as anybody , unless the thing to be observe be a dog , or a horse , or a maiden . and the last of those three I look at , somehow , without know that I take notice , and greatly afraid to do it , only I know afterwards   when the time of life be in I , not indeed , what the maiden be like , but how she differ from other . 
 yet I have speak about the spring , and the failure of fair promise , because I take it to my heart as token of what would come to I in the budding of my year and hope . and even then , be much possess , and full of a foolish melancholy , I feel a sad delight at be doom to blight and loneliness ; not but that I manage still   when mother be urgent upon I   to eat my share of victual , and cuff a man for laziness , and see that a ploughshare make no leap , and sleep of a night without dream . and my mother half - believe , in her fondness and affection , that what the parish say be true about a mad dog have bite I , and yet argue that it must be false   because God would have prevent he , my mother give I little rest , when I be in the room with she . not that she worry I with question , nor openly regard I with any unusual meaning , but that I know she be watch slyly whenever I take a spoon up ; and every hour or so she manage to place a pan of water by I , quite as if by accident , and sometimes even to spill a little upon my shoe or coat - sleeve . but Betty Muxworthy be bad ; for , have no fear about my health , she make a villainous joke of it , and use to rush into the kitchen , bark like a dog , and pant , exclaim that I have bite she , and justice she would have on I , if it cost she a twelvemonth 's wage . and she always take care to do this thing just when I have cross my leg in the corner after supper , and lean my head against the oven , to begin to think of Lorna . 
 however , in all thing there be comfort , if we do not look too hard for it ; and now I have much satisfaction , in my uncouth state , from labour , by the hour together , at the hedging and the ditching , meet the bitter wind face to face , feel my strength increase , and hop that some one would be proud of it . in the rustling rush of every gust , in the graceful bend of every tree , even in the ' lord and lady , ' clump in the scoop of the hedgerow , and most of all in the soft primrose , wring by the wind , but steal back , and smile when the wrath be pass in all of these , and many other there be ache ecstasy , delicious pang of Lorna . 
 but however cold the weather be , and however hard the wind blow , one thing   more than all the rest   worried and perplex I . this be , that I could not settle , turn and twist as I might , how soon I ought to go again upon a visit to Glen Doone . for I like not at all the falseness of it   albeit against murderer , the creeping out of sight , and hiding , and feel as a spy might . and even more than this . I fear how Lorna might regard it ; whether I might seem to she a prone and blunt intruder , a country youth not skilled in manner , as among the quality , even when they rob we . for I be not sure myself , but that it might be very bad manner to go again too early without an invitation ; and my hand and face be chap so badly by the bitter wind , that Lorna might count they unsightly thing , and wish to see no more of they . 
 however , I could not bring myself to consult any one upon this point , at least in our own neighbourhood , nor even to speak of it near home . but the east wind hold through the month , my hand and face grow bad and bad , and it have occur to I by this time that possibly Lorna might have chap , if she come abroad at all , and so might like to talk about they and show her little hand to I , I resolve to take another opinion , so far as might be upon this matter , without disclose the circumstance . 
 now the wise person in all our part be reckon to be a certain wise woman , well know all over Exmoor by the name of Mother Melldrum . her real name be Maple Durham , as I learn long afterwards ; and she come of an ancient family , but neither of Devon nor Somerset . nevertheless she be quite at home with our proper mode of divination ; and know that we like they well as each man do his own religion she would always practise they for the people of the country . and all the while , she would let we know that she keep a high and nobler mode for those who look down upon this one , not have be breed and bear to it . 
 Mother Melldrum have two house , or rather she have none at all , but two home wherein to find she , accord to the time of year . in summer she live in a pleasant cave , face the cool side of the hill , far inland near Hawkridge and close above Tarr - step , a wonderful crossing of Barle river , make   as everybody know   by Satan , for a wager . but throughout the winter , she find sea - air agreeable , and a place where thing could be have on credit , and more occasion of talk . not but what she could have credit   for every one be afraid of she   in the neighbourhood of Tarr - step ; only there be no one handy own thing worth take . 
 therefore , at the fall of the leaf , when the wood grow damp and irksome , the wise woman always set her face to the warm cliff of the Channel ; where shelter be , and dry fern bedding , and folk to be see in the distance , from a bank upon which the sun shine . and there , as I know from our John Fry   who have be to she about rheumatism , and sheep possess with an evil spirit , and wart on the hand of his son , young John , any one who choose might find she , towards the close of a winter day , gather stick and brown fern for fuel , and talk to herself the while , in a hollow stretch behind the cliff ; which foreigner , who come and go without see much of Exmoor , have call the Valley of Rocks . 
 this valley , or goyal , as we term it , be small for a valley , lie to the west of Linton , about a mile from the town perhaps , and away towards Ley Manor . our homefolk always call it the Danes , or the Denes , which be no more , they tell I , than a hollow place , even as the word ' den ' be . however , let that pass , for I know very little about it ; but the place itself be a pretty one , though nothing to frighten anybody , unless he hath live in a gallipot . it be a green rough - sided hollow , bend at the middle , touch with stone at either crest , and dot here and there with slab in and out the bramble . on the right hand be an upward crag , call by some the Castle , easy enough to scale , and give great view of the Channel . face this , from the inland side and the elbow of the valley , a queer old pile of rock arise , bold behind one another , and quite enough to affright a man , if it only be ten time large . this be call the Devil 's cheese - ring , or the Devil 's cheese - knife , which mean the same thing , as our father be use to eat their cheese from a scoop ; and perhaps in old time the upmost rock   which have fall away since I know it   be like to such an implement , if Satan eat cheese untoaste . 
 but all the middle of this valley be a place to rest in ; to sit and think that trouble be not , if we would not make they . to know the sea outside the hill , but never to behold it ; only by the sound of wave to pity sailor labour . then to watch the sheltered sun , come warmly round the turn , like a guest expect , full of gentle glow and gladness , cast shadow far away as a thing to hug itself , and awaken life from dew , and hope from every spread bud . and then to fall asleep and dream that the fern be all asparagus . 
 alas , I be too young in those day much to care for creature comfort , or to let pure palate have thing that would improve it . anything go down with I , as it do with most of we . too late we know the good from bad ; the knowledge be no pleasure then ; be memory 's medicine rather than the wine of hope . 
 now Mother Melldrum keep she winter in this vale of rock , shelter from the wind and rain within the Devil 's Cheese - ring , which add greatly to her fame because all else , for mile around , be afraid to go near it after dark , or even on a gloomy day . under eave of lichene rock she have a winding passage , which none that ever I know of durst enter but herself . and to this place I go to seek she , in spite of all misgiving , upon a Sunday in Lenten season , when the sheep be fold . 
 our parson   as if he have know my intent   have preach a beautiful sermon about the Witch of Endor , and the peril of they that meddle wantonly with the unseen Powers ; and therein he refer especially to the strange noise in the neighbourhood , and upbraid we for want of faith , and many other backsliding . we listen to he very earnestly , for we like to hear from our better about thing that be beyond we , and to be rouse up now and then , like sheep with a good dog after they , who can pull some wool without bite . nevertheless we could not see how our want of faith could have make that noise , especially at night time , notwithstanding which we believe it , and hop to do a little well . 
 and so we all come home from church ; and most of the people din with we , as they always do on Sundays , because of the distance to go home , with only word inside they . the parson , who always sit next to mother , be afraid that he might have vex we , and would not have the good piece of meat , accord to his custom . but soon we put he at his ease , and show he we be proud of he ; and then he make no more to do , but accept the good of the sirloin . 




 CHAPTER xviii 

 WITCHERY lead to WITCHCRAFT 

 although wellnigh the end of March , the wind blow wild and pierce , as I go on foot that afternoon to Mother Melldrum 's dwelling . it be safe not to take a horse , lest   if anything vex she   she should put a spell upon he ; as have be do to Farmer Snowe 's stable by the wise woman of Simonsbath . 
 the sun be low on the edge of the hill by the time I enter the valley , for I could not leave home till the cattle be tend , and the distance be seven mile or more . the shadow of rock fall far and deep , and the brown dead fern be flutter , and bramble with their sere leaf hang , sway their tatter to and fro , with a red look on they . in patch underneath the crag , a few wild goat be browse ; then they toss their horn , and flee , and leap on ledge , and star at I . moreover , the sound of the sea come up , and go the length of the valley , and there it lap on a butt of rock , and murmur like a shell . 
 take thing one with another , and feel all the lonesomeness , and have no stick with I , I be much inclined to go briskly back , and come at a well season . and when I behold a tall grey shape , of something or another , move at the low end of the valley , where the shade be , it give I such a stroke of fear , after many other , that my thumb which lie in mother 's Bible   bring in my big pocket for the sake of safety   shake so much that it come out , and I could not get it in again . ' this serve I right , ' I say to myself , ' for tamper with Beelzebub . oh that I have listen to parson ! ' 
 and thereupon I strike aside ; not like to run away quite , as some people might call it ; but seek to look like a wanderer who be come to see the valley , and have see almost enough of it . Herein I should have succeed , and go home , and then be angry at my want of courage , but that on the very turn and bending of my footstep , the woman in the distance lift up her staff to I , so that I be bind to stop . 
 and now , be bring face to face , by the will of God   as one might say   with anything that might come of it , I keep myself quite straight and stiff , and thrust away all white feather , trust in my Bible still , hop that it would protect I , though I have disobey it . but upon that remembrance , my conscience take I by the leg , so that I could not go forward . 
 all this while , the fearful woman be come near and more near to I ; and I be glad to sit down on a rock because my knee be shake so . I try to think of many thing , but none of they would come to I ; and I could not take my eye away , though I pray God to be near I . 
 but when she be come so nigh to I that I could descry her feature , there be something in her countenance that make I not dislike she . she look as if she have be visit by many trouble , and have feel they one by one , yet hold enough of kindly nature still to grieve for other . long white hair , on either side , be fall down below her chin ; and through her wrinkle clear bright eye seem to spread themselves upon I . though I have plenty of time to think , I be take by surprise no less , and unable to say anything ; yet eager to hear the silence break , and long for a noise or two . 
 ' thou art not come to I , ' she say , look through my simple face , as if it be but glass , ' to be strike for bone - shave , nor to be bless for barn - gun . give I forth thy hand , John Ridd ; and tell why thou art come to I . ' 
 but I be so much amazed at she know my name and all about I , that I fear to place my hand in her power , or even my tongue by speak . 
 ' have no fear of I , my son ; I have no gift to harm thee ; and if I have , it should be idle . now , if thou hast any wit , tell I why I love thee . ' 
 ' I never have any wit , mother , ' I answer in our Devonshire way ; ' and never set eye on thee before , to the furth of my knowledge . ' 
 ' and yet I know thee as well , John , as if thou wert my grandson . remember you the old Oare oak , and the bog at the head of Exe , and the child who would have die there , but for thy strength and courage , and most of all thy kindness ? that be my granddaughter , John ; and all I have on earth to love . ' 
 now that she come to speak of it , with the place and that , so clearly , I remember all about it   a thing that happen last August , and think how stupid I must have be not to learn more of the little girl who have fall into the black pit , with a basketful of whortleberry , and who might have be gulfe if her little dog have not spy I in the distance . I carry she on my back to mother ; and then we dress she all anew , and take she where she order we ; but she do not tell we who she be , nor anything more than her christian name , and that she be eight year old , and fond of fried batatas . and we do not seek to ask she more ; as our manner be with visitor . 
 but think of this little story , and see how she look at I , I lose my fear of Mother Melldrum , and begin to like she ; partly because I have help she grandchild , and partly that if she be so wise , no need would have be for I to save the little thing from drown . therefore I stand up and say , though scarcely yet establish in my power against her , 
 ' good mother , the shoe she lose be in the mire , and not with we . and we could not match it , although we give she a pair of sister Lizzie 's . ' 
 ' my son , what care I for her shoe ? how simple thou art , and foolish ! accord to the thought of some . now tell I , for thou canst not lie , what have bring thee to I . ' 
 be so ashamed and bashful , I be half - inclined to tell she a lie , until she say that I could not do it ; and then I know that I could not . 
 ' I be come to know , ' I say , look at a rock the while , to keep my voice from shake , ' when I may go to see Lorna Doone . ' 
 no more could I say , though my mind be charge to ask fifty other question . but although I look away , it be plain that I have ask enough . I feel that the wise woman gaze at I in wrath as well as sorrow ; and then I grow angry that any one should seem to make light of Lorna . 
 ' John Ridd , ' say the woman , observe this   for now I face she bravely , ' of whom art thou speak ? be it a child of the man who slay your father ? ' 
 ' I can not tell , mother . how should I know ? and what be that to thee ? ' 
 ' it be something to thy mother , John , and something to thyself , I trow ; and nothing bad could befall thee . ' 
 I wait for she to speak again , because she have speak so sadly that it take my breath away . 
 ' John Ridd , if thou hast any value for thy body or thy soul , thy mother , or thy father 's name , have nought to do with any Doone . ' 
 she gaze at I in earnest so , and raise her voice in say it , until the whole valley , curve like a great bell echo ' Doone , ' that it seem to I my heart be go for every one and everything . if it be God 's will for I to have no more of Lorna , let a sign come out of the rock , and I would try to believe it . but no sign come , and I turn to the woman , and long that she have be a man . 
 ' you poor thing , with bone and blade , pail of water , and door - key , what know you about the destiny of a maiden such as Lorna ? chilblain you may treat , and bone - shave , ringworm , and the scalding ; even scabby sheep may limp the well for your striking . John the Baptist and his cousin , with the wool and hyssop , be for mare , and ail dog , and fowl that have the jaundice . look at I now , Mother Melldrum , be I like a fool ? ' 
 ' that thou art , my son . alas that it be any other ! now behold the end of that ; John Ridd , mark the end of it . ' 
 she point to the castle - rock , where upon a narrow shelf , betwixt we and the come star , a bitter fight be rag . a fine fat sheep , with an honest face , have clomb up very carefully to browse on a bit of juicy grass , now the dew of the land be upon it . to he , from an upper crag , a lean black goat come hurry , with leap , and skirmish of the horn , and an angry noise in his nostril . the goat have graze the place before , to the utmost of his liking , crop in and out with jerk , as their manner be of feed . nevertheless he fall on the sheep with fury and great malice . 
 the simple wether be much inclined to retire from the contest , but look around in vain for any way to peace and comfort . his enemy stand between he and the last leap he have take ; there be nothing leave he but to fight , or be hurl into the sea , five hundred foot below . 
 ' lie down , lie down ! ' I shout to he , as if he be a dog , for I have see a battle like this before , and know that the sheep have no chance of life except from his great weight , and the difficulty of move he . 
 ' lie down , lie down , John Ridd ! ' cry Mother Melldrum , mock I , but without a sign of smile . 
 the poor sheep turn , upon my voice , and look at I so piteously that I could look no long ; but run with all my speed to try and save he from the combat . he see that I could not be in time , for the goat be buck to leap at he , and so the good wether stoop his forehead , with the harmless horn curl aside of it ; and the goat fling his heel up , and rush at he , with quick sharp jump and trick of movement , and the point of his long horn always foremost , and his little scut cock like a gun - hammer . 
 as I run up the steep of the rock , I could not see what they be do , but the sheep must have fight very bravely at last , and yield his ground quite slowly , and I hop almost to save he . but just as my head top the platform of rock , I see he fling from it backward , with a sad low moan and a gurgle . his body make quite a short noise in the air , like a bucket throw down a well shaft , and I could not tell when it strike the water , except by the echo among the rock . so wroth be I with the goat at the moment   be somewhat scant of breath and unable to consider , that I catch he by the right hind - leg , before he could turn from his victory , and hurl he after the sheep , to learn how he like his own compulsion . 




 CHAPTER XIX 

 another DANGEROUS INTERVIEW 

 although I leave the Denes at once , have little heart for further question of the wise woman , and be afraid to visit her house under the Devil 's cheese - ring   to which she kindly invite I , and although I run most part of the way , it be very late for farm - house time upon a Sunday evening before I be back at Plover 's barrow . my mother have great desire to know all about the matter ; but I could not reconcile it with my respect so to frighten she . therefore I try to sleep it off , keep my own counsel ; and when that prove of no avail , I strove to work it away , it might be , by heavy outdoor labour , and weariness , and good feeding . these indeed have some effect , and help to pass a week or two , with more pain of hand than heart to I . 
 but when the weather change in earnest , and the frost be go , and the south - west wind blow softly , and the lamb be at play with the daisy , it be more than I could do to keep from thought of Lorna . for now the field be spread with growth , and the water clothe with sunshine , and light and shadow , step by step , wander over the furzy clef . all the side of the hilly wood be gather in and out with green , silver - grey , or russet point , accord to the several manner of the tree begin . and if one stand beneath an elm , with any heart to look at it , lo ! all the ground be strew with flake   too small to know their meaning , and all the spray above be rasp and tremble with a redness . and so I stop beneath the tree , and carve L.D. upon it , and wonder at the bud of thought that seem to swell inside I . 
 the upshot of it all be this , that as no Lorna come to I , except in dream or fancy , and as my life be not worth live without constant sign of she , forth I must again to find she , and say more than a man can tell . therefore , without wait long for the moving of the spring , dress I be in grand attire   so far as I have get it , and think my appearance good , although with doubt about it   be force to dress in the hay - tallat , round the corner of the wood - stack go I very knowingly for Lizzie 's eye be wondrous sharp and then I be sure of meet none who would care or dare to speak of I . 
 it lie upon my conscience often that I have not make dear Annie secret to this history ; although in all thing I could trust she , and she love I like a lamb . many and many a time I try , and more than once begin the thing ; but there come a dryness in my throat , and a knock under the roof of my mouth , and a longing to put it off again , as perhaps might be the wise . and then I would remember too that I have no right to speak of Lorna as if she be common property . 
 this time I long to take my gun , and be half resolve to do so ; because it seem so hard a thing to be shoot at and have no chance of shooting ; but when I come to remember the steepness and the slippery nature of the waterslide , there seem but little likelihood of keep dry the powder . therefore I be arm with nothing but a good stout holly staff , season well for many a winter in our back - kitchen chimney . 
 although my heart be leap high with the prospect of some adventure , and the fear of meet Lorna , I could not but be gladden by the softness of the weather , and the welcome way of everything . there be that power all round , that power and that goodness , which make we come , as it be , outside our bodily self , to share they . over and beside we breathe the joy of hope and promise ; under foot be trouble past ; in the distance bower newness tempt we ever forward . we quicken with largesse of life , and spring with vivid mystery . 
 and , in good sooth , I have to spring , and no mystery about it , ere ever I get to the top of the rift lead into Doone - glade . for the stream be rush down in strength , and rave at every corner ; a mort of rain have fall last night and no wind come to wipe it . however , I reach the head ere dark with more difficulty than danger , and sit in a place which comfort my back and leg desirably . 
 Hereupon I grow so happy at be on dry land again , and come to look for Lorna , with pretty tree around I , that what do I do but fall asleep with the holly - stick in front of I , and my good coat sink in a bed of moss , with water and wood - sorrel . Mayhap I have not do so , nor yet enjoy the spring so much , if so be I have not take three part of a gallon of cider at home , at Plover 's barrow , because of the lowness and sink ever since I meet Mother Melldrum . 
 there be a little runnel go softly down beside I , fall from the upper rock by the mean of moss and grass , as if it fear to make a noise , and have a mother sleep . now and then it seem to stop , in fear of its own dropping , and wait for some order ; and the blade of grass that straighten to it turn their point a little way , and offer their allegiance to wind instead of water . yet before their carkle edge bent more than a drive saw , down the water come again with heavy drop and pat of run , and bright anger at neglect . 
 this be very pleasant to I , now and then , to gaze at , blink as the water blink , and fall back to sleep again . suddenly my sleep be break by a shade cast over I ; between I and the low sunlight Lorna Doone be stand . 
 ' Master Ridd , be you mad ? ' she say , and take my hand to move I . 
 ' not mad , but half asleep , ' I answer , feign not to notice she , that so she might keep hold of I . 
 ' come away , come away , if you care for life . the patrol will be here directly . be quick , Master Ridd , let I hide thee . ' 
 ' I will not stir a step , ' say I , though be in the great fright that might be well imagine , ' unless you call I " John . " ' 
 ' well , John , then Master John Ridd , be quick , if you have any to care for you . ' 
 ' I have many that care for I , ' I say , just to let she know ; ' and I will follow you , Mistress Lorna , albeit without any hurry , unless there be peril to more than I . ' 
 without another word she lead I , though with many timid glance towards the upper valley , to , and into , her little bower , where the inlet through the rock be . I be almost sure that I speak before   though I can not now go seek for it , and my memory be but a wear - out tub   of a certain deep and perilous pit , in which I be like to drown myself through hurry and fright of boyhood . and even then I wonder greatly , and be vex with Lorna for send I in that heedless manner into such an entrance . but now it be clear that she have be right and the fault mine own entirely ; for the entrance to the pit be only to be find by seek it . inside the niche of native stone , the plain thing of all to see , at any rate by day light , be the stairway hew from rock , and lead up the mountain , by mean of which I have escape , as before related . to the right side of this be the mouth of the pit , still look very formidable ; though Lorna laugh at my fear of it , for she draw her water thence . but on the left be a narrow crevice , very difficult to espy , and have a sweep of grey ivy lay , like a slouching beaver , over it . a man here come from the brightness of the outer air , with eye daze by the twilight , would never think of see this and follow it to its meaning . 
 Lorna raise the screen for I , but I have much ado to pass , on account of bulk and stature . instead of be proud of my size   as it seem to I she ought to be   Lorna laugh so quietly that I be ready to knock my head or elbow against anything , and say no more about it . however , I get through at last without a word of compliment , and break into the pleasant room , the lone retreat of Lorna . 
 the chamber be of unhewn rock , round , as near as might be , eighteen or twenty foot across , and gay with rich variety of fern and moss and lichen . the fern be in its winter still , or coil for the spring - tide ; but moss be in abundant life , some feathering , and some goblete , and some with fringe of red to it . overhead there be no ceiling but the sky itself , flake with little cloud of April whitely wander over it . the floor be make of soft low grass , mix with moss and primrose ; and in a niche of shelter move the delicate wood - sorrel . here and there , around the side , be ' chair of live stone , ' as some latin writer say , whose name have quite escape I ; and in the midst a tiny spring arise , with crystal bead in it , and a soft voice as of a laugh dream , and dimple like a sleeping babe . then , after go round a little , with surprise of daylight , the water overwelle the edge , and softly go through line of light to shadow and an untold bourne . 
 while I be gaze at all these thing with wonder and some sadness , Lorna turn upon I lightly   as her manner be   and say , 
 ' where be the new - lay egg , Master Ridd ? or hath blue hen cease lay ? ' 
 I do not altogether like the way in which she say it with a sort of dialect , as if my speech could be laugh at . 
 ' here be some , ' I answer , speak as if in spite of she . ' I would have bring thee twice as many , but that I fear to crush they in the narrow way , Mistress Lorna . ' 
 and so I lay she out two dozen upon the moss of the rock - ledge , unwind the wisp of hay from each as it come safe out of my pocket . Lorna look with grow wonder , as I add one to one ; and when I have place they side by side , and bid she now to tell they , to my amazement what do she do but burst into a flood of tear . 
 ' what have I do ? ' I ask , with shame , scarce dare even to look at she , because her grief be not like Annie 's a thing that could be coax away , and leave a joy in go ' oh , what have I do to vex you so ? ' 
 ' it be nothing do by you , Master Ridd , ' she answer , very proudly , as if nought I do could matter ; ' it be only something that come upon I with the scent of the pure true clover - hay . moreover , you have be too kind ; and I be not use to kindness . ' 
 some sort of awkwardness be on I , at her word and weeping , as if I would like to say something , but fear to make thing bad perhaps than they be already . therefore I abstain from speech , as I would in my own pain . and as it happen , this be the way to make she tell I more about it . not that I be curious , beyond what pity urge I and the strange affair around she ; and now I gaze upon the floor , lest I should seem to watch she ; but none the less for that I know all that she be do . 
 Lorna go a little way , as if she would not think of I nor care for one so careless ; and all my heart give a sudden jump , to go like a mad thing after she ; until she turn of her own accord , and with a little sigh come back to I . her eye be soft with trouble 's shadow , and the proud lift of her neck be go , and beauty 's vanity bear down by woman 's want of sustenance . 
 ' Master Ridd , ' she say in the soft voice that ever flow between two lip , ' have I do aught to offend you ? ' 
 Hereupon it go hard with I , not to catch she up and kiss she , in the manner in which she be look ; only it smite I suddenly that this would be a low advantage of her trust and helplessness . she seem to know what I would be at , and to doubt very greatly about it , whether as a child of old she might permit the usage . all sort of thing go through my head , as I make myself look away from she , for fear of be tempt beyond what I could bear . and the upshot of it be that I say , within my heart and through it , ' John Ridd , be on thy very good manner with this lonely maiden . ' 
 Lorna like I all the well for my good forbearance ; because she do not love I yet , and have not think about it ; at least so far as I know . and though her eye be so beauteous , so very soft and kindly , there be   to my apprehension   some great power in they , as if she would not have a thing , unless her judgment leap with it . 
 but now her judgment leap with I , because I have behave so well ; and be of quick urgent nature such as I delight in , for the change from mine own slowness she , without any let or hindrance , sit over against I , now raise and now drop fringe over those sweet eye that be the road - light of her tongue , Lorna tell I all about everything I wish to know , every little thing she know , except indeed that point of point , how Master Ridd stand with she . 
 although it weary I no whit , it might be wearisome for folk who can not look at Lorna , to hear the story all in speech , exactly as she tell it ; therefore let I put it shortly , to the good of my remembrance . 
 Nay , pardon I , whosoever thou art , for seem fickle and rude to thee ; I have try to do as first propose , to tell the tale in my own word , as of another 's fortune . but , lo ! I be beset at once with many heavy obstacle , which grow as I go onward , until I know not where I be , and mingle past and present . and two of these difficulty only be enough to stop I ; the one that I must coldly speak without the force of pity , the other that I , off and on , confuse myself with Lorna , as might be well expect . 
 therefore let she tell the story , with her own sweet voice and manner ; and if ye find it wearisome , seek in yourself the weariness . 




 CHAPTER XX 

 LORNA begin she STORY 

 ' I can not go through all my thought so as to make they clear to you , nor have I ever dwelt on thing , to shape a story of they . I know not where the beginning be , nor where the middle ought to be , nor even how at the present time I feel , or think , or ought to think . if I look for help to those around I , who should tell I right and wrong   be old and much wise , I meet sometimes with laughter , and at other time with anger . 
 ' there be but two in the world who ever listen and try to help I ; one of they be my grandfather , and the other be a man of wisdom , whom we call the Counsellor . my grandfather , Sir Ensor Doone , be very old and harsh of manner   except indeed to I ; he seem to know what be right and wrong , but not to want to think of it . the Counsellor , on the other hand , though full of life and subtlety , treat my question as of play , and not gravely worth his while to answer , unless he can make wit of they . 
 ' and among the woman there be none with whom I can hold converse , since my Aunt Sabina die , who take such pain to teach I . she be a lady of high repute and lofty way , and learning , but grieve and harass more and more by the coarseness , and the violence , and the ignorance around she . in vain she strove , from year to year , to make the young man hearken , to teach they what become their birth , and give they sense of honour . it be her favourite word , poor thing ! and they call she " Old Aunt Honour . " very often she use to say that I be her only comfort , and I be sure she be my only one ; and when she die it be more to I than if I have lose a mother . 
 ' for I have no remembrance now of father or of mother , although they say that my father be the eld son of Sir Ensor Doone , and the brave and the good of they . and so they call I heiress to this little realm of violence ; and in sorry sport sometimes , I be their Princess or their queen . 
 ' many people live here , as I be force to do , would perhaps be very happy , and perhaps I ought to be so . we have a beauteous valley , shelter from the cold of winter and power of the summer sun , untroubled also by the storm and mist that veil the mountain ; although I must acknowledge that it be apt to rain too often . the grass moreover be so fresh , and the brook so bright and lively , and flower of so many hue come after one another that no one need be dull , if only leave alone with they . 
 ' and so in the early day perhaps , when morning breathe around I , and the sun be go upward , and light be play everywhere , I be not so far beside they all as to live in shadow . but when the evening gather down , and the sky be spread with sadness , and the day have spend itself ; then a cloud of lonely trouble fall , like night , upon I . I can not see the thing I quest for of a world beyond I ; I can not join the peace and quiet of the depth above I ; neither have I any pleasure in the brightness of the star . 
 ' what I want to know be something none of they can tell I what be I , and why set here , and when shall I be with they ? I see that you be surprised a little at this my curiosity . perhaps such question never spring in any wholesome spirit . but they be in the depth of mine , and I can not be quit of they . 
 ' meantime , all around I be violence and robbery , coarse delight and savage pain , reckless joke and hopeless death . be it any wonder that I can not sink with these , that I can not so forget my soul , as to live the life of brute , and die the death more horrible because it dream of wake ? there be none to lead I forward , there be none to teach I right ; young as I be , I live beneath a curse that last for ever . ' 
 here Lorna break down for awhile , and cry so very piteously , that doubt of my knowledge , and of any power to comfort , I do my good to hold my peace , and try to look very cheerful . then think that might be bad manner , I go to wipe her eye for she . 
 ' Master Ridd , ' she begin again , ' I be both ashamed and vex at my own childish folly . but you , who have a mother , who think   you say   so much of you , and sister , and a quiet home ; you can not tell   it be not likely   what a lonely nature be . how it leap in mirth sometimes , with only heaven touch it ; and how it fall away desponding , when the dreary weight creep on . 
 ' it do not happen many time that I give way like this ; more shame now to do so , when I ought to entertain you . sometimes I be so full of anger , that I dare not trust to speech , at thing they can not hide from I ; and perhaps you would be much surprised that reckless man would care so much to elude a young girl 's knowledge . they use to boast to Aunt Sabina of pillage and of cruelty , on purpose to enrage she ; but they never boast to I . it even make I smile sometimes to see how awkwardly they come and offer for temptation to I shin packet , half conceal , of ornament and finery , of ring , or chain , or jewel , lately belong to other people . 
 ' but when I try to search the past , to get a sense of what befell I ere my own perception form ; to feel back for the line of childhood , as a trace of gossamer , then I only know that nought live long than God will it . so may after sin go by , for we be child always , as the Counsellor have tell I ; so may we , beyond the cloud , seek this infancy of life , and never find its memory . 
 ' but I be talk now of thing which never come across I when any work be toward . it might have be a good thing for I to have have a father to beat these roving out of I ; or a mother to make a home , and teach I how to manage it . for , be leave with none I think ; and nothing ever come of it . nothing , I mean , which I can grasp and have with any surety ; nothing but faint image , and wonderment , and wandering . but often , when I be neither search back into remembrance , nor ask of my parent , but occupy by trifle , something like a sign , or message , or a token of some meaning , seem to glance upon I . whether from the rustle wind , or sound of distant music , or the singing of a bird , like the sun on snow it strike I with a pain of pleasure . 
 ' and often when I wake at night , and listen to the silence , or wander far from people in the grayness of the evening , or stand and look at quiet water have shadow over it , some vague image seem to hover on the skirt of vision , ever change place and outline , ever flit as I follow . this so move and hurry I , in the eagerness and longing , that straightway all my chance be lose ; and memory , scar like a wild bird , fly . or be I as a child perhaps , chase a flown cageling , who among the branch free play and peep at the offer cage   as a home not to be urge on he , and mean to take his time of come , if he come at all ? 
 ' often too I wonder at the odd of fortune , which make I   helpless as I be , and fond of peace and read   the heiress of this mad domain , the sanctuary of unholiness . it be not likely that I shall have much power of authority ; and yet the Counsellor creep up to be my Lord of the Treasury ; and his son aspire to my hand , as of a Royal alliance . well , " honour among thief , " they say ; and mine be the first honour : although among decent folk perhaps , honesty be well . 
 ' we should not be so quiet here , and safe from interruption but that I have beg one privilege rather than command it . this be that the low end , just this narrowing of the valley , where it be most hard to come at , might be look upon as mine , except for purpose of guard . therefore none beside the sentry ever trespass on I here , unless it be my grandfather , or the Counsellor or Carver . 
 ' by your face , Master Ridd , I see that you have hear of Carver Doone . for strength and courage and resource he bear the first repute among we , as might well be expect from the son of the Counsellor . but he differ from his father , in be very hot and savage , and quite free from argument . the Counsellor , who be my uncle , give his son the good advice ; commend all the virtue , with eloquence and wisdom ; yet himself abstain from they accurately and impartially . 
 ' you must be tired of this story , and the time I take to think , and the weakness of my telling ; but my life from day to day show so little variance . among the rider there be none whose safe return I watch for I mean none more than other and indeed there seem no risk , all be now so feared of we . neither of the old man be there whom I can revere or love   except alone my grandfather , whom I love with trembling : neither of the woman any whom I like to deal with , unless it be a little maiden whom I save from starve . 
 ' a little cornish girl she be , and shape in western manner , not so very much less in width than if you take her lengthwise . her father seem to have be a miner , a Cornishman   as she declare   of more than average excellence , and well than any two man to be find in Devonshire , or any four in Somerset . very few thing can have be beyond his power of performance , and yet he leave his daughter to starve upon a peat - rick . she do not know how this be do , and look upon it as a mystery , the meaning of which will some day be clear , and redound to her father 's honour . his name be Simon Carfax , and he come as the captain of a gang from one of the cornish stannarie . Gwenny Carfax , my young maid , well remember how her father be bring up from Cornwall . her mother have be bury , just a week or so before ; and he be sad about it , and have be off his work , and be ready for another job . then people come to he by night , and say that he must want a change , and everybody lose their wife , and work be the way to mend it . so what with grief , and over - think , and the inside of a square bottle , Gwenny say they bring he off , to become a mighty captain , and choose the country round . the last she see of he be this , that he go down a ladder somewhere on the wild of Exmoor , leave she with bread and cheese , and his travel - hat to see to . and from that day to this he never come above the ground again ; so far as we can hear of . 
 ' but Gwenny , hold to his hat , and have eat the bread and cheese   when he come no more to help she , dwelt three day near the mouth of the hole ; and then it be close over , the while that she be sleep . with weakness and with want of food , she lose herself distressfully , and go away for mile or more , and lie upon a peat - rick , to die before the raven . 
 ' that very day I chance to return from Aunt Sabina 's die - place ; for she would not die in Glen Doone , she say , lest the angel fear to come for she ; and so she be take to a cottage in a lonely valley .
