I be allow to visit she , for even we durst not refuse the wish of the dying ; and if a priest have be desire , we should have make bold with he . return very sorrowful , and care now for nothing , I find this little stray thing lie , her arm upon she , and not a sign of life , except the way that she be bite . black root - stuff be in her mouth , and a piece of dirty sheep 's wool , and at her foot an old egg - shell of some bird of the moorland . 
 ' I try to raise she , but she be too square and heavy for I ; and so I put food in her mouth , and leave she to do right with it . and this she do in a little time ; for the victual be very choice and rare , be what I have take over to tempt poor Aunt Sabina . Gwenny eat they without delay , and then be ready to eat the basket and the ware that contain they . 
 ' Gwenny take I for an angel though I be little like one , as you see , Master Ridd ; and she follow I , expect that I would open wing and fly when we come to any difficulty . I bring she home with I , so far as this can be a home , and she make herself my sole attendant , without so much as ask I . she have beat two or three other girl , who use to wait upon I , until they be afraid to come near the house of my grandfather . she seem to have no kind of fear even of our roughest man ; and yet she look with reverence and awe upon the Counsellor . as for the wickedness , and theft , and revelry around she , she say it be no concern of her , and they know their own business well . by this way of regard man she have win upon our rider , so that she be almost free from all control of place and season , and be allow to pass where none even of the youth may go . be so wide , and short , and flat , she have none to pay her compliment ; and , be there any , she would scorn they , as not be Cornishmen . sometimes she wander far , by moonlight , on the moor and up the river , to give her father   as she say   another chance of find she , and she come back not a wit defeat , or discourage , or depressed , but confident that he be only wait for the proper time . 
 ' Herein she set I good example of a patience and contentment hard for I to imitate . oftentime I be vex by thing I can not meddle with , yet which can not be keep from I , that I be at the point of fly from this dreadful valley , and risk all that can betide I in the unknown outer world . if it be not for my grandfather , I would have do so long ago ; but I can not bear that he should die with no gentle hand to comfort he ; and I fear to think of the conflict that must ensue for the government , if there be a dispute succession . 
 ' ah I ! we be to be pity greatly , rather than condemn , by people whose thing we have take from they ; for I have read , and seem almost to understand about it , that there be place on the earth where gentle peace , and love of home , and knowledge of one 's neighbour prevail , and be , with reason , look for as the usual state of thing . there honest folk may go to work in the glory of the sunrise , with hope of come home again quite safe in the quiet evening , and find all their child ; and even in the darkness they have no fear of lie down , and drop off to slumber , and hearken to the wind of night , not as to an enemy try to find entrance , but a friend who come to tell the value of their comfort . 
 ' of all this golden ease I hear , but never see the like of it ; and , haply , I shall never do so , be bear to turbulence . once , indeed , I have the offer of escape , and kinsman 's aid , and high place in the gay , bright world ; and yet I be not tempt much , or , at least , dare not to trust it . and it end very sadly , so dreadfully that I even shrink from tell you about it ; for that one terror change my life , in a moment , at a blow , from childhood and from thought of play and commune with the flower and tree , to a sense of death and darkness , and a heavy weight of earth . be content now , Master Ridd ask I nothing more about it , so your sleep be sounder . ' 
 but I , John Ridd , be young and new , and very fond of hear thing to make my blood to tingle , have no more of manner than to urge poor Lorna onwards , hop , perhaps , in depth of heart , that she might have to hold by I , when the bad come to the bad of it . therefore she go on again . 




 CHAPTER XXI 

 LORNA end she STORY 

 ' it be not a twelvemonth yet , although it seem ten year agone , since I blow the downy globe to learn the time of day , or set beneath my chin the veining of the varnish buttercup , or fire the fox - glove cannonade , or make a captive of myself with dandelion fetter ; for then I have not very much to trouble I in earnest , but go about , romance gravely , play at bo - peep with fear , make for myself strong hero of gray rock or fir - tree , add to my own importance , as the child love to do . 
 ' as yet I have not truly learn the evil of our living , the scorn of law , the outrage , and the sorrow cause to other . it even be a point with all to hide the roughness from I , to show I but the gallant side , and keep in shade the other . my grandfather , Sir Ensor Doone , have give strictest order , as I discover afterwards , that in my presence all should be seemly , kind , and vigilant . nor be it very difficult to keep most part of the mischief from I , for no Doone ever rob at home , neither do they quarrel much , except at time of gambling . and though Sir Ensor Doone be now so old and grow feeble , his own way he will have still , and no one dare deny he . even our fierce and most mighty swordsman , sear from all sense of right or wrong , yet have plentiful sense of fear , when bring before that white - haired man . not that he be rough with they , or querulous , or rebukeful ; but that he have a strange soft smile , and a gaze they can not answer , and a knowledge deeply far than they have of themselves . under his protection , I be as safe from all those man   some of whom be but little akin to I   as if I sleep beneath the roof of the King 's Lord Justiciary . 
 ' but now , at the time I speak of , one evening of last summer , a horrible thing befell , which take all play of childhood from I . the fifteenth day of last July be very hot and sultry , long after the time of sundown ; and I be pay heed of it , because of the old saying that if it rain then , rain will fall on forty day thereafter . I have be long by the waterside at this low end of the valley , plait a little crown of woodbine crockete with sprig of heath to please my grandfather , who like to see I gay at supper - time . be proud of my tiara , which have cost some trouble , I set it on my head at once , to save the chance of crush , and carry my gray hat , venture by a path not often trod . for I must be home at the supper - time , or grandfather would be exceed wrath ; and the bad of his anger be that he never condescend to show it . 
 ' therefore , instead of the open mead , or the winding of the river , I make short cut through the ash - tree covert which lie in the middle of our vale , with the water skirt or cleave it . you have never be up so far as that at least to the good of my knowledge but you see it like a long gray spot , from the top of the cliff above we . here I be not likely to meet any of our people because the young one be afraid of some ancient tale about it , and the old one have no love of tree where gunshot be uncertain . 
 ' it be more almost than dusk , down below the tree - leaf , and I be eager to go through , and be again beyond it . for the gray dark hang around I , scarcely show shadow ; and the little light that glimmer seem to come up from the ground . for the earth be strow with the winter - spread and coil of last year 's foliage , the lichene claw of chalky twig , and the numberless decay which give a light in its decaying . I , for my part , hasten shyly , ready to draw back and run from hare , or rabbit , or small field - mouse . 
 ' at a sudden turn of the narrow path , where it stop again to the river , a man leap out from behind a tree , and stop I , and seize hold of I . I try to shriek , but my voice be still ; I could only hear my heart . 
 ' " now , Cousin Lorna , my good cousin , " he say , with ease and calmness ; " your voice be very sweet , no doubt , from all that I can see of you . but I pray you keep it still , unless you would give to dusty death your very good cousin and trusty guardian , Alan Brandir of Loch Awe . " 
 ' " you my guardian ! " I say , for the idea be too ludicrous ; and ludicrous thing always strike I first , through some fault of nature . 
 ' " I have in truth that honour , madam , " he answer , with a sweeping bow ; " unless I err in take you for Mistress Lorna Doone . " 
 ' " you have not mistake I . my name be Lorna Doone . " 
 ' he look at I , with gravity , and be inclined to make some claim to close consideration upon the score of kinship ; but I shrunk back , and only say , " yes , my name be Lorna Doone . " 
 ' " then I be your faithful guardian , Alan Brandir of Loch Awe ; call Lord Alan Brandir , son of a worthy peer of Scotland . now will you confide in I ? " 
 ' " I confide in you ! " I cry , look at he with amazement ; " why , you be not old than I be ! " 
 ' " yes I be , three year at least . you , my ward , be not sixteen . I , your worshipful guardian , be almost nineteen year of age . " 
 ' upon hear this I look at he , for that seem then a venerable age ; but the more I look the more I doubt , although he be dress quite like a man . he lead I in a courtly manner , step at his tall to an open place beside the water ; where the light come as in channel , and be make the most of by glance wave and fair white stone . 
 ' " now be I to your liking , cousin ? " he ask , when I have gaze at he , until I be almost ashamed , except at such a stripling . " do my Cousin Lorna judge kindly of her guardian , and her near kinsman ? in a word , be our admiration mutual ? " 
 ' " truly I know not , " I say ; " but you seem good - natured , and to have no harm in you . do they trust you with a sword ? " 
 ' for in my usage among man of stature and strong presence , this pretty youth , so tricked and slender , seem nothing but a doll to I . although he scar I in the wood , now that I see he in good twilight , lo ! he be but little great than my little self ; and so tasselled and so ruffle with a mint of bravery , and a green coat bar with red , and a slim sword hang under he , it be the utmost I could do to look at he half - gravely . 
 ' " I fear that my presence hath scarce enough of ferocity about it , "   he give a jerk to his sword as he speak , and clank it on the brook - stone ; " yet do I assure you , cousin , that I be not without some prowess ; and many a master of defence hath this good sword of mine disarm . now if the bold and big robber in all this charming valley durst so much as breathe the scent of that flower coronal , which doth not adorn but be adorn " here he talk some nonsense " I would cleave he from head to foot , ere ever he could fly or cry . " 
 ' " hush ! " I say ; " talk not so loudly , or thou mayst have to do both thyself , and do they both in vain . " 
 ' for he be quite forget now , in his bravery before I , where he stand , and with whom he speak , and how the summer lightning shine above the hill and down the hollow . and as I gaze on this slight fair youth , clearly one of high birth and breeding   albeit over - boastful , a chill of fear creep over I ; because he have no strength or substance , and would be no more than a pin - cushion before the great sword of the Doones . 
 ' " I pray you be not vex with I , " he answer , in a soft voice ; " for I have travel far and sorely , for the sake of see you . I know right well among whom I be , and that their hospitality be more of the knife than the salt - stand . nevertheless I be safe enough , for my foot be the fleet in Scotland , and what be these hill to I ? tush ! I have see some border foray among wilder spirit and crafty man than these be . once I mind some year agone , when I be quite a stripling lad " 
 ' " worshipful guardian , " I say , " there be no time now for history . if thou art in no haste , I be , and can not stay here idle . only tell I how I be akin and under wardship to thee , and what purpose bring thee here . " 
 ' " in order , cousin all thing in order , even with fair lady . first , I be thy uncle 's son , my father be thy mother 's brother , or at least thy grandmother 's unless I be deceive in that which I have guess , and no other man . for my father , be a lead lord in the council of King Charles the Second , appoint I to learn the law , not for my livelihood , thank God , but because he feel the lack of it in affair of state . but first your leave , young Mistress Lorna ; I can not lay down legal maxim , without aid of smoke . " 
 ' he lean against a willow - tree , and draw from a gild box a little dark thing like a stick , place it between his lip , and then strike a flint on steel make fire and catch it upon touchwood . with this he kindle the tip of the stick , until it glow with a ring of red , and then he breathe forth curl of smoke , blue and smell on the air like spice . I have never see this do before , though acquaint with tobacco - pipe ; and it make I laugh , until I think of the peril that must follow it . 
 ' " Cousin , have no fear , " he say ; " this make I all the safe ; they will take I for a glow - worm , and thee for the flower it shin upon . but to return of law I learn as you may suppose , but little ; although I have capacity . but the thing be far too dull for I . all I care for be adventure , move chance , and hot encounter ; therefore all of law I learn be how to live without it . nevertheless , for amusement 's sake , as I must need be at my desk an hour or so in the afternoon , I take to the sporting branch of the law , the pitfall , and the ambuscade ; and of all the trap to be lay therein , pedigree be the rare . there be scarce a man worth a cross of butter , but what you may find a hole in his shield within four generation . and so I strike our own escutcheon , and it sound hollow . there be a point but hee not that ; enough that be curious now , I follow up the quarry , and I be come to this at last we , even we , the lord of Loch Awe , have an outlaw for our cousin , and I would we have more , if they be like you . " 
 ' " Sir , " I answer , be amuse by his manner , which be new to I   for the Doones be much in earnest , ' surely you count it no disgrace to be of kin to Sir Ensor Doone , and all his honest family ! " 
 ' " if it be so , it be in truth the very high honour and would heal ten hole in our escutcheon . what noble family but spring from a captain among robber ? trade alone can spoil our blood ; robbery purifie it . the robbery of one age be the chivalry of the next . we may start anew , and vie with even the nobility of France , if we can once enrol but half the Doones upon our lineage . " 
 ' " I like not to hear you speak of the Doones , as if they be no more than that , " I exclaim , be now unreasonable ; " but will you tell I , once for all , sir , how you be my guardian ? " 
 ' " that I will do . you be my ward because you be my father 's ward , under the scottish law ; and now my father be so deaf , I have succeed to that right at least in my own opinion under which claim I be here to neglect my trust no long , but to lead you away from scene and deed which   though of good repute and comely   be not the good for young gentlewoman . there speak I not like a guardian ? after that can you mistrust I ? " 
 ' " but , " say I , " good Cousin Alan   if I may so call you , it be not meet for young gentlewoman to go away with young gentleman , though fifty time their guardian . but if you will only come with I , and explain your tale to my grandfather , he will listen to you quietly , and take no advantage of you . " 
 ' " I thank you much , kind Mistress Lorna , to lead the goose into the fox 's den ! but , set by all thought of danger , I have other reason against it . now , come with your faithful guardian , child . I will pledge my honour against all harm , and to bear you safe to London . by the law of the realm , I be now entitle to the custody of your fair person , and of all your chattel . " 
 ' " but , sir , all that you have learn of law , be how to live without it . " 
 ' " fairly meet , fair cousin mine ! your wit will do I credit , after a little sharpening . and there be none to do that well than your aunt , my mother . although she know not of my coming , she be long to receive you . come , and in a few month ' time you shall set the mode at Court , instead of pin here , and weave coronal of daisy . " 
 ' I turn aside , and think a little . although he seem so light of mind , and gay in dress and manner , I could not doubt his honesty ; and see , beneath his jaunty air , true mettle and ripe bravery . Scarce have I think of his project twice , until he speak of my aunt , his mother , but then the form of my dear friend , my sweet Aunt Sabina , seem to come and bid I listen , for this be what she pray for . moreover I feel   though not as now   that Doone Glen be no place for I or any proud young maiden . but while I think , the yellow lightning spread behind a bulk of cloud , three time ere the flash be do , far off and void of thunder ; and from the pile of cloud before it , cut as from black paper , and light to depth of blackness by the blaze behind it , a form as of an aged man , sit in a chair loose - mantle , seem to lift a hand and warn . 
 ' this mind I of my grandfather , and all the care I owe he . moreover , now the storm be rise and I begin to grow afraid ; for of all thing awful to I thunder be the dreadfulest . it doth so growl , like a lion come , and then so roll , and roar , and rumble , out of a thickening darkness , then crack like the last trump overhead through cloven air and terror , that all my heart lie low and quiver , like a weed in water . I listen now for the distant rolling of the great black storm , and hear it , and be hurry by it . but the youth before I wave his roll tobacco at it , and drawl in his dainty tone and manner , 
 ' " the sky be have a smoke , I see , and dropping spark , and grumble . I should have think these Exmoor hill too small to gather thunder . " 
 ' " I can not go , I will not go with you , Lord Alan Brandir , " I answer , be vex a little by those word of his . " you be not grave enough for I , you be not old enough for I . my Aunt Sabina would not have wish it ; nor would I leave my grandfather , without his full permission . I thank you much for come , sir ; but be go at once by the way you come ; and pray how do you come , sir ? " 
 ' " fair cousin , you will grieve for this ; you will mourn , when you can not mend it . I would my mother have be here , soon would she have persuade you . and yet , " he add , with the smile of his accustomed gaiety , " it would have be an unco thing , as we say in Scotland , for her ladyship to have wait upon you , as her graceless son have do , and hop to do again ere long . down the cliff I come , and up they I must make way back again . now adieu , fair Cousin Lorna , I see you be in haste tonight ; but I be right proud of my guardianship . give I just one flower for token " here he kiss his hand to I , and I throw he a truss of woodbine " adieu , fair cousin , trust I well , I will soon be here again . " 
 ' " that thou never shalt , sir , " cry a voice as loud as a culverin ; and Carver Doone have Alan Brandir as a spider hath a fly . the boy make a little shriek at first , with the sudden shock and the terror ; then he look , methought , ashamed of himself , and set his face to fight for it . very bravely he strove and struggle , to free one arm and grasp his sword ; but as well might an infant bury alive attempt to lift his gravestone . Carver Doone , with his great arm wrap around the slim gay body , smile   as I see by the flash from heaven   at the poor young face turn up to he ; then   as a nurse bear off a child , who be loath to go to bed , he lift the youth from his foot , and bear he away into the darkness . 
 ' I be young then . I be old now ; old by ten year , in thought , although it be not a twelvemonth since . if that black deed be do again , I could follow , and could combat it , could throw weak arm on the murderer , and strive to be murder also . I be now at home with violence ; and no dark death surprise I . 
 ' but , be as I be that night , the horror overcome I . the crash of thunder overhead , the last despairing look , the death - piece frame with blaze of lightning my young heart be so affrighted that I could not gasp . my breath go from I , and I know not where I be , or who , or what . only that I lay , and cower , under great tree full of thunder ; and could neither count , nor moan , nor have my foot to help I . 
 ' yet hearken , as a coward do , through the brushing of the wind , and echo of far noise , I hear a sharp sound as of iron , and a fall of heavy wood . no unmanly shriek come with it , neither cry for mercy . Carver Doone know what it be ; and so do Alan Brandir . ' 
 here Lorna Doone could tell no more , be overcome with weeping . only through her tear she whisper , as a thing too bad to tell , that she have see that giant Carver , in a few day afterwards , smoke a little round brown stick , like those of her poor cousin . I could not press she any more with question , or for clearness ; although I long very much to know whether she have speak of it to her grandfather or the Counsellor . but she be now in such condition , both of mind and body , from the force of her own fear multiply by tell it , that I do nothing more than coax she , at a distance humbly ; and so that she could see that some one be at least afraid of she . this   although I know not woman in those day , as now I do , and never shall know much of it , this , I say , so bring she round , that all her fear be now for I , and how to get I safely off , without mischance to any one . and sooth to say , in spite of longing just to see if Master Carver could have serve I such a trick as it grow towards the dusk , I be not well pleased to be there ; for it seem a lawless place , and some of Lorna 's fright stay with I as I talk it away from she . 




 CHAPTER XXII 

 after hear that tale from Lorna , I go home in sorry spirit , have add fear for she , and misery about , to all my other ailment . and be it not quite certain now that she , be own full cousin to a peer and lord of Scotland   although he be a dead one , must have nought to do with I , a yeoman 's son , and bind to be the father of more yeoman ? I have be very sorry when first I hear about that poor young popinjay , and would gladly have fight hard for he ; but now it strike I that after all he have no right to be there , prowl   as it be   for Lorna , without any invitation : and we farmer love not trespass . still , if I have see the thing , I must have try to save he . 
 moreover , I be greatly vex with my own hesitation , stupidity , or shyness , or whatever else it be , which have hold I back from say , ere she tell her story , what be in my heart to say , videlicet , that I must die unless she let I love she . not that I be fool enough to think that she would answer I accord to my liking , or begin to care about I for a long time yet ; if indeed she ever should , which I hardly dare to hope . but that I have hear from man more skillful in the matter that it be wise to be in time , that so the maid may begin to think , when they know that they be think of . and , to tell the truth , I have bitter fear , on account of her wondrous beauty , lest some young fellow of high birth and fine part , and finish , might steal in before poor I , and cut I out altogether . think of which , I use to double my great fist , without know it , and keep it in my pocket ready . 
 but the bad of all be this , that in my great dismay and anguish to see Lorna weep so , I have promise not to cause she any further trouble from anxiety and fear of harm . and this , be bring to practice , mean that I be not to show myself within the precinct of Glen Doone , for at least another month . unless indeed   as I contrive to edge into the agreement   anything should happen to increase her present trouble and every day 's uneasiness . in that case , she be to throw a dark mantle , or covering of some sort , over a large white stone which hang within the entrance to her retreat I mean the outer entrance and which , though unseen from the valley itself , be   as I have observe   conspicuous from the height where I stand with Uncle Reuben . 
 now come home so sad and weary , yet try to console myself with the thought that love o'erleapeth rank , and must still be lord of all , I find a shameful thing go on , which make I very angry . for it need must happen that young Marwood de Whichehalse , only son of the Baron , rid home that very evening , from chase of the Exmoor bustard , with his hound and serve - man , should take the short cut through our farmyard , and be dry from his exercise , should come and ask for drink . and it need must happen also that there should be none to give it to he but my sister Annie . I more than suspect that he have hear some report of our Annie 's comeliness , and have a mind to satisfy himself upon the subject . now , as he take the large ox - horn of our quarantine - apple cider   which we always keep apart from the rest , be too good except for the quality , he let his finger dwell on Annie 's , by some sort of accident , while he lift his beaver gallantly , and gaze on her face in the light from the west . then what do Annie do   as she herself tell I afterwards   but make she very well curtsey to he , be pleased that he be pleased with she , while she think what a fine young man he be and so much breeding about he ! and in truth he be a dark , handsome fellow , hasty , reckless , and changeable , with a look of sad destiny in his black eye that would make any woman pity he . what he be think of our Annie be not for I to say , although I may think that you could not have find another such maiden on Exmoor , except   of course   my Lorna . 
 though young Squire Marwood be so thirsty , he spend much time over his cider , or at any rate over the ox - horn , and he make many bow to Annie , and drink health to all the family , and speak of I as if I have be his very good friend at Blundell 's ; whereas he know well enough all the time that we have nought to say to one another ; he be three year old , and therefore of course disdain I . but while he be cast about perhaps for some excuse to stop long , and Annie be begin to fear lest mother should come after she , or Eliza be at the window , or Betty up in pig ' house , suddenly there come up to they , as if from the very heart of the earth , that long , low , hollow , mysterious sound which I speak of in winter . 
 the young man start in his saddle , let the horn fall on the horse - step , and gaze all around in wonder ; while as for Annie , she turn like a ghost , and try to slam the door , but fail through the violence of her trembling ;   for never till now have any one hear it so close at hand as you might say   or in the mere fall of the twilight . and by this time there be no man , at least in our parish , but know for the Parson himself have tell we so that it be the devil groan because the Doones be too many for he . 
 Marwood de Whichehalse be not so alarmed but what he see a fine opportunity . he leap from his horse , and lay hold of dear Annie in a highly comforting manner ; and she never would tell we about it   be so shy and modest , whether in breathe his comfort to she he try to take some from her pure lip . I hope he do not , because that to I would seem not the deed of a gentleman , and he be of good old family . 
 at this very moment , who should come into the end of the passage upon they but the heavy writer of these doing I , John Ridd myself , and walk the fast , it may be , on account of the noise I mention . I enter the house with some wrath upon I at see the gazehound in the yard ; for it seem a cruel thing to I to harass the bird in the breeding - time . and to my amazement there I see Squire Marwood among the milk - pan with his arm around our Annie 's waist , and Annie all blush and coax he off , for she be not come to scold yet . 
 perhaps I be wrong ; God know , and if I be , no doubt I shall pay for it ; but I give he the flat of my hand on his head , and down he go in the thick of the milk - pan . he would have have my fist , I doubt , but for have be at school with I ; and after that it be like enough he would never have speak another word . as it be , he lie stunned , with the cream run on he ; while I take poor Annie up and carry she in to mother , who have hear the noise and be frightened . 
 concern this matter I ask no more , but hold myself ready to bear it out in any form convenient , feel that I have do my duty , and care not for the consequence ; only for several day dear Annie seem frightened rather than grateful . but the odd result of it be that Eliza , who have so despise I , and make very rude verse about I , now come try to sit on my knee , and kiss I , and give I the good of the pan . however , I would not allow it , because I hate sudden change . 
 another thing also astonish I namely , a beautiful letter from Marwood de Whichehalse himself   send by a groom soon afterwards , in which he apologise to I , as if I have be his equal , for his rudeness to my sister , which be not intend in the least , but come of their common alarm at the moment , and his desire to comfort she . also he beg permission to come and see I , as an old schoolfellow , and set everything straight between we , as should be among honest Blundellites . 
 all this be so different to my idea of fight out a quarrel , when once it be upon a man , that I know not what to make of it , but bow to high breeding . only one thing I resolve upon , that come when he would he should not see Annie . and to do my sister justice , she have no desire to see he . 
 however , I be too easy , there be no doubt of that , be very quick to forgive a man , and very slow to suspect , unless he hath once lie to I . moreover , as to Annie , it have always seem to I   much against my wish   that some shrewd love of a waiting sort be between she and Tom Faggus : and though Tom have make his fortune now , and everybody respect he , of course he be not to be compare , in that point of respectability , with those people who hang the robber when fortune turn against they . 
 so young Squire Marwood come again , as though I have never smite he , and speak of it in as light a way as if we be still at school together . it be not in my nature , of course , to keep any anger against he ; and I know what a condescension it be for he to visit we . and it be a very grievous thing , which touch small landowner , to see an ancient family day by day decaying : and when we hear that Ley Barton itself , and all the Manor of Lynton be under a heavy mortgage debt to John Lovering of Weare - Gifford , there be not much , in our little way , that we would not gladly do or suffer for the benefit of De Whichehalse . 
 meanwhile the work of the farm be toward , and every day give we more ado to dispose of what itself be do . for after the long dry skeltering wind of March and part of April , there have be a fortnight of soft wet ; and when the sun come forth again , hill and valley , wood and meadow , could not make enough of he . many a spring have I see since then , but never yet two spring alike , and never one so beautiful . or be it that my love come forth and touch the world with beauty ? 
 the spring be in our valley now ; creep first for shelter shyly in the pause of the bluster wind . there the lamb come bleat to she , and the orchis lift up , and the thin dead leaf of clover lie for the new one to spring through . there the stiff thing that sleep , the stubby oak , and the saplin'd beech , drop their brown defiance to she , and prepare for a soft reply . 
 while her over - eager child   who have start forth to meet she , through the frost and shower of sleet , catkin'd hazel , gold - glove withy , youthful elder , and old woodbine , with all the tribe of good hedge - climber   who must hasten while haste they may   be there one of they that do not claim the merit of come first ? 
 there she stay and hold her revel , as soon as the fear of frost be go ; all the air be a fount of freshness , and the earth of gladness , and the laugh water prattle of the kindness of the sun . 
 but all this make it much hard for we , ply the hoe and rake , to keep the field with room upon they for the corn to tiller . the winter wheat be well enough , be sturdy and strong - sided ; but the spring wheat and the barley and the oats be overrun by ill weed grow fast . therefore , as the old saying be , 
 Farmer , that thy wife may thrive , let not burr and burdock wive ; and if thou wouldst keep thy son , see that bine and gith have none . 
 so we be compel to go down the field and up it , strike in and out with care where the green blade hang together , so that each have space to move in and to spread its root abroad . and I do assure you now , though you may not believe I , it be hard work to keep John Fry , Bill Dadds , and Jem Slocomb all in a line and all move nimbly to the tune of my own tool , than it be to set out in the morning alone , and hoe half an acre by dinner - time . for , instead of keep the good ash move , they would for ever be find something to look at or to speak of , or at any rate , to stop with ; blame the shape of their tool perhaps , or talk about other people 's affair ; or , what be most irksome of all to I , take advantage as married man , and whispering joke of no excellence about my having , or have not , or be ashamed of a sweetheart . and this go so far at last that I be force to take two of they and knock their head together ; after which they work with a well will . 
 when we meet together in the evening round the kitchen chimney - place , after the man have have their supper and their heavy boot be go , my mother and Eliza would do their very utmost to learn what I be think of . not that we keep any fire now , after the crock be empty ; but that we love to see the ash cool , and to be together . at these time Annie would never ask I any crafty question   as Eliza do , but would sit with her hair untwined , and one hand underneath her chin , sometimes look softly at I , as much as to say that she know it all and I be no bad off than she . but strange to say my mother dream not , even for an instant , that it be possible for Annie to be think of such a thing . she be so very good and quiet , and careful of the linen , and clever about the cookery and fowl and bacon - curing , that people use to laugh , and say she would never look at a bachelor until her mother order she . but I   perhaps from my own condition and the sense of what it be   feel no certainty about this , and even have another opinion , as be say before . 
 often I be much inclined to speak to she about it , and put she on her guard against the approach of Tom Faggus ; but I could not find how to begin , and fear to make a breach between we ; know that if her mind be set , no word of mine would alter it ; although they need must grieve she deeply . moreover , I feel that , in this case , a certain homely devonshire proverb would come home to I ; that one , I mean , which record that the crock be call the kettle smutty . not , of course , that I compare my innocent maid to a highwayman ; but that Annie might think she bad , and would be too apt to do so , if indeed she love Tom Faggus . and our Cousin Tom , by this time , be live a quiet and godly life ; have retire almost from the trade   except when he need excitement , or come across public officer , and have win the esteem of all whose purse be in his power . 
 perhaps it be needless for I to say that all this time while my month be run or rather crawl , for never month go so slow as that with I neither weed , nor seed , nor cattle , nor my own mother 's anxiety , nor any care for my sister , keep I from look once every day , and even twice on a Sunday , for any sign of Lorna . for my heart be ever weary ; in the bud valley , and by the crystal water , look at the lamb in fold , or the heifer on the mill , labour in trickle furrow , or among the beaded blade ; halt fresh to see the sun lift over the golden - vapoure ridge ; or doffing hat , from sweat of brow , to watch he sink in the low gray sea ; be it as it would of day , of work , or night , or slumber , it be a weary heart I bore , and fear be on the brink of it . 
 all the beauty of the spring go for happy man to think of ; all the increase of the year be for other eye to mark . not a sign of any sunrise for I from my fount of life , not a breath to stir the dead leaf fall on my heart 's Spring . 




 CHAPTER XXIII 

 a ROYAL invitation 

 although I have , for the most part , so very stout an appetite , that none but mother see any need of encourage I to eat , I could only manage one true good meal in a day , at the time I speak of . mother be in despair at this , and tempt I with the whole of the rack , and even talk of send to Porlock for a druggist who come there twice in a week ; and Annie spend all her time in cooking , and even Lizzie sing song to I ; for she could sing very sweetly . but my conscience tell I that Betty Muxworthy have some reason upon her side . 
 ' Latt the young ozebird aloun , zay I. Makk zuch ado about un , wi ' hogs'-puddens , and hock - bit , and lambs'-mate , and whaten bradd indade , and brewer ' ale avore dinner - time , and she not to zit wi ' no winder aupen draive I mad ' e doo , the ov'ee , zuch a passel of vooul . do ' un good to starve a bit ; and takk zome on 's wackedness out ov un . ' 
 but mother do not see it so ; and she even send for Nicholas Snowe to bring his three daughter with he , and have ale and cake in the parlour , and advise about what the bee be do , and when a swarm might be look for . be vex about this and have to stop at home nearly half the evening , I lose good manner so much as to ask he   even in our own house !   what he mean by not mend the swing - hurdle where the Lynn stream flow from our land into his , and which he be bind to maintain . but he look at I in a superior manner , and say , ' business , young man , in business time . ' 
 I have other reason for be vex with Farmer Nicholas just now , viz . that I have hear a rumour , after church one Sunday when most of all we sorrow over the sin of one another that Master Nicholas Snowe have be see to gaze tenderly at my mother , during a passage of the sermon , wherein the parson speak well and warmly about the duty of christian love . now , put one thing with another , about the bee , and about some duck , and a bullock with a broken knee - cap , I more than suspect that Farmer Nicholas be cast sheep 's eye at my mother ; not only to save all further trouble in the matter of the hurdle , but to override I altogether upon the difficult question of dam . and I know quite well that John Fry 's wife never come to help at the washing without declare that it be a sin for a well - look woman like mother , with plenty to live on , and only three child , to keep all the farmer for mile around so unsettled in their mind about she . Mother use to answer ' oh fie , Mistress Fry ! be good enough to mind your own business . ' but we always see that she smooth her apron , and do her hair up afterwards , and that Mistress Fry go home at night with a cold pig 's foot or a bowl of drip . 
 therefore , on that very night , as I could not well speak to mother about it , without seem undutiful , after light the three young lady for so in sooth they call themselves all the way home with our stable - lanthorn , I beg good leave of Farmer Nicholas   who have hang some way behind we   to say a word in private to he , before he enter his own house . 
 ' Wi ' all the plaisure in laife , my zon , ' he answer very graciously , think perhaps that I be prepared to speak concern Sally . 
 ' now , Farmer Nicholas Snowe , ' I say , scarce know how to begin it , ' you must promise not to be vex with I , for what I be go to say to you . ' 
 ' vaxed wi ' thee ! Noo , noo , my lad . I ' ave a knowed thee too long for that . and thy veyther be my good friend , afore thee . never wrong his neighbour , never spak an unkind word , never have no maneness in he . tuk a vancy to a nice young ' ooman , and never kep she in doubt about it , though there wadn't   mooch to zettle on she . spak his maind laike a man , he do , and right happy he be wi ' she . ah , well a day ! ah , God knoweth well . I never shall zee his laike again . and he be the good judge of a dung - heap anywhere in this county . ' 
 ' well , Master Snowe , ' I answer he , ' it be very handsome of you to say so . and now I be go to be like my father , I be go to speak my mind . ' 
 ' raight there , lad ; raight enough , I reckon . we have have enough of pralimbinary . ' 
 ' then what I want to say be this I will not   have any one court my mother . ' 
 ' Coortin ' of thy mother , lad ? ' cry Farmer Snowe , with as much amazement as if the thing be impossible ; ' why , who ever hath be dooin ' of it ? ' 
 ' yes , courting of my mother , sir . and you know good who come do it . ' 
 ' wull , wull ! what will boy be up to next ? Zhud a ' think herzelf wor the proper judge . no thank ' ee , lad , no need of thy light . know the wai to my own door , at laste ; and have a raight to goo there . ' and he shut I out without so much as offer I a drink of cider . 
 the next afternoon , when work be over , I have see to the horse , for now it be foolish to trust John Fry , because he have so many child , and his wife have take to scold ; and just as I be say to myself that in five day more my month would be do , and myself free to seek Lorna , a man come rid up from the ford where the road go through the Lynn stream . as soon as I see that it be not Tom Faggus , I go no far to meet he , count that it must be some traveller bind for Brendon or Cheriton , and likely enough he would come and beg for a draught of milk or cider ; and then on again , after ask the way . 
 but instead of that , he stop at our gate , and stand up from his saddle , and halloe as if he be somebody ; and all the time he be flourish a white thing in the air , like the band our parson weareth . so I cross the court - yard to speak with he . 
 ' Service of the king ! ' he saith ; ' service of our lord the King ! come hither , thou great yokel , at risk of fine and imprisonment . ' 
 although not pleased with this , I go to he , as become a loyal man ; quite at my leisure , however , for there be no man bear who can hurry I , though I hasten for any woman . 
 ' Plover Barrows farm ! ' say he ; ' God only know how tired I be . be there any where in this cursed county a curse place call Plover Barrows farm ? for last twenty mile at least they tell I ' twere only half a mile far , or only just round corner . now tell I that , and I fain would thwack thee if thou wert not thrice my size . ' 
 ' Sir , ' I reply , ' you shall not have the trouble . this be Plover 's Barrows farm , and you be kindly welcome . Sheep 's kidney be for supper , and the ale get bright from the tapping . but why do you think ill of we ? we like not to be curse so . ' 
 ' Nay , I think no ill , ' he say ; ' sheep 's kidney be good , uncommon good , if they do they without burn . but I be so gall in the saddle ten day , and never a comely meal of it . and when they hear " King 's service " cry , they give I the bad of everything . all the way down from London , I have a rogue of a fellow in front of I , eat the fat of the land before I , and every one bow down to he . he could go three mile to my one though he never change his horse . he might have rob I at any minute , if I have be worth the trouble . a red mare he rideth , strong in the loin , and point quite small in the head . I shall live to see he hang yet . ' 
 all this time he be rid across the straw of our courtyard , get his weary leg out of the leather , and almost afraid to stand yet . a coarse - grain , hard - faced man he be , some forty year of age or so , and of middle height and stature . he be dress in a dark brown riding suit , none the well for Exmoor mud , but fit he very differently from the fashion of our tailor . across the holster lie his cloak , make of some red skin , and shin from the sweating of the horse . as I look down on his stiff bright head - piece , small quick eye and black needly beard , he seem to despise I   too much , as I think   for a mere ignoramus and country bumpkin . 
 ' Annie , have down the cut ham , ' I shout , for my sister be come to the door by chance , or because of the sound of a horse in the road , ' and cut a few rasher of hung deer 's meat . there be a gentleman come to sup , Annie . and fetch the hop out of the tap with a skewer that it may run more sparkling . ' 
 ' I wish I may go to a place never mean for I , ' say my new friend , now wipe his mouth with the sleeve of his brown riding coat , ' if ever I fall among such good folk . you be the right sort , and no error therein . all this shall go in your favour greatly , when I make deposition . at least , I mean , if it be as good in the eating as in the hearing . ' tis a supper quite fit for Tom Faggus himself , the man who hath steal my victual so . and that hang deer 's meat , now be it of the red deer run wild in these part ? ' 
 ' to be sure it be , sir , ' I answer ; ' where should we get any other ? ' 
 ' right , right , you be right , my son . I have hear that the flavour be marvellous . some of they come and scar I so , in the fog of the morning , that I hunger for they ever since . ha , ha , I see their haunch . but the young lady will not forget art sure she will not forget it ? ' 
 ' you may trust she to forget nothing , sir , that may tempt a guest to his comfort . ' 
 ' in faith , then , I will leave my horse in your hand , and be off for it . half the pleasure of the mouth be in the nose beforehand . but stay , almost I forget my business , in the hurry which thy tongue hath spread through my lately despairing belly . hungry I be , and sore of body , from my heel right upward , and sorest in front of my doublet , yet may I not rest nor bite barley - bread , until I have see and touch John Ridd . God grant that he be not far away ; I must eat my saddle , if it be so . ' 
 ' have no fear , good sir , ' I answer ; ' you have see and touch John Ridd . I be he , and not one likely to go beneath a bushel . ' 
 ' it would take a large bushel to hold thee , John Ridd . in the name of the king , his Majesty , Charles the Second , these present ! ' 
 he touch I with the white thing which I have first see he wave , and which I now behold to be sheepskin , such as they call parchment . it be tie across with cord , and fasten down in every corner with unsightly dab of wax . by order of the messenger   for I be over - frightened now to think of do anything , I break enough of seal to keep an Easter ghost from rise ; and there I see my name in large ; God grant such another shock may never befall I in my old age . 
 ' Read , my son ; read , thou great fool , if indeed thou canst read , ' say the officer to encourage I ; ' there be nothing to kill thee , boy , and my supper will be spoil . stare not at I so , thou fool ; thou art big enough to eat I ; read , read , read . ' 
 ' if you please , sir , what be your name ? ' I ask ; though why I ask he I know not , except from fear of witchcraft . 
 ' Jeremy Stickles be my name , lad , nothing more than a poor apparitor of the worshipful Court of King 's Bench . and at this moment a starve one , and no supper for I unless thou wilt read . ' 
 be compel in this way , I read pretty nigh as follow ; not that I give the whole of it , but only the gist and the emphasis , 
 ' to our good subject , John Ridd , etc . ' describe I ever so much well than I know myself ' by these present , greet . these be to require thee , in the name of our lord the King , to appear in person before the Right Worshipful , the Justices of his Majesty 's Bench at Westminster , lay aside all thine own business , and there to deliver such evidence as be within thy cognisance , touch certain matter whereby the peace of our say lord the King , and the well - being of this realm , be , be , or otherwise may be impeach , impugn , imperille , or otherwise detrimente . as witness these present . ' and then there be four seal , and then a signature I could not make out , only that it begin with a J , and end with some other writing , do almost in a circle . Underneath be add in a different handwriting ' charge will be bear . the matter be full urgent . ' 
 the messenger watch I , while I read so much as I could read of it ; and he seem well pleased with my surprise , because he have expect it . then , not know what else to do , I look again at the cover , and on the top of it I see , ' Ride , Ride , Ride ! on his gracious Majesty 's business ; spur and spare not . ' 
 it may be suppose by all who know I , that I be take hereupon with such a giddiness in my head and noisiness in my ear , that I be force to hold by the crook drive in below the thatch for holding of the hay - rake . there be scarcely any sense leave in I , only that the thing be come by power of Mother Melldrum , because I despise her warning , and have again seek Lorna . but the officer be grieve for I , and the danger to his supper . 
 ' my son , be not afraid , ' he say ; ' we be not go to skin thee . only thou tell all the truth , and it shall be but never mind , I will tell thee all about it , and how to come out harmless , if I find thy victual good , and no delay in serve they . ' 
 ' we do our good , sir , without bargain , ' say I , ' to please our visitor . ' 
 but when my mother see that parchment   for we could not keep it from she   she fall away into her favourite bed of stock gilly - flower , which she have be tend ; and when we bring she round again , do nothing but exclaim against the wickedness of the age and people . ' it be useless to tell she ; she know what it be , and so should all the parish know . the King have hear what her son be , how sober , and quiet , and diligent , and the strong young man in England ; and be himself such a reprobate God forgive she for say so he could never rest till he get poor Johnny , and make he as dissolute as himself . and if he do that ' here mother go off into a fit of crying ; and Annie mind her face , while Lizzie see that her gown be in comely order . 
 but the character of the King improve , when Master Jeremy Stickles   be really move by the look of it , and no bad man after all   lay it clearly before my mother that the King on his throne be unhappy , until he have see John Ridd . that the fame of John have go so far , and his size , and all his virtue that verily by the God who make he , the King be overcome with it . 
 then mother lie back in her garden chair , and smile upon the whole of we , and most of all on Jeremy ; look only shyly on I , and speak through some break of tear . ' his Majesty shall have my John ; his Majesty be very good : but only for a fortnight . I want no title for he . Johnny be enough for I ; and Master John for the work man . ' 
 now though my mother be so willing that I should go to London , expect great promotion and high glory for I , I myself be deeply go into the pit of sorrow . for what would Lorna think of I ? here be the long month just expire , after world of wait ; there would be her lovely self , peep softly down the glen , and fear to encourage I ; yet there would be nobody else , and what an insult to she ! dwell upon this , and see no chance of escape from it , I could not find one wink of sleep ; though Jeremy Stickles   who sleep close by   snore loud enough to spare I some . for I feel myself to be , as it be , in a place of some importance ; in a situation of trust , I may say ; and bind not to depart from it . for who could tell what the King might have to say to I about the Doones and I feel that they be at the bottom of this strange appearance or what his Majesty might think , if after receive a message from he   trusty under so many seal   I be to violate his faith in I as a churchwarden 's son , and falsely spread his word abroad ? 
 perhaps I be not wise in build such a wall of scruple . nevertheless , all that be there , and weigh upon I heavily . and at last I make up my mind to this , that even Lorna must not know the reason of my going , neither anything about it ; but that she might know I be go a long way from home , and perhaps be sorry for it . now how be I to let she know even that much of the matter , without break compact ? 
 puzzle on this , I fall asleep , after the proper time to get up ; nor be I to be see at breakfast time ; and mother   be quite strange to that   be very uneasy about it . but Master Stickles assure she that the King 's writ often have that effect , and the symptom be a good one . 
 ' now , Master Stickles , when must we start ? ' I ask he , as he lounge in the yard gaze at our turkey poult pick and run in the sun to the tune of their father 's gobble . ' your horse be greatly foundered , sir , and be hardly fit for the road to - day ; and Smiler be sled yesterday all up the high Cleve ; and none of the rest can carry I . ' 
 ' in a few more year , ' reply the King 's officer , contemplate I with much satisfaction ; " twill be a cruelty to any horse to put thee on his back , John . ' 
 Master Stickles , by this time , be quite familiar with we , call I ' Jack , ' and Eliza ' Lizzie , ' and what I like the least of all , our pretty Annie ' Nancy . ' 
 ' that will be as God please , sir , ' I answer he , rather sharply ; ' and the horse that suffer will not be thine . but I wish to know when we must start upon our long travel to London town . I perceive that the matter be of great despatch and urgency . ' 
 ' to be sure , so it be , my son . but I see a yearling turkey there , he I mean with the hop in his walk , who   if I know aught of fowl   would roast well to - morrow . Thy mother must have preparation : it be no more than reasonable . now , have that turkey kill to - night   for his fatness make I long for he , and we will have he for dinner to - morrow , with , perhaps , one of his brother ; and a few more collop of red deer 's flesh for supper , and then on the Friday morning , with the grace of God , we will set our face to the road , upon his Majesty 's business . ' 
 ' Nay , but good sir , ' I ask with some trembling , so eager be I to see Lorna ; ' if his Majesty 's business will keep till Friday , may it not keep until Monday ? we have a litter of suck - pig , excellently choice and white , six week old , come Friday . there be too many for the sow , and one of they needeth roasting . think you not it would be a pity to leave the woman to carve it ? ' 
 ' my son Jack , ' reply Master Stickles , ' never be I in such quarter yet : and God forbid that I should be so unthankful to he as to hurry away . and now I think on it , Friday be not a day upon which pious people love to commence an enterprise . I will choose the young pig to - morrow at noon , at which time they be will not to gambol ; and we will celebrate his birthday by carve he on Friday . after that we will gird our loin , and set forth early on Saturday . ' 
 now this be little well to I than if we have set forth at once . Sunday be the very first day upon which it would be honourable for I to enter Glen Doone . but though I try every possible mean with Master Jeremy Stickles , offer he the choice for dinner of every beast that be on the farm , he durst not put off our departure later than the Saturday . and nothing else but love of we and of our hospitality would have so persuade he to remain with we till then . therefore now my only chance of see Lorna , before I go , lie in watch from the cliff and espy she , or a signal from she . 
 this , however , I do in vain , until my eye be weary and often would delude themselves with hope of what they ache for . but though I lie hide behind the tree upon the crest of the stony fall , and wait so quiet that the rabbit and squirrel play around I , and even the keen - eyed weasel take I for a trunk of wood it be all as one ; no cast of colour change the white stone , whose whiteness now be hateful to I ; nor do wreath or skirt of maiden break the loneliness of the vale . 




 CHAPTER XXIV 

 a safe pass for king 's MESSENGER 

 a journey to London seem to we in those bygone day as hazardous and dark an adventure as could be force on any man . I mean , of course , a poor man ; for to a great nobleman , with ever so many outrider , attendant , and retainer , the risk be not so great , unless the highwayman know of their come beforehand , and so combined against they . to a poor man , however , the risk be not so much from those gentleman of the road as from the more ignoble footpad , and the landlord of the less hostel , and the loose unguarded soldier , over and above the pitfall and the quagmire of the way ; so that it be hard to settle , at the first outgoing whether a man be wise to pray more for his neck or for his head . 
 but nowadays it be very different . not that highway - man be scarce , in this the reign of our good Queen Anne ; for in truth they thrive as well as ever , albeit they deserve it not , be less upright and courteous but that the road be much improve , and the grow use of stage - waggon   some of which will travel as much as forty mile in a summer day   have turn our ancient idea of distance almost upside down ; and I doubt whether God be pleased with our fly so fast away from he . however , that be not my business ; nor do it lie in my mouth to speak very strongly upon the subject , see how much I myself have do towards make of road upon Exmoor . 
 to return to my story   and , in truth , I lose that road too often , it would have take ten King 's messenger to get I away from Plover 's barrow without one goodbye to Lorna , but for my sense of the trust and reliance which his Majesty have repose in I . and now I feel most bitterly how the very arrangement which seem so wise , and indeed ingenious , may by the force of event become our most fatal obstacle . for lo ! I be block entirely from go to see Lorna ; whereas we should have fix it so that I as well might have the power of signal my necessity . 
 it be too late now to think of that ; and so I make up my mind at last to keep my honour on both side , both to the King and to the maiden , although I might lose everything except a heavy heart for it . and indeed , more heart than mine be heavy ; for when it come to the tug of parting , my mother be like , and so be Annie , to break down altogether . but I bade they be of good cheer , and smile in the brisk manner upon they , and say that I should be back next week as one of his Majesty 's great captain , and tell they not to fear I then . upon which they smile at the idea of ever be afraid of I , whatever dress I might have on ; and so I kiss my hand once more , and ride away very bravely . but bless your heart , I could no more have do so than fly all the way to London if Jeremy Stickles have not be there . 
 and not to take too much credit to myself in this matter , I must confess that when we be come to the turn in the road where the moor begin , and whence you see the last of the yard , and the rick and the poultry round they and can   by know the place   obtain a glance of the kitchen window under the walnut - tree , it go so hard with I just here that I even make pretence of a stone in ancient Smiler 's shoe , to dismount , and to bend my head awhile . then , know that those I have leave behind would be watch to see the last of I , and might have false hope of my come back , I mount again with all possible courage , and ride after Jeremy Stickles . 
 Jeremy , see how much I be down , do his good to keep I up with joke , and tale , and light discourse , until , before we have ride a league , I begin to long to see the thing he be describe . the air , the weather , and the thought of go to a wondrous place , add to the fine company at least so Jeremy say it be of a man who know all London , make I feel that I should be ungracious not to laugh a little . and be very simple then I laugh no more a little , but something quite considerable   though free from consideration   at the strange thing Master Stickles tell I , and his strange way of tell they . and so we become very excellent friend , for he be much pleased with my laughing . 
 not wish to thrust myself more forward than need be in this narrative , I have scarcely think it become or right to speak of my own adornment . but now , what with the brave clothe I have on , and the well one still that be pack up in the bag behind the saddle , it be almost beyond I to forbear say that I must have look very pleasing . and many a time I wish , go along , that Lorna could only be here and there , watch behind a furze - bush , look at I , and wonder how much my clothe have cost . for mother would have no stint in the matter , but have assemble at our house , immediately upon knowledge of what be to be about London , every man know to be a good stitcher upon our side of Exmoor . and for three day they have work their good , without stint of beer or cider , accord to the constitution of each . the result , so they all declare , be such as to create admiration , and defy competition in London . and to I it seem that they be quite right ; though Jeremy Stickles turn up his nose , and feign to be deaf in the business . 
 now be that matter as you please for the point be not worth argue certain it be that my appearance be well than it have be before . for be in the good clothe , one try to look and to act   so far as may be   up to the quality of they . not only for the fear of soil they , but that they enlarge a man 's perception of his value . and it strike I that our sin arise , partly from disdain of other , but mainly from contempt of self , both work the despite of God . but man of mind may not be measure by such paltry rule as this . 
 by dinner - time we arrive at Porlock , and din with my old friend , Master Pooke , now grow rich and portly . for though we have plenty of victual with we we be not to begin upon they , until all chance of victual among our friend be leave behind . and during that first day we have no need to meddle with our store at all ; for as have be settle before we leave home , we lay that night at Dunster in the house of a worthy tanner , first cousin to my mother , who receive we very cordially , and undertake to return old Smiler to his stable at Plover 's barrow , after one day 's rest . 
 Thence we hire to Bridgwater ; and from Bridgwater on to Bristowe , break the journey between the two . but although the whole way be so new to I , and such a perpetual source of conflict , that the remembrance still abide with I , as if it be but yesterday , I must not be so long in tell as it be in travel , or you will wish I far ; both because Lorna be nothing there , and also because a man in our neighbourhood have do the whole of it since my time , and feign to think nothing of it . however , one thing , in common justice to a person who have be traduce , I be bind to mention . and this be , that be two of we , and myself of such magnitude , we never could have make our journey without either fight or run , but for the free pass which dear Annie , by some mean   I know not what , have procure from Master Faggus . and when I let it be know , by some hap , that I be the own cousin of Tom Faggus , and honour with his society , there be not a house upon the road but be proud to entertain I , in spite of my fellow - traveller , bear the red badge of the King . 
 ' I will keep this close , my son Jack , ' he say , have strip it off with a carving - knife ; ' your flag be the good to fly . the man who starve I on the way down , the same shall feed I fat go home . ' 
 therefore we pursue our way , in excellent condition , have thrive upon the credit of that very popular highwayman , and be surround with regret that he have leave the profession , and sometimes beg to intercede that he might help the road again . for all the landlord on the road declare that now small ale be drunk , nor much of spirit call for , because the farmer need not prime to meet only common rider , neither be these worth the while to get drunk with afterwards . Master Stickles himself undertake , as an officer of the King 's Justices to plead this case with Squire Faggus   as everybody call he now , and to induce he , for the general good , to return to his proper ministry . 
 it be a long and weary journey , although the road be wondrous good on the farth side of Bristowe , and scarcely any man need be bogge , if he keep his eye well open , save , perhaps , in Berkshire . in consequence of the pass we have , and the vintner 's knowledge of it , we only meet two public rider , one of whom make off straightway when he see my companion 's pistol and the stout carbine I bear ; and the other come to a parley with we , and prove most kind and affable , when he know himself in the presence of the cousin of Squire Faggus . ' God save you , gentleman , ' he cry , lift his hat politely ; ' many and many a happy day I have work this road with he . such time will never be again . but commend I to his love and prayer . King my name be , and King my nature . say that , and none will harm you . ' and so he make off down the hill , be a perfect gentleman , and a very good horse he be rid . 
 the night be fall very thick by the time we be come to Tyburn , and here the King 's officer decide that it would be wise to halt , because the way be unsafe by night across the field to Charing village . I for my part be nothing loth , and prefer to see London by daylight . 
 and after all , it be not worth see , but a very hideous and dirty place , not at all like Exmoor . some of the shop be very fine , and the sign above they fine still , so that I be never weary of stand still to look at they . but in do this there be no ease ; for before one could begin almost to make out the meaning of they , either some of the wayfarer would bustle and scowl , and draw their sword , or the owner , or his apprentice boy , would rush out and catch hold of I , cry , ' buy , buy , buy ! what d'ye lack , what d'ye lack ? buy , buy , buy ! ' at first I mistook the meaning of this for so we pronounce the word ' boy ' upon Exmoor and I answer with some indignation , ' Sirrah , I be no boy now , but a man of one - and - twenty year ; and as for lack , I lack naught from thee , except what thou hast not good manner . ' 
 the only thing that please I much , be the river Thames , and the hall and church of Westminster , where there be brave thing to be see , and braver still to think about . but whenever I wander in the street , what with the noise the people make , the number of the coach , the running of the footman , the swaggering of great courtier , and the thrusting aside of everybody , many and many a time I long to be back among the sheep again , for fear of lose temper . they be welcome to the wall for I , as I take care to tell they , for I could stand without the wall , which perhaps be more than they could do . though I say this with the good intention , mean no discourtesy , some of they be vex at it ; and one young lord , be flush with drink , draw his sword and make at I . but I strike it up with my holly stick , so that it fly on the roof of a house , then I take he by the belt with one hand , and lay he in the kennel . this cause some little disturbance ; but none of the rest see fit to try how the matter might be with they . 
 now this be the year of our Lord 1683 , more than nine year and a half since the death of my father , and the beginning of this history , all London be in a great ferment about the dispute between the Court of the King and the City . the King , or rather perhaps his party   for they say that his Majesty care for little except to have plenty of money and spend it , be quite resolve to be supreme in the appointment of the chief officer of the corporation . but the citizen maintain that   under their charter   this right lie entirely with themselves ; upon which a writ be issue against they for forfeiture of their charter ; and the question be now be try in the court of his Majesty 's bench . 
 this seem to occupy all the attention of the judge , and my case   which have appear so urgent   be put off from time to time , while the Court and the City contend . and so hot be the conflict and hate between they , that a sheriff have be fin by the King in 100,000 pound , and a former lord mayor have even be sentence to the pillory , because he would not swear falsely . hence the courtier and the citizen scarce could meet in the street with patience , or without rail and frequent blow . 
 now although I hear so much of this matter , for nothing else be talk of , and it seem to I more important even than the churchwardenship of Oare , I could not for the life of I tell which side I should take to . for all my sense of position , and of confidence repose in I , and of my father 's opinion , lie heavily in one scale , while all my reason and my heart go down plump against injustice , and seem to win the other scale . even so my father have be , at the breaking out of the civil war , when he be less than my age now , and even less skilled in politic ; and my mother tell I after this , when she see how I myself be doubting , and vex with myself for do so , that my father use to thank God often that he have not be call upon to take one side or other , but might remain obscure and quiet . and yet he always consider himself to be a good , sound Royalist . 
 but now as I stay there , only desirous to be hear and to get away , and scarcely even guess yet what be want of I   for even Jeremy Stickles know not , or pretend not to know , thing come to a dreadful pass between the King and all the people who dare to have an opinion . for about the middle of June , the judge give their sentence , that the City of London have forfeit its charter , and that its franchise should be take into the hand of the king . Scarcely be this judgment forth , and all man hotly talk of it , when a far bad thing befell . news of some great conspiracy be spread at every corner , and that a man in the malting business have try to take up the brewer 's work , and lop the King and the Duke of York . everybody be shock at this , for the King himself be not dislike so much as his adviser ; but everybody be more than shocked , grieve indeed to the heart with pain , at hear that Lord William Russell and Mr. Algernon Sidney have be seize and send to the Tower of London , upon a charge of high treason . 
 have no knowledge of these great man , nor of the matter how far it be true , I have not very much to say about either of they or it ; but this silence be not share   although the ignorance may have be   by the hundred of people around I . such a commotion be astir , such universal sense of wrong , and stern resolve to right it , that each man grasp his fellow 's hand , and lead he into the vintner 's . even I , although at that time give to excess in temperance , and afraid of the name of cordial , be hard set   I do assure you   not to be drunk at interval without coarse discourtesy . 
 however , that   as Betty Muxworthy use to say , when argue down , and ready to take the mop for it   be neither here nor there . I have naught to do with great history and be sorry for those who have to write it ; because they be sure to have both friend and enemy in it , and can not act as they would towards they , without damage to their own conscience . 
 but as great event draw little one , and the rattle of the churn decide the uncertainty of the fly , so this movement of the town , and eloquence , and passion have more than I guess at the time , to do with my own little fortune . for in the first place it be fix   perhaps from down right contumely , because the citizen love he so   that Lord Russell should be try neither at Westminster nor at Lincoln 's Inn , but at the Court of Old Bailey , within the precinct of the city . this keep I hang on much long ; because although the good nobleman be to be try by the Court of Common Pleas , yet the officer of King 's Bench , to whom I daily apply myself , be in counsel with their fellow , and put I off from day to day . 
 now I have hear of the law 's delay , which the great of all great poet   know much of the law himself , as indeed of everything   have specially mention , when not expect , among the many ill of life . but I never think at my year to have such bitter experience of the evil ; and it seem to I that if the lawyer fail to do their duty , they ought to pay people for wait upon they , instead of make they pay for it . but here I be , now in the second month live at my own charge in the house of a worthy fellmonger at the sign of the Seal and Squirrel , abut upon the Strand road which lead from Temple Bar to Charing . here I do very well indeed , have a mattress of good skin - dressing , and plenty to eat every day of my life , but the butter be something to cry ' but ' thrice at   accord to a conceit of our school day , and the milk must have come from cow drive to water . however , these evil be light compare with the heavy bill send up to I every Saturday afternoon ; and know how my mother have pinch to send I nobly to London , and have tell I to spare for nothing , but live bravely with the good of they , the tear very nearly come into my eye , as I think , while I eat , of so rob she . 
 at length , be quite at the end of my money , and see no other help for it , I determine to listen to clerk no more , but force my way up to the Justices , and insist upon be hear by they , or discharge from my recognisance . for so they have term the bond or deed which I have be force to execute , in the presence of a chief clerk or notary , the very day after I come to London . and the purport of it be , that on pain of a heavy fine or escheatment , I would hold myself ready and present , to give evidence when call upon . have deliver I up to sign this , Jeremy Stickles be quit of I , and go upon other business , not but what he be kind and good to I , when his time and pursuit allow of it . 




 CHAPTER XXV 

 a great MAN ATTENDS to BUSINESS 

 have see Lord Russell murder in the field of Lincoln 's Inn , or rather have go to see it , but turn away with a sickness and a bitter flood of tear for a whiter and a nobler neck never fall before low beast I stride away towards Westminster , cure of half my indignation at the death of Charles the First . many people hurry past I , chiefly of the more tender sort , revolt at the butchery . in their ghastly face , as they turn they back , lest the sight should be come after they , great sorrow be to be see , and horror , and pity , and some anger . 
 in Westminster Hall I find nobody ; not even the crowd of crawl varlet , who use to be crave evermore for employment or for payment . I knock at three door , one after other , of lobby go out of it , where I have formerly see some officer and people press in and out , but for my trouble I take nothing , except some thump from echo . and at last an old man tell I that all the lawyer be go to see the result of their own work , in the field of Lincoln 's Inn . 
 however , in a few day ' time , I have well fortune ; for the court be sit and full of business , to clear off the arrear of work , before the lawyer ' holiday . as I be wait in the hall for a good occasion , a man with horsehair on his head , and a long blue bag in his left hand , touch I gently on the arm , and lead I into a quiet place . I follow he very gladly , be confident that he come to I with a message from the Justiciaries . but after take pain to be sure that none could overhear we , he turn on I suddenly , and ask , 
 ' now , John , how be your dear mother ? ' 
 ' worshipful sir ' I answer he , after recover from my surprise at his knowledge of our affair , and kindly interest in they , ' it be two month now since I have see she . would to God that I only know how she be fare now , and how the business of the farm go ! ' 
 ' Sir , I respect and admire you , ' the old gentleman reply , with a bow very low and genteel ; ' few young court - gallant of our time be so reverent and dutiful . oh , how I do love my mother ! ' here he turn up his eye to heaven , in a manner that make I feel for he and yet with a kind of wonder . 
 ' I be very sorry for you , sir , ' I answer most respectfully , not mean to trespass on his grief , yet wonder at his mother 's age ; for he seem to be at least threescore ; ' but I be no court - gallant , sir ; I be only a farmer 's son , and learn how to farm a little . ' 
 ' Enough , John ; quite enough , ' he cry , ' I can read it in thy countenance . Honesty be write there , and courage and simplicity . but I fear that , in this town of London , thou art apt to be take in by people of no principle . ah I ! ah I ! the world be bad , and I be too old to improve it . ' 
 then find he so good and kind , and anxious to improve the age , I tell he almost everything ; how much I pay the fellmonger , and all the thing I have be to see ; and how I long to get away , before the corn be ripen ; yet how   despite of these desire   I feel myself bind to walk up and down , be under a thing call ' recognisance . ' in short , I tell he everything ; except the nature of my summon   which I have no right to tell , and that I be out of money . 
 my tale be tell in a little archway , apart from other lawyer ; and the other lawyer seem to I to shift themselves , and to look askew , like sheep through a hurdle , when the rest be feed . 
 ' what ! good God ! ' my lawyer cry , smite his breast indignantly with a roll of something learn ; ' in what country do we live ? under what law be we govern ? no case before the court whatever ; no primary deposition , so far as we be furnish ; not even a King 's writ issue and here we have a fine young man drag from his home and adore mother , during the height of agriculture , at his own cost and charge ! I have hear of many grievance ; but this the very bad of all . nothing short of a Royal Commission could be warranty for it . this be not only illegal , sir , but most gravely unconstitutional . ' 
 ' I have not tell you , worthy sir , ' I answer he , in a low tone , ' if I could have think that your sense of right would be move so painfully . but now I must beg to leave you , sir for I see that the door again be open . I beg you , worshipful sir , to accept ' 
 upon this he put forth his hand and say , ' nay , nay , my son , not two , not two : ' yet look away , that he might not scare I . 
 ' to accept , kind sir , my very good thank , and most respectful remembrance . ' and with that , I lay my hand in his .
