' and if , sir , any circumstance of business or of pleasure should bring you to our part of the world , I trust you will not forget that my mother and myself   if ever I get home again   will do our good to make you comfortable with our poor hospitality . ' 
 with this I be haste away from he , but he hold my hand and look round at I . and he speak without cordiality . 
 ' young man , a general invitation be no entry for my fee book . I have spend a good hour of business - time in master thy case , and state my opinion of it . and be a member of the bar , call six - and - thirty year agone by the honourable society of the Inner Temple , my fee be at my own discretion ; albeit an honorarium . for the honour of the profession , and my position in it , I ought to charge thee at least five guinea , although I would have accept one , offer with good will and delicacy . now I will enter it two , my son , and half a crown for my clerk 's fee . ' 
 say this , he draw forth from his deep , blue bag , a red book have clasp to it , and endorse in gold letter ' fee - book ' ; and before I could speak   be frighten so   he have enter on a page of it , ' to consideration of ease as state by John Ridd , and advise thereupon , two guineas . ' 
 ' but sir , good sir , ' I stammer forth , not have two guineas leave in the world , yet grieve to confess it , ' I know not that I be to pay , learn sir . I never think of it in that way . ' 
 ' wound of God ! in what way think you that a lawyer listen to your rigmarole ? ' 
 ' I think that you listen from kindness , sir , and compassion of my grievous case , and a sort of like for I . ' 
 ' a lawyer like thee , young curmudgeon ! a lawyer afford to feel compassion gratis ! either thou art a very deep knave , or the greenest of all greenhorn . well , I suppose , I must let thee off for one guinea , and the clerk 's fee . a bad business , a shocking business ! ' 
 now , if this man have continue kind and soft , as when he hear my story , I would have pawn my clothe to pay he , rather than leave a debt behind , although contract unwittingly . but when he use harsh language so , know that I do not deserve it , I begin to doubt within myself whether he deserve my money . therefore I answer he with some readiness , such as come sometimes to I , although I be so slow . 
 ' Sir , I be no curmudgeon : if a young man have call I so , it would not have be well with he . this money shall be pay , if due , albeit I have no desire to incur the debt . you have advise I that the Court be liable for my expense , so far as they be reasonable . if this be a reasonable expense , come with I now to Lord Justice Jeffreys , and receive from he the two guineas , or   it may be   five , for the counsel you have give I to deny his jurisdiction . ' with these word , I take his arm to lead he , for the door be open still . 
 ' in the name of God , boy , let I go . Worthy sir , pray let I go . my wife be sick , and my daughter die in the name of God , sir , let I go . ' 
 ' Nay , nay , ' I say , have fast hold of he , ' I can not let thee go unpaid , sir . right be right ; and thou shalt have it . ' 
 ' ruin be what I shall have , boy , if you drag I before that devil . he will strike I from the bar at once , and starve I , and all my family . here , lad , good lad , take these two guineas . Thou hast despoil the spoiler . never again will I trust mine eye for knowledge of a greenhorn . ' 
 he slip two guineas into the hand which I have hook through his elbow , and speak in an urgent whisper again , for the people come crowd around we ' for God 's sake let I go , boy ; another moment will be too late . ' 
 ' learn sir , ' I answer he , ' twice you speak , unless I err , of the necessity of a clerk 's fee , as a thing to be lament . ' 
 ' to be sure , to be sure , my son . you have a clerk as much as I have . there it be . now I pray thee , take to the study of the law . possession be nine point of it , which thou hast of I . self - possession be the tenth , and that thou hast more than the other nine . ' 
 be flatter by this , and by the feeling of the two guineas and half - crown , I drop my hold upon Counsellor Kitch   for he be no less a man than that , and he be out of sight in a second of time , wig , blue bag , and family . and before I have time to make up my mind what I should do with his money   for of course I mean not to keep it   the crier of the Court   as they tell I   come out , and want to know who I be . I tell he , as shortly as I could , that my business lie with his Majesty 's bench , and be very confidential ; upon which he take I inside with warning , and show I to an under - clerk , who show I to a high one , and the high clerk to the head one . 
 when this gentleman understand all about my business   which I tell he without complaint   he frown at I very heavily , as if I have do he an injury . 
 ' John Ridd , ' he ask I with a stern glance , ' be it your deliberate desire to be bring into the presence of the Lord Chief Justice ? ' 
 ' surely , sir , it have be my desire for the last two month and more . ' 
 ' then , John , thou shalt be . but mind one thing , not a word of thy long detention , or thou mayst get into trouble . ' 
 ' how , sir ? for be detain against my own wish ? ' I ask he ; but he turn away , as if that matter be not worth his arguing , as , indeed , I suppose it be not , and lead I through a little passage to a door with a curtain across it . 
 ' now , if my Lord cross - question you , ' the gentleman whisper to I , ' answer he straight out truth at once , for he will have it out of thee . and mind , he love not to be contradict , neither can he bear a hang - dog look . take little heed of the other two ; but note every word of the middle one ; and never make he speak twice . ' 
 I thank he for his good advice , as he move the curtain and thrust I in , but instead of enter withdraw , and leave I to bear the brunt of it . 
 the chamber be not very large , though lofty to my eye , and dark , with wooden panel round it . at the further end be some raise seat , such as I have see in church , line with velvet , and have broad elbow , and a canopy over the middle seat . there be only three man sit here , one in the centre , and one on each side ; and all three be do up wonderfully with fur , and robe of state , and curl of thick gray horsehair , crimp and gather , and plait down to their shoulder . each man have an oak desk before he , set at a little distance , and spread with pen and paper . instead of writing , however , they seem to be laugh and talk , or rather the one in the middle seem to be tell some good story , which the other receive with approval . by reason of their great peruke it be hard to tell how old they be ; but the one who be speak seem the young , although he be the chief of they . a thick - set , burly , and bulky man , with a blotchy broad face , and great square jaw , and fierce eye full of blaze ; he be one to be dread by gentle soul , and to be abhor by the noble . 
 between I and the three lord judge , some few lawyer be gather up bag and paper and pen and so forth , from a narrow table in the middle of the room , as if a case have be dispose of , and no other be call on . but before I have time to look round twice , the stout fierce man espy I , and shout out with a flashing stare ' 
 ' how now , countryman , who art thou ? ' 
 ' may it please your worship , ' I answer he loudly , ' I be John Ridd , of Oare parish , in the shire of Somerset , bring to this London , some two month back by a special messenger , whose name be Jeremy Stickles ; and then bind over to be at hand and ready , when call upon to give evidence , in a matter unknown to I , but touch the peace of our lord the King , and the well - being of his subject . three time I have meet our lord the King , but he hath say nothing about his peace , and only hold it towards I , and every day , save Sunday , I have walk up and down the great hall of Westminster , all the business part of the day , expect to be call upon , yet no one hath call upon I . and now I desire to ask your worship , whether I may go home again ? ' 
 ' well , do , John , ' reply his lordship , while I be pant with all this speech ; ' I will go bail for thee , John , thou hast never make such a long speech before ; and thou art a spunky Briton , or thou couldst not have make it now . I remember the matter well , and I myself will attend to it , although it arise before my time ' he be but newly Chief Justice ' but I can not take it now , John . there be no fear of lose thee , John , any more than the Tower of London . I grieve for his Majesty 's exchequer , after keep thee two month or more . ' 
 ' Nay , my lord , I crave your pardon . my mother hath be keep I . not a groat have I receive . ' 
 ' Spank , be it so ? ' his lordship cry , in a voice that shake the cobwebs , and the frown on his brow shake the heart of man , and mine as much as the rest of they , ' Spank , be his Majesty come to this , that he starve his own approver ? ' 
 ' my lord , my lord , ' whisper Mr. Spank , the chief - officer of evidence , ' the thing hath be overlook , my lord , among such grave matter of treason . ' 
 ' I will overlook thy head , foul Spank , on a spike from Temple Bar , if ever I hear of the like again . vile varlet , what art thou pay for ? Thou hast swindle the money thyself , foul Spank ; I know thee , though thou art new to I . Bitter be the day for thee that ever I come across thee . answer I not one word more and I will have thee on a hurdle . ' and he swing himself to and fro on his bench , with both hand on his knee ; and every man wait to let it pass , know well than to speak to he . 
 ' John Ridd , ' say the Lord Chief Justice , at last recover a sort of dignity , yet dare Spank from the corner of his eye to do so much as look at he , ' thou hast be shamefully use , John Ridd . answer I not boy ; not a word ; but go to Master Spank , and let I know how he behave to thee ; ' here he make a glance at Spank , which be worth at least ten pound to I ; ' be thou here again to - morrow , and before any other case be take , I will see justice do to thee . now be off boy ; thy name be Ridd , and we be well rid of thee . ' 
 I be only too glad to go , after all this temp ; as you may well suppose . for if ever I see a man 's eye become two hole for the devil to glare from , I see it that day ; and the eye be those of the Lord Chief Justice Jeffreys . 
 Mr. Spank be in the lobby before I , and before I have recover myself for I be vex with my own terror he come up sidle and fawn to I , with a heavy bag of yellow leather . 
 ' Good Master Ridd , take it all , take it all , and say a good word for I to his lordship . he hath take a strange fancy to thee ; and thou must make the most of it . we never see man meet he eye to eye so , and yet not contradict he , and that be just what he loveth . abide in London , Master Ridd , and he will make thy fortune . his joke upon thy name prove that . and I pray you remember , Master Ridd , that the spank be sixteen in family . ' 
 but I would not take the bag from he , regard it as a sort of bribe to pay I such a lump of money , without so much as ask how great have be my expense . therefore I only tell he that if he would kindly keep the cash for I until the morrow , I would spend the rest of the day in count   which always be sore work with I   how much it have stand I in board and lodging , since Master Stickles have render I up ; for until that time he have bear my expense . in the morning I would give Mr. Spank a memorandum , duly sign , and attest by my landlord , include the breakfast of that day , and in exchange for this I would take the exact amount from the yellow bag , and be very thankful for it . 
 ' if that be thy way of use opportunity , ' say Spank , look at I with some contempt , ' thou wilt never thrive in these time , my lad . even the Lord Chief Justice can be little help to thee ; unless thou know well than that how to help thyself . ' 
 it matter not to I . the word ' approver ' stuck in my gorge , as use by the Lord Chief Justice ; for we look upon an approver as a very low thing indeed . I would rather pay for every breakfast , and even every dinner , eat by I since here I come , than take money as an approver . and indeed I be much disappointed at be take in that light , have understand that I be send for as a trusty subject , and humble friend of his Majesty . 
 in the morning I meet Mr. Spank wait for I at the entrance , and very desirous to see I . I show he my bill , make out in fair copy , and he laugh at it , and say , ' take it twice over , Master Ridd ; once for thine own sake , and once for his Majesty 's ; as all his loyal tradesman do , when they can get any . his Majesty know and be proud of it , for it show their love of his countenance ; and he say , " bis dat qui cito dat , " then how can I grumble at give twice , when I give so slowly ? ' 
 ' Nay , I will take it but once , ' I say ; ' if his Majesty love to be rob , he need not lack of his desire , while the spank be sixteen in family . ' 
 the clerk smile cheerfully at this , be proud of his child 's ability ; and then have pay my account , he whisper , 
 ' he be all alone this morning , John , and in rare good humour . he hath be promise the handling of poor Master Algernon Sidney , and he say he will soon make republic of he ; for his state shall shortly be headless . he be chuckle over his joke , like a pig with a nut ; and that always make he pleasant . John Ridd , my lord ! ' with that he swing up the curtain bravely , and accord to special order , I stand , face to face , and alone with Judge Jeffreys . 




 CHAPTER XXVI 

 JOHN be drained and CAST ASIDE 

 his lordship be busy with some letter , and do not look up for a minute or two , although he know that I be there . meanwhile I stand wait to make my bow ; afraid to begin upon he , and wonder at his great bull - head . then he close his letter , well - pleased with their import , and fix his bold broad stare on I , as if I be an oyster open , and he would know how fresh I be . 
 ' may it please your worship , ' I say , ' here I be accord to order , await your good pleasure . ' 
 ' thou art make to weight , John , more than order . how much dost thou tip the scale to ? ' 
 ' only twelvescore pound , my lord , when I be in wrestle trim . and sure I must have lose weight here , fret so long in London . ' 
 ' ha , ha ! much fret be there in thee ! hath his Majesty see thee ? ' 
 ' yes , my lord , twice or even thrice ; and he make some jest concern I . ' 
 ' a very bad one , I doubt not . his humour be not so dainty as mine , but apt to be coarse and unmannerly . now John , or Jack , by the look of thee , thou art more use to be call . ' 
 ' yes , your worship , when I be with old Molly and Betty Muxworthy . ' 
 ' peace , thou forward varlet ! there be a deal too much of thee . we shall have to try short common with thee , and thou art a very long common . ha , ha ! where be that rogue Spank ? Spank must hear that by - and - by . it be beyond thy great thick head , Jack . ' 
 ' not so , my lord ; I have be at school , and have very bad joke make upon I . ' 
 ' ha , ha ! it hath hit thee hard . and faith , it would be hard to miss thee , even with harpoon . and thou lookest like to blubber , now . Capital , in faith ! I have thee on every side , Jack , and thy side be manifold ; many - fold at any rate . thou shalt have double expense , Jack , for the wit thou hast provoke in I . ' 
 ' heavy good lack heavy payment , be a proverb down our way , my lord . ' 
 ' ah , I hurt thee , I hurt thee , Jack . the harpoon hath no tickle for thee . now , Jack Whale , have haul thee hard , we will proceed to examine thee . ' here all his manner be change , and he look with his heavy brow bent upon I , as if he have never laugh in his life , and would allow none else to do so . 
 ' I be ready to answer , my lord , ' I reply , ' if he ask I nought beyond my knowledge , or beyond my honour . ' 
 ' hadst well answer I everything , lump . what hast thou to do with honour ? now be there in thy neighbourhood a certain nest of robber , miscreant , and outlaws , whom all man fear to handle ? ' 
 ' yes , my lord . at least , I believe some of they be robber , and all of they be outlaw . ' 
 ' and what be your high sheriff about , that he doth not hang they all ? or send they up for I to hang , without more to do about they ? ' 
 ' I reckon that he be afraid , my lord ; it be not safe to meddle with they . they be of good birth , and reckless ; and their place be very strong . ' 
 ' good birth ! what be Lord Russell of , Lord Essex , and this Sidney ? ' tis the sure heirship to the block to be the chip of a good one . what be the name of this pestilent race , and how many of they be there ? ' 
 ' they be the Doones of Bagworthy forest , may it please your worship . and we reckon there be about forty of they , beside the woman and child . ' 
 ' Forty Doones , all forty thief ! and woman and child ! thunder of God ! how long have they be there then ? ' 
 ' they may have be there thirty year , my lord ; and indeed they may have be forty . before the great war break out they come , long back than I can remember . ' 
 ' ay , long before thou wast bear , John . good , thou speakest plainly . woe betide a liar , whenso I get hold of he . ye want I on the Western Circuit ; by God , and ye shall have I , when London traitor be spin and swung . there be a family call De Whichehalse live very nigh thee , John ? ' 
 this he say in a sudden manner , as if to take I off my guard , and fix his great thick eye on I . and in truth I be much astonish . 
 ' yes , my lord , there be . at least , not so very far from we . Baron de Whichehalse , of Ley Manor . ' 
 ' Baron , ha ! of the Exchequer eh , lad ? and taketh due instead of his Majesty . somewhat which halt there ought to come a little far , I trow . it shall be see to , as well as the witch which make it so to halt . riotous knave in West England , drunken outlaw , you shall dance , if ever I play pipe for you . John Ridd , I will come to Oare parish , and rout out the Oare of Babylon . ' 
 ' although your worship be so learn , ' I answer see that now he be begin to make thing uneasy ; ' your worship , though be Chief Justice , do little justice to we . we be downright good and loyal folk ; and I have not see , since here I come to this great town of London , any who may better we , or even come anigh we , in honesty , and goodness , and duty to our neighbour . for we be very quiet folk , not prate our own virtue ' 
 ' enough , good John , enough ! know thou not that modesty be the maidenhood of virtue , lose even by her own approval ? now hast thou ever hear or think that De Whichehalse be in league with the Doones of Bagworthy ? ' 
 say these word rather slowly , he skewer his great eye into mine , so that I could not think at all , neither look at he , nor yet away . the idea be so new to I that it set my wit all wander ; and look into I , he see that I be grope for the truth . 
 ' John Ridd , thine eye be enough for I . I see thou hast never dream of it . now hast thou ever see a man whose name be Thomas Faggus ? ' 
 ' yes , sir , many and many a time . he be my own worthy cousin ; and I fear he that hath intention ' here I stop , have no right there to speak about our Annie . 
 ' Tom Faggus be a good man , ' he say ; and his great square face have a smile which show I he have meet my cousin ; ' Master Faggus hath make mistake as to the title to property , as lawyer oftentime may do ; but take he all for all , he be a thoroughly straightforward man ; present his bill , and have it pay , and make no charge for draw it . nevertheless , we must tax his cost , as of any other solicitor . ' 
 ' to be sure , to be sure , my lord ! ' be all that I could say , not understand what all this mean . 
 ' I fear he will come to the gallow , ' say the Lord Chief Justice , sink his voice below the echo ; ' tell he this from I , Jack . he shall never be condemn before I ; but I can not be everywhere , and some of our Justices may keep short memory of his dinner . tell he to change his name , turn parson , or do something else , to make it wrong to hang he . Parson be the good thing , he hath such command of feature , and he might take his tithe on horseback . now a few more thing , John Ridd ; and for the present I have do with thee . ' 
 all my heart leap up at this , to get away from London so : and yet I could hardly trust to it . 
 ' be there any sound round your way of disaffection to his Majesty , his most gracious Majesty ? ' 
 ' no , my lord : no sign whatever . we pray for he in church perhaps , and we talk about he afterwards , hop it may do he good , as it be intend . but after that we have naught to say , not know much about he at least till I get home again . ' 
 ' that be as it should be , John . and the less you say the well . but I have hear of thing in Taunton , and even near to you in Dulverton , and even nigh still upon Exmoor ; thing which be of the pillory kind , and even more of the gallow . I see that you know naught of they . nevertheless , it will not be long before all England hear of they . now , John , I have take a liking to thee , for never man tell I the truth , without fear or favour , more thoroughly and truly than thou hast do . keep thou clear of this , my son . it will come to nothing ; yet many shall swing high for it . even I could not save thee , John Ridd , if thou wert mix in this affair . keep from the Doones , keep from De Whichehalse , keep from everything which lead beyond the sight of thy knowledge . I mean to use thee as my tool ; but I see thou art too honest and simple . I will send a sharp down ; but never let I find thee , John , either a tool for the other side , or a tube for my word to pass through . ' 
 here the Lord Justice give I such a glare that I wish myself well rid of he , though thankful for his warning ; and see how he have make upon I a long abide mark of fear , he smile again in a jocular manner , and say , 
 ' now , get thee go , Jack . I shall remember thee ; and I trow , thou wilt'st not for many a day forget I . ' 
 ' my lord , I be never so glad to go ; for the hay must be in , and the rick unthatche , and none of they can make spar like I , and two man to twist every hay - rope , and mother think it all right , and listen right and leave to lie , and cheat at every pig she kill , and even the skin of the sheep to go ' 
 ' John Ridd , I think none could come nigh your folk in honesty , and goodness , and duty to their neighbour ! ' 
 ' sure enough , my lord ; but by our folk , I mean ourselves , not the man nor woman neither ' 
 ' that will do , John . go thy way . not man , nor woman neither , be well than they need be . ' 
 I wish to set this matter right ; but his worship would not hear I , and only drive I out of court , say that man be thief and liar , no more in one place than another , but all alike all over the world , and woman not far behind they . it be not for I to dispute this point   though I be not yet persuade of it , both because my lord be a Judge , and must know more about it , and also that be a man myself I might seem to be defend myself in an unbecoming manner . therefore I make a low bow , and go ; in doubt as to which have the right of it . 
 but though he have so far dismiss I , I be not yet quite free to go , inasmuch as I have not money enough to take I all the way to Oare , unless indeed I should go afoot , and beg my sustenance by the way , which seem to be below I . therefore I get my few clothe pack , and my few debt pay , all ready to start in half an hour , if only they would give I enough to set out upon the road with . for I doubt not , be young and strong , that I could walk from London to Oare in ten day or in twelve at most , which be not much long than horse - work ; only I have be a fool , as you will say when you hear it . for after receive from Master Spank the amount of the bill which I have deliver less indeed by fifty shilling than the money my mother have give I , for I have spend fifty shilling , and more , in see the town and treat people , which I could not charge to his Majesty I have first pay all my debt thereout , which be not very many , and then suppose myself to be an established creditor of the Treasury for my come need , and already scent the country air , and foresee the joy of my mother , what have I do but spend half my balance , ay and more than three - quarter of it , upon present for mother , and Annie , and Lizzie , John Fry , and his wife , and Betty Muxworthy , Bill Dadds , Jim Slocombe , and , in a word , half of the rest of the people at Oare , include all the Snowe family , who must have thing good and handsome ? and if I must while I be about it , hide nothing from those who read I , I have actually buy for Lorna a thing the price of which quite frighten I , till the shopkeeper say it be nothing at all , and that no young man , with a lady to love he , could dare to offer her rubbish , such as the Jew sell across the way . now the mere idea of beautiful Lorna ever love I , which he talk about as patly   though of course I never mention she   as if it be a settled thing , and he know all about it , that mere idea so drive I abroad , that if he have ask three time as much , I could never have count the money . 
 now in all this I be a fool of course not for remember my friend and neighbour , which a man have a right to do , and indeed be bind to do , when he come from London but for not be certify first what cash I have to go on with . and to my great amazement , when I go with another bill for the victual of only three day more , and a week 's expense on the homeward road reckon very narrowly , Master Spank not only refuse to grant I any interview , but send I out a piece of blue paper , look like a butcher 's ticket , and bear these word and no more , ' John Ridd , go to the devil . he who will not when he may , when he will , he shall have nay . ' from this I conclude that I have lose favour in the sight of Chief Justice Jeffreys . perhaps because my evidence have not prove of any value ! perhaps because he mean to let the matter lie , till cast on he . 
 anyhow , it be a reason of much grief , and some anger to I , and very great anxiety , disappointment , and suspense . for here be the time of the hay go past , and the harvest of small corn come on , and the trout now rise at the yellow Sally , and the blackbird eat our white - heart cherry   I be sure , though I could not see they , and who be to do any good for mother , or stop she from weep continually ? and more than this , what be become of Lorna ? perhaps she have cast I away altogether , as a flouter and a changeling ; perhaps she have drown herself in the black well ; perhaps   and that be bad of all   she be even married , child as she be , to that vile Carver Doone , if the Doones ever care about marry ! that last thought send I down at once to watch for Mr. Spank again , resolve that if I could catch he , spank he I would to a pretty good tune , although sixteen in family . 
 however , there be no such thing as to find he ; and the usher vow   have order I doubt   that he be go to the sea for the good of his health , have sadly overwork himself ; and that none but a poor devil like himself , who never have handling of money , would stay in London this foul , hot weather ; which be likely to bring the plague with it . here be another new terror for I , who have hear of the plague of London , and the horrible thing that happen ; and so go back to my lodging at once , I open my clothe and seek for spot , especially as be so long at a hairy fellmonger 's ; but find none , I fall down and thank God for that same , and vow to start for Oare to - morrow , with my carbine load , come weal come woe , come sun come shower ; though all the parish should laugh at I , for beg my way home again , after the brave thing say of my going , as if I have be the King 's cousin . 
 but I be save in some degree from this lowering of my pride , and what matter more , of mother 's ; for go to buy with my last crown - piece   after all demand be pay   a little shot and powder , more needful on the road almost than even shoe or victual , at the corner of the street I meet my good friend Jeremy Stickles , newly come in search of I . I take he back to my little room mine at least till to - morrow morning and tell he all my story , and how much I feel aggrieve by it . but he surprise I very much , by show no surprise at all . 
 ' it be the way of the world , Jack . they have get all they can from thee , and why should they feed thee far ? we feed not a dead pig , I trow , but baste he well with brine and rue . Nay , we do not victual he upon the day of killing ; which they have do to thee . thou art a lucky man , John ; thou hast get one day 's wage , or at any rate half a day , after thy work be render . God have mercy on I , John ! the thing I see be manifold ; and so be my regard of they . what use to insist on this , or make a special point of that , or hold by something say of old , when a different mood be on ? I tell thee , Jack , all man be liar ; and he be the least one who press not too hard on they for lie . ' 
 this be all quite dark to I , for I never look at thing like that , and never would own myself a liar , not at least to other people , nor even to myself , although I might to God sometimes , when trouble be upon I . and if it come to that , no man have any right to be call a ' liar ' for smooth over thing unwitting , through duty to his neighbour . 
 ' five pound thou shalt have , Jack , ' say Jeremy Stickles suddenly , while I be all abroad with myself as to be a liar or not ; ' five pound , and I will take my chance of wring it from that great rogue Spank . ten I would have make it , John , but for bad luck lately . put back your bit of paper , lad ; I will have no acknowledgment . John Ridd , no nonsense with I ! ' 
 for I be ready to kiss his hand , to think that any man in London   the mean and most suspicious place , upon all God 's earth   should trust I with five pound , without even a receipt for it ! it overcome I so that I sob ; for , after all , though big in body , I be but a child at heart . it be not the five pound that move I , but the way of give it ; and after so much bitter talk , the great trust in my goodness . 




 CHAPTER XXVII 

 HOME again AT last 

 it be the beginning of wheat - harvest , when I come to Dunster town , have walk all the way from London , and be somewhat footsore . for though five pound be enough to keep I in food and lodge upon the road , and leave I many a shilling to give to far poor traveller , it would have be nothing for horse - hire , as I know too well by the price Jeremy Stickles have pay upon our way to London . now I never see a pretty town than Dunster look that evening ; for sooth to say , I have almost lose all hope of reach it that night , although the castle be long in view . but be once there , my trouble be go , at least as regard wayfaring ; for mother 's cousin , the worthy tanner   with whom we have sleep on the way to London , be in such indignation at the plight in which I come back to he , afoot , and weary , and almost shoeless not to speak of upper thing that he swear then , by the mercy of God , that if the scheme abrewe round he , against those bloody Papists , should come to any head or shape , and show good chance of succeed , he would risk a thousand pound , as though it be a penny . 
 I tell he not to do it , because I have hear otherwise , but be not at liberty to tell one - tenth of what I know , and indeed have see in London town . but of this he take no heed , because I only nod at he ; and he could not make it out . for it take an old man , or at least a middle - aged one , to nod and wink , with any power on the brain of other man . however , I think I make he know that the bad state in which I come to his town , and the great shame I have work for he among the folk round the card - table at the Luttrell Arms , be not to be , even there , attribute to King Charles the Second , nor even to his counsellor , but to my own speed of travelling , which have beat post - horse . for be much distraught in mind , and desperate in body , I have make all the way from London to Dunster in six day , and no more . it may be one hundred and seventy mile , I can not tell to a furlong or two , especially as I lose my way more than a dozen time ; but at any rate there in six day I be , and most kindly they receive I . the tanner have some excellent daughter , I forget how many ; very pretty damsel , and well set up , and able to make good pastry . but though they ask I many question , and make a sort of lord of I , and offer to darn my stocking   which in truth require it , I fall asleep in the midst of they , although I would not acknowledge it ; and they say , ' poor cousin ! he be weary ' , and lead I to a blessed bed , and kiss I all round like swan 's down . 
 in the morning all the Exmoor hill , the thought of which have frighten I at the end of each day 's travel , seem no more than bushel to I , as I look forth the bedroom window , and thank God for the sight of they . and even so , I have not to climb they , at least by my own labour . for my most worthy uncle   as we oft call a parent 's cousin , find it impossible to keep I for the day , and own indeed that I be right in hastening to my mother , vow that walk I should not , even though he lose his Saturday hide from Minehead and from Watchett . accordingly he send I forth on the very strong nag he have , and the maiden come to wish I God - speed , and kiss their hand at the doorway . it make I proud and glad to think that after see so much of the world , and have hold my own with it , I be come once more among my own people , and find they kind , and more warm - hearted , ay and well look too , than almost any I have happen upon in the mighty city of London . 
 but how shall I tell you the thing I feel , and the swelling of my heart within I , as I draw near , and more near , to the place of all I love and own , to the haunt of every warm remembrance , the nest of all the fledgling hope in a word , to home ? the first sheep I behold on the moor with a great red J.R. on his side   for mother would have they mark with my name , instead of her own as they should have be , I do assure you my spirit leap , and all my sight come to my eye . I shout out , ' Jem , boy ! ' for that be his name , and a rare hand he be at fighting and he know I in spite of the stranger horse ; and I lean over and stroke his head , and swear he should never be mutton . and when I be pass he set off at full gallop , to call the rest of the J.R. 's together , and tell they young master be come home at last . 
 but bless your heart , and my own as well , it would take I all the afternoon to lay before you one - tenth of the thing which come home to I in that one half - hour , as the sun be sink , in the real way he ought to sink . I touch my horse with no spur nor whip , feel that my slow wit would go , if the sight come too fast over they . here be the pool where we wash the sheep , and there be the hollow that ooze away , where I have shoot three wild duck . here be the peat - rick that hide my dinner , when I could not go home for it , and there be the bush with the thyme grow round it , where Annie have find a great swarm of our bee . and now be the corner of the dry stone wall , where the moor give over in earnest , and the partridge whisk from it into the corn land , and call that their supper be ready , and look at our house and the rick as they run , and would wait for that comfort till winter . 
 and there I see but let I go Annie be too much for I . she nearly pull I off my horse , and kiss the very mouth of the carbine . 
 ' I know you would come . oh John ! oh John ! I have wait here every Saturday night ; and I see you for the last mile or more , but I would not come round the corner , for fear that I should cry , John , and then not cry when I get you . now I may cry as much as I like , and you need not try to stop I , John , because I be so happy . but you must not   cry yourself , John ; what will mother think of you ? she will be so jealous of I . ' 
 what mother think I can not tell ; and indeed I doubt if she think at all for more than half an hour , but only manage to hold I tight , and cry , and thank God now and then , but with some fear of his take I , if she should be too grateful . moreover she think it be my own doing , and I ought to have the credit of it , and she even come down very sharply upon John 's wife , Mrs. Fry , for say that we must not be too proud , for all of it be the Lord 's doing . however , dear mother be ashamed of that afterwards , and ask Mrs. Fry 's humble pardon ; and perhaps I ought not to have mention it . 
 Old Smiler have tell they that I be come all the rest , I mean , except Annie for have escape from his halter - ring , he be come out to graze in the lane a bit ; when what should he see but a strange horse come with young master and mistress upon he , for Annie must need get up behind I , there be only sheep to look at she . then Smiler give we a stare and a neigh , with his tail quite stiff with amazement , and then   whether in joy or through indignation   he fling up his hind foot and gallop straight home , and set every dog wild with barking . 
 now , methink , quite enough have be say concern this mighty return of the young John Ridd   which be know up at Cosgate that evening , and feel that I can not describe it , how can I hope that any one else will labour to imagine it , even of the few who be able ? for very few can have travel so far , unless indeed they whose trade it be , or very unsettled people . and even of those who have do so , not one in a hundred can have such a home as I have to come home to . 
 Mother weep again , with grief and some wrath , and so do Annie also , and even little Eliza , and all be unsettle in loyalty , and talk about a republic , when I tell they how I have be leave without money for travel homeward , and expect to have to beg my way , which Farmer Snowe would have hear of . and though I could see they be disappoint at my failure of any promotion , they all declare how glad they be , and how much well they like I to be no more than what they be accustom to . at least , my mother and Annie say so , without wait to hear any more ; but Lizzie do not answer to it , until I have open my bag and show the beautiful present I have for she . and then she kiss I , almost like Annie , and vow that she think very little of captain . 
 for Lizzie 's present be the good of all , I mean , of course , except Lorna 's   which I carry in my breast all the way , hop that it might make her love I , from have lain so long , close to my heart . for I have bring Lizzie something dear , and a precious heavy book it be , and much beyond my understanding ; whereas I know well that to both the other my gift would be dear , for mine own sake . and happy people could not be find than the whole of we be that evening . 




 CHAPTER XXVIII 

 JOHN have hope of LORNA 

 much as I long to know more about Lorna , and though all my heart be yearn , I could not reconcile it yet with my duty to mother and Annie , to leave they on the follow day , which happen to be a Sunday . for lo , before breakfast be out of our mouth , there come all the man of the farm , and their wife , and even the two crow - boy , dress as if go to Barnstaple fair , to inquire how Master John be , and whether it be true that the King have make he one of his body - guard ; and if so , what be to be do with the belt for the championship of the West - Counties wrestling , which I have hold now for a year or more , and none be ready to challenge it . strange to say , this last point seem the most important of all to they ; and none ask who be to manage the farm , or answer for their wage ; but all ask who be to wear the belt . 
 to this I reply , after shake hand twice over all round with all of they , that I mean to wear the belt myself , for the honour of Oare parish , so long as ever God give I strength and health to meet all - comer ; for I have never be ask to be body - guard , and if ask I would never have do it . some of they cry that the King must be maze , not to keep I for his protection , in these violent time of Popery . I could have tell they that the King be not in the least afraid of Papists , but on the contrary , very fond of they ; however , I hold my tongue , remember what Judge Jeffreys bid I . 
 in church , the whole congregation , man , woman , and child   except , indeed , the Snowe girl , who only look when I be not watch , turn on I with one accord , and star so steadfastly , to get some reflection of the King from I , that they forget the time to kneel down and the parson be force to speak to they . if I cough , or move my book , or bow , or even say ' amen , ' glance be exchange which mean ' that he hath learn in London town , and most likely from his Majesty . ' 
 however , all this go off in time , and people become even angry with I for not be sharp   as they say , or smart , or a whit more fashionable , for all the great company I have see , and all the wondrous thing waste upon I . 
 but though I may have be none the wiser by reason of my stay in London , at any rate I be much the well in virtue of come home again . for now I have learn the joy of quiet , and the gratitude for good thing round we , and the love we owe to other   even those who must be kind , for their indulgence to we . all this , before my journey , have be too much as a matter of course to I ; but have miss it now I know that it be a gift , and might be lose . moreover , I have pin so much , in the dust and heat of that great town , for tree , and field , and running water , and the sound of country life , and the air of country wind , that never more could I grow weary of those soft enjoyment ; or at least I think so then . 
 to awake as the summer sun come slant over the hill - top , with hope on every beam adance to the laughter of the morning ; to see the leaf across the window ruffle on the fresh new air , and the tendril of the powdery vine turn from their beaded sleep . then the lustrous meadow far beyond the thatch of the garden - wall , yet see beneath the hanging scollop of the walnut - tree , all awake , dress in pearl , all amazed at their own glistening , like a maid at her own idea . down they troop the low kine , walk each with a step of character   even as man and woman do , yet all alike with toss of horn , and spread of udder ready . from they without a word , we turn to the farm - yard proper , see on the right , and dryly straw from the petty rush of the pitch - paved runnel . round it stand the snug out - building , barn , corn - chamber , cider - press , stable , with a blinker'd horse in every doorway munching , while his driver tighten buckle , whistle and look down the lane , dally to begin his labour till the milkmaid be go by . here the cock come forth at last ; where have he be linger ? egg may tell to - morrow he clap his wing and shout ' cock - a - doodle ' ; and no other cock dare look at he . two or three go sidle off , wait till their spur be grow ; and then the crowd of partlet come , chatter how their lord have dream , and crow at two in the morning , and pray that the old brown rat would only dare to face he . but while the cock be crow still , and the pullet world admire he , who come up but the old turkey - cock , with all his family round he . then the geese at the low end begin to thrust their breast out , and mum their down - bit , and look at the gander and scream shrill joy for the conflict ; while the duck in pond show nothing but tail , in proof of their strict neutrality . 
 while yet we dread for the come event , and the fight which would jar on the morning , behold the grandmother of sow , gruffly grunt right and leave with muzzle which no ring may tame   not be matrimonial , hulk across between the two , move all each side at once , and then all of the other side as if she be chin down the middle , and afraid of spill the salt from she . as this mighty view of lard hide each combatant from the other , gladly each retire and boast how he would have slay his neighbour , but that old sow drive the other away , and no wonder he be afraid of she , after all the chick she have eat . 
 and so it go on ; and so the sun come , strong from his drink of dew ; and the cattle in the byre , and the horse from the stable , and the man from cottage - door , each have have his rest and food , all smell alike of hay and straw , and every one must hie to work , be it drag , or draw , or delve . 
 so think I on the Monday morning ; while my own work lie before I , and I be plot how to quit it , void of harm to every one , and let my love have work a little hard perhaps of all work , and yet as sure as sunrise . I know that my first day 's task on the farm would be strictly watch by every one , even by my gentle mother , to see what I have learn in London . but could I let still another day pass , for Lorna to think I faithless ? 
 I feel much inclined to tell dear mother all about Lorna , and how I love she , yet have no hope of win she . often and often , I have long to do this , and have do with it . but the thought of my father 's terrible death , at the hand of the Doones , prevent I . and it seem to I foolish and mean to grieve mother , without any chance of my suit ever speed . if once Lorna love I , my mother should know it ; and it would be the great happiness to I to have no concealment from she , though at first she be sure to grieve terribly . but I see no more chance of Lorna love I , than of the man in the moon come down ; or rather of the moon come down to the man , as relate in old mythology . 
 now the merriment of the small bird , and the clear voice of the water , and the lowing of cattle in meadow , and the view of no house   except just our own and a neighbour 's , and the knowledge of everybody around , their kindness of heart and simplicity , and love of their neighbour 's doing , all these could not help or please I at all , and many of they be much against I , in my secret depth of longing and dark tumult of the mind . many people may think I foolish , especially after come from London , where many nice maid look at I   on account of my bulk and stature , and I might have be fit up with a sweetheart , in spite of my west - country twang , and the smallness of my purse ; if only I have say the word . but nay ; I have contempt for a man whose heart be like a shirt - stud   such as I see in London card , fit into one to - day , sit bravely on the breast ; pluck out on the morrow morn , and the place that know it , go . 
 now , what do I do but take my chance ; reckless whether any one heed I or not , only crave Lorna 's heed , and time for ten word to she . therefore I leave the man of the farm as far away as might be , after make they work with I   which no man round our part could do , to his own satisfaction , and then know they to be well weary , very unlike to follow I and still more unlike to tell of I , for each have his London present I stride right away , in good trust of my speed , without any more misgiving ; but resolve to face the bad of it , and to try to be home for supper . 
 and first I go , I know not why , to the crest of the break highland , whence I have agree to watch for any mark or signal . and sure enough at last I see   when it be too late to see   that the white stone have be cover over with a cloth or mantle , the sign that something have arise to make Lorna want I . for a moment I stand amazed at my evil fortune ; that I should be too late , in the very thing of all thing on which my heart be set ! then after eye sorrowfully every crick and cranny to be sure that not a single flutter of my love be visible , off I set , with small respect either for my knee or neck , to make the round of the outer cliff , and come up my old access . 
 nothing could stop I ; it be not long , although to I it seem an age , before I stand in the niche of rock at the head of the slippery watercourse , and gaze into the quiet glen , where my foolish heart be dwell . notwithstanding doubt of right , notwithstanding sense of duty , and despite all manly strive , and the great love of my home , there my heart be ever dwell , know what a fool it be , and content to know it . 
 many bird come twitter round I in the gold of August ; many tree show twinkle beauty , as the sun go lower ; and the line of water fall , from wrinkle into dimple . little heeding , there I crouch ; though with sense of everything that afterwards should move I , like a picture or a dream ; and everything go by I softly , while my heart be gaze . 
 at last , a little figure come , not insignificant   I mean , but look very light and slender in the move shadow , gently here and softly there , as if vague of purpose , with a gloss of tender movement , in and out the wealth of tree , and liberty of the meadow . who be I to crouch , or doubt , or look at she from a distance ; what matter if they kill I now , and one tear come to bury I ? therefore I rush out at once , as if shot - gun be unknown yet ; not from any real courage , but from prisone love burst forth . 
 I know not whether my own Lorna be afraid of what I look , or what I might say to she , or of her own thought of I ; all I know be that she look frightened , when I hop for gladness . perhaps the power of my joy be more than maiden like to own , or in any way to answer to ; and to tell the truth , it seem as if I might now forget myself ; while she would take good care of it . this make a man grow thoughtful ; unless , as some low fellow do , he believe all woman hypocrite . 
 therefore I go slowly towards she , take back in my impulse ; and say all I could come to say , with some distress in do it . 
 ' Mistress Lorna , I have hope that you be in need of I . ' 
 ' oh , yes ; but that be long ago ; two month ago , or more , sir . ' and say this she look away , as if it all be over . but I be now so dazed and frighten , that it take my breath away , and I could not answer , feel sure that I be rob and some one else have win she . and I try to turn away , without another word , and go . 
 but I could not help one stupid sob , though mad with myself for allow it , but it come too sharp for pride to stay it , and it tell a world of thing . Lorna hear it , and run to I , with her bright eye full of wonder , pity , and great kindness , as if amazed that I have more than a simple liking for she . then she hold out both hand to I ; and I take and look at they . 
 ' Master Ridd , I do not mean , ' she whisper , very softly , ' I do not mean to vex you . ' 
 ' if you would be loath to vex I , none else in this world can do it , ' I answer out of my great love , but fear yet to look at she , mine eye not be strong enough . 
 ' come away from this bright place , ' she answer , tremble in her turn ; ' I be watch and spy of late . come beneath the shadow , John . ' 
 I would have leap into the valley of the shadow of death   as describe by the late John Bunyan , only to hear she call I ' John ' ; though Apollyon be lurk there , and despair should lock I in . 
 she steal across the silent grass ; but I stride hotly after she ; fear be all beyond I now , except the fear of lose she . I could not but behold her manner , as she go before I , all her grace , and lovely sweetness , and her sense of what she be . 
 she lead I to her own rich bower , which I tell of once before ; and if in spring it be a sight , what be it in summer glory ? but although my mind have notice of its fairness and its wonder , not a heed my heart take of it , neither dwelt it in my presence more than flow water . all that in my presence dwelt , all that in my heart be feel , be the maiden move gently , and afraid to look at I . 
 for now the power of my love be abide on she , new to she , unknown to she ; not a thing to speak about , nor even to think clearly ; only just to feel and wonder , with a pain of sweetness . she could look at I no more , neither could she look away , with a study manner only to let fall her eye , and blush , and be put out with I , and still more with herself . 
 I leave she quite alone ; though close , though tingle to have hold of she . even her right hand be drop and lie among the moss . neither do I try to steal one glimpse below her eyelid . life and death to I be hang on the first glance I should win ; yet I let it be so . 
 after long or short I know not , yet ere I be weary , ere I yet begin to think or wish for any answer Lorna slowly raise her eyelid , with a gleam of dew below they , and look at I doubtfully . any look with so much in it never meet my gaze before . 
 ' darling , do you love I ? ' be all that I could say to she . 
 ' yes , I like you very much , ' she answer , with her eye go from I , and her dark hair fall over , so as not to show I thing . 
 ' but do you love I , Lorna , Lorna ; do you love I more than all the world ? ' 
 ' no , to be sure not . now why should I ? ' 
 ' in truth , I know not why you should . only I hop that you do , Lorna . either love I not at all , or as I love you for ever . ' 
 ' John I love you very much ; and I would not grieve you . you be the brave , and the kind , and the simple of all man I mean of all people I like you very much , Master Ridd , and I think of you almost every day . ' 
 ' that will not do for I , Lorna . not almost every day I think , but every instant of my life , of you . for you I would give up my home , my love of all the world beside , my duty to my dear one , for you I would give up my life , and hope of life beyond it . do you love I so ? ' 
 ' not by any mean , ' say Lorna ; ' no , I like you very much , when you do not talk so wildly ; and I like to see you come as if you would fill our valley up , and I like to think that even Carver would be nothing in your hand but as to like you like that , what should make it likely ? especially when I have make the signal , and for some two month or more you have never even answer it ! if you like I so ferociously , why do you leave I for other people to do just as they like with I ? ' 
 ' to do as they like ! oh , Lorna , not to make you marry Carver ? ' 
 ' no , Master Ridd , be not frightened so ; it make I fear to look at you . ' 
 ' but you have not marry Carver yet ? say quick ! why keep I wait so ? ' 
 ' of course I have not , Master Ridd . should I be here if I have , think you , and allow you to like I so , and to hold my hand , and make I laugh , as I declare you almost do sometimes ? and at other time you frighten I . ' 
 ' do they want you to marry Carver ? tell I all the truth of it . ' 
 ' not yet , not yet . they be not half so impetuous as you be , John . I be only just seventeen , you know , and who be to think of marry ? but they want I to give my word , and be formally betroth to he in the presence of my grandfather . it seem that something frighten they . there be a youth name Charleworth Doone , every one call he " Charlie " ; a headstrong and a gay young man , very gallant in his look and manner ; and my uncle , the Counsellor , choose to fancy that Charlie look at I too much , come by my grandfather 's cottage . ' 
 here Lorna blush so that I be frightened , and begin to hate this Charlie more , a great deal more , than even Carver Doone . 
 ' he have well not , ' say I ; ' I will fling he over it , if he dare . he shall see thee through the roof , Lorna , if at all he see thee . ' 
 ' Master Ridd , you be bad than Carver ! I think you be so kind - hearted . well , they want I to promise , and even to swear a solemn oath   a thing I have never do in my life   that I would we d my eld cousin , this same Carver Doone , who be twice as old as I be , be thirty - five and upwards . that be why I give the token that I wish to see you , Master Ridd . they point out how much it be for the peace of all the family , and for mine own benefit ; but I would not listen for a moment , though the Counsellor be most eloquent , and my grandfather beg I to consider , and Carver smile his pleasantest , which be a truly frightful thing . then both he and his crafty father be for use force with I ; but Sir Ensor would not hear of it ; and they have put off that extreme until he shall be past its knowledge , or , at least , beyond prevent it . and now I be watch , and spy , and follow , and half my little liberty seem to be take from I . I could not be here speak with you , even in my own nook and refuge , but for the aid , and skill , and courage of dear little Gwenny Carfax . she be now my chief reliance , and through she alone I hope to baffle all my enemy , since other have forsake I . ' 
 tear of sorrow and reproach be lurk in her soft dark eye , until in few word I tell she that my seem negligence be nothing but my bitter loss and wretched absence far away ; of which I have so vainly striven to give any tiding without danger to she . when she hear all this , and see what I have bring from London   which be nothing less than a ring of pearl with a sapphire in the midst of they , as pretty as could well be find , she let the gentle tear flow fast , and come and sit so close beside I , that I tremble like a fold sheep at the bleating of her lamb . but recover comfort quickly , without more ado , I raise her left hand and observe it with a nice regard , wonder at the small blue vein , and curve , and taper whiteness , and the point it finish with . my wonder seem to please she much , herself so well accustomed to it , and not fond of watch it . and then , before she could say a word , or guess what I be up to , as quick as ever I turn hand in a bout of wrestling , on her finger be my ring sapphire for the vein of blue , and pearl to match white finger . 
 ' oh , you crafty Master Ridd ! ' say Lorna , look up at I , and blush now a far bright blush than when she speak of Charlie ; ' I think that you be much too simple ever to do this sort of thing . no wonder you can catch the fish , as when first I see you . ' 
 ' have I catch you , little fish ? or must all my life be spend in hopeless angling for you ? ' 
 ' neither one nor the other , John ! you have not catch I yet altogether , though I like you dearly John ; and if you will only keep away , I shall like you more and more . as for hopeless angling , John that all other shall have until I tell you otherwise . ' 
 with the large tear in her eye tear which seem to I to rise partly from her want to love I with the power of my love she put her pure bright lip , half smile , half prone to reply to tear , against my forehead line with trouble , doubt , and eager longing . and then she draw my ring from off that snowy twig her finger , and hold it out to I ; and then , see how my face be fall , thrice she touch it with her lip , and sweetly give it back to I . ' John , I dare not take it now ; else I should be cheat you . I will try to love you dearly , even as you deserve and wish . keep it for I just till then . something tell I I shall earn it in a very little time . perhaps you will be sorry then , sorry when it be all too late , to be love by such as I be . ' 
 what could I do at her mournful tone , but kiss a thousand time the hand which she put up to warn I , and vow that I would rather die with one assurance of her love , than without it live for ever with all beside that the world could give ? upon this she look so lovely , with her dark eyelash tremble , and her soft eye full of light , and the colour of clear sunrise mount on her cheek and brow , that I be force to turn away , be overcome with beauty . 
 ' dearest darling , love of my life , ' I whisper through her cloud of hair ; ' how long must I wait to know , how long must I linger doubt whether you can ever stoop from your birth and wondrous beauty to a poor , coarse hind like I , an ignorant unlettered yeoman ' 
 ' I will not have you revile yourself , ' say Lorna , very tenderly just as I have mean to make she . ' you be not rude and unlettered , John . you know a great deal more than I do ; you have learn both Greek and Latin , as you tell I long ago , and you have be at the very good school in the West of England . none of we but my grandfather , and the Counsellor   who be a great scholar , can compare with you in this . and though I have laugh at your manner of speech , I only laugh in fun , John ; I never mean to vex you by it , nor know that it have do so . ' 
 ' naught you say can vex I , dear , ' I answer , as she lean towards I in her generous sorrow ; ' unless you say " Begone , John Ridd ; I love another more than you . " ' 
 ' then I shall never vex you , John . never , I mean , by say that . now , John , if you please , be quiet ' 
 for I be carry away so much by hear she call I ' John ' so often , and the music of her voice , and the way she bend toward I , and the shadow of soft weeping in the sunlight of her eye , that some of my great hand be creep in a manner not to be imagine , and far less explain , toward the lithesome , wholesome curve underneath her mantle - fold , and out of sight and harm , as I think ; not be her front waist . however , I be dash with that , and pretend not to mean it ; only to pluck some lady - fern , whose elegance do I no good . 
 ' now , John , ' say Lorna , be so quick that not even a lover could cheat she , and observe my confusion more intently than she need have do . ' Master John Ridd , it be high time for you to go home to your mother . I love your mother very much from what you have tell I about she , and I will not have she cheat . ' 
 ' if you truly love my mother , ' say I , very craftily ' the only way to show it be by truly love I . ' 
 upon that she laugh at I in the sweetest manner , and with such provoking way , and such come - and - go of glance , and beginning of quick blush , which she try to laugh away , that I know , as well as if she herself have tell I , by some knowledge   void of reasoning , and the surer for it , I know quite well , while all my heart be burn hot within I , and mine eye be shy of her , and her eye be shy of mine ; for certain and for ever this I know as in a glory that Lorna Doone have now begin and would go on to love I . 




 CHAPTER XXIX 

 reap LEADS to revel 

 although I be under interdict for two month from my darling ' one for your sake , one for mine , ' she have whisper , with her head withdraw , yet not so very far from I light heart be not on Exmoor than I bear for half the time , and even for three quarter . for she be safe ; I know that daily by a mode of signal well - contrived between we now , on the strength of our experience . ' I have nothing now to fear , John , ' she have say to I , as we part ; ' it be true that I be spy and watch , but Gwenny be too keen for they . while I have my grandfather to prevent all violence ; and little gwenny to keep watch on those who try to watch I ; and you , above all other , John , ready at a moment , if the bad come to the bad this neglect Lorna Doone be never in such case before . therefore do not squeeze my hand , John ; I be safe without it , and you do not know your strength . ' 
 ah , I know my strength right well . Hill and valley scarcely seem to be step and land for I ; fierce cattle I would play with , make they go backward , and afraid of hurt they , like John Fry with his terrier ; even rooted tree seem to I but as stick I could smite down , except for my love of everything . the love of all thing be upon I , and a softness to they all , and a sense of have something even such as they have . 
 then the golden harvest come , wave on the broad hill - side , and nestle in the quiet nook scoop from out the fringe of wood . a wealth of harvest such as never gladden all our country - side since my father cease to reap , and his sickle hang to rust . there have not be a man on Exmoor fit to work that reaping - hook since the time its owner fall , in the prime of life and strength , before a sterner reaper . but now I take it from the wall , where mother proudly store it , while she watch I , hardly know whether she should smile or cry . 
 all the parish be assemble in our upper courtyard ; for we be to open the harvest that year , as have be settle with Farmer Nicholas , and with Jasper Kebby , who hold the third or little farm . we start in proper order , therefore , as our practice be : first , the parson Josiah Bowden , wear his gown and cassock , with the parish Bible in his hand , and a sickle strap behind he . as he stride along well and stoutly , be a man of substance , all our family come next , I lead mother with one hand , in the other bear my father 's hook , and with a loaf of our own bread and a keg of cider upon my back . behind we Annie and Lizzie walk , wear wreath of corn - flower , set out very prettily , such as mother would have wear if she have be a farmer 's wife , instead of a farmer 's widow . be as she be , she have no adornment , except that her widow 's hood be off , and her hair allow to flow , as if she have be a maiden ; and very rich bright hair it be , in spite of all her trouble . 
 after we , the maidens come , milkmaid and the rest of they , with Betty Muxworthy at their head , scold even now , because they would not walk fitly . but they only laugh at she ; and she know it be no good to scold , with all the man behind they . 
 then the snowe come troop forward ; Farmer Nicholas in the middle , walk as if he would rather walk to a wheatfield of his own , yet content to follow lead , because he know himself the leader ; and sign every now and then to the people here and there , as if I be nobody . but to see his three great daughter , strong and handsome wench , make upon either side , as if somebody would run off with they this be the very thing that teach I how to value Lorna , and her pure simplicity . 
 after the Snowes come Jasper Kebby , with his wife , new - married ; and a very honest pair they be , upon only a hundred acre , and a right of common . after these the man come hotly , without decent order , try to spy the girl in front , and make good joke about they , at which their wife laugh heartily , be jealous when alone perhaps . and after these man and their wife come all the child toddle , pick flower by the way , and chatter and ask question , as the child will . there must have be threescore of we , take one with another , and the lane be full of people . when we be come to the big field - gate , where the first sickle be to be , Parson Bowden heave up the rail with the sleeve of his gown do green with it ; and he say that everybody might hear he , though his breath be short , ' in the name of the Lord , amen ! ' 
 ' amen ! so be it ! ' cry the clerk , who be far behind , be only a shoemaker . 
 then Parson Bowden read some verse from the parish Bible , tell we to lift up our eye , and look upon the field already white to harvest ; and then he lay the Bible down on the square head of the gate - post , and despite his gown and cassock , three good swipe he cut off corn , and lay they right end onwards . all this time the rest be huddle outside the gate , and along the lane , not dare to interfere with parson , but whisper how well he do it . 
 when he have stow the corn like that , mother enter , lean on I , and we both say , ' thank the Lord for all his mercy , and these the first - fruit of his hand ! ' and then the clerk give out a psalm verse by verse , do very well ; although he sneeze in the midst of it , from a beard of wheat thrust up his nose by the rival cobbler at Brendon . and when the psalm be sing , so strongly that the foxglove on the bank be shake , like a chime of bell , at it , Parson take a stoop of cider , and we all fall to at reap . 
 of course I mean the man , not woman ; although I know that up the country , woman be allow to reap ; and right well they reap it , keep row for row with man , comely , and in due order , yet , meseem , the man must ill attend to their own reaping - hook , in fear lest the other cut themselves , be the weak vessel . but in our part , woman do what seem their proper business , follow well behind the man , out of harm of the swing hook , and stoop with their breast and arm up they catch the swathe of corn , where the reaper cast they , and tuck they together tightly with a wisp lay under they , this they fetch around and twist , with a knee to keep it close ; and lo , there be a goodly sheaf , ready to set up in stook ! after these the child come , gather each for his little self , if the farmer be right - minded ; until each hath a bundle make as big as himself and long , and tumble now and again with it , in the deep part of the stubble . 
 we , the man , keep march onwards down the flank of the yellow wall , with knee bent wide , and leave arm bow and right arm flash steel . each man in his several place , keep down the rig or chine , on the right side of the reaper in front , and the left of the man that follow he , each make far sweep and inroad into the golden breadth and depth , each cast leftward his rich clearance on his foregoer 's double track . 
 so like half a wedge of wildfowl , to and fro we sweep the field ; and when to either hedge we come , sickle want whetting , and throat require moistening , and back be in need of easing , and every man have much to say , and woman want praise . then all return to the other end , with reap - hook beneath our arm , and dog leave to mind jacket . 
 but now , will you believe I well , or will you only laugh at I ? for even in the world of wheat , when deep among the varnished crispness of the jointed stalk , and below the feather yielding of the graceful head , even as I grip the swathe and sweep the sickle round they , even as I fling they by to rest on brother stubble , through the whirling yellow world , and eagerness of reap , come the vision of my love , as with downcast eye she wonder at my power of passion . and then the sweet remembrance glow bright than the sun through wheat , through my very depth of heart , of how she raise those beam eye , and ripen in my breast rich hope . even now I could descry , like high wave in the distance , the rounded head and fold shadow of the wood of Bagworthy . perhaps she be walk in the valley , and softly gaze up at they . oh , to be a bird just there ! I could see a bright mist hang just above the Doone Glen . perhaps it be shed its drizzle upon she . oh , to be a drop of rain ! the very breeze which bow the harvest to my bosom gently , might have come direct from Lorna , with her sweet voice laden . ah , the flaw of air that wander where they will around she , fan her bright cheek , play with lash , even revel in her hair and reveal her beauty man be but a breath , we know , would I be such breath as that ! 
 but confound it , while I ponder , with delicious dream suspend , with my right arm hang frustrate and the giant sickle droop , with my left arm bow for clasp something more germane than wheat , and my eye not mind business , but intent on distant wood confound it , what be the man about , and why be I leave vapoure ? they have take advantage of I , the rogue ! they be go to the hedge for the cider - jar ; they have have up the sledd of bread and meat , quite softly over the stubble , and if I can believe my eye   so daze with Lorna 's image , they be sit down to an excellent dinner , before the church clock have go eleven ! 
 ' John Fry , you big villain ! ' I cry , with John hang up in the air by the scruff of his neck - cloth , but hold still by his knife and fork , and a goose - leg in between his lip , ' John Fry , what mean you by this , sir ? ' 
 ' latt I dowun , or I can not   tell ' e , ' John answer with some difficulty . so I let he come down , and I must confess that he have reason on his side . ' plaise your worship ' John call I so , ever since I return from London , firmly believe that the King have make I a magistrate at least ; though I be to keep it secret ' we zeed as how your worship be take with thinkin ' of King 's business , in the middle of the whate - rigg : and so uz ze , " Latt un coom to his zell , we have well zave taime , by takke our dinner " ; and here we be , praise your worship , and hopp no offence with thick iron spoon full of vried tatie . ' 
 I be glad enough to accept the ladle full of fried batata , and to make the good of thing , which be generally do by let man have their own way . therefore I manage to dine with they , although it be so early . 
 for accord to all that I can find , in a long life and a varied one , twelve o'clock be the real time for a man to have his dinner . then the sun be at his noon , call halt to look around , and then the plant and leaf be turn , each with a little leisure time , before the work of the afternoon . then be the balance of east and west , and then the right and leave side of a man be in due proportion , and contribute fairly with harmonious fluid . and the health of this mode of life , and its reclaim virtue be well set forth in our ancient rhyme , 
 Sunrise , breakfast ; sun high , dinner ; Sundown , sup ; make a saint of a sinner . 
 Whish , the wheat fall ! Whirl again ; ye have have good dinner ; give your master and mistress plenty to supply another year . and in truth we do reap well and fairly , through the whole of that afternoon , I not only keep lead , but keep the man up to it . we get through a matter of ten acre , ere the sun between the shock break his light on wheaten plume , then hang his red cloak on the cloud , and fall into grey slumber . 
 see this we wipe our sickle , and our breast and forehead , and soon be on the homeward road , look forward to good supper . 
 of course all the reaper come at night to the harvest - supper , and Parson Bowden to say the grace as well as to help to carve for we . and some help be need there , I can well assure you ; for the reaper have brave appetite , and most of their wife have baby be force to eat as a duty . neither fail they of this duty ; cut and come again be the order of the evening , as it have be of the day ; and I have no time to ask question , but help meat and ladle gravy . all the while our darling Annie , with her sleeve tuck up , and her comely figure panting , be run about with a bucket of tatie mash with lard and cabbage . even Lizzie have leave her book , and be serve out beer and cider ; while mother help plum - pudding largely on pewter - plate with the mutton . and all the time , Betty Muxworthy be grunt in and out everywhere , not have space to scold even , but change the dish , serve the meat , poke the fire , and cook more . but John Fry would not stir a peg , except with his knife and fork , have all the air of a visitor , and his wife to keep he eat , till I think there would be no end of it . 
 then have eat all they could , they prepare themselves , with one accord , for the business now of drinking . but first they lift the neck of corn , dress with ribbon gaily , and set it upon the mantelpiece , each man with his horn a - froth ; and then they sing a song about it , every one shout in the chorus louder than harvest thunderstorm . some be in the middle of one verse , and some at the end of the next one ; yet somehow all manage to get together in the mighty roar of the burden . and if any farmer up the country would like to know Exmoor harvest - song as sing in my time and will be sing long after I be garner home , lo , here I set it down for he , omit only the dialect , which perchance might puzzle he . 

  exmoor harvest - SONG 

  1 

  the corn , oh the corn , ' tis the ripening of the corn ! 
  go unto the door , my lad , and look beneath the moon , 
  thou canst see , beyond the woodrick , how it be yelloon : 
  ' tis the harvesting of wheat , and the barley must be shorn . 

   Chorus 

  the corn , oh the corn , and the yellow , mellow corn ! 
  here be to the corn , with the cup upon the board ! 
  we have   be reap all the day , and we will reap again the morn 
  and fetch it home to mow - yard , and then we will thank the Lord . 

  2 

  the wheat , oh the wheat , ' tis the ripening of the wheat ! 
  all the day it have be hang down its heavy head , 
  bow over on our bosom with a beard of red : 
  ' tis the harvest , and the value make the labour sweet . 

   Chorus 

  the wheat , oh the wheat , and the golden , golden wheat ! 
  here be to the wheat , with the loaf upon the board ! 
  we have   be reap all the day , and we never will be beat , 
  but fetch it all to mow - yard , and then we will thank the Lord . 

  3 

  the barley , oh the barley , and the barley be in prime ! 
  all the day it have be rustle , with its bristle brown , 
  wait with its beard abowing , till it can be mow ! 
  ' tis the harvest and the barley must abide its time . 

   Chorus 

  the barley , oh the barley , and the barley ruddy brown ! 
  here be to the barley , with the beer upon the board ! 
  we will go amowing , soon as ever all the wheat be down ; 
  when all be in the mow - yard , we will stop , and thank the Lord . 

  4 

  the oat , oh the oat , ' tis the ripening of the oats ! 
  all the day they have be dance with their flake of white , 
  wait for the girding - hook , to be the nag ' delight : 
  ' tis the harvest , let they dangle in their skirt coat . 

   Chorus 

  the oat , oh the oat , and the silver , silver oat ! 
  here be to the oats with the blackstone on the board ! 
  we will go among they , when the barley have be lay in rote : 
  when all be home to mow - yard , we will kneel and thank the Lord . 

  5 

  the corn , oh the corn , and the blessing of the corn ! 
  come unto the door , my lad , and look beneath the moon , 
  we can see , on hill and valley , how it be yelloon , 
  with a breadth of glory , as when our Lord be bear . 

   Chorus 

  the corn , oh the corn , and the yellow , mellow corn ! 
  thank for the corn , with our bread upon the board ! 
  so shall we acknowledge it , before we reap the morn , 
  with our hand to heaven , and our knee unto the Lord . 
 now we sing this song very well the first time , have the parish choir to lead we , and the clarionet , and the parson to give we the time with his cup ; and we sing it again the second time , not so but what you might praise it   if you have be with we all the evening , although the parson be go then , and the clerk not fit to compare with he in the matter of keep time . but when that song be in its third singing , I defy any man   however sober   to have make out one verse from the other , or even the burden from the verse , inasmuch as every man present , ay , and woman too , sing as become convenient to they , in utterance both of word and tune .
