 and in truth , there be much excuse for they ; because it be a noble harvest , fit to thank the Lord for , without his think we hypocrite . for we have more land in wheat , that year , than ever we have before , and twice the crop to the acre ; and I could not help now and then remember , in the midst of the merriment , how my father in the churchyard yonder would have glory to behold it . and my mother , who have leave we now , happen to return just then , be call to have her health drunk   for the twentieth time at least , I know by the sadness in her eye that she be think just as I be . presently , therefore , I slip away from the noise , and mirth , and smoke   although of that last there be not much , except from Farmer Nicholas , and cross the courtyard in the moonlight , I go , just to cool myself , as far as my father 's tombstone . 




 CHAPTER XXX 

 ANNIE get the best of it 

 I have long outgrow unwholesome feeling as to my father 's death , and so have Annie ; though Lizzie   who must have love he least   still entertain some evil will , and long for a punishment . therefore I be surprised   and indeed , startle would not be too much to say , the moon be somewhat fleecy , to see our Annie sit there as motionless as the tombstone , and with all her good fallal upon she , after stow away the dish . 
 my nerve , however , be good and strong , except at least in love matter , wherein they always fail I , and when I meet with witch ; and therefore I go up to Annie , although she look so white and pure ; for I have see she before with those thing on , and it strike I who she be . 
 " what be you do here , Annie ? " I inquire rather sternly , be vex with she for have go so very near to frighten I . 
 " nothing at all , " say our Annie shortly . and indeed it be truth enough for a woman . not that I dare to believe that woman be such liar as man say ; only that I mean they often see thing round the corner , and know not which be which of it . and indeed I never have know a woman   though right enough in their meaning   purely and perfectly true and transparent , except only my Lorna ; and even so , I might not have love she , if she have be ugly . 
 ' why , how so ? ' say I ; ' Miss Annie , what business have you here , do nothing at this time of night ? and leave I with all the trouble to entertain our guest ! ' 
 ' you seem not to I to be do it , John , ' Annie answer softly ; ' what business have you here do nothing , at this time of night ? ' 
 I be take so aback with this , and the extreme impertinence of it , from a mere young girl like Annie , that I turn round to march away and have nothing more to say to she . but she jump up , and catch I by the hand , and throw herself upon my bosom , with her face all wet with tear . 
 ' oh , John , I will tell you . I will tell you . only do not   be angry , John . ' 
 ' angry ! no indeed , ' say I ; ' what right have I to be angry with you , because you have your secret ? every chit of a girl think now that she have a right to her secret . ' 
 ' and you have none of your own , John ; of course you have none of your own ? all your go out at night ' 
 ' we will not quarrel here , poor Annie , ' I answer , with some loftiness ; ' there be many thing upon my mind , which girl can have no notion of . ' 
 ' and so there be upon mine , John . oh , John , I will tell you everything , if you will look at I kindly , and promise to forgive I . oh , I be so miserable ! ' 
 now this , though she be behave so badly , move I much towards she ; especially as I long to know what she have to tell I . therefore I allow she to coax I , and to kiss I , and to lead I away a little , as far as the old yew - tree ; for she would not tell I where she be . 
 but even in the shadow there , she be very long before beginning , and seem to have two mind about it , or rather perhaps a dozen ; and she lay her cheek against the tree , and sob till it be pitiful ; and I know what mother would say to she for spoil her good frock so . 
 ' now will you stop ? ' I say at last , hard than I mean it , for I know that she would go on all night , if any one encourage she : and though not well acquainted with woman , I understand my sister ; or else I must be a bear fool except , of course , that I never profess to understand Eliza . 
 ' yes , I will stop , ' say Annie , panting ; ' you be very hard on I , John ; but I know you mean it for the good . if somebody else I be sure I do not   know who , and have no right to know , no doubt , but she must be a wicked thing if somebody else have be take so with a pain all round the heart , John , and no power of tell it , perhaps you would have coax , and kiss she , and come a little nearer , and make opportunity to be very loving . ' 
 now this be so exactly what I have try to do to Lorna , that my breath be almost take away at Annie 's so describe it . for a while I could not say a word , but wonder if she be a witch , which have never be in our family : and then , all of a sudden , I see the way to beat she , with the devil at my elbow . 
 ' from your knowledge of these thing , Annie , you must have have they do to you . I demand to know this very moment who have take such liberty . ' 
 ' then , John , you shall never know , if you ask in that manner . besides , it be no liberty in the least at all , Cousins have a right to do thing and when they be one 's godfather ' here Annie stop quite suddenly have so betray herself ; but meet I in the full moonlight , be resolve to face it out , with a good face put upon it . 
 ' alas , I fear it would come to this , ' I answer very sadly ; ' I know he have be here many a time , without show himself to I . there be nothing mean than for a man to sneak , and steal a young maid 's heart , without her people know it . ' 
 ' you be not do anything of that sort yourself then , dear John , be you ? ' 
 ' only a common highwayman ! ' I answer , without heed she ; ' a man without an acre of his own , and liable to hang upon any common , and no other right of common over it ' 
 ' John , ' say my sister , ' be the Doones privilege not to be hang upon common land ? ' 
 at this I be so thunderstruck , that I leap in the air like a shot rabbit , and rush as hard as I could through the gate and across the yard , and back into the kitchen ; and there I ask Farmer Nicholas Snowe to give I some tobacco , and to lend I a spare pipe . 
 this he do with a grateful manner , be now some five - fourth go ; and so I smoke the very first pipe that ever have enter my lip till then ; and beyond a doubt it do I good , and spread my heart at leisure . 
 meanwhile the reaper be mostly go , to be up betime in the morning ; and some be lead by their wife ; and some have to lead their wife themselves , accord to the capacity of man and wife respectively . but Betty be as lively as ever , bustle about with every one , and look out for the chance of groat , which the well off might be free with . and over the kneading - pan next day , she drop three and sixpence out of her pocket ; and Lizzie could not tell for her life how much more might have be in it . 
 now by this time I have almost finish smoke that pipe of tobacco , and wonder at myself for have so despise it hitherto , and make up my mind to have another trial to - morrow night , it begin to occur to I that although dear Annie have behave so very badly and rudely , and almost take my breath away with the suddenness of her allusion , yet it be not kind of I to leave she out there at that time of night , all alone , and in such distress . any of the reaper go home might be get so far beyond fear of ghost as to venture into the churchyard ; and although they would know a great deal well than to insult a sister of mine when sober , there be no tell what they might do in their present state of rejoice . moreover , it be only right that I should learn , for Lorna 's sake , how far Annie , or any one else , have penetrate our secret . 
 therefore , I go forth at once , bear my pipe in a skilful manner , as I have see Farmer Nicholas do ; and mark , with a new kind of pleasure , how the ring and wreath of smoke hover and flutter in the moonlight , like a lark upon his carol . Poor Annie be go back again to our father 's grave , and there she sit upon the turf , sob very gently , and not wish to trouble any one . so I raise she tenderly , and make much of she , and console she , for I could not scold she there ; and perhaps after all she be not to be blame so much as Tom Faggus himself be . Annie be very grateful to I , and kiss I many time , and beg my pardon ever so often for her rudeness to I . and then have go so far with it , and find I so complaisant , she must need try to go a little far , and to lead I away from her own affair , and into mine concern Lorna . but although it be clever enough of she she be not deep enough for I there ; and I soon discover that she know nothing , not even the name of my darling ; but only suspect from thing she have see , and put together like a woman . upon this I bring she back again to Tom Faggus and his doing . 
 ' my poor Annie , have you really promise he to be his wife ? ' 
 ' then after all you have no reason , John , no particular reason , I mean , for slight poor Sally Snowe so ? ' 
 ' without even ask mother or I ! oh , Annie , it be wrong of you ! ' 
 ' but , darling , you know that mother wish you so much to marry Sally ; and I be sure you could have she to - morrow . she dot on the very ground ' 
 ' I dare say he tell you that , Annie , that he dot on the ground you walk upon but do you believe he , child ? ' 
 ' you may believe I , I assure you , John , and half the farm to be settle upon she , after the old man 's time ; and though she give herself little air , it be only do to entice you ; she have the very good hand in the dairy John , and the light at a turn - over cake ' 
 ' now , Annie , do not   talk nonsense so . I wish just to know the truth about you and Tom Faggus . do you mean to marry he ? ' 
 ' I to marry before my brother , and leave he with none to take care of he ! who can do he a red deer collop , except Sally herself , as I can ? come home , dear , at once , and I will do you one ; for you never eat a morsel of supper , with all the people you have to attend upon . ' 
 this be true enough ; and see no chance of anything more than cross question and crooked purpose , at which a girl be sure to beat I , I even allow she to lead I home , with the thought of the collop uppermost . but I never count upon be beat so thoroughly as I be ; for know I now to be off my guard , the young hussy stop at the farmyard gate , as if with a bri entangle she , and while I be stoop to take it away , she look I full in the face by the moonlight , and jerk out quite suddenly , 
 ' can your love do a collop , John ? ' 
 ' no , I should hope not , ' I answer rashly ; ' she be not a mere cook - maid I should hope . ' 
 ' she be not half so pretty as Sally Snowe ; I will answer for that , ' say Annie . 
 ' she be ten thousand time as pretty as ten thousand Sally Snowes , ' I reply with great indignation . 
 ' oh , but look at Sally 's eye ! ' cry my sister rapturously . 
 ' look at Lorna Doone 's , ' say I ; ' and you would never look again at Sally 's . ' 
 ' oh Lorna Doone . Lorna Doone ! ' exclaim our Annie half - frightened , yet clap her hand with triumph , at have find I out so : ' Lorna Doone be the lovely maiden , who have steal poor somebody 's heart so . ah , I shall remember it ; because it be so queer a name . but stop , I have well write it down . lend I your hat , poor boy , to write on . ' 
 ' I have a great mind to lend you a box on the ear , ' I answer she in my vexation , ' and I would , if you have not be cry so , you sly good - for - nothing baggage . as it be , I shall keep it for Master Faggus , and add interest for keep . ' 
 ' oh no , John ; oh no , John , ' she beg I earnestly , be sober in a moment . ' your hand be so terribly heavy , John ; and he never would forgive you ; although he be so good - hearted , he can not put up with an insult . promise I , dear John , that you will not strike he ; and I will promise you faithfully to keep your secret , even from mother , and even from Cousin Tom himself . ' 
 ' and from Lizzie ; most of all , from Lizzie , ' I answer very eagerly , know too well which of my relation would be hard with I . 
 ' of course from little Lizzie , ' say Annie , with some contempt ; ' a young thing like she can not be keep too long , in my opinion , from the knowledge of such subject . and besides , I should be very sorry if Lizzie have the right to know your secret , as I have , dearest John . not a soul shall be the wiser for your have trust I , John ; although I shall be very wretched when you be late away at night , among those dreadful people . ' 
 ' well , ' I reply , ' it be no use cry over spilt milk Annie . you have my secret , and I have your ; and I scarcely know which of the two be likely to have the bad time of it , when it come to mother 's ear . I could put up with perpetual scolding but not with mother 's sad silence . ' 
 ' that be exactly how I feel , John . ' and as Annie say it she brighten up , and her soft eye shine upon I ; ' but now I shall be much happy , dear ; because I shall try to help you . no doubt the young lady deserve it , John . she be not after the farm , I hope ? ' 
 ' she ! ' I exclaim ; and that be enough , there be so much scorn in my voice and face . 
 ' then , I be sure , I be very glad , ' Annie always make the good of thing ; ' for I do believe that Sally Snowe have take a fancy to our dairy - place , and the pattern of our cream - pan ; and she ask so much about our meadow , and the colour of the milk ' 
 ' then , after all , you be right , dear Annie ; it be the ground she dot upon . ' 
 ' and the thing that walk upon it , ' she answer I with another kiss ; ' Sally have take a wonderful fancy to our good cow , " nipple - pin . " but she never shall have she now ; what a consolation ! ' 
 we enter the house quite gently thus , and find Farmer Nicholas Snowe asleep , little dream how his plan have be overset between we . and then Annie say to I very slyly , between a smile and a blush , 
 ' do not   you wish Lorna Doone be here , John , in the parlour along with mother ; instead of those two fashionable milkmaid , as Uncle Ben will call they , and poor stupid Mistress Kebby ? ' 
 ' that indeed I do , Annie . I must kiss you for only thinking of it . dear I , it seem as if you have know all about we for a twelvemonth . ' 
 ' she love you , with all her heart , John . no doubt about that of course . ' and Annie look up at I , as much as to say she would like to know who could help it . 
 ' that be the very thing she will not   do , ' say I , know that Annie would love I all the more for it , ' she be only begin to like I , Annie ; and as for love , she be so young that she only love her grandfather . but I hope she will come to it by - and - by . ' 
 ' of course she must , ' reply my sister , ' it will be impossible for she to help it . ' 
 ' ah well ! I do not   know , ' for I want more assurance of it . ' Maidens be such wondrous thing ! " 
 ' not a bit of it , ' say Annie , cast her bright eye downward : ' love be as simple as milking , when people know how to do it . but you must not let she alone too long ; that be my advice to you . what a simpleton you must have be not to tell I long ago . I would have make Lorna wild about you , long before this time , Johnny . but now you go into the parlour , dear , while I do your collop . faith Snowe be not come , but Polly and Sally . Sally have make up her mind to conquer you this very blessed evening , John . only look what a thing of a scarf she have on ; I should be quite ashamed to wear it . but you will not   strike poor Tom , will you ? ' 
 ' not i , my darling , for your sweet sake . ' 
 and so dear Annie , have grow quite brave , give I a little push into the parlour , where I be quite abash to enter after all I have hear about Sally . and I make up my mind to examine she well , and try a little courting with she , if she should lead I on , that I might be in practice for Lorna . but when I perceive how grandly and richly both the young damsel be apparelle ; and how , in their curtsey to I , they retreat , as if I be make up to they , in a way they have learn from Exeter ; and how they begin to talk of the Court , as if they have be there all their life , and the late mode of the Duchess of this , and the profile of the Countess of that , and the last good saying of my Lord something ; instead of butter , and cream , and egg , and thing which they understand ; I know there must be somebody in the room besides Jasper Kebby to talk at . 
 and so there be ; for behind the curtain draw across the window - seat no less a man than Uncle Ben be sit half asleep and weary ; and by his side a little girl very quiet and very watchful . my mother lead I to Uncle Ben , and he take my hand without rise , mutter something not over - polite , about my be big than ever . I ask he heartily how he be , and he say , ' well enough , for that matter ; but none the well for the noise you great clod have be make . ' 
 ' I be sorry if we have disturb you , sir , ' I answer very civilly ; ' but I know not that you be here even ; and you must allow for harvest time . ' 
 ' so it seem , ' he reply ; ' and allow a great deal , include waste and drunkenness . now   if you can see so small a thing , after empty flagon much large   this be my granddaughter , and my heiress ' here he glance at mother ' my heiress , little Ruth Huckaback . ' 
 ' I be very glad to see you , Ruth , ' I answer , offer she my hand , which she seem afraid to take , ' welcome to Plover 's barrow , my good cousin Ruth . ' 
 however , my good cousin Ruth only arise , and make I a curtsey , and lift her great brown eye at I , more in fear , as I think , than kinship . and if ever any one look unlike the heiress to great property , it be the little girl before I . 
 ' come out to the kitchen , dear , and let I chuck you to the ceiling , ' I say , just to encourage she ; ' I always do it to little girl ; and then they can see the ham and bacon . ' but Uncle Reuben burst out laugh ; and Ruth turn away with a deep rich colour . 
 ' do you know how old she be , you numskull ? ' say Uncle Ben , in his dry drawl ; ' she be seventeen last July , sir . ' 
 ' on the first of July , grandfather , ' Ruth whisper , with she back still to I ; ' but many people will not believe it . ' 
 here mother come up to my rescue , as she always love to do ; and she say , ' if my son may not dance Miss Ruth , at any rate he may dance with she . we have only be wait for you , dear John , to have a little harvest dance , with the kitchen door throw open . you take Ruth ; Uncle Ben take Sally ; Master Debby pair off with Polly ; and neighbour Nicholas will be good enough , if I can awake he , to stand up with fair Mistress Kebby . Lizzie will play we the virginal . will not   you , Lizzie dear ? ' 
 ' but who be to dance with you , madam ? ' Uncle Ben ask , very politely . ' I think you must rearrange your figure . I have not dance for a score of year ; and I will not dance now , while the mistress and the owner of the harvest sit aside neglect . ' 
 ' Nay , Master Huckaback , ' cry Sally Snowe , with a saucy toss of her hair ; ' Mistress Ridd be too kind a great deal , in hand you over to I . you take she ; and I will fetch Annie to be my partner this evening . I like dance very much well with girl , for they never squeeze and rumple one . oh , it be so much nicer ! ' 
 ' have no fear for I , my dear , ' our mother answer smile : ' Parson Bowden promise to come back again ; I expect he every minute ; and he intend to lead I off , and to bring a partner for Annie too , a very pretty young gentleman . now begin ; and I will join you . ' 
 there be no disobeying she , without rudeness ; and indeed the girl ' foot be already jig ; and Lizzie give herself wonderful air with a roll of learn music ; and even while Annie be do my collop , her pretty round instep be arch itself , as I could see from the parlour - door . so I take little Ruth , and I spin she around , as the sound of the music come lively and ringing ; and after we come all the rest with much laughter , beg I not to jump over she ; and anon my grave partner begin to smile sweetly , and look up at I with the bright of eye , and drop I the prettiest curtsey ; till I think what a great stupe I must have be to dream of put she in the cheese - rack . but one thing I could not at all understand ; why mother , who use to do all in her power to throw I across Sally Snowe , should now do the very opposite ; for she would not allow I one moment with Sally , not even to cross in the dance , or whisper , or go anywhere near a corner   which as I say , I intend to do , just by way of practice , while she keep I , all the evening , as close as possible with Ruth Huckaback , and come up and praise I so to Ruth , time and again , that I declare I be quite ashamed . although of course I know that I deserve it all , but I could not well say that . 
 then Annie come sail down the dance , with her beautiful hair flow round she ; the light figure in all the room , and the sweetest , and the lovely . she be blush , with her fair cheek red beneath her dear blue eye , as she meet my glance of surprise and grief at the partner she be lean on . it be Squire Marwood de Whichehalse . I would soon have see she with Tom Faggus , as indeed I have expect , when I hear of Parson Bowden . and to I it seem that she have no right to be dance so with any other ; and to this effect I contrive to whisper ; but she only say , ' see to yourself , John . no , but let we both enjoy ourselves . you be not dance with Lorna , John . but you seem uncommonly happy . ' 
 ' tush , ' I say ; ' could I flip about so , if I have my love with I ? ' 




 CHAPTER XXXI 

 JOHN FRY 'S ERRAND 

 we keep up the dance very late that night , mother be in such wonderful spirit , that she would not hear of our go to bed : while she glance from young Squire Marwood , very deep in his talk with our Annie , to I and Ruth Huckaback who be begin to be very pleasant company . alas , poor mother , so proud as she be , how little she dream that her good scheme already be hopelessly go awry ! 
 be force to be up before daylight next day , in order to begin right early , I would not go to my bedroom that night for fear of disturb my mother , but determine to sleep in the tallat awhile , that place be cool , and airy , and refresh with the smell of sweet hay . moreover , after my dwelling in town , where I have feel like a horse on a lime - kiln , I could not for a length of time have enough of country life . the mooing of a calf be music , and the chuckle of a fowl be wit , and the snore of the horse be news to I . 
 ' Wult have thee own wai , I reckon , ' say Betty , be cross with sleepiness , for she have wash up everything ; ' slape in hog - pound , if thee laike , Jan. ' 
 let she have the last word of it   as be the due of woman   I stand in the court , and wonder awhile at the glory of the harvest moon , and the yellow world it shine upon . then I see , as sure as ever I be stand there in the shadow of the stable , I see a short wide figure glide across the foot of the courtyard , between I and the six - bar gate . instead of run after it , as I should have do , I begin to consider who it could be , and what on earth be do there , when all our people be in bed , and the reaper go home , or to the linhay close against the wheatfield . 
 have make up my mind at last , that it could be none of our people though not a dog be bark and also that it must have be either a girl or a woman , I run down with all speed to learn what might be the meaning of it . but I come too late to learn , through my own hesitation , for this be the low end of the courtyard , not the approach from the parish highway , but the end of the sledd - way , across the field where the brook go down to the Lynn stream , and where Squire Faggus have save the old drake . and of course the dry channel of the brook , be scarcely any water now , afford plenty of place to hide , lead also to a little coppice , beyond our cabbage - garden , and so far on to the parish highway . 
 I see at once that it be vain to make any pursuit by moonlight ; and resolve to hold my own counsel about it   though puzzle not a little   and to keep watch there another night , back I return to the tallatt - ladder , and sleep without leave off till morning . 
 now many people may wish to know , as indeed I myself do very greatly , what have bring Master Huckaback over from Dulverton , at that time of year , when the clothing business be most active on account of harvest wage , and when the new wheat be begin to sample from the early part up the country   for he meddle as well in corn - dealing   and when we could not attend to he properly by reason of our occupation . and yet more surprising it seem to I that he should have bring his granddaughter also , instead of the troop of dragoon , without which he have vow he would never come here again . and how he have manage to enter the house together with his granddaughter , and be sit quite at home in the parlour there , without any knowledge or even suspicion on my part . that last question be easily solve , for mother herself have admit they by mean of the little passage , during a chorus of the harvest - song which might have drown an earthquake : but as for his meaning and motive , and apparent neglect of his business , none but himself could interpret they ; and as he do not see fit to do so , we could not be rude enough to inquire . 
 he seem in no hurry to take his departure , though his visit be so inconvenient to we , as himself indeed must have notice : and presently Lizzie , who be the sharp among we , say in my hearing that she believe he have purposely time his visit so that he might have liberty to pursue his own object , whatsoever it be , without interruption from we . Mother gaze hard upon Lizzie at this , have form a very different opinion ; but Annie and myself agree that it be worth look into . 
 now how could we look into it , without watch Uncle Reuben , whenever he go abroad , and try to catch he in his speech , when he be take his ease at night . for , in spite of all the disgust with which he have speak of harvest wassailing , there be not a man come into our kitchen who like it well than he do ; only in a quiet way , and without too many witness . now to endeavour to get at the purpose of any guest , even a treacherous one   which we have no right to think Uncle Reuben   by mean of observe he in his cup , be a thing which even the low of people would regard with abhorrence . and to my mind it be not clear whether it would be fair - play at all to follow a visitor even at a distance from home and clear of our premise ; except for the purpose of fetch he back , and give he more to go on with . nevertheless we could not but think , the time be wild and disjoint , that Uncle Ben be not use fairly the part of a guest in our house , to make long expedition we know not whither , and involve we in trouble we know not what . 
 for his mode be directly after breakfast to pray to the Lord a little   which use not to be his practice , and then to go forth upon dolly , the which be our Annie 's pony , very quiet and respectful , with a bag of good victual hang behind he , and two great cavalry pistol in front . and he always wear his mean clothe as if expect to be rob , or to disarm the temptation thereto ; and he never take his golden chronometer neither his bag of money . so much the girl find out and tell I   for I be never at home myself by day ; and they very craftily spur I on , have less noble idea perhaps , to hit upon Uncle Reuben 's track , and follow , and see what become of he . for he never return until dark or more , just in time to be in before we , who be come home from the harvest . and then dolly always seem very weary , and stain with a muck from beyond our parish . 
 but I refuse to follow he , not only for the loss of a day 's work to myself , and at least half a day to the other man , but chiefly because I could not think that it would be upright and manly . it be all very well to creep warily into the valley of the Doones , and heed everything around I , both because they be public enemy , and also because I risk my life at every step I take there . but as to track a feeble old man   however subtle he might be , a guest moreover of our own , and a relative through my mother . ' once for all , ' I say , ' it be below I , and I will not   do it . ' 
 thereupon , the girl , know my way , cease to torment I about it : but what be my astonishment the very next day to perceive that instead of fourteen reaper , we be only thirteen left , directly our breakfast be do with or mower rather I should say , for we be go into the barley now . 
 ' who have be and leave his scythe ? ' I ask ; ' and here be a tin cup never be handle ! ' 
 ' Whoy , dudn't   ee knaw , Maister Jan , ' say Bill Dadds , look at I queerly , ' as Jan Vry wur gane avore braxvass . ' 
 ' oh , very well , ' I answer , ' John know what he be do . ' for John Fry be a kind of foreman now , and it would not do to say anything that might lessen his authority . however , I make up my mind to rope he , when I should catch he by himself , without peril to his dignity . 
 but when I come home in the evening , late and almost weary , there be no Annie cook my supper , nor Lizzie by the fire reading , nor even little Ruth Huckaback watch the shadow and ponder . upon this , I go to the girl ' room , not in the very good of temper , and there I find all three of they in the little place set apart for Annie , eagerly listen to John Fry , who be tell some great adventure . John have a great jug of ale beside he , and a horn well drain ; and he clearly look upon himself as a hero , and the maid seem to be of the same opinion . 
 ' well do , John , ' my sister be say , ' capitally do , John Fry . how very brave you have be , John . now quick , let we hear the rest of it . ' 
 ' what do all this nonsense mean ? ' I say , in a voice which frighten they , as I could see by the light of our own mutton candle : ' John Fry , you be off to your wife at once , or you shall have what I owe you now , instead of to - morrow morning . ' 
 John make no answer , but scratch his head , and look at the maiden to take his part . 
 ' it be you that must be off , I think , ' say Lizzie , look straight at I with all the impudence in the world ; ' what right have you to come in here to the young lady ' room , without an invitation even ? ' 
 ' very well , Miss Lizzie , I suppose mother have some right here . ' and with that , I be go away to fetch she , know that she always take my side , and never would allow the house to be turn upside down in that manner . but Annie catch hold of I by the arm , and little Ruth stand in the doorway ; and Lizzie say , ' do not   be a fool , John . we know thing of you , you know ; a great deal more than you dream of . ' 
 upon this I glance at Annie , to learn whether she have be tell , but her pure true face reassure I at once , and then she say very gently , 
 ' Lizzie , you talk too fast , my child . no one know anything of our John which he need be ashamed of ; and work as he do from light to dusk , and earn the living of all of we , he be entitle to choose his own good time for go out and for come in , without consult a little girl five year young than himself . now , John , sit down , and you shall know all that we have do , though I doubt whether you will approve of it . ' 
 upon this I kiss Annie , and so do Ruth ; and John Fry look a deal more comfortable , but Lizzie only make a face at we . then Annie begin as follow : 
 ' you must know , dear John , that we have be extremely curious , ever since Uncle Reuben come , to know what he be come for , especially at this time of year , when he be at his busy . he never vouchsafe any explanation , neither give any reason , true or false , which show his entire ignorance of all feminine nature . if Ruth have know , and refuse to tell we , we should have be much easy , because we must have get it out of Ruth before two or three day be over . but darling Ruth know no more than we do , and indeed I must do she the justice to say that she have be quite as inquisitive . well , we might have put up with it , if it have not be for his take dolly , my own pet dolly , away every morning , quite as if she belong to he , and keep she out until close upon dark , and then bring her home in a frightful condition . and he even have the impudence , when I tell he that dolly be my pony , to say that we owe he a pony , ever since you take from he that little horse upon which you find he strap so snugly ; and he mean to take dolly to Dulverton with he , to run in his little cart . if there be law in the land he shall not . surely , John , you will not let he ? ' 
 ' that I will not , ' say I , ' except upon the condition which I offer he once before . if we owe he the pony , we owe he the strap . ' 
 Sweet Annie laugh , like a bell , at this , and then she go on with her story . 
 ' well , John , we be perfectly miserable . you can not understand it , of course ; but I use to go every evening , and hug poor dolly , and kiss she , and beg she to tell I where she have be , and what she have see , that day . but never have belong to Balaam , darling dolly be quite unsuccessful , though often she strove to tell I , with her ear down , and both eye roll . then I make John Fry tie her tail in a knot , with a piece of white ribbon , as if for adornment , that I might trace she among the hill , at any rate for a mile or two . but Uncle Ben be too deep for that ; he cut off the ribbon before he start , say he would have no Doones after he . and then , in despair , I apply to you , know how quick of foot you be , and I get Ruth and Lizzie to help I , but you answer we very shortly ; and a very poor supper you have that night , accord to your desert . 
 ' but though we be dash to the ground for a time , we be not wholly discomfited . our determination to know all about it seem to increase with the difficulty . and Uncle Ben 's manner last night be so dry , when we try to romp and to lead he out , that it be much bad than Jamaica ginger grate into a poor spray finger . so we send he to bed at the early moment , and hold a small council upon he . if you remember you , John , have now take to smoke   which be a hateful practice , have go forth grumble about your bad supper and not take it as a good lesson . ' 
 ' why , Annie , ' I cry , in amazement at this , ' I will never trust you again for a supper . I think you be so sorry . ' 
 ' and so I be , dear ; very sorry . but still we must do our duty . and when we come to consider it , Ruth be the cleverest of we all ; for she say that surely we must have some man we could trust about the farm to go on a little errand ; and then I remember that old John Fry would do anything for money . ' 
 ' not for money , plaize , miss , ' say John Fry , take a pull at the beer ; ' but for the love of your swate face . ' 
 ' to be sure , John ; with the king 's behind it . and so Lizzie run for John Fry at once , and we give he full direction , how he be to slip out of the barley in the confusion of the breakfast , so that none might miss he ; and to run back to the black combe bottom , and there he would find the very same pony which Uncle Ben have be tie upon , and there be no fast upon the farm . and then , without wait for any breakfast unless he could eat it either run or trot , he be to travel all up the black combe , by the track Uncle Reuben have take , and up at the top to look forward carefully , and so to trace he without be see . ' 
 ' ay ; and raight wull a doo'd un , ' John cry , with his mouth in the bullock 's horn . 
 ' well , and what do you see , John ? ' I ask , with great anxiety ; though I mean to have show no interest . 
 ' John be just at the very point of it , ' Lizzie answer I sharply , ' when you choose to come in and stop he . ' 
 ' then let he begin again , ' say I ; ' thing be go so far , it be now my duty to know everything , for the sake of you girl and mother . ' 
 ' Hem ! ' cry Lizzie , in a nasty way ; but I take no notice of she , for she be always bad to deal with . therefore John Fry begin again , be heartily glad to do so , that his story might get out of the tumble which all our talk have make in it . but as he could not tell a tale in the manner of my Lorna   although he tell it very well for those who understand he   I will take it from his mouth altogether , and state in brief what happen . 
 when John , upon his forest pony , which he have much ado to hold   its mouth be like a bucket , be come to the top of the long black combe , two mile or more from Plover 's barrow , and wind to the southward , he stop his little nag short of the crest , and get off and look ahead of he , from behind a tump of whortle . it be a long flat sweep of moorland over which he be gaze , with a few bog here and there , and brushy place round they . of course , John Fry , from his shepherd life and reclaim of stray cattle , know as well as need be where he be , and the spread of the hill before he , although it be beyond our beat , or , rather , I should say , beside it . not but what we might have graze there have it be our pleasure , but that it be not worth our while , and scarcely worth Jasper Kebby 's even ; all the land be crop   as one might say   with desolation . and nearly all our knowledge of it spring from the unaccountable trick of cow who have young calf with they ; at which time they have wild desire to get away from the sight of man , and keep calf and milk for one another , although it be in a barren land . at least , our cow have get this trick , and I have hear other people complain of it . 
 John Fry , as I say , know the place well enough , but he like it none the more for that , neither do any of our people ; and , indeed , all the neighbourhood of Thomshill and Larksborough , and most of all Black Barrow Down lie under grave imputation of have be enchant with a very evil spell . moreover , it be know , though folk be loath to speak of it , even on a summer morning , that Squire Thom , who have be murder there , a century ago or more , have be see by several shepherd , even in the middle day , walk with his severed head carry in his left hand , and his right arm lift towards the sun . 
 therefore it be very bold in John   as I acknowledge   to venture across that moor alone , even with a fast pony under he , and some whisky by his side . and he would never have do so   of that I be quite certain , either for the sake of Annie 's sweet face , or of the golden guinea , which the three maiden have subscribe to reward his skill and valour . but the truth be that he could not resist his own great curiosity . for , carefully spy across the moor , from behind the tuft of whortle , at first he could discover nothing have life and motion , except three or four wild cattle rove in vain search for nourishment , and a diseased sheep banish hither , and some carrion crow keep watch on she . but when John be take his very last look , be only too glad to go home again , and acknowledge himself baffle , he think he see a figure move in the farth distance upon Black Barrow down , scarcely a thing to be sure of yet , on account of the want of colour . but as he watch , the figure pass between he and a naked cliff , and appear to be a man on horseback , make his way very carefully , in fear of bog and serpent . for all about there it be adder ' ground , and large black serpent dwell in the marsh , and can swim as well as crawl . 
 John know that the man who be rid there could be none but Uncle Reuben , for none of the Doones ever pass that way , and the shepherd be afraid of it . and now it seem an unkind place for an unarmed man to venture through , especially after an armed one who might not like to be spy upon , and must have some dark object in visit such drear solitude . nevertheless John Fry so ache with unbearable curiosity to know what an old man , and a stranger , and a rich man , and a peaceable could possibly be after in that mysterious manner . moreover , John so throb with hope to find some wealthy secret , that come what would of it he resolve to go to the end of the matter . 
 therefore he only wait awhile for fear of be discover , till Master Huckaback turn to the left and enter a little gully , whence he could not survey the moor . then John remount and cross the rough land and the stony place , and pick his way among the morass as fast as ever he dare to go ; until , in about half an hour , he draw nigh the entrance of the gully . and now it behove he to be most wary ; for Uncle Ben might have stop in there , either to rest his horse or have reach the end of his journey . and in either case , John have little doubt that he himself would be pistol , and nothing more ever hear of he . therefore he make his pony come to the mouth of it sideways , and lean over and peer in around the rocky corner , while the little horse crop at the briar . 
 but he soon perceive that the gully be empty , so far at least as its course be straight ; and with that he hasten into it , though his heart be not work easily . when he have trace the wind hollow for half a mile or more , he see that it fork , and one part lead to the leave up a steep red bank , and the other to the right , be narrow and slightly tend downward . some yellow sand lie here and there between the starve grass , and this he examine narrowly for a trace of Master Huckaback . 
 at last he see that , beyond all doubt , the man he be pursue have take the course which lead down hill ; and down the hill he must follow he . and this John do with deep misgiving , and a hearty wish that he have never start upon so perilous an errand . for now he know not where he be , and scarcely dare to ask himself , have hear of a horrible hole , somewhere in this neighbourhood , call the Wizard 's Slough . therefore John ride down the slope , with sorrow , and great caution . and these grow more as he go onward , and his pony rear against he , be scar , although a native of the roughest moorland . and John have just make up his mind that God mean this for a warning , as the passage seem dark and deep , when suddenly he turn a corner , and see a scene which stop he . 
 for there be the Wizard 's Slough itself , as black as death , and bubble , with a few scant yellow reed in a ring around it . outside these , bright water - grass of the lively green be creep , tempt any unwary foot to step , and plunge , and founder . and on the marge be blue campanula , sundew , and forget - I - not , such as no child could resist . on either side , the hill fall back , and the ground be break with tuft of rush , and flag , and mare - tail , and a few rough alder - tree overclogged with water . and not a bird be see or hear , neither rail nor water - hen , wag - tail nor reed - warbler . 
 of this horrible quagmire , the bad upon all Exmoor , John have hear from his grandfather , and even from his mother , when they want to keep he quiet ; but his father have fear to speak of it to he , be a man of piety , and up to the trick of the evil one . this make John the more desirous to have a good look at it now , only with his girth well up , to turn away and flee at speed , if anything should happen . and now he prove how well it be to be wary and wide - awake , even in lonesome place . for at the other side of the Slough , and a few land - yard beyond it , where the ground be less noisome , he have observe a felled tree lie over a great hole in the earth , with stave of wood , and slab of stone , and some yellow gravel around it . but the flag of reed around the morass partly screen it from his eye , and he could not make out the meaning of it , except that it mean no good , and probably be witchcraft . yet dolly seem not to be harm by it , for there she be as large as life , tie to a stump not far beyond , and flip the fly away with her tail . 
 while John be tremble within himself , lest dolly should get scent of his pony , and neigh and reveal their presence , although she could not see they , suddenly to his great amazement something white arise out of the hole , under the brown trunk of the tree . see this his blood go back within he , yet he be not able to turn and flee , but root his face in among the loose stone , and keep his quivering shoulder back , and pray to God to protect he . however , the white thing itself be not so very awful , be nothing more than a long - coned night - cap with a tassel on the top , such as criminal wear at hang - time . but when John see a man 's face under it , and a man 's neck and shoulder slowly rise out of the pit , he could not doubt that this be the place where the murderer come to life again , accord to the Exmoor story . he know that a man have be hang last week , and that this be the ninth day after it . 
 therefore he could bear no more , thoroughly brave as he have be , neither do he wait to see what become of the gallow - man ; but climb on his horse with what speed he might , and ride away at full gallop . neither do he dare go back by the way he come , fear to face Black Barrow down ! therefore he strike up the other track lead away towards Cloven Rocks , and after rid hard for an hour and drink all his whisky , he luckily fall in with a shepherd , who lead he on to a public - house somewhere near Exeford . and here he be so unmanned , the excitement be over , that nothing less than a gallon of ale and half a gammon of bacon , bring he to his right mind again . and he take good care to be home before dark , have follow a well - know sheep track . 
 when John Fry finish his story at last , after many exclamation from Annie , and from Lizzie , and much praise of his gallantry , yet some little disappointment that he have not stay there a little long , while he be about it , so as to be able to tell we more , I say to he very sternly , 
 ' now , John , you have dream half this , my man . I firmly believe that you fall asleep at the top of the black combe , after drink all your whisky , and never go on the moor at all . you know what a liar you be , John . ' 
 the girl be exceedingly angry at this , and lay their hand before my mouth ; but I wait for John to answer , with my eye fix upon he steadfastly . 
 ' bain't   for I to denai , ' say John , look at I very honestly , ' but what a maight tull a lai , now and awhile , zame as other man doth , and most of arl they as spak again it ; but this here be no lai , Maister Jan. I wush to God it wor , boy : a maight slape this naight the well . ' 
 ' I believe you speak the truth , John ; and I ask your pardon . now not a word to any one , about this strange affair . there be mischief brewing , I can see ; and it be my place to attend to it . several thing come across I now only I will not tell you . ' 
 they be not at all content with this ; but I would give they no well ; except to say , when they plague I greatly , and vow to sleep at my door all night , 
 ' now , my dear , this be foolish of you . too much of this matter be know already . it be for your own dear sake that I be bind to be cautious . I have an opinion of my own ; but it may be a very wrong one ; I will not ask you to share it with I ; neither will I make you inquisitive . ' 
 Annie pout , and Lizzie frown , and Ruth look at I with her eye wide open , but no other mark of regard I . and I see that if any one of the three   for John Fry be go home with the tremble   could be trust to keep a secret , that one be Ruth Huckaback . 




 CHAPTER XXXII 

 feeding of the pigs 

 the story tell by John Fry that night , and my conviction of its truth , make I very uneasy , especially as follow upon the warning of Judge Jeffreys , and the hint receive from Jeremy Stickles , and the outburst of the tanner at Dunster , as well as sundry tale and rumour , and sign of secret understanding , see and hear on market - day , and at place of entertainment . we know for certain that at Taunton , Bridgwater , and even Dulverton , there be much disaffection towards the king , and regret for the day of the Puritans . albeit I have tell the truth , and the pure and simple truth , when , upon my examination , I have assure his lordship , that to the good of my knowledge there be nothing of the sort with we . 
 but now I be begin to doubt whether I might not have be mistake ; especially when we hear , as we do , of arm be land at Lynmouth , in the dead of the night , and of the tramp of man have reach some one 's ear , from a hill where a famous echo be . for it must be plain to any conspirator   without the example of the Doones   that for the secret muster of man and the stowing of unlawful arm , and communication by beacon light , scarcely a fitter place could be find than the wild of Exmoor , with deep ravine run far inland from an unwatched and mostly a sheltered sea . for the Channel from Countisbury Foreland up to Minehead , or even far , though rocky , and gusty , and full of current , be safe from great roller and the sweeping power of the south - west storm , which prevail with we more than all the other , and make sad work on the opposite coast . 
 but even suppose it probable that something against King Charles the Second   or rather against his roman adviser , and especially his brother   be now in preparation amongst we , be it likely that Master Huckaback , a wealthy man , and a careful one , know moreover to the Lord Chief Justice , would have anything to do with it ? to this I could make no answer ; Uncle Ben be so close a man , so avaricious , and so revengeful , that it be quite impossible to say what course he might pursue , without know all the chance of gain , or rise , or satisfaction to he . that he hat the Papists I know full well , though he never speak much about they ; also that he have follow the march of Oliver Cromwell 's army , but more as a suttler   people say   than as a real soldier ; and that he would go a long way , and risk a great deal of money , to have his revenge on the Doones ; although their name never pass his lip during the present visit . 
 but how be it likely to be as to the Doones themselves ? which side would they probably take in the come movement , if movement indeed it would be ? so far as they have any religion at all , by birth they be Roman Catholics so much I know from Lorna ; and indeed it be well know all around , that a priest have be fetch more than once to the valley , to soothe some poor outlaw 's departure . on the other hand , they be not likely to entertain much affection for the son of the man who have banish they and confiscate their property . and it be not at all impossible that desperate man , such as they be , have nothing to lose , but estate to recover , and not be hold by religion much , should cast away all regard for the birth from which they have be cast out , and make common cause with a protestant rise , for the chance of revenge and replacement . 
 however I do not mean to say that all these thing occur to I as clearly as I have set they down ; only that I be in general doubt , and very sad perplexity . for mother be so warm , and innocent , and kind so to every one , that know some little by this time of the english constitution , I fear very greatly lest she should be punish for harbouring malcontent . as well as possible I know , that if any poor man come to our door , and cry , ' officer be after I ; for God 's sake take and hide I , ' mother would take he in at once , and conceal , and feed he , even though he have be very violent ; and , to tell the truth , so would both my sister , and so indeed would I do . whence it will be clear that we be not the sort of people to be safe among disturbance . 
 before I could quite make up my mind how to act in this difficulty , and how to get at the right of it   for I would not spy after Uncle Reuben , though I feel no great fear of the Wizard 's Slough , and none of the man with the white night - cap , a difference come again upon it , and a change of chance . for Uncle Ben go away as suddenly as he first have come to we , give no reason for his departure , neither claim the pony , and indeed leave something behind he of great value to my mother . for he beg she to see to his young grand - daughter , until he could find opportunity of fetch she safely to Dulverton . Mother be overjoy at this , as she could not help display ; and Ruth be quite as much delighted , although she durst not show it . for at Dulverton she have to watch and keep such ward on the victual , and the in and out of the shopman , that it go entirely against her heart , and she never could enjoy herself . truly she be an altered girl from the day she come to we ; catch our unsuspicious manner , and our free goodwill , and hearty noise of laugh . 
 by this time , the harvest be do , and the thatch of the rick make sure against south - western tempest , and all the reaper be go , with good money and thankfulness , I begin to burn in spirit for the sight of Lorna . I have beg my sister Annie to let Sally Snowe know , once for all , that it be not in my power to have any thing more to do with she . of course our Annie be not to grieve Sally , neither to let it appear for a moment that I suspect her kind view upon I , and her strong regard for our dairy : only I think it right upon our part not to waste Sally 's time any long , be a handsome wench as she be , and many young fellow glad to marry she . 
 and Annie do this uncommonly well , as she herself tell I afterwards , have take Sally in the sweetest manner into her pure confidence , and open half her bosom to she , about my very sad love affair . not that she let Sally know , of course , who it be , or what it be ; only that she make she understand , without hint at any desire of it , that there be no chance now of have I . Sally change colour a little at this , and then go on about a red cow which have pass seven needle at milk time . 
 Inasmuch as there be two sort of month well recognise by the calendar , to wit the lunar and the solar , I make bold to regard both my month , in the absence of any provision , as intend to be strictly lunar . therefore upon the very day when the eight week be expire forth I go in search of Lorna , take the pearl ring hopefully , and all the new - lay egg I could find , and a dozen and a half of small trout from our brook . and the pleasure it give I to catch those trout , think as every one come forth and dance upon the grass , how much she would enjoy he , be more than I can now describe , although I well remember it . and it strike I that after accept my ring , and say how much she love I , it be possible that my queen might invite I even to stay and sup with she : and so I arrange with dear Annie beforehand , who be now the great comfort to I , to account for my absence if I should be late . 
 but alas , I be utterly disappoint ; for although I wait and wait for hour , with an equal amount both of patience and peril , no Lorna ever appear at all , nor even the faint sign of she . and another thing occur as well , which vex I more than it need have do , for so small a matter . and this be that my little offering of the trout and the new - lay egg be carry off in the cool manner by that vile Carver Doone . for think to keep they the fresher and nice , away from so much handling , I lay they in a little bed of reed by the side of the water , and place some dog - leaf over they . and when I have quite forget about they , and be watch from my hiding - place beneath the willow - tree   for I like not to enter Lorna 's bower , without her permission ; except just to peep that she be not there , and while I be turn the ring in my pocket , have just see the new moon , I become aware of a great man come eisurely down the valley . he have a broad - brim hat , and a leather jerkin , and heavy jack - boot to his middle thigh , and what be bad of all for I , on his shoulder he bear a long carbine . have nothing to meet he withal but my staff , and desire to avoid disturbance , I retire promptly into the chasm , keep the tree betwixt we that he might not descry I , and watch from behind the jut of a rock , where now I have scrap myself a neat little hole for the purpose . 
 presently the great man reappear , be now within fifty yard of I , and the light still good enough , as he draw near for I to descry his feature : and though I be not a judge of man 's face , there be something in his which turn I cold , as though with a kind of horror . not that it be an ugly face ; nay , rather it seem a handsome one , so far as mere form and line might go , full of strength , and vigour , and will , and steadfast resolution . from the short black hair above the broad forehead , to the long black beard descend below the curt , bold chin , there be not any curve or glimpse of weakness or of afterthought . nothing playful , nothing pleasant , nothing with a track of smile ; nothing which a friend could like , and laugh at he for have . and yet he might have be a good man   for I have know very good man so fortified by their own strange idea of God : I say that he might have seem a good man , but for the cold and cruel hankering of his steel - blue eye . 
 now let no one suppose for a minute that I see all this in a moment ; for I be very slow , and take a long time to digest thing ; only I like to set down , and have do with it , all the result of my knowledge , though they be not manifold . but what I say to myself , just then , be no more than this : ' what a fellow to have Lorna ! ' have my sense of right so outraged   although , of course , I would never allow she to go so far as that , I almost long that he might thrust his head in to look after I . for there I be , with my ash staff club , ready to have at he , and not ill incline to do so ; if only he would come where strength , not firearm , must decide it . however , he suspect nothing of my dangerous neighbourhood , but walk his round like a sentinel , and turn at the brink of the water . 
 then as he march back again , along the margin of the stream , he espy my little hoard , cover up with dog - leaf . he see that the leaf be upside down , and this of course draw his attention . I see he stoop , and lie bare the fish , and the egg set a little way from they and in my simple heart , I think that now he know all about I . but to my surprise , he seem well - pleased ; and his harsh short laughter come to I without echo , 
 ' ha , ha ! Charlie boy ! Fisherman Charlie , have I catch thee set bait for Lorna ? now , I understand thy fishing , and the robbing of Counsellor 's hen roost . may I never have good roasting , if I have it not to - night and roast thee , Charlie , afterwards ! ' 
 with this he calmly pack up my fish , and all the good of dear Annie 's egg ; and go away chuckle steadfastly , to his home , if one may call it so . but I be so thoroughly grieve and mortify by this most impudent robbery , that I start forth from my rocky screen with the intention of pursue he , until my well sense arrest I , barely in time to escape his eye . for I say to myself , that even suppose I could contend unarmed with he , it would be the great folly in the world to have my secret access know , and perhaps a fatal barrier place between Lorna and myself , and I know not what trouble bring upon she , all for the sake of a few egg and fish . it be well to bear this trifling loss , however ignominious and goad to the spirit , than to risk my love and Lorna 's welfare , and perhaps be shoot into the bargain . and I think that all will agree with I , that I act for the wise , in withdraw to my shelter , though deprive of egg and fish . 
 have wait   as I say   until there be no chance whatever of my love appear , I hasten homeward very sadly ; and the wind of early autumn moan across the moorland . all the beauty of the harvest , all the gaiety be go , and the early fall of dusk be like a weight upon I . nevertheless , I go every evening thenceforward for a fortnight ; hop , every time in vain to find my hope and comfort . and meanwhile , what perplex I most be that the signal be replace , in order as agree upon , so that Lorna could scarcely be restrain by any rigour . 
 one time I have a narrow chance of be shoot and settle with ; and it befell I thus . I be wait very carelessly , be now a little desperate , at the entrance to the glen , instead of watch through my sight - hole , as the proper practice be . suddenly a ball go by I , with a whizz and whistle , pass through my hat and sweep it away all fold up . my soft hat flutter far down the stream , before I have time to go after it , and with the help of both wind and water , be fifty yard go in a moment . at this I have just enough mind leave to shrink back very suddenly , and lurk very still and closely ; for I know what a narrow escape it have be , as I hear the bullet , hard set by the powder , sing mournfully down the chasm , like a drone banish out of the hive . and as I peer through my little cranny , I see a wreath of smoke still float where the thickness be of the withy - bed ; and presently Carver Doone come forth , have stop to reload his piece perhaps , and run very swiftly to the entrance to see what he have shoot . 
 sore trouble have I to keep close quarter , from the slipperiness of the stone beneath I with the water slide over it . my foe come quite to the verge of the fall , where the river begin to comb over ; and there he stop for a minute or two , on the utmost edge of dry land , upon the very spot indeed where I have fall senseless when I clomb it in my boyhood . I could hear he breathe hard and grunt , as in doubt and discontent , for he stand within a yard of I , and I keep my right fist ready for he , if he should discover I . then at the foot of the waterslide , my black hat suddenly appear , toss in white foam , and flutter like a raven wound . now I have doubt which hat to take , when I leave home that day ; till I think that the black become I good , and might seem kind to Lorna . 
 ' have I kill thee , old bird , at last ? ' my enemy cry in triumph ; " tis the third time I have shoot at thee , and thou wast beginning to mock I . no more of thy cursed croaking now , to wake I in the morning . ha , ha ! there be not many who get three chance from Carver Doone ; and none ever go beyond it . ' 
 I laugh within myself at this , as he stride away in his triumph ; for be not this his third chance of I , and he no whit the wiser ? and then I think that perhaps the chance might some day be on the other side . 
 for to tell the truth , I be heartily tired of lurk and play bo - peep so long ; to which nothing could have reconcile I , except my fear for Lorna . and here I see be a man of strength fit for I to encounter , such as I have never meet , but would be glad to meet with ; have find no man of late who need not my mercy at wrestling , or at single - stick . and grow more and more uneasy , as I find no Lorna , I would have try to force the Doone Glen from the upper end , and take my chance of get back , but for Annie and her prayer . 
 now that same night I think it be , or at any rate the next one , that I notice Betty Muxworthy go on most strangely . she make the queer sign to I , when nobody be look , and lay her finger on her lip , and point over her shoulder . but I take little heed of she , be in a kind of dudgeon , and oppress with evil luck ; believe too that all she want be to have some little grumble about some petty grievance . 
 but presently she poke I with the heel of a fire - bundle , and pass close to my ear whisper , so that none else could hear she , ' Larna Doo - un . ' 
 by these word I be so startled , that I turn round and star at she ; but she pretend not to know it , and begin with all her might to scour an empty crock with a besom . 
 ' oh , Betty , let I help you ! that work be much too hard for you , ' I cry with a sudden chivalry , which only win rude answer . 
 ' zeed I adooe of thic , every naight last ten year , Jan , wiout vindin ' out how hard it wor . but if zo bee thee want to help , carr peg 's bucket for I . Massy , if I be not   forget to fade the peg till now . ' 
 favour I with another wink , to which I now pay the keen heed , Betty go and fetch the lanthorn from the hook inside the door . then when she have kindle it , not allow I any time to ask what she be after , she go outside , and point to the great bock of wash , and riddling , and brown hulkage   for we ground our own corn always , and though she know that Bill Dadds and Jem Slocombe have full work to carry it on a pole   with another to help to sle it , she say to I as quietly as a maiden might ask one to carry a glove , ' Jan Ridd , carr thic thing for I . ' 
 so I carry it for she , without any word ; wonder what she be up to next , and whether she have ever hear of be too hard on the willing horse . and when we come to hog - pound , she turn upon I suddenly , with the lanthorn she be bear , and see that I have the bock by one hand very easily . 
 ' Jan Ridd , ' she say , ' there be no other man in England cud a ' dood it . now thee shalt have Larna . ' 
 while I be wonder how my chance of have Lorna could depend upon my power to carry pig 's wash , and how Betty could have any voice in the matter   which seem to depend upon her decision , and in short , while I be all abroad as to her knowledge and everything , the pig , who have be fast asleep and dream in their emptiness , awake with one accord at the goodness of the smell around they . they have resign themselves , as even pig do , to a kind of fast , hop to break their fast more sweetly on the morrow morning . but now they tumble out all headlong , pig below and pig above , pig point - blank and pig across , pig courant and pig rampant , but all alike prepare to eat , and all in good cadence squeaking . 
 ' tak smarl boocket , and bale un out ; wad ' e waste sich stoof as thic here be ? ' so Betty set I to feed the pig , while she hold the lanthorn ; and know what she be , I see that she would not tell I another word until all the pig be serve . and in truth no man could well look at they , and delay to serve they , they be all express appetite in so forcible a manner ; some run to and fro , and rub , and squealing as if from starvation , some rush down to the oaken trough , and poke each other away from they ; and the kind of all put up their fore - foot on the top - rail on the hog - pound , and blink their little eye , and grunt prettily to coax we ; as who would say , ' I trust you now ; you will be kind , I know , and give I the first and the very good of it . ' 
 ' Oppen ge - at now , wull ' e , Jan ? Maind , young sow wi ' the baible back arlway hath first toorn of it , 'cos I bring she up on my lap , I do . Zuck , zuck , zuck ! how her stickth her tail up ; do I good to zee un ! now thiccy trough , thee zany , and tak thee girt leg out o ' the wai . wish they wud gie thee a good baite , mak thee hop a bit vaster , I reckon . hit that there girt ozebird over 's back wi ' the broomstick , he be rob of my young zow . Choog , choog , choog ! and a drap more left in the dripping - pail . ' 
 ' come now , Betty , ' I say , when all the pig be at it suck , swill , munching , guzzling , thrusting , and oust , and spill the food upon the back of their brother   as great man do with their charity , ' come now , Betty , how much long be I to wait for your message ? surely I be as good as a pig . ' 
 ' dunno as thee be , Jan. no straikiness in thy bakkon . and now I come to think of it , Jan , thee ze , a wake agone last Vriday , as how I have get a girt be - ard . wull ' e stick to that now , Maister Jan ? ' 
 ' no , no , Betty , certainly not ; I make a mistake about it . I should have say a become mustachio , such as you may well be proud of . ' 
 ' then thee be a laiar , Jan Ridd . Zay so , laike a man , lad . ' 
 ' not exactly that , Betty ; but I make a great mistake ; and I humbly ask your pardon ; and if such a thing as a crown - piece , Betty ' 
 ' no fai , no fai ! ' say Betty , however she put it into her pocket ; ' now tak my advice , Jan ; thee marry Zally Snowe . ' 
 ' not with all England for her dowry . oh , Betty , you know well . ' 
 ' ah 's I ! I know much bad , Jan. Break thy poor mother 's heart it will . and to think of arl the danger ! dost love Larna now so much ? ' 
 ' with all the strength of my heart and soul . I will have she , or I will die , Betty . ' 
 ' wull . Thee will die in either case . but it baint for I to argify . and do her love thee too , Jan ? ' 
 ' I hope she do , Betty I hope she do . what do you think about it ? ' 
 ' ah , then I may hold my tongue to it . Knaw what boy and maiden be , as well as I know young peg . I myzell be o ' that zort one taime every bit so well as you be . ' and Betty hold the lanthorn up , and defy I to deny it ; and the light through the horn show a gleam in her eye , such as I have never seer there before . ' no odd , no odd about that , ' she continue ; ' mak a fool of myzell to spake of it . Arl go into churchyard . but it be a lucky foolery for thee , my boy , I can tull ' ee . for I love to see the love in thee . Coom'th over I as the spring do , though I be naigh three score . now , Jan , I will tell thee one thing , can not   abear to zee thee vrette so . Hould thee head down , same as they peg do . ' 
 so I bent my head quite close to she ; and she whisper in my ear , ' Goo of a marning , thee girt soft . she can not   get out of an avening now , her hath zent word to I , to tull ' ee . ' 
 in the glory of my delight at this , I bestow upon Betty a chaste salute , with all the pig for witness ; and she take it not amiss , consider how long she have be out of practice . but then she fall back , like a broom on its handle , and star at I , feign anger . 
 ' oh fai , oh fai ! Lunnon impudence , I doubt . I vear thee hast go on zadly , Jan. ' 




 CHAPTER XXXIII 

 an early morning call 

 of course I be up the very next morning before the October sunrise , and away through the wild and the woodland towards the Bagworthy water , at the foot of the long cascade . the rise of the sun be noble in the cold and warmth of it ; peep down the spread of light , he raise his shoulder heavily over the edge of grey mountain , and waver length of upland . beneath his gaze the dew - fog dip , and creep to the hollow place ; then steal away in line and column , hold skirt , and cling subtly at the shelter corner , where rock hang over grass - land ; while the brave line of the hill come forth , one beyond other gliding . 
 then the wood arise in fold , like drapery of awakened mountain , stately with a depth of awe , and memory of the tempest . Autumn 's mellow hand be on they , as they own already , touch with gold , and red , and olive ; and their joy towards the sun be less to a bridegroom than a father . 
 yet before the float impress of the wood could clear itself , suddenly the gladsome light leap over hill and valley , cast amber , blue , and purple , and a tint of rich red rise ; accord to the scene they light on , and the curtain fling around ; yet all alike dispel fear and the cloven hoof of darkness , all on the wing of hope advance , and proclaim , ' God be here . ' then life and joy sprang reassure from every crouch hollow ; every flower , and bud , and bird , have a fluttering sense of they ; and all the flashing of God 's gaze merge into soft beneficence . 
 so perhaps shall break upon we that eternal morning , when crag and chasm shall be no more , neither hill and valley , nor great unvintaged ocean ; when glory shall not scare happiness , neither happiness envy glory ; but all thing shall arise and shine in the light of the Father 's countenance , because itself be rise . 
 who maketh his sun to rise upon both the just and the unjust . and surely but for the save clause , Doone Glen have be in darkness . now , as I stand with scanty breath for few man could have win that climb at the top of the long defile , and the bottom of the mountain gorge all of myself , and the pain of it , and the cark of my discontent fall away into wonder and rapture . for I can not help see thing now and then , slow - witted as I have a right to be ; and perhaps because it come so rarely , the sight dwell with I like a picture . 
 the bar of rock , with the water - cleft breaking steeply through it , stand bold and bare , and dark in shadow , grey with red gully down it . but the sun be begin to glisten over the comb of the eastern highland , and through an archway of the wood hang with old nest and ivy . the line of many a lean tree be throw , from the cliff of the foreland , down upon the sparkle grass at the foot of the western crag . and through the dewy meadow 's breast , fringe with shade , but touch on one side with the sun - smile , run the crystal water , curve in its brightness like divert hope . 
 on either bank , the blade of grass , make their last autumn growth , prick their spear and crisp their tufting with the pearly purity . the tenderness of their green appear under the glaucous mantle ; while that grey suffusion , which be the blush of green life , spread its damask chastity . even then my soul be lift , worry though my mind be : who can see such large kind doing , and not be ashamed of human grief ? 
 not only unashamed of grief , but much abash with joy , be I , when I see my Lorna come , purer than the morning dew , than the sun more bright and clear . that which make I love she so , that which lift my heart to she , as the Spring wind lift the cloud , be the gayness of her nature , and its inborn playfulness . and yet all this with maiden shame , a conscious dream of thing unknown , and a sense of fate about they . 
 down the valley still she come , not witte that I look at she , have cease   through my own misprison   to expect I yet awhile ; or at least she tell herself so . in the joy of awakened life and brightness of the morning , she have cast all care away , and seem to float upon the sunrise , like a buoyant silver wave . suddenly at sight of I , for I leap forth at once , in fear of seem to watch her unawares , the bloom upon her cheek be deepen , and the radiance of her eye ; and she come to meet I gladly . 
 ' at last then , you be come , John . I think you have forget I . I could not make you understand they have keep I prisoner every evening : but come into my house ; you be in danger here . ' 
 meanwhile I could not answer , be overcome with joy , but follow to her little grotto , where I have be twice before . I know that the crown moment of my life be come that Lorna would own her love for I . 
 she make for awhile as if she dream not of the meaning of my gaze , but try to speak of other thing , falter now and then , and mantle with a rich damask below her long eyelash . 
 ' this be not what I come to know , ' I whisper very softly , ' you know what I be come to ask . ' 
 ' if you be come on purpose to ask anything , why do you delay so ? ' she turn away very bravely , but I see that her lip be tremble . 
 ' I delay so long , because I fear ; because my whole life hang in balance on a single word ; because what I have near I now may never more be near I after , though more than all the world , or than a thousand world , to I . ' as I speak these word of passion in a low soft voice , Lorna tremble more and more ; but she make no answer , neither yet look up at I . 
 ' I have love you long and long , ' I pursue , be reckless now , ' when you be a little child , as a boy I worship you : then when I see you a comely girl , as a stripling I adore you : now that you be a full - grown maiden all the rest I do , and more I love you more than tongue can tell , or heart can hold in silence . I have wait long and long ; and though I be so far below you I can wait no long ; but must have my answer . ' 
 ' you have be very faithful , John , ' she murmur to the fern and moss ; ' I suppose I must reward you . ' 
 ' that will not do for I , ' I say ; ' I will not have reluctant liking , nor assent for pity 's sake ; which only mean endurance . I must have all love , or none , I must have your heart of heart ; even as you have mine , Lorna . ' 
 while I speak , she glance up shyly through her flutter lash , to prolong my doubt one moment , for her own delicious pride . then she open wide upon I all the glorious depth and softness of her love eye , and fling both arm around my neck , and answer with her heart on mine , 
 ' darling , you have win it all . I shall never be my own again . I be your , my own one , for ever and for ever . ' 
 I be sure I know not what I do , or what I say thereafter , be overcome with transport by her word and at her gaze . only one thing I remember , when she raise her bright lip to I , like a child , for I to kiss , such a smile of sweet temptation meet I through her flow hair , that I almost forget my manner , give she no time to breathe . 
 ' that will do , ' say Lorna gently , but violently blush ; ' for the present that will do , John . and now remember one thing , dear . all the kindness be to be on my side ; and you be to be very distant , as behove to a young maiden ; except when I invite you . but you may kiss my hand , John ; oh , yes , you may kiss my hand , you know . ah to be sure ! I have forget ; how very stupid of I ! ' 
 for by this time I have take one sweet hand and gaze on it , with the pride of all the world to think that such a lovely thing be mine ; and then I slip my little ring upon the wedding finger ; and this time Lorna keep it , and look with fondness on its beauty , and clung to I with a flood of tear . 
 ' every time you cry , ' say I , draw she close to I ' I shall consider it an invitation not to be too distant . there now , none shall make you weep . darling , you shall sigh no more , but live in peace and happiness , with I to guard and cherish you : and who shall dare to vex you ? ' but she draw a long sad sigh , and look at the ground with the great tear roll , and press one hand upon the trouble of her pure young breast . 
 ' it can never , never be , ' she murmur to herself alone : ' who be I , to dream of it ? something in my heart tell I it can be so never , never . ' 




 CHAPTER XXXIV 

 two negative make an AFFIRMATIVE 

 there be , however , no possibility of depress I at such a time . to be love by Lorna , the sweet , the pure , the playful one , the fair creature on God 's earth and the most enchanting , the lady of high birth and mind ; that I , a mere clumsy , blunder yeoman , without wit , or wealth , or lineage , should have win that love heart to be my own for ever , be a thought no fear could lessen , and no chance could steal from I . 
 therefore at her own entreaty take a very quick adieu , and by her own invitation an exceed kind one , I hurry home with deep exulting , yet some sad misgiving , for Lorna have make I promise now to tell my mother everything ; as indeed I always mean to do , when my suit should be go too far to stop .
