I know , of course , that my dear mother would be greatly move and vex , the heirship of Glen Doone not be a very desirable dower , but in spite of that , and all disappointment as to little Ruth Huckaback , feel my mother 's tenderness and deep affection to I , and forgiving nature , I doubt not that before very long she would view the matter as I do . moreover , I feel that if once I could get she only to look at Lorna , she would so love and glory in she , that I should obtain all praise and thank , perchance without deserve they . 
 unluckily for my design , who should be sit down at breakfast with my mother and the rest but Squire Faggus , as everybody now begin to entitle he . I notice something odd about he , something uncomfortable in his manner , and a lack of that ease and humour which have be will not to distinguish he . he take his breakfast as it come , without a single joke about it , or preference of this to that ; but with sly soft look at Annie , who seem unable to sit quiet , or to look at any one steadfastly . I fear in my heart what be come on , and feel truly sorry for poor mother . after breakfast it become my duty to see to the ploughing of a barley - stubble ready for the sowing of a french grass , and I ask Tom Faggus to come with I , but he refuse , and I know the reason . be resolve to allow he fair field to himself , though with great displeasure that a man of such illegal repute should marry into our family , which have always be count so honest , I carry my dinner upon my back , and spend the whole day with the furrow . 
 when I return , Squire Faggus be go ; which appear to I but a sorry sign , inasmuch as if mother have take kindly to he and his intention , she would surely have make he remain awhile to celebrate the occasion . and presently no doubt be leave : for Lizzie come run to meet I , at the bottom of the woodrick , and cry , 
 ' oh , John , there be such a business . mother be in such a state of mind , and Annie cry her eye out . what do you think ? you would never guess , though I have suspect it , ever so long . ' 
 ' no need for I to guess , ' I reply , as though with some indifference , because of her self - important air ; ' I know all about it long ago . you have not be cry much , I see . I should like you well if you have . ' 
 ' why should I cry ? I like Tom Faggus . he be the only one I ever see with the spirit of a man . ' 
 this be a cut , of course , at I . Mr. Faggus have win the goodwill of Lizzie by his hatred of the Doones , and vow that if he could get a dozen man of any courage to join he , he would pull their stronghold about their ear without any more ado . this malice of his seem strange to I , as he have never suffer at their hand , so far at least as I know ; be it to be attribute to his jealousy of outlaws who excel he in his business ? not be good at repartee , I make no answer to Lizzie , have find this course more irksome to she than the very good invective : and so we enter the house together ; and mother send at once for I , while I be try to console my darling sister Annie . 
 ' oh , John ! speak one good word for I , ' she cry with both hand lay in mine , and her tearful eye look up at I . 
 ' not one , my pet , but a hundred , ' I answer , kindly embrace she : ' have no fear , little sister : I be go to make your case so bright , by comparison , I mean , that mother will send for you in five minute , and call you her good , her most dutiful child , and praise Cousin Tom to the sky , and send a man on horseback after he ; and then you will have a hard task to intercede for I , my dear . ' 
 ' oh , John , dear John , you will not   tell she about Lorna oh , not to - day , dear . ' 
 ' yes , to - day , and at once , Annie . I want to have it over , and be do with it . ' 
 ' oh , but think of she , dear . I be sure she could not bear it , after this great shock already . ' 
 ' she will bear it all the well , ' say I ; ' the one will drive the other out . I know exactly what mother be . she will be desperately savage first with you , and then with I , and then for a very little while with both of we together ; and then she will put one against the other   in her mind I mean   and consider which be most to blame ; and in do that she will be compel to find the good in either 's case , that it may beat the other ; and so as the plea come before her mind , they will gain upon the charge , both of we be her child , you know : and before very long   particularly if we both keep out of the way   she will begin to think that after all she have be a little too hasty , and then she will remember how good we have always be to she ; and how like our father . upon that , she will think of her own love - time , and sigh a good bit , and cry a little , and then smile , and send for both of we , and beg our pardon , and call we her two darling . ' 
 ' now , John , how on earth can you know all that ? ' exclaim my sister , wipe her eye , and gaze at I with a soft bright smile . ' who on earth can have tell you , John ? People to call you stupid indeed ! why , I feel that all you say be quite true , because you describe so exactly what I should do myself ; I mean I mean if I have two child , who have behave as we have do . but tell I , darling John , how you learn all this . ' 
 ' never you mind , ' I reply , with a nod of some conceit , I fear : ' I must be a fool if I do not know what mother be by this time . ' 
 now inasmuch as the thing befell accord to my prediction , what need for I to dwell upon it , after say how it would be ? moreover , I would regret to write down what mother say about Lorna , in her first surprise and tribulation ; not only because I be grieve by the gross injustice of it , and frighten mother with her own word   repeat deeply after she ; but rather because it be not well , when people repent of hasty speech , to enter it against they . 
 that be say to be the angel ' business ; and I doubt if they can attend to it much , without do injury to themselves . 
 however , by the afternoon , when the sun begin to go down upon we , our mother sit on the garden bench , with her head on my great otter - skin waistcoat   which be waterproof , and her right arm round our Annie 's waist , and scarcely know which of we she ought to make the most of , or which deserve most pity . not that she have forgive yet the rival to her love Tom Faggus , I mean , and Lorna but that she be begin to think a tattle well of they now , and a vast deal well of her own child . 
 and it help she much in this regard , that she be not think half so well as usual of herself , or rather of her own judgment ; for in good truth she have no self , only as it come home to she , by no very distant road , but by way of her child . a well mother never live ; and can I , after search all thing , add another word to that ? 
 and indeed poor Lizzie be not so very bad ; but behave   on the whole   very well for she . she be much to be pity , poor thing , and great allowance make for she , as belong to a well - grow family , and a very comely one ; and feel her own shortcoming . this make her leap to the other extreme , and reassert herself too much , endeavour to exalt the mind at the expense of the body ; because she have the invisible one   so far as can be decide   in well share than the visible . not but what she have her point , and very comely point of body ; lovely eye to wit , and very beautiful hand and foot   almost as good as Lorna 's , and a neck as white as snow ; but Lizzie be not gifted with our gait and port , and bound health . 
 now , while we sit on the garden bench , under the great ash - tree , we leave dear mother to take her own way , and talk at her own pleasure . child almost always be more wide - awake than their parent . the father and the mother laugh ; but the young one have the good of they . and now both Annie know , and I , that we have get the good of mother ; and therefore we let she lay down the law , as if we have be two dolly . 
 ' Darling John , ' my mother say , ' your case be a very hard one . a young and very romantic girl God send that I be right in my charitable view of she have meet an equally simple boy , among great danger and difficulty , from which my son have save she , at the risk of his life at every step . of course , she become attach to he , and look up to he in every way , as a superior being ' 
 ' come now , mother , ' I say ; ' if you only see Lorna , you would look upon I as the low dirt ' 
 ' no doubt I should , ' my mother answer ; ' and the king and queen , and all the royal family . well , this poor angel , have make up her mind to take compassion upon my son , when he have save her life so many time , persuade he to marry she out of pure pity , and throw his poor mother overboard . and the sad part of it all be this ' 
 ' that my mother will never , never , never understand the truth , ' say I. 
 ' that be all I wish , ' she answer ; ' just to get at the simple truth from my own perception of it . John , you be very wise in kiss I ; but perhaps you would not be so wise in bring Lorna for an afternoon , just to see what she think of I . there be a good saddle of mutton now ; and there be some very good sausage leave , on the blue dish with the anchor , Annie , from the last little sow we kill . ' 
 ' as if Lorna would eat sausage ! ' say I , with appearance of high contempt , though rejoice all the while that mother seem to have her name so pat ; and she pronounce it in a manner which make my heart leap to my ear : ' Lorna to eat sausage ! ' 
 ' I do not   see why she should not , ' my mother answer smile , ' if she mean to be a farmer 's wife , she must take to farmer 's way , I think . what do you say , Annie ? ' 
 ' she will eat whatever John desire , I should hope , ' say Annie gravely ; ' particularly as I make they . ' 
 ' oh that I could only get the chance of try she ! ' I answer , ' if you could once behold she , mother , you would never let she go again . and she would love you with all her heart , she be so good and gentle . ' 
 ' that be a lucky thing for I ' ; say this my mother weep , as she have be do off and on , when no one seem to look at she ; ' otherwise I suppose , John , she would very soon turn I out of the farm , have you so completely under her thumb , as she seem to have . I see now that my time be over . Lizzie and I will seek our fortune . it be wise so . ' 
 ' now , mother , ' I cry ; ' will you have the kindness not to talk any nonsense ? everything belong to you ; and so , I hope , your child do . and you , in turn , belong to we ; as you have prove ever since oh , ever since we can remember . why do you make Annie cry so ? you ought to know well than that . ' 
 mother upon this go over all the thing she have do before ; how many time I know not ; neither do it matter . only she seem to enjoy it more , every time of do it . and then she say she be an old fool ; and Annie   like a thorough girl   pull her one grey hair out . 




 CHAPTER XXXV 

 ruth be not like LORNA 

 although by our mother 's reluctant consent a large part of the obstacle between Annie and her lover appear to be remove , on the other hand Lorna and myself gain little , except as regard comfort of mind , and some ease to the conscience . moreover , our chance of frequent meeting and delightful converse be much impaired , at least for the present ; because though mother be not aware of my narrow escape from Carver Doone , she make I promise never to risk my life by needless visit . and upon this point , that be to say , the necessity of the visit , she be well content , as she say , to leave I to my own good sense and honour ; only beg I always to tell she of my intention beforehand . this pledge , however , for her own sake , I decline to give ; know how wretched she would be during all the time of my absence ; and , on that account , I promise instead , that I would always give she a full account of my adventure upon return . 
 now my mother , as might be expect , begin at once to cast about for some mean of relieve I from all further peril , and herself from great anxiety . she be full of plan for fetch Lorna , in some wonderful manner , out of the power of the Doones entirely , and into her own hand , where she be to remain for at least a twelve - month , learn all mother and Annie could teach she of dairy business , and farm - house life , and the good mode of packing butter . and all this arise from my happen to say , without mean anything , how the poor dear have long for quiet , and a life of simplicity , and a rest away from violence ! bless thee , mother now long in heaven , there be no need to bless thee ; but it often make a dimness now in my well - wear eye , when I think of thy love - kindness , warmth , and romantic innocence . 
 as to steal my beloved from that vile Glen Doone , the deed itself be not impossible , nor beyond my daring ; but in the first place would she come , leave her old grandfather to die without her tendence ? and even if , through fear of Carver and that wicked Counsellor , she should consent to fly , would it be possible to keep she without a regiment of soldier ? would not the Doones at once ride forth to scour the country for their queen , and find she   as they must do , burn our house , and murder we , and carry she back triumphantly ? 
 all this I lay before my mother , and to such effect that she acknowledge , with a sigh that nothing else remain for I   in the present state of matter   except to keep a careful watch upon Lorna from safe distance , observe the policy of the Doones , and wait for a tide in their affair . meanwhile I might even fall in love   as mother unwisely hint   with a certain more peaceful heiress , although of inferior blood , who would be daily at my elbow . I be not sure but what dear mother herself would have be disappoint , have I prove myself so fickle ; and my disdain and indignation at the mere suggestion do not so much displease she ; for she only smile and answer , 
 ' well , it be not for I to say ; God know what be good for we . liking will not come to order ; otherwise I should not be where I be this day . and of one thing I be rather glad ; Uncle Reuben well deserve that his pet scheme should miscarry . he who call my boy a coward , an ignoble coward , because he would not join some crack - brain plan against the valley which shelter his beloved one ! and all the time this dreadful " coward " risk his life daily there , without a word to any one ! how glad I be that you will not have , for all her miserable money , that little dwarfish granddaughter of the insolent old miser ! ' 
 she turn , and by her side be stand poor Ruth Huckaback herself , white , and sad , and look steadily at my mother 's face , which become as red as a plum while her breath desert she . 
 ' if you please , madam , ' say the little maiden , with her large calm eye unwavere , ' it be not my fault , but God Almighty 's , that I be a little dwarfish creature . I know not that you regard I with so much contempt on that account ; neither have you tell my grandfather , at least within my hearing , that he be an insolent old miser . when I return to Dulverton , which I trust to do to - morrow   for it be too late to - day , I shall be careful not to tell he your opinion of he , lest I should thwart any scheme you may have upon his property . I thank you all for your kindness to I , which have be very great , far more than a little dwarfish creature could , for her own sake , expect . I will only add for your further guidance one more little truth . it be by no mean certain that my grandfather will settle any of his miserable money upon I . if I offend he , as I would in a moment , for the sake of a brave and straightforward man ' here she give I a glance which I scarcely know what to do with ' my grandfather , upright as he be , would leave I without a shilling . and I often wish it be so . so many misery come upon I from the miserable money ' here she break down , and burst out cry , and run away with a faint good - bye ; while we three look at one another , and feel that we have the bad of it . 
 ' impudent little dwarf ! ' say my mother , recover her breath after ever so long . ' oh , John , how thankful you ought to be ! what a life she would have lead you ! ' 
 ' well , I be sure ! ' say Annie , throw her arm around poor mother : ' who could have think that little atomy have such an outrageous spirit ! for my part I can not think how she can have be sly enough to hide it in that crafty manner , that John might think she an angel ! ' 
 ' well , for my part , ' I answer , laugh , ' I never admire Ruth Huckaback half , or a quarter so much before . she be rare stuff . I would have be glad to have marry she to - morrow , if I have never see my Lorna . ' 
 ' and a nice nobody I should have be , in my own house ! ' cry mother : ' I never can be thankful enough to darle Lorna for save I . do you see how her eye flash ? ' 
 ' that I do ; and very fine they be . now nine maiden out of ten would have feign not to have hear one word that be say , and have bear black malice in their heart . come , Annie , now , would not you have do so ? ' 
 ' I think , ' say Annie , ' although of course I can not tell , you know , John , that I should have be ashamed at hear what be never mean for I , and should have be almost as angry with myself as anybody . ' 
 ' so you would , ' reply my mother ; ' so any daughter of mine would have do , instead of rail and revile . however , I be very sorry that any word of mine which the poor little thing choose to overhear should have make she so forget herself . I shall beg her pardon before she go , and I shall expect she to beg mine . ' 
 ' that she will never do , ' say I ; ' a more resolute little maiden never yet have right upon her side ; although it be a mere accident . I might have say the same thing myself , and she be hard upon you , mother dear . ' 
 after this , we say no more , at least about that matter ; and little Ruth , the next morning , leave we , in spite of all that we could do . she vow an everlasting friendship to my young sister Eliza ; but she look at Annie with some resentment , when they say good - bye , for be so much tall . at any rate so Annie fancy , but she may have be quite wrong . I ride beside the little maid till far beyond Exeford , when all danger of the moor be past , and then I leave she with John Fry , not wish to be too particular , after all the talk about her money . she have tear in her eye when she bid I farewell , and she send a kind message home to mother , and promise to come again at Christmas , if she could win permission . 
 upon the whole , my opinion be that she have behave uncommonly well for a maid whose self - love be outraged , with spirit , I mean , and proper pride ; and yet with a great endeavour to forgive , which be , meseem , the hard of all thing to a woman , outside of her own family . 
 after this , for another month , nothing worthy of notice happen , except of course that I find it needful , accord to the strict good sense and honour , to visit Lorna immediately after my discourse with mother , and to tell she all about it . my beauty give I one sweet kiss with all her heart   as she always do , when she kiss at all , and I beg for one more to take to our mother , and before leave , I obtain it . it be not for I to tell all she say , even suppose   what be not likely   that any one care to know it , be more and more peculiar to ourselves and no one else . but one thing that she say be this , and I take good care to carry it , word for word , to my mother and Annie : 
 ' I never can believe , dear John , that after all the crime and outrage work by my reckless family , it ever can be mean for I to settle down to peace and comfort in a simple household . with all my heart I long for home ; any home , however dull and wearisome to those use to it , would seem a paradise to I , if only free from brawl and tumult , and such as I could call my own . but even if God would allow I this , in lieu of my wild inheritance , it be quite certain that the Doones never can and never will . ' 
 again , when I tell she how my mother and Annie , as well as myself , long to have she at Plover 's barrow , and teach she all the quiet duty in which she be sure to take such delight , she only answer with a bright blush , that while her grandfather be live she would never leave he ; and that even if she be free , certain ruin be all she should bring to any house that receive she , at least within the utmost reach of her amiable family . this be too plain to be deny , and see my dejection at it , she tell I bravely that we must hope for well time , if possible , and ask how long I would wait for she . 
 ' not a day if I have my will , ' I answer very warmly ; at which she turn away confused , and would not look at I for awhile ; ' but all my life , ' I go on to say , ' if my fortune be so ill . and how long would you wait for I , Lorna ? ' 
 ' till I could get you , ' she answer slyly , with a smile which be bright to I than the bright wit could be . ' and now , ' she continue , ' you bind I , John , with a very beautiful ring to you , and when I dare not wear it , I carry it always on my heart . but I will bind you to I , you dearest , with the very poor and plainest thing that ever you set eye on . I could give you fifty fair one , but they would not be honest ; and I love you for your honesty , and nothing else of course , John ; so do not   you be conceited . look at it , what a queer old thing ! there be some ancient mark upon it , very grotesque and wonderful ; it look like a cat in a tree almost , but never mind what it look like . this old ring must have be a giant 's ; therefore it will fit you perhaps , you enormous John . it have be on the front of my old glass necklace   which my grandfather find they take away , and very soon make they give back again   ever since I can remember ; and long before that , as some woman tell I . now you seem very greatly amazed ; pray what think my lord of it ? ' 
 ' that be worth fifty of the pearl thing which I give you , you darling ; and that I will not take it from you . ' 
 ' then you will never take I , that be all . I will have nothing to do with a gentleman ' 
 ' no gentleman , dear a yeoman . ' 
 ' very well , a yeoman nothing to do with a yeoman who will not accept my love - gage . so , if you please , give it back again , and take your lovely ring back . ' 
 she look at I in such a manner , half in earnest , half in jest , and three time three in love , that in spite of all good resolution , and her own faint protest , I be force to abandon all firm idea , and kiss she till she be quite ashamed , and her head hang on my bosom , with the night of her hair shed over I . then I place the pearl ring back on the soft elastic bend of the finger she hold up to scold I ; and on my own small finger draw the heavy hoop she have give I . I consider this with satisfaction , until my darling recover herself ; and then I begin very gravely about it , to keep she   if I could   from chide I : 
 ' Mistress Lorna , this be not the ring of any giant . it be nothing more nor less than a very ancient thumb - ring , such as once in my father 's time be plough up out of the ground in our farm , and send to learn doctor , who tell we all about it , but keep the ring for their trouble . I will accept it , my own one love ; and it shall go to my grave with I . ' and so it shall , unless there be villain who would dare to rob the dead . 
 now I have speak about this ring   though I scarcely mean to do so , and would rather keep to myself thing so very holy   because it hold an important part in the history of my Lorna . I ask she where the glass necklace be from which the ring be fasten , and which she have wear in her childhood , and she answer that she hardly know , but remember that her grandfather have beg she to give it up to he , when she be ten year old or so , and have promise to keep it for she until she could take care of it ; at the same time give she back the ring , and fasten it from her pretty neck , and tell she to be proud of it . and so she always have be , and now from her sweet breast she take it , and it become John Ridd 's delight . 
 all this , or at least great part of it , I tell my mother truly , accord to my promise ; and she be greatly pleased with Lorna for have be so good to I , and for speak so very sensibly ; and then she look at the great gold ring , but could by no mean interpret it . only she be quite certain , as indeed I myself be , that it must have belong to an ancient race of great consideration , and high rank , in their time . upon which I be for take it off , lest it should be degrade by a common farmer 's finger . but mother say ' no , ' with tear in her eye ; ' if the common farmer have win the great lady of the ancient race , what be ring and old - world trinket , when compare to the live jewel ? ' be quite of her opinion in this , and love the ring   which have no gem in it   as the token of my priceless gem , I resolve to wear it at any cost , except when I should be plough , or do thing likely to break it ; although I must own that it feel very queer   for I never have throttle a finger before , and it look very queer , for a length of time , upon my great hard - work hand . 
 and before I get use to my ring , or people could think that it belong to I   plain and ungarnished though it be , and before I go to see Lorna again , have fail to find any necessity , and remember my duty to mother , we all have something else to think of , not so pleasant , and more puzzling . 




 CHAPTER XXXVI 

 JOHN return to business 

 now November be upon we , and we have keep Allhallowmass , with roasting of skewered apple   like so many shuttlecock , and after that the day of Fawkes , as become good protestant , with merry bonfire and burn batata , and plenty of good feeding in honour of our religion ; and then while we be at wheat - sowing , another visitor arrive . 
 this be Master Jeremy Stickles , who have be a good friend to I   as describe before   in London , and have earn my mother 's gratitude , so far as ever he choose to have it . and he seem inclined to have it all ; for he make our farm - house his headquarter , and keep we quite at his beck and call , go out at any time of the evening , and come back at any time of the morning , and always expect we to be ready , whether with horse , or man , or maiden , or fire , or provision . we know that he be employ somehow upon the service of the king , and have at different station certain trooper and orderly quite at his disposal ; also we know that he never go out , nor even sleep in his bedroom , without heavy firearm well load , and a sharp sword nigh his hand ; and that he hold a great commission , under royal signet , require all good subject , all officer of whatever degree , and especially justice of the peace , to aid he to the utmost , with person , beast , and chattel , or to answer it at their peril . 
 now Master Jeremy Stickles , of course , know well what woman be , durst not open to any of they the nature of his instruction . but , after awhile , perceive that I could be rely upon , and that it be a great discomfort not to have I with he , he take I aside in a lonely place , and tell I nearly everything ; have bind I first by oath , not to impart to any one , without his own permission , until all be over . 
 but at this present time of writing , all be over long ago ; ay and forget too , I ween , except by those who suffer . therefore may I tell the whole without any breach of confidence . Master Stickles be go forth upon his usual night journey , when he meet I come home , and I say something half in jest , about his zeal and secrecy ; upon which he look all round the yard , and lead I to an open space in the clover field adjoining . 
 ' John , ' he say , ' you have some right to know the meaning of all this , be trust as you be by the Lord Chief Justice . but he find you scarcely supple enough , neither gift with due brain . ' 
 ' thank God for that same , ' I answer , while he tap his head , to signify his own much large allowance . then he make I bind myself , which in an evil hour I do , to retain his secret ; and after that he go on solemnly , and with much importance , 
 ' there be some people fit to plot , and other to be plot against , and other to unravel plot , which be the high gift of all . this last hath fall to my share , and a very thankless gift it be , although a rare and choice one . much of peril too attend it ; dare courage and great coolness be as needful for the work as ready wit and spotless honour . therefore his Majesty 's adviser have choose I for this high task , and they could not have choose a well man . although you have be in London , Jack , much long than you wish it , you be wholly ignorant , of course , in matter of state , and the public weal . ' 
 ' well , ' say I , ' no doubt but I be , and all the well for I . although I hear a deal of they ; for everybody be talk , and ready to come to blow ; if only it could be do without danger . but one say this , and one say that ; and they talk so much about Birminghams , and tantivie , and Whigs and Tories , and protestant flail and such like , that I be only too glad to have my glass and clink my spoon for answer . ' 
 ' right , John , thou art right as usual . let the King go his own gait . he hath too many mistress to be ever England 's master . nobody need fear he , for he be not like his father : he will have his own way , ' tis true , but without stop other folk of theirs : and well he know what woman be , for he never ask they question . now hear you much in London town about the Duke of Monmouth ? ' 
 ' not so very much , ' I answer ; ' not half so much as in Devonshire : only that he be a hearty man , and a very handsome one , and now be banish by the Tories ; and most people wish he be come back , instead of the Duke of York , who be try boot in Scotland . ' 
 ' thing be change since you be in town . the whig be get up again , through the folly of the Tories kill poor Lord Russell ; and now this Master Sidney   if my Lord condemn he   will make it bad again . there be much disaffection everywhere , and it must grow to an outbreak . the King hath many troop in London , and meaneth to bring more from Tangier ; but he can not command these country place ; and the train band can not help he much , even if they would . now , do you understand I , John ? ' 
 ' in truth , not I. I see not what Tangier hath to do with Exmoor ; nor the Duke of Monmouth with Jeremy Stickles . ' 
 ' thou great clod , put it the other way . Jeremy Stickles may have much to do about the Duke of Monmouth . the whig have fail of Exclusion , and have be punish bitterly for the blood they shed , be ripe for any violence . and the turn of the balance be now to they . see - saw be the fashion of England always ; and the Whigs will soon be the top - sawyer . ' 
 ' but , ' say I , still more confused , ' " the King be the top - sawyer , " accord to our proverb . how then can the Whigs be ? ' 
 ' thou art a hopeless ass , John . well to sew with a chestnut than to teach thee the constitution . let it be so , let it be . I have see a boy of five year old more apt at politic than thou . Nay , look not offend , lad . it be my fault for be over - deep to thee . I should have consider thy intellect . ' 
 ' Nay , Master Jeremy , make no apology . it be I that should excuse myself ; but , God know , I have no politic . ' 
 ' stick to that , my lad , ' he answer ; ' so shalt thou die easy . now , in ten word   without party , or try thy poor brain too much , I be here to watch the gathering of a secret plot , not so much against the King as against the due succession . ' 
 ' now I understand at last . but , Master Stickles , you might have say all that an hour ago almost . ' 
 ' it would have be well , if I have , to thee , ' he reply with much compassion ; ' thy hat be nearly off thy head with the swelling of brain I have give thee . blow , blow , be thy business , Jack . there thou art in thine element . and , haply , this business will bring thee plenty even for thy great head to take . now hearken to one who wish thee well , and plainly see the end of it stick thou to the win side , and have naught to do with the other one . ' 
 ' that , ' say I , in great haste and hurry , ' be the very thing I want to do , if I only know which be the win side , for the sake of Lorna that be to say , for the sake of my dear mother and sister , and the farm . ' 
 ' ha ! ' cry Jeremy Stickles , laugh at the redness of my face ' Lorna , saidst thou ; now what Lorna ? be it the name of a maiden , or a light - o'-love ? ' 
 ' keep to your own business , ' I answer , very proudly ; ' spy as much as e'er thou wilt , and use our house for do it , without ask leave or telling ; but if I ever find thee spy into my affair , all the King 's lifeguard in London , and the dragoon thou bringest hither , shall not save thee from my hand or one finger be enough for thee . ' 
 be carry beyond myself by his insolence about Lorna , I look at Master Stickles so , and spake in such a voice , that all his dare courage and his spotless honour quail within he , and he shrink as if I would strike so small a man . 
 then I leave he , and go to work at the sack upon the corn - floor , to take my evil spirit from I before I should see mother . for   to tell the truth   now my strength be full , and trouble be gather round I , and people take advantage so much of my easy temper , sometimes when I be over - try , a sudden heat run over I , and a glowing of all my muscle , and a tingling for a mighty throw , such as my utmost self - command , and fear of hurt any one , could but ill refrain . afterwards , I be always very sadly ashamed of myself , know how poor a thing bodily strength be , as compare with power of mind , and that it be a coward 's part to misuse it upon weak folk . for the present there be a little breach between Master Stickles and I , for which I blame myself very sorely . but though , in full memory of his kindness and faithfulness in London , I ask his pardon many time for my foolish anger with he , and offer to undergo any penalty he would lay upon I , he only say it be no matter , there be nothing to forgive . when people say that , the truth often be that they can forgive nothing . 
 so for the present a breach be make between Master Jeremy and myself , which to I seem no great loss , inasmuch as it relieve I from any privity to his dealing , for which I have small liking . all I fear be lest I might , in any way , be ungrateful to he ; but when he would have no more of I , what could I do to help it ? however , in a few day ' time I be of good service to he , as you shall see in its proper place . 
 but now my own affair be throw into such disorder that I could think of nothing else , and have the great difficulty in hide my uneasiness . for suddenly , without any warning , or a word of message , all my Lorna 's signal cease , which I have be accustom to watch for daily , and as it be to feed upon they , with a glowing heart . the first time I stand on the woode crest , and find no change from yesterday , I could hardly believe my eye , or think at least that it must be some great mistake on the part of my love . however , even that oppress I with a heavy heart , which grow heavy , as I find from day to day no token . 
 three time I go and wait long at the bottom of the valley , where now the stream be brown and angry with the rain of autumn , and the weeping tree hang leafless . but though I wait at every hour of day , and far into the night , no light footstep come to meet I , no sweet voice be in the air ; all be lonely , drear , and drench with sodden desolation . it seem as if my love be dead , and the wind be at her funeral . 
 once I seek far up the valley , where I have never be before , even beyond the copse where Lorna have find and lose her brave young cousin . follow up the river channel , in shelter of the evening fog , I gain a corner within stone 's throw of the last outlying cot . this be a gloomy , low , square house , without any light in the window , roughly build of wood and stone , as I see when I draw nearer . for know it to be Carver 's dwelling   or at least suspect so , from some word of Lorna 's , I be lead by curiosity , and perhaps by jealousy , to have a close look at it . therefore , I creep up the stream , lose half my sense of fear , by reason of anxiety . and in truth there be not much to fear , the sky be now too dark for even a shooter of wild fowl to make good aim . and nothing else but gun could hurt I , as in the pride of my strength I think , and in my skill of single - stick . 
 nevertheless , I go warily , be now almost among this nest of cockatrice . the back of Carver 's house abut on the wave of the rush stream ; and see a loop - hole , vacant for musket , I look in , but all be quiet . so far as I could judge by listen , there be no one now inside , and my heart for a moment leap with joy , for I have fear to find Lorna there . then I take a careful survey of the dwelling , and its window , and its door , and aspect , as if I have be a robber meaning to make privy entrance . it be well for I that I do this , as you will find hereafter . 
 have impress upon my mind   a slow but , perhaps retentive mind , all the bearing of the place , and all its opportunity , and even the curve of the stream along it , and the bush near the door , I be much inclined to go far up , and understand all the village . but a bar of red light across the river , some forty yard on above I , and cross from the opposite side like a chain , prevent I . in that second house there be a gathering of loud and merry outlaw , make as much noise as if they have the law upon their side . some , indeed , as I approach , be lay down both right and wrong , as purely , and with as high a sense , as if they know the difference . cold and trouble as I be , I could hardly keep from laugh . 
 before I betook myself home that night , and ease dear mother 's heart so much , and make her pale face spread with smile , I have resolve to penetrate Glen Doone from the upper end , and learn all about my Lorna . not but what I might have enter from my unsuspected channel , as so often I have do ; but that I see fearful need for know something more than that . here be every sort of trouble gather upon I , here be Jeremy Stickles steal upon every one in the dark ; here be Uncle Reuben plot Satan only could tell what ; here be a white night - cap man come bodily from the grave ; here be my own sister Annie commit to a highwayman , and mother in distraction ; most of all here , there , and where be my Lorna steal , dungeone , perhaps outraged . it be no time for shilly shally , for the balance of this and that , or for a man with blood and muscle to pat his nose and ponder . if I leave my Lorna so ; if I let those black - soul'd villain work their pleasure on my love ; if the heart that clave to mine could find no vigour in it then let maidens cease from man , and rest their faith in tabby - cat . 
 rudely roll these idea in my heavy head and brain I resolve to let the morrow put they into form and order , but not contradict they . and then , as my constitution will   be like that of England , I sleep , and there be no stop I . 




 CHAPTER XXXVII 

 a very desperate venture 

 that the enterprise now resolve upon be far more dangerous than any hitherto attempt by I , need no further proof than this : I go and make my will at Porlock , with a middling honest lawyer there ; not that I have much to leave , but that none could say how far the farm , and all the farming stock , might depend on my disposition . it make I smile when I remember how particular I be , and how for the life of I I be puzzle to bequeath most part of my clothe , and hat , and thing altogether my own , to Lorna , without the shrewd old lawyer know who she be and where she live . at last , indeed , I flatter myself that I have baffle old Tape 's curiosity ; but his wrinkle smile and his speech at part make I again uneasy . 
 ' a very excellent will , young sir . an admirably just and virtuous will ; all your effect to your near of kin ; filial and fraternal duty thoroughly exemplified ; nothing divert to alien channel , except a small token of esteem and reverence to an elderly lady , I presume : and which may or may not be valid , or invalid , on the ground of uncertainty , or the absence of any legal status on the part of the legatee . ha , ha ! yes , yes ! few young man be so free from exceptionable entanglement . two guineas be my charge , sir : and a rare good will for the money . very prudent of you , sir . do you credit in every way . well , well ; we all must die ; and often the young before the old . ' 
 not only do I think two guineas a great deal too much money for a quarter of an hour 's employment , but also I dislike particularly the word with which he conclude ; they sound , from his grating voice , like the evil omen of a croak raven . nevertheless I still abode in my fix resolve to go , and find out , if I die for it , what be become of Lorna . and herein I lay no claim to courage ; the matter be simply a choice between two evil , of which by far the great one be , of course , to lose my darling . 
 the journey be a great deal long to fetch around the southern hill , and enter by the Doone - gate , than to cross the low land and steal in by the water - slide . however , I durst not take a horse   for fear of the Doones who might be abroad upon their usual business , but start betime in the evening , so as not to hurry , or waste any strength upon the way . and thus I come to the robber ' highway , walk circumspectly , scan the sky - line of every hill , and search the fold of every valley , for any move figure . 
 although it be now well on towards dark , and the sun be down an hour or so , I could see the robber ' road before I , in a trough of the wind hill , where the brook plough down from the high barrow , and the cove bank be roof with furze . at present , there be no one pass , neither post nor sentinel , so far as I could descry ; but I think it safe to wait a little , as twilight melt into night ; and then I creep down a seam of the highland , and stand upon the Doone - track . 
 as the road approach the entrance , it become more straight and strong , like a channel cut from rock , with the water brawl darkly along the naked side of it . not a tree or bush be leave , to shelter a man from bullet : all be stern , and stiff , and rugge , as I could not help perceive , even through the darkness , and a smell as of churchyard mould , a sense of be box in and coope , make I long to be out again . 
 and here I be , or seem to be , particularly unlucky ; for as I draw near the very entrance , lightly of foot and warily , the moon   which have often be my friend   like an enemy break upon I , top the eastward ridge of rock , and fill all the open space with the play of waver light . I shrink back into the shadowy quarter on the right side of the road ; and gloomily employ myself to watch the triple entrance , on which the moonlight fall askew . 
 all across and before the three rude and beetle archway hang a felled oak overhead , black , and thick , and threaten . this , as I hear before , could be let fall in a moment , so as to crush a score of man , and bar the approach of horse . behind this tree , the rocky mouth be span , as by a gallery with brushwood and pile timber , all upon a ledge of stone , where thirty man might lurk unseen , and fire at any invader . from that rampart it would be impossible to dislodge they , because the rock fall sheer below they twenty foot , or it may be more ; while overhead it tower three hundred , and so jut over that nothing could be cast upon they ; even if a man could climb the height . and the access to this portculli place if I may so call it , be no portculli there be through certain rocky chamber know to the tenant only . 
 but the cleverest of their device , and the most puzzling to an enemy , be that , instead of one mouth only , there be three to choose from , with nothing to betoken which be the proper access ; all be pretty much alike , and all unfenced and yawning . and the common rumour be that in time of any danger , when any force be know to be on muster in their neighbourhood , they change their entrance every day , and divert the other two , by mean of slide door to the chasm and dark abyss . 
 now I could see those three rough arch , jagged , black , and terrible ; and I know that only one of they could lead I to the valley ; neither give the river now any further guidance ; but dive underground with a sullen roar , where it meet the cross - bar of the mountain . have no mean at all of judging which be the right way of the three , and know that the other two would lead to almost certain death , in the ruggedness and darkness , for how could a man , among precipice and bottomless depth of water , without a ray of light , have any chance to save his life ? I do declare that I be half inclined to go away , and have do with it . 
 however , I know one thing for certain , to wit , that the long I stay debate the more would the enterprise pall upon I , and the less my relish be . and it strike I that , in time of peace , the middle way be the likeliest ; and the other diverge right and leave in their farth part might be make to slide into it   not far from the entrance , at the pleasure of the warder . also I take it for good oman that I remember   as rarely happen   a very fine line in the Latin grammar , whose emphasis and meaning be ' middle road be safe . ' 
 therefore , without more hesitation , I plunge into the middle way , hold a long ash staff before I , shodden at the end with iron . presently I be in black darkness grope along the wall , and feel a deal more fear than I wish to feel ; especially when upon look back I could no long see the light , which I have forsake . then I stumble over something hard , and sharp , and very cold , moreover so grievous to my leg that it need my very good doctrine and humour to forbear from swear , in the manner they use in London . but when I arise and feel it , and know it to be a culverin , I be somewhat reassure thereby , inasmuch as it be not likely that they would plant this engine except in the real and true entrance . 
 therefore I go on again , more painfully and wearily , and presently find it to be good that I have receive that knock , and bear it with such patience ; for otherwise I might have blunder full upon the sentry , and be shoot without more ado . as it be , I have barely time to draw back , as I turn a corner upon they ; and if their lanthorn have be in its place , they could scarce have fail to descry I , unless indeed I have see the gleam before I turn the corner . 
 there seem to be only two of they , of size indeed and stature as all the Doones must be , but I need not have fear to encounter they both , have they be unarmed , as I be . it be plain , however , that each have a long and heavy carbine , not in his hand   as it should have be , but stand close beside he . therefore it behove I now to be exceedingly careful , and even that might scarce avail , without luck in proportion . so I keep well back at the corner , and lay one cheek to the rock face , and keep my outer eye round the jut , in the wary mode I could compass , watch my opportunity : and this be what I see . 
 the two villain look very happy which villain have no right to be , but often be , meseemeth they be sit in a niche of rock , with the lanthorn in the corner , quaff something from glass measure , and play at push - pin , or shepherd 's chess , or basset ; or some trivial game of that sort . each be smoke a long clay pipe , quite of new London shape , I could see , for the shadow be throw out clearly ; and each would laugh from time to time , as he fancy he get the well of it . one be sit with his knee up , and leave hand on his thigh ; and this one have his back to I , and seem to be the stouter . the other lean more against the rock , half sit and half astraddle , and wear leathern overall , as if newly come from rid . I could see his face quite clearly by the light of the open lanthorn , and a handsomer or a bold face I have seldom , if ever , set eye upon ; insomuch that it make I very unhappy to think of his be so near my Lorna . 
 ' how long be I to stand crouch here ? ' I ask of myself , at last , be tired of hear they cry , ' score one , ' ' score two , ' ' no , by , Charlie , ' ' by , I say it be , Phelps . ' and yet my only chance of slip by they unperceive be to wait till they quarrel more , and come to blow about it . presently , as I make up my mind to steal along towards they   for the cavern be pretty wide , just there , Charlie , or Charleworth Doone , the young and tall man , reach forth his hand to seize the money , which he swear he have win that time . upon this , the other jerk his arm , vow that he have no right to it ; whereupon Charlie fling at his face the content of the glass he be sip , but miss he and hit the candle , which sputter with a flare of blue flame   from the strength perhaps of the spirit   and then go out completely . at this , one swore , and the other laugh ; and before they have settle what to do , I be past they and round the corner . 
 and then , like a giddy fool as I be , I need must give they a startler the whoop of an owl , do so exactly , as John Fry have teach I , and echo by the roof so fearfully , that one of they drop the tinder box ; and the other catch up his gun and cock it , at least as I judge by the sound they make . and then , too late , I know my madness , for if either of they have fire , no doubt but what all the village would have rise and rush upon I . however , as the luck of the matter go , it prove for my advantage ; for I hear one say to the other , 
 ' curse it , Charlie , what be that ? it scar I so , I have drop my box ; my flint be go , and everything . will the brimstone catch from your pipe , my lad ? ' 
 ' my pipe be out , Phelps , ever so long . damn it , I be not afraid of an owl , man . give I the lanthorn , and stay here . I be not half do with you yet , my friend . ' 
 ' well say , my boy , well say ! go straight to Carver 's , mind you . the other sleepy head be snore , as there be nothing up to - night . no dally now under Captain 's window . Queen will have nought to say to you ; and Carver will punch your head into a new wick for your lanthorn . ' 
 ' will he though ? two can play at that . ' and so after some rude jest , and laughter , and a few more oath , I hear Charlie   or at any rate somebody   come toward I , with a loose and not too sober footfall . as he reel a little in his gait , and I would not move from his way one inch , after his talk of Lorna , but only long to grasp he   if common sense permit it , his braid coat come against my thumb , and his leathern gaiter brush my knee . if he have turn or notice it , he would have be a dead man in a moment ; but his drunkenness save he . 
 so I let he reel on unharmed ; and thereupon it occur to I that I could have no well guide , pass as he would exactly where I wish to be ; that be to say under Lorna 's window . therefore I follow he without any especial caution ; and soon I have the pleasure of see his form against the moonlit sky . down a steep and winding path , with a handrail at the corner   such as they have at Ilfracombe , Master Charlie trip along and indeed there be much tripping , and he must have be an active fellow to recover as he do and after he walk I , much hop   for his own poor sake   that he might not turn and espy I . 
 but Bacchus   of whom I read at school , with great wonder about his meaning and the same I may say of Venus   that great deity preserve Charlie , his pious worshipper , from regard consequence . so he lead I very kindly to the top of the meadow land , where the stream from underground break forth , seethe quietly with a little hiss of bubble . hence I have fair view and outline of the robber ' township , spread with bush here and there , but not heavily overshadow . the moon , approach now the full , bring the form in manner forth , clothe each with character , as the moon   more than the sun   do , to an eye accustom . 
 I know that the Captain 's house be first , both from what Lorna have say of it , and from my mother 's description , and now again from see Charlie halt there for a certain time , and whistle on his finger , and hurry on , fear consequence . the tune that he whistle be strange to I , and linger in my ear , as have something very new and striking , and fantastic in it . and I repeat it softly to myself , while I mark the position of the house and the beauty of the village . for the stream , in lieu of any street , pass between the house , and afford perpetual change , and twinkle , and reflection moreover by its sleepy murmur soothe all the dweller there , this and the snugness of the position , wall with rock and spread with herbage , make it look , in the quiet moonlight , like a little paradise . and to think of all the inmate there , sleep with good conscience , have ply their useful trade of make other work for they , enjoy life without much labour , yet with great renown . 
 Master Charlie go down the village , and I follow he carefully , keep as much as possible in the shadowy place , and watch the window of every house , lest any light should be burn . as I pass Sir Ensor 's house , my heart leap up , for I spy a window , high than the rest above the ground , and with a faint light move . this could hardly fail to be the room wherein my darling lie ; for here that impudent young fellow have gaze while he be whistle . and here my courage grow tenfold , and my spirit fear no evil for lo , if Lorna have be surrender to that scoundrel , Carver , she would not have be at her grandfather 's house , but in Carver 's accursed dwelling . 
 warm with this idea , I hurry after Charleworth Doone , be resolve not to harm he now , unless my own life require it . and while I watch from behind a tree , the door of the farth house be open ; and sure enough it be Carver 's self , who stand bareheade , and half undressed in the doorway . I could see his great black chest , and arm , by the light of the lamp he bear . 
 ' who want I this time of night ? ' he grumble , in a deep gruff voice ; ' any young scamp prowl after the maid shall have sore bone for his trouble . ' 
 ' all the fair maid be for thee , be they , Master Carver ? ' Charlie answer , laugh ; ' we young scamp must be well - content with coarser stuff than thou wouldst have . ' 
 ' would have ? ay , and will have , ' the great beast mutter angrily . ' I bide my time ; but not very long . only one word for thy good , Charlie . I will fling thee senseless into the river , if ever I catch thy girl - face there again . ' 
 ' Mayhap , Master Carver , it be more than thou couldst do . but I will not keep thee ; thou art not pleasant company to - night . all I want be a light for my lanthorn , and a glass of schnapp , if thou hast it . ' 
 ' what be become of thy light , then ? good for thee I be not on duty . ' 
 ' a great owl fly between I and Phelps , as we watch beside the culvern , and so scared be he at our fierce bright eye that he fall and knock the light out . ' 
 ' likely tale , or likely lie , Charles ! we will have the truth to - morrow . here take thy light , and be go with thee . all virtuous man be in bed now . ' 
 ' then so will I be , and why art thou not ? ha , have I earn my schnapps now ? ' 
 ' if thou hast , thou hast pay a bad debt ; there be too much in thee already . be off ! my patience be do with . ' 
 then he slam the door in the young man 's face , have kindle his lanthorn by this time : and Charlie go up to the watchplace again , mutter as he pass I , ' bad look - out for all of we , when that surly old beast be Captain . no gentle blood in he , no hospitality , not even pleasant language , nor a good new oath in his frowsy pate ! I have   a mind to cut the whole of it ; and but for the girl I would so . ' 
 my heart be in my mouth , as they say , when I stand in the shade by Lorna 's window , and whisper her name gently . the house be of one story only , as the other be , with pine - end stand forth the stone , and only two rough window upon that western side of it , and perhaps both of they be Lorna 's . the Doones have be their own builder , for no one should know their in and out ; and of course their work be clumsy . as for their window , they steal they mostly from the house round about . but though the window be not very close , I might have whisper long enough , before she would have answer I ; frighten as she be , no doubt by many a rude overture . and I durst not speak aloud because I see another watchman post on the western cliff , and command all the valley . and now this man   have no companion for drinking or for gambling   espy I against the wall of the house , and advance to the brink , and challenge I . 
 ' who be you there ? Answer ! one , two , three ; and I fire at thee . ' 
 the nozzle of his gun be point full upon I , as I could see , with the moonlight strike on the barrel ; he be not more than fifty yard off , and now he begin to reckon . be almost desperate about it , I begin to whistle , wonder how far I should get before I lose my windpipe : and as luck would have it , my lip fall into that strange tune I have practise last ; the one I have hear from Charlie . my mouth would scarcely frame the note , be parch with terror ; but to my surprise , the man fall back , drop his gun , and salute . oh , sweetest of all sweet melody ! 
 that tune be Carver Doone 's passport   as I hear long afterwards , which Charleworth Doone have imitate , for decoy of Lorna . the sentinel take I for that vile Carver ; who be like enough to be prowl there , for private talk with Lorna ; but not very likely to shout forth his name , if it might be avoid . the watchman , perceive the danger perhaps of intrude on Carver 's privacy , not only retire along the cliff , but withdraw himself to good distance . 
 meanwhile he have do I the kind service ; for Lorna come to the window at once , to see what the cause of the shout be , and draw back the curtain timidly . then she open the rough lattice ; and then she watch the cliff and tree ; and then she sigh very sadly . 
 ' oh , Lorna , do not   you know I ? ' I whisper from the side , be afraid of startle she by appear over suddenly . 
 quick though she always be of thought , she know I not from my whisper , and be shut the window hastily when I catch it back , and show myself . 
 ' John ! ' she cry , yet with sense enough not to speak aloud : ' oh , you must be mad , John . ' 
 ' as mad as a March hare , ' say I , ' without any news of my darling . you know I would come : of course you do . ' 
 ' well , I think , perhaps you know : now , John , you need not eat my hand . do you see they have put iron bar across ? ' 
 ' to be sure . do you think I should be content , even with this lovely hand , but for these vile iron bar . I will have they out before I go . now , darling , for one moment just the other hand , for a change , you know . ' 
 so I get the other , but be not honest ; for I keep they both , and feel their delicate beauty trembling , as I lay they to my heart . 
 ' oh , John , you will make I cry directly ' she have be cry long ago ' if you go on in that way . you know we can never have one another ; every one be against it . why should I make you miserable ? try not to think of I any more . ' 
 ' and will you try the same of I , Lorna ? ' 
 ' oh yes , John ; if you agree to it . at least I will try to try it . ' 
 ' then you will not   try anything of the sort , ' I cry with great enthusiasm , for her tone be so nice and melancholy : ' the only thing we will try to try , be to belong to one another . and if we do our good , Lorna , God alone can prevent we . ' 
 she cross herself , with one hand draw free as I speak so boldly ; and something swell in her little throat , and prevent she from answer . 
 ' now tell I , ' I say ; ' what mean all this ? why be you so pen up here ? why have you give I no token ? have your grandfather turn against you ? be you in any danger ? ' 
 ' my poor grandfather be very ill : I fear that he will not live long . the Counsellor and his son be now the master of the valley ; and I dare not venture forth , for fear of anything they might do to I . when I go forth , to signal for you , Carver try to seize I ; but I be too quick for he . little Gwenny be not allow to leave the valley now ; so that I could send no message . I have be so wretched , dear , lest you should think I false to you . the tyrant now make sure of I . you must watch this house , both night and day , if you wish to save I . there be nothing they would shrink from ; if my poor grandfather oh , I can not bear to think of myself , when I ought to think of he only ; die without a son to tend he , or a daughter to shed a tear . ' 
 ' but surely he have son enough ; and a deal too many , ' I be go to say , but stop myself in time : ' why do none of they come to he ? ' 
 ' I know not . I can not tell . he be a very strange old man ; and few have ever love he . he be black with wrath at the Counsellor , this very afternoon but I must not keep you here you be much too brave , John ; and I be much too selfish : there , what be that shadow ? ' 
 ' nothing more than a bat , darling , come to look for his sweetheart . I will not stay long ; you tremble so : and yet for that very reason , how can I leave you , Lorna ? ' 
 ' you must you must , ' she answer ; ' I shall die if they hurt you . I hear the old nurse move . Grandfather be sure to send for I . keep back from the window . ' 
 however , it be only Gwenny Carfax , Lorna 's little handmaid : my darling bring she to the window and present she to I , almost laugh through her grief . 
 ' oh , I be so glad , John ; Gwenny , I be so glad you come . I have want long to introduce you to my " young man , " as you call he . it be rather dark , but you can see he . I wish you to know he again , Gwenny . ' 
 ' Whoy ! ' cry Gwenny , with great amazement , stand on tiptoe to look out , and star as if she be weigh I : ' she be big nor any Doone ! Heared as she have bate our cornish champion awrastle . ' twadn't   fair play nohow : no , no ; do not   tell I , ' twadn't   fair play nohow . ' 
 ' true enough , Gwenny , ' I answer she ; for the play have be very unfair indeed on the side of the Bodmin champion ; ' it be not a fair bout , little maid ; I be free to acknowledge that . ' by that answer , or rather by the construction she put upon it , the heart of the cornish girl be win , more than by gold and silver . 
 ' I shall knoo thee again , young man ; no fear of that , ' she answer , nod with an air of patronage . ' now , missis , gae on coortin ' , and I wall gae outside and watch for ' ee . ' Though express not over delicately , this proposal arise , no doubt , from Gwenny 's sense of delicacy ; and I be very thankful to she for take her departure . 
 ' she be the good little thing in the world , ' say Lorna , softly laugh ; ' and the queer , and the true . nothing will bribe she against I . if she seem to be on the other side , never , never doubt she . now no more of your " coortin ' , " John ! I love you far too well for that . yes , yes , ever so much ! if you will take a mean advantage of I . and as much as ever you like to imagine ; and then you may double it , after that . only go , do go , good John ; kind , dear , darling John ; if you love I , go . ' 
 ' how can I go without settle anything ? ' I ask very sensibly . ' how shall I know of your danger now ? Hit upon something ; you be so quick . anything you can think of ; and then I will go , and not frighten you . ' 
 ' I have be think long of something , ' Lorna answer rapidly , with that peculiar clearness of voice which make every syllable ring like music of a several note , ' you see that tree with the seven rook ' nest bright against the cliff there ? can you count they , from above , do you think ? from a place where you will be safe , dear ' 
 ' no doubt , I can ; or if I can not , it will not take I long to find a spot , whence I can do it . ' 
 ' Gwenny can climb like any cat . she have be up there in the summer , watch the young bird , day by day , and dare the boy to touch they . there be neither bird , nor egg there now , of course , and nothing do . if you see but six rook ' nest ; I be in peril and want you . if you see but five , I be carry off by Carver . ' 
 ' Good God ! ' say I , at the mere idea ; in a tone which frighten Lorna . 
 ' fear not , John , ' she whisper sadly , and my blood grow cold at it : ' I have mean to stop he ; or at least to save myself . if you can come within one day of that man 's get hold of I , you will find I quite unharmed . after that you will find I dead , or alive , accord to circumstance , but in no case such that you need blush to look at I . ' 
 her dear sweet face be full of pride , as even in the gloom I see : and I would not trespass on her feeling by such a thing , at such a moment , as an attempt at any caress . I only say , ' God bless you , darling ! ' and she say the same to I , in a very low sad voice . and then I steal below Carver 's house , in the shadow from the eastern cliff ; and know enough of the village now to satisfy all necessity , betook myself to my well - know track in return from the valley ; which be neither down the waterslide   a course I fear in the darkness   nor up the cliff at Lorna 's bower ; but a way of my own inventing , which there be no need to dwell upon . 
 a weight of care be off my mind ; though much of trouble hang there still . one thing be quite certain if Lorna could not have John Ridd , no one else should have she . and my mother , who sit up for I , and with I long time afterwards , agree that this be comfort . 




 CHAPTER xxxviii 

 a good turn for JEREMY 

 John Fry have now six shilling a week of regular and permanent wage , besides all harvest and shearing money , as well as a cottage rent - free , and enough of garden - ground to rear pot - herb for his wife and all his family . now the wage appoint by our justice , at the time of session , be four - and - sixpence a week for summer , and a shilling less for the winter - time ; and we could be fin , and perhaps imprison , for give more than the sum so fix . therefore John Fry be look upon as the rich man upon Exmoor , I mean of course among labourer , and there be many joke about rob he , as if he be the mint of the king ; and Tom Faggus promise to try his hand , if he come across John on the highway , although he have cease from business , and be seek a Royal pardon . 
 now be it accord to human nature , or be it a thing contradictory   as I would fain believe ? but anyhow , there be , upon Exmoor , no more discontented man , no man more sure that he have not his worth , neither half so sore about it , than , or as , John Fry be . and one thing he do which I could not wholly   or indeed I may say , in any measure   reconcile with my sense of right , much as I labour to do John justice , especially because of his roguery ; and this be , that if we say too much , or accuse he at all of laziness   which he must have know to be in he , he regularly turn round upon we , and quite compel we to hold our tongue , by threaten to lay information against we for pay he too much wage ! 
 now I have not mention all this of John Fry , from any disrespect for his memory   which be green and honest amongst we , far less from any desire to hurt the feeling of his grandchild ; and I will do they the justice , once for all , to avow , thus publicly , that I have know a great many big rogue , and most of themselves in the number . but I have refer , with moderation , to this little flaw in a worthy character   or foible , as we call it , when a man be dead   for this reason only that without it there be no explain John 's dealing with Jeremy Stickles . 
 Master Jeremy , be full of London and Norwich experience , fall into the error of suppose that we clod and yokel be the simple of the simple , and could be cheat at his good pleasure . now this be not so : when once we suspect that people have that idea of we , we indulge they in it to the top of their bent , and grieve that they should come out of it , as they do at last in amazement , with less money than before , and the laugh now set against they . 
 ever since I have offend Jeremy , by threaten he   as before relate   in case of his meddling with my affair , he have more and more ally himself with simple - minded John , as he be pleased to call he . John Fry be everything : it be ' run and fetch my horse , John ' ' John , be my pistol prim well ? ' ' I want you in the stable , John , about something very particular ' , until except for the rudeness of it , I be long to tell Master Stickles that he ought to pay John 's wage . John for his part be not backward , but give himself the most wonderful air of secrecy and importance , till half the parish begin to think that the affair of the nation be in his hand , and he scorn the sight of a dungfork . 
 it be not likely that this should last ; and be the only man in the parish with any knowledge of politic , I give John Fry to understand that he must not presume to talk so freely , as if he be at least a constable , about the constitution ; which could be no affair of his , and might bring we all into trouble . at this he only toss his nose , as if he have be in London at least three time for my one ; which vex I so that I promise he the thick end of the plough - whip if even the name of a knight of the shire should pass his lip for a fortnight . 
 now I do not suspect in my stupid noddle that John Fry would ever tell Jeremy Stickles about the sight at the Wizard 's Slough and the man in the white nightcap ; because John have swear on the blade of his knife not to breathe a word to any soul , without my full permission . however , it appear that John relate , for a certain consideration , all that he have see , and doubtless more which have accrue to it . upon this Master Stickles be much astonish at Uncle Reuben 's proceeding , have always account he a most loyal , keen , and wary subject . 
 all this I learn upon recover Jeremy 's good grace , which come to pass in no other way than by the saving of his life . be bind to keep the strict watch upon the seven rook ' nest , and yet not bear to be idle and to waste my mother 's store , I contrive to keep my work entirely at the western corner of our farm , which be near to Glen Doone , and whence I could easily run to a height command the view I covet . 
 one day Squire Faggus have drop in upon we , just in time for dinner ; and very soon he and King 's messenger be as thick as need be . Tom have bring his beloved mare to show she off to Annie , and he mount his pretty sweetheart upon she , after give Winnie notice to be on her very good behaviour . the squire be in great spirit , have just accomplish a purchase of land which be worth ten time what he give for it ; and this he do by a merry trick upon old Sir Roger Bassett , who never suppose he to be in earnest , as not possess the money . the whole thing be do on a bumper of claret in a tavern where they meet ; and the old knight have once pledge his word , no lawyer could hold he back from it . they could only say that Master Faggus , be attaint of felony , be not a capable grantee . ' I will soon cure that , ' quoth Tom , ' my pardon have be ready for month and month , so soon as I care to sue it . ' 
 and now he be tell our Annie , who listen very rosily , and believe every word he say , that , have be ruin in early innocence by the mean of lawyer , it be only just , and fair turn for turn , that have become a match for they by long practice upon the highway , he should reinstate himself , at their expense , in society . and now he would go to London at once , and sue out his pardon , and then would his lovely darling Annie , etc . , etc . thing which I have no right to hear , and in which I be not want . 
 therefore I stride away up the lane to my afternoon 's employment , sadly compare my love with theirs   which now appear so prosperous , yet heartily glad for Annie 's sake ; only remember now and then the old proverb ' wrong never come right . ' 
 I work very hard in the copse of young ash , with my billhook and a shearing - knife ; cut out the sapling where they stool too close together , make spar to keep for thatch , wall - crook to drive into the cob , stile for close sheep hurdle , and handle for rake , and hoe , and two - bill , of the large and straighter stuff . and all the less I bind in faggot , to come home on the sledd to the woodrick . it be not to be suppose that I do all this work , without many peep at the seven rook ' nest , which prove my Lorna 's safety . indeed , whenever I want a change , either from cleave , or hew too hard , or stoop too much at bind , I be up and away to the ridge of the hill , instead of stand and do nothing . 
 soon I forget about Tom and Annie ; and fall to thinking of Lorna only ; and how much I would make of she ; and what I should call our child ; and how I would educate they , to do honour to her rank ; yet all the time I work none the bad , by reason of meditation . fresh - cut spar be not so good as those of a little seasoning ; especially if the sap be not go down at the time of cut . therefore we always find it needful to have plenty still in stock . 
 it be very pleasant there in the copse , slop to the west as it be , and the sun descend brightly , with rock and bank to dwell upon . the stem of mottled and dimpled wood , with twig come out like elbow , hang and clung together closely , with a mode of bend in , as child do at some danger ; overhead the shrunken leaf quiver and rustle ripely , have many point like star , and rise and fall delicately , as finger play sad music . along the bed of the slanting ground , all between the stool of wood , there be heap of dead brown leaf , and shelter mat of lichen , and drift of spot stick go rotten , and tuft of rush here and there , full of fray and feathering . 
 all by the hedge run a little stream , a thing that could barely name itself , flow scarce more than a pint in a minute , because of the sunny weather . yet have this rill little crook and cranny dark and bravely bearded , and a gallant rush through a reeden pipe the stem of a flag that be ground ; and here and there divide thread , from the point of a branching stick , into mighty pool of rock   as large as a grown man 's hat almost   nap with moss all around the side and hang with cord grass . along and down the tiny bank , and nod into one another , even across main channel , hang the brown arcade of fern ; some with gold tongue languish ; some with countless ear - drop jerking , some with great quille rib uprising and long saw aflappe ; other cup , and fan over with the grace of yield , even as a hollow fountain spread by wind that have lose their way . 
 deeply each beyond other , plume , stoop , glancing , glistening , weave soft pillow lace , coye to the wind and water , when their fleeting image dance , or by which their beauty move , God have make no lovelier thing ; and only he take heed of they . 
 it be time to go home to supper now , and I feel very friendly towards it , have be hard at work for some hour , with only the voice of the little rill , and some hare and a pheasant for company . the sun be go down behind the black wood on the farth cliff of Bagworthy , and the russet of the tuft and spear - bed be become gray , while the greyness of the saple ash grow brown against the sky ; the hollow curve of the little stream become black beneath the grass and the fairy fan innumerable , while outside the hedge our clover be crimp its leaf in the dewfall , like the cocked hat of wood - sorrel , when , thank God for all this scene , because my love have gift I with the key to all thing lovely , I prepare to follow their example , and to rest from labour . 
 therefore I wipe my bill - hook and shearing - knife very carefully , for I hate to leave tool dirty ; and be doubt whether I should try for another glance at the seven rook ' nest , or whether it would be too dark for it . it be now a quarter of an hour mayhap , since I have make any chop noise , because I have be assort my spar , and tie they in bundle , instead of ply the bill - hook ; and the gentle tinkle of the stream be loud than my doing . to this , no doubt , I owe my life , which then   without my dream it   be in no little jeopardy . 
 for , just as I be twist the bine of my very last faggot , before tuck the cleft tongue under , there come three man outside the hedge , where the western light be yellow ; and by it I could see that all three of they carry firearm . these man be not walk carelessly , but follow down the hedge - trough , as if to stalk some enemy : and for a moment it strike I cold to think it be I they be look for . with the swiftness of terror I conclude that my visit to Glen Doone be know , and now my life be the forfeit . 
 it be a most lucky thing for I , that I hear their clothe catch in the bramble , and see their hat under the rampart of ash , which be make by what we call ' splashing , ' and lucky , for I that I stand in a goyal , and have the dark coppice behind I . to this I have no time to fly , but with a sort of instinct , throw myself flat in among the thick fern , and hold my breath , and lie still as a log . for I have see the light gleam on their gun - barrel , and know the fault of the neighbourhood , would fain avoid swell their number . then the three man come to the gap in the hedge , where I have be in and out so often ; and stand up , and look in over . 
 it be all very well for a man to boast that , in all his life , he have never be frightened , and believe that he never could be so . there may be man of that nature I will not dare to deny it ; only I have never know they . the fright I be now in be horrible , and all my bone seem to creep inside I ; when lie there helpless , with only a billet and the comb of fern to hide I , in the dusk of early evening , I see three face in the gap ; and what be bad , three gun - muzzle . 
 ' somebody be at work here ' it be the deep voice of Carver Doone ; ' jump up , Charlie , and look about ; we must have no witness . ' 
 ' give I a hand behind , ' say Charlie , the same handsome young Doone I have see that night ; ' this bank be too devilish steep for I . ' 
 ' nonsense , man ! ' cry Marwood de Whichehalse , who to my amazement be the third of the number ; ' only a hind cutting faggot ; and of course he hath go home long ago . blind man 's holiday , as we call it . I can see all over the place ; and there be not even a rabbit there . ' 
 at that I draw my breath again , and thank God I have get my coat on . 
 ' squire be right , ' say Charlie , who be stand up high   on a root perhaps , ' there be nobody there now , captain ; and lucky for the poor devil that he keepeth workman 's hour . even his chopper be go , I see . '
