 ' no dog , no man , be the rule about here , when it come to coppice work , ' continue young de Whichehalse ; there be not a man would dare work there , without a dog to scare the pixy . ' 
 ' there be a big young fellow upon this farm , ' Carver Doone mutter sulkily , ' with whom I have an account to settle , if ever I come across he . he hath a cursed spite to we , because we shoot his father . he be go to bring the lumper upon we , only he be afeare , last winter . and he hath be in London lately , for some traitorous job , I doubt . ' 
 ' oh , you mean that fool , John Ridd , ' answer the young squire ; ' a very simple clod - hopper . no treachery in he I warrant ; he hath not the head for it . all he care about be wrestle . as strong as a bull , and with no more brain . ' 
 ' a bullet for that bull , ' say Carver ; and I could see the grin on his scornful face ; ' a bullet for ballast to his brain , the first time I come across he . ' 
 ' nonsense , captain ! I will not   have he shoot , for he be my old school - fellow , and hath a very pretty sister . but his cousin be of a different mould , and ten time as dangerous . ' 
 ' we shall see , lad , we shall see , ' grumble the great black - bearded man . ' Ill bode for the fool that would hinder I . but come , let we onward . no lingering , or the viper will be in the bush from we . body and soul , if he give we the slip , both of you shall answer it . ' 
 ' no fear , captain , and no hurry , ' Charlie answer gallantly , ' would I be as sure of live a twelvemonth as he be of die within the hour ! extreme unction for he in my bullet patch . remember , I claim to be his confessor , because he hath insult I . ' 
 ' thou art welcome to the job for I , ' say Marwood , as they turn away , and keep along the hedge - row ; ' I love to meet a man sword to sword ; not to pop at he from a foxhole . ' 
 what answer be make I could not hear , for by this time the stout ashen hedge be between we , and no other gap to be find in it , until at the very bottom , where the corner of the copse be . yet I be not quit of danger now ; for they might come through that second gap , and then would be sure to see I , unless I creep into the uncut thicket , before they could enter the clearing . but in spite of all my fear , I be not wise enough to do that . and in truth the word of Carver Doone have fill I with such anger , know what I do about he and his pretence to Lorna ; and the sight of Squire Marwood , in such outrageous company , have so move my curiosity , and their threat against some unknown person so arouse my pity , that much of my prudence be forget , or at least the well part of courage , which love danger at long distance . 
 therefore , hold fast my bill - hook , I drop myself very quietly into the bed of the runnel , be resolve to take my chance of their entrance at the corner , where the water dive through the hedge - row . and so I follow they down the fence , as gently as a rabbit go , only I be inside it , and they on the outside ; but yet so near that I hear the branch rustle as they push they . 
 perhaps I have never love fern so much as when I come to the end of that little gully , and stoop betwixt two patch of they , now my chief shelter , for cattle have be through the gap just there , in quest of fodder and coolness , and have leave but a mound of trodden earth between I and the outlaws . I mean at least on my left hand   upon which side they be , for in front where the brook run out of the copse be a good stiff hedge of holly . and now I pray Heaven to lead they straight on ; for if they once turn to their right , through the gap , the muzzle of their gun would come almost against my forehead . 
 I hear they , for I durst not look ; and could scarce keep still for trembling I hear they trample outside the gap , uncertain which track they should follow . and in that fearful moment , with my soul almost look out of my body , expect notice to quit it , what do you think I do ? I count the thread in a spider 's web , and the fly he have lately eat , as their skeleton shake in the twilight . 
 ' we shall see he well in there , ' say Carver , in his horrible gruff voice , like the creaking of the gallow chain ; ' sit there , behind holly hedge , lad , while he cometh down yonder hill ; and then our good - evening to he ; one at his body , and two at his head ; and good aim , lest we baulk the devil . ' 
 ' I tell you , captain , that will not do , ' say Charlie , almost whispering : ' you be very proud of your skill , we know , and can hit a lark if you see it : but he may not come until after dark , and we can not be too nigh to he . this holly hedge be too far away . he cross down here from Slocomslade , not from Tibbacot , I tell you ; but along that track to the left there , and so by the foreland to Glenthorne , where his boat be in the cove . do you think I have track he so many evening , without know his line to a hair ? will you fool away all my trouble ? ' 
 ' come then , lad , we will follow thy lead . thy life for his , if we fail of it . ' 
 ' after I then , right into the hollow ; thy leg be grow stiff , captain . ' 
 ' so shall thy body be , young man , if thou leadest I astray in this . ' 
 I hear they stumble down the hill , which be steep and rocky in that part ; and peer through the hedge , I see they enter a covert , by the side of the track which Master Stickles follow , almost every evening , when he leave our house upon business . and then I know who it be they be come on purpose to murder a thing which I might have guess long before , but for terror and cold stupidity . 
 ' oh that God , ' I think for a moment , wait for my blood to flow ; ' oh that God have give I brain , to meet such cruel dastard accord to their villainy ! the power to lie , and the love of it ; the stealth to spy , and the glory in it ; above all , the quiet relish for blood , and joy in the death of an enemy these be what any man must have , to contend with the Doones upon even term . and yet , I thank God that I have not any of these . ' 
 it be no time to dwell upon that , only to try , if might be , to prevent the crime they be bind upon . to follow the armed man down the hill would have be certain death to I , because there be no covert there , and the last light hang upon it . it seem to I that my only chance to stop the mischief pende be to compass the round of the hill , as fast as foot could be lay to ground ; only keep out of sight from the valley , and then down the rock , and across the brook , to the track from slocombslade : so as to stop the King 's messenger from travel any far , if only I could catch he there . 
 and this be exactly what I do ; and a terrible run I have for it , fear at every step to hear the echo of shot in the valley , and drop down the scrubby rock with tear and violent scratching . then I cross Bagworthy stream , not far below Doone - valley , and breast the hill towards Slocombslade , with my heart very heavily pant . why Jeremy choose to ride this way , instead of the more direct one   which would have be over Oare - hill , be more than I could account for : but I have nothing to do with that ; all I want be to save his life . 
 and this I do by about a minute ; and   which be the hard thing of all   with a great horse - pistol at my head as I seize upon his bridle . 
 ' Jeremy , Jerry , ' be all I could say , be so fearfully short of breath ; for I have cross the ground quick than any horse could . 
 ' speak just in time , John Ridd ! ' cry Master Stickles , still however point the pistol at I : ' I might have know thee by thy size , John . what art do here ? ' 
 ' come to save your life . for God 's sake , go no far . three man in the covert there , with long gun , wait for thee . ' 
 ' ha ! I have be watch of late . that be why I point at thee , John . back round this corner , and get thy breath , and tell I all about it . I never see a man so hurried . I could beat thee now , John . ' 
 Jeremy Stickles be a man of courage , and presence of mind , and much resource : otherwise he would not have be appoint for this business ; nevertheless he tremble greatly when he hear what I have to tell he . but I take good care to keep back the name of young Marwood de Whichehalse ; neither do I show my knowledge of the other man ; for reason of my own not very hard to conjecture . 
 ' we will let they cool their heel , John Ridd , ' say Jeremy , after think a little . ' I can not fetch my musketeer either from Glenthorne or Lynmouth , in time to seize the fellow . and three desperate Doones , well - arm , be too many for you and I . one result this attempt will have , it will make we attack they soon than we have intend . and one more it will have , good John , it will make I thy friend for ever . shake hand my lad , and forgive I freely for have be so cold to thee . Mayhap , in the trouble come , it will help thee not a little to have do I this good turn . ' 
 upon this he shake I by the hand , with a pressure such as we feel not often ; and have learn from I how to pass quite beyond view of his enemy , he ride on to his duty , whatever it might be . for my part I be inclined to stay , and watch how long the three fusilier would have the patience to lie in wait ; but see less and less use in that , as I grow more and more hungry , I swing my coat about I , and go home to Plover 's barrow . 




 CHAPTER XXXIX 

 trouble state and a foolish joke 

 Stickles take I aside the next day , and open all his business to I , whether I would or not . but I give he clearly to understand that he be not to be vex with I , neither to regard I as in any way dishonest , if I should use for my own purpose , or for the benefit of my friend , any part of the knowledge and privity thus enforce upon I . to this he agree quite readily ; but upon the express provision that I should do nothing to thwart his scheme , neither unfold they to any one ; but otherwise be allow to act accord to my own conscience , and as consist with the honour of a loyal gentleman for so he be pleased to term I . now what he say lay in no great compass and may be sum in small still ; especially as people know the chief part of it already . disaffection to the king , or rather dislike to his brother James , and fear of roman ascendancy , have exist now for several year , and of late be spread rapidly ; partly through the downright arrogance of the Tory faction , the cruelty and austerity of the Duke of York , the corruption of justice , and confiscation of ancient right and charter ; partly through jealousy of the french king , and his potent voice in our affair ; and partly   or perhaps one might even say , mainly   through that natural tide in all political channel , which verily move as if it have the moon itself for its mistress . no soon be a thing do and fix , be set far in advance perhaps of all that be do before   like a new mole in the sea , but immediately the water retire , lest they should undo it ; and every one say how fine it be , but leave other people to walk on it . then after awhile , the vague endless ocean , have retire and lain still without a breeze or murmur , fret and heave again with impulse , or with lash lay on it , and in one great surge advance over every rampart . 
 and so there be at the time I speak of , a great surge in England , not roll yet , but seething ; and one which a thousand Chief Justices , and a million Jeremy Stickles , should never be able to stop or turn , by string up man in front of it ; any more than a rope of onion can repulse a volcano . but the bad of it be that this great movement take a wrong channel at first ; not only miss legitimate line , but roar out that the back ditchway be the true and establish course of it . 
 against this rash and random current nearly all the ancient mariner of the State be set ; not to allow the brave ship to drift there , though some little boat might try it . for the present there seem to be a pause , with no open onset , but people on the shore expecting , each accord to his wish , and the feel of his own finger , whence the rush of wind should come which might direct the water . 
 now , to reduce high figure of speech into our own little numeral , all the town of Somersetshire and half the town of Devonshire be full of push eager people , ready to swallow anything , or to make other swallow it . whether they believe the folly about the black box , and all that stuff , be not for I to say ; only one thing I know , they pretend to do so , and persuade the ignorant rustic . Taunton , Bridgwater , Minehead , and Dulverton take the lead of the other town in utterance of their discontent , and threat of what they mean to do if ever a Papist dare to climb the protestant throne of England . on the other hand , the Tory leader be not as yet under apprehension of an immediate outbreak , and fear to damage their own cause by premature coercion , for the struggle be not very likely to begin in earnest during the life of the present king ; unless he should   as some people hop   be so far embolden as to make public profession of the faith which he hold   if any . so the Tory policy be to watch , not indeed permit their opponent to gather strength , and muster in armed force or with order , but be well apprised of all their scheme and intended movement , to wait for some bold overt act , and then to strike severely . and as a Tory watchman or spy , as the Whigs would call he Jeremy Stickles be now among we ; and his duty be threefold . 
 first , and most ostensibly , to see to the levying of poundage in the little haven of Lynmouth , and far up the coast , which be now become a place of resort for the folk whom we call smuggler , that be to say , who land their good without regard to King 's revenue as by law establish . and indeed there have be no officer appoint to take toll , until one have be send to Minehead , not so very long before . the excise as well   which have be order in the time of the Long Parliament   have be little heed by the people hereabouts . 
 second , his duty be   though only the Doones have discover it   to watch those outlaw narrowly , and report of their manner   which be scanty , doing   which be too manifold , reputation   which be execrable , and politic , whether true to the King and the Pope , or otherwise . 
 Jeremy Stickles ' third business be entirely political ; to learn the temper of our people and the gentle family , to watch the movement of the train band   which could not always be trust , to discover any collecting of arm and drilling of man among we , to prevent   if need be , by open force   any importation of gunpowder , of which there have be some rumour ; in a word , to observe and forestall the enemy . 
 now in provide for this last - mention service , the Government have make a great mistake , doubtless through their anxiety to escape any public attention . for all the disposable force at their emissary 's command amount to no more than a score of musketeer , and these so divide along the coast as scarcely to suffice for the duty of sentinel . he hold a commission , it be true , for the employment of the train - band , but upon the understanding that he be not to call upon they   except as a last resource , for any political object ; although he might use they against the Doones as private criminal , if find needful ; and suppose that he could get they . 
 ' so you see , John , ' he say in conclusion , ' I have more work than tool to do it with . I be heartily sorry I ever accept such a mixed and meagre commission . at the bottom of it lie   I be well convinced   not only the desire to keep thing quiet , but the paltry jealousy of the military people . because I be not a Colonel , forsooth , or a captain in his Majesty 's service , it would never do to trust I with a company of soldier ! and yet they would not send either Colonel or Captain , for fear of a stir in the rustic mind . the only thing that I can do with any chance of success , be to rout out these vile Doone fellow , and burn their house over their head . now what think you of that , John Ridd ? ' 
 ' destroy the town of the Doones , ' I say , ' and all the Doones inside it ! surely , Jeremy , you would never think of such a cruel act as that ! ' 
 ' a cruel act , John ! it would be a mercy for at least three county . no doubt you folk , who live so near , be well accustomed to they , and would miss your liveliness in come home after nightfall , and the joy of find your sheep and cattle right , when you not expect it . but after awhile you might get use to the dullness of be safe in your bed , and not lose your sister and sweetheart . surely , on the whole , it be as pleasant not to be rob as to be rob . ' 
 ' I think we should miss they very much , ' I answer after consideration ; for the possibility of have no Doones have never yet occur to I , and we all be so thoroughly use to they , and allow for it in our year 's reckoning ; ' I be sure we should miss they very sadly ; and something bad would come of it . ' 
 ' thou art the staunch of all staunch Tories , ' cry Stickles , laugh , as he shake my hand ; ' thou believest in the divine right of robber , who be good enough to steal thy own fat sheep . I be a jolly Tory , John , but thou art ten time jolly : oh ! the grief in thy face at the thought of be rob no long ! ' 
 he laugh in a very unseemly manner ; while I descry nothing to laugh about . for we always like to see our way ; and a sudden change upset we . and unless it be in the loss of the farm , or the death of the king , or of Betty Muxworthy , there be nothing that could so unsettle our mind as the loss of the Doones of Bagworthy . 
 and beside all this , I be think , of course , and think more than all the rest , about the trouble that might ensue to my own beloved Lorna . if an attack of Glen Doone be make by savage soldier and rude train - band , what might happen , or what might not , to my delicate , innocent darling ? therefore , when Jeremy Stickles again place the matter before I , commend my strength and courage and skill   to flatter I of the high , and finish by say that I would be worth at least four common man to he , I cut he short as follow : 
 ' Master Stickles , once for all , I will have naught to do with it . the reason why be no odd of thine , nor in any way disloyal . only in thy plan remember that I will not strike a blow , neither give any counsel , neither guard any prisoner . ' 
 ' not strike a blow , ' cry Jeremy , ' against thy father 's murderer , John ! ' 
 ' not a single blow , Jeremy ; unless I know the man who do it , and he glory in his sin . it be a foul and dastard deed , yet not do in cold blood ; neither in cold blood will I take God 's task of avenge it . ' 
 ' very well , John , ' answer Master Stickles , ' I know thine obstinacy . when thy mind be make up , to argue with thee be pelt a rock with peppercorn . but thou hast some other reason , lad , unless I be much mistaken , over and above thy merciful nature and christian forgiveness . anyhow , come and see it , John . there will be good sport , I reckon ; especially when we thrust our claw into the nest of the raven . many a yeoman will find his daughter , and some of the Porlock lad their sweetheart . a nice young maiden , now , for thee , John ; if indeed , any ' 
 ' no more of this ! ' I answer very sternly : ' it be no business of thine , Jeremy ; and I will have no joking upon this matter . ' 
 ' good , my lord ; so be it . but one thing I tell thee in earnest . we will have thy old double - deal uncle , Huckaback of Dulverton , and march he first to assault Doone Castle , sure as my name be Stickles . I hear that he hath often vow to storm the valley himself , if only he could find a dozen musketeer to back he . now , we will give he chance to do it , and prove his loyalty to the King , which lie under some suspicion of late . ' 
 with regard to this , I have nothing to say ; for it seem to I very reasonable that Uncle Reuben should have first chance of recover his steal good , about which he have make such a sad to - do , and promise himself such vengeance . I make bold , however , to ask Master Stickles at what time he intend to carry out this great and hazardous attempt . he answer that he have several thing require first to be set in order , and that he must make an inland journey , even as far as Tiverton , and perhaps Crediton and Exeter , to collect his force and ammunition for they . for he mean to have some of the yeomanry as well as of the train band , so that if the Doones should sally forth , as perhaps they would , on horseback , cavalry might be there to meet they , and cut they off from return . 
 all this make I very uncomfortable , for many and many reason , the chief and foremost be of course my anxiety about Lorna . if the attack succeed , what be to become of she ? who would rescue she from the brutal soldier , even suppose that she escape from the hand of her own people , during the danger and ferocity ? and in small way , I be much put out ; for instance , who would ensure our corn - rick , sheep , and cattle , ay , and even our fat pig , now come on for bacon , against the spread all over the country of unlicensed marauder ? the Doones have their right , and understand they , and take they accord to prescription , even as the parson have , and the lord of manor , and the King himself , God save he ! but how be these low soldiering fellow   half - starve at home very likely , and only too glad of the fat of the land , and ready , accord to our proverb , to burn the paper they fry in , who be they to come hectoring and heroing over we , and Heliogabalising , with our pretty sister to cook for they , and be chuck under chin perhaps afterwards ? there be nothing England hat so much , accord to my sense of it , as that fellow take from plough - tail , cart - tail , pot - house and parish - stock , should be hoist and foist upon we   after a few month ' drilling , and their lying shape into truckle   as defender of the public weal , and hero of the universe . 
 in another way I be vex , moreover for after all we must consider the opinion of our neighbour namely , that I know quite well how everybody for ten mile round   for my fame must have be at least that wide , after all my wrestling , would lift up hand and cry out thus ' black shame on John Ridd , if he let they go without he ! ' 
 put all these thing together , as well as many other , which your own wit will suggest to you , it be impossible but what you will freely acknowledge that this unfortunate John Ridd be now in a cloven stick . there be Lorna , my love and life , bind by her duty to that old vil nay , I mean to her good grandfather , who could now do little mischief , and therefore deserve all praise Lorna bind , at any rate , by her womanly feeling , if not by sense of duty , to remain in the thick danger , with nobody to protect she , but everybody to covet she , for beauty and position . here be all the country rouse with violent excitement , at the chance of snap at the Doones ; and not only get tit for tat ; but every young man promise his sweetheart a gold chain , and his mother at least a shilling . and here be our own mow - yard , well fill than we could remember , and perhaps every sheaf in it destine to be burn or steal , before we have finish the bread we have bake . 
 among all these trouble , there be , however , or seem to be , one comfort . Tom Faggus return from London very proudly and very happily , with a royal pardon in black and white , which everybody admire the more , because no one could read a word of it . the squire himself acknowledge cheerfully that he could soon take fifty purse than read a single line of it . some people indeed go so far as to say that the parchment be make from a sheep Tom have steal , and that be why it prevaricate so in give he a character . but I , know something by this time , of lawyer , be able to contradict they ; affirm that the wolf have more than the sheep to do with this matter . 
 for , accord to our old saying , the three learn profession live by roguery on the three part of a man . the doctor maul our body ; the parson starve our soul , but the lawyer must be the adroit knave , for he have to ensnare our mind . therefore he take a careful delight in cover his trap and engine with a spread of dead - leaf word , whereof himself know little more than half the way to spell they . 
 but now Tom Faggus , although have wit to gallop away on his strawberry mare , with the speed of terror , from lawyer   have pay they with money too honest to stop , yet fall into a reckless adventure , ere ever he come home , from which any lawyer would have save he , although he ought to have need none beyond common thought for dear Annie . now I be , and ever have be , so vex about this story that I can not tell it pleasantly   as I try to write in general   in my own word and manner . therefore I will let John Fry   whom I have rob of another story , to which he be more entitle , and whom I have rob of many speech   which he think very excellent , lest I should grieve any one with his lack of education , the last lack he ever feel , by the bye , now with your good leave , I will allow poor John to tell this tale , in his own word and style ; which he have a perfect right to do , have be the first to tell we . for Squire Faggus keep it close ; not trust even Annie with it   or at least she say so ; because no man know much of his sweetheart 's tongue , until she have bear he a child or two . 
 only before John begin his story , this I would say , in duty to he , and in common honesty , that I dare not write down some few of his word , because they be not convenient , for dialect or other cause ; and that I can not find any way of spelling many of the word which I do repeat , so that people , not bear on Exmoor , may know how he pronounce they ; even if they could bring their lip and their leg to the proper attitude . and in this I speak advisedly ; have observe some thousand time that the manner a man have of spread his leg , and bend his knee , or stiffening , and even the way he will set his heel , make all the difference in his tone , and time of cast his voice aright , and power of come home to you . 
 we always like John 's story , not for any wit in they ; but because we laugh at the man , rather than the matter . the way he hold his head be enough , with his chin fix hard like a certainty   especially during his big lie , not a sign of a smile in his lip or nose , but a power of not laugh ; and his eye not turn to anybody , unless somebody have too much of it   as young girl always do   and go over the brink of laughter . thereupon it be good to see John Fry ; how he look gravely first at the laughter , as much as to ask , ' what be it now ? ' then if the fool go laugh more , as he or she be sure to do upon that dry inquiry , John would look again , to be sure of it , and then at somebody else to learn whether the laugh have company ; then if he get another grin , all his mirth come out in glory , with a sudden break ; and he wipe his lip , and be grave again . 
 now John , be too much encourage by the girl   of which I could never break they , come into the house that December evening , with every inch of he full of a tale . Annie see it , and Lizzie , of course ; and even I , in the gloom of great evil , perceive that John be a loaded gun ; but I do not care to explode he . now nothing prim he so hotly as this : if you want to hear all John Fry have hear , the sure of all sure way to it be , to pretend not to care for a word of it . 
 ' I wor over to Exeford in the morning , ' John begin from the chimney - corner , look straight at Annie ; ' for to zee a little calve , Jan , as we cuddn't   get thee to lave houze about . Meesus have get a quare vancy vor un , from wutt she have heer'd of the brade . now zit quite , wull ' e Miss Luzzie , or a ' wunt goo on no vurder . vaine little tayl I will tull ' ee , if so be thee zit quite . wull , as I coom down the hill , I zeed a saight of volk astappe of the ro - udwai . arl on them wi ' girt goon , or two man out of dree wi ' them . rackon there wor dree score on them , tak smarl and beg togather laike ; latt aloun the woman and chiller ; zum on em wi ' match blow , tother wi ' flint - lack . " Wutt be up now ? " I say to Bill Blacksmith , as have knowledge of I : " be the King acoomin ? if she be , do ' ee want to shutt ' un ? " 
 ' " Thee not knaw ! " say Bill Blacksmith , just the zame as I be a tullin of it : " whai , man , we expex Tam Faggus , and zum on us mane to shutt ' un . " 
 ' " Shutt ' un wi'out a warrant ! " say I : ' sure ' ee knaw well nor thic , Bill ! a man may not   shutt to another man , wi'out have a warrant , Bill . warship ze so , last taime I zeed un , and nothing to the contrairy . " 
 ' " haw , haw ! never frout about that , " saith Bill , zame as I be tullin you ; " we have warrant and warship enow , dree or vour on them . and more nor a dizzen warranty ; fro'ut I know to contrairy . Shutt ' un , we mane ; and shutt ' un , we will " Whai , Miss Annie , good Lord , whuttiver mak ' ee stear so ? ' 
 ' nothing at all , John , ' our Annie answer ; ' only the horrible ferocity of that miserable blacksmith . ' 
 ' that be nayther here nor there , ' John continue , with some wrath at his own interruption : ' Blacksmith knawe whutt the Squire have be ; and veare to lose his own custom , if Squire tuk to shooin ' again . Shutt any man I would myzell as intervared wi ' my trade laike . " lucky for thee , " say Bill Blacksmith , " as thee bee'st so shart and fat , Jan. Dree on we wor a gooin ' to shutt ' ee , till we zeed how fat thee waz , Jan. " 
 ' " Lor now , Bill ! " I answer ' un , wi ' a girt cold swat upon I : ' shutt I , Bill ; and my own waife niver drame of it ! " ' 
 here John Fry look round the kitchen ; for he have never say anything of the kind , I doubt ; but now make it part of his discourse , from think that Mistress Fry be come , as she generally do , to fetch he . 
 ' wull do then , Jan Vry , ' say the woman , who have enter quietly , but be only our old Molly . ' Wutt handsome manner thee hast gat , Jan , to spake so well of thy waife laike ; after arl the laife she lead thee ! ' 
 ' putt thee pot on the fire , old ' ooman , and bile thee own bakkon , ' John answer she , very sharply : ' nobody no raight to meddle wi ' a man 's bad ooman but himzell . Wull , here be all these here man awaitin ' , zum wi ' harse , zum wi'out ; the common volk wi ' long girt gun , and tha quarlity wi ' girt broad - sword . who wor there ? Whay latt I zee . there wor Squire Maunder , ' here John assume his full historical key , ' he wi ' the pot to his vittle - place ; and Sir Richard Blewitt shake over the zaddle , and Squaire Sandford of Lee , he wi ' the long nose and one eye , and Sir Gronus Batchildor over to Ninehead Court , and ever so many more on them , tulle up how they be arl gooin ' to be promote , for kitching of Tom Faggus . 
 ' " hope to God , " say I to myzell , " poor Tom wun't   coom here to - day : arl up with she , if ' a doeth : and who be there to suckzade ' un ? " mark I now , all these charps be good to shutt ' un , as her coom crass the watter ; the watter be waide enow there and stony , but no deep than my knee - place . 
 ' " Thee cas'n goo no vurder , " Bill Blacksmith saith to I : " nawbody ' low to crass the vord , until such time as Faggus coom ; plaise God we may mak sure of ' un . " 
 ' " amen , zo be it , " say I ; " God knoweth I be never in any hurry , and would zooner stop nor goo on most taime . " 
 ' Wi ' that I pull my vittle out , and zat a horsebarck , atin ' of them , and oncommon good they be . " will not   we have ' un this taime just , " saith Tim Potter , as keepeth the bull there ; " and yet I be zorry for ' un . but a man must kape the law , she must ; zo be she can only learn it . and now poor Tom will swing as high as the top of they girt hash there . " 
 ' " just thee kitch ' un virst , " say I ; " maisure rope , wi ' the body to maisure by . " 
 ' " Hurrah ! here be another now , " saith Bill Blacksmith , grin ; " another coom to help we . what a grave gentleman ! a warship of the pace , at laste ! " 
 ' for a gentleman , on a cue - ball horse , be come slowly down the hill on tother zide of watter , look at we in a friendly way , and with a long papper stand forth the lining of his coat laike . horse stappe to drink in the watter , and gentleman spak to ' un kindly , and then they coom raight on to ussen , and the gentleman 's face wor so long and so grave , we veare ' a wor gooin ' to prache to we . 
 ' " coort o ' King 's Bench , " saith one man ; " Checker and play , " saith another ; " Spishal Commission , I doubt , " saith Bill Blacksmith ; " back by the Mayor of Taunton . " 
 ' " any Justice of the King 's Peace , good people , to be find near here ? " say the gentleman , lift his hat to we , and very gracious in his manner . 
 ' " your honour , " saith Bill , with his hat off his head ; " there be sax or zeven warship here : arl on them very wise ' un . Squaire Maunder there be the zinnyer . " 
 ' so the gentleman ride up to Squire Maunder , and raise his cocked hat in a manner that take the squire out of countenance , for he could not do the like of it . 
 ' " Sir , " say he , " good and worshipful sir , I be here to claim your good advice and valour ; for purpose of justice . I hold his Majesty 's commission , to make to cease a notorious rogue , whose name be Thomas Faggus . " with that he offer his commission ; but Squire Maunder tell the truth , that he could not rade even word in print , much less write karakter .   then the other magistrate ride up , and put their head together , how to meet the London gentleman without loss of importance . there wor one of them as could rade purty vair , and she make out King 's mark upon it : and he bow upon his horse to the gentleman , and he lay his hand on his heart and say , " worshipful sir , we , as have the honour of his gracious Majesty 's commission , be entirely at your service , and crave instruction from you . "

 ' then a waving of hat begin , and a bowing , and making of leg to wan anather , sich as nayver wor zeed afore ; but none of them arl , for air and brading , cud coom anaigh the gentleman with the long grave face . 
 ' " your warship have post the man right well , " saith he with anather bow all round ; ' surely that big rogue will have no chance leave among so many valiant musketeer . ha ! what see I there , my friend ? rust in the pan of your gun ! that gun would never go off , sure as I be the King 's Commissioner . and I see another just as bad ; and lo , there the third ! pardon I , gentleman , I have be so used to his Majesty 's ordnance - yard . but I fear that bold rogue would ride through all of you , and laugh at your worship 's beard , by George . " 
 ' " but what shall we do ? " Squire Maunder ax ; " I vear there be no oil here . " 
 ' " discharge your piece , gentleman , and let the man do the same ; or at least let we try to discharge they , and load again with fresh powder . it be the fog of the morning hath spoil the priming . that rogue be not in sight yet : but God know we must not be asleep with he , or what will his Majesty say to I , if we let he slip once more ? " 
 ' " excellent , wondrous well say , good sir , " Squire Maunder answer he ; " I never should have think of that now . Bill Blacksmith , tell all the man to be ready to shoot up into the air , directly I give the word . now , be you ready there , Bill ? " 
 ' " all ready , your worship , " saith Bill , salute like a soldier . 
 ' " then , one , two , dree , and shutt ! " cry Squire Maunder , stand up in the iron of his stirrup . 
 ' thereupon they all blaze out , and the noise of it go all round the hill ; with a girt thick cloud arise , and all the air smelling of powder . before the cloud be go so much as ten yard on the wind , the gentleman on the cue - bald horse shut up his face like a pair of nut - crack , as wide as it be long before , and out he pull two girt pistol longside of zaddle , and clap'th one to Squire Maunder 's head , and tother to Sir Richard Blewitt 's . 
 ' " hand forth your money and all your warrant , " he saith like a clap of thunder ; " gentleman , have you now the wit to apprehend Tom Faggus ? " 
 ' Squire Maunder swear so that he ought to be fin ; but he pull out his purse none the slow for that , and so do Sir Richard Blewitt . 
 ' " first man I see go to load a gun , I will gi'e ' un the bullet to do it with , " say Tom ; for you see it be he and no other , look quietly round upon all of they . then he rob all the rest of their warship , as pleasant as might be ; and he saith , " now , gentleman , do your duty : serve your warrant afore you imprison I ; " with that he make they give up all the warrant , and he stick they in the band of his hat , and then he make a bow with it . 
 ' " good morning to your warship now , and a merry Christmas all of you ! and the merry both for rich and poor , when gentleman see their almsgiving . lest you deny yourself the pleasure , I will aid your warship . and to save you the trouble of follow I , when your gun be load this be my strawberry mare , gentleman , only with a little cream on she . gentleman all , in the name of the king , I thank you . " 
 ' all this while he be cast their money among the poor folk by the handful ; and then he spak kaindly to the red mare , and wor over the back of the hill in two zecond , and good part of two maile away , I reckon , afore ever a gun wor load . ' 
    the truth of this story be well establish by first - rate 
  tradition . 




 CHAPTER XL 

 two FOOLS together 

 that story of John Fry 's , instead of cause any amusement , give we great disquietude ; not only because it show that Tom Faggus could not resist sudden temptation and the delight of wildness , but also that we greatly fear lest the King 's pardon might be annul , and all his kindness cancel , by a reckless deed of that sort . it be true   as Annie insist continually , even with tear , to wear in her argument   that Tom have not bring away anything , except the warrant , which be of no use at all , after receipt of the pardon ; neither have he use any violence , except just to frighten people ; but could it be establish , even towards Christmas - time , that Tom have a right to give alm , right and leave , out of other people 's money ? 
 Dear Annie appear to believe that it could ; say that if the rich continually choose to forget the poor , a man who force they to remember , and so to do good to themselves and to other , be a public benefactor , and entitle to every blessing . but I know , and so Lizzie know John Fry be now out of hear that this be not sound argument . for , if it come to that , any man might take the King by the throat , and make he cast away among the poor the money which he want sadly for her grace the Duchess , and the beautiful Countess , of this , and of that . Lizzie , of course , know nothing about his Majesty 's diversion , which be not fit for a young maid 's thought ; but I now put the form of the argument as it occur to I . 
 therefore I say , once for all   and both my sister always listen when I use the deep voice from my chest : 
 ' Tom Faggus hath do wrong herein ; wrong to himself , and to our Annie . all he need have do be to show his pardon , and the magistrate would have rejoice with he . he might have lead a most godly life , and have be respect by everybody ; and know how brave Tom be , I think that he would have do as much . now if I be in love with a maid ' I put it thus for the sake of poor Lizzie ' never would I so imperil my life , and her fortune in life along with I , for the sake of a poor diversion . a man 's first duty be to the woman , who be force to hang upon he ' 
 ' oh , John , not that horrible word , ' cry Annie , to my great surprise , and serious interruption ; ' oh , John , any word but that ! ' and she burst forth cry terribly . 
 ' what word , Lizzie ? what do the wench mean ? ' I ask , in the sad vexation ; see no good to ask Annie at all , for she carry on most dreadfully . 
 ' do not   you know , you stupid lout ? ' say Lizzie , complete my wonderment , by the scorn of her quick intelligence ; ' if you do not   know , axe about ? ' 
 and with that , I be force to be content ; for Lizzie take Annie in such a manner   on purpose to vex I , as I could see   with her head droop down , and her hair come over , and tear and sob rise and fall , to boot , without either order or reason , that see no good for a man to do   since neither of they be Lorna , I even go out into the courtyard , and smoke a pipe , and wonder what on earth be the meaning of woman . 
 now in this I be wrong and unreasonable   as all woman will acknowledge ; but sometimes a man be so put out , by the way they take on about nothing , that he really can not help think , for at least a minute , that woman be a mistake for ever , and hence be for ever mistaken . nevertheless I could not see that any of these great thought and idea apply at all to my Lorna ; but that she be a different being ; not woman enough to do anything bad , yet enough of a woman for man to adore . 
 and now a thing come to pass which test my adoration pretty sharply , inasmuch as I would far liefer face Carver Doone and his father , nay , even the roar lion himself with his hoof and flame nostril , than have meet , in cold blood , Sir Ensor Doone , the founder of all the colony , and the fear of the very fierce . 
 but that I be force to do at this time , and in the manner follow . when I go up one morning to look for my seven rook ' nest , behold there be but six to be see ; for the topmost of they all be go , and the most conspicuous . I look , and look , and rub my eye , and turn to try they by other sight ; and then I look again ; yes , there could be no doubt about it ; the signal be make for I to come , because my love be in danger . for I to enter the valley now , during the broad daylight , could have bring no comfort , but only harm to the maiden , and certain death to myself . yet it be more than I could do to keep altogether at distance ; therefore I run to the near place where I could remain unseen , and watch the glen from the woode height , for hour and hour , impatiently . 
 however , no impatience of mine make any difference in the scene upon which I be gaze . in the part of the valley which I could see , there be nothing move , except the water , and a few steal cow , go sadly along , as if know that they have no honest right there . it sink very heavily into my heart , with all the bed of dead leaf around it , and there be nothing I care to do , except blow on my finger , and long for more wit . 
 for a frost be begin , which make a great difference to Lorna and to myself , I trow ; as well as to all the five million people who dwell in this island of England ; such a frost as never I see before ,   neither hope ever to see again ; a time when it be impossible to milk a cow for icicle , or for a man to shave some of his beard   as I like to do for Lorna 's sake , because she be so smooth   without blunt his razor on hard gray ice . no man could ' keep yatt '   as we say , even though he abandon his work altogether , and thump himself , all on the chest and the front , till his frozen hand would have be bleed except for the cold that keep still all his vein . 
    if John Ridd live until the year 1740   as so strong a man 
  be bind to do , he must have see almost a hard frost ; 
  and perhaps it put an end to he ; for then he would be some 
  fourscore year old . but tradition make he ' keep yatt , ' 
  as he say , up to fivescore year . ED . 
 however , at present there be no frost , although for a fortnight threatening ; and I be too young to know the meaning of the way the dead leaf hung , and the worm - cast prickle like woman 's comb , and the leaden tone upon everything , and the dead weight of the sky . will Watcombe , the old man at Lynmouth , who have be half over the world almost , and who talk so much of the Gulf - stream , have   as I afterwards call to mind   foretold a very bitter winter this year . but no one would listen to he because there be not so many hip and haw as usual ; whereas we have all learn from our grandfather that Providence never send very hard winter , without have furnish a large supply of berry for the bird to feed upon . 
 it be lucky for I , while I wait here , that our very good sheep - dog , old Watch , have choose to accompany I that day . for otherwise I must have have no dinner , be unpersuade , even by that , to quit my survey of the valley . however , by aid of poor Watch , I contrive to obtain a supply of food ; for I send he home with a note to Annie fasten upon his chest ; and in less than an hour back he come , proud enough to wag his tail off , with his tongue hang out from the speed of his journey , and a large lump of bread and of bacon fasten in a napkin around his neck . I have not tell my sister , of course , what be toward ; for why should I make she anxious ? 
 when it grow towards dark , I be just begin to prepare for my circuit around the hill ; but suddenly Watch give a long low growl ; I keep myself close as possible , and order the dog to be silent , and presently see a short figure approach from a thickly - woode hollow on the left side of my hiding - place . it be the same figure I have see once before in the moonlight , at Plover 's barrow ; and prove , to my great delight , to be the little maid Gwenny Carfax . she start a moment , at see I , but more with surprise than fear ; and then she lay both her hand upon mine , as if she have know I for twenty year . 
 ' young man , ' she say , ' you must come with I . I be gwain ' all the way to fetch thee . old man be die ; and she can not   die , or at least she will not , without first consider thee . ' 
 ' consider I ! ' I cry ; ' what can Sir Ensor Doone want with consider I ? have Mistress Lorna tell he ? ' 
 ' all concern thee , and thy doing ; when she know old man be so near his end . that vex he be about thy low blood , a ' think she would come to life again , on purpose for to bate ' ee . but after all , there can not   be scarcely such bad luck as that . now , if her strook thee , thou must take it ; there be no denaying of un . Fire I have see afore , hot and red , and rag ; but I never see cold fire afore , and it maketh I burn and shiver . ' 
 and in truth , it make I both burn and shiver , to know that I must either go straight to the presence of Sir Ensor Doone , or give up Lorna , once for all , and rightly be despise by she . for the first time of my life , I think that she have not act fairly . why not leave the old man in peace , without vex he about my affair ? but presently I see again that in this matter she be right ; that she could not receive the old man 's blessing   suppose that he have one to give , which even a bad man might suppose , while she deceive he about herself , and the life she have undertake . 
 therefore , with great misgiving of myself , but no ill thought of my darling , I send Watch home , and follow Gwenny ; who lead I along very rapidly , with her short broad form glide down the hollow , from which she have first appear . here at the bottom , she enter a thicket of gray ash stub and black holly , with rock around it gnarl with root , and hang with mask of ivy . here in a dark and lonely corner , with a pixie ring before it , she come to a narrow door , very brown and solid , look like a trunk of wood at a little distance . this she open , without a key , by stoop down and press it , where the threshold meet the jamb ; and then she run in very nimbly , but I be force to be bent in two , and even so without comfort . the passage be close and difficult , and as dark as any black pitch ; but it be not long   be it as it might , and in that there be some comfort . we come out soon at the other end , and be at the top of Doone valley . in the chilly dusk air , it look most untempting , especially during that state of mind under which I be labour . as we cross towards the Captain 's house , we meet a couple of great Doones lounge by the waterside . Gwenny say something to they , and although they star very hard at I , they let I pass without hindrance . it be not too much to say that when the little maid open Sir Ensor 's door , my heart thump , quite as much with terror as with hope of Lorna 's presence . 
 but in a moment the fear be go , for Lorna be tremble in my arm , and my courage rise to comfort she . the darling fear , beyond all thing else , lest I should be offend with she for what she have say to her grandfather , and for drag I into his presence ; but I tell she almost a falsehood   the first , and the last , that ever I do tell she , to wit , that I care not that much and show she the tip of my thumb as I say it for old Sir Ensor , and all his wrath , so long as I have his granddaughter 's love . 
 now I try to think this as I say it , so as to save it from be a lie ; but somehow or other it do not answer , and I be vex with myself both way . but Lorna take I by the hand as bravely as she could , and lead I into a little passage where I could hear the river moaning and the branch rustle . 
 here I pass as long a minute as fear ever cheat time of , say to myself continually that there be nothing to be frighten at , yet grow more and more afraid by reason of so reasoning . at last my Lorna come back very pale , as I see by the candle she carry , and whisper , ' now be patient , dearest . never mind what he say to you ; neither attempt to answer he . look at he gently and steadfastly , and , if you can , with some show of reverence ; but above all thing , no compassion ; it drive he almost mad . now come ; walk very quietly . ' 
 she lead I into a cold , dark room , rough and very gloomy , although with two candle burn . I take little heed of the thing in it , though I mark that the window be open . that which I heed be an old man , very stern and comely , with death upon his countenance ; yet not lie in his bed , but set upright in a chair , with a loose red cloak throw over he . upon this his white hair fall , and his pallid finger lie in a ghastly fashion without a sign of life or movement or of the power that keep he up ; all rigid , calm , and relentless . only in his great black eye , fix upon I solemnly , all the power of his body dwelt , all the life of his soul be burn . 
 I could not look at he very nicely , be afeare of the death in his face , and most afeared to show it . and to tell the truth , my poor blue eye fall away from the blackness of his , as if it have be my coffin - plate . therefore I make a low obeisance , and try not to shiver . only I groan that Lorna think it good manner to leave we two together . 
 ' ah , ' say the old man , and his voice seem to come from a cavern of skeleton ; ' be you that great John Ridd ? ' 
 ' John Ridd be my name , your honour , ' be all that I could answer ; ' and I hope your worship be well . ' 
 ' child , have you sense enough to know what you have be do ? ' 
 ' yes , I know right well , ' I answer , ' that I have set mine eye far above my rank . ' 
 ' be you ignorant that Lorna Doone be bear of the old family remain in North Europe ? ' 
 ' I be ignorant of that , your worship ; yet I know of her high descent from the Doones of Bagworthy . ' 
 the old man 's eye , like fire , probe I whether I be jest ; then perceive how grave I be , and think that I could not laugh   as many people suppose of I , he take on himself to make good the deficiency with a very bitter smile . 
 ' and know you of your own low descent from the Ridds of Oare ? ' 
 ' Sir , ' I answer , be as yet unaccustomed to this style of speech , ' the Ridds , of Oare , have be honest man twice as long as the Doones have be rogue . ' 
 ' I would not answer for that , John , ' Sir Ensor reply , very quietly , when I expect fury . ' if it be so , thy family be the very old in Europe . now hearken to I , boy , or clown , or honest fool , or whatever thou art ; hearken to an old man 's word , who have not many hour to live . there be nothing in this world to fear , nothing to revere or trust , nothing even to hope for ; least of all , be there aught to love . ' 
 ' I hope your worship be not quite right , ' I answer , with great misgiving ; ' else it be a sad mistake for anybody to live , sir . ' 
 ' therefore , ' he continue , as if I have never speak , ' though it may seem hard for a week or two , like the loss of any other toy , I deprive you of nothing , but add to your comfort , and   if there be such a thing   to your happiness , when I forbid you ever to see that foolish child again . all marriage be a wretched farce , even when man and wife belong to the same rank of life , have temper well assorted , similar like and dislike , and about the same pittance of mind . but when they be not so match , the farce would become a long , dull tragedy , if anything be worth lament . there , I have reason enough with you ; I be not in the habit of reasoning . though I have little confidence in man 's honour , I have some reliance in woman 's pride . you will pledge your word in Lorna 's presence never to see or to seek she again ; never even to think of her more . now call she , for I be weary . ' 
 he keep his great eye fix upon I with their icy fire   as if he scorn both life and death , and on his haughty lip some slight amusement at my trouble ; and then he raise one hand   as if I be a poor dumb creature , and point to the door . although my heart rebel and kindle at his proud disdain , I could not disobey he freely ; but make a low salute , and go straightway in search of Lorna . 
 I find my love   or not my love ; accord as now she should behave ; for I be very desperate , be put upon so sadly ; Lorna Doone be cry softly at a little window , and listen to the river 's grief . I lay my heavy arm around she , not with any air of claim or of force her thought to I , but only just to comfort she , and ask what she be think of . to my arm she make no answer , neither to my seek eye ; but to my heart , once for all , she speak with her own upon it . not a word , nor sound between we ; not even a kiss be interchange ; but man , or maid , who have ever love hath learn our understanding . 
 therefore it come to pass , that we see fit to enter Sir Ensor 's room in the follow manner . Lorna , with her right hand swallow entirely by the palm of mine , and her waist retire from view by mean of my left arm . all one side of her hair come down , in a way to be remember , upon the left and fairest part of my favourite otter - skin waistcoat ; and her head as well would have lain there doubtless , but for the danger of walk so . I , for my part , be too far go to lag behind in the matter ; but carry my love bravely , fear neither death nor hell , while she abode beside I . 
 Old Sir Ensor look much astonish . for forty year he have be obey and fear by all around he ; and he know that I have fear he vastly , before I get hold of Lorna . and indeed I be still afraid of he ; only for love Lorna so , and have to protect she . 
 then I make he a bow , to the very good of all I have learn both at Tiverton and in London ; after that I wait for he to begin , as become his age and rank in life . 
 ' Ye two fool ! ' he say at last , with a depth of contempt which no word may express ; ' ye two fool ! ' 
 ' may it please your worship , ' I answer softly ; ' maybe we be not such fool as we look . but though we be , we be well content , so long as we may be two fool together . ' 
 ' why , John , ' say the old man , with a spark , as of smile in his eye ; ' thou art not altogether the clumsy yokel , and the clod , I take thee for . ' 
 ' oh , no , grandfather ; oh , dear grandfather , ' cry Lorna , with such zeal and flashing , that her hand go forward ; ' nobody know what John Ridd be , because he be so modest . I mean , nobody except I , dear . ' and here she turn to I again , and rise upon tiptoe , and kiss I . 
 ' I have see a little o ' the world , ' say the old man , while I be half ashamed , although so proud of Lorna ; ' but this be beyond all I have see , and nearly all I have hear of . it be more fit for southern climate than for the fog of Exmoor . ' 
 ' it be fit for all the world , your worship ; with your honour 's good leave , and will , ' I answer in humility , be still ashamed of it ; ' when it happen so to people , there be nothing that can stop it , sir . ' 
 now Sir Ensor Doone be lean back upon his brown chair - rail , which be build like a triangle , as in old farmhouse   from one of which it have come , no doubt , free from expense or gratitude ; and as I speak he cough a little ; and he sigh a good deal more ; and perhaps his die heart desire to open time again , with such a lift of warmth and hope as he descry in our eye , and arm . I could not understand he then ; any more than a baby play with his grandfather 's spectacle ; nevertheless I wonder whether , at his time of life , or rather on the brink of death , he be think of his youth and pride . 
 ' fool you be ; be fool for ever , ' say Sir Ensor Doone , at last ; while we fear to break his thought , but let each other know our own , with little way of pressure ; ' it be the good thing I can wish you ; boy and girl , be boy and girl , until you have grandchild . ' 
 partly in bitterness he speak , and partly in pure weariness , and then he turn so as not to see we ; and his white hair fall , like a shroud , around he . 




 CHAPTER XLI 

 COLD COMFORT 

 all thing be full of flaw , all thing be full of hole , the strength of all thing be in shortness . if Sir Ensor Doone have dwell for half an hour upon himself , and an hour perhaps upon Lorna and I , we must both have weary of he , and require change of air . but now I long to see and know a great deal more about he , and hop that he might not go to Heaven for at least a week or more . however , he be too good for this world   as we say of all people who leave it ; and I verily believe his heart be not a bad one , after all . 
 evil he have do , no doubt , as evil have be do to he ; yet how many have do evil , while receive only good ! be that as it may ; and not vex a question   settle for ever without our vote , let we own that he be , at least , a brave and courteous gentleman . 
 and his loss arouse great lamentation , not among the Doones alone , and the woman they have carry off , but also of the general public , and many even of the magistrate , for several mile round Exmoor . and this , not only from fear lest one more wicked might succeed he   as appear indeed too probable , but from true admiration of his strong will , and sympathy with his misfortune . 
 I will not deceive any one , by say that Sir Ensor Doone give   in so many word   his consent to my resolve about Lorna . this he never do , except by his speech last write down ; from which as he mention grandchild , a lawyer perhaps might have argue it . not but what he may have mean to bestow on we his blessing ; only that he die next day , without take the trouble to do it . 
 he call indeed for his box of snuff , which be a very high thing to take ; and which he never take without be in very good humour , at least for he . and though it would not go up his nostril , through the failure of his breath , he be pleased to have it there , and not to think of die . 
 ' will your honour have it wipe ? ' I ask he very softly , for the brown appearance of it spoil   to my idea   his white mostacchio ; but he seem to shake his head ; and I think it keep his spirit up . I have never before see any one do , what all of we have to do some day ; and it greatly keep my spirit down , although it do not so very much frighten I . 
 for it take a man but a little while , his instinct being of death perhaps , at least as much as of life   which account for his slay his fellow man so , and every other creature , it do not take a man very long to enter into another man 's death , and bring his own mood to suit it . he know that his own be sure to come ; and nature be fond of the practice . hence it come to pass that I , after ease my mother 's fear , and see a little to business , return   as if draw by a polar needle   to the death - bed of Sir Ensor . 
 there be some little confusion , people want to get away , and people try to come in , from downright curiosity   of all thing the most hateful , and other make great to - do , and talk of their own time to come , tell their own age , and so on . but every one seem to think , or feel , that I have a right to be there ; because the woman take that view of it . as for Carver and Counsellor , they be mind their own affair , so as to win the succession ; and never find it in their business   at least so long as I be there   to come near the die man . 
 he , for his part , never ask for any one to come near he , not even a priest , nor a monk or friar ; but seem to be go his own way , peaceful , and well content . only the chief of the woman say that from his face she believe and know that he like to have I at one side of his bed , and Lorna upon the other . an hour or two ere the old man die , when only we two be with he , he look at we both very dimly and softly , as if he wish to do something for we , but have leave it now too late . Lorna hop that he want to bless we ; but he only frown at that , and let his hand drop downward , and crook one knot finger . 
 ' he want something out of the bed , dear , ' Lorna whisper to I ; ' see what it be , upon your side , there . ' 
 I follow the bent of his poor shrunken hand , and seek among the piling ; and there I feel something hard and sharp , and draw it forth and give it to he . it flash , like the spray of a fountain upon we , in the dark winter of the room . he could not take it in his hand , but let it hang , as daisy do ; only make Lorna see that he mean she to have it . 
 ' why , it be my glass necklace ! ' Lorna cry , in great surprise ; ' my necklace he always promise I ; and from which you have get the ring , John . but grandfather keep it , because the child want to pull it from my neck . may I have it now , dear grandfather ? not unless you wish , dear . ' 
 Darling Lorna weep again , because the old man could not tell she   except by one very feeble nod   that she be do what he wish . then she give to I the trinket , for the sake of safety ; and I stow it in my breast . he seem to I to follow this , and to be well content with it . 
 before Sir Ensor Doone be bury , the great frost of the century have set in , with its iron hand , and step of stone , on everything . how it come be not my business , nor can I explain it ; because I never have watch the sky ; as people now begin to do , when the ground be not to their liking . though of all this I know nothing , and less than nothing I may say   because I ought to know something ; I can hear what people tell I ; and I can see before my eye . 
 the strong man break three good pickax , ere they get through the hard brown sod , streak with little map of gray where old Sir Ensor be to lie , upon his back , await the darkness of the Judgment - day . it be in the little chapel - yard ; I will not tell the name of it ; because we be now such protestant , that I might do it an evil turn ; only it be the little place where Lorna 's Aunt Sabina lay . 
 here be I , remain long , with a little curiosity ; because some people tell I plainly that I must be damn for ever by a Papist funeral ; and here come Lorna , scarcely breathe through the thick of stuff around she , yet with all her little breath steam on the air , like frost . 
 I stand apart from the ceremony , in which of course I be not entitle , either by birth or religion , to bear any portion ; and indeed it would have be wise in I to have keep away altogether ; for now there be no one to protect I among those wild and lawless man ; and both Carver and the Counsellor have vow a fearful vengeance on I , as I hear from Gwenny . they have not dare to meddle with I while the chief lay die ; nor be it in their policy , for a short time after that , to endanger their succession by an open breach with Lorna , whose tender age and beauty hold so many of the youth in thrall . 
 the ancient outlaw 's funeral be a grand and move sight ; more perhaps from the sense of contrast than from that of fitness . to see those dark and mighty man , inure to all of sin and crime , reckless both of man and God , yet now with head devoutly bent , clasped hand , and downcast eye , follow the long black coffin of their common ancestor , to the place where they must join he when their sum of ill be do ; and to see the feeble priest chant , over the dead form , word the living would have laugh at , sprinkle with his little broom drop that could not purify ; while the child , rob in white , swing their smoking censer slowly over the cold and twilight grave ; and after see all , to ask , with a shudder unexpresse , ' be this the end that God intend for a man so proud and strong ? ' 
 not a tear be shed upon he , except from the sweetest of all sweet eye ; not a sigh pursue he home . except in hot anger , his life have be cold , and bitter , and distant ; and now a week have exhaust all the sorrow of those around he , a grief flow less from affection than fear . aged man will show his tombstone ; mother haste with their infant by it ; child shrink from the name upon it , until in time his history shall lapse and be forget by all except the great Judge and God . 
 after all be over , I stride across the moor very sadly ; try to keep the cold away by virtue of quick movement . not a flake of snow have fall yet ; all the earth be cake and hard , with a dry brown crust upon it ; all the sky be bank with darkness , hard , austere , and frown . the fog of the last three week be go , neither do any rime remain ; but all thing have a look of sameness , and a kind of furzy colour . it be freeze hard and sharp , with a pierce wind to back it ; and I have observe that the holy water freeze upon Sir Ensor 's coffin . 
 one thing strike I with some surprise , as I make off for our fireside   with a strong determination to heave an ash - tree up the chimney - place , and that be how the bird be go , rather than fly as they use to fly . all the bird be set in one direction , steadily journey westward , not with any heat of speed , neither fly far at once ; but all   as if on business bind , partly run , partly fly , partly flutter along ; silently , and without a voice , neither prick head nor tail . this movement of the bird go on , even for a week or more ; every kind of thrush pass we , every kind of wild fowl , even plover go away , and crow , and snipe and wood - cock . and before half the frost be over , all we have in the snowy ditch be hare so tame that we could pat they ; partridge that come to hand , with a dry noise in their crop ; heath - poult , make cup of snow ; and a few poor hopping redwing , flip in and out the hedge , have lose the power to fly . and all the time their great black eye , set with gold around they , seem to look at any man , for mercy and for comfort . 
 Annie take a many of they , all that she could find herself , and all the boy would bring she ; and she make a great hutch near the fire , in the back - kitchen chimney - place . here , in spite of our old Betty   who sadly want to roast they , Annie keep some fifty bird , with bread and milk , and raw chop meat , and all the seed she could think of , and lump of rotten apple , place to tempt they , in the corner . some get on , and some die off ; and Annie cry for all that die , and bury they under the woodrick ; but , I do assure you , it be a pretty thing to see , when she go to they in the morning . there be not a bird but know she well , after one day of comfort ; and some would come to her hand , and sit , and shut one eye , and look at she . then she use to stroke their head , and feel their breast , and talk to they ; and not a bird of they all be there but like to have it do to he . and I do believe they would eat from her hand thing unnatural to they , lest she should he grieve and hurt by not know what to do for they . one of they be a noble bird , such as I never have see before , of very fine bright plumage , and large than a missel - thrush . he be the hard of all to please : and yet he try to do his good . I have hear since then , from a man who know all about bird , and beast , and fish , that he must have be a norwegian bird , call in this country a Roller , who never come to England but in the most tremendous winter . 
 another little bird there be , whom I long to welcome home , and protect from enemy , a little bird no native to we , but than any native dearer . but lo , in the very night which follow old Sir Ensor 's funeral , such a storm of snow begin as never have I hear nor read of , neither could have dream it . at what time of night it first begin be more than I can say , at least from my own knowledge , for we all go to bed soon after supper , be cold and not inclined to talk . at that time the wind be moan sadly , and the sky as dark as a wood , and the straw in the yard swirling round and round , and the cow huddle into the great cowhouse , with their chin upon one another . but we , be blinder than they , I suppose , and not have have a great snow for year , make no preparation against the storm , except that the lamb ewe be in shelter . 
 it strike I , as I lay in bed , that we be act foolishly ; for an ancient shepherd have drop in and take supper with we , and foretold a heavy fall and great disaster to live stock . he say that he have know a frost beginning , just as this have do , with a black east wind , after day of raw cold fog , and then on the third night of the frost , at this very time of year   to wit on the 15th of December   such a snow set in as kill half of the sheep and many even of the red deer and the forest pony . it be three - score year agone ,   he say ; and cause he have to remember it , inasmuch as two of his toe have be lose by frost - nip , while he dig out his sheep on the other side of the Dunkery . Hereupon mother nod at he , have hear from her father about it , and how three man have be freeze to death , and how badly their stocking come off from they . 
    the frost of 1625 . 
 remember how the old man look , and his manner of listen to the wind and shake his head very ominously   when Annie give he a glass of schnapps , I grow quite uneasy in my bed , as the room get cold and colder ; and I make up my mind , if it only please God not to send the snow till the morning , that every sheep , and horse , and cow , ay , and even the poultry , should be bring in snug , and with plenty to eat , and fodder enough to roast they . 
 alas what use of man 's resolve , when they come a day too late ; even if they may avail a little , when they be most punctual ! 
 in the bitter morning I arise , to follow out my purpose , know the time from the force of habit , although the room be so dark and gray . an odd white light be on the rafter , such as I never have see before ; while all the length of the room be grisly , like the heart of a mouldy oat - rick . I go to the window at once , of course ; and at first I could not understand what be do outside of it . it face due east   as I may have say , with the walnut - tree partly shelter it ; and generally I could see the yard , and the woodrick , and even the church beyond . 
 but now , half the lattice be quite block up , as if plaster with gray lime ; and little fringe , like fern , come through , where the joining of the lead be ; and in the only undarkened part , countless dot come swarm , cluster , beat with a soft , low sound , then glide down in a slippery manner , not as drop of rain do , but each distinct from his neighbour . inside the iron frame   which fit , not to say too comfortably , and go along the stonework , at least a peck of snow have enter , follow its own bend and fancy ; light as any cobweb . 
 with some trouble , and great care , lest the ancient frame should yield , I spread the lattice open ; and see at once that not a moment must be lose , to save our stock . all the earth be flat with snow , all the air be thick with snow ; more than this no man could see , for all the world be snow . 
 I shut the window and dress in haste ; and when I enter the kitchen , not even Betty , the early of all early bird , be there . I rake the ash together a little , just to see a spark of warmth ; and then set forth to find John Fry , Jem Slocombe , and Bill Dadds . but this be easy thought than do ; for when I open the courtyard door , I be take up to my knee at once , and the power of the drift cloud prevent sight of anything . however , I find my way to the woodrick , and there get hold of a fine ash - stake , cut by myself not long ago . with this I plough along pretty well , and thunder so hard at John Fry 's door , that he think it be the Doones at least , and cock his blunderbuss out of the window . 
 John be very loth to come down , when he see the meaning of it ; for he value his life more than anything else ; though he try to make out that his wife be to blame . but I settle his doubt by tell he , that I would have he on my shoulder naked , unless he come in five minute ; not that he could do much good , but because the other man would be sure to skulk , if he set they the example . with spade , and shovel , and pitch - fork , and a round of roping , we four set forth to dig out the sheep ; and the poor thing know that it be high time . 




 CHAPTER XLII 

 the great WINTER 

 it must have snow most wonderfully to have make that depth of cover in about eight hour . for one of Master Stickles ' man , who have be out all the night , say that no snow begin to fall until nearly midnight . and here it be , block up the door , stop the way , and the water course , and make it very much bad to walk than in a saw - pit newly use . however , we trudge along in a line ; I first , and the other man after I ; try to keep my track , but find leg and strength not up to it . Most of all , John Fry be groan ; certain that his time be come , and send message to his wife , and blessing to his child . for all this time it be snow hard than it ever have snow before , so far as a man might guess at it ; and the leaden depth of the sky come down , like a mine turn upside down on we . not that the flake be so very large ; for I have see much large flake in a shower of March , while sow pea ; but that there be no room between they , neither any relaxing , nor any change of direction . 
 Watch , like a good and faithful dog , follow we very cheerfully , leap out of the depth , which take he over his back and ear already , even in the level place ; while in the drift he might have sink to any distance out of sight , and never find his way up again . however , we help he now and then , especially through the gap and gateway ; and so after a deal of flounder , some laughter , and a little swearing , we come all safe to the low meadow , where most of our flock be hurdle . 
 but behold , there be no flock at all ! none , I mean , to be see anywhere ; only at one corner of the field , by the eastern end , where the snow drive in , a great white billow , as high as a barn , and as broad as a house . this great drift be roll and curl beneath the violent blast , tufte and comb with rustle swirl , and carve   as in pattern of cornice   where the groove chisel of the wind sweep round . ever and again the temp snatch little whiff from the channel edge , twirl they round and make they dance over the chime of the monster pile , then let they lie like herring - bone , or the seam of sand where the tide have be . and all the while from the smothering sky , more and more fiercely at every blast , come the pelting , pitiless arrow , wing with murky white , and point with the barb of frost . 
 but although for people who have no sheep , the sight be a very fine one   so far at least as the weather permit any sight at all ; yet for we , with our flock beneath it , this great mount have but little charm . Watch begin to scratch at once , and to howl along the side of it ; he know that his charge be bury there , and his business take from he . but we four man set to in earnest , dig with all our might and main , shovel away at the great white pile , and fetch it into the meadow . each man make for himself a cave , scoop at the soft , cold flux , which slide upon he at every stroke , and throw it out behind he , in pile of castle fancy . at last we drive our tunnel in   for we work indeed for the life of we , and all converge towards the middle , hold our tool and listen . 
 the other man hear nothing at all ; or declare that they hear nothing , be anxious now to abandon the matter , because of the chill in their foot and knee . but I say , ' go , if you choose all of you . I will work it out by myself , you pie - crust , ' and upon that they grip their shovel , be more or less of Englishmen ; and the least drop of english blood be worth the good of any other when it come to last out . 
 but before we begin again , I lay my head well into the chamber ; and there I hear a faint ' ma - a - ah , ' come through some ell of snow , like a plaintive , bury hope , or a last appeal . I shout aloud to cheer he up , for I know what sheep it be , to wit , the most valiant of all the wether , who have meet I when I come home from London , and be so glad to see I . and then we all fall to again ; and very soon we haul he out . Watch take charge of he at once , with an air of the noble patronage , lie on his frozen fleece , and lick all his face and foot , to restore his warmth to he . then fight Tom jump up at once , and make a little butt at Watch , as if nothing have ever ail he , and then set off to a shallow place , and look for something to nibble at . 
 far in , and close under the bank , where they have huddle themselves for warmth , we find all the rest of the poor sheep pack , as closely as if they be in a great pie . it be strange to observe how their vapour and breath , and the moisture exude from their wool have scoop , as it be , a coved room for they , line with a ribbing of deep yellow snow . also the churn snow beneath their foot be as yellow as gamboge . two or three of the weakly hogget be dead , from want of air , and from pressure ; but more than three - score be as lively as ever ; though cramp and stiff for a little while . 
 ' however shall we get them home ? ' John Fry ask in great dismay , when we have clear about a dozen of they ; which we be force to do very carefully , so as not to fetch the roof down . ' no manner of man to draive ' un , drough all they girt driftnesse . ' 
 ' you see to this place , John , ' I reply , as we lean on our shovel a moment , and the sheep come rub round we ; ' let no more of they out for the present ; they be well where they be . watch , here boy , keep they ! '
