 Watch come , with his little scut of a tail cock as sharp as duty , and I set he at the narrow mouth of the great snow antre . all the sheep sidle away , and get close , that the other sheep might be bite first , as the foolish thing imagine ; whereas no good sheep - dog even so much as lip a sheep to turn it . 
 then of the outer sheep   all now snow and frizzle like a lawyer 's wig   I take the two fine and heavy , and with one beneath my right arm , and the other beneath my left , I go straight home to the upper sheppey , and set they inside and fasten they . sixty and six I take home in that way , two at a time on each joumey ; and the work grow hard and hard each time , as the drift of the snow be deepen . no other man should meddle with they ; I be resolve to try my strength against the strength of the element ; and try it I do , ay , and prove it . a certain fierce delight burn in I , as the struggle grow hard ; but rather would I die than yield ; and at last I finish it . People talk of it to this day ; but none can tell what the labour be , who have not feel that snow and wind . 
 of the sheep upon the mountain , and the sheep upon the western farm , and the cattle on the upper barrow , scarcely one in ten be save ; do what we would for they , and this be not through any neglect   now that our wit be sharpen , but from the pure impossibility of find they at all . that great snow never cease a moment for three day and night ; and then when all the earth be fill , and the topmost hedge be unseen , and the tree break down with weight   wherever the wind have not lighten they , a brilliant sun break forth and show the loss of all our custom . 
 all our house be quite snow up , except where we have purge a way , by dint of constant shovelling . the kitchen be as dark and dark than the cider - cellar , and long line of furrowed scollop run even up to the chimney - stack . several window fall right inward , through the weight of the snow against they ; and the few that stand , bulge in , and bent like an old bruise lanthorn . we be oblige to cook by candle - light ; we be force to read by candle - light ; as for bake , we could not do it , because the oven be too chill ; and a load of faggot only bring a little wet down the side of it . 
 for when the sun burst forth at last upon that world of white , what he bring be neither warmth , nor cheer , nor hope of soften ; only a clear shaft of cold , from the violet depth of sky . long - draw alley of white haze seem to lead towards he , yet such as he could not come down , with any warmth remain . broad white curtain of the frost - fog loop around the low sky , on the verge of hill and valley , and above the laden tree . only round the sun himself , and the spot of heaven he claim , cluster a bright purple - blue , clear , and calm , and deep . 
 that night such a frost ensue as we have never dream of , neither read in ancient book , or history of Frobisher . the kettle by the fire froze , and the crock upon the hearth - cheek ; many man be kill , and cattle rigid in their head - rope . then I hear that fearful sound , which never I have hear before , neither since have hear   except during that same winter , the sharp yet solemn sound of tree burst open by the frost - blow . our great walnut lose three branch , and have be die ever since ; though grow meanwhile , as the soul do . and the ancient oak at the cross be rent , and many score of ash tree . but why should I tell all this ? the people who have not see it   as I have   will only make face , and disbelieve ; till such another frost come ; which perhaps may never be . 
 this terrible weather keep Tom Faggus from come near our house for week ; at which indeed I be not vex a quarter so much as Annie be ; for I have never half approve of he , as a husband for my sister ; in spite of his purchase from Squire Bassett , and the grant of the Royal pardon . it may be , however , that Annie take the same view of my love for Lorna , and could not augur well of it ; but if so , she hold her peace , though I be not so sparing . for many thing contribute to make I less good - humour now than my real nature be ; and the very least of all these thing would have be enough to make some people cross , and rude , and fractious . I mean the red and painful chapping of my face and hand , from work in the snow all day , and lie in the frost all night . for be of a fair complexion , and a ruddy nature , and pretty plump withal , and feed on plenty of hot victual , and always force by my mother to sit near the fire than I wish , it be wonderful to see how the cold run revel on my cheek and knuckle . and I fear that Lorna   if it should ever please God to stop the snowing   might take this for a proof of low and rustic blood and breeding . 
 and this I say be the small thing ; for it be far more serious that we be lose half our stock , do all we would to shelter they . even the horse in the stable   muster all together for the sake of breath and steaming   have long icicle from their muzzle , almost every morning . but of all thing the very grave , to my apprehension , be the impossibility of hearing , or have any token of or from my love one . not that those three day alone of snow   tremendous as it be   could have block the country so ; but that the sky have never cease , for more than two day at a time , for full three week thereafter , to pour fresh pile of fleecy mantle ; neither have the wind relax a single day from shake they . as a rule , it snow all day , clear up at night , and freeze intensely , with the star as bright as jewel , earth spread out in lustrous twilight , and the sound in the air as sharp and crackling as artillery ; then in the morning , snow again ; before the sun could come to help . 
 it matter not what way the wind be . often and often the vane go round , and we hop for change of weather ; the only change be that it seem   if possible   to grow colder . indeed , after a week or so , the wind would regularly box the compass   as the sailor call it   in the course of every day , follow where the sun should be , as if to make a mock of he . and this of course immensely add to the peril of the drift ; because they shift every day ; and no skill or care might learn they . 
 I believe it be on Epiphany morning , or somewhere about that period , when Lizzie run into the kitchen to I , where I be thaw my goose - grease , with the dog among the ash the live dog , I mean , not the iron one , for they we have give up long ago , and have catch I , by way of wonder   for generally I be out shovel long before my ' young lady ' have her nightcap off , she positively kiss I , for the sake of warm her lip perhaps , or because she have something proud to say . 
 ' you great fool , John , ' say my lady , as Annie and I use to call she , on account of her air and grace ; ' what a pity you never read , John ! ' 
 ' much use , I should think , in read ! ' I answer , though pleased with her condescension ; ' read , I suppose , with roof come in , and only this chimney leave stick out of the snow ! ' 
 ' the very time to read , John , ' say Lizzie , look grander ; ' our bad trouble be the need , whence knowledge can deliver we . ' 
 ' amen , ' I cry out ; ' be you parson or clerk ? whichever you be , good - morning . ' 
 thereupon I be bent on my usual round   a very small one nowadays , but Eliza take I with both hand , and I stop of course ; for I could not bear to shake the child , even in play , for a moment , because her back be tender . then she look up at I with her beautiful eye , so large , unhealthy and delicate , and strangely shadow outward , as if to spread their meaning ; and she say , 
 ' now , John , this be no time to joke . I be almost freeze in bed last night ; and Annie like an icicle . feel how cold my hand be . now , will you listen to what I have read about climate ten time bad than this ; and where none but clever man can live ? ' 
 ' impossible for I to listen now , I have hundred of thing to see to ; but I will listen after breakfast to your foreign climate , child . now attend to mother 's hot coffee . ' 
 she look a little disappointed , but she know what I have to do ; and after all she be not so utterly unreasonable ; although she do read book . and when I have do my morning 's work , I listen to she patiently ; and it be out of my power to think that all she say be foolish . 
 for I know common sense pretty well , by this time , whether it happen to be my own , or any other person 's , if clearly lay before I . and Lizzie have a particular way of set forth very clearly whatever she wish to express and enforce . but the queer part of it all be this , that if she could but have dream for a moment what would be the first application make I by of her lesson , she would rather have bite her tongue off than help I to my purpose . 
 she tell I that in the Arctic Regions , as they call some place , a long way north , where the Great Bear lie all across the heaven , and no sun be up , for whole month at a time , and yet where people will go explore , out of pure contradiction , and for the sake of novelty , and love of be freeze that here they always have such winter as we be have now . it never cease to freeze , she say ; and it never cease to snow ; except when it be too cold ; and then all the air be choke with glitter spike ; and a man 's skin might come off of he , before he could ask the reason . nevertheless the people there   although the snow be fifty foot deep , and all their breath fall behind they frozen , like a log of wood drop from their shoulder , yet they manage to get along , and make the time of the year to each other , by a little cleverness . for see how the snow be spread , lightly over everything , cover up the hill and valley , and the foreskin of the sea , they contrive a way to crown it , and to glide like a flake along . through the sparkle of the whiteness , and the wreath of windy tossing , and the up and down of cold , any man might get along with a boat on either foot , to prevent his sinking . 
 she tell I how these boat be make ; very strong and very light , of rib with skin across they ; five foot long , and one foot wide ; and turn up at each end , even as a canoe be . but she do not tell I , nor do I give it a moment 's think myself , how hard it be to walk upon they without early practice . then she tell I another thing equally useful to I ; although I would not let she see how much I think about it . and this concern the use of sledge , and their power of gliding , and the lightness of their following ; all of which I could see at once , through knowledge of our own farm - sled ; which we employ in lieu of wheel , use in flat district . when I have hear all this from she , a mere chit of a girl as she be , unfit to make a snowball even , or to fry snow pancake , I look down on she with amazement , and begin to wish a little that I have give more time to book . 
 but God shape all our fitness , and give each man his meaning , even as he guide the waver line of snow descending . our Eliza be mean for book ; our dear Annie for love and cooking ; I , John Ridd , for sheep , and wrestling , and the thought of Lorna ; and mother to love all three of we , and to make the good of her child . and now , if I must tell the truth , as at every page I try to do   though God know it be hard enough , I have feel through all this weather , though my life be Lorna 's , something of a satisfaction in so do duty to my kind and good of mother , and to none but she . for   if you come to think of it   a man 's young love be very pleasant , very sweet , and tickling ; and take he through the core of heart ; without his knowing how or why . then he dwell upon it sideways , without people look , and build up all sort of fancy , grow hot with work so at his own imagining . so his love be a crystal Goddess , set upon an obelisk ; and whoever will not bow the knee   yet without glance at she , the lover make it a sacred rite either to kick or to stick he . I be not speak of I and Lorna , but of common people . 
 then   if you come to think again   lo ! or I will not say lo ! for no one can behold it only feel , or but remember , what a real mother be . ever loving , ever soft , ever turn sin to goodness , vice into virtue ; blind to all nine - tenth of wrong ; through a telescope behold   though herself so nigh to they   faintest decimal of promise , even in her vile child . ready to thank God again , as when her babe be bear to she ; leap   as at kingdom - come   at a wandering syllable of Gospel for she lose one . 
 all this our mother be to we , and even more than all of this ; and hence I feel a pride and joy in do my sacred duty towards she , now that the weather compel I . and she be as grateful and delighted as if she have no more claim upon I than a stranger 's sheep might have . yet from time to time I groan within myself and by myself , at thinking of my sad debarment from the sight of Lorna , and of all that might have happen to she , now she have no protection . 
 therefore , I fall to at once , upon that hint from Lizzie , and be use to thatching - work , and the making of trap , and so on , before very long I build myself a pair of strong and light snow - shoe , frame with ash and rib of withy , with half - tan calf - skin stretch across , and an inner sole to support my foot . at first I could not walk at all , but flounder about most piteously , catch one shoe in the other , and both of they in the snow - drift , to the great amusement of the girl , who be come to look at I . but after a while I grow more expert , discover what my error be , and alter the inclination of the shoe themselves , accord to a print which Lizzie find in a book of adventure . and this make such a difference , that I cross the farmyard and come back again   though turn be the bad thing of all   without so much as fall once , or get my staff entangle . 
 but oh , the aching of my ankle , when I go to bed that night ; I be force to help myself upstairs with a couple of mopstick ! and I rub the joint with neatsfoot oil , which comfort they greatly . and likely enough I would have abandon any further trial , but for Lizzie 's ridicule , and pretend sympathy ; ask if the strong John Ridd would have old Betty to lean upon . therefore I set to again , with a fix resolve not to notice pain or stiffness , but to warm they out of I . and sure enough , before dark that day , I could get along pretty freely ; especially improve every time , after leave off and rest . the astonishment of poor John Fry , Bill Dadds , and Jem Slocombe , when they see I come down the hill upon they , in the twilight , where they be clear the furze rick and truss it for cattle , be more than I can tell you ; because they do not let I see it , but run away with one accord , and flounder into a snowdrift . they believe , and so do every one else   especially when I grow able to glide along pretty rapidly , that I have steal Mother Melldrum 's sieve , on which she be say to fly over the foreland at midnight every Saturday . 
 upon the follow day , I hold some council with my mother ; not like to go without her permission , yet scarcely dare to ask for it . but here she disappoint I , on the right side of disappointment ; say that she have see my pining   which she never could have do ; because I have be too hard at work , and rather than watch I grieve so , for somebody or other , who now be all in all to I , I might go upon my course , and God 's protection go with I ! at this I be amazed , because it be not at all like mother ; and know how well I have behave , ever since the time of our snow up , I be a little move to tell she that she could not understand I . however my sense of duty keep I , and my knowledge of the catechism , from say such a thing as that , or even think twice of it . and so I take she at her word , which she be not prepare for ; and tell she how proud I be of her trust in Providence , and how I could run in my new snow - shoe , I take a short pipe in my mouth , and start forth accordingly . 




 CHAPTER XLIII 

 not TOO soon 

 when I start on my road across the hill and valley   which now be pretty much alike , the utmost I could hope to do be to gain the crest of hill , and look into the Doone Glen . hence I might at least descry whether Lorna still be safe , by the six nest still remain , and the view of the Captain 's house . when I be come to the open country , far beyond the shelter homestead , and in the full brunt of the wind , the keen blast of the cold break on I , and the mighty breadth of snow . moor and highland , field and common , cliff and vale , and watercourse , over all the rolling fold of misty white be flung . there be nothing square or jagged left , there be nothing perpendicular ; all the rugged line be ease , and all the breach smoothly fill . curve , and mound , and rounded heaving , take the place of rock and stump ; and all the country look as if a woman 's hand have be on it . 
 through the sparkle breadth of white , which seem to glance my eye away , and outside the hump of laden tree , bow their back like a woodman , I contrive to get along , half - slide and half - walk , in place where a plain - shodden man must have sink , and wait freezing till the thaw should come to he . for although there have be such violent frost , every night , upon the snow , the snow itself , have never thaw , even for an hour , have never coat over . hence it be as soft and light as if all have fall yesterday . in place where no drift have be , but rather off than on to they , three foot be the least of depth ; but where the wind have chase it round , or any draught lead like a funnel , or anything oppose it ; there you might very safely say that it run up to twenty foot , or thirty , or even fifty , and I believe some time a hundred . 
 at last I get to my spy - hill   as I have begin to call it , although I never should have know it but for what it look on . and even to know this last again require all the eye of love , soever sharp and vigilant . for all the beautiful Glen Doone   shape from out the mountain , as if on purpose for the Doones , and look in the summer - time like a sharp cut vase of green   now be besnowe half up the side , and at either end so , that it be more like the white basin wherein we boil plum - pudding . not a patch of grass be there , not a black branch of a tree ; all be white ; and the little river flow beneath an arch of snow ; if it manage to flow at all . 
 now this be a great surprise to I ; not only because I believe Glen Doone to be a place outside all frost , but also because I think perhaps that it be quite impossible to be cold near Lorna . and now it strike I all at once that perhaps her ewer be freeze   as mine have be for the last three week , require ember around it , and perhaps her window would not shut , any more than mine would ; and perhaps she want blanket . this idea work I up to such a chill of sympathy , that see no Doones now about , and doubt if any gun would go off , in this state of the weather , and know that no man could catch I up   except with shoe like mine , I even resolve to slide the cliff , and bravely go to Lorna . 
 it help I much in this resolve , that the snow come on again , thick enough to blind a man who have not spend his time among it , as I have do now for day and day . therefore I take my neatsfoot oil , which now be clog like honey , and rub it hard into my leg - joint , so far as I could reach they . and then I set my back and elbow well against a snowdrift , hang far adown the cliff , and say some of the Lord 's Prayer , throw myself on Providence . before there be time to think or dream , I land very beautifully upon a ridge of run - up snow in a quiet corner . my good shoe , or boot , preserve I from go far beneath it ; though one of they be sadly strain , where a grub have gnaw the ash , in the early summer - time . have set myself aright , and be in good spirit , I make boldly across the valley   where the snow be furrow hard , be now afraid of nobody . 
 if Lorna have look out of the window she would not have know I , with those boot upon my foot , and a well - clean sheepskin over I , bear my own   J.R.   in red , just between my shoulder , but cover now in snow - flake . the house be partly drift up , though not so much as ours be ; and I cross the little stream almost without know that it be under I . at first , be pretty safe from interference from the other hut , by virtue of the blind snow and the difficulty of walk , I examine all the window ; but these be coat so with ice , like fern and flower and dazzle star , that no one could so much as guess what might be inside of they . moreover I be afraid of pry narrowly into they , as it be not a proper thing where a maiden might be ; only I want to know just this , whether she be there or not . 
 take nothing by this movement , I be force , much against my will , to venture to the door and knock , in a hesitate manner , not be sure but what my answer might be the mouth of a carbine . however it be not so , for I hear a pattering of foot and a whispering go on , and then a shrill voice through the keyhole , ask , ' who be there ? ' 
 ' only I , John Ridd , ' I answer ; upon which I hear a little laughter , and a little sobbing , or something that be like it ; and then the door be open about a couple of inch , with a bar behind it still ; and then the little voice go on , 
 ' put thy finger in , young man , with the old ring on it . but mind thee , if it be the wrong one , thou shalt never draw it back again . ' 
 laugh at Gwenny 's mighty threat , I show my finger in the opening ; upon which she let I in , and bar the door again like lightning . 
 ' what be the meaning of all this , Gwenny ? ' I ask , as I slip about on the floor , for I could not stand there firmly with my great snow - shoe on . 
 ' man enough , and bad man too , ' the cornish girl make answer . we be shut in here , and starving , and durstn't   let anybody in upon we . I wish thou wer't good to ate , young man : I could manage most of thee . ' 
 I be so frightened by her eye , full of wolfish hunger , that I could only say ' good God ! ' have never see the like before . then draw I forth a large piece of bread , which I have bring in case of accident , and place it in her hand . she leap at it , as a starve dog leap at sight of his supper , and she set her tooth in it , and then withhold it from her lip , with something very like an oath at her own vile greediness ; and then away round the corner with it , no doubt for her young mistress . I meanwhile be occupy , to the good of my ability , in take my snow - shoe off , yet wonder much within myself why Lorna do not come to I . 
 but presently I know the cause , for Gwenny call I , and I run , and find my darling quite unable to say so much as , ' John , how be you ? ' between the hunger and the cold , and the excitement of my coming , she have faint away , and lie back on a chair , as white as the snow around we . in betwixt her delicate lip , Gwenny be thrust with all her strength the hard brown crust of the rye - bread , which she have snatch from I so . 
 ' get water , or get snow , ' I say ; ' do not   you know what fainting be , you very stupid child ? ' 
 ' never heerd on it , in Cornwall , ' she answer , trust still to the bread ; ' be un the same as bleed ? ' 
 ' it will be directly , if you go on squeeze away with that crust so . eat a piece : I have get some more . leave my darling now to I . ' 
 hear that I have some more , the starve girl could resist no long , but tear it in two , and have swallow half before I have coax my Lorna back to sense , and hope , and joy , and love . 
 ' I never expect to see you again . I have make up my mind to die , John ; and to die without your know it . ' 
 as I repel this fearful thought in a manner highly fortifying , the tender hue flow back again into her famished cheek and lip , and a soft brilliance glisten from the depth of her dark eye . she give I one little shrunken hand , and I could not help a tear for it . 
 ' after all , Mistress Lorna , ' I say , pretend to be gay , for a smile might do she good ; ' you do not love I as Gwenny do ; for she even want to eat I . ' 
 ' and shall , afore I have do , young man , ' Gwenny answer laughing ; ' you come in here with they red chake , and make we think o ' sirloin . ' 
 ' eat up your bit of brown bread , Gwenny . it be not good enough for your mistress . bless her heart , I have something here such as she never taste the like of , be in such appetite . look here , Lorna ; smell it first . I have have it ever since Twelfth Day , and keep it all the time for you . Annie make it . that be enough to warrant it good cooking . ' 
 and then I show my great mince - pie in a bag of tissue paper , and I tell they how the mince - meat be make of golden pippin finely shre , with the undercut of the sirloin , and spice and fruit accordingly and far beyond my knowledge . but Lorna would not touch a morsel until she have thank God for it , and give I the kind kiss , and put a piece in Gwenny 's mouth . 
 I have eat many thing myself , with very great enjoyment , and keen perception of their merit , and some thank to God for they . but I never do enjoy a thing , that have find its way between my own lip , half , or even a quarter as much as I now enjoy behold Lorna , sit proudly upwards   to show that she be faint no more   enter into that mince - pie , and move all her pearl of tooth   inside her little mouth - place   exactly as I tell she . for I be afraid lest she should be too fast in go through it , and cause herself more damage so , than she get of nourishment . but I have no need to fear at all , and Lorna could not help laugh at I for think that she have no self - control . 
 some creature require a deal of food   I myself among the number , and some can do with a very little ; make , no doubt , the good of it . and I have often notice that the plump and most perfect woman never eat so hard and fast as the skinny and three - cornered one . these last be often ashamed of it , and eat most when the man be absent . hence it come to pass that Lorna , be the lovely of all maiden , have as much as she could do to finish her own half of pie ; whereas Gwenny Carfax   though generous more than greedy , eat up her without wink , after finish the brown loaf ; and then I beg to know the meaning of this state of thing . 
 ' the meaning be sad enough , ' say Lorna ; ' and I see no way out of it . we be both to be starve until I let they do what they like with I . 
 ' that be to say until you choose to marry Carver Doone , and be slowly kill by he ? ' 
 ' slowly ! no , John , quickly . I hate he so intensely , that less than a week would kill I . ' 
 ' not a doubt of that , ' say Gwenny ; ' oh , she hat he nicely then ; but not half so much as I do . ' 
 I tell they that this state of thing could be endure no long , on which point they agree with I , but see no mean to help it . for even if Lorna could make up her mind to come away with I and live at Plover 's Barrows farm , under my good mother 's care , as I have urge so often , behold the snow be all around we , heap as high as mountain , and how could any delicate maiden ever get across it ? 
 then I speak with a strange tingle upon both side of my heart , know that this undertaking be a serious one for all , and might burn our farm down , 
 ' if I warrant to take you safe , and without much fright or hardship , Lorna , will you come with I ? ' 
 ' to be sure I will , dear , ' say my beauty , with a smile and a glance to follow it ; ' I have small alternative , to starve , or go with you , John . ' 
 ' Gwenny , have you courage for it ? will you come with your young mistress ? ' 
 ' will I stay behind ? ' cry Gwenny , in a voice that settle it . and so we begin to arrange about it ; and I be much excited . it be useless now to leave it long ; if it could be do at all , it could not be too quickly do . it be the Counsellor who have order , after all other scheme have fail , that his niece should have no food until she would obey he . he have strictly watch the house , take turn with Carver , to ensure that none come nigh it bear food or comfort . but this evening , they have think it needless to remain on guard ; and it would have be impossible , because themselves be busy offer high festival to all the valley , in right of their own commandership . and Gwenny say that nothing make she so nearly mad with appetite as the account she receive from a woman of all the dish prepare . nevertheless she have answer bravely , 
 ' go and tell the Counsellor , and go and tell the Carver , who send you to spy upon we , that we shall have a fine dish than any set before they . ' and so in truth they do , although so little dream it ; for no Doone that be ever bear , however much of a Carver , might vie with our Annie for mince - meat . 
 now while we sit reflect much , and talk a good deal more , in spite of all the cold for I never be in a hurry to go , when I have Lorna with I she say , in her silvery voice , which always lead I so along , as if I be a slave to a beautiful bell , 
 ' now , John , we be waste time , dear . you have praise my hair , till it curl with pride , and my eye till you can not see they , even if they be brown diamond which I have hear for the fiftieth time at least ; though I never see such a jewel . do not   you think it be high time to put on your snow - shoe , John ? ' 
 ' certainly not , ' I answer , ' till we have settle something more . I be so cold when I come in ; and now I be as warm as a cricket . and so be you , you lively soul ; though you be not upon my hearth yet . ' 
 ' remember , John , ' say Lorna , nestle for a moment to I ; ' the severity of the weather make a great difference between we . and you must never take advantage . ' 
 ' I quite understand all that , dear . and the hard it freeze the well , while that understanding continue . now do try to be serious . ' 
 ' I try to be serious ! and I have be try fifty time , and could not bring you to it , John ! although I be sure the situation , as the Counsellor say at the beginning of a speech , the situation , to say the least , be serious enough for anything . come , Gwenny , imitate he . ' 
 Gwenny be fame for her imitation of the Counsellor make a speech ; and she begin to shake her hair , and mount upon a footstool ; but I really could not have this , though even Lorna order it . the truth be that my darling maiden be in such wild spirit , at see I so unexpected , and at the prospect of release , and of what she have never know , quiet life and happiness , that like all warm and love nature , she could scarce control herself . 
 ' come to this frozen window , John , and see they light the stack - fire . they will little know who look at they . now be very good , John . you stay in that corner , dear , and I will stand on this side ; and try to breathe yourself a peep - hole through the lovely spear and banner . oh , you do not   know how to do it . I must do it for you . Breathe three time , like that , and that ; and then you rub it with your finger , before it have time to freeze again . ' 
 all this she do so beautifully , with her lip put up like cherry , and her finger bend half back , as only girl can bend they , and her little waist throw out against the white of the snow - up window , that I make she do it three time over ; and I stop she every time and let it freeze again , that so she might be the long . now I know that all her love be mine , every bit as much as mine be her ; yet I must have she to show it , dwell upon every proof , lengthen out all certainty . perhaps the jealous heart be loath to own a life worth twice its own . be that as it may , I know that we thaw the window nicely . 
 and then I see , far down the stream   or rather down the bed of it , for there be no stream visible , a little form of fire arise , red , and dark , and flickering . presently it catch on something , and go upward boldly ; and then it strike into many fork , and then it fall , and rise again . 
 ' do you know what all that be , John ? ' ask Lorna , smile cleverly at the manner of my staring . 
 ' how on earth should I know ? papist burn Protestants in the flesh ; and protestant burn Papists in effigy , as we mock they . Lorna , be they go to burn any one to - night ? ' 
 ' no , you dear . I must rid you of these thing . I see that you be bigote . the Doones be fire Dunkery beacon , to celebrate their new captain . ' 
 ' but how could they bring it here through the snow ? if they have sledge , I can do nothing . ' 
 ' they bring it before the snow begin . the moment poor grandfather be go , even before his funeral , the young man , have none to check they , begin at once upon it . they have always bear a grudge against it ; not that it ever do they harm ; but because it seem so insolent . " can not   a gentleman go home , without a smoke behind he ? " I have often hear they say . and though they have do it no serious harm , since they throw the fireman on the fire , many , many year ago , they have often promise to bring it here for their candle ; and now they have do it . ah , now look ! the tar be kindle . ' 
 Though Lorna take it so in joke , I look upon it very gravely , know that this heavy outrage to the feeling of the neighbourhood would cause more stir than a hundred sheep steal , or a score of house sack . not of course that the beacon be of the small use to any one , neither stop anybody from steal , nay , rather it be like the parish knell , which begin when all be over , and depress all the survivor ; yet I know that we value it , and be proud , and speak of it as a mighty institution ; and even more than that , our vestry have vote , within the last two year , seven shilling and six - pence to pay for it , in proportion with other parish . and one of the man who attend to it , or at least who be pay for do so , be our Jem Slocombe 's grandfather . 
 however , in spite of all my regret , the fire go up very merrily , blaze red and white and yellow , as it leap on different thing . and the light dance on the snow - drift with a misty lilac hue . I be astonish at its burning in such mighty depth of snow ; but Gwenny say that the wicked man have be three day hard at work , clearing , as it be , a cock - pit , for their fire to have its way . and now they have a mighty pile , which must have cover five land - yard square , heap up to a goodly height , and eager to take fire . 
 in this I see great obstacle to what I wish to manage . for when this pyramid should be kindle thoroughly , and pour light and blaze round , would not all the valley be like a white room full of candle ? think thus , I be half inclined to abide my time for another night : and then my second thought convince I that I would be a fool in this . for lo , what an opportunity ! all the Doones would be drunk , of course , in about three hour ' time , and get more and more in drink as the night go on . as for the fire , it must sink in about three hour or more , and only cast uncertain shadow friendly to my purpose . and then the outlaws must cower round it , as the cold increase on they , help the weight of the liquor ; and in their jollity any noise would be cheer as a false alarm . Most of all , and which decide once for all my action , when these wild and reckless villain should be hot with ardent spirit , what be door , or wall , to stand betwixt they and my Lorna ? 
 this thought quicken I so much that I touch my darling reverently , and tell she in a few short word how I hop to manage it . 
 ' Sweetest , in two hour ' time , I shall be again with you . keep the bar up , and have Gwenny ready to answer any one . you be safe while they be dining , dear , and drinking health , and all that stuff ; and before they have do with that , I shall be again with you . have everything you care to take in a very little compass , and Gwenny must have no baggage . I shall knock loud , and then wait a little ; and then knock twice , very softly . ' 
 with this I fold she in my arm ; and she look frightened at I ; not have perceive her danger ; and then I tell Gwenny over again what I have tell her mistress : but she only nod her head and say , ' young man , go and teach thy grandmother . ' 




 CHAPTER XLIV 

 BROUGHT HOME AT last 

 to my great delight I find that the weather , not often friendly to lover , and lately seem so hostile , have in the most important matter do I a signal service . for when I have promise to take my love from the power of those wretch , the only way of escape apparent lie through the main Doone - gate . for though I might climb the cliff myself , especially with the snow to aid I , I durst not try to fetch Lorna up they , even if she be not half - starve , as well as partly frozen ; and as for Gwenny 's door , as we call it   that be to say , the little entrance from the woode hollow , it be snow up long ago to the level of the hill around . therefore I be at my wit 's end how to get they out ; the passage by the Doone - gate be long , and dark , and difficult , and lead to such a weary circuit among the snowy moor and hill . 
 but now , be homeward - bind by the short possible track , I slip along between the bonfire and the boundary cliff , where I find a caved way of snow behind a sort of avalanche : so that if the Doones have be keep watch   which they be not do , but revel , they could scarcely have discover I . and when I come to my old ascent , where I have often scale the cliff and make across the mountain , it strike I that I would just have a look at my first and painful entrance , to wit , the water - slide . I never for a moment imagine that this could help I now ; for I never have dare to descend it , even in the fine weather ; still I have a curiosity to know what my old friend be like , with so much snow upon he . but , to my very great surprise , there be scarcely any snow there at all , though plenty curl high overhead from the cliff , like bolster over it . probably the sweeping of the north - east wind up the narrow chasm have keep the shower from block it , although the water have no power under the bitter grip of frost . all my water - slide be now less a slide than path of ice ; furrow where the water run over flute ridge ; seam where wind have toss and comb they , even while congeal ; and cross with little step wherever the freezing torrent linger . and here and there the ice be fibre with the trail of sludge - weed , slant from the side , and mat , so as to make resting - place . 
 Lo it be easy track and channel , as if for the very purpose make , down which I could guide my sledge with Lorna sit in it . there be only two thing to be fear ; one lest the roll of snow above should fall in and bury we ; the other lest we should rush too fast , and so be carry headlong into the black whirlpool at the bottom , the middle of which be still unfrozen , and look more horrible by the contrast . against this danger I make provision , by fix a stout bar across ; but of the other we must take our chance , and trust ourselves to Providence . 
 I hasten home at my utmost speed , and tell my mother for God 's sake to keep the house up till my return , and to have plenty of fire blaze , and plenty of water boiling , and food enough hot for a dozen people , and the good bed air with the warming - pan . dear mother smile softly at my excitement , though her own be not much less , I be sure , and enhance by sore anxiety . then I give very strict direction to Annie , and praise she a little , and kiss she ; and I even endeavour to flat Eliza , lest she should be disagreeable . 
 after this I take some brandy , both within and about I ; the former , because I have sharp work to do ; and the latter in fear of whatever might happen , in such great cold , to my comrade . also I carry some other provision , grieve much at their coldness : and then I go to the upper linhay , and take our new light pony - sledd , which have be make almost as much for pleasure as for business ; though God only know how our girl could have find any pleasure in bumping along so . on the snow , however , it run as sweetly as if it have be make for it ; yet I durst not take the pony with it ; in the first place , because his hoof would break through the ever - shift surface of the light and pile snow ; and secondly , because these pony , come from the forest , have a dreadful trick of neighing , and most of all in frosty weather . 
 therefore I gird my own body with a dozen turn of hay - rope , twist both the end in under at the bottom of my breast , and wind the hay on the skew a little , that the hempen thong might not slip between , and so cut I in the drawing . I put a good piece of spare rope in the sledd , and the cross - seat with the back to it , which be stuff with our own wool , as well as two or three fur coat ; and then , just as I be start , out come Annie , in spite of the cold , pant for fear of miss I , and with nothing on her head , but a lanthorn in one hand . 
 ' oh , John , here be the most wonderful thing ! mother have never show it before ; and I can not   think how she could make up her mind . she have get it in a great well of a cupboard , with camphor , and spirit , and lavender . Lizzie say it be a most magnificent sealskin cloak , worth fifty pound , or a farthing . ' 
 ' at any rate it be soft and warm , ' say I , very calmly fling it into the bottom of the sledd . ' tell mother I will put it over Lorna 's foot . ' 
 ' Lorna 's foot ! oh , you great fool , ' cry Annie , for the first time revile I ; ' over her shoulder ; and be proud , you very stupid John . ' 
 ' it be not good enough for her foot , ' I answer , with strong emphasis ; ' but do not   tell mother I say so , Annie . only thank she very kindly . ' 
 with that I draw my trace hard , and set my ashen staff into the snow , and strike out with my good foot foremost   the good one at snow - shoe , I mean , and the sledd come after I as lightly as a dog might follow ; and Annie , with the lanthorn , seem to be leave behind and wait like a pretty lamp - post . 
 the full moon rise as bright behind I as a paten of pure silver , cast on the snow long shadow of the few thing leave above , burden rock , and shaggy foreland , and the labour tree . in the great white desolation , distance be a mocking vision ; hill look nigh , and valley far ; when hill be far and valley nigh . and the misty breath of frost , pierce through the rib of rock , strike to the pith of tree , creep to the heart of man , lie along the hollow place , like a serpent sloughing . even as my own gaunt shadow   travesty as if I be the moonlight 's daddy - longleg , go before I down the slope ; even I , the shadow 's master , who have try in vain to cough , when cough bring good liquorice , feel a pressure on my bosom , and a husking in my throat . 
 however , I go on quietly , and at a very tidy speed ; be only too thankful that the snow have cease , and no wind as yet arise . and from the ring of low white vapour gird all the verge of sky , and from the rosy blue above , and the shaft of starlight set upon a quivering bow , as well as from the moon itself and the light behind it , have learn the sign of frost from its bitter twinge , I know that we should have a night as keen as ever England feel . nevertheless , I have work enough to keep I warm if I manage it . the question be , could I contrive to save my darling from it ? 
 dare not to risk my sledd by any fall from the valley - cliff , I drag it very carefully up the steep incline of ice , through the narrow chasm , and so to the very brink and verge where first I have see my Lorna , in the fishing day of boyhood . as I then have a trident fork , for stick of the loach , so I now have a strong ash stake , to lay across from rock to rock , and break the speed of descend . with this I moor the sledd quite safe , at the very lip of the chasm , where all be now substantial ice , green and black in the moonlight ; and then I set off up the valley , skirt along one side of it . 
 the stack - fire still be burn strongly , but with more of heat than blaze ; and many of the young Doones be play on the verge of it , the child make ring of fire , and their mother watch they . all the grave and reverend warrior have hear of rheumatism , be inside of log and stone , in the two low house , with enough of candle burn to make our list of sheep come short . 
 all these I pass , without the small risk or difficulty , walk up the channel of drift which I speak of once before . and then I cross , with more of care , and to the door of Lorna 's house , and make the sign , and listen , after take my snow - shoe off . 
 but no one come , as I expect , neither could I espy a light . and I seem to hear a faint low sound , like the moaning of the snow - wind . then I knock again more loudly , with a knocking at my heart : and receive no answer , set all my power at once against the door . in a moment it fly inward , and I glide along the passage with my foot still slippery . there in Lorna 's room I see , by the moonlight flow in , a sight which drive I beyond sense . 
 Lorna be behind a chair , crouch in the corner , with her hand up , and a crucifix , or something that look like it . in the middle of the room lie Gwenny Carfax , stupid , yet with one hand clutch the ankle of a struggle man . another man stand above my Lorna , try to draw the chair away . in a moment I have he round the waist , and he go out of the window with a mighty crash of glass ; luckily for he that window have no bar like some of they . then I take the other man by the neck ; and he could not plead for mercy . I bear he out of the house as lightly as I would bear a baby , yet squeeze his throat a little more than I fain would do to an infant . by the bright moonlight I see that I carry Marwood de Whichehalse . for his father 's sake I spar he , and because he have be my schoolfellow ; but with every muscle of my body string with indignation , I cast he , like a skittle , from I into a snowdrift , which close over he . then I look for the other fellow , toss through Lorna 's window , and find he lie stunned and bleed , neither able to groan yet . Charleworth Doone , if his gush blood do not much mislead I . 
 it be no time to linger now ; I fasten my shoe in a moment , and catch up my own darling with her head upon my shoulder , where she whisper faintly ; and tell Gwenny to follow I , or else I would come back for she , if she could not walk the snow , I run the whole distance to my sledd , care not who might follow I . then by the time I have set up Lorna , beautiful and smile , with the seal - skin cloak all over she , sturdy Gwenny come along , have trudge in the track of my snow - shoe , although with two bag on her back . I set she in beside her mistress , to support she , and keep warm ; and then with one look back at the glen , which have be so long my home of heart , I hang behind the sledd , and launch it down the steep and dangerous way . 
 though the cliff be black above we , and the road unseen in front , and a great white grave of snow might at a single word come down , Lorna be as calm and happy as an infant in its bed . she know that I be with she ; and when I tell she not to speak , she touch my hand in silence . Gwenny be in a much great fright , have never see such a thing before , neither know what it be to yield to pure love 's confidence . I could hardly keep she quiet , without make a noise myself . with my staff from rock to rock , and my weight throw backward , I break the sledd 's too rapid way , and bring my grown love safely out , by the selfsame road which first have lead I to her girlish fancy , and my boyish slavery . 
 unpursue , yet look back as if some one must be after we , we skirt round the black whirling pool , and gain the meadow beyond it . here there be hard collar work , the track be all uphill and rough ; and Gwenny want to jump out , to lighten the sledd and to push behind . but I would not hear of it ; because it be now so deadly cold , and I fear that Lorna might get frozen , without have Gwenny to keep she warm . and after all , it be the sweetest labour I have ever know in all my life , to be sure that I be pull Lorna , and pull she to our own farmhouse . 
 Gwenny 's nose be touch with frost , before we have go much far , because she would not keep it quiet and snug beneath the sealskin . and here I have to stop in the moonlight   which be very dangerous   and rub it with a clove of snow , as Eliza have teach I ; and Gwenny scold all the time , as if myself have freeze it . Lorna be now so far oppress with all the trouble of the evening , and the joy that follow they , as well as by the pierce cold and difficulty of breathing , that she lie quite motionless , like fairest wax in the moonlight when we steal a glance at she , beneath the dark fold of the cloak ; and I think that she be fall into the heavy snow - sleep , whence there be no awaking . 
 therefore , I draw my trace tight , and set my whole strength to the business ; and we slip along at a merry pace , although with many jolting , which must have send my darling out into the cold snowdrift but for the short strong arm of Gwenny . and so in about an hour 's time , in spite of many hindrance , we come home to the old courtyard , and all the dog salute we . my heart be quiver , and my cheek as hot as the Doones ' bonfire , with wonder both what Lorna would think of our farm - yard , and what my mother would think of she . upon the former subject my anxiety be waste , for Lorna neither see a thing , nor even open her heavy eye . and as to what mother would think of she , she be certain not to think at all , until she have cry over she . 
 and so indeed it come to pass . even at this length of time , I can hardly tell it , although so bright before my mind , because it move my heart so . the sledd be at the open door , with only Lorna in it ; for Gwenny Carfax have jump out , and hang back in the clearing , give any reason rather than the only true one that she would not be intrude . at the door be all our people ; first , of course , Betty Muxworthy , teach I how to draw the sledd , as if she have be bear in it , and flourish with a great broom , wherever a speck of snow lay . then dear Annie , and old Molly   who be very quiet , and count almost for nobody , and behind they , mother , look as if she want to come first , but doubt how the manner lay . in the distance Lizzie stand , fearful of encouraging , but unable to keep out of it . 
 Betty be go to poke her broom right in under the sealskin cloak , where Lorna lie unconscious , and where her precious breath hang frozen , like a silver cobweb ; but I catch up Betty 's broom , and fling it clean away over the corn chamber ; and then I put the other by , and fetch my mother forward . 
 ' you shall see she first , ' I say : ' be she not your daughter ? hold the light there , Annie . ' 
 dear mother 's hand be quick and tremble , as she open the shin fold ; and there she see my Lorna sleep , with her black hair all dishevel , and she bent and kiss her forehead , and only say , ' God bless she , John ! ' and then she be take with violent weeping , and I be force to hold she . 
 ' Us may tich of she now , I rackon , ' say Betty in her most jealous way ; ' Annie , tak she by the head , and I will tak she by the toesen . no taime to stand here like girt gawk . don'ee tak on zo , missus . Ther be vainer vish in the zea Lor , but , she be a booty ! ' 
 with this , they carry she into the house , Betty chatter all the while , and go on now about Lorna 's hand , and the other crowd round she , so that I think I be not want among so many woman , and should only get the bad of it , and perhaps do harm to my darling . therefore I go and bring Gwenny in , and give she a potful of bacon and pea , and an iron spoon to eat it with , which she do right heartily . 
 then I ask she how she could have be such a fool as to let those two vile fellow enter the house where Lorna be ; and she account for it so naturally , that I could only blame myself . for my agreement have be to give one loud knock   if you happen to remember   and after that two little knock . well these two drunken rogue have come ; and one , be very drunk indeed , have give a great thump ; and then nothing more to do with it ; and the other , be three - quarter drunk , have follow his leader   as one might say   but feebly , and make two of it . whereupon up jump Lorna , and declare that her John be there . 
 all this Gwenny tell I shortly , between the while of eating , and even while she lick the spoon ; and then there come a message for I that my love be sensible , and be seek all around for I . then I tell Gwenny to hold her tongue   whatever she do among we , and not to trust to woman 's word ; and she tell I they all be liar , as she have find out long ago ; and the only thing to believe in be an honest man , when find . thereupon I could have kiss she as a sort of tribute , like to be appreciate ; yet the pea upon her lip make I think about it ; and think be fatal to action . so I go to see my dear . 
 that sight I shall not forget ; till my die head fall back , and my breast can lift no more . I know not whether I be then more blessed , or harrow by it . for in the settle be my Lorna , prop with pillow round she , and her clear hand spread sometimes to the blaze fireplace . in her eye no knowledge be of anything around she , neither in her neck the sense of lean towards anything . only both her lovely hand be entreat something , to spare she , or to love she ; and the line of supplication quiver in her sad white face . 
 ' all go away , except my mother , ' I say very quietly , but so that I would be obey ; and everybody know it . then mother come to I alone ; and she say , ' the frost be in her brain ; I have hear of this before , John . ' ' Mother , I will have it out , ' be all that I could answer she ; ' leave she to I altogether ; only you sit there and watch . ' for I feel that Lorna know I , and no other soul but I ; and that if not interfere with , she would soon come home to I . therefore I sit gently by she , leave nature , as it be , to her own good time and will . and presently the glance that watch I , as at distance and in doubt , begin to flutter and to brighten , and to deepen into kindness , then to beam with trust and love , and then with gather tear to falter , and in shame to turn away . but the small entreating hand find their way , as if by instinct , to my great project palm ; and tremble there , and rest there . 
 for a little while we linger thus , neither wish to move away , neither care to look beyond the presence of the other ; both alike so full of hope , and comfort , and true happiness ; if only the world would let we be . and then a little sob disturb we , and mother try to make believe that she be only cough . but Lorna , guess who she be , jump up so very rashly that she almost set she frock on fire from the great ash log ; and away she run to the old oak chair , where mother be by the clock - case pretend to be knit , and she take the work from mother 's hand , and lay they both upon her head , kneel humbly , and look up . 
 ' God bless you , my fair mistress ! ' say mother , bend nearer , and then as Lorna 's gaze prevail , ' God bless you , my sweet child ! ' 
 and so she go to mother 's heart by the very near road , even as she have come to mine ; I mean the road of pity , smooth by grace , and youth , and gentleness . 




 CHAPTER XLV 

 a CHANGE long NEEDED 

 Jeremy Stickles be go south , ere ever the frost set in , for the purpose of muster force to attack the Doone Glen . but , of course , this weather have put a stop to every kind of movement ; for even if man could have bear the cold , they could scarcely be bring to face the peril of the snow - drift . and to tell the truth I care not how long this weather last , so long as we have enough to eat , and could keep ourselves from freeze . not only that I do not want Master Stickles back again , to make more disturbance ; but also that the Doones could not come prowl after Lorna while the snow lie pile between we , with the surface soft and dry . of course they would very soon discover where their lawful queen be , although the track of sledd and snow - shoe have be quite obliterate by another shower , before the reveller could have grow half as drunk as they intend . but Marwood de Whichehalse , who have be snow up among they   as Gwenny say , after help to strip the beacon , that young squire be almost certain to have recognise I , and to have tell the vile Carver . and it give I no little pleasure to think how mad that Carver must be with I , for rob he of the lovely bride whom he be starve into matrimony . however , I be not pleased at all with the prospect of the consequence ; but set all hand on to thresh the corn , ere the Doones could come and burn the rick . for I know that they could not come yet , inasmuch as even a forest pony could not traverse the country , much less the heavy horse need to carry such man as they be . and hundred of the forest pony die in this hard weather , some be bury in the snow , and more of they starve for want of grass . 
 go through this state of thing , and lay down the law about it   subject to correction , I very soon persuade Lorna that for the present she be safe , and   which make she still more happy   that she be not only welcome , but as gladden to our eye as the flower of May . of course , so far as regard myself , this be not a hundredth part of the real truth ; and even as regard other , I might have say it ten time over . for Lorna have so win they all , by her kind and gentle way , and her mode of hearken to everybody 's trouble , and reply without word , as well as by her beauty , and simple grace of all thing , that I could almost wish sometimes the rest would leave she more to I . but mother could not do enough ; and Annie almost worship she ; and even Lizzie could not keep her bitterness towards she ; especially when she find that Lorna know as much of book as need be . 
 as for John Fry , and Betty , and Molly , they be a perfect plague when Lorna come into the kitchen . for betwixt their curiosity to see a live Doone in the flesh   when certain not to eat they , and their high respect for birth   with or without honesty , and their intense desire to know all about Master John 's sweetheart   drop , as they say , from the snow - cloud , and most of all their admiration of a beauty such as never even their angel could have see betwixt and between all this , I say , there be no get the dinner cook , with Lorna in the kitchen . 
 and the bad of it be that Lorna take the strange of all strange fancy for this very kitchen ; and it be hard to keep she out of it . not that she have any special bent for cooking , as our Annie have ; rather indeed the contrary , for she like to have her food ready cook ; but that she love the look of the place , and the cheerful fire burning , and the rack of bacon to be see , and the richness , and the homeliness , and the pleasant smell of everything . and who know but what she may have like   as the very good of maiden do   to be admire , now and then , between the time of business ? 
 therefore if you want Lorna   as I be always sure to do , God know how many time a day , the very sure place to find she be our own old kitchen . not gossip , I mean , nor loitering , neither seek into thing , but seem to be quite at home , as if she have know it from a child , and seem   to my eye at least   to light it up , and make life and colour out of all the dullness ; as I have see the break sun do among brown shock of wheat . 
 but any one who wish to learn whether girl can change or not , as the thing around they change   while yet their heart be steadfast , and for ever anchor , he should just have see my Lorna , after a fortnight of our life , and freedom from anxiety . it be possible that my company although I be account stupid by folk who do not know my way may have have something to do with it ; but upon this I will not say much , lest I lose my character . and indeed , as regard company , I have all the threshing to see to , and more than half to do myself   though any one would have think that even John Fry must work hard this weather , else I could not hope at all to get our corn into such compass that a good gun might protect it . 
 but to come back to Lorna again   which I always long to do , and must long for ever , all the change between night and day , all the shift of cloud and sun , all the difference between black death and brightsome liveliness , scarcely may suggest or equal Lorna 's transformation . quick she have always be and ' peart '   as we say on Exmoor   and gift with a leap of thought too swift for I to follow ; and hence you may find fault with much , when I report her saying . but through the whole have always run , as a black string go through pearl , something dark and touch with shadow , colour as with an early end . 
 but , now , behold ! there be none of this ! there be no get she , for a moment , even to be serious . all her bright young wit be flash , like a newly - awaken flame , and all her high young spirit leap , as if dance to its fire . and yet she never speak a word which give more pain than pleasure . 
 and even in her outward look there be much of difference . whether it be our warmth , and freedom , and our harmless love of God , and trust in one another ; or whether it be our air , and water , and the pea - feed bacon ; anyhow my Lorna grow rich and more lovely , more perfect and more firm of figure , and more light and buoyant , with every pass day that lay its tribute on her cheek and lip . I be allow one kiss a day ; only one for manner ' sake , because she be our visitor ; and I might have it before breakfast , or else when I come to say ' good - night ! ' accord as I decide . and I decide every night , not to take it in the morning , but put it off till the evening time , and have the pleasure to think about , through all the day of working . but when my darling come up to I in the early daylight , fresh than the daystar , and with no one look ; only her bright eye smile , and sweet lip quite ready , be it likely I could wait , and think all day about it ? for she wear a frock of Annie 's , nicely make to fit she , take in at the waist and curve I never could explain it , not be a mantua - maker ; but I know how her figure look in it , and how it come towards I . 
 but this be neither here nor there ; and I must on with my story . those day be very sacred to I , and if I speak lightly of they , trust I , ' tis with lip alone ; while from heart reproach peep sadly at the flippant trick of mind . 
 although it be the long winter ever know in our part   never have cease to freeze for a single night , and scarcely for a single day , from the middle of December till the second week in March , to I it be the very short and the most delicious ; and verily I do believe it be the same to Lorna . but when the Ides of March be come   of which I do remember something dim from school , and something clear from my favourite writer   lo , there be increase signal of a change of weather . 
 one lead feature of that long cold , and a thing remark by every one   however unobservant   have be the hollow moaning sound ever present in the air , morning , noon , and night - time , and especially at night , whether any wind be stir , or whether it be a perfect calm . our people say that it be a witch curse all the country from the cavern by the sea , and that frost and snow would last until we could catch and drown she . but the land , be thoroughly block with snow , and the inshore part of the sea with ice   float in great field along , Mother Melldrum   if she it be   have the cavern all to herself , for there be no getting at she . and speak of the sea remind I of a thing report to we , and on good authority ; though people might be find hereafter who would not believe it , unless I tell they that from what I myself beheld of the channel I place perfect faith in it : and this be , that a dozen sailor at the beginning of March cross the ice , with the aid of pole from Clevedon to Penarth , or where the Holm rock bar the flotage . 
 but now , about the tenth of March , that miserable moan noise , which have both foregone and accompany the rigour , die away from out the air ; and we , be now so used to it , think at first that we must be deaf . and then the fog , which have hang about   even in full sunshine   vanish , and the shroud hill shine forth with brightness manifold . and now the sky at length begin to come to its true manner , which we have not see for month , a mixture   if I so may speak   of various expression . whereas till now from Allhallows - tide , six week ere the great frost set in , the heavens have wear one heavy mask of ashen gray when cloud , or else one amethystine tinge with a hazy rim , when cloudless . so it be pleasant to behold , after that monotony , the fickle sky which suit our England , though abuse by foreign folk . 
 and soon the dapple soften sky give some earnest of its mood ; for a brisk south wind arise , and the blessed rain come drive , cold indeed , yet most refreshing to the skin , all parch with snow , and the eyeball so long dazzle . neither be the heart more sluggish in its thankfulness to God . People have begin to think , and somebody have prophesy , that we should have no spring this year , no seed - time , and no harvest ; for that the Lord have send a judgment on this country of England , and the nation dwell in it , because of the wickedness of the Court , and the encouragement show to Papists . and this be prove , they say , by what have happen in the town of London ; where , for more than a fortnight , such a chill of darkness lie that no man might behold his neighbour , even across the narrow street ; and where the ice upon the Thames be more than four foot thick , and crush London Bridge in twain . now to these prophet I pay no heed , believe not that Providence would freeze we for other people 's sin ; neither see how England could for many generation have enjoy good sunshine , if Popery mean frost and fog . besides , why could not Providence settle the business once for all by freeze the Pope himself ; even though   accord to our view   he be destine to extreme of heat , together with all who follow he ? 
 not to meddle with that subject , be beyond my judgment , let I tell the thing I see , and then you must believe I . the wind , of course , I could not see , not have the power of a pig ; but I could see the laden branch of the great oak move , hop to shake off the load pack and saddle on they . and hereby I may note a thing which some one may explain perhaps in the after age , when people come to look at thing . this be that in desperate cold all the tree be pull awry , even though the wind have scatter the snow burden from they . of some sort the branch bend downwards , like an archway ; of other sort the bough curve upwards , like a red deer 's frontlet . this I know no reason   for ; but be ready to swear that I see it . 
    the reason be very simple , as all nature 's reason be ; 
  though the subject have not yet be investigate thoroughly . 
  in some tree the vascular tissue be more open on the upper 
  side , in other on the under side , of the spreading 
  branch ; accord to the form of growth , and habit of the 
  sap . hence in very severe cold , when the vessel 
   comparatively empty   be constrict , some have more power 
  of contraction on the upper side , and some upon the under . 
 now when the first of the rain begin , and the old familiar softness spread upon the window glass , and run a little way in channel   though from the coldness of the glass it freeze before reach the bottom , know at once the difference from the short sharp thud of snow , we all run out , and fill our eye and fill our heart with gazing . true , the snow be pile up now all in mountain round we ; true , the air be still so cold that our breath freeze on the doorway , and the rain be turn to ice wherever it strike anything ; nevertheless that it be rain there be no denying , as we watch it across black doorway , and could see no sign of white . Mother , who have make up her mind that the farm be not worth have after all those prophesie , and that all of we must starve , and hole be scratch in the snow for we , and no use to put up a tombstone   for our church have be shut up long ago   mother fall upon my breast , and sob that I be the clever fellow ever bear of woman . and this because I have condemn the prophet for a pack of fool ; not see how business could go on , if people stop to hearken to they . 
 then Lorna come and glorify I , for I have predict a change of weather , more to keep their spirit up , than with real hope of it ; and then come Annie blushing shyly , as I look at she , and say that Winnie would soon have four leg now . this refer to some stupid joke make by John Fry or somebody , that in this weather a man have no leg , and a horse have only two . 
 but as the rain come down upon we from the southwest wind , and we could not have enough of it , even put our tongue to catch it , as little child might do , and begin to talk of primrose ; the very noble thing of all be to hear and see the gratitude of the poor beast yet remain and the few survive bird . from the cowhouse lowing come , more than of fifty milking time ; moo and moo , and a turn - up noise at the end of every bellow , as if from the very heart of kine . then the horse in the stable , pack as closely as they could stick , at the risk of kicking , to keep the warmth in one another , and their spirit up by discourse ; these begin with one accord to lift up their voice , snort , snaffling , whinnying , and neighing , and trot to the door to know when they should have work again . to whom , as if in answer , come the feeble bleating of the sheep , what few , by dint of great care , have keep their fleece on their back , and their four leg under they . 
 neither be it a trifling thing , let whoso will say the contrary , to behold the duck and geese marching forth in handsome order from their bed of fern and straw . what a goodly noise they keep , what a flapping of their wing , and a jerking of their tail , as they stand right up and try with a whistling in their throat to imitate a cockscrow ! and then how daintily they take the wet upon their dusty plume , and duck their shoulder to it , and begin to dress themselves , and lay their groove bill on the snow , and dabble for more ooziness ! 
 Lorna have never see , I dare say , anything like this before , and it be all that we could do to keep she from rush forth with only little lambswool shoe on , and kiss every one of they . ' oh , the dear thing , oh , the dear thing ! ' she keep say continually , ' how wonderfully clever they be ! only look at that one with his foot up , give order to the other , John ! ' 
 ' and I must give order to you , my darling , ' I answer , gaze on her face , so brilliant with excitement ; ' and that be , that you come in at once , with that worrisome cough of your ; and sit by the fire , and warm yourself . ' 
 ' oh , no , John ! not for a minute , if you please , good John . I want to see the snow go away , and the green meadow come forth . and here come our favourite robin , who have live in the oven so long , and sing we a song every morning . I must see what he think of it ! ' 
 ' you will do nothing of the sort , ' I answer very shortly , be only too glad of a cause for have she in my arm again . so I catch she up , and carry she in ; and she look and smile so sweetly at I instead of pout   as I have fear   that I find myself unable to go very fast along the passage . and I set she there in her favourite place , by the sweet - scented wood - fire ; and she pay I porterage without my even ask she ; and for all the beauty of the rain , I be fain to stay with she ; until our Annie come to say that my advice be want . 
 now my advice be never much , as everybody know quite well ; but that be the way they always put it , when they want I to work for they . and in truth it be time for I to work ; not for other , but myself , and   as I always think   for Lorna . for the rain be now come down in earnest ; and the top of the snow be freeze at last , and glaze as hard as a china cup , by mean of the sun and frost afterwards , all the rain run right away from the steep incline , and all the outlet be block with ice set up like table , it threaten to flood everything . already it be ponde up , like a tide advance at the threshold of the door from which we have watch the duck - bird ; both because great pile of snow trend in that direction , in spite of all our scraping , and also that the gulley hole , where the water of the shoot go out   I mean when it be water   now be choke with lump of ice , as big as a man 's body . for the ' shoot , ' as we call our little runnel of everlasting water , never know to freeze before , and always ready for any man either to wash his hand , or drink , where it spout from a trough of bark , set among white flint - stone ; this at last have give in , and its music cease to lull we , as we lay in bed . 
 it be not long before I manage to drain off this threatening flood , by open the old sluice - hole ; but I have much hard work to keep the stable , and the cow - house , and the other shed , from flooding . for we have a sapient practice   and I never see the contrary round about our part , I mean , of keep all room underground , so that you step down to they . we say that thus we keep they warm , both for cattle and for man , in the time of winter , and cool in the summer - time . this I will not contradict , though have my own opinion ; but it seem to I to be a relic of the time when people in the western country live in cave beneath the ground , and block the mouth with neat - skin . 
 let that question still abide , for man who study ancient time to inform I , if they will ; all I know be , that now we have no blessing for the system . if after all their cold and starve , our weak cattle now should have to stand up to their knee in water , it would be certain death to they ; and we have lose enough already to make we poor for a long time ; not to speak of our kind love for they . and I do assure you , I love some horse , and even some cow for that matter , as if they have be my blood - relation ; know as I do their virtue . and some of these be lose to we ; and I could not bear to think of they . therefore I work hard all night to try and save the rest of they . 




 CHAPTER XLVI 

 SQUIRE FAGGUS make some lucky hit 

 through that season of bitter frost the red deer of the forest , have nothing to feed upon , and no shelter to rest in , have grow accustomed to our rick of corn , and hay , and clover . there we might see a hundred of they almost any morning , come for warmth , and food , and comfort , and scarce willing to move away . and many of they be so tame , that they quietly present themselves at our back door , and stand there with their coat quite stiff , and their flank draw in and panting , and icicle sometimes on their chin , and their great eye fasten wistfully upon any merciful person ; crave for a bit of food , and a drink of water ; I suppose that they have not sense enough to chew the snow and melt it ; at any rate , all the spring be frozen , and river hide out of sight , these poor thing suffer even more from thirst than they do from hunger . 
 but now there be no fear of thirst , and more chance indeed of drown ; for a heavy gale of wind arise , with violent rain from the south - west , which last almost without a pause for three night and two day . at first the rain make no impression on the bulk of snow , but run from every slop surface and freeze on every flat one , through the coldness of the earth ; and so it become impossible for any man to keep his leg without the help of a shodden staff . after a good while , however , the air grow very much warm , this state of thing begin to change , and a bad one to succeed it ; for now the snow come thunder down from roof , and rock , and ivie tree , and flood begin to roar and foam in every trough and gulley . the drift that have be so white and fair , look yellow , and smirch , and muddy , and lose their graceful curve , and mould line , and airiness . but the strange sight of all to I be in the bed of stream , and brook , and especially of the Lynn river . it be worth go mile to behold such a thing , for a man might never have the chance again . 
 vast drift of snow have fill the valley , and pile above the river - course , fifty foot high in many place , and in some as much as a hundred . these have freeze over the top , and glance the rain away from they , and be sustain by rock and tree , span the water mightily . but meanwhile the wax flood , swell from every moorland hollow and from every spout crag , have dash away all icy fetter , and be roll gloriously . under white fantastic arch , and long tunnel freak and fret , and between pellucid pillar jag with nodding architrave , the red impetuous torrent rush , and the brown foam whirl and flash . I be half inclined to jump in and swim through such glorious scenery ; for nothing use to please I more than swim in a flooded river . but I think of the rock , and I think of the cramp , and more than all , of Lorna ; and so , between one thing and another , I let it roll on without I . 
 it be now high time to work very hard ; both to make up for the farm - work lose during the month of frost and snow , and also to be ready for a great and vicious attack from the Doones , who would burn we in our bed at the early opportunity . of farm - work there be little yet for even the most zealous man to begin to lay his hand to ; because when the ground appear through the crust of bubble snow   as at last it do , though not as my Lorna have expect , at the first few drop of rain   it be all so soaked and sodden , and as we call it , ' mucksy , ' that to meddle with it in any way be to do more harm than good . nevertheless , there be yard work , and house work , and tendence of stock , enough to save any man from idleness . 
 as for Lorna , she would come out . there be no keep she in the house . she have take up some peculiar notion that we be do more for she than she have any right to , and that she must earn she live by the hard work of her hand . it be quite in vain to tell she that she be expect to do nothing , and far bad than vain   for it make her cry sadly   if any one assure she that she could do no good at all . she even begin upon mother 's garden before the snow be clean go from it , and sow a beautiful row of pea , every one of which the mouse eat .
