 but though it be very pretty to watch she work for her very life , as if the maintenance of the household hang upon her labour , yet I be grieve for many reason , and so be mother also . in the first place , she be too fair and dainty for this rough , rude work ; and though it make her cheek so bright , it surely must be bad for she to get her little foot so wet . moreover , we could not bear the idea that she should labour for her keep ; and again   which be the bad of all thing   mother 's garden lie expose to a dark deceitful coppice , where a man might lurk and watch all the fair gardener 's doing . it be true that none could get at her thence , while the brook which run between pour so great a torrent . still the distance be but little for a gun to carry , if any one could be brutal enough to point a gun at Lorna . I think that none could be find to do it ; but mother , have more experience , be not so certain of mankind . 
 now in spite of the flood , and the slough be out , and the state of the road most perilous , Squire Faggus come at last , rid his famous strawberry mare . there be a great ado between he and Annie , as you may well suppose , after some four month of parting . and so we leave they alone awhile , to coddle over their rapture . but when they be tired of that , or at least have time enough to do so , mother and I go in to know what news Tom have bring with he . though he do not seem to want we yet , he make himself agreeable ; and so we send Annie to cook the dinner while her sweetheart should tell we everything . 
 Tom Faggus have very good news to tell , and he tell it with such force of expression as make we laugh very heartily . he have take up his purchase from old Sir Roger Bassett of a nice bit of land , to the south of the moor , and in the parish of Molland . when the lawyer know thoroughly who he be , and how he have make his money , they behave uncommonly well to he , and show great sympathy with his pursuit . he put they up to a thing or two ; and they poke he in the rib , and laugh , and say that he be quite a boy ; but of the right sort , none the less . and so they make old Squire Bassett pay the bill for both side ; and all he get for three hundred acre be a hundred and twenty pound ; though Tom have pay five hundred . but lawyer know that this must be so , in spite of all their endeavour ; and the old gentleman , who now expect to find a bill for he to pay , almost think himself a rogue , for get anything out of they . 
 it be true that the land be poor and wild , and the soil exceed shallow ; lie on the slope of rock , and burn up in hot summer . but with we , hot summer be thing know by tradition only   as this great winter may be ; we generally have more moisture , especially in July , than we well know what to do with . I have know a fog for a fortnight at the summer solstice , and farmer talk in church about it when they ought to be pray . but it always contrive to come right in the end , as other visitation do , if we take they as true visit , and receive they kindly . 
 now this farm of Squire Faggus   as he truly now have a right to be call   be of the very fine pasture , when it get good store of rain . and Tom , who have ride the Devonshire road with many a reeking jacket , know right well that he might trust the climate for that matter . the herbage be of the very sweetest , and the short , and the close , have perhaps from ten to eighteen inch of wholesome soil between it and the solid rock . Tom see at once what it be fit for the breeding of fine cattle . 
 be such a hand as he be at make the most of everything , both his own and other people 's   although so free in scatter , when the humour lie upon he   he have actually turn to his own advantage that extraordinary weather which have so impoverished every one around he . for he teach his Winnie   who know his meaning as well as any child could , and obey not only his word of mouth , but every glance he give she   to go forth in the snowy evening when horse be seek everywhere   be they wild or tame   for fodder and for shelter ; and to whinny to the forest pony , mile away from home perhaps , and lead they all with rare appetite and promise of abundance , to her master 's homestead . he shod good Winnie in such a manner that she could not sink in the snow ; and he clothe she over the loin with a sheep - skin dye to her own colour , which the wild horse be never tired of come up and sniff at ; take it for an especial gift , and proof of inspiration . and Winnie never come home at night without at least a score of pony trot shyly after she , toss their head and their tail in turn , and make believe to be very wild , although hard pinch by famine . of course Tom would get they all into his pound in about five minute , for he himself could neigh in a manner which go to the heart of the wild horse . and then he feed they well , and turn they into his great cattle pen , to abide their time for breaking , when the snow and frost should be over . 
 he have get more than three hundred now , in this sagacious manner ; and he say it be the fine sight to see their mode of carry on , how they would snort , and stamp , and fume , and prick their ear , and rush backwards , and lash themselves with their long rough tail , and shake their jagged mane , and scream , and fall upon one another , if a strange man come anigh they . but as for feeding time , Tom say it be well than fifty play to watch they , and the trick they be up to , to cheat their feeder , and one another . I ask he how on earth he have manage to get fodder , in such impassable weather , for such a herd of horse ; but he say that they live upon straw and sawdust ; and he know that I do not believe he , any more than about his star - shaving . and this be just the thing he love to mystify honest people , and be a great deal too know . however , I may judge he harshly , because I myself tell everything . 
 I ask he what he mean to do with all that enormous lot of horse , and why he have not exert his wit to catch the red deer as well . he say that the latter would have be against the law of venery , and might have bring he into trouble , but as for dispose of his stud , it would give he little difficulty . he would break they , when the spring weather come on , and deal with they as they require , and keep the handsomest for breeding . the rest he would despatch to London , where he know plenty of horse - dealer ; and he doubt not that they would fetch he as much as ten pound apiece all round , be now in great demand . I tell he I wish that he might get it ; but as it prove afterwards , he do . 
 then he press we both on another point , the time for his marriage to Annie ; and mother look at I to say when , and I look back at mother . however , know something of the world , and unable to make any further objection , by reason of his prosperity , I say that we must even do as the fashionable people do , and allow the maid herself to settle , when she would leave home and all . and this I speak with a very bad grace , be perhaps of an ancient cast , and over fond of honesty I mean , of course , among low people . 
 but Tom pay little heed to this , know the world a great deal well than ever I could pretend to do ; and be ready to take a thing , upon which he have set his mind , whether it come with a good grace , or whether it come with a bad one . and see that it would be awkward to provoke my anger , he leave the room , before more word , to submit himself to Annie . 
 upon this I go in search of Lorna , to tell she of our cousin 's arrival , and to ask whether she would think fit to see he , or to dine by herself that day ; for she should do exactly as it please she in everything , while remain still our guest . but I rather wish that she might choose not to sit in Tom 's company , though she might be introduce to he . not but what he could behave quite as well as could , and much well , as regard elegance and assurance , only that his honesty have not be as one might desire . but Lorna have some curiosity to know what this famous man be like , and declare that she would by all mean have the pleasure of dining with he , if he do not object to her company on the ground of the Doones ' dishonesty ; moreover , she say that it would seem a most foolish air on her part , and one which would cause the great pain to Annie , who have be so good to she , if she should refuse to sit at table with a man who hold the King 's pardon , and be now a pattern of honesty . 
 against this I have not a word to say ; and could not help acknowledge in my heart that she be right , as well as wise , in her decision . and afterwards I discover that mother would have be much displeased , if she have decide otherwise . 
 accordingly she turn away , with one of her very sweetest smile   whose beauty none can describe   say that she must not meet a man of such fashion and renown , in her common gardening frock ; but must try to look as nice as she could , if only in honour of dear Annie . and truth to tell , when she come to dinner , everything about she be the neat and prettiest that can possibly be imagine . she contrive to match the colour so , to suit one another and her own , and yet with a certain delicate harmony of contrast , and the shape of everything be so nice , so that when she come into the room , with a crown of win modesty upon the consciousness of beauty , I be quite as proud as if the Queen of England enter . 
 my mother could not help remark , though she know that it be not mannerly , how like a princess Lorna look , now she have her good thing on ; but two thing catch Squire Faggus 's eye , after he have make a most gallant bow , and receive a most graceful courtesy ; and he keep his bright bold gaze upon they , first on one , and then on the other , until my darling be hot with blush , and I be ready to knock he down if he have not be our visitor . but here again I should have be wrong , as I be apt to be in those day ; for Tom intend no harm whatever , and his gaze be of pure curiosity ; though Annie herself be vex with it . the two object of his close regard , be first , and most worthily , Lorna 's face , and secondly , the ancient necklace restore to she by Sir Ensor Doone . 
 now wish to save my darling 's comfort , and to keep thing quiet , I shout out that dinner be ready , so that half the parish could hear I ; upon which my mother laugh , and chid I , and despatch her guest before she . and a very good dinner we make , I remember , and a very happy one ; attend to the woman first , as now be the manner of eating ; except among the workman . with they , of course , it be needful that the man   who have his hour fix   should be serve first , and make the utmost of his time for feeding , while the woman may go on , as much as ever they please , afterwards . but with we , who be not bind to time , there be no such reason to be quote ; and the woman be the weak vessel , should be the first to begin to fill . and so we always arrange it . 
 now , though our Annie be a graceful maid , and Lizzie a very learn one , you should have see how differently Lorna manage her dining ; she never take more than about a quarter of a mouthful at a time , and she never appear to be chew that , although she must have do so . indeed , she appear to dine as if it be a matter of no consequence , and as if she could think of other thing more than of her business . all this , and her own manner of eat , I describe to Eliza once , when I want to vex she for something very spiteful that she have say ; and I never succeed so well before , for the girl be quite outrageous , have her own perception of it , which make my observation ten time as bitter to she . and I be not sure but what she cease to like poor Lorna from that day ; and if so , I be quite pay out , as I well deserve , for my bit of satire . 
 for it strike I that of all human dealing , satire be the very low , and most mean and common . it be the equivalent in word of what bullying be in deed ; and no more bespeak a clever man , than the other do a brave one . these two wretched trick exalt a fool in his own low esteem , but never in his neighbour 's ; for the deep common sense of our nature tell that no man of a genial heart , or of any spread of mind , can take pride in either . and though a good man may commit the one fault or the other , now and then , by way of outlet , he be sure to have compunction soon , and to scorn himself more than the sufferer . 
 now when the young maiden be go for we have quite a high dinner of fashion that day , with Betty Muxworthy waiting , and Gwenny Carfax at the gravy and only mother , and Tom , and I remain at the white deal table , with brandy , and schnapp , and hot water jug ; Squire Faggus say quite suddenly , and perhaps on purpose to take we aback , in case of our hiding anything , ' what do you know of the history of that beautiful maiden , good mother ? ' 
 ' not half so much as my son do , ' mother answer , with a soft smile at I ; ' and when John do not choose to tell a thing , wild horse will not pull it out of he . ' 
 ' that be not at all like I , mother , ' I reply rather sadly ; ' you know almost every word about Lorna , quite as well as I do . ' 
 ' almost every word , I believe , John ; for you never tell a falsehood . but the few unknown may be of all the most important to I . ' 
 to this I make no answer , for fear of go beyond the truth , or else of make mischief . not that I have , or wish to have , any mystery with mother ; neither be there in pure truth , any mystery in the matter ; to the utmost of my knowledge . and the only thing that I have keep back , solely for mother 's comfort , be the death of poor Lord Alan Brandir   if indeed he be dead   and the connection of Marwood de Whichehalse with the dealing of the Doones , and the threat of Carver Doone against my own prosperity ; and , may be , one or two little thing harrow more than edify . 
 ' come , come , ' say Master Faggus , smile very pleasantly , ' you two understand each other , if any two on earth do . ah , if I have only have a mother , how different I might have be ! ' and with that he sigh , in the tone which always overcome mother upon that subject , and have something to do with his get Annie ; and then he produce his pretty box , full of roll tobacco , and offer I one , as I now have join the goodly company of smoker . so I take it , and watch what he do with his own , lest I might go wrong about mine . 
 but when our cylinder be both light , and I enjoy mine wonderfully , and astonishing mother by my skill , Tom Faggus tell we that he be sure he have see my Lorna 's face before , many and many year ago , when she be quite a little child , but he could not remember where it be , or anything more about it at present ; though he would try to do so afterwards . he could not be mistaken , he say , for he have notice her eye especially ; and have never see such eye before , neither again , until this day . I ask he if he have ever venture into the Doone - valley ; but he shake his head , and reply that he value his life a deal too much for that . then we put it to he , whether anything might assist his memory ; but he say that he know not of aught to do so , unless it be another glass of schnapp . 
 this be provide , he grow very wise , and tell we clearly and candidly that we be both very foolish . for he say that we be keep Lorna , at the risk not only of our stock , and the house above our head , but also of our precious life ; and after all be she worth it , although so very beautiful ? upon which I tell he , with indignation , that her beauty be the least part of her goodness , and that I would thank he for his opinion when I have request it . 
 ' Bravo , our John Ridd ! ' he answer ; ' fool will be fool till the end of the chapter ; and I might be as big a one , if I be in thy shoe , John . nevertheless , in the name of God , do not   let that helpless child go about with a thing worth half the county on she . ' 
 ' she be worth all the county herself , ' say I , ' and all England put together ; but she have nothing worth half a rick of hay upon she ; for the ring I give her cost only , ' and here I stop , for mother be look , and I never would tell she how much it have cost I ; though she have try fifty time to find out . 
 ' Tush , the ring ! ' Tom Faggus cry , with a contempt that move I : ' I would never have stop a man for that . but the necklace , you great oaf , the necklace be worth all your farm put together , and your Uncle Ben 's fortune to the back of it ; ay , and all the town of Dulverton . ' 
 ' what , ' say I , ' that common glass thing , which she have have from her childhood ! ' 
 ' Glass indeed ! they be the fine brilliant ever I set eye on ; and I have handle a good many . ' 
 ' surely , ' cry mother , now flush as red as Tom 's own cheek with excitement , ' you must be wrong , or the young mistress would herself have know it . ' 
 I be greatly pleased with my mother , for call Lorna ' the young mistress ' ; it be not do for the sake of her diamond , whether they be glass or not ; but because she feel as I have do , that Tom Faggus , a man of no birth whatever , be speak beyond his mark , in call a lady like Lorna a helpless child ; as well as in his general tone , which display no deference . he might have be use to the quality , in the way of stop their coach , or roystere at hotel with they ; but he never have meet a high lady before , in equality , and upon virtue ; and we both feel that he ought to have know it , and to have thank we for the opportunity , in a word , to have behave a great deal more humbly than he have even try to do . 
 ' trust I , ' answer Tom , in his lofty manner , which Annie say be ' so noble , ' but which seem to I rather flashy , ' trust I , good mother , and simple John , for know brilliant , when I see they . I would have stop an eight - horse coach , with four carabine out - rider , for such a booty as that . but alas , those day be over ; those be day worth live in . ah , I never shall know the like again . how fine it be by moonlight ! ' 
 ' Master Faggus , ' begin my mother , with a manner of some dignity , such as she could sometimes use , by right of her integrity , and thorough kindness to every one , ' this be not the tone in which you have hitherto speak to I about your former pursuit and life , I fear that the spirit ' but here she stop , because the spirit be her own , and Tom be our visitor , ' what I mean , Master Faggus , be this : you have win my daughter 's heart somehow ; and you win my consent to the matter through your honest sorrow , and manly undertaking to lead a different life , and touch no property but your own . Annie be my eld daughter , and the child of a most upright man . I love she good of all on earth , next to my boy John here ' here mother give I a mighty squeeze , to be sure that she would have I at least ' and I will not risk my Annie 's life with a man who yearn for the highway . ' 
 have make this very long speech   for she , mother come home upon my shoulder , and weep so that   but for heed she   I would have take Tom by the nose , and throw he , and Winnie after he , over our farm - yard gate . for I be violent when rouse ; and freely hereby acknowledge it ; though even my enemy will own that it take a great deal to rouse I . but I do consider the grief and tear   when justly cause   of my dear friend , to be a great deal to rouse I . 




 CHAPTER XLVII 

 JEREMY in DANGER 

 nothing very long abide , as the great of all writer   in whose extent I be for ever lose in raptured wonder , and yet for ever quite at home , as if his heart be mine , although his brain so different , in a word as Mr. William Shakespeare , in every one of his work insist , with a humour melancholy . and if my journey to London lead to nothing else of advancement , it take I a hundred year in front of what I might else have be , by the most simple accident . 
 two woman be scold one another across the road , very violently , both from upstair window ; and I in my hurry for quiet life , and not know what might come down upon I , quicken my step for the near corner . but suddenly something fall on my head ; and at first I be afraid to look , especially as it weigh heavily . but hear no breakage of ware , and only the other scold laugh heartily , I turn I about and espy a book , which one have cast at the other , hop to break her window . so I take the book , and tender it at the door of the house from which it have fall ; but the watchman come along just then , and the man at the door declare that it never come from their house , and beg I to say no more . this I promise readily , never wish to make mischief ; and I say , ' good sir , now take the book ; I will go on to my business . ' but he answer that he would do no such thing ; for the book alone , be hurl so hard , would convict his people of a lewd assault ; and he beg I , if I would do a good turn , to put the book under my coat and go . and so I do : in part at least . for I do not put the book under my coat , but go along with it openly , look for any to challenge it . now this book , so acquire , have be not only the joy of my young day , and main delight of my manhood , but also the comfort , and even the hope , of my now decline year . in a word , it be next to my Bible to I , and write in equal English ; and if you espy any goodness whatever in my own loose style of writing , you must not thank I , John Ridd , for it , but the writer who hold the champion 's belt in wit , as I once do in wrestling . 
 now , as nothing very long abide , it can not be expect that a woman 's anger should last very long , if she be at all of the proper sort . and my mother , be one of the very good , could not long retain she wrath against the Squire Faggus especially when she come to reflect , upon Annie 's suggestion , how natural , and one might say , how inevitable it be that a young man fond of adventure and change and win good profit by jeopardy , should not settle down without some regret to a fix abode and a life of sameness , however safe and respectable . and even as Annie put the case , Tom deserve the great credit for vanquish so nobly these yearning of his nature ; and it seem very hard to upbraid he , consider how good his motif be ; neither could Annie understand how mother could reconcile it with her knowledge of the Bible , and the one sheep that be lose , and the hundredth piece of silver , and the man that go down to Jericho . 
 whether Annie 's logic be good and sound , I be sure I can not tell ; but it seem to I that she ought to have let the Jericho traveller alone , inasmuch as he rather fall among Tom Fagusses , than resemble they . however , her reasoning be too much for mother to hold out against ; and Tom be replace , and more than that , be regard now as an injure man . but how my mother contrive to know , that because she have be too hard upon Tom , he must be right about the necklace , be a point which I never could clearly perceive , though no doubt she could explain it . 
 to prove herself right in the conclusion , she go herself to fetch Lorna , that the trinket might be examine , before the day grow dark . my darling come in , with a very quick glance and smile at my cigarro   for I be have the third by this time , to keep thing in amity ; and I wave it towards she , as much as to say , ' you see that I can do it . ' and then mother lead she up to the light , for Tom to examine her necklace . 
 on the shapely curve of her neck it hang , like dewdrop upon a white hyacinth ; and I be vex that Tom should have the chance to see it there . but even if she have read my thought , or outrun they with her own , Lorna turn away , and softly take the jewel from the place which so much adorn they . and as she turn away , they sparkle through the rich dark wave of hair . then she lay the glitter circlet in my mother 's hand ; and Tom Faggus take it eagerly , and bear it to the window . 
 ' do not   you go out of sight , ' I say ; ' you can not resist such thing as those , if they be what you think they . ' 
 ' Jack , I shall have to trounce thee yet . I be now a man of honour , and entitle to the duello . what will you take for it , Mistress Lorna ? at a hazard , say now . ' 
 ' I be not accustomed to sell thing , sir , ' reply Lorna , who do not like he much , else she would have answer sportively , ' what be it worth , in your opinion ? ' 
 ' do you think it be worth five pound , now ? ' 
 ' oh , no ! I never have so much money as that in all my life . it be very bright , and very pretty ; but it can not be worth five pound , I be sure . ' 
 ' what a chance for a bargain ! oh , if it be not for Annie , I could make my fortune . ' 
 ' but , sir , I would not sell it to you , not for twenty time five pound . my grandfather be so kind about it ; and I think it belong to my mother . ' 
 ' there be twenty - five rise diamond in it , and twenty - five large brilliant that can not be match in London . how say you , Mistress Lorna , to a hundred thousand pound ? ' 
 my darling 's eye so flash at this , bright than any diamond , that I say to myself , ' well , all have fault ; and now I have find out Lorna 's she be fond of money ! ' and then I sigh rather heavily ; for of all fault this seem to I one of the bad in a woman . but even before my sigh be finish , I have cause to condemn myself . for Lorna take the necklace very quietly from the hand of Squire Faggus , who have not half do with admire it , and she go up to my mother with the sweetest smile I ever see . 
 ' dear kind mother , I be so glad , ' she say in a whisper , coax mother out of sight of all but I ; ' now you will have it , will not   you , dear ? and I shall be so happy ; for a thousandth part of your kindness to I no jewel in the world can match . ' 
 I can not lay before you the grace with which she do it , all the air of seek favour , rather than confer it , and the high - breed fear of give offence , which be of all fear the noble . Mother know not what to say . of course she would never dream of take such a gift as that ; and yet she see how sadly Lorna would be disappoint . therefore , mother do , from habit , what she almost always do , she call I to help she . but know that my eye be full for anything noble move I so , quite as rashly as thing pitiful I pretend not to hear my mother , but to see a wild cat in the dairy . 
 therefore I can not tell what mother say in reply to Lorna ; for when I come back , quite eager to let my love know how I worship she , and how deeply I be ashamed of myself , for meanly wrong she in my heart , behold Tom Faggus have get again the necklace which have such charm for he , and be deliver all around   but especially to Annie , who be wonder at his learning   a dissertation on precious stone , and his sentiment about those in his hand . he say that the work be very ancient , but undoubtedly very good ; the cutting of every line be true , and every angle be in its place . and this he say , make all the difference in the lustre of the stone , and therefore in its value . for if the facet be ill - match , and the point of light so ever little out of perfect harmony , all the lustre of the jewel would be loose and wavering , and the central fire dull ; instead of answer , as it should , to all possibility of gaze , and overpower any eye intent on its deep mystery . we laugh at the squire 's dissertation ; for how should he know all these thing , be nothing well , and indeed much bad than a mere Northmolton blacksmith ? he take our laughter with much good nature ; have Annie to squeeze his hand and convey her grief at our ignorance : but he say that of one thing he be quite certain , and therein I believe he . to wit , that a trinket of this kind never could have belong to any ignoble family , but to one of the very high and most wealthy in England . and look at Lorna , I feel that she must have come from a high source than the very good of diamond . 
 Tom Faggus say that the necklace be make , he would answer for it , in Amsterdam , two or three hundred year ago , long before London jeweller have begin to meddle with diamond ; and on the gold clasp he find some letter , do in some invert way , the meaning of which be beyond he ; also a bearing of some kind , which he believe be a mountain - cat . and thereupon he declare that now he have earn another glass of schnapp , and would mistress Lorna mix it for he ? 
 I be amazed at his impudence ; and Annie , who think this her business , do not look well please ; and I hop that Lorna would tell he at once to go and do it for himself . but instead of that she rise to do it with a soft humility , which go direct to the heart of Tom ; and he leap up with a curse at himself , and take the hot water from she , and would not allow she to do anything except to put the sugar in ; and then he bow to she grandly . I know what Lorna be think of ; she be think all the time that her necklace have be take by the Doones with violence upon some great robbery ; and that Squire Faggus know it , though he would not show his knowledge ; and that this be perhaps the reason why mother have refuse it so . 
 we say no more about the necklace for a long time afterwards ; neither do my darling wear it , now that she know its value , but do not know its history . she come to I the very next day , try to look cheerful , and beg I if I love she   never mind how little   to take charge of it again , as I once have do before , and not even to let she know in what place I store it . I tell she that this last request I could not comply with ; for have be round her neck so often , it be now a sacred thing , more than a million pound could be . therefore it should dwell for the present in the neighbourhood of my heart ; and so could not be far from she . at this she smile her own sweet smile , and touch my forehead with her lip , and wish that she could only learn how to deserve such love as mine . 
 Tom Faggus take his good departure , which be a kind farewell to I , on the very day I be speak of , the day after his arrival . Tom be a thoroughly upright man , accord to his own standard ; and you might rely upon he always , up to a certain point I mean , to be there or thereabout . but sometimes thing be too many for Tom , especially with ardent spirit , and then he judge , perhaps too much , with only himself for the jury . at any rate , I would trust he fully , for candour and for honesty , in almost every case in which he himself could have no interest . and so we get on very well together ; and he think I a fool ; and I try my good not to think anything bad of he . 
 Scarcely be Tom clean out of sight , and Annie 's tear not dry yet   for she always make a point of cry upon his departure , when in come Master Jeremy Stickles , splash with mud from head to foot , and not in the very good of humour , though happy to get back again . 
 ' curse those fellow ! ' he cry , with a stamp which send the water hiss from his boot upon the ember ; ' a pretty plight you may call this , for his Majesty 's Commissioner to return to his headquarters in ! Annie , my dear , ' for he be always very affable with Annie , ' will you help I off with my overall , and then turn your pretty hand to the gridiron ? not a blessed morsel have I touch for more than twenty - four hour . ' 
 ' surely then you must be quite starve , sir , ' my sister reply with the great zeal ; for she do love a man with an appetite ; ' how glad I be that the fire be clear ! ' but Lizzie , who happen to be there , say with her peculiar smile , 
 ' Master Stickles must be use to it ; for he never come back without tell we that . ' 
 ' hush ! ' cry Annie , quite shocked with she ; ' how would you like to be use to it ? now , Betty , be quick with the thing for I . pork , or mutton , or deer 's meat , sir ? we have some cure since the autumn . ' 
 ' oh , deer 's meat , by all mean , ' Jeremy Stickles answer ; ' I have taste none since I leave you , though dream of it often . well , this be well than be chase over the moor for one 's life , John . all the way from Landacre Bridge , I have ride a race for my precious life , at the peril of my limb and neck . three great Doones gallop after I , and a good job for I that they be so big , or they must have overtake I . just go and see to my horse , John , that be an excellent lad . he deserve a good turn this day , from I ; and I will render it to he . ' 
 however he leave I to do it , while he make himself comfortable : and in truth the horse require care ; he be blow so that he could hardly stand , and plaster with mud , and steam so that the stable be quite full with it . by the time I have put the poor fellow to right , his master have finish dinner , and be in a more pleasant humour , have even offer to kiss Annie , out of pure gratitude , as he say ; but Annie answer with spirit that gratitude must not be show by increase the obligation . Jeremy make reply to this that his only way to be grateful then be to tell we his story : and so he do , at great length than I can here repeat it ; for it do not bear particularly upon Lorna 's fortune . 
 it appear that as he be rid towards we from the town of Southmolton in Devonshire , he find the road very soft and heavy , and the flood out in all direction ; but meet with no other difficulty until he come to Landacre Bridge . he have only a single trooper with he , a man not of the militia but of the King 's army , whom Jeremy have bring from Exeter . as these two descend towards the bridge they observe that both the Kensford water and the River Barle be pour down in mighty flood from the melting of the snow . so great indeed be the torrent , after they unite , that only the parapet of the bridge could be see above the water , the road across either bank be cover and very deep on the hither side . the trooper do not like the look of it , and propose to ride back again , and round by way of Simonsbath , where the stream be small . but Stickles would not have it so , and dash into the river , swam his horse for the bridge , and gain it with some little trouble ; and there he find the water not more than up to his horse 's knee perhaps . on the crown of the bridge he turn his horse to watch the trooper 's passage , and to help he with direction ; when suddenly he see he fall headlong into the torrent , and hear the report of a gun from behind , and feel a shock to his own body , such as lift he out of the saddle . turn round he behold three man , rise up from behind the hedge on one side of his onward road , two of they ready to load again , and one with his gun unfire , wait to get good aim at he . then Jeremy do a gallant thing , for which I doubt whether I should have have the presence of mind in danger . he see that to swim his horse back again would be almost certain death ; as afford such a target , where even a wound must be fatal . therefore he strike the spur into the nag , and ride through the water straight at the man who be point the long gun at he . if the horse have be carry off his leg , there must have be an end of Jeremy ; for the other man be get ready to have another shot at he . but luckily the horse gallop right on without any need for swimming , be himself excited , no doubt , by all he have see and hear of it . and Jeremy lay almost flat on his neck , so as to give little space for good aim , with the mane toss wildly in front of he . now if that young fellow with the gun have his brain as ready as his flint be , he would have shoot the horse at once , and then have Stickles at his mercy ; but instead of that he let fly at the man , and miss he altogether , be scar perhaps by the pistol which Jeremy show he the mouth of . and gallop by at full speed , Master Stickles try to leave his mark behind he , for he change the aim of his pistol to the big man , who be load his gun and curse like ten cannon . but the pistol miss fire , no doubt from the flood which have gurgle in over the holster ; and Jeremy see three horse tether at a gate just up the hill , know that he have not yet escape , but have more of danger behind he . he try his other great pistol at one of the horse tether there , so as to lessen   if possible   the number of his pursuer . but the powder again fail he ; and he durst not stop to cut the bridle , bear the man come up the hill . so he even make the most of his start , thank God that his weight be light , compare at least to what theirs be . 
 and another thing he have notice which give he some hope of escape , to wit that the horse of the Doones , although very handsome animal , be suffer still from the bitter effect of the late long frost , and the scarcity of fodder . ' if they do not catch I up , or shoot I , in the course of the first two mile , I may see my home again ' ; this be what he say to himself as he turn to mark what they be about , from the brow of the steep hill . he see the flood valley shin with the breadth of water , and the trooper 's horse on the other side , shake his drench flank and neighing ; and half - way down the hill he see the three Doones mount hastily . and then he know that his only chance lie in the stoutness of his steed . 
 the horse be in pretty good condition ; and the rider know he thoroughly , and how to make the most of he ; and though they have travel some mile that day through very heavy ground , the bath in the river have wash the mud off , and be some refreshment . therefore Stickles encourage his nag , and put he into a good hard gallop , head away towards Withycombe . at first he have think of turn to the right , and make off for Withypool , a mile or so down the valley ; but his good sense tell he that no one there would dare to protect he against the Doones , so he resolve to go on his way ; yet fast than he have intend . 
 the three villain come after he , with all the speed they could muster , make sure from the badness of the road that he must stick fast ere long , and so be at their mercy . and this be Jeremy 's chief fear , for the ground be soft and thoroughly rotten , after so much frost and snow , the poor horse have terrible work of it , with no time to pick the way ; and even more good luck than skill be need to keep he from founder . how Jeremy pray for an Exmoor fog   such as he have often swear at , that he might turn aside and lurk , while his pursuer go past he ! but no fog come , nor even a storm to damp the priming of their gun ; neither be wood or coppice nigh , nor any place to hide in ; only hill , and moor , and valley ; with fly shadow over they , and great bank of snow in the corner . at one time poor Stickles be quite in despair ; for after leap a little brook which cross the track at Newland , be stick fast in a ' dancing bog , ' as we call they upon Exmoor . the horse have break through the crust of moss and sedge and marishwee , and could do nothing but wallow and sink , with the black water spirt over he . and Jeremy , struggle with all his might , see the three villain now top the crest , less than a furlong behind he ; and hear they shout in their savage delight . with the calmness of despair , he yet resolve to have one more try for it ; and scramble over the horse 's head , gain firm land , and tug at the bridle . the poor nag reply with all his power to the call upon his courage , and rear his forefeet out of the slough , and with strain eyeball gaze at he . ' now , ' say Jeremy , ' now , my fine fellow ! ' lift he with the bridle , and the brave beast gather the roll of his loin , and spring from his quagmire haunch . one more spring , and he be on earth again , instead of be under it ; and Jeremy leap on his back , and stoop , for he know that they would fire . two bullet whistle over he , as the horse , mad with fright , dash forward ; and in five minute more he have come to the Exe , and the pursuer have fall behind he . the Exe , though a much small stream than the Barle , now run in a foaming torrent , unbridged , and too wide for leap . but Jeremy 's horse take the water well ; and both he and his rider be lightened , as well as comfort by it . and as they pass towards Lucott hill , and strike upon the fount of Lynn , the horse of the three pursuer begin to tire under they . then Jeremy Stickles know that if he could only escape the slough , he be safe for the present ; and so he stand up in his stirrup , and give they a loud halloo , as if they have be so many fox . 
 their only answer be to fire the remain charge at he ; but the distance be too great for any aim from horseback ; and the drop bullet idly plough the sod upon one side of he . he acknowledge it with a wave of his hat , and lay one thumb to his nose , in the manner fashionable in London for expression of contempt . however , they follow he yet far ; hop to make he pay out dearly , if he should only miss the track , or fall upon morass . but the neighbourhood of our Lynn stream be not so very boggy ; and the King 's messenger now know his way as well as any of his pursuer do ; and so he arrive at Plover 's barrow , thankful , and in rare appetite . 
 ' but be the poor soldier drown ? ' ask Annie ; ' and you never go to look for he ! oh , how very dreadful ! ' 
 ' Shot , or drown ; I know not which . thank God it be only a trooper . but they shall pay for it , as dearly as if it have be a captain . ' 
 ' and how be it you be strike by a bullet , and only shake in your saddle ? have you a coat of mail on , or of milanese chain - armour ? now , Master Stickles , have you ? ' 
 ' no , Mistress Lizzie ; we do not wear thing of that kind nowadays . you be apt , I perceive , at romance . but I happen to have a little flat bottle of the good stoneware sling beneath my saddle - cloak , and fill with the very good eau de vie , from the George Hotel , at Southmolton . the brand of it now be upon my back . oh , the murderous scoundrel , what a brave spirit they have spill ! ' 
 ' you have well set to and thank God , ' say I , ' that they have not spill a braver one . ' 




 CHAPTER XLVIII 

 every MAN must DEFEND himself 

 it be only right in Jeremy Stickles , and of the simple common sense , that he would not tell , before our girl , what the result of his journey be . but he lead I aside in the course of the evening , and tell I all about it ; say that I know , as well as he do , that it be not woman 's business . this I take , as it be mean , for a gentle caution that Lorna   whom he have not see as yet   must not be inform of any of his doing . Herein I quite agree with he ; not only for his furtherance , but because I always think that woman , of whatever mind , be good when least they meddle with the thing that appertain to man . 
 Master Stickles complain that the weather have be against he bitterly , close all the road around he ; even as it have do with we . it have take he eight day , he say , to get from Exeter to Plymouth ; whither he find that most of the troop have be draft off from Exeter . when all be tell , there be but a battalion of one of the King 's horse regiment , and two company of foot soldier ; and their commander have order , later than the date of Jeremy 's commission , on no account to quit the southern coast , and march inland . therefore , although they would gladly have come for a brush with the celebrated Doones , it be more than they durst attempt , in the face of their instruction . however , they spar he a single trooper , as a companion of the road , and to prove to the justice of the county , and the lord lieutenant , that he have their approval . 
 to these authority Master Stickles now be force to address himself , although he would rather have have one trooper than a score from the very well train band . for these train band have afford very good soldier , in the time of the civil war , and for some year afterwards ; but now their discipline be go ; and the young generation have see no real fighting . each would have his own opinion , and would want to argue it ; and if he be not allow , he go about his duty in such a temper as to prove that his own way be the good . 
 neither be this the bad of it ; for Jeremy make no doubt but what   if he could only get the militia to turn out in force   he might manage , with the help of his own man , to force the stronghold of the enemy ; but the truth be that the officer , know how hard it would be to collect their man at that time of the year , and in that state of the weather , begin with one accord to make every possible excuse . and especially they press this point , that Bagworthy be not in their county ; the Devonshire people affirm vehemently that it lie in the shire of Somerset , and the Somersetshire folk averring , even with imprecation , that it lie in Devonshire . now I believe the truth to be that the boundary of the two county , as well as of Oare and Brendon parish , be define by the Bagworthy river ; so that the disputant on both side be both right and wrong . 
 upon this , Master Stickles suggest , and as I think very sensibly , that the two county should unite , and equally contribute to the extirpation of this pest , which sham and injure they both alike . but hence arise another difficulty ; for the man of Devon say they would march when Somerset have take the field ; and the son of Somerset reply that indeed they be quite ready , but what be their cousin of Devonshire do ? and so it come to pass that the King 's Commissioner return without any army whatever ; but with promise of two hundred man when the road should be more passable . and meanwhile , what be we to do , abandon as we be to the mercy of the Doones , with only our own hand to help we ? and herein I grieve at my own folly , in have let Tom Faggus go , whose wit and courage would have be worth at least half a dozen man to we . upon this matter I hold long council with my good friend Stickles ; tell he all about Lorna 's presence , and what I know of her history . he agree with I that we could not hope to escape an attack from the outlaws , and the more especially now that they know himself to be return to we . also he praise I for my forethought in have thresh out all our corn , and hide the produce in such a manner that they be not likely to find it . furthermore , he recommend that all the entrance to the house should at once be strengthen , and a watch must be maintain at night ; and he think it wiser that I should go   late as it be   to Lynmouth , if a horse could pass the valley , and fetch every one of his mount trooper , who might now be quarter there . also if any man of courage , though capable only of handle a pitchfork , could be find in the neighbourhood , I be to try to summon they . but our district be so thinly people , that I have little faith in this ; however my errand be give I , and I set forth upon it ; for John Fry be afraid of the water . 
 know how fiercely the flood be out , I resolve to travel the high road , by Cosgate and through Countisbury ; therefore I swam my horse through the Lynn , at the ford below our house   where sometimes you may step across , and thence gallop up and along the hill . I could see all the inland valley ribbon'd with broad water ; and in every winding crook , the bank of snow that feed they ; while on my right the turbid sea be flake with April shower . but when I descend the hill towards Lynmouth , I fear that my journey be all in vain . 
 for the East Lynn   which be our river   be ramp and roar frightfully , lash whole trunk of tree on the rock , and rend they , and grind they . and into it rush , from the opposite side , a torrent even madder ; upset what it come to aid ; shatter wave with boiling billow , and scatter wrath with fury . it be certain death to attempt the passage : and the little wooden footbridge have be carry away long ago . and the man I be seek must be , of course , on the other side of this deluge , for on my side there be not a single house . 
 I follow the bank of the flood to the beach , some two or three hundred yard below ; and there have the luck to see will watcombe on the opposite side , caulk an old boat . though I could not make he hear a word , from the deafen roar of the torrent , I get he to understand at last that I want to cross over . upon this he fetch another man , and the two of they launch a boat ; and paddle well out to sea , fetched round the mouth of the frantic river . the other man prove to be Stickles 's chief mate ; and so he go back and fetch his comrade , bring their weapon , but leave their horse behind . as it happen there be but four of they ; however , to have even these be a help ; and I start again at full speed for my home ; for the man must follow afoot , and cross our river high up on the moorland . 
 this take they a long way round , and the track be rather bad to find , and the sky already darken ; so that I arrive at Plover 's barrow more than two hour before they . but they have do a sagacious thing , which be well worth the delay ; for by hoist their flag upon the hill , they fetch the two watchman from the Foreland , and add they to their number . 
 it be lucky that I come home so soon ; for I find the house in a great commotion , and all the woman tremble . when I ask what the matter be , Lorna , who seem the most self - possess , answer that it be all her fault , for she alone have frighten they . and this in the follow manner . she have steal out to the garden towards dusk , to watch some favourite hyacinth just push up , like a baby 's tooth , and just attract the fatal notice of a great house - snail at night - time . Lorna at last have discover the glutton , and be bear he off in triumph to the tribunal of the duck , when she descry two glitter eye glare at she steadfastly , from the elder - bush beyond the stream . the elder be smooth its wrinkle leaf , be at least two month behind time ; and among they this calm cruel face appear ; and she know it be the face of Carver Doone . 
 the maiden , although so use to terror   as she tell I once before , lose all presence of mind hereat , and could neither shriek nor fly , but only gaze , as if bewitch . then Carver Doone , with his deadly smile , gloat upon her horror , lift his long gun , and point full at Lorna 's heart . in vain she strove to turn away ; fright have stricken her stiff as stone . with the inborn love of life , she try to cover the vital part wherein the wing death must lodge for she know Carver 's certain aim but her hand hang numb , and heavy ; in nothing but her eye be life . 
 with no sign of pity in his face , no quiver of relent , but a well - pleased grin at all the charming palsy of his victim , Carver Doone lower , inch by inch , the muzzle of his gun . when it point to the ground , between her delicate arched instep , he pull the trigger , and the bullet fling the mould all over she . it be a refinement of bullying , for which I swear to God that night , upon my knee , in secret , that I would smite down Carver Doone or else he should smite I down . Base beast ! what large humanity , or what dream of divinity , could make a man put up with this ? 
 my darling   the lovely , and most harmless , in the world of maidens , fall away on a bank of grass , and weep at her own cowardice ; and tremble , and wonder where I be ; and what I would think of this . good God ! what could I think of it ? she over - rat my slow nature , to admit the question . 
 while she lean there , quite unable yet to save herself , Carver come to the brink of the flood , which alone be between they ; and then he stroke his jet - black beard , and wait for Lorna to begin . very likely , he think that she would thank he for his kindness to she . but she be now recover the power of her nimble limb ; and ready to be off like hope , and wonder at her own cowardice . 
 ' I have spar you this time , ' he say , in his deep calm voice , ' only because it suit my plan ; and I never yield to temper . but unless you come back to - morrow , pure , and with all you take away , and teach I to destroy that fool , who have destroy himself for you , your death be here , your death be here , where it have long be wait . ' 
 although his gun be empty , he strike the breech of it with his finger ; and then he turn away , not deign even once to look back again ; and Lorna see his giant figure stride across the meadow - land , as if the ridd be nobody , and he the proper owner . both mother and I be greatly hurt at hearing of this insolence : for we have own that meadow , from the time of the great Alfred ; and even when that good king lie in the Isle of Athelney , he have a Ridd along with he . 
 now I speak to Lorna gently , see how much she have be try ; and I praise she for her courage , in not have run away , when she be so unable ; and my darling be pleased with this , and smile upon I for say it ; though she know right well that , in this matter , my judgment be not impartial . but you may take this as a general rule , that a woman like praise from the man whom she love , and can not stop always to balance it . 
 now expect a sharp attack that night when Jeremy Stickles the more expect , after the word of Carver , which seem to be mean to mislead we we prepare a great quantity of knuckle of pork , and a ham in full cut , and a fillet of hung mutton . for we would almost surrender rather than keep our garrison hungry . and all our man be exceedingly brave ; and count their round of the house in half - pint . 
 before the maiden go to bed , Lorna make a remark which seem to I a very clever one , and then I wonder how on earth it have never occur to I before . but first she have do a thing which I could not in the least approve of : for she have go up to my mother , and throw herself into her arm , and beg to be allow to return to Glen Doone . 
 ' my child , be you unhappy here ? ' mother ask she , very gently , for she have begin to regard she now as a daughter of her own . 
 ' oh , no ! too happy , by far too happy , Mrs. Ridd . I never know rest or peace before , or meet with real kindness . but I can not be so ungrateful , I can not be so wicked , as to bring you all into deadly peril , for my sake alone . let I go : you must not pay this great price for my happiness . ' 
 ' dear child , we be pay no price at all , ' reply my mother , embrace she ; ' we be not threaten for your sake only . ask John , he will tell you . he know every bit about politic , and this be a political matter . ' 
 dear mother be rather proud in her heart , as well as terribly frightened , at the importance now accrue to Plover 's Barrows farm ; and she often declare that it would be as famous in history as the Rye House , or the meal - tub , or even the great black box , in which she be a firm believer : and even my knowledge of politic could not move she upon that matter . ' such thing have happen before , ' she would say , shake her head with its wisdom , ' and why might they not happen again ? woman would be woman , and man would be man , to the end of the chapter ; and if she have be in Lucy Water 's place , she would keep it quiet , as she have do ' ; and then she would look round , for fear , lest either of her daughter have hear she ; ' but now , can you give I any reason , why it may not have be so ? you be so fearfully positive , John : just as man always be . ' ' no , ' I use to say ; ' I can give you no reason , why it may not have be so , mother . but the question be , if it be so , or not ; rather than what it might have be . and , I think , it be pretty good proof against it , that what nine man of every ten in England would only too gladly believe , if true , be nevertheless keep dark from they . ' ' there you be again , John , ' mother would reply , ' all about man , and not a single word about woman . if you have any argument at all , you would own that marriage be a question upon which woman be the good judge . ' ' oh ! ' I would groan in my spirit , and go ; leave my dearest mother quite sure , that now at last she must have convince I . but if mother have know that Jeremy Stickles be work against the black box , and its issue , I doubt whether he would have fare so well , even though he be a visitor . however , she know that something be do and something of importance ; and she trust in God for the rest of it . only she use te tell I , very seriously , of an evening , ' the very least they can give you , dear John , be a coat of arm . be sure you take nothing less , dear ; and the farm can well support it . ' 
 but lo ! I have leave Lorna ever so long , anxious to consult I upon political matter . she come to I , and her eye alone ask a hundred question , which I rather have answer upon her lip than trouble her pretty ear with they . therefore I tell she nothing at all , save that the attack   if any should be   would not be make on her account ; and that if she should hear , by any chance , a trifle of a noise in the night , she be to wrap the clothe around she , and shut her beautiful eye again . on no account , whatever she do , be she to go to the window . she like my expression about her eye , and promise to do the very good she could and then she creep so very close , that I need must have she close ; and with her head on my breast she ask , 
 ' can not   you keep out of this fight , John ? ' 
 ' my own one , ' I answer , gaze through the long black lash , at the depth of radiant love ; ' I believe there will be nothing : but what there be I must see out . ' 
 ' shall I tell you what I think , John ? it be only a fancy of mine , and perhaps it be not worth tell . ' 
 ' let we have it , dear , by all mean . you know so much about their way . ' 
 ' what I believe be this , John . you know how high the river be , high than ever they be before , and twice as high , you have tell I . I believe that Glen Doone be flood , and all the house under water . ' 
 ' you little witch , ' I answer ; ' what a fool I must be not to think of it ! of course it be : it must be . the torrent from all the Bagworthy forest , and all the valley above it , and the great drift in the glen itself , never could have outlet down my famous waterslide . the valley must be under water twenty foot at least . well , if ever there be a fool , I be he , for not have thought of it . ' 
 ' I remember once before , ' say Lorna , reckon on her finger , ' when there be heavy rain , all through the autumn and winter , five or it may be six year ago , the river come down with such a rush that the water be two foot deep in our room , and we all have to camp by the cliff - edge . but you think that the flood be high now , I believe I hear you say , John . ' 
 ' I do not   think about it , my treasure , ' I answer ; ' you may trust I for understand flood , after our work at Tiverton . and I know that the deluge in all our valley be such that no live man can remember , neither will ever behold again . consider three month of snow , snow , snow , and a fortnight of rain on the top of it , and all to be drain in a few day away ! and great barricade of ice still in the river block they up , and ponde they . you may take my word for it , Mistress Lorna , that your pretty bower be six foot deep . ' 
 ' well , my bower have serve its time ' , say Lorna , blush as she remember all that have happen there ; ' and my bower now be here , John . but I be so sorry to think of all the poor woman flood out of their house and shelter in the snowdrift . however , there be one good of it : they can not send many man against we , with all this trouble upon they . ' 
 ' you be right , ' I reply ; ' how clever you be ! and that be why there be only three to cut off Master Stickles . and now we shall beat they , I make no doubt , even if they come at all . and I defy they to fire the house : the thatch be too wet for burn . ' 
 we send all the woman to bed quite early , except Gwenny Carfax and our old Betty . these two we allow to stay up , because they might be useful to we , if they could keep from quarrel . for my part , I have little fear , after what Lorna have tell I , as to the result of the combat . it be not likely that the Doones could bring more than eight or ten man against we , while their home be in such danger : and to meet these we have eight good man , include Jeremy , and myself , all well armed and resolute , besides our three farm - servant , and the parish - clerk , and the shoemaker . these five could not be trust much for any valiant conduct , although they speak very confidently over their can of cider . neither be their weapon fit for much execution , unless it be at close quarter , which they would be likely to avoid . Bill Dadds have a sickle , Jem Slocombe a flail , the cobbler have borrow the constable 's staff   for the constable would not attend , because there be no warrant , and the parish clerk have bring his pitch - pipe , which be enough to break any man 's head . but John Fry , of course , have his blunderbuss , load with tin - tack and marble , and more likely to kill the man who discharge it than any other person : but we know that John have it only for show , and to describe its quality . 
 now it be my great desire , and my chief hope , to come across Carver Doone that night , and settle the score between we ; not by any shot in the dark , but by a conflict man to man . as yet , since I come to full - grow power , I have never meet any one whom I could not play teetotum with : but now at last I have find a man whose strength be not to be laugh at . I could guess it in his face , I could tell it in his arm , I could see it in his stride and gait , which more than all the rest betray the substance of a man . and be so well use to wrestling , and to judge antagonist , I feel that here   if anywhere   I have find my match . 
 therefore I be not content to abide within the house , or go the round with the trooper ; but betook myself to the rick yard , know that the Doones be likely to begin their onset there . for they have a pleasant custom , when they visit farm - house , of light themselves towards pick up anything they want , or stab the inhabitant , by first create a blaze in the rick yard . and though our rick be all now of mere straw   except indeed two of prime clover - hay , and although on the top they be so wet that no firebrand might hurt they ; I be both unwilling to have they burn , and fearful that they might kindle , if well rouse up with fire upon the windward side . 
 by the bye , these Doones have get the bad of this pleasant trick one time . for happen to fire the rick of a lonely farm call Yeanworthy , not far above Glenthorne , they approach the house to get people 's good , and to enjoy their terror . the master of the farm be lately dead , and have leave , inside the clock - case , loaded , the great long gun , wherewith he have use to sport at the duck and the goose on the shore . now Widow Fisher take out this gun , and not care much what become of she   for she have love her husband dearly , she lay it upon the window - sill , which look upon the rick - yard ; and she back up the butt with a chest of oak drawer , and she open the window a little back , and let the muzzle out on the slope . presently five or six fine young Doones come dance a reel   as their manner be   betwixt she and the flame rick . upon which she pull the trigger with all the force of her thumb , and a quarter of a pound of duck - shot go out with a blaze on the dancer . you may suppose what their dancing be , and their reel how change to staggering , and their music none of the sweetest . one of they fall into the rick , and be burn , and bury in a ditch next day ; but the other be set upon their horse , and carry home on a path of blood . and strange to say , they never avenge this very dreadful injury ; but have hear that a woman have fire this desperate shot among they , they say that she ought to be a Doone , and inquire how old she be . 
 now I have not be so very long wait in our mow - yard , with my good gun ready , and a big club by I , before a heaviness of sleep begin to creep upon I . the flow of water be in my ear , and in my eye a hazy spreading , and upon my brain a closure , as a cobbler sew a vamp up . so I lean back in the clover - rick , and the dust of the seed and the smell come round I , without any trouble ; and I doze about Lorna , just once or twice , and what she have say about new - mow hay ; and then back go my head , and my chin go up ; and if ever a man be bl with slumber , down it come upon I , and away go I into it . 
 now this be very vile of I , and against all good resolution , even such as I would have swear to an hour ago or less . but if you have be in the water as I have , ay , and have long fight with it , after a good day 's work , and then great anxiety afterwards , and brain - work   which be not fair for I , and upon that a stout supper , mayhap you would not be so hard on my sleep ; though you feel it your duty to wake I . 




 CHAPTER XLIX 

 MAIDEN sentinel be BEST 

 it be not likely that the outlaws would attack out premise until some time after the moon be rise ; because it would be too dangerous to cross the flooded valley in the darkness of the night . and but for this consideration , I must have strive hard against the stealthy approach of slumber . but even so , it be very foolish to abandon watch , especially in such as I , who sleep like any dormouse . moreover , I have choose the very bad place in the world for such employment , with a goodly chance of awakening in a bed of solid fire . 
 and so it might have be , nay , it must have be , but for Lorna 's vigilance . her light hand upon my arm awake I , not too readily ; and leap up , I seize my club , and prepare to knock down somebody . 
 ' who be that ? ' I cry ; ' stand back , I say , and let I have fair chance at you . ' 
 ' be you go to knock I down , dear John ? ' reply the voice I love so well ; ' I be sure I should never get up again , after one blow from you , John . ' 
 ' my darling , be it you ? ' I cry ; ' and break all your order ? come back into the house at once : and nothing on your head , dear ! ' 
 ' how could I sleep , while at any moment you might be kill beneath my window ? and now be the time of real danger ; for man can see to travel . ' 
 I see at once the truth of this . the moon be high and clearly light all the water valley . to sleep any long might be death , not only to myself , but all . 
 ' the man on guard at the back of the house be fast asleep , ' she continue ; ' Gwenny , who let I out , and come with I , have hear he snore for two hour . I think the woman ought to be the watch , because they have have no travelling . where do you suppose little Gwenny be ? ' 
 ' surely not go to Glen Doone ? ' I be not sure , however : for I could believe almost anything of the cornish maiden 's hardihood . 
 ' no , ' reply Lorna , ' although she want even to do that . but of course I would not hear of it , on account of the swollen water . but she be perch on yonder tree , which command the Barrow valley . she say that they be almost sure to cross the streamlet there ; and now it be so wide and large , that she can trace it in the moonlight , half a mile beyond she . if they cross , she be sure to see they , and in good time to let we know . ' 
 ' what a shame , ' I cry , ' that the man should sleep , and the maiden be the soldier ! I will sit in that tree myself , and send little Gwenny back to you . go to bed , my good and dear ; I will take good care not to sleep again . ' 
 ' please not to send I away , dear John , ' she answer very mournfully ; ' you and I have be together through peril bad than this . I shall only be more timid , and more miserable , indoor . ' 
 ' I can not let you stay here , ' I say ; ' it be altogether impossible . do you suppose that I can fight , with you among the bullet , Lorna ? if this be the way you mean to take it , we have well go both to the apple - room , and lock ourselves in , and hide under the tile , and let they burn all the rest of the premise . ' 
 at this idea Lorna laugh , as I could see by the moonlight ; and then she say , 
 ' you be right , John . I should only do more harm than good : and of all thing I hate fight most , and disobedience next to it . therefore I will go indoor , although I can not go to bed . but promise I one thing , dearest John . you will keep yourself out of the way , now will not   you , as much as you can , for my sake ? ' 
 ' of that you may be quite certain , Lorna . I will shoot they all through the hay - rick . ' 
 ' that be right , dear , ' she answer , never doubt but what I could do it ; ' and then they can not see you , you know . but do not   think of climb that tree , John ; it be a great deal too dangerous . it be all very well for Gwenny ; she have no bone to break . ' 
 ' none worth break , you mean , I suppose . very well ; I will not climb the tree , for I should defeat my own purpose , I fear ; be such a conspicuous object . now go indoor , darling , without more word . the more you linger , the more I shall keep you . ' 
 she laugh her own bright laugh at this , and only say , ' God keep you , love ! ' and then away she trip across the yard , with the step I love to watch so . and thereupon I shoulder arm , and resolve to tramp till morning . for I be vex at my own neglect , and that Lorna should have to right it . 
 but before I have be long on duty , make the round of the rick and stable , and hail Gwenny now and then from the bottom of her tree , a short wide figure steal towards I , in and out the shadow , and I see that it be no other than the little maid herself , and that she bear some tiding . 
 ' ten on them cross the watter down yonner , ' say Gwenny , put her hand to her mouth , and seem to regard it as good news rather than otherwise : ' be arl crap up by hedgerow now . I could shutt dree on them from the bar of the gate , if so be I have your goon , young man . ' 
 ' there be no time to lose , Gwenny . run to the house and fetch Master Stickles , and all the man ; while I stay here , and watch the rick - yard . ' 
 perhaps I be wrong in heed the rick at such a time as that ; especially as only the clover be of much importance . but it seem to I like a sort of triumph that they should be even able to boast of have fire our mow - yard . therefore I stand in a nick of the clover , whence we have cut some truss , with my club in hand , and gun close by . 
 the robber ride into our yard as coolly as if they have be invite , have lift the gate from the hinge first on account of its be fasten . then they actually open our stable - door , and turn our honest horse out , and put their own rogue in the place of they . at this my breath be quite take away ; for we think so much of our horse . by this time I could see our trooper , wait in the shadow of the house , round the corner from where the Doones be , and expect the order to fire . but Jeremy Stickles very wisely keep they in readiness , until the enemy should advance upon they . 
 ' two of you lazy fellow go , ' it be the deep voice of Carver Doone , ' and make we a light , to cut their throat by . only one thing , once again . if any man touch Lorna , I will stab he where he stand . she belong to I . there be two other young damsel here , whom you may take away if you please . and the mother , I hear , be still comely . now for our right . we have bear too long the insolence of these yokel . kill every man , and every child , and burn the cursed place down . ' 
 as he speak thus blasphemously , I set my gun against his breast ; and by the light buckle from his belt , I see the little ' sight ' of brass gleam alike upon either side , and the sleek round barrel glimmering . the aim be sure as death itself . if I only draw the trigger   which go very lighily   Carver Doone would breathe no more . and yet will you believe I ? I could not pull the trigger . would to God that I have do so ! 
 for I never have take human life , neither do bodily harm to man ; beyond the little bruise , and the trifling ache and pain , which follow a good and honest bout in the wrestling ring . therefore I drop my carbine , and grasp again my club , which seem a more straight - forward implement . 
 presently two young man come towards I , bear brand of resine hemp , kindle from Carver 's lamp . the foremost of they set his torch to the rick within a yard of I , and smoke conceal I from he . I strike he with a back - handed blow on the elbow , as he bent it ; and I hear the bone of his arm break , as clearly as ever I hear a twig snap . with a roar of pain he fall on the ground , and his torch drop there , and sing he . the other man stand amazed at this , not have yet gain sight of I ; till I catch his firebrand from his hand , and strike it into his countenance . with that he leap at I ; but I catch he , in a manner learn from early wrestling , and snap his collar - bone , as I lay he upon the top of his comrade . 
 this little success so encourage I , that I be half inclined to advance , and challenge Carver Doone to meet I ; but I bear in mind that he would be apt to shoot I without ceremony ; and what be the utmost of human strength against the power of powder ? moreover , I remember my promise to sweet Lorna ; and who would be leave to defend she , if the rogue get rid of I ? 
 while I be hesitate thus   for I always continue to hesitate , except in actual conflict , a blaze of fire light up the house , and brown smoke hang around it . six of our man have let go at the Doones , by Jeremy Stickles ' order , as the villain come swagger down in the moonlight ready for rape or murder . two of they fall , and the rest hang back , to think at their leisure what this be . they be not use to this sort of thing : it be neither just nor courteous . 
 be unable any long to contain myself , as I think of Lorna 's excitement at all this noise of firing , I come across the yard , expect whether they would shoot at I . however , no one shot at I ; and I go up to Carver Doone , whom I know by his size in the moonlight , and I take he by the beard , and say , ' do you call yourself a man ? ' 
 for a moment he be so astonished that he could not answer . none have ever dare , I suppose , to look at he in that way ; and he see that he have meet his equal , or perhaps his master . and then he try a pistol at I , but I be too quick for he . 
 ' now , Carver Doone , take warning , ' I say to he , very soberly ; ' you have show yourself a fool by your contempt of I . I may not be your match in craft ; but I be in manhood . you be a despicable villain . lie low in your native muck . ' 
 and with that word , I lay he flat upon his back in our straw - yard , by a trick of the inner heel , which he could not have resist   though his strength have be twice as great as mine , unless he be a wrestler . see he down the other run , though one of they make a shot at I , and some of they get their horse , before our man come up ; and some go away without they . and among these last be Captain Carver who arise , while I be feel myself   for I have a little wound , and stride away with a train of curse enough to poison the light of the moon . 
 we gain six very good horse , by this attempt rapine , as well as two young prisoner , whom I have smite by the clover - rick . and two dead Doones be leave behind , whom   as we bury they in the churchyard , without any service over they , I for my part be most thankful that I have not kill . for to have the life of a fellow - man lay upon one 's conscience deserve he his death , or deserve it not be to my sense of right and wrong the heavy of all burden ; and the one that wear most deeply inward , with the dwelling of the mind on this view and on that of it . 
 I be inclined to pursue the enemy and try to capture more of they ; but Jeremy Stickles would not allow it , for he say that all the advantage would be upon their side , if we go hurry after they , with only the moon to guide we . and who could tell but what there might be another band of they , ready to fall upon the house , and burn it , and seize the woman , if we leave they unprotected ? when he put the case thus , I be glad enough to abide by his decision . and one thing be quite certain , that the Doones have never before receive so rude a shock , and so violent a blow to their supremacy , since first they have build up their power , and become the Lords of Exmoor . I know that Carver Doone would gnash those mighty tooth of his , and curse the man around he , for the blunder   which be in truth his own   of over - confidence and carelessness . and at the same time , all the rest would feel that such a thing have never happen , while old Sir Ensor be alive ; and that it be cause by nothing short of gross mismanagement . 
 I scarcely know who make the great fuss about my little wound , mother , or Annie , or Lorna . I be heartily ashamed to be so treat like a milksop ; but most unluckily it have be impossible to hide it . for the ball have cut along my temple , just above the eyebrow ; and be fire so near at hand , the powder too have scar I . therefore it seem a great deal bad than it really be ; and the sponging , and the plastering , and the sobbing , and the moaning , make I quite ashamed to look Master Stickles in the face . 
 however , at last I persuade they that I have no intention of give up the ghost that night ; and then they all fall to , and thank God with an emphasis quite unknown in church . and hereupon Master Stickles say , in his free and easy manner   for no one court his observation , that I be the lucky of all mortal in have a mother , and a sister , and a sweetheart , to make much of I . for his part , he say , he be just as well off in not have any to care for he . for now he might go and get shoot , or stab , or knock on the head , at his pleasure , without any one be offend . I make bold , upon this , to ask he what be become of his wife ; for I have hear he speak of have one . he say that he neither know nor care ; and perhaps I should be like he some day . that Lorna should hear such sentiment be very grievous to I . but she look at I with a smile , which prove her contempt for all such idea ; and lest anything still more unfit might be say , I dismiss the question . 
 but Master Stickles tell I afterwards , when there be no one with we , to have no faith in any woman , whatever she might seem to be . for he assure I that now he possess very large experience , for so small a matter ; be thoroughly acquaint with woman of every class , from lady of the high blood , to Bonarobas , and peasant ' wife : and that they all might be divide into three head and no more ; that be to say as follow . first , the very hot and passionate , who be only contemptible ; second , the cold and indifferent , who be simply odious ; and third , the mixture of the other two , who have the bad quality of both . as for reason , none of they have it ; it be like a seal book to they , which if they ever try to open , they begin at the back of the cover .
