 now I do not like to hear such thing ; and to I they appear to be insolent , as well as narrow - minded . for if you come to that , why might not man , as well as woman , be divide into the same three class , and be pronounce upon by woman , as being even more devoid than their gentle judge of reason ? moreover , I know , both from my own sense , and from the great of all great poet , that there be , and always have be , plenty of woman , good , and gentle , warm - hearted , love , and lovable ; very keen , moreover , at see the right , be it by reason , or otherwise . and upon the whole , I prefer they much to the people of my own sex , as goodness of heart be more important than to show good reason for have it . and so I say to Jeremy , 
 ' you have be ill - treat , perhaps , Master Stickles , by some woman or other ? ' 
 ' ah , that have I , ' he reply with an oath ; ' and the last on earth who should serve I so , the woman who be my wife . a woman whom I never strike , never wrong in any way , never even let she know that I like another well . and yet when I be at Berwick last , with the regiment on guard there against those vile moss - trooper , what do that woman do but fly in the face of all authority , and of my especial business , by run away herself with the big of all moss - trooper ? not that I care a groat about she ; and I wish the fool well rid of she : but the insolence of the thing be such that everybody laugh at I ; and back I go to London , lose a far well and safe job than this ; and all through she . come , let us have another onion . ' 
 Master Stickles 's view of the matter be so entirely unromantic , that I scarcely wonder at Mistress Stickles for have run away from he to an adventurous moss - trooper . for nine woman out of ten must have some kind of romance or other , to make their life endurable ; and when their love have lose this attractive element , this soft dew - fog   if such it be , the love itself be apt to languish ; unless its bloom be well replace by the bud hope of child . now Master Stickles neither have , nor wish to have , any child . 
 without wait for any warrant , only say something about ' captus in flagrante delicto , ' if that be the way to spell it Stickles send our prisoner off , bind and look miserable , to the jail at Taunton . I be desirous to let they go free , if they would promise amendment ; but although I have take they , and surely therefore have every right to let they go again , Master Stickles say , ' not so . ' he assure I that it be a matter of public polity ; and of course , not know what he mean , I could not contradict he ; but think that surely my private right ought to be respect . for if I throw a man in wrestling , I expect to get his stake ; and if I take a man prisoner why , he ought , in common justice , to belong to I , and I have a good right to let he go , if I think proper to do so . however , Master Stickles say that I be quite benighted , and know nothing of the Constitution ; which be the very thing I know , beyond any man in our parish ! 
 nevertheless , it be not for I to contradict a commissioner ; and therefore I let my prisoner go , and wish they a happy deliverance . Stickles reply , with a merry grin , that if ever they get it , it would be a jail deliverance , and the bliss of dancing ; and he lay his hand to his throat in a manner which seem to I most uncourteous . however , his foresight prove too correct ; for both those poor fellow be execute , soon after the next assize . Lorna have do she very good to earn another chance for they ; even go down on her knee to that common Jeremy , and plead with great tear for they . however , although much move by she , he vow that he durst do nothing else . to set they free be more than his own life be worth ; for all the country know , by this time , that two captive Doones be rope to the cider - press at Plover 's barrow . Annie bind the broken arm of the one whom I have knock down with the club , and I myself support it ; and then she wash and rub with lard the face of the other poor fellow , which the torch have injure ; and I fetch back his collar - bone to the good of my ability . for before any surgeon could arrive , they be off with a well - arm escort . that day we be reinforce so strongly from the station along the coast , even as far as Minehead , that we not only fear no further attack , but even talk of assault Glen Doone , without wait for the train - band . however , I think that it would be mean to take advantage of the enemy in the thick of the flood and confusion ; and several of the other think so too , and do not like fight in water . therefore it be resolve to wait and keep a watch upon the valley , and let the flood go down again . 




 CHAPTER L 

 a MERRY meeting a SAD one 

 now the business I have most at heart   as every one know by this time   be to marry Lorna as soon as might be , if she have no objection , and then to work the farm so well , as to nourish all our family . and herein I see no difficulty ; for Annie would soon be off our hand , and somebody might come and take a fancy to little Lizzie   who be grow up very nicely now , though not so fine as Annie ; moreover , we be almost sure to have great store of hay and corn after so much snow , if there be any truth in the old saying , 
 " a foot deep of rain will kill hay and grain ; but three foot of snow will make they come mo ' . " 
 and although it be too true that we have lose a many cattle , yet even so we have not lose money ; for the few remain fetched such price as be never know before . and though we grumble with all our heart , and really believe , at one time , that starvation be upon we , I doubt whether , on the whole , we be not the fat , and the rich , and the wiser for that winter . and I might have say the happy , except for the sorrow which we feel at the failure among our neighbour . the snowe lose every sheep they have , and nine out of ten horn cattle ; and poor Jasper Kebby would have be force to throw up the lease of his farm , and perhaps to go to prison , but for the help we give he . 
 however , my dear mother would have it that Lorna be too young , as yet , to think of be marry : and indeed I myself be compel to admit that her form be become more perfect and lovely ; though I have not think it possible . and another difficulty be , that as we have all be protestant from the time of Queen Elizabeth , the maiden must be convert first , and teach to hate all papist . now Lorna have not the small idea of ever be convert . she say that she love I truly , but want not to convert I ; and if I love she equally , why should I wish to convert she ? with this I be tolerably content , not see so very much difference between a creed and a credo , and believe God to be our Father , in Latin as well as English . moreover , my darling know but little of the popish way whether excellent or otherwise inasmuch as the Doones , though they steal their house , or at least the joiner 's work , have never be tempt enough by the devil to steal either church or chapel . 
 Lorna come to our little church , when Parson Bowden reappear after the snow be over ; and she say that all be very nice , and very like what she have see in the time of her Aunt Sabina , when they go far away to the little chapel , with a shilling in their glove . it make the tear come into her eye , by the force of memory , when Parson Bowden do the thing , not so gracefully nor so well , yet with pleasant imitation of her old Priest 's sacred rite . 
 ' he be a worthy man , ' she say , be use to talk in the service time , and my mother be oblige to cough : ' I like he very much indeed : but I wish he would let I put his thing the right way on his shoulder . ' 
 everybody in our parish , who could walk at all , or hire a boy and a wheelbarrow , ay , and half the folk from Countisbury , Brendon , and even Lynmouth , be and be to be find that Sunday , in our little church of Oare . People who would not come anigh we , when the Doones be threaten with carbine and with fire - brand , flock in their very good clothe , to see a lady Doone go to church . now all this come of that vile John Fry ; I know it as well as possible ; his tongue be bad than the clacker of a charity - school bell , or the ladle in the frying - pan , when the bee be swarm . 
 however , Lorna be not troubled ; partly because of her natural dignity and gentleness ; partly because she never dream that the people be come to look at she . but when we come to the Psalms of the day , with some vague sense of be star at more than ought to be , she drop the heavy black lace fringe of the velvet hat she wear , and conceal from the congregation all except her bright red lip , and the oval snowdrift of her chin . I touch her hand , and she press mine ; and we feel that we be close together , and God see no harm in it . 
 as for Parson Bowden   as worthy a man as ever live , and one who could shoot fly , he scarcely know what he be do , without the clerk to help he . he have bear it very well indeed , when I return from London ; but to see a live Doone in his church , and a lady Doone , and a lovely Doone , moreover one engage to I , upon whom he almost look as the squire of his parish   although not rightly an Armiger , and to feel that this lovely Doone be a Papist , and therefore of high religion as all our parson think and that she know exactly how he ought to do all the service , of which he himself know little ; I wish to express my firm belief that all these thing together turn Parson Bowden 's head a little , and make he look to I for order . 
 my mother , the very good of woman , be   as I could well perceive   a little annoyed and vex with thing . for this particular occasion , she have procure from Dulverton , by special message to Ruth Huckaback   whereof more anon , a head - dress with a feather never see before upon Exmoor , to the good of every one 's knowledge . it come from a bird call a flame something a flame oh , or a flame ah , I will not be positive but I can assure you that it do flame ; and dear mother have no other thought , but that all the congregation would neither see nor think of any other mortal thing , or immortal even , to the very end of the sermon . 
 Herein she be so disappointed , that no soon do she get home , but upstairs she go at speed , not even stop at the mirror in our little parlour , and fling the whole thing into a cupboard , as I know by the bang of the door , have ease the lock for she lately . Lorna see there be something wrong ; and she look at Annie and Lizzie   as more likely to understand it   with her former timid glance ; which I know so well , and which have first enslave I . 
 ' I know not what ail mother , ' say Annie , who look very beautiful , with lilac lute - string ribbon , which I see the Snowe girl envy ; ' but she have not attend to one of the prayer , nor say " amen , " all the morning . never fear , darle Lorna , it be nothing about you . it be something about our John , I be sure ; for she never worry herself very much about anybody but he . ' and here Annie make a look at I , such as I have have five hundred of . 
 ' you keep your opinion to yourself , ' I reply ; because I know the dear , and her little bit of jealousy ; ' it happen that you be quite wrong , this time . Lorna , come with I , my darling . ' 
 ' oh yes , Lorna ; go with he , ' cry Lizzie , drop her lip , in a way which you must see to know its meaning ; ' John want nobody now but you ; and none can find fault with his taste , dear . ' 
 ' you little fool , I should think not , ' I answer , very rudely ; for , betwixt the lot of they , my Lorna 's eyelash be quiver ; ' now , dear angel , come with I ; and snap your hand at the whole of they . ' 
 my angel do come , with a sigh , and then with a smile , when we be alone ; but without any unangelic attempt at snap her sweet white finger . 
 these little thing be enough to show that while every one so admire Lorna , and so kindly take to she , still there would , just now and then , be petty and paltry flash of jealousy concern she ; and perhaps it could not be otherwise among so many woman . however , we be always doubly kind to she afterwards ; and although her mind be so sensitive and quick that she must have suffer , she never allow we to perceive it , nor lower herself by resent it . 
 possibly I may have mention that little Ruth Huckaback have be ask , and have even promise to spend she Christmas with we ; and this be the more desirable , because she have leave we through some offence , or sorrow , about thing say of she . now my dear mother , be the kind and well - hearted of all woman , could not bear that poor dear Ruth   who would some day have such a fortune , should be entirely lose to we . ' it be our duty , my dear child , ' she say more than once about it , ' to forgive and forget , as freely as we hope to have it do to we . if dear little Ruth have not behave quite as we might have expect , great allowance should be make for a girl with so much money . design people get hold of she , and flatter she , and coax she , to obtain a base influence over she ; so that when she fall among simple folk , who speak the honest truth of she , no wonder the poor child be vex , and give herself air , and so on . Ruth can be very useful to we in a number of little way ; and I consider it quite a duty to pardon her freak of petulance . ' 
 now one of the little way in which Ruth have be very useful , be the purchase of the scarlet feather of the flame bird ; and now that the house be quite safe from attack , and the mark on my forehead be heal , I be beg , over and over again , to go and see Ruth , and make all thing straight , and pay for the gorgeous plumage . this last I be very desirous to do , that I might know the price of it , have make a small bet on the subject with Annie ; and have hold counsel with myself , whether or not it be possible to get something of the kind for Lorna , of still more distinguished appearance . of course she could not wear scarlet as yet , even if I have wish it ; but I believe that people of fashion often wear purple for mourning ; purple too be the royal colour , and Lorna be by right a queen ; therefore I be quite resolve to ransack Uncle Reuben 's store , in search of some bright purple bird , if nature have kindly provide one . 
 all this , however , I keep to myself , intend to trust Ruth Huckaback , and no one else in the matter . and so , one beautiful spring morning , when all the earth be kiss with scent , and all the air caress with song , up the lane I stoutly ride , well armed , and well provide . 
 now though it be part of my life to heed , it be no part of my tale to tell , how the wheat be come on . I reckon that you , who read this story , after I be dead and go   and before that none shall read it , will say , ' tush ! what be his wheat to we ? we be not wheat : we be human being : and all we care for be human doing . ' this may be very good argument , and in the main , I believe that it be so . nevertheless , if a man be to tell only what he think and do , and not what come around he , he must not mention his own clothe , which his father and mother buy for he . and more than my own clothe to I , ay , and as much as my own skin , be the work of nature round about , whereof a man be the small . 
 and now I will tell you , although most likely only to be laugh at , because I can not put it in the style of Mr. Dryden whom to compare to Shakespeare ! but if once I begin upon that , you will never hear the last of I nevertheless , I will tell you this ; not wish to be rude , but only just because I know it ; the more a man can fling his arm   so to say   round Nature 's neck , the more he can upon her bosom , like an infant , lie and suck , the more that man shall earn the trust and love of all his fellow man . 
 in this matter be no jealousy   when the man be dead ; because thereafter all other know how much of the milk be have ; and he can suck no long ; and they value he accordingly , for the nourishment he be to they . even as when we keep a roaster of the suck - pig , we choose , and praise at table most , the favourite of its mother . fifty time have I see this , and smile , and praise our people 's taste , and offer they more of the vital . 
 now here be I upon Shakespeare   who die , of his own fruition , at the age of fifty - two , yet live more than fifty thousand man , within his little span of life , when all the while I ought to be rid as hard as I can to Dulverton . but , to tell the truth , I could not ride hard , be hold at every turn , and often without any turn at all , by the beauty of thing around I . these thing grow upon a man if once he stop to notice they . 
 it want yet two hour to noon , when I come to Master Huckaback 's door , and strike the panel smartly . know nothing of their manner , only that people in a town could not be expect to entertain   as we do in farm - house , have , moreover , keen expectation of Master Huckaback 's avarice , I have bring some stuff to eat , make by Annie , and pack by Lorna , and require no thinking about it . 
 Ruth herself come and let I in , blush very heartily ; for which colour I praise her health , and my praise heighten it . that little thing have lovely eye , and could be trust thoroughly . I do like an obstinate little woman , when she be sure that she be right . and indeed if love have never speed I straight to the heart of Lorna   compare to whom , Ruth be no more than the thief be to the candle , who know but what I might have yield to the law of nature , that thorough trimmer of balance , and verify the proverb that the giant love the dwarf ? 
 ' I take the privilege , Mistress Ruth , of salute you accord to kinship , and the ordering of the Canons . ' and therewith I buss she well , and put my arm around her waist , be so terribly restrict in the matter of Lorna , and know the use of practice . not that I have any warmth all that be darle Lorna 's only out of pure gallantry , and my knowledge of London fashion . Ruth blush to such a pitch at this , and look up at I with such a gleam ; as if I must have my own way ; that all my love of kiss sunk , and I feel that I be wrong she . only my mother have tell I , when the girl be out of the way , to do all I could to please darle Ruth , and I have go about it accordingly . 
 now Ruth as yet have never hear a word about dear Lorna ; and when she lead I into the kitchen   where everything look beautiful , and tell I not to mind , for a moment , about the scrubbing of my boot , because she would only be too glad to clean it all up after I , and tell I how glad she be to see I , blush more at every word , and recall some of they , and stoop down for pot and pan , when I look at she too ruddily all these thing come upon I so , without any legal notice , that I could only look at Ruth , and think how very good she be , and how bright her handle be ; and wonder if I have wrong she . once or twice , I begin this I say upon my honour to endeavour to explain exactly , how we be at Plover 's barrow ; how we all have be bind to fight , and have defeat the enemy , keep their queen amongst we . but Ruth would make some great mistake between Lorna and Gwenny Carfax , and give I no chance to set she aright , and care about nothing much , except some news of Sally Snowe . 
 what could I do with this little thing ? all my sense of modesty , and value for my dinner , be against my over - press all the graceful hint I have give about Lorna . Ruth be just a girl of that sort , who will not believe one word , except from her own seeing ; not so much from any doubt , as from the practice of use eye which have be in business . 
 I ask Cousin Ruth   as we use to call she , though the cousinship be distant   what be become of Uncle Ben , and how it be that we never hear anything of or from he now . she reply that she hardly know what to make of her grandfather 's manner of carry on , for the last half - year or more . he be apt to leave his home , she say , at any hour of the day or night ; go none know whither , and return no one might say when . and his dress , in her opinion , be enough to frighten a hodman , of a scavenger of the road , instead of the decent suit of kersey , or of Sabbath doeskin , such as have win the respect and reverence of his fellow - townsman . but the bad of all thing be , as she confess with tear in her eye , that the poor old gentleman have something weigh heavily on his mind . 
 ' it will shorten his day , Cousin Ridd , ' she say , for she never would call I Cousin John ; ' he have no enjoyment of anything that he eat or drink , nor even in count his money , as he use to do all Sunday ; indeed no pleasure in anything , unless it be smoke his pipe , and think and star at bit of brown stone , which he pull , every now and then , out of his pocket . and the business he use to take such pride in be now leave almost entirely to the foreman , and to I . ' 
 ' and what will become of you , dear Ruth , if anything happen to the old man ? ' 
 ' I be sure I know not , ' she answer simply ; ' and I can not bear to think of it . it must depend , I suppose , upon dear grandfather 's pleasure about I . ' 
 ' it must rather depend , ' say I , though have no business to say it , ' upon your own good pleasure , Ruth ; for all the world will pay court to you . ' 
 ' that be the very thing which I never could endure . I have beg dear grandfather to leave no chance of that . when he have threaten I with poverty , as he do sometimes , I have always meet he truly , with the answer that I fear one thing a great deal bad than poverty ; namely , to be an heiress . but I can not make he believe it . only think how strange , Cousin Ridd , I can not make he believe it . ' 
 ' it be not strange at all , ' I answer ; ' consider how he value money . neither would any one else believe you , except by look into your true , and very pretty eye , dear . ' 
 now I beg that no one will suspect for a single moment , either that I do not mean exactly what I say , or mean a single atom more , or would not have say the same , if Lorna have be stand by . what I have always like in Ruth , be the calm , straightforward gaze , and beauty of her large brown eye . indeed I have speak of they to Lorna , as the only one to be compare   though not for more than a moment   to her own , for truth and light , but never for depth and softness . but now the little maiden drop they , and turn away , without reply . 
 ' I will go and see to my horse , ' I say ; ' the boy that have take he seem surprised at his have no horn on his forehead . perhaps he will lead he into the shop , and feed he upon broadcloth . ' 
 ' oh , he be such a stupid boy , ' Ruth answer with great sympathy : ' how quick of you to observe that now : and you call yourself " Slow John Ridd ! " I never do see such a stupid boy : sometimes he spoil my temper . but you must be back in half an hour , at the late , Cousin Ridd . you see I remember what you be ; when once you get among horse , or cow , or thing of that sort . ' 
 ' thing of that sort ! well do , Ruth ! one would think you be quite a Cockney . ' 
 Uncle Reuben do not come home to his dinner ; and his granddaughter say she have strictest order never to expect he . therefore we have none to dine with we , except the foreman of the shop , a worthy man , name Thomas Cockram , fifty year of age or so . he seem to I to have strong intention of his own about little Ruth , and on that account to regard I with a wholly undue malevolence . and perhaps , in order to justify he , I may have be more attentive to she than otherwise need have be ; at any rate , Ruth and I be pleasant ; and he the very opposite . 
 ' my dear Cousin Ruth , ' I say , on purpose to vex Master Cockram , because he eye we so heavily , and squint to unluckily , ' we have long be look for you at our Plover 's Barrows farm . you remember how you use to love hunt for egg in the morning , and hide up in the tallat with Lizzie , for I to seek you among the hay , when the sun be down . ah , Master Cockram , those be the thing young people find their pleasure in , not in sell a yard of serge , and give twopence - halfpenny change , and write ' settle " at the bottom , with a pencil that have black their tooth . now , Master Cockram , you ought to come as far as our good farm , at once , and eat two new - lay egg for breakfast , and be make to look quite young again . our good Annie would cook for you ; and you should have the hot new milk and the pope 's eye from the mutton ; and every foot of you would become a yard in about a fortnight . ' and hereupon , I spread my chest , to show he an example . Ruth could not keep her countenance : but I see that she think it wrong of I ; and would scold I , if ever I give she the chance of take those little liberty . however , he deserve it all , accord to my young idea , for his great impertinence in aim at my cousin . 
 but what I say be far less grievous to a man of honest mind than little Ruth 's own behaviour . I could hardly have believe that so thoroughly true a girl , and one so proud and upright , could have get rid of any man so cleverly as she get rid of Master Thomas Cockram . she give he not even a glass of wine , but commend to his notice , with a sweet and thoughtful gravity , some invoice which must be correct , before her dear grandfather should return ; and to amend which three great ledger must be search from first to last . Thomas Cockram wink at I , with the bad of his two wrong eye ; as much as to say , ' I understand it ; but I can not help myself . only you look out , if ever ' and before he have finish wink , the door be shut behind he . then Ruth say to I in the simple manner , ' you have ride far today , Cousin Ridd ; and have far to ride to get home again . what will dear Aunt Ridd say , if we send you away without nourishment ? all the key be in my keeping , and dear grandfather have the fine wine , not to be match in the west of England , as I have hear good judge say ; though I know not wine from cider . do you like the wine of Oporto , or the wine of xere ? ' 
 ' I know not one from the other , fair cousin , except by the colour , ' I answer : ' but the sound of Oporto be nobler , and rich . suppose we try wine of Oporto . ' 
 the good little creature go and fetch a black bottle of an ancient cast , cover with dust and cobwebs . these I be anxious to shake aside ; and indeed I think that the wine would be well for be rouse up a little . Ruth , however , would not hear a single word to that purport ; and see that she know more about it , I leave she to manage it . and the result be very fine indeed , to wit , a sparkling rosy liquor , dance with little flake of light , and scented like new violet . with this I be so pleased and gay , and Ruth so glad to see I gay , that we quite forget how the time go on ; and though my fair cousin would not be persuade to take a second glass herself , she keep on fill mine so fast that it be never empty , though I do my good to keep it so . 
 ' what be a little drop like this to a man of your size and strength , Cousin Ridd ? ' she say , with her cheek just brush with rose , which make she look very beautiful ; ' I have hear you say that your head be so thick or rather so clear , you ought to say that no liquor ever move it . ' 
 ' that be right enough , ' I answer ; ' what a witch you must be , dear Ruth , to have remember that now ! ' 
 ' oh , I remember every word I have ever hear you say , Cousin Ridd ; because your voice be so deep , you know , and you talk so little . now it be useless to say " no " . these bottle hold almost nothing . dear grandfather will not come home , I fear , until long after you be go . what will aunt Ridd think of I , I be sure ? you be all so dreadfully hospitable . now not another " no , " Cousin Ridd . we must have another bottle . ' 
 ' well , must be must , ' I answer , with a certain resignation . ' I can not bear bad manner , dear ; and how old be you next birthday ? ' 
 ' Eighteen , dear John ; ' say Ruth , come over with the empty bottle ; and I be pleased at she call I ' John , ' and have a great mind to kiss she . however , I think of my Lorna suddenly , and of the anger I should feel if a man go on with she so ; therefore I lay back in my chair , to wait for the other bottle . 
 ' do you remember how we dance that night ? ' I ask , while she be open it ; ' and how you be afraid of I first , because I look so tall , dear ? ' 
 ' yes , and so very broad , Cousin Ridd . I think that you would eat I . but I have come to know , since then , how very kind and good you be . ' 
 ' and will you come and dance again , at my wedding , Cousin Ruth ? ' 
 she nearly let the bottle fall , the last of which she be slop carefully into a vessel of bright glass ; and then she raise her hand again , and finish it judiciously . and after that , she take the window , to see that all her work be clear ; and then she pour I out a glass and say , with very pale cheek , but else no sign of meaning about she , ' what do you ask I , Cousin Ridd ? ' 
 ' nothing of any importance , Ruth ; only we be so fond of you . I mean to be marry as soon as I can . will you come and help we ? ' 
 ' to be sure I will , Cousin Ridd unless , unless , dear grandfather can not spare I from the business . ' she go away ; and her breast be heave , like a rick of under - carry hay . and she stand at the window long , try to make yawn of sigh . 
 for my part , I know not what to do . and yet I could think about it , as I never could with Lorna ; with whom I be always in a whirl , from the power of my love . so I think some time about it ; and perceive that it be the manly way , just to tell she everything ; except that I fear she like I . but it seem to I unaccountable that she do not even ask the name of my intended wife . perhaps she think that it must be Sally ; or perhaps she fear to trust her voice . 
 ' come and sit by I , dear Ruth ; and listen to a long , long story , how thing have come about with I . ' 
 ' no , thank you , Cousin Ridd , ' she answer ; ' at least I mean that I shall be happy that I shall be ready to hear you to listen to you , I mean of course . but I would rather stay where I be , and have the air or rather be able to watch for dear grandfather come home . he be so kind and good to I . what should I do without he ? ' 
 then I tell she how , for year and year , I have be attach to Lorna , and all the danger and difficulty which have so long beset we , and how I hop that these be pass , and no other might come between we , except on the score of religion ; upon which point I trust soon to overcome my mother 's objection . and then I tell she how poor , and helpless , and alone in the world , my Lorna be ; and how sad all her youth have be , until I bring she away at last . and many other little thing I mention , which there be no need for I again to dwell upon . Ruth hear it all without a word , and without once look at I ; and only by her attitude could I guess that she be weep . then when all my tale be tell , she ask in a low and gentle voice , but still without show her face to I , 
 ' and do she love you , Cousin Ridd ? do she say that she love you with with all her heart ? ' 
 ' certainly , she do , ' I answer . ' do you think it impossible for one like she to do so ? ' 
 she say no more ; but cross the room before I have time to look at she , and come behind my chair , and kiss I gently on the forehead . 
 ' I hope you may be very happy , with I mean in your new life , ' she whisper very softly ; ' as happy as you deserve to be , and as happy as you can make other be . now how I have be neglect you ! I be quite ashamed of myself for think only of grandfather : and it make I so low - spirited . you have tell I a very nice romance , and I have never even help you to a glass of wine . here , pour it for yourself , dear cousin ; I shall be back again directly . ' 
 with that she be out of the door in a moment ; and when she come back , you would not have think that a tear have dim those large bright eye , or wander down those pale clear cheek . only her hand be cold and tremble : and she make I help myself . 
 Uncle Reuben do not appear at all ; and Ruth , who have promise to come and see we , and stay for a fortnight at our house   if her grandfather could spare she , now discover , before I leave , that she must not think of do so . perhaps she be right in decide thus ; at any rate it have now become improper for I to press she . and yet I now desire tenfold that she should consent to come , think that Lorna herself would work the speedy cure of her pass whim . 
 for such , I try to persuade myself , be the nature of Ruth 's regard for I : and upon look back I could not charge myself with any misconduct towards the little maiden . I have never seek her company , I have never trifle with she   at least until that very day , and be so engross with my own love , I have scarcely ever thought of she . and the maiden would never have think of I , except as a clumsy yokel , but for my mother 's and sister 's meddling , and their wily suggestion . I believe they have tell the little soul that I be deeply in love with she ; although they both stoutly deny it . but who can place trust in a woman 's word , when it come to a question of match - make ? 




 CHAPTER LI 

 a visit from the counsellor 

 now while I be rid home that evening , with a tender conscience about Ruth , although not a wound one , I guess but little that all my thought be need much for my own affair . so however it prove to be ; for as I come in , soon after dark , my sister Eliza meet I at the corner of the cheese - room , and she say , ' do not   go in there , John , ' point to mother 's room ; ' until I have have a talk with you . ' 
 ' in the name of Moses , ' I inquire , have pick up that phrase at Dulverton ; ' what be you at about I now ? there be no peace for a quiet fellow . ' 
 ' it be nothing we be at , ' she answer ; ' neither may you make light of it . it be something very important about Mistress Lorna Doone . ' 
 ' let we have it at once , ' I cry ; ' I can bear anything about Lorna , except that she do not care for I . ' 
 ' it have nothing to do with that , John . and I be quite sure that you never need fear anything of that sort . she perfectly weary I sometimes , although her voice be so soft and sweet , about your endless perfection . ' 
 ' bless her little heart ! ' I say ; ' the subject be inexhaustible . ' 
 ' no doubt , ' reply Lizzie , in the dry manner ; ' especially to your sister . however this be no time to joke . I fear you will get the bad of it , John . do you know a man of about Gwenny 's shape , nearly as broad as he be long , but about six time the size of Gwenny , and with a length of snow - white hair , and a thickness also ; as the copse be last winter . he never can comb it , that be quite certain , with any comb yet invent . ' 
 ' then you go and offer your service . there be few thing you can not scarify . I know the man from your description , although I have never see he . now where be my Lorna ? ' 
 ' your Lorna be with Annie , have a good cry , I believe ; and Annie too glad to second she . she know that this great man be here , and know that he want to see she . but she beg to defer the interview , until dear John 's return . ' 
 ' what a nasty way you have of tell the very commonest piece of news ! ' I say , on purpose to pay she out . ' what man will ever fancy you , you unlucky little snapper ? now , no more nursery talk for I . I will go and settle this business . you have well go and dress your doll ; if you can give they clothe unpoisone . ' Hereupon Lizzie burst into a perfect roar of tear ; feel that she have the bad of it . and I take she up , and beg her pardon ; although she scarcely deserve it ; for she know that I be out of luck , and she might have spar her satire . 
 I be almost sure that the man who be come must be the Counsellor himself ; of whom I feel much keener fear than of his son Carver . and know that his visit bode ill to I and Lorna , I go and seek my dear ; and lead she with a heavy heart , from the maiden 's room to mother 's , to meet our dreadful visitor . 
 Mother be stand by the door , make curtsey now and then , and listen to a long harangue upon the right of state and land , which the Counsellor   have find that she be the owner of her property , and know nothing of her title to it   be encourage to deliver it . my dear mother stand gaze at he , spell - bind by his eloquence , and only hop that he would stop . he be shake his hair upon his shoulder , in the power of his word , and his wrath at some little thing , which he declare to be quite illegal . 
 then I venture to show myself , in the flesh , before he ; although he feign not to see I ; but he advance with zeal to Lorna ; hold out both hand at once . 
 ' my darling child , my dearest niece ; how wonderfully well you look ! Mistress Ridd , I give you credit . this be the country of good thing . I never would have believe our Queen could have look so royal . surely of all virtue , hospitality be the fine , and the most romantic . Dearest Lorna , kiss your uncle ; it be quite a privilege . ' 
 ' perhaps it be to you , sir , ' say Lorna , who could never quite check her sense of oddity ; ' but I fear that you have smoke tobacco , which spoil reciprocity . ' 
 ' you be right , my child . how keen your scent be ! it be always so with we . your grandfather be note for his olfactory power . ah , a great loss , dear Mrs. Ridd , a terrible loss to this neighbourhood ! as one of our great writer say I think it must be Milton " we ne'er shall look upon his like again . " ' 
 ' with your good leave sir , ' I break in , ' Master Milton could never have write so sweet and simple a line as that . it be one of the great Shakespeare . ' 
 ' woe be I for my neglect ! ' say the Counsellor , bow airily ; ' this must be your son , Mistress Ridd , the great John , the wrestler . and one who meddle with the Muses ! ah , since I be young , how everything be change , madam ! except indeed the beauty of woman , which seem to I to increase every year . ' here the old villain bow to my mother ; and she blush , and make another curtsey , and really do look very nice . 
 ' now though I have quote the poet amiss , as your son inform I   for which I tender my good thank , and must amend my reading , I can hardly be wrong in assume that this young armiger must be the too attractive cynosure to our poor little maiden . and for my part , she be welcome to he . I have never be one of those who dwell upon distinction of rank , and birth , and such like ; as if they be in the heart of nature , and must be eternal . in early youth , I may have think so , and be full of that little pride . but now I have long account it one of the first axiom of political economy you be follow I , Mistress Ridd ? ' 
 ' well , sir , I be do my good ; but I can not quite keep up with you . ' 
 ' never mind , madam ; I will be slow . but your son 's intelligence be so quick ' 
 ' I see , sir ; you think that mine must be . but no ; it all come from his father , sir . his father be that quick and clever ' 
 ' ah , I can well suppose it , madam . and a credit he be to both of you . now , to return to our mutton a figure which you will appreciate I may now be regard , I think , as this young lady 's legal guardian ; although I have not have the honour of be formally appoint such . her father be the eld son of Sir Ensor Doone ; and I happen to be the second son ; and as young maiden can not be baronet , I suppose I be " Sir Counsellor . " be it so , Mistress Ridd , accord to your theory of genealogy ? ' 
 ' I be sure I do not   know , sir , ' my mother answer carefully ; ' I know not anything of that name , sir , except in the Gospel of Matthew : but I see not why it should be otherwise . ' 
 ' Good , madam ! I may look upon that as your sanction and approval : and the College of Heralds shall hear of it . and in return , as Lorna 's guardian , I give my full and ready consent to her marriage with your son , madam . ' 
 ' oh , how good of you , sir , how kind ! well , I always do say , that the learned people be , almost always , the good and kind , and the most simple - hearted . ' 
 ' Madam , that be a great sentiment . what a goodly couple they will be ! and if we can add he to our strength ' 
 ' oh no , sir , oh no ! ' cry mother : ' you really must not think of it . he have always be bring up so honest ' 
 ' Hem ! that make a difference . a decide disqualification for domestic life among the Doones . but , surely , he might get over those prejudice , madam ? ' 
 ' oh no , sir ! he never can : he never can indeed . when he be only that high , sir , he could not steal even an apple , when some wicked boy try to mislead he . ' 
 ' ah , ' reply the Counsellor , shake his white head gravely ; ' then I greatly fear that his case be quite incurable . I have know such case ; violent prejudice , breed entirely of education , and anti - economical to the last degree . and when it be so , it be desperate : no man , after imbibe idea of that sort , can in any way be useful . ' 
 ' oh yes , sir , John be very useful . he can do as much work as three other man ; and you should see he load a sledd , sir . ' 
 ' I be speak , madam , of high usefulness , power of the brain and heart . the main thing for we upon earth be to take a large view of thing . but while we talk of the heart , what be my niece Lorna do , that she do not come and thank I , for my perhaps too prompt concession to her youthful fancy ? ah , if I have want thank , I should have be more stubborn . ' 
 Lorna , be challenge thus , come up and look at her uncle , with her noble eye fix full upon his , which beneath his white eyebrow glisten , like dormer window pile with snow . 
 ' for what be I to thank you , uncle ? ' 
 ' my dear niece , I have tell you . for remove the heavy obstacle , which to a mind so well regulate could possibly have exist , between your dutiful self and the object of your affection . ' 
 ' well , uncle , I should be very grateful , if I think that you do so from love of I ; or if I do not know that you have something yet conceal from I . ' 
 ' and my consent , ' say the Counsellor , ' be the more meritorious , the more liberal , frank , and candid , in the face of an exist fact , and a very clearly establish one ; which might have appear to weak mind in the light of an impediment ; but to my loftier view of matrimony seem quite a recommendation . ' 
 ' what fact do you mean , sir ? be it one that I ought to know ? ' 
 ' in my opinion it be , good niece . it form , to my mind , so fine a basis for the invariable harmony of the matrimonial state . to be brief as I always endeavour to be , without become obscure you two young people   ah , what a gift be youth ! one can never be too thankful for it   you will have the rare advantage of commence married life , with a subject of common interest to discuss , whenever you weary of well , say of one another ; if you can now , by any mean , conceive such a possibility . and perfect justice mete out : mutual goodwill result , from the sense of reciprocity . ' 
 ' I do not understand you , sir . why can you not say what you mean , at once ? ' 
 ' my dear child , I prolong your suspense . curiosity be the most powerful of all feminine instinct ; and therefore the most delightful , when not prematurely satisfied . however , if you must have my strong reality , here they be . your father slay dear John 's father , and dear John 's father slay your . ' 
 have say thus much , the Counsellor lean back upon his chair , and shade his calm white - bearded eye from the ray of our tallow candle . he be a man who like to look , rather than to be look at . but Lorna come to I for aid ; and I go up to Lorna and mother look at both of we . 
 then feel that I must speak first   as no one would begin it , I take my darling round the waist , and lead she up to the Counsellor ; while she try to bear it bravely ; yet must lean on I , or do . 
 ' now , Sir Counsellor Doone , ' I say , with Lorna squeeze both my hand , I never yet know how   consider that she be walk all the time , or something like it ; ' you know right well , Sir Counsellor , that Sir Ensor Doone give approval . ' I can not tell what make I think of this : but so it come upon I . 
 ' approval to what , good rustic John ? to the slaughter so reciprocal ? ' 
 ' no , sir , not to that ; even if it ever happen ; which I do not believe . but to the love betwixt I and Lorna ; which your story shall not break , without more evidence than your word . and even so , shall never break ; if Lorna think as I do . ' 
 the maiden give I a little touch , as much as to say , ' you be right , darling : give it to he , again , like that . ' however , I hold my peace , well know that too many word do mischief . 
 then mother look at I with wonder , be herself too amazed to speak ; and the Counsellor look , with great wrath in his eye , which he try to keep from burn . 
 ' how say you then , John Ridd , ' he cry , stretch out one hand , like Elijah ; ' be this a thing of the sort you love ? be this what you be use to ? ' 
 ' so please your worship , ' I answer ; ' no kind of violence can surprise we , since first come Doones upon Exmoor . up to that time none hear of harm ; except of take a purse , maybe , or cut a strange sheep 's throat . and the poor folk who do this be hang , with some benefit of clergy . but ever since the Doones come first , we be use to anything . ' 
 ' Thou varlet , ' cry the Counsellor , with the colour of his eye quite change with the sparkle of his fury ; ' be this the way we be to deal with such a low - breed clod as thou ? to question the doing of our people , and to talk of clergy ! what , dream you not that we could have clergy , and of the right sort , too , if only we care to have they ? tush ! be I to spend my time argue with a plough - tail Bob ? ' 
 ' if your worship will hearken to I , ' I answer very modestly , not wish to speak harshly , with Lorna look up at I ; ' there be many thing that might be say without any kind of argument , which I would never wish to try with one of your worship 's learning . and in the first place it seem to I that if our father hat one another bitterly , yet neither win the victory , only mutual discomfiture ; surely that be but a reason why we should be wise than they , and make it up in this generation by goodwill and loving ' 
 ' oh , John , you wise than your father ! ' mother break upon I here ; ' not but what you might be as wise , when you come to be old enough . ' 
 ' young people of the present age , ' say the Counsellor severely , ' have no right feeling of any sort , upon the simple matter . Lorna Doone , stand forth from contact with that heir of parricide ; and state in your own mellifluous voice , whether you regard this slaughter as a pleasant trifle . ' 
 ' you know , without any word of mine , ' she answer very softly , yet not withdraw from my hand , ' that although I have be season well to every kind of outrage , among my gentle relative , I have not yet so purely lose all sense of right and wrong as to receive what you have say , as lightly as you declare it . you think it a happy basis for our future concord . I do not quite think that , my uncle ; neither do I quite believe that a word of it be true . in our happy valley , nine - tenth of what be say be false ; and you be always will not to argue that true and false be but a blind turn upon a pivot . without any failure of respect for your character , good uncle , I decline politely to believe a word of what you have tell I . and even if it be prove to I , all I can say be this , if my John will have I , I be his for ever . ' 
 this long speech be too much for she ; she have overrate her strength about it , and the sustenance of irony . so at last she fall into my arm , which have long be wait for she ; and there she lie with no other sound , except a gurgling in her throat . 
 ' you old villain , ' cry my mother , shake her fist at the Counsellor , while I could do nothing else but hold , and bend across , my darling , and whisper to deaf ear ; ' what be the good of the quality ; if this be all that come of it ? out of the way ! you know the word that make the deadly mischief ; but not the way that heal they . give I that bottle , if hand you have ; what be the use of Counsellors ? ' 
 I see that dear mother be carry away ; and indeed I myself be something like it ; with the pale face upon my bosom , and the heaving of the heart , and the heat and cold all through I , as my darling breathe or lay . meanwhile the Counsellor stand back , and seem a little sorry ; although of course it be not in his power to be at all ashamed of himself . 
 ' my sweet love , my darling child , ' our mother go on to Lorna , in a way that I shall never forget , though I live to be a hundred ; ' pretty pet , not a word of it be true , upon that old liar 's oath ; and if every word be true , poor chick , you should have our John all the more for it . you and John be make by God and mean for one another , whatever fall between you . little lamb , look up and speak : here be your own John and I ; and the devil take the Counsellor . ' 
 I be amazed at mother 's word , be so unlike she ; while I love she all the more because she forget herself so . in another moment in ran Annie , ay and Lizzie also , know by some mystic sense   which I have often notice , but never could explain   that something be astir , belong to the world of woman , yet foreign to the eye of man . and now the Counsellor , be well - bear , although such a heartless miscreant , beckon to I to come away ; which I , be smother with woman , be only too glad to do , as soon as my own love would let go of I . 
 ' that be the bad of they , ' say the old man ; when I have lead he into our kitchen , with an apology at every step , and give he hot schnapps and water , and a cigarro of brave Tom Faggus : ' you never can say much , sir , in the way of reasoning   however gently mean and put   but what these woman will fly out . it be wise to put a wild bird in a cage , and expect he to sit and look at you , and chirp without a feather rumple , than it be to expect a woman to answer reason reasonably . ' say this , he look at his puff of smoke as if it contain more reason . 
 ' I be sure I do not know , sir , ' I answer accord to a phrase which have always be my favourite , on account of its general truth : moreover , he be now our guest , and have right to be treat accordingly : ' I be , as you see , not acquaint with the way of woman , except my mother and sister . ' 
 ' except not even they , my son , say the Counsellor , now have finish his glass , without much consultation about it ; ' if you once understand your mother and sister why you understand the lot of they . ' 
 he make a twist in his cloud of smoke , and dash his finger through it , so that I could not follow his meaning , and in manner like not to press he . 
 ' now of this business , John , ' he say , after get to the bottom of the second glass , and have a trifle or so to eat , and praise our chimney - corner ; ' take you on the whole , you know , you be wonderfully good people ; and instead of give I up to the soldier , as you might have do , you be do your good to make I drunk . ' 
 ' not at all , sir , ' I answer ; ' not at all , your worship . let I mix you another glass . we rarely have a great gentleman by the side of our ember and oven . I only beg your pardon , sir , that my sister Annie   who know where to find all the good pan and the lard   could not wait upon you this evening ; and I fear they have do it with dripping instead , and in a pan with the bottom burn . but old Betty quite lose her head sometimes , by dint of over - scolding . ' 
 ' my son , ' reply the Counsellor , stand across the front of the fire , to prove his strict sobriety : ' I mean to come down upon you to - night ; but you have turn the table upon I . not through any skill on your part , nor through any paltry weakness as to love   and all that stuff , which boy and girl spin top at , or knock doll ' nose together , but through your simple way of take I , as a man to be believe ; combine with the comfort of this place , and the choice tobacco and cordial . I have not enjoy an evening so much , God bless I if I know when ! ' 
 ' your worship , ' say I , ' make I more proud than I well know what to do with . of all the thing that please and lead we into happy sleep at night , the first and chief be to think that we have please a visitor . ' 
 ' then , John , thou hast deserve good sleep ; for I be not pleased easily . but although our family be not so high now as it hath be , I have enough of the gentleman leave to be pleased when good people try I . my father , Sir Ensor , be well than I in this great element of birth , and my son Carver be far bad . aetas parentum , what be it , my boy ? I hear that you have be at a grammar - school . ' 
 ' so I have , your worship , and at a very good one ; but I only get far enough to make more tail than head of Latin . ' 
 ' let that pass , ' say the Counsellor ; ' John , thou art all the wiser . ' and the old man shake his hoary lock , as if Latin have be his ruin . I look at he sadly , and wonder whether it might have so ruin I , but for God 's mercy in stop it . 




 CHAPTER LII 

 the way to make the cream rise 

 that night the reverend Counsellor , not be in such state of mind as ought to go alone , kindly take our good old bedstead , carve in panel , well enough , with the woman of Samaria . I set he up , both straight and heavy , so that he need but close both eye , and keep his mouth just open ; and in the morning he be thankful for all that he could remember . 
 I , for my part , scarcely know whether he really have begin to feel goodwill towards we , and to see that nothing else could be of any use to he ; or whether he be merely act , so as to deceive we . and it have strike I , several time , that he have make a great deal more of the spirit he have take than the quantity would warrant , with a man so wise and solid . neither do I quite understand a little story which Lorna tell I , how that in the night awake , she have hear , or seem to hear , a sound of feeling in her room ; as if there have be some one groping carefully among the thing within her drawer or wardrobe - closet . but the noise have cease at once , she say , when she sit up in bed and listen ; and know how many mouse we have , she take courage and fall asleep again . 
 after breakfast , the Counsellor   who look no whit the bad for schnapp , but even more grave and venerable   follow our Annie into the dairy , to see how we manage the clot cream , of which he have eat a basinful . and thereupon they talk a little ; and Annie think he a fine old gentleman , and a very just one ; for he have nobly condemn the people who speak against Tom Faggus . 
 ' your honour must plainly understand , ' say Annie , be now alone with he , and spread out her light quick hand over the pan , like butterfly , ' that they be bring in here to cool , after be set in the basin - hole , with the wood - ash under they , which I show you in the back - kitchen . and they must have very little heat , not enough to simmer even ; only just to make the bubble rise , and the scum upon the top set thick ; and after that , it clot as firm oh , as firm as my two hand be . ' 
 ' have you ever hear , ' ask the Counsellor , who enjoy this talk with Annie , ' that if you pass across the top , without break the surface , a string of bead , or polished glass , or anything of that kind , the cream will set three time as solid , and in thrice the quantity ? ' 
 ' no , sir ; I have never hear that , ' say Annie , star with all her simple eye ; ' what a thing it be to read book , and grow learn ! but it be very easy to try it : I will get my coral necklace ; it will not be witchcraft , will it , sir ? ' 
 ' certainly not , ' the old man reply ; ' I will make the experiment myself ; and you may trust I not to be hurt , my dear . but coral will not do , my child , neither will anything colour . the bead must be of plain common glass ; but the bright they be the well . ' 
 ' then I know the very thing , ' cry Annie ; ' as bright as bright can be , and without any colour in it , except in the sun or candle light . Dearest Lorna have the very thing , a necklace of some old glass - bead , or I think they call they jewel : she will be too glad to lend it to we . I will go for it , in a moment . ' 
 ' my dear , it can not be half so bright as your own pretty eye . but remember one thing , Annie , you must not say what it be for ; or even that I be go to use it , or anything at all about it ; else the charm will be break . bring it here , without a word ; if you know where she keep it . ' 
 ' to be sure I do , ' she answer ; ' John use to keep it for she . but she take it away from he last week , and she wear it when I mean when somebody be here ; and he say it be very valuable , and speak with great learning about it , and call it by some particular name , which I forget at this moment . but valuable or not , we can not hurt it , can we , sir , by pass it over the cream - pan ? ' 
 ' hurt it ! ' cry the Counsellor : ' nay , we shall do it good , my dear . it will help to raise the cream : and you may take my word for it , young maiden , none can do good in this world , without in turn receive it . ' pronounce this great sentiment , he look so grand and benevolent , that Annie   as she say afterwards   could scarce forbear from kiss he , yet fear to take the liberty . therefore , she only run away to fetch my Lorna 's necklace . 
 now as luck would have it whether good luck or otherwise , you must not judge too hastily , my darling have take it into her head , only a day or two before , that I be far too valuable to be trust with her necklace . now that she have some idea of its price and quality , she have begin to fear that some one , perhaps even Squire Faggus   in whom her faith be illiberal , might form design against my health , to win the bauble from I . so , with many pretty coaxing , she have lead I to give it up ; which , except for her own sake , I be glad enough to do , mislike a charge of such importance . 
 therefore Annie find it sparkle in the little secret hole , near the head of Lorna 's bed , which she herself have recommend for its safe custody ; and without a word to any one she bring it down , and dance it in the air before the Counsellor , for he to admire its lustre . 
 ' oh , that old thing ! ' say the gentleman , in a tone of some contempt ; ' I remember that old thing well enough . however , for want of a well , no doubt it will answer our purpose . three time three , I pass it over . Crinkleum , crankum , grass and clover ! what be you fear of , you silly child ? ' 
 ' good sir , it be perfect witchcraft ! I be sure of that , because it rhyme . oh , what would mother say to I ? shall I ever go to heaven again ? oh , I see the cream already ! ' 
 ' to be sure you do ; but you must not look , or the whole charm will be break , and the devil will fly away with the pan , and drown every cow you have get in it . ' 
 ' oh , sir , it be too horrible . how could you lead I to such a sin ? away with thee , witch of Endor ! ' 
 for the door begin to creak , and a broom appear suddenly in the opening , with our Betty , no doubt , behind it . but Annie , in the great terror , slam the door , and bolt it , and then turn again to the Counsellor ; yet look at his face , have not the courage to reproach he . for his eye roll like two blaze barrel , and his white shag brow be knit across they , and his forehead scowl in black furrow , so that Annie say that if she ever see the devil , she see he then , and no mistake . whether the old man wish to scare she , or whether he be try not to laugh , be more than I can tell you . 
 ' now , ' he say , in a deep stern whisper ; ' not a word of this to a live soul ; neither must you , nor any other enter this place for three hour at least . by that time the charm will have do its work : the pan will be cream to the bottom ; and you will bless I for a secret which will make your fortune . put the bauble under this pannikin ; which none must lift for a day and a night . have no fear , my simple wench ; not a breath of harm shall come to you , if you obey my order . ' 
 ' oh , that I will , sir , that I will : if you will only tell I what to do . ' 
 ' go to your room , without so much as a single word to any one . bolt yourself in , and for three hour now , read the Lord 's Prayer backwards . ' 
 Poor Annie be only too glad to escape , upon these condition ; and the Counsellor kiss she upon the forehead and tell she not to make her eye red , because they be much too sweet and pretty . she drop they at this , with a sob and a curtsey , and run away to her bedroom ; but as for read the Lord 's Prayer backwards , that be much beyond she ; and she have not do three word quite right , before the three hour expire . 
 meanwhile the Counsellor be go . he bid our mother adieu , with so much dignity of bearing , and such warmth of gratitude , and the high - breed courtesy of the old school   now fast disappear , that when he be go , dear mother fall back on the chair which he have use last night , as if it would teach she the grace . and for more than an hour she make believe not to know what there be for dinner . 
 ' oh , the wickedness of the world ! oh , the lie that be tell of people or rather I mean the falsehood because a man be well bear , and have well manner ! why , Lorna , how be it that you never speak about your charming uncle ? do you notice , Lizzie , how his silver hair be wave upon his velvet collar , and how white his hand be , and every nail like an acorn ; only pink like shell - fish , or at least like shell ? and the way he bow , and drop his eye , from his pure respect for I ! and then , that he would not even speak , on account of his emotion ; but press my hand in silence ! oh , Lizzie , you have read I beautiful thing about Sir Gallyhead , and the rest ; but nothing to equal Sir Counsellor . ' 
 ' you have well marry he , madam , ' say I , come in very sternly ; though I know I ought not to say it : ' he can repay your adoration . he have steal a hundred thousand pound . ' 
 ' John , ' cry my mother , ' you be mad ! ' and yet she turn as pale as death ; for woman be so quick at turn ; and she inkle what it be . 
 ' of course I be , mother ; mad about the marvel of Sir Galahad . he have go off with my Lorna 's necklace . fifty farm like our can never make it good to Lorna . ' 
 Hereupon ensue grim silence . Mother look at Lizzie 's face , for she could not look at I ; and Lizzie look at I , to know : and as for I , I could have stamp almost on the heart of any one . it be not the value of the necklace I be not so low a hound as that nor be it even the damned folly show by every one of we it be the thought of Lorna 's sorrow for her ancient plaything ; and even more , my fury at the breach of hospitality . 
 but Lorna come up to I softly , as a woman should always come ; and she lay one hand upon my shoulder ; and she only look at I . she even seem to fear to look , and drop her eye , and sigh at I . without a word , I know by that , how I must have look like Satan ; and the evil spirit leave my heart ; when she have make I think of it . 
 ' Darling John , do you want I to think that you care for my money , more than for I ? ' 
 I lead she away from the rest of they , be desirous of explain thing , when I see the depth of her nature open , like an everlasting well , to I . but she would not let I say a word , or do anything by ourselves , as it be : she say , ' your duty be to your mother : this blow be on she , and not on I . ' 
 I see that she be right ; though how she know it be beyond I ; and I ask she just to go in front , and bring my mother round a little . for I must let my passion pass : it may drop its weapon quickly ; but it can not come and go , before a man have time to think . 
 then Lorna go up to my mother , who be still in the chair of elegance ; and she take she by both hand , and say , 
 ' Dearest mother , I shall fret so , if I see you fret . and to fret will kill I , mother . they have always tell I so . ' 
 poor mother bent on Lorna 's shoulder , without thought of attitude , and lay her cheek on Lorna 's breast , and sob till Lizzie be jealous , and come with two pocket - handkerchief . as for I , my heart be light   if they would only dry their eye , and come round by dinnertime   than it have be since the day on which Tom Faggus discover the value of that blessed and cursed necklace . none could say that I want Lorna for her money now . and perhaps the Doones would let I have she ; now that her property be go . 
 but who shall tell of Annie 's grief ? the poor little thing would have stake her life upon find the trinket , in all its beauty , lie under the pannikin . she proudly challenge I to lift it which I have do , long ere that , of course if only I would take the risk of the spell for my incredulity . I tell she not to talk of spell , until she could spell a word backwards ; and then to look into the pan where the charmed cream should be . she would not acknowledge that the cream be the same as all the rest be : and indeed it be not quite the same , for the point of poor Lorna 's diamond have make a few star - ray across the rich firm crust of yellow . 
 but when we raise the pannikin , and there be nothing under it , poor Annie fall against the wall , which have be whiten lately ; and her face put all the white to scorn . my love , who be as fond of she , as if she have know she for fifty year , hereupon run up and catch she , and abuse all diamond . I will dwell no more upon Annie 's grief , because we feel it all so much . but I could not help tell she , if she want a witch , to seek good Mother Melldrum , a legitimate performer . 
 that same night Master Jeremy Stickles   of whose absence the Counsellor must have know   come back , with all equipment ready for the grand attack . now the Doones know , quite as well as we do , that this attack be threaten ; and that but for the wonderful weather it would have be make long ago . therefore we , or at least our people   for I be doubtful about go , be sure to meet with a good resistance , and due preparation . 
 it be very strange to hear and see , and quite impossible to account for , that now some hundred of country people   who fear to whisper so much as a word against the Doones a year ago , and would soon have think of attack a church , in service time , than Glen Doone   now sharpen their old cutlass , and lay pitch - fork on the grindstone , and brag at every village cross , as if each would kill ten Doones himself , neither care to wipe his hand afterwards . and this fierce bravery , and tall contempt , have be grow ever since the news of the attack upon our premise have take good people by surprise ; at least as concern the issue . 
 Jeremy Stickles laugh heartily about Annie 's new manner of charming the cream ; but he look very grave at the loss of the jewel , so soon as he know their value . 
 ' my son , ' he exclaim , ' this be very heavy . it will go ill with all of you to make good this loss , as I fear that you will have to do . ' 
 ' what ! ' cry I , with my blood run cold . ' we make good the loss , Master Stickles ! every farthing we have in the world , and the labour of our life to boot , will never make good the tenth of it . ' 
 ' it would cut I to the heart , ' he answer , lay his hand on mine , ' to hear of such a deadly blow to you and your good mother . and this farm ; how long , John , have it be in your family ? ' 
 ' for at least six hundred year , ' I say , with a foolish pride that be only too like to end in groan ; ' and some people say , by a Royal grant , in the time of the great King Alfred . at any rate , a Ridd be with he throughout all his hiding - time . we have always hold by the King and crown : surely none will turn we out , unless we be guilty of treason ? ' 
 ' my son , ' reply Jeremy very gently , so that I could love he for it , ' not a word to your good mother of this unlucky matter . keep it to yourself , my boy , and try to think but little of it . after all , I may be wrong : at any rate , least say well mend . ' 
 ' but Jeremy , dear Jeremy , how can I bear to leave it so ? do you suppose that I can sleep , and eat my food , and go about , and look at other people , as if nothing at all have happen ? and all the time have it on my mind , that not an acre of all the land , nor even our old sheep - dog , belong to we , of right at all ! it be more than I can do , Jeremy . let I talk , and know the bad of it . ' 
 ' very well , ' reply Master Stickles , see that both the door be close ; ' I think that nothing could move you , John ; or I never would have tell you . likely enough I be quite wrong ; and God send that I be so . but what I guess at some time back seem more than a guess , now that you have tell I about these wondrous jewel . now will you keep , as close as death , every word I tell you ? ' 
 ' by the honour of a man , I will . until you yourself release I . ' 
 ' that be quite enough , John . from you I want no oath ; which , accord to my experience , tempt a man to lie the more , by make it more important . I know you now too well to swear you , though I have the power . now , my lad , what I have to say will scare your mind in one way , and ease it in another . I think that you have be hard pressed I can read you like a book , John by something which that old villain say , before he steal the necklace . you have try not to dwell upon it ; you have even try to make light of it for the sake of the woman : but on the whole it have grieve you more than even this dastard robbery . ' 
 ' it would have do so , Jeremy Stickles , if I could once have believe it . and even without much belief , it be so against our manner , that it make I miserable . only think of love Lorna , only think of kiss she ; and then remember that her father have destroy the life of mine ! ' 
 ' only think , ' say Master Stickles , imitate my very voice , ' of Lorna love you , John , of Lorna kiss you , John ; and all the while say to herself , " this man 's father murder mine . " now look at it in Lorna 's way as well as in your own way . how one - side all man be ! ' 
 ' I may look at it in fifty way , and yet no good will come of it . Jeremy , I confess to you , that I try to make the good of it ; partly to baffle the Counsellor , and partly because my darling need my help , and bear it so , and behave to I so nobly . but to you in secret , I be not ashamed to say that a woman may look over this easy than a man may . ' 
 ' because her nature be large , my son , when she truly love ; although her mind be small . now , if I can ease you from this secret burden , will you bear , with strength and courage , the other which I plant on you ? ' 
 ' I will do my good , ' say I. 
 ' no man can do more , ' say he and so begin his story . 




 CHAPTER LIII 

 JEREMY find out something 

 ' you know , my son , ' say Jeremy Stickles , with a good pull at his pipe , because he be go to talk so much , and put his leg well along the settle ; ' it have be my duty , for a weary time than I care to think of   and which would have be unbearable , except for your great kindness , to search this neighbourhood narrowly , and learn everything about everybody . now the neighbourhood itself be queer ; and people have different way of thinking from what we be use to in London . for instance now , among your folk , when any piece of news be tell , or any man 's conduct speak of , the very first question that arise in your mind be this " be this action kind and good ? " long after that , you say to yourself , " do the law enjoin or forbid this thing ? " now here be your fundamental error : for among all truly civilised people the foremost of all question be , " how stand the law herein ? " and if the law approve , no need for any further questioning . that this be so , you may take my word : for I know the law pretty thoroughly . 
 ' very well ; I need not say any more about that , for I have show that you be all quite wrong . I only speak of this savage tendency , because it explain so many thing which have puzzle I among you , and most of all your kindness to man whom you never see before ; which be an utterly illegal thing . it also explain your toleration of these outlaw Doones so long . if your view of law have be correct , and law an element of your life , these robber could never have be indulge for so many year amongst you : but you must have abate the nuisance . ' 
 ' now , Stickles , ' I cry , ' this be too bad ! ' he be deliver himself so grandly . ' why you yourself have be amongst we , as the balance , and sceptre , and sword of law , for nigh upon a twelvemonth ; and have you abate the nuisance , or even care to do it , until they begin to shoot at you ? ' 
 ' my son , ' he reply , ' your argument be quite beside the purpose , and only tend to prove more clearly that which I have say of you . however , if you wish to hear my story , no more interruption . I may not have a chance to tell you , perhaps for week , or I know not when , if once those yellow and red arrive , and be bless to they , the lubber ! well , it may be six month ago , or it may be seven , at any rate a good while before that cursed frost begin , the mere name of which send a shiver down every bone of my body , when I be rid one afternoon from Dulverton to Watchett ' 
 ' Dulverton to Watchett ! ' I cry . ' now what do that remind I of ? I be sure , I remember something ' 
 ' remember this , John , if anything that another word from thee , and thou hast no more of mine . well , I be a little weary perhaps , have be plague at Dulverton with the grossness of the people . for they would tell I nothing at all about their fellow - townsman , your worthy Uncle Huckaback , except that he be a God - fear man , and they only wish I be like he . I bless myself for a stupid fool , in think to have pump they ; for by this time I might have know that , through your western homeliness , every man in his own country be something more than a prophet . and I feel , of course , that I have do more harm than good by question ; inasmuch as every soul in the place would run straightway and inform he that the King 's man from the other side of the forest have be sift out his way and work . ' 
 ' ah , ' I cry , for I could not help it ; ' you begin to understand at last , that we be not quite such a set of oafs , as you at first believe we . ' 
 ' I be rid on from Dulverton , ' he resume , with great severity , yet threaten I no more , which check I more than fifty threat : ' and it be late in the afternoon , and I be grow weary . the road   if road it could be call   ' turn suddenly down from the high land to the very brink of the sea ; and round a little jut of cliff , I meet the roar of the breaker . my horse be scared , and leap aside ; for a northerly wind be pip , and drive hunk of foam across , as child scatter snow - ball . but he only sink to his fetlock in the dry sand , pile with pop - weed : and I try to make he face the wave ; and then I look about I . 
 ' Watchett town be not to be see , on account of a little foreland , a mile or more upon my course , and stand to the right of I . there be room enough below the cliff   which be nothing there to your , John , for horse and man to get along , although the tide be run high with a northerly gale to back it . but close at hand and in the corner , draw above the yellow sand and long eye - brow of rackweed , as snug a little house blink on I as ever I see , or wish to see . 
 ' you know that I be not luxurious , neither in any way give to the common lust of the flesh , John . my father never allow his hair to grow a fourth part of an inch in length , and he be a thoroughly godly man ; and I try to follow in his footstep , whenever I think about it . nevertheless , I do assure you that my view of that little house and the way the light be twinkle , so different from the cold and darkness of the rolling sea , move the ancient Adam in I , if he could be find to move . I love not a house with too many window : be out of house and door some three - quarter of my time , when I get inside a house I like to feel the difference . air and light be good for people who have any lack of they ; and if a man once talk about they , ' tis enough to prove his need of they . but , as you well know , John Ridd , the horse who have be at work all day , with the sunshine in his eye , sleep well in dark stable , and need no moon to help he . 
 ' see therefore that this same inn have four window , and no more , I think to myself how snug it be , and how beautiful I could sleep there . and so I make the old horse draw hand , which he be only too glad to do , and we clomb above the spring - tide mark , and over a little piece of turf , and strike the door of the hostelry . some one come and peep at I through the lattice overhead , which be full of bull ' eye ; and then the bolt be draw back , and a woman meet I very courteously . a dark and foreign - look woman , very hot of blood , I doubt , but not altogether a bad one . and she wait for I to speak first , which an Englishwoman would not have do . 
 ' " can I rest here for the night ? " I ask , with a lift of my hat to she ; for she be no provincial dame , who would stare at I for the courtesy ; " my horse be weary from the slough , and myself but little well : beside that , we both be famish . " 
 ' " yes , sir , you can rest and welcome . but of food , I fear , there be but little , unless of the common order . our fisher would have draw the net , but the wave be violent . however , we have what you call it ? I never can remember , it be so hard to say the flesh of the hog salt . " 
 ' " Bacon ! " say I ; " what can be well ? and half dozen of egg with it , and a quart of fresh - draw ale . you make I rage with hunger , madam . be it cruelty , or hospitality ? " 
 ' " ah , good ! " she reply , with a merry smile , full of southern sunshine : " you be not of the man round here ; you can think , and you can laugh ! " 
 ' " and most of all , I can eat , good madam . in that way I shall astonish you ; even more than by my intellect . " 
 ' she laugh aloud , and swing her shoulder , as your native can not do ; and then she call a little maid to lead my horse to stable . however , I prefer to see that matter do myself , and tell she to send the little maid for the frying - pan and the egg - box .
