 ' whether it be my natural wit and elegance of manner ; or whether it be my London freedom and knowledge of the world ; or   which be perhaps the most probable , because the least pleasing supposition   my ready and permanent appetite , and appreciation of garlic I leave you to decide , John : but perhaps all three combine to recommend I to the grace of my charming hostess . when I say " charming , " I mean of course by manner and by intelligence , and most of all by cook ; for as regard external charm   most fleeting and fallacious   her have cease to cause distress , for I can not say how many year . she say that it be the climate for even upon that subject she request my opinion and I answer , " if there be a change , let madam blame the season . " 
 ' however , not to dwell too much upon our little pleasantry   for I always get on with these foreign woman well than with your Molls and Pegs , I became , not inquisitive , but reasonably desirous to know , by what strange hap or hazard , a clever and a handsome woman , as she must have be some day , a woman moreover with great contempt for the rustic mind around she , could have settle here in this lonely inn , with only the wave for company , and a boorish husband who slave all day in turn a potter 's wheel at Watchett . and what be the meaning of the emblem set above her doorway , a very unattractive cat sit in a ruined tree ? 
 ' however , I have not very long to strain my curiosity ; for when she find out who I be , and how I hold the King 's commission , and might be call an officer , her desire to tell I all be more than equal to mine of hear it . many and many a day , she have long for some one both skilful and trustworthy , most of all for some one bear warrant from a court of justice . but the magistrate of the neighbourhood would have nothing to say to she , declare that she be a crack - brain woman , and a wicked , and even a foreign one . 
 ' with many grimace she assure I that never by her own free - will would she have live so many year in that hateful country , where the sky for half the year be fog , and rain for nearly the other half . it be so the very night when first her evil fortune bring she there ; and so no doubt it would be , long after it have kill she . but if I wish to know the reason of she be there , she would tell I in few word , which I will repeat as briefly . 
 ' by birth she be an italian , from the mountain of Apulia , who have go to Rome to seek her fortune , after be badly treat in some love - affair . her christian name be Benita ; as for her surname , that could make no difference to any one . be a quick and active girl , and resolve to work down her trouble , she find employment in a large hotel ; and rise gradually , begin to send money to her parent . and here she might have thriven well , and marry well under sunny sky , and be a happy woman , but that some black day send thither a rich and noble english family , eager to behold the Pope . it be not , however , their fervent longing for the Holy Father which have bring they to St. Peter 's roof ; but rather their own bad luck in make their home too hot to hold they . for although in the main good Catholics , and pleasant receiver of anything , one of their number have give offence , by the folly of try to think for himself . some bitter feud have be among they , Benita know not how it be ; and the sister of the nobleman who have die quite lately be married to the rival claimant , whom they all detest . it be something about divide land ; Benita know not what it be . 
 ' but this Benita do know , that they be all great people , and rich , and very liberal ; so that when they offer to take she , to attend to the child , and to speak the language for they , and to comfort the lady , she be only too glad to go , little foresee the end of it . moreover , she love the child so , from their pretty way and that , and the thing they give she , and the style of their dress , that it would have break her heart almost never to see the dear again . 
 ' and so , in a very evil hour , she accept the service of the noble Englishman , and send her father an old shoe fill to the tongue with money , and trust herself to fortune . but even before she go , she know that it could not turn out well ; for the laurel leaf which she throw on the fire would not crackle even once , and the horn of the goat come wrong in the twist , and the heel of her foot be shin . this make her sigh at the starting - time ; and after that what could you hope for ? 
 ' however , at first all thing go well . my Lord be as gay as gay could be : and never would come inside the carriage , when a decent horse could be get to ride . he would gallop in front , at a reckless pace , without a weapon of any kind , delight with the pure blue air , and throw his heart around he . Benita have never see any man so admirable , and so childish . as innocent as an infant ; and not only content , but noisily happy with anything . only other people must share his joy ; and the shadow of sorrow scatter it , though it be but the shade of poverty . 
 ' here Benita weep a little ; and I like she none the less , and believe she ten time more ; in virtue of a tear or two . 
 ' and so they travel through Northern Italy , and throughout the south of France , make their way anyhow ; sometimes in coach , sometimes in cart , sometimes upon mule - back , sometimes even a - foot and weary ; but always as happy as could be . the child laugh , and grow , and throve   especially the young lady , the elder of the two , and Benita begin to think that omen must not be rely upon . but suddenly her faith in omen be confirm for ever . 
 ' my Lord , who be quite a young man still , and laugh at english arrogance , ride on in front of his wife and friend , to catch the first of a famous view , on the french side of the Pyrenee hill . he kiss his hand to his wife , and say that he would save she the trouble of come . for those two be so one in one , that they could make each other know whatever he or she have feel . and so my Lord go round the corner , with a fine young horse leap up at the step . 
 ' they wait for he , long and long ; but he never come again ; and within a week , his mangle body lie in a little chapel - yard ; and if the priest only say a quarter of the prayer they take the money for , God know they can have no throat leave ; only a relaxation . 
 ' my lady dwell for six month more it be a melancholy tale   what true tale be not so ?   scarcely able to believe that all her fright be not a dream . she would not wear a piece or shape of any mourning - clothe ; she would not have a person cry , or any sorrow among we . she simply disbelieve the thing , and trust God to right it . the Protestants , who have no faith , can not understand this feeling . enough that so it be ; and so my Lady go to heaven . 
 ' for when the snow come down in autumn on the root of the Pyrenees , and the chapel - yard be white with it , many people tell the lady that it be time for she to go . and the strong plea of all be this , that now she bear another hope of repeat her husband 's virtue . so at the end of October , when wolf come down to the farm - land , the little english family go home towards their England . 
 ' they land somewhere on the Devonshire coast , ten or eleven year agone , and stay some day at Exeter ; and set out thence in a hire coach , without any proper attendance , for Watchett , in the north of Somerset . for the lady own a quiet mansion in the neighbourhood of that town , and her one desire be to find refuge there , and to meet her lord , who be sure to come   she say   when he hear of his new infant . therefore with only two serve - man and two maid   include Benita , the party set forth from Exeter , and lie the first night at Bampton . 
 ' on the following morn they start bravely , with earnest hope of arrive at their journey 's end by daylight . but the road be soft and very deep , and the slough be out in place ; and the heavy coach break down in the axle , and need mending at Dulverton ; and so they lose three hour or more , and would have be wise to sleep there . but her ladyship would not hear of it ; she must be home that night , she say , and her husband would be wait . how could she keep he wait now , after such a long , long time ? 
 ' therefore , although it be afternoon , and the year now come to December , the horse be put to again , and the heavy coach go up the hill , with the lady and her two child , and Benita , sit inside of it ; the other maid , and two serve - man   each man with a great blunderbuss   mount upon the outside ; and upon the horse three exeter postilion . much have be say at Dulverton , and even back at Bampton , about some great freebooter , to whom all Exmoor owe suit and service , and pay they very punctually . both the serve - man be scared , even over their ale , by this . but the lady only say , " drive on ; I know a little of highwayman : they never rob a lady . " 
 ' through the fog and through the muck the coach go on , as good it might ; sometimes founder in a slough , with half of the horse splash it , and some - time knuckle up on a bank , and strain across the middle , while all the horse kick at it . however , they go on till dark as well as might be expect . but when they come , all thank God , to the pitch and slope of the sea - bank , lead on towards Watchett town , and where my horse have shy so , there the little boy jump up , and clap his hand at the water ; and there   as Benita say   they meet their fate , and could not fly it . 
 ' although it be past the dusk of day , the silver light from the sea flow in , and show the cliff , and the gray sand - line , and the drift of wreck , and wrack - weed . it show they also a troop of horseman , wait under a rock hard by , and ready to dash upon they . the postilion lash towards the sea , and the horse strove in the depth of sand , and the serve - man cock their blunder - bus , and cower away behind they ; but the lady stand up in the carriage bravely , and neither scream nor speak , but hide her son behind she . meanwhile the driver drive into the sea , till the lead horse be swim . 
 ' but before the wave come into the coach , a score of fierce man be round it . they curse the postilion for mad coward , and cut the trace , and seize the wheel - horse , all - wild with dismay in the wet and the dark . then , while the carriage be heel over , and well - nigh upset in the water , the lady exclaim , " I know that man ! he be our ancient enemy ; " and Benita   foresee that all their box would be turn inside out , or carry away , snatch the most valuable of the jewel , a magnificent necklace of diamond , and cast it over the little girl 's head , and bury it under her travelling - cloak , hop to save it . then a great wave , crest with foam , roll in , and the coach be throw on its side , and the sea rush in at the top and the window , upon shrieking , and clash , and faint away . 
 ' what follow Benita know not , as one might well suppose , herself be stun by a blow on the head , beside be palsy with terror . " see , I have the mark now , " she say , " where the jamb of the door come down on I ! " but when she recover her sens , she find herself lie upon the sand , the robber be out of sight , and one of the serve - man be bath her forehead with sea water . for this she rat he well , have take already too much of that article ; and then she arise and run to her mistress , who be sit upright on a little rock , with her dead boy 's face to her bosom , sometimes gaze upon he , and sometimes quest round for the other one . 
 ' although there be torch and link around , and she look at her child by the light of they , no one dare to approach the lady , or speak , or try to help she . each man whisper his fellow to go , but each hang back himself , and mutter that it be too awful to meddle with . and there she would have sit all night , with the fine little fellow stone dead in her arm , and her tearless eye dwell upon he , and her heart but not her mind thinking , only that the italian woman steal up softly to her side , and whisper , " it be the will of God . " 
 ' " so it always seem to be , " be all the word the mother ' answer ; and then she fall on Benita 's neck ; and the man be ashamed to be near her weeping ; and a sailor lie down and bellow . surely these man be the good . 
 ' before the light of the morning come along the tide to Watchett my Lady have meet her husband . they take she into the town that night , but not to her own castle ; and so the power of womanhood   which be itself maternity   come over swiftly upon she . the lady , whom all people love   though at certain time particular , lie in Watchett little churchyard , with son and heir at her right hand , and a little babe , of sex unknown , sleep on her bosom . 
 ' this be a miserable tale , ' say Jeremy Stickles brightly ; ' hand I over the schnapp , my boy . what fool we be to spoil our eye for other people 's trouble ! enough of our own to keep they clean , although we all be chimney - sweep . there be nothing like good holland , when a man become too sensitive . restore the action of the gland ; that be my rule , after weeping . let I make you another , John . you be quite low - spirited . ' 
 but although Master Jeremy carry on so   as become his manhood , and laugh at the sailor 's bellowing ; bless his heart , I know as well that tear be in his brave keen eye , as if I have dare to look for they , or to show mine own . 
 ' and what be the lady 's name ? ' I ask ; ' and what become of the little girl ? and why do the woman stay there ? ' 
 ' well ! ' cry Jeremy Stickles , only too glad to be cheerful again : ' talk of a woman after that ! as we use to say at school " who drag whom , how many time , in what manner , round the wall of what ? " but to begin , last first , my John   as become a woman : Benita stay in that bless place , because she could not get away from it . the Doones if Doones indeed they be , about which you of course know well take every stiver out of the carriage : wet or dry they take it . and Benita could never get her wage : for the whole affair be in Chancery , and they have appoint a receiver . ' 
 ' whew ! ' say I , know something of London , and sorry for Benita 's chance . 
 ' so the poor thing be compel to drop all thought of Apulia , and settle down on the brink of Exmoor , where you get all its evil , without the good to balance they . she marry a man who turn a wheel for make the blue Watchett ware , partly because he could give she a house , and partly because he prove himself a good soul towards my Lady . there they be , and have three child ; and there you may go and visit they . ' 
 ' I understand all that , Jeremy , though you do tell thing too quickly , and I would rather have John Fry 's style ; for he leave one time for his word to melt . now for my second question . what become of the little maid ? ' 
 ' you great oaf ! ' cry Jeremy Stickles : ' you be rather more likely to know , I should think , than any one else in all the kingdom . ' 
 ' if I know , I should not ask you . Jeremy Stickles , do try to be neither conceited nor thick - headed . ' 
 ' I will when you be neither , ' answer Master Jeremy ; ' but you occupy all the room , John . no one else can get in with you there . ' 
 ' very well then , let I out . take I down in both way . ' 
 ' if ever you be take down ; you must have your double joint ready now . and yet in other way you will be as proud and set up as Lucifer . as certain sure as I stand here , that little maid be Lorna Doone . ' 




 CHAPTER LIV 

 MUTUAL DISCOMFITURE 

 it must not be suppose that I be altogether so thick - headed as Jeremy would have make I out . but it be part of my character that I like other people to think I slow , and to labour hard to enlighten I , while all the time I can say to myself , ' this man be shallow than I be ; it be pleasant to see his shoal come up while he be sound mine so ! ' not that I would so behave , God forbid , with anybody   be it man or woman   who in simple heart approach I , with no gauge of intellect . but when the upper hand be take , upon the faith of one 's patience , by a man of even small wit   not that Jeremy be that , neither could he have live to be think so , why , it naturally happen , that we knuckle under , with an ounce of indignation . 
 Jeremy 's tale would have move I greatly both with sorrow and anger , even without my guess at first , and now my firm belief , that the child of those unlucky parent be indeed my Lorna . and as I think of the lady 's trouble , and her faith in Providence , and her cruel , childless death , and then imagine how my darling would be overcome to hear it , you may well believe that my quick reply to Jeremy Stickles 's banter be but as the flourish of a drum to cover the sound of pain . 
 for when he describe the heavy coach and the person in and upon it , and the breaking down at Dulverton , and the place of their destination , as well as the time and the weather , and the season of the year , my heart begin to burn within I , and my mind replace the picture , first of the foreign lady's - maid by the pump caress I , and then of the coach struggle up the hill , and the beautiful dame , and the fine little boy , with the white cockade in his hat ; but most of all the little girl , dark - haired and very lovely , and have even in those day the rich soft look of Lorna . 
 but when he speak of the necklace throw over the head of the little maiden , and of her disappearance , before my eye arise at once the flashing of the beacon - fire , the lonely moor embrown with the light , the tramp of the outlaw cavalcade , and the helpless child head - downward , lie across the robber 's saddle - bow . 
 then I remember my own mad shout of boyish indignation , and marvel at the strange long way by which the event of life come round . and while I think of my own return , and childish attempt to hide myself from sorrow in the sawpit , and the agony of my mother 's tear , it do not fail to strike I as a thing of oman , that the selfsame day should be , both to my darling and myself , the black and most miserable of all youthful day . 
 the King 's Commissioner think it wise , for some good reason of his own , to conceal from I , for the present , the name of the poor lady suppose to be Lorna 's mother ; and know that I could easily now discover it , without he , I let that question abide awhile . indeed I be half afraid to hear it , remember that the nobler and the wealthy she prove to be , the small be my chance of win such a wife for plain John Ridd . not that she would give I up : that I never dream of . but that other would interfere ; or indeed I myself might find it only honest to relinquish she . that last thought be a dreadful blow , and take my breath away from I . 
 Jeremy Stickles be quite decide and of course the discovery be his , he have a right to be so that not a word of all these thing must be impart to Lorna herself , or even to my mother , or any one whatever . ' keep it tight as wax , my lad , ' he cry , with a wink of great expression ; ' this belong to I , mind ; and the credit , ay , and the premium , and the right of discount , be altogether mine . it would have take you fifty year to put two and two together so , as I do , like a clap of thunder . ah , God have give some man brain ; and other have good farm and money , and a certain skill in the low beast . each must use his special talent . you work your farm : I work my brain . in the end , my lad , I shall beat you . ' 
 ' then , Jeremy , what a fool you must be , if you cudgel your brain to make money of this , to open the barn - door to I , and show I all your threshing . ' 
 ' not a whit , my son . quite the opposite . two man always thresh well than one . and here I have you bind to use your flail , one two , with mine , and yet in strict honour bind not to bushel up , till I tell you . ' 
 ' but , ' say I , be much amuse by a Londoner 's brave , yet uncertain , use of simple rural metaphor , for he have wholly forget the winnowing : ' surely if I bushel up , even when you tell I , I must take half - measure . ' 
 ' so you shall , my boy , ' he answer , ' if we can only cheat those confound knave of Equity . you shall take the beauty , my son , and the elegance , and the love , and all that and , my boy , I will take the money . ' 
 this he say in a way so dry , and yet so richly unctuous , that be gift somehow by God , with a kind of sense of queerness , I fall back in my chair , and laugh , though the underside of my laugh be tear . 
 ' now , Jeremy , how if I refuse to keep this half as tight as wax . you bind I to no such partnership , before you tell the story ; and I be not sure , by any mean , of your right to do so afterwards . ' 
 ' tush ! ' he reply : ' I know you too well , to look for meanness in you . if from pure goodwill , John Ridd , and anxiety to relieve you , I make no condition precedent , you be not the man to take advantage , as a lawyer might . I do not even want your promise . as sure as I hold this glass , and drink your health and love in another drop   force on I by pathetic word , so surely will you be bind to I , until I do release you . tush ! I know man well by this time : a mere look of trust from one be worth another 's ten thousand oath . ' 
 ' Jeremy , you be right , ' I answer ; ' at least as regard the issue . although perhaps you be not right in lead I into a bargain like this , without my own consent or knowledge . but suppose that we should both be shoot in this grand attack on the valley   for I mean to go with you now , heart and soul , be Lorna to remain untold of that which change all her life ? ' 
 ' both shoot ! ' cry Jeremy Stickles : ' my goodness , boy , talk not like that ! and those Doones be curse good shot too . Nay , nay , the yellow shall go in front ; we attack on the Somerset side , I think . I from a hill will reconnoitre , as behove a general , you shall stick behind a tree , if we can only find one big enough to hide you . you and I to be shoot , John Ridd , with all this inferior food for powder anxious to be devour ? ' 
 I laugh , for I know his cool hardihood , and never - flinch courage ; and sooth to say no coward would have dare to talk like that . 
 ' but when one come to think of it , ' he continue , smile at himself ; ' some provision should be make for even that unpleasant chance . I will leave the whole in writing , with order to be open , etc . , etc . now no more of that , my boy ; a cigarro after schnapp , and go to meet my yellow boy . ' 
 his ' yellow boy , ' as he call the Somersetshire train band , be even now come down the valley from the London Road , as every one since I go up to town , grandly entitle the lane to the moor . there be one good point about these man , that have no discipline at all , they make pretence to none whatever . Nay , rather they ridicule the thing , as below man of any spirit . on the other hand , Master Stickles 's trooper look down on these native fellow from a height which I hope they may never tumble , for it would break the neck of all of they . 
 now these fine native come along , sing , for their very life , a song the like of which set down here would oust my book from modest people , and make everybody say , ' this man never can have love Lorna . ' therefore , the less of that the well ; only I think , ' what a difference from the goodly psalm of the ale house ! ' 
 have finish their canticle , which contain more mirth than melody , they draw themselves up , in a sort of way suppose by they to be military , each man with heel and elbow strike into those of his neighbour , and salute the King 's Commissioner . ' why , where be your officer ? ' ask Master Stickles ; ' how be it that you have no officer ? ' upon this there arise a general grin , and a knowing look pass along their face , even up to the man by the gatepost . ' be you go to tell I , or not , ' say Jeremy , ' what be become of your officer ? ' 
 ' Plaise zur , ' say one little fellow at last , be nod at by the rest to speak , in right of his know eloquence ; ' hus tould Harfizers , as a wor no nade of un , now King 's man hiszell wor coom , a puppose vor to command we laike . ' 
 ' and do you mean to say , you villain , ' cry Jeremy , scarce know whether to laugh , or to swear , or what to do ; ' that your officer take their dismissal thus , and let you come on without they ? ' 
 ' what could them do ? ' ask the little man , with reason certainly on his side : ' hus zent them about their business , and they be glad enough to goo . ' 
 ' well ! ' say poor Jeremy , turn to I ; ' a pretty state of thing , John ! Threescore cobbler , and farming man , plasterer , tailor , and kettle - to - mend ; and not a man to keep order among they , except my bless self , John ! and I trow there be not one among they could hit all in - door fly . the Doones will make riddle of all of we . ' 
 however , he have well hope when the son of Devon appear , as they do in about an hour 's time ; fine fellow , and eager to prove themselves . these have not discard their officer , but march in good obedience to they , and be quite prepared to fight the man of Somerset   if need be   in addition to the Doones . and there be scarcely a man among they but could have trounce three of the yellow man , and would have do it gladly too , in honour of the red facing . 
 ' do you mean to suppose , Master Jeremy Stickles , ' say I , look on with amazement , behold also all our maiden at the upstair window wonder ; ' that we , my mother a widow woman , and I a young man of small estate , can keep and support all these precious fellow , both yellow one , and red one , until they have take the Doone Glen ? ' 
 ' God forbid it , my son ! ' he reply , lay a finger upon his lip : ' nay , nay , I be not of the shabby order , when I have the string of government . kill your sheep at famine price , and knead your bread at a figure express the rigour of last winter . let Annie make out the bill every day , and I at night will double it . you may take my word for it , Master John , this spring - harvest shall bring you in three time as much as last autumn 's do . if they cheat you in town , my lad , you shall have your change in the country . take thy bill , and write down quickly . ' 
 however this do not meet my view of what an honest man should do ; and I go to consult my mother about it , as all the account would be make in her name . 
 dear mother think that if the King pay only half again as much as other people would have to pay , it would be perhaps the proper thing ; the half be due for loyalty : and here she quote an ancient saying , 
 the King and his staff . be a man and a half : 
 which , accord to her judgment , rule beyond dispute the law of the present question . to argue with she after that   which she bring up with such triumph   would have be bad than useless . therefore I just tell Annie to make the bill at a third below the current market price ; so that the upshot would be fair . she promise I honestly that she would ; but with a twinkle in her bright blue eye , which she must have catch from Tom Faggus . it always have appear to I that stern and downright honesty upon money matter be a thing not understand of woman ; be they as good as good can be . 
 the yellow and the red together number a hundred and twenty man , most of whom sleep in our barn and stack ; and besides these we have fifteen trooper of the regular army . you may suppose that all the country be turn upside down about it ; and the folk who come to see they drill by no mean a needless exercise be a great plague than the soldier . the officer too of the devonshire hand be such a torment to we , that we almost wish their man have dismiss they , as the Somerset troop have do with theirs . for we could not keep they out of our house , be all young man of good family , and therefore not to be meet with bar . and have now three lovely maiden   for even Lizzie might be call so , when she care to please , mother and I be at wit 's end , on account of those bless officer . I never get a wink of sleep ; they come whistle under the window so ; and directly I go out to chase they , there be nothing but a cat to see . 
 therefore all of we be right glad   except perhaps Farmer Snowe , from whom we have buy some victual at rare price , when Jeremy Stickles give order to march , and we begin to try to do it . a good deal of boasting go overhead , as our man defile along the lane ; and the thick broad patin of pennywort jut out between the stone , ready to heal their bruise . the parish choir come part of the way , and the singing - loft from Countisbury ; and they keep our soldier ' spirit up with some of the most pugnacious Psalms . Parson Bowden march ahead , lead all our van and file , as against the Papists ; and promise to go with we , till we come to bullet distance . therefore we march bravely on , and child come to look at we . and I wonder where Uncle Reuben be , who ought to have lead the culverin   whereof we have no less than three , if Stickles could only have find he ; and then I think of little Ruth ; and without any fault on my part , my heart go down within I . 
 the culverin be lay on bark ; and all our horse pull they , and look round every now and then , with their ear curve up like a squirrel'd nut , and their nose toss anxiously , to know what sort of plough it be man have be pleased to put behind they man , whose endless whim and wildness they could never understand , any more than they could satisfy . however , they pull their very good as all our horse always do and the culverin go up the hill , without smack of whip , or swearing . it have be arrange , very justly , no doubt , and quite in keep with the spirit of the Constitution , but as it prove not too wisely , that either body of man should act in its own county only . so when we reach the top of the hill , the son of Devon march on , and across the track lead into Doone - gate , so as to fetch round the western side , and attack with their culverin from the cliff , whence the sentry have challenge I on the night of my pass the entrance . meanwhile the yellow lad be to stay upon the eastern highland , whence Uncle Reuben and myself have reconnoitre so long ago ; and whence I have leap into the valley at the time of the great snow - drift . and here they be not to show themselves ; but keep their culverin in the wood , until their cousin of Devon appear on the opposite parapet of the glen . 
 the third culverin be entrust to the fifteen trooper ; who , with ten pick soldier from either train hand , make in all five - and - thirty man , be to assault the Doone - gate itself , while the outlaws be place between two fire from the eastern cliff and the western . and with this force go Jeremy Stickles , and with it go myself , as know more about the passage than any other stranger do . therefore , if I have put it clearly , as I strive to do , you will see that the Doones must repulse at once three simultaneous attack , from an army numbering in the whole one hundred and thirty - five man , not include the Devonshire officer ; fifty man on each side , I mean , and thirty - five at the head of the valley . 
 the tactic of this grand campaign appear to I so clever , and beautifully order , that I commend Colonel Stickles , as everybody now call he , for his great ability and mastery of the art of war . he admit that he deserve high praise ; but say that he be not by any mean equally certain of success , so large a proportion of his force be only a raw militia , brave enough no doubt for anything , when they see their way to it ; but know little of gunnery , and wholly unused to be shoot at . whereas all the Doones be practise marksman , be compel when lad   like the Balearic slinger   to strike down their meal before taste they . and then Colonel Stickles ask I , whether I myself could stand fire ; he know that I be not a coward , but this be a different question . I tell he that I have be shoot at , once or twice before ; but nevertheless dislike it , as much as almost anything . upon that he say that I would do ; for that when a man get over the first blush of diffidence , he soon begin to look upon it as a puff of destiny . 
 I wish I could only tell what happen , in the battle of that day , especially as nearly all the people round these part , who never see gun - fire in it , have get the tale so much amiss ; and some of they will even stand in front of my own hearth , and contradict I to the tooth ; although at the time they be not bear , nor their father put into breech . but in truth , I can not tell , exactly , even the part in which I help , how then can I be expect , time by time , to lay before you , all the little in and out of place , where I myself be not ? only I can contradict thing , which I know could not have be ; and what I plainly see should not be controvert in my own house . 
 now we five - and - thirty man lie back a little way round the corner , in the hollow of the track which lead to the strong Doone - gate . our culverin be in amongst we , load now to the muzzle , and it be not comfortable to know that it might go off at any time . although the yeomanry be not come   accord to arrangement , some of we have horse there ; besides the horse who drag the cannon , and now be sniff at it . and there be plenty of spectator to mind these horse for we , as soon as we should charge ; inasmuch as all our friend and neighbour , who have so keenly prepare for the battle , now resolve to take no part , but look on , and praise the winner . 
 at last we hear the loud bang - bang , which prove that Devon and Somerset be pour their indignation hot into the den of malefactor , or at least so we suppose ; therefore at double quick march we advance round the bend of the cliff which have hide we , hop to find the gate undefended , and to blow down all barrier with the fire of our cannon . and indeed it seem likely at first to be so , for the wild and mountainous gorge of rock appear to be all in pure loneliness , except where the coloured coat of our soldier , and their metal trapping , shine with the sun behind they . therefore we shout a loud hurrah , as for an easy victory . 
 but while the sound of our cheer ring back among the crag above we , a shrill clear whistle cleave the air for a single moment , and then a dozen carbine bellow , and all among we fly murderous lead . several of our man roll over , but the rest rush on like Britons , Jeremy and myself in front , while we hear the horse plunge at the loaded gun behind we . ' now , my lad , ' cry Jeremy , ' one dash , and we be beyond they ! ' for he see that the foe be overhead in the gallery of brushwood . 
 our man with a brave shout answer he , for his courage be fine example ; and we leap in under the foot of the foe , before they could load their gun again . but here , when the foremost among we be past , an awful crash ring behind we , with the shriek of man , and the din of metal , and the horrible screaming of horse . the trunk of the tree have be launch overhead , and crash into the very midst of we . our cannon be under it , so be two man , and a horse with his poor back break . another horse vainly struggle to rise , with his thigh - bone smashed and protrude . 
 now I lose all presence of mind at this , for I love both those good horse , and shout for any to follow I , dash headlong into the cavern . some five or six man come after I , the foremost of whom be Jeremy , when a storm of shot whistle and pat around I , with a blaze of light and a thunderous roar . on I leap , like a madman , and pounce on one gunner , and hurl he across his culverin ; but the other have flee , and a heavy oak door fall to with a bang , behind they . so utterly be my sens go , and naught but strength remain , that I catch up the cannon with both hand , and dash it , breech - first , at the doorway . the solid oak burst with the blow , and the gun stick fast , like a builder 's putlog . 
 but here I look round in vain for any one to come and follow up my success . the scanty light show I no figure move through the length of the tunnel behind I ; only a heavy groan or two go to my heart , and chill it . so I hurry back to seek Jeremy , fear that he must be smite down . 
 and so indeed I find he , as well as three other poor fellow , strike by the charge of the culverin , which have pass so close beside I . two of the four be as dead as stone , and grow cold already , but Jeremy and the other could manage to groan , just now and then . so I turn my attention to they , and think no more of fighting . 
 have so many wounded man , and so many dead among we , we loiter at the cavern 's mouth , and look at one another , wish only for somebody to come and take command of we . but no one come ; and I be griefe so much about poor Jeremy , besides be wholly unused to any violence of bloodshed , that I could only keep his head up , and try to stop he from bleed . and he look up at I pitifully , be perhaps in a haze of thought , as a calf look at a butcher . 
 the shot have take he in the mouth ; about that no doubt could be , for two of his tooth be in his beard , and one of his lip be want . I lay his shattered face on my breast , and nurse he , as a woman might . but he look at I with a jerk at this ; and I see that he want coolness . 
 while here we stay , quite out of danger   for the fellow from the gallery could by no mean shoot we , even if they remain there , and the oaken door whence the other flee be block up by the culverin , a boy who have no business there   be in fact our clerk 's apprentice to the art of shoe - making   come round the corner upon we in the manner which boy , and only boy , can use with grace and freedom ; that be to say , with a sudden rush , and a sidelong step , and an impudence , 
 ' got the bad of it ! ' cry the boy ; ' well be off all of you . Zoomerzett and Devon a vighting ; and the Doones have drashe them both . Maister Ridd , even thee be drashe . ' 
 we few , who yet remain of the force which be to have win the Doone - gate , gaze at one another , like so many fool , and nothing more . for we still have some faint hope of win the day , and recover our reputation , by mean of what the other man might have do without we . and we could not understand at all how Devonshire and Somerset , be embark in the same cause , should be fight with one another . 
 find nothing more to be do in the way of carry on the war , we lay poor Master Stickles and two more of the wound upon the carriage of bark and hurdle , whereon our gun have lie ; and we roll the gun into the river , and harness the horse yet alive , and put the other out of their pain , and sadly wend homeward , feel ourselves to be thoroughly beat , yet ready to maintain that it be no fault of ours whatever . and in this opinion the woman join , be only too glad and thankful to see we home alive again . 
 now , this enterprise have fail so , I prefer not to dwell too long upon it ; only just to show the mischief which lie at the root of the failure . and this mischief be the vile jealousy betwixt red and yellow uniform . now I try to speak impartially , belong no more to Somerset than I do to Devonshire , live upon the border , and bear of either county . the tale be tell I by one side first ; and then quite to a different tune by the other ; and then by both together , with very hot word of reviling , and a desire to fight it out again . and put this with that , the truth appear to be as follow : 
 the man of Devon , who bear red facing , have a long way to go round the hill , before they could get into due position on the western side of the Doone Glen . and know that their cousin in yellow would claim the whole of the glory , if allow to be first with the firing , these worthy fellow wait not to take good aim with their cannon , see the other about to shoot ; but fettle it anyhow on the slope , point in a general direction ; and trust in God for aimworthiness , lay the rope to the breech , and fire . now as Providence ordain it , the shot , which be a casual mixture of anything consider hard for instance , jug - bottom and knob of door the whole of this pernicious dose come scatter and shatter among the unfortunate yellow man upon the opposite cliff ; kill one and wound two . 
 now what do the man of Somerset do , but instead of wait for their friend to send round and beg pardon , train their gun full mouth upon they , and with a vicious meaning shoot . not only this , but they loudly cheer , when they see four or five red coat lie low ; for which savage feel not even the remark of the devonshire man concern their coat could entirely excuse they . now I need not tell the rest of it , for the tale make a man discontent . enough that both side wax hotter and hotter with the fire of destruction . and but that the gorge of the cliff lay between , very few would have live to tell of it ; for our western blood become stiff and firm , when churn with the sense of wrong in it . 
 at last the Doones   who must have laugh at the thunder pass overhead   recall their man from the gallery , issue out of Gwenny 's gate   which have be wholly overlook   and fall on the rear of the Somerset man , and slay four beside their cannon . then while the survivor run away , the outlaws take the hot culverin , and roll it down into their valley . thus , of the three gun set forth that morning , only one ever come home again , and that be the gun of the devonshire man , who drag it home themselves , with the view of make a boast about it . 
 this be a melancholy end of our brave setting out , and everybody blame every one else ; and several of we want to have the whole thing over again , as then we must have right it . but upon one point all agree , by some reason not clear to I , that the root of the evil be to be find in the way Parson Bowden go up the hill , with his hat on , and no cassock . 




 CHAPTER LV 

 get into CHANCERY 

 two of the Devonshire officer   Captains Pyke and Dallan   now take command of the man who be leave , and order all to go home again , commend much the bravery which have be display on all side , and the loyalty to the king , and the english constitution . this last word always seem to I to settle everything when say , because nobody understand it , and yet all can puzzle their neighbour . so the Devonshire man , have bean to sow   which they ought to have do on Good Friday   go home ; and our Somerset friend only stay for two day more to backbite they . 
 to I the whole thing be purely grievous ; not from any sense of defeat   though that be bad enough   but from the pain and anguish cause by death , and wound , and mourning . ' surely we have woe enough , ' I use to think of an evening , when the poor fellow could not sleep or rest , or let other rest around they ; ' surely all this smell of wound be not incense man should pay to the God who make they . death , when it come and be do with , may be a bliss to any one ; but the doubt of life or death , when a man lie , as it be , like a trunk upon a sawpit and a grisly head look up at he , and the groan of pain be cleave he , this would be beyond all bear but for Nature 's sap sweet hope . ' 
 Jeremy Stickles lie and toss , and thrust up his foot in agony , and bit with his lipless mouth the clothe , and be proud to see blood upon they . he look at we ever so many time , as much as to say , ' Fools , let I die , then I shall have some comfort ' ; but we nod at he sagely , especially the woman , try to convey to he , on no account to die yet . and then we talk to one another   on purpose for he to hear we , how brave he be , and not the man to knock under in a hurry , and how he should have the victory yet ; and how well he look , consider . 
 these thing cheer he a little now , and a little more next time ; and every time we go on so , he take it with less impatience . then once when he have be very quiet , and not even try to frown at we , Annie lean over , and kiss his forehead , and spread the pillow and sheet , with a curve as delicate as his own white ear ; and then he feebly lift hand , and pray to God to bless she . and after that he come round gently ; though never to the man he have be , and never to speak loud again . 
 for a time   as I may have imply before   Master Stickles 's authority , and manner of levy duty , have not be take kindly by the people round our neighbourhood . the manor of East Lynn and West Lynn , and even that of Woolhanger although just then all three be at issue about some right of wreck , and the hanging of a sheep - stealer   a man of no great eminence , yet claim by each for the sake of his clothe   these three , have their right impugn , or even supersede , as they declare by the quartering of soldier in their neighbourhood , unite very kindly to oppose the King 's Commissioner . however , Jeremy have contrive to conciliate the whole of they , not so much by anything engage in his deportment or delicate address , as by hold out bright hope that the plunder of the Doone Glen might become divisible among the adjoining manor . now I have never discover a thing which the lord of manor   at least in our part of the world   do not believe to belong to themselves , if only they could get their right . and it do seem natural enough that if the Doones be oust , and a nice collection of prey remain , this should be part among the people have ancient right of plunder . nevertheless , Master Jeremy know that the soldier would have the first of it , and the King what they could not carry . 
 and perhaps he be punish justly for language so misleading , by the general indignation of the people all around we , not at his failure , but at himself , for that which he could in no wise prevent . and the steward of the manor ride up to our house on purpose to reproach he , and be greatly vex with all of we , because he be too ill to see they . 
 to myself   though by right the last to be think of , among so much pain and trouble   Jeremy 's wound be a great misfortune , in more way than one . in the first place , it defer my chance of impart either to my mother or to mistress Lorna my firm belief that the maid I love be not spring from the race which have slay my father ; neither could he in any way have offend against her family . and this discovery I be yearn more and more to declare to they ; be force to see   even in the midst of all our warlike trouble   that a certain difference be grow betwixt they both , and betwixt they and I . for although the word of the Counsellor have seem to fail among we , be bravely meet and scatter , yet our courage be but as wind fling wide the tare - seed , when the sower cast they from his bag . the crop may not come evenly , many place may long lie bare , and the field be all in patch ; yet almost every vetch will spring , and tiller out , and stretch across the scattering where the wind puff . 
 and so dear mother and darle Lorna now have be for many a day thinking , worry , and wear , about the matter between we . neither like to look at the other , as they use to do ; with mother admire Lorna 's eye , and grace , and form of breeding ; and Lorna love mother 's goodness , softness , and simplicity . and the sad and most hurtful thing be that neither could ask the other of the shadow fall between they . and so it go on , and deepen . 
 in the next place Colonel Stickles 's illness be a grievous thing to we , in that we have no one now to command the trooper . ten of these be still alive , and so well approve to we , that they could never fancy aught , whether for dinner or supper , without its be forth - come . if they want trout they should have it ; if colloped venison , or broil ham , or salmon from Lynmouth and Trentisoe , or truffle from the woodside , all these be at the warrior ' service , until they lust for something else . even the wounded man eat nobly ; all except poor Jeremy , who be force to have a young eld shoot , with the pith draw , for to feed he . and once , when they want pickle loach   from my description of it , I take up my boyish sport again , and pronge they a good jarful . therefore , none of they could complain ; and yet they be not satisfied ; perhaps for want of complain . 
 be that as it might , we know that if they once resolve to go   as they might do at any time , with only a corporal over they   all our house , and all our good , ay , and our own precious life , would and must be at the mercy of embitter enemy . for now the Doones , have drive back , as every one say , five hundred man though not thirty have ever fight with they be in such feather all round the country , that nothing be too good for they . offering pour in at the Doone gate , fast than Doones could away with they , and the sympathy both of Devon and Somerset become almost oppressive . and perhaps this wealth of congratulation , and mutual good feeling between plundered and victim , save we from any piece of spite ; kindliness have win the day , and every one love every one . 
 but yet another cause arise , and this the strong one of all , to prove the need of Stickles 's aid , and calamity of his illness . and this come to our knowledge first , without much time to think of it . for two man appear at our gate one day , strip to their shirt , and void of horse , and look very sorrowful . now have some fear of attack from the Doones , and scarce know what their trick might be , we receive these stranger cautiously , desire to know who they be before we let they see all our premise . 
 however , it soon become plain to we that although they might not be honest fellow , at any rate they be not Doones ; and so we take they in , and fed , and leave they to tell their business . and this they be glad enough to do ; as man who have be maltreat almost always be . and it be not for we to contradict they , lest our victual should go amiss . 
 these two very worthy fellow nay , more than that by their own account , be downright martyrs be come , for the public benefit , from the Court of Chancery , sit for everybody 's good , and boldly redress evil . this court have a power of scent unknown to the common - law practitioner , and slowly yet surely track its game ; even as the great lumber dog , now introduce from Spain , and call by some people ' pointer , ' differ from the swift gaze - hound , who see his prey and run he down in the manner of the common lawyer . if a man 's ill fate should drive he to make a choice between these two , let he rather be chase by the hound of law , than track by the dog of Equity . 
 now , as it fall in a very black day   for all except the lawyer   his Majesty 's Court of Chancery , if that be what it call itself , gain scent of poor Lorna 's life , and of all that might be make of it . whether through that brave young lord who run into such peril , or through any of his friend , or whether through that deep old Counsellor , whose game none might penetrate ; or through any disclosure of the italian woman , or even of Jeremy himself ; none just now could tell we ; only this truth be too clear Chancery have hear of Lorna , and then have see how rich she be ; and never delay in one thing , have open mouth , and swallow she . 
 the Doones , with a share of that dry humour which be in they hereditary , have welcome the two apparitor   if that be the proper name for they   and lead they kindly down the valley , and tell they then to serve their writ . mislike the look of thing , these poor man begin to fumble among their clothe ; upon which the Doones cry , ' off with they ! let we see if your message he on your skin . ' and with no more manner than that , they strip , and lash they out of the valley ; only bid they come to we , if they want Lorna Doone ; and to we they come accordingly . neither be they sure at first but that we should treat they so ; for they have no knowledge of the west country , and think it quite a godless place , wherein no writ be holy . 
 we however comfort and cheer they so considerably , that , in gratitude , they show their writ , to which they have stick like leech . and these be twofold ; one address to Mistress Lorna Doone , so call , and bid she keep in readiness to travel whenever call upon , and commit herself to nobody , except the accredited messenger of the right honourable Court ; while the other be address to all subject of his Majesty , have custody of Lorna Doone , or any power over she . and this last threaten and exhort , and hold out hope of recompense , if she be render truly . my mother and I hold consultation , over both these document , with a mixture of some wrath and fear , and a fork of great sorrow to stir they . and now have Jeremy Stickles 's leave , which he give with a nod when I tell he all , and at last make he understand it , I lay bare to my mother as well what I know , as what I merely surmise , or guess , concern Lorna 's parentage . all this she receive with great tear , and wonder , and fervent thank to God , and still more fervent praise of her son , who have nothing whatever to do with it . however , now the question be , how to act about these writ . and herein it be most unlucky that we could not have Master Stickles , with his knowledge of the world , and especially of the law - court , to advise we what to do , and to help in do it . and firstly of the first I say , ' we have rogue to deal with ; but try we not to rogue they . ' 
 to this , in some measure , dear mother agree , though she could not see the justice of it , yet think that it might be wise , because of our want of practice . and then I say , ' now we be bind to tell Lorna , and to serve her citation upon she , which these good fellow have give we . ' 
 ' then go , and do it thyself , my son , ' mother reply with a mournful smile , misdoubte what the end might be . so I take the slip of brown parchment , and go to seek my darling . 
 Lorna be in her favourite place , the little garden which she tend with such care and diligence . see how the maiden love it , and be happy there , I have labour hard to fence it from the danger of the wood . and here she have correct I , with well taste , and sense of pleasure , and the joy of musing . for I mean to shut out the brook , and build my fence inside of it ; but Lorna say no ; if we must have a fence , which could not but be injury , at any rate leave the stream inside , and a pleasant bank beyond it . and soon I perceive that she be right , though not so much as afterwards ; for the fairest of all thing in a garden , and in summer - time most useful , be a brook of crystal water ; where a man may come and meditate , and the flower may lean and see themselves , and the ray of the sun be purfied . now partly with her own white hand , and partly with Gwenny 's red one , Lorna have make of this sunny spot a haven of beauty to dwell in . it be not only that colour lie in the harmony we would seek of they , neither be it the height of plant , slop to one another ; nor even the delicate tone of foliage follow suit , and neighbouring . even the breathing of the wind , soft and gentle in and out , move thing that need not move , and pass long - stalk one , even this be not enough among the flush of fragrance , to tell a man the reason of his quiet satisfaction . but so it shall for ever be . as the river we float upon   with wine , and flower , and music ,   be nothing at the well - spring but a bubble without reason . 
 feel many thing , but think without much to guide I , over the grass - plat lay between , I go up to Lorna . she in a shower of damask rose , raise her eye and look at I . and even now , in those sweet eye , so deep with loving - kindness , and soft maiden dreaming , there seem to be a slight unwilling , half confess withdrawal ; overcome by love and duty , yet a painful thing to see . 
 ' darling , ' I say , ' be your spirit good ? be you strong enough to - day , to bear a tale of cruel sorrow ; but which perhaps , when your tear be shed , will leave you all the happy ? ' 
 ' what can you mean ? ' she answer trembling , not have be vey strong of late , and now surprised at my manner ; ' be you come to give I up , John ? ' 
 ' not very likely , ' I reply ; ' neither do I hope such a thing would leave you all the happy . oh , Lorna , if you can think that so quickly as you seem to have do , now you have every prospect and strong temptation to it . you be far , far above I in the world , and I have no right to claim you . perhaps , when you have hear these tiding you will say , " John Ridd , begone ; your life and mine be part . " ' 
 ' will I ? ' cry Lorna , with all the brightness of her playful way return : ' you very foolish and jealous John , how shall I punish you for this ? be I to forsake every flower I have , and not even know that the world go round , while I look up at you , the whole day long and say , " John , I love , love , love you ? " ' 
 during these word she lean upon I , half in gay imitation of what I have so often make she do , and half in depth of earnestness , as the thrice - repeat word grow strong , and grow warm , with and to her heart . and as she look up at the finish , say , ' you , ' so musically , I be much inclined to clasp her round ; but remember who she be , forbore ; at which she seem surprised with I . 
 ' Mistress Lorna , I reply , with I know not what temptation , make little of her caress , though more than all my heart to I : ' Mistress Lorna , you must keep your rank and proper dignity . you must never look at I with anything but pity now . ' 
 ' I shall look at you with pity , John , ' say Lorna , try to laugh it off , yet not know what to make of I , ' if you talk any more of this nonsense , know I as you ought to do . I shall even begin to think that you , and your friend , be weary of I , and of so long support I ; and be only seek cause to send I back to my old misery . if it be so , I will go . my life matter little to any one . ' here the great bright tear arise ; but the maiden be too proud to sob . 
 ' Sweetest of all sweet love , ' I cry , for the sign of a tear defeat I ; ' what possibility could make I ever give up Lorna ? ' 
 ' dearest of all dear , ' she answer ; ' if you dearly love I , what possibility could ever make I give you up , dear ? ' 
 upon that there be no more forbear , but I kiss and clasp she , whether she be Countess , or whether Queen of England ; mine she be , at least in heart ; and mine she should be wholly . and she be of the same opinion , nothing be say between we . 
 ' now , Lorna , ' say I , as she hang on my arm , willing to trust I anywhere , ' come to your little plant - house , and hear my moving story . ' 
 ' no story can move I much , dear , ' she answer rather faintly , for any excitement stay with she ; ' since I know your strength of kindness , scarcely any tale can move I , unless it be of yourself , love ; or of my poor mother . ' 
 ' it be of your poor mother , darling . can you bear to hear it ? ' and yet I wonder why she do not say as much of her father . 
 ' yes , I can bear anything . but although I can not see she , and have long forget , I could not bear to hear ill of she . ' 
 ' there be no ill to hear , sweet child , except of evil do to she . Lorna , you be of an ill - star race . ' 
 ' well that than a wicked race , ' she answer with her usual quickness , leap at conclusion ; ' tell I I be not a Doone , and I will but I can not love you more . ' 
 ' you be not a Doone , my Lorna , for that , at least , I can answer ; though I know not what your name be . ' 
 ' and my father your father what I mean be ' 
 ' your father and mine never meet one another . your father be kill by an accident in the pyrenean mountain , and your mother by the Doones ; or at least they cause her death , and carry you away from she . ' 
 all this , come as in one breath upon the sensitive maiden , be more than she could bear all at once ; as any but a fool like I must of course have know . she lie back on the garden bench , with her black hair shed on the oaken bark , while her colour go and come and only by that , and her quiver breath , could any one say that she live and think . and yet she press my hand with her , that I might tell she all of it . 




 CHAPTER LVI 

 JOHN become TOO popular 

 no flower that I have ever see , either in shifting of light and shade , or in the pearly morning , may vie with a fair young woman 's face when tender thought and quick emotion vary , enrich , and beautify it . thus my Lorna hearken softly , almost without word or gesture , yet with sigh and glance tell , and the pressure of my hand , how each word be move she . 
 when at last my tale be do , she turn away , and weep bitterly for the sad fate of her parent . but to my surprise she speak not even a word of wrath or rancour . she seem to take it all as fate . 
 ' Lorna , darling , ' I say at length , for man be more impatient in trial of time than woman be , ' do you not even wish to know what your proper name be ? ' 
 ' how can it matter to I , John ? ' she answer , with a depth of grief which make I seem a trifl . ' it can never matter now , when there be none to share it . ' 
 ' poor little soul ! ' be all I say in a tone of pure pity ; and to my surprise she turn upon I , catch I in her arm , and love I as she have never do before . 
 ' Dearest , I have you , ' she cry ; ' you , and only you , love . have you I want no other . all my life be one with your . oh , John , how can I treat you so ? ' 
 blush through the wet of weeping , and the gloom of ponder , yet she would not hide her eye , but fold I , and dwell on I . 
 ' I can not believe , ' in the pride of my joy , I whisper into one little ear , ' that you could ever so love I , beauty , as to give up the world for I . ' 
 ' would you give up your farm for I , John ? ' cry Lorna , leap back and look , with her wondrous power of light at I ; ' would you give up your mother , your sister , your home , and all that you have in the world and every hope of your life , John ? ' 
 ' of course I would . without two thought . you know it ; you know it , Lorna . ' 
 ' it be true that I do , ' she answer in a tone of deep sadness ; ' and it be this power of your love which have make I love you so . no good can come of it , no good . God 's face be set against selfishness . ' 
 as she speak in that low tone I gaze at the clear line of her face   where every curve be perfect   not with love and wonder only , but with a strange new sense of awe . 
 ' darling , ' I say , ' come near to I . give I surety against that . for God 's sake never frighten I with the thought that he would part we . ' 
 ' do it then so frighten you ? ' she whisper , come close to I ; ' I know it , dear ; I have know it long ; but it never frighten I . it make I sad , and very lonely , till I can remember . ' 
 ' till you can remember what ? ' I ask , with a long , deep shudder ; for we be so superstitious . 
 ' until I do remember , love , that you will soon come back to I , and be my own for ever . this be what I always think of , this be what I hope for . ' 
 although her eye be so glorious , and beam with eternity , this distant sort of beatitude be not much to my liking . I want to have my love on earth ; and my dear wife in my own home ; and child in good time , if God should please to send we any . and then I would be to they , exactly what my father be to I . and beside all this , I doubt much about be fit for heaven ; where no plough be , and no cattle , unless sacrifice bull go thither . 
 therefore I say , ' now kiss I , Lorna ; and do not   talk any nonsense . ' and the darling come and do it ; be kindly obedient , as the other world often make we . 
 ' you sweet love , ' I say at this , be slave to her soft obedience ; ' do you suppose I should be content to leave you until Elysium ? ' 
 ' how on earth can I tell , dear John , what you will be content with ? ' 
 ' you , and only you , ' say I ; ' the whole of it lie in a syllable . now you know my entire want ; and want must be my comfort . ' 
 ' but surely if I have money , sir , and birth , and rank , and all sort of grandeur , you would never dare to think of I . ' 
 she draw herself up with an air of pride , as she gravely pronounce these word , and give I a scornful glance , or try ; and turn away as if to enter some grand coach or palace ; while I be so amazed and grieve in my raw simplicity especially after the way in which she have first receive my news , so loving and warm - hearted , that I never say a word , but star and think , ' how do she mean it ? ' 
 she see the pain upon my forehead , and the wonder in my eye , and leave coach and palace too , back she fly to I in a moment , as simple as simplest milkmaid . 
 ' oh , you fearful stupid , John , you inexpressibly stupid , John , ' she cry with both arm round my neck , and her lip upon my forehead ; ' you have call yourself thick - head , John , and I never would believe it . but now I do with all my heart . will you never know what I be , love ? ' 
 ' no , Lorna , that I never shall . I can understand my mother well , and one at least of my sister , and both the Snowe girl very easily , but you I never understand ; only love you all the more for it . ' 
 ' then never try to understand I , if the result be that , dear John . and yet I be the very simple of all foolish simple creature . Nay , I be wrong ; therein I yield the palm to you , my dear . to think that I can act so ! no wonder they want I in London , as an ornament for the stage , John . ' 
 now in after day , when I hear of Lorna as the rich , and noble , and lovely lady to be find in London , I often remember that little scene , and recall every word and gesture , wonder what lie under it . even now , while it be quite impossible once to doubt those clear deep eye , and the bright lip tremble so ; nevertheless I feel how much the world would have to do with it ; and that the good and true people can not shake themselves quite free . however , for the moment , I be very proud and show it . 
 and herein differ fact from fancy , thing as they befall we from thing as we would have they , human end from human hope ; that the first be move by a thousand and the last on two wheel only , which   be name   be desire and fear . hope of course be nothing more than desire with a telescope , magnify distant matter , overlook near one ; open one eye on the object , close the other to all objection . and if hope be the future tense of desire , the future of fear be religion at least with too many of we . 
 whether I be right or wrong in these small morality , one thing be sure enough , to wit , that hope be the fast traveller , at any rate , in the time of youth . and so I hop that Lorna might be prove of blameless family , and honourable rank and fortune ; and yet none the less for that , love I and belong to I . so I lead she into the house , and she fall into my mother 's arm ; and I leave they to have a good cry of it , with Annie ready to help they . 
 if Master Stickles should not mend enough to gain his speech a little , and declare to we all he know , I be to set out for Watchett , rid upon horseback , and there to hire a cart with wheel , such as we have not begin , as yet , to use on Exmoor . for all our work go on broad wood , with runner and with earthboard ; and many of we still look upon wheel   though mention in the Bible   as the invention of the evil one , and Pharoah 's especial property . 
 now , instead of get well , Colonel Stickles grow bad and bad , in spite of all our tendance of he , with simple and with nourishment , and no poisonous medicine , such as doctor would have give he . and the fault of this lie not with we , but purely with himself and his unquiet constitution . for he rouse himself up to a perfect fever , when through Lizzie 's giddiness he learn the very thing which mother and Annie be hide from he , with the utmost care ; namely , that Sergeant Bloxham have take upon himself to send direct to London by the Chancery officer , a full report of what have happen , and of the illness of his chief , together with an urgent prayer for a full battalion of King 's troop , and a plenary commander . 
 this Sergeant Bloxham , be senior of the survive soldier , and a very worthy man in his way , but a trifle over - zealous , have succeed to the captaincy upon his master 's disablement . then , with desire to serve his country and show his education , he sit up most part of three night , and write this very wonderful report by the aid of our stable lanthorn . it be a very fine piece of work , as three man to whom he read it   but only one at a time   pronounce , be under seal of secrecy . and all might have go well with it , if the author could only have hold his tongue , when near the ear of woman . but this be beyond his sense as it seem , although so good a writer . for have hear that our Lizzie be a famous judge of literature   as indeed she tell almost every one , he could not contain himself , but must have her opinion upon his work . 
 Lizzie sit on a log of wood , and listen with all her ear up , have make proviso that no one else should be there to interrupt she . and she put in a syllable here and there , and many a time she take out one   for the Sergeant overload his gun , more often than undercharge it ; like a liberal man of letter , and then she declare the result so good , so chaste , and the style to be so elegant , and yet so fervent , that the Sergeant break his pipe in three , and fall in love with she on the spot . now this have lead I out of my way ; as thing be always do , partly through their own perverseness , partly through my kind desire to give fair turn to all of they , and to all the people who do they . if any one expect of I a strict and well - drill story , stand ' at attention ' all the time , with hand at the side like two wen on my trunk , and eye go neither right nor leave ; I trow that man have be disappoint many a page ago , and have leave I to my evil way ; and if not , I love his charity . therefore let I seek his grace , and get back , and just begin again . 
 that great despatch be send to London by the Chancery officer , whom we fit up with clothe , and for three day fatten they ; which in strict justice they need much , as well as in point of equity . they be kind enough to be pleased with we , and accept my new shirt generously ; and urgent as their business be , another week   as they both declare   could do no harm to nobody , and might set they upon their leg again . and know , although they be London man , that fish do live in water , these two fellow go fish all day , but never land anything . however , their holiday be cut short ; for the Sergeant , have finish now his narrative of proceeding , be not the man to let it hang fire , and be quench perhaps by Stickles . 
 therefore , have do their business , and serve both citation , these two good man have a pannier of victual put up by dear Annie , and borrow two of our horse , ride to Dunster , where they leave they , and hire on towards London . we have not time to like they much , and so we do not miss they , especially in our great anxiety about poor Master Stickles . 
 Jeremy lie between life and death , for at least a fortnight . if the link of chain have fly upwards   for half a link of chain it be which take he in the mouth so , even one inch upwards , the poor man could have need no one except Parson Bowden ; for the bottom of his skull , which hold the brain as in the egg - cup , must have clean go from he . but strike he horizontally , and a little upon the skew , the metal come out at the back of his neck , and   the powder not be strong , I suppose   it lodge in his leather collar . 
 now the rust of this iron hang in the wound , or at least we think so ; though since I have talk with a man of medicine , I be not so sure of it . and our chief aim be to purge this rust ; when rather we should have stop the hole , and let the oxide do its bad , with a plug of new flesh on both side of it . 
 at last I prevail upon he by argument , that he must get well , to save himself from be ignobly and unjustly supersede ; and hereupon I revile Sergeant Bloxham more fiercely than Jeremy 's self could have do , and indeed to such a pitch that Jeremy almost forgive he , and become much milder . and after that his fever and the inflammation of his wound , diminish very rapidly . 
 however , not know what might happen , or even how soon poor Lorna might be take from our power , and , fall into lawyer ' hand , have cause to wish herself most heartily back among the robber , I set forth one day for Watchett , take advantage of the visit of some trooper from an outpost , who would make our house quite safe . I ride alone , be fully prim , and have no misgiving . for it be say that even the Doones have begin to fear I , since I cast their culverin through the door , as above related ; and they could not but believe , from my be still untouched   although so large an object   in the thickest of their fire , both of gun and cannon , that I must bear a charmed life , proof against ball and bullet . however , I know that Carver Doone be not a likely man to hold any superstitious opinion ; and of he I have an instinctive dread , although quite ready to face he . 
 rid along , I meditate upon Lorna 's history ; how many thing be now begin to unfold themselves , which have be obscure and dark ! for instance , Sir Ensor Doone 's consent , or to say the least his indifference , to her marriage with a yeoman ; which in a man so proud   though die   have greatly puzzle both of we . but now , if she not only prove to be no grandchild of the Doone , but even descend from his enemy , it be natural enough that he should feel no great repugnance to her humiliation . and that Lorna 's father have be a foe to the house of Doone I gather from her mother 's cry when she behold their leader . moreover that fact would supply their motive in carry off the unfortunate little creature , and rear she among they , and as one of their own family ; yet hide her true birth from she . she be a ' great card , ' as we say , when play all - four at Christmas - time ; and if one of they could marry she , before she learn of right and wrong , vast property , enough to buy pardon for a thousand Doones , would be at their mercy . and since I be come to know Lorna well , and she to know I thoroughly many thing have be outspoken , which her early bashfulness have keep cover from I . attempt I mean to pledge her love to this one , or that other ; some of which perhaps might have be successful , if there have not be too many . 
 and then , as her beauty grow rich and bright , Carver Doone be smite strongly , and would hear of no one else as a suitor for she ; and by the terror of his claim drive off all the other . here too may the explanation of a thing which seem to be against the law of human nature , and upon which I long , but dare not to cross - question Lorna . how could such a lovely girl , although so young , and brave , and distant , have escape the vile affection of a lawless company ? 
 but now it be as clear as need be . for any proven violence would have utterly vitiate all claim upon her grand estate ; at least as those claim must be urge before a court of equity . and therefore all the elder   with view upon her real estate   keep strict watch on the younger , who confine their view to her personality . 
 now I do not mean to say that all this , or the hundred other thing which come , crowd consideration , be half as plain to I at the time , as I have set they down above . far be it from I to deceive you so . no doubt my thought be then dark and hazy , like an oil - lamp full of fungus ; and I have trim they , as when they burn , with scissor sharpen long afterwards . all I mean to say be this , that jog along to a certain tune of the horse 's foot , which we call ' three - halfpence and twopence , ' I see my way a little into some thing which have puzzle I . 
 when I knock at the little door , whose sill be gritty and grime with sand , no one come for a very long time to answer I , or to let I in . not wish to be unmannerly , I wait a long time , and watch the sea , from which the wind be blow ; and whose many lip of wave though the tide be half - way out speak to and refresh I . after a while I knock again , for my horse be become hungry ; and a good while after that again , a voice come through the key - hole , 
 ' who be that wish to enter ? ' 
 ' the boy who be at the pump , ' say I , ' when the carriage break down at Dulverton . the boy that live at oh ah ; and some day you would come seek for he . ' 
 ' oh , yes , I remember certainly . my leetle boy , with the fair white skin . I have desire to see he , oh many , yes , many time . ' 
 she be open the door , while say this , and then she start back in affright that the little boy should have grow so . 
 ' you can not be that leetle boy . it be quite impossible . why do you impose on I ? ' 
 ' not only be I that little boy , who make the water to flow for you , till the nebule come upon the glass ; but also I be come to tell you all about your little girl . ' 
 ' come in , you very great leetle boy , ' she answer , with her dark eye brighten . and I go in , and look at she . she be alter by time , as much as I be . the slight and graceful shape be go ; not that I remember anything of her figure , if you please ; for boy of twelve be not yet prone to note the shape of woman ; but that her lithe straight gait have strike I as be so unlike our people . now her time for walk so be past , and transmit to her child . yet her face be comely still , and full of strong intelligence . I gaze at she , and she at I ; and we be sure of one another . 
 ' now what will ye please to eat ? ' she ask , with a lively glance at the size of my mouth : ' that be always the first thing you people ask , in these barbarous place . ' 
 ' I will tell you by - and - by , ' I answer , mislike this satire upon we ; ' but I might begin with a quart of ale , to enable I to speak , madam . ' 
 ' very well . one quevart of be - or ; ' she call out to a little maid , who be her eld child , no doubt . ' it be to be expect , sir . be - or , be - or , be - or , all day long , with you englishman ! ' 
 ' Nay , ' I reply , ' not all day long , if madam will excuse I . only a pint at breakfast - time , and a pint and a half at eleven o'clock , and a quart or so at dinner . and then no more till the afternoon ; and half a gallon at supper - time . no one can object to that . ' 
 ' well , I suppose it be right , ' she say , with an air of resignation ; ' God know . but I do not understand it . it be " good for business , " as you say , to preclude everything . ' 
 ' and it be good for we , madam , ' I answer with indignation , for beer be my favourite beverage ; ' and I be a credit to beer , madam ; and so be all who trust to it . ' 
 ' at any rate , you be , young man . if beer have make you grow so large , I will put my child upon it ; it be too late for I to begin . the smell to I be hateful . '
