 now I only set down that to show how perverse those foreign people be . they will drink their wretched heartless stuff , such as they call claret , or wine of Medoc , or Bordeaux , or what not , with no more meaning than sour rennet , stir with the pulp from the cider press , and strain through the cap of our Betty . this be very well for they ; and as good as they deserve , no doubt , and mean perhaps by the will of God , for those unhappy native . but to bring it over to England and set it against our home - brew ale   not to speak of wine from Portugal   and sell it at ten time the price , as a cure for british bile , and a great enlightenment ; this I say be the vile feature of the age we live in . 
 Madam Benita Odam for the name of the man who turn the wheel prove to be John Odam show I into a little room contain two chair and a fir - wood table , and sit down on a three - legged seat and study I very steadfastly . this she have a right to do ; and I , have all my clothe on now , be not disconcert . it would not become I to repeat her judgment upon my appearance , which she deliver as calmly as if I be a pig at market , and as proudly as if her own pig . and she ask I whether I have ever get rid of the black mark on my breast . 
 not want to talk about myself   though very fond of do so , when time and season favour   I lead she back to that fearful night of the day when first I have see she . she be not desirous to speak of it , because of her own little child ; however , I draw she gradually to recollection of Lorna , and then of the little boy who die , and the poor mother bury with he . and her strong hot nature kindle , as she dwell upon these thing ; and my wrath wax within I ; and we forget reserve and prudence under the sense of so vile a wrong . she tell I   as nearly as might be   the very same story which she have tell to Master Jeremy Stickles ; only she dwell upon it more , because of my know the outset . and be a woman , with an inkling of my situation , she enlarge upon the little maid , more than to dry Jeremy . 
 ' would you know she again ? ' I ask , be stir by these account of Lorna , when she be five year old : ' would you know she as a full - grown maiden ? ' 
 ' I think I should , ' she answer ; ' it be not possible to say until one see the person ; but from the eye of the little girl , I think that I must know she . oh , the poor young creature ! be it to be believe that the cannibal devour she ! what a people you be in this country ! meat , meat , meat ! ' 
 as she raise her hand and eye in horror at our carnivorous propensity , to which she clearly attribute the disappearance of Lorna , I could scarce help laugh , even after that sad story . for though it be say at the present day , and will doubtless be say hereafter , that the Doones have devour a baby once , as they come up Porlock hill , after fight hard in the market - place , I know that the tale be utterly false ; for cruel and brutal as they be , their taste be very correct and choice , and indeed one might say fastidious . nevertheless I could not stop to argue that matter with she . 
 ' the little maid have not be devour , ' I say to Mistress Odam : ' and now she be a tall young lady , and as beautiful as can be . if I sleep in your good hostel to - night after go to Watchett town , will you come with I to oare to - morrow , and see your little maiden ? ' 
 ' I would like and yet I fear . this country be so barbarous . and I be good to eat my God , there be much picking on my bone ! ' 
 she survey herself with a glance so mingled of pity and admiration , and the truth of her word be so apparent   only that it would have take a week to get at the bone , before pick   that I nearly lose good manner ; for she really seem to suspect even I of cannibal inclination . however , at last I make her promise to come with I on the morrow , presume that Master Odam could by any mean be persuade to keep her company in the cart , as propriety demand . have little doubt that Master Odam be entirely at his wife 's command , I look upon that matter as settle , and set off for Watchett , to see the grave of Lorna 's poor mother , and to hire a cart for the morrow . 
 and here   as so often happen with man   I succeed without any trouble or hindrance , where I have look for both of they , namely , in find a suitable cart ; whereas the other matter , in which I could have expect no difficulty , come very near to defeat I . for when I hear that Lorna 's father be the Earl of Dugal as Benita impress upon I with a strong enforcement , as much as to say , ' who be you , young man , to come even ask about she ? ' then I never think but that everybody in Watchett town must know all about the tombstone of the Countess of Dugal . 
 this , however , prove otherwise . for Lord Dugal have never live at Watchett Grange , as their place be call ; neither have his name become familiar as its owner . because the Grange have only devolve to he by will , at the end of a long entail , when the last of the Fitz - Pains die out ; and though he like the idea of it , he have go abroad , without take seisin . and upon news of his death , John Jones , a rich gentleman from Llandaff , have take possession , as next of right , and hush up all the story . and though , even at the bad of time , a lady of high rank and wealth could not be rob , and as bad as murder , and then bury in a little place , without move some excitement , yet it have be give out , on purpose and with diligence , that this be only a foreign lady travel for her health and pleasure , along the seacoast of England . and as the poor thing never speak , and several of her servant and her baggage look so foreign , and she herself die in a collar of lace unlike any make in England , all Watchett , without hesitation , pronounce she to be a foreigner . and the English serve man and maid , who might have clear up everything , either be bribe by Master Jones , or else decamp of their own accord with the relic of the baggage . so the poor Countess of Dugal , almost in sight of her own grand house , be bury in an unknown grave , with her pair of infant , without a plate , without a tombstone   bad than all   without a tear , except from the hire italian woman . 
 surely my poor Lorna come of an ill - star family . 
 now in spite of all this , if I have only take Benita with I , or even tell she what I wish , and crave her direction , there could have be no trouble . but I do assure you that among the stupid people at Watchett   compare with whom our folk of Oare , exceed dense though be , be as Hamlet against Dogberry   what with one of they and another , and the firm conviction of all the town that I could be come only to wrestle , I do assure you   as I say before   that my wit almost go out of I . and what vex I yet more about it be , that I see my own mistake , in come myself to seek out the matter , instead of send some unknown person . for my face and form be know at that time   and still be so   to nine people out of every ten live in forty mile of I . not through any excellence , or anything of good desert , in either the one or the other , but simply because folk will be fool on the rivalry of wrestling . the art be a fine one in itself , and demand a little wit of brain , as well as strength of body ; it bind the man who study it to temperance , and chastity , to self - respect , and most of all to an even and sweet temper ; for I have throw strong man than myself   when I be a mere sapling , and before my strength grow hard on I   through their loss of temper . but though the art be an honest one , surely they who excel therein have a right   like all the rest of man - kind   to their own private life . 
 be that either way and I will not speak too strongly , for fear of indulge my own annoyance anyhow , all Watchett town care ten time as much to see John Ridd , as to show he what he want . I be lead to every public - house , instead of to the churchyard ; and twenty table be ready for I , in lieu of a single gravestone . ' Zummerzett thou bee'st , Jan Ridd , and Zummerzett thou shalt be . Thee carl theezell a Davonsheer man ! Whoy , thee live in Zummerzett ; and in Zummerzett thee wast barn , lad . ' and so it go on , till I be weary ; though very much oblige to they . 
 Dull and solid as I be , and with a wild duck wait for I at good Mistress Odam 's , I see that there be nothing for it but to yield to these good people , and prove I a man of Somerset , by eat a dinner at their expense . as for the churchyard , none would hear of it ; and I grieve for broach the matter . 
 but how be I to meet Lorna again , without have do the thing of all thing which I have promise to see to ? it would never do to tell she that so great be my popularity , and so strong the desire to feed I , that I could not attend to her mother . Least of all could I say that every one in Watchett know John Ridd ; while none have hear of the Countess of Dugal . and yet that be about the truth , as I hint very delicately to mistress Odam that evening . but she   be vex about her wild duck , and not have english idea on the matter of sport , and so on   make a poor unwitting face at I . nevertheless Master Odam restore I to my self - respect ; for he star at I till I go to bed ; and he break his hose with excitement . for be in the leg - line myself , I want to know what the muscle be of a man who turn a wheel all day . I have never see a treadmill   though they have one now at Exeter , and it touch I much to learn whether it be good exercise . and herein , from what I see of Odam , I incline to think that it do great harm ; as move the muscle too much in a line , and without variety . 




 CHAPTER LVII 

 LORNA KNOWS she nurse 

 have obtain from Benita Odam a very close and full description of the place where her poor mistress lay , and the mark whereby to know it , I hasten to Watchett the follow morning , before the sun be up , or any people be about . and so , without interruption , I be in the churchyard at sunrise . 
 in the farth and dark nook , overgrow with grass , and overhang by a weeping - tree a little bank of earth betoken the rounding off of a hapless life . there be nothing to tell of rank , or wealth , of love , or even pity ; nameless as a peasant lie the last   as suppose   of a mighty race . only some unskilful hand , probably Master Odam 's under his wife 's teaching , have carve a rude L. , and a ruder D. , upon a large pebble from the beach , and set it up as a headstone . 
 I gather a little grass for Lorna and a sprig of the weeping - tree , and then return to the Forest Cat , as Benita 's lonely inn be call . for the way be long from Watchett to Oare ; and though you may ride it rapidly , as the Doones have do on that fatal night , to travel on wheel , with one horse only , be a matter of time and of prudence . therefore , we set out pretty early , three of we and a baby , who could not well be leave behind . the wife of the man who own the cart have undertake to mind the business , and the other baby , upon condition of have the key of all the tap leave with she . 
 as the manner of journeying over the moor have be describe oft enough already , I will say no more , except that we all arrive before dusk of the summer 's day , safe at Plover 's barrow . Mistress Benita be delighted with the change from her dull hard life ; and she make many excellent observation , such as seem natural to a foreigner look at our country . 
 as luck would have it , the first who come to meet we at the gate be Lorna , with nothing whatever upon her head   the weather be summerly   but her beautiful hair shed round she ; and wear a sweet white frock tuck in , and show her figure perfectly . in her joy she run straight up to the cart ; and then stop and gaze at Benita . at one glance her old nurse know she : ' oh , the eye , the eye ! ' she cry , and be over the rail of the cart in a moment , in spite of all her substance . Lorna , on the other hand , look at she with some doubt and wonder , as though have right to know much about she , and yet unable to do so . but when the foreign woman say something in roman language , and fling new hay from the cart upon she , as if in a romp of childhood , the young maid cry , ' oh , Nita , Nita ! ' and fall upon her breast , and weep ; and after that look round at we . 
 this be so , there could be no doubt as to the power of prove Lady Lorna 's birth , and right , both by evidence and token . for though we have not the necklace now thank to Annie 's wisdom we have the ring of heavy gold , a very ancient relic , with which my maid   in her simple way   have pledge herself to I . and Benita know this ring as well as she know her own finger , have hear a long history about it ; and the effigy on it of the wild cat be the bearing of the house of Lorne . 
 for though Lorna 's father be a nobleman of high and goodly lineage , her mother be of yet more ancient and renowned descent , be the last in line direct from the great and kingly chief of Lorne . a wild and headstrong race they be , and must have everything their own way . hot blood be ever among they , even of one household ; and their sovereignty   which more than once have defy the King of Scotland   wan and fall among themselves , by continual quarrelling . and it be of a piece with this , that the Doones   who be an offset , by the mother 's side , hold in co - partnership some large property , which have come by the spindle , as we say   should fall out with the Earl of Lorne , the last but one of that title . 
 the daughter of this nobleman have marry Sir Ensor Doone ; but this , instead of healing matter , lead to fiercer conflict . I never could quite understand all the in and out of it ; which none but a lawyer may go through , and keep his head at the end of it . the motif of mankind be plain than the motion they produce . especially when charity   such as find among we   sit to judge the former , and be never weary of it ; while reason do not care to trace the latter complication , except for fee or title . 
 therefore it be enough to say , that know Lorna to be direct in heirship to vast property , and bear especial spite against the house of which she be the last , the Doones have bring she up with full intention of lawful marriage ; and have carefully seclude she from the wild of their young gallant . of course , if they have be next in succession , the child would have go down the waterfall , to save any further trouble ; but there be an intercept branch of some honest family ; and they be outlaw , would have a poor chance   though the law love outlaw   against they . only Lorna be of the stock ; and Lorna they must marry . and what a triumph against the old earl , for a cursed Doone to succeed he ! 
 as for their outlawry , great robbery , and grand murder , the veri child , nowadays , must know that money heal the whole of that . even if they have murder people of a good position , it would only cost about twice as much to prove their motif loyal . but they have never slay any man above the rank of yeoman ; and folk even say that my father be the high of their victim ; for the death of Lorna 's mother and brother be never set to their account . 
 pure pleasure it be to any man , to reflect upon all these thing . how truly we discern clear justice , and how well we deal it . if any poor man steal a sheep , have ten child starve , and regard it as mountain game   as a rich man do a hare , to the gallow with he . if a man of rank beat down a door , smite the owner upon the head , and honour the wife with attention , it be a thing to be grateful for , and to slouch smitten head the low . 
 while we be full of all these thing , and wonder what would happen next , or what we ought ourselves to do , another very important matter call for our attention . this be no less than Annie 's marriage to the Squire Faggus . we have try to put it off again ; for in spite of all advantage , neither my mother nor myself have any real heart for it . not that we dwell upon Tom 's short - coming or rather perhaps his go too far , at the time when he work the road so . all that be cover by the King 's pardon , and universal respect of the neighbourhood . but our scruple be this and the more we talk the more it grow upon we that we both have great misgiving as to his future steadiness . 
 for it would be a thousand pity , we say , for a fine , well - grow , and pretty maiden   such as our Annie be , useful too , in so many way , and lively , and warm - hearted , and mistress of 500 pound , to throw herself away on a man with a kind of a turn for drinking . if that last be even hint , Annie would be most indignant , and ask , with cheek as red as rose , who have ever see Master Faggus any the bad for liquor indeed ? her own opinion be , in truth , that he take a great deal too little , after all his hard work , and hard riding , and come over the hill to be insult ! and if ever it lie in her power , and with no one to grudge he his trumpery glass , she would see that poor Tom have the nourishment which his cough and his lung require . 
 his lung be quite as sound as mine , this matter be out of all argument ; so mother and I look at one another , as much as to say , ' let she go upstairs , she will cry and come down more reasonable . ' and while she be go , we use to say the same thing over and over again ; but without perceive a cure for it . and we almost always finish up with the following reflection , which sometimes come from mother 's lip , and sometimes from my own : ' well , well , there be no telling . none can say how a man may alter ; when he take to matrimony . but if we could only make Annie promise to be a little firm with he ! ' 
 I fear that all this talk on our part only hurry matter forward , Annie be more determined every time we pity she . and at last Tom Faggus come , and speak as if he be on the King 's road , with a pistol at my head , and one at mother 's . ' no more fast and loose , ' he cry . ' either one thing or the other . I love the maid , and she love I ; and we will have one another , either with your leave , or without it . how many more time be I to dance over these vile hill , and leave my business , and get nothing more than a sigh or a kiss , and " Tom , I must wait for mother " ? you be famous for be straightforward , you Ridds . just treat I as I would treat you now . ' 
 I look at my mother ; for a glance from she would have send Tom out of the window ; but she check I with her hand , and say , ' you have some ground of complaint , sir ; I will not deny it . now I will be as straight - forward with you , as even a Ridd be suppose to be . my son and myself have all along dislike your marriage with Annie . not for what you have be so much , as for what we fear you will be . have patience , one moment , if you please . we do not fear your taking to the highway life again ; for that you be too clever , no doubt , now that you have property . but we fear that you will take to drinking , and to squander money . there be many example of this around we ; and we know what the fate of the wife be . it have be hard to tell you this , under our own roof , and with our own ' here mother hesitate . 
 ' spirit , and cider , and beer , ' I break in ; ' out with it , like a Ridd , mother ; as he will have all of it . ' 
 ' spirit , and cider , and beer , ' say mother very firmly after I ; and then she give way and say , ' you know , Tom , you be welcome to every drop and more of it . ' 
 now Tom must have have a far sweet temper than ever I could claim ; for I should have thrust my glass away , and never have take another drop in the house where such a check have meet I . but instead of that , Master Faggus reply , with a pleasant smile , 
 ' I know that I be welcome , good mother ; and to prove it , I will have some more . ' 
 and thereupon be mix himself another glass of holland with lemon and hot water , yet pour it very delicately . 
 ' oh , I have be so miserable take a little more , Tom , ' say mother , hand the bottle . 
 ' yes , take a little more , ' I say ; ' you have mix it over weak , Tom . ' 
 ' if ever there be a sober man , ' cry Tom , comply with our request ; ' if ever there be in Christendom a man of perfect sobriety , that man be now before you . shall we say to - morrow week , mother ? it will suit your washing day . ' 
 ' how very thoughtful you be , Tom ! now John would never have think of that , in spite of all his steadiness . ' 
 ' certainly not , ' I answer proudly ; ' when my time come for Lorna , I shall not study Betty Muxworthy . ' 
 in this way the Squire get over we ; and Farmer Nicholas Snowe be send for , to counsel with mother about the matter and to set his two daughter sew . 
 when the time for the wedding come , there be such a stir and commotion as have never be know in the parish of Oare since my father 's marriage . for Annie 's beauty and kindliness have make she the pride of the neighbourhood ; and the present send she , from all around , be enough to stock a shop with . Master Stickles , who now could walk , and who certainly owe his recovery , with the blessing of God , to Annie , present she with a mighty Bible , silver - clasp , and very handsome , beat the parson 's out and out , and for which he have send to Taunton . even the common trooper , have taste her cookery many time   to help out their poor ration , club together , and must have give at least a week 's pay apiece , to have turn out what they do for she . this be no less than a silver pot , well - design , but suit surely rather to the bridegroom 's taste than bride 's . in a word , everybody give her thing . 
 and now my Lorna come to I , with a spring of tear in appeal eye for she be still somewhat childish , or rather , I should say , more childish now than when she live in misery and she place her little hand in mine , and she be half afraid to speak , and drop her eye for I to ask . 
 ' what be it , little darling ? ' I ask , as I see her breath come fast ; for the small emotion move her form . 
 ' you do not   think , John , you do not   think , dear , that you could lend I any money ? ' 
 ' all I have get , ' I answer ; ' how much do you want , dear heart ? ' 
 ' I have be calculate ; and I fear that I can not do any good with less than ten pound , John . ' 
 here she look up at I , with horror at the grandeur of the sum , and not know what I could think of it . but I keep my eye from she . ' ten pound ! ' I say in my deep voice , on purpose to have it out in comfort , when she should be frightened ; ' what can you want with ten pound , child ? ' 
 ' that be my concern , say Lorna , pluck up her spirit at this : ' when a lady ask for a loan , no gentleman pry into the cause of she ask it . ' 
 ' that may be as may be , ' I answer in a judicial manner ; ' ten pound , or twenty , you shall have . but I must know the purport . ' 
 ' then that you never shall know , John . I be very sorry for ask you . it be not of the small consequence . oh , dear , no . ' herewith she be run away . 
 ' oh , dear , yes , ' I reply ; ' it be of very great consequence ; and I understand the whole of it . you want to give that stupid Annie , who have lose you a hundred thousand pound , and who be go to be marry before we , dear God only can tell why , be my young sister you want to give she a wedding present . and you shall do it , darling ; because it be so good of you . do not   you know your title , love ? how humble you be with we humble folk . you be Lady Lorna something , so far as I can make out yet : and you ought not even to speak to we . you will go away and disdain we . ' 
 ' if you please , talk not like that , John . I will have nothing to do with it , if it come between you and I , John . ' 
 ' you can not help yourself , ' say i. and then she vow that she could and would . and rank and birth be banish from between our lip in no time . 
 ' what can I get she good enough ? I be sure I do not know , ' she ask : ' she have be so kind and good to I , and she be such a darling . how I shall miss she , to be sure ! by the bye , you seem to think , John , that I shall be rich some day . ' 
 ' of course you will . as rich as the french King who keep ours . would the Lord Chancellor trouble himself about you , if you be poor ? ' 
 ' then if I be rich , perhaps you would lend I twenty pound , dear John . ten pound would be very mean for a wealthy person to give she . ' 
 to this I agree , upon condition that I should make the purchase myself , whatever it might be . for nothing could be easy than to cheat Lorna about the cost , until time should come for she pay I . and this be well than to cheat she for the benefit of our family . for this end , and for many other , I set off to Dulverton , bear more commission , more message , and more question than a man of thrice my memory might carry so far as the corner where the sawpit be . and to make thing bad , one girl or other would keep on run up to I , or even after I   when start   with something or other she have just think of , which she could not possibly do without , and which I must be sure to remember , as the most important of the whole . 
 to my dear mother , who have partly outlive the exceed value of trifle , the most important matter seem to ensure Uncle Reuben 's countenance and presence at the marriage . and if I succeed in this , I might well forget all the maiden ' trumpery . this she would have be wise to tell I when they be out of hearing ; for I leave she to fight her own battle with they ; and laugh at her predicament , promise to do the good I could for all , so far as my wit would go . 
 Uncle Reuben be not at home , but Ruth , who receive I very kindly , although without any expression of joy , be sure of his return in the afternoon , and persuade I to wait for he . and by the time that I have finish all I could recollect of my order , even with paper to help I , the old gentleman ride into the yard , and be more surprised than pleased to see I . but if he be surprised , I be more than that I be utterly astonish at the change in his appearance since the last time I have see he . from a hale , and rather heavy man , gray - haired , but plump , and ruddy , he be alter to a shrunken , wizene , tremble , and almost decrepit figure . instead of curly and comely lock , grizzle indeed , but plentiful , he have only a few lank white hair scatter and flatten upon his forehead . but the great change of all be in the expression of his eye , which have be so keen , and restless , and bright , and a little sarcastic . bright indeed they still be , but with a slow unhealthy lustre ; their keenness be turn to perpetual outlook , their restlessness to a haggard want . as for the humour which once gleam there   which people who fear it call sarcasm   it have be succeed by stare of terror , and then mistrust , and shrink . there be none of the interest in mankind , which be needful even for satire . 
 ' now what can this be ? ' think I to myself , ' have the old man lose all his property , or take too much to strong water ? ' 
 ' come inside , John Ridd , ' he say ; ' I will have a talk with you . it be cold out here ; and it be too light . come inside , John Ridd , boy . ' 
 I follow he into a little dark room , quite different from Ruth Huckaback 's . it be close from the shop by an old division of boarding , hang with tan canvas ; and the smell be very close and faint . here there be a ledger desk , and a couple of chair , and a long - legged stool . 
 ' take the stool , ' say Uncle Reuben , show I in very quietly , ' it be fitter for your height , John . wait a moment ; there be no hurry . ' 
 then he slip out by another door , and close it quickly after he , tell the foreman and waiting - man that the business of the day be do . they have well all go home at once ; and he would see to the fastening . of course they be only too glad to go ; but I wonder at his send they , with at least two hour of daylight leave . 
 however , that be no business of mine , and I wait , and ponder whether fair Ruth ever come into this dirty room , and if so , how she keep her hand from it . for Annie would have have it upside down in about two minute , and scrub , and brush , and dust , until it look quite another place ; and yet all this do without scold and crossness ; which be the curse of clean woman , and ten time bad than the dusty dust . 
 Uncle Ben come reel in , not from any power of liquor , but because he be stiff from horseback , and weak from work and worry . 
 ' let I be , John , let I be , ' he say , as I go to help he ; ' this be an unkind dreary place ; but many a hundred of good gold Carolus have be turn in this place , John . ' 
 ' not a doubt about it , sir , ' I answer in my loud and cheerful manner ; ' and many another hundred , sir ; and may you long enjoy they ! ' 
 ' my boy , do you wish I to die ? ' he ask , come up close to my stool , and regard I with a shrewd though blear - eyed gaze ; ' many do . do you , John ? ' 
 ' come , ' say I , ' do not   ask such nonsense . you know well than that , Uncle Ben . or else , I be sorry for you . I want you to live as long as possible , for the sake of ' here I stop . 
 ' for the sake of what , John ? I know it be not for my own sake . for the sake of what , my boy ? ' 
 ' for the sake of Ruth , ' I answer ; ' if you must have all the truth . who be to mind she when you be go ? ' 
 ' but if you know that I have gold , or a manner of get gold , far more than ever the sailor get out of the spanish galleon , far more than ever be hear of ; and the secret be to be your , John ; your after I and no other soul 's then you would wish I dead , John . ' here he eye I as if a speck of dust in my eye should not escape he . 
 ' you be wrong , Uncle Ben ; altogether wrong . for all the gold ever hear or dream of , not a wish would cross my heart to rob you of one day of life . ' 
 at last he move his eye from mine ; but without any word , or sign , to show whether he believe , or disbelieve . then he go to a chair , and sit with his chin upon the ledger - desk ; as if the effort of probe I have be too much for his weary brain . ' dream of ! all the gold ever dream of ! as if it be but a dream ! ' he mutter ; and then he close his eye to think . 
 ' Good Uncle Reuben , ' I say to he , ' you have be a long way to - day , sir . let I go and get you a glass of good wine . Cousin Ruth know where to find it . ' 
 ' how do you know how far I have be ? ' he ask , with a vicious look at I . ' and Cousin Ruth ! you be very pat with my granddaughter 's name , young man ! ' 
 ' it would be hard upon I , sir , not to know my own cousin 's name . ' 
 ' very well . let that go by . you have behave very badly to Ruth . she love you ; and you love she not . ' 
 at this I be so wholly amazed not at the thing itself , I mean , but at his knowledge of it that I could not say a single word ; but look , no doubt , very foolish . 
 ' you may well be ashamed , young man , ' he cry , with some triumph over I , ' you be the big of all fool , as well as a conceited coxcomb . what can you want more than Ruth ? she be a little damsel , truly ; but fine man than you , John Ridd , with all your boasted strength and wrestling , have wed small maiden . and as for quality , and value bot ! one inch of Ruth be worth all your seven foot put together . ' 
 now I be not seven foot high ; nor ever be six foot eight inch , in my very prime of life ; and nothing vex I so much as to make I out a giant , and above human sympathy , and human scale of weakness . it cost I hard to hold my tongue ; which luckily be not in proportion to my stature . and only for Ruth 's sake I hold it . but Uncle Ben   be old and wear   be vex by not have any answer , almost as much as a woman be . 
 ' you want I to go on , ' he continue , with a look of spite at I , ' about my poor Ruth 's love for you , to feed your cursed vanity . because a set of ass call you the fine man in England ; there be no maid   I suppose   who be not in love with you . I believe you be as deep as you be long , John Ridd . shall I ever get to the bottom of your character ? ' 
 this be a little too much for I . any insult I could take   with goodwill   from a white - haired man , and one who be my relative ; unless it touch my love for Lorna , or my conscious modesty . now both of these be touch to the quick by the sentence of the old gentleman . therefore , without a word , I go ; only make a bow to he . 
 but woman who be   beyond all doubt   the mother of all mischief , also nurse that babe to sleep , when he be too noisy . and there be Ruth , as I take my horse   with a trunk of frippery on he , poor little Ruth be at the bridle , and rust all the knop of our town - go harness with tear . 
 ' good - bye dear , ' I say , as she bend her head away from I ; ' shall I put you up on the saddle , dear ? ' 
 ' Cousin Ridd , you may take it lightly , ' say Ruth , turn full upon I , ' and very likely you be right , accord to your nature ' this be the only cut thing the little soul ever say to I ' but oh , Cousin Ridd , you have no idea of the pain you will leave behind you . ' 
 ' how can that be so , Ruth , when I be as good as order to be off the premise ? ' 
 ' in the first place , Cousin Ridd , grandfather will be angry with himself , for have so ill - use you . and now he be so weak and poorly , that he be always repent . in the next place I shall scold he first , until he admit his sorrow ; and when he have admit it , I shall scold myself for scold he . and then he will come round again , and think that I be hard on he ; and end perhaps by hat you for he be like a woman now , John . ' 
 that last little touch of self - knowledge in Ruth , which she deliver with a gleam of some secret pleasantry , make I stop and look closely at she : but she pretend not to know it . ' there be something in this child , ' I think , ' ve ry different from other girl . what it be I can not tell ; for one very seldom get at it . ' 
 at any rate the upshot be that the good horse go back to stable , and have another feed of corn , while my wrath sink within I . there be two thing , accord to my experience   which may not hold with another man   fit beyond any other to take hot temper out of we . the first be to see our favourite creature feeding , and lick up their food , and happily snuffle over it , yet spar time to be grateful , and show taste and perception ; the other be to go garden boldly , in the spring of the year , without any misgiving about it , and hop the utmost of everything . if there be a third anodyne , approach these two in power , it be to smoke good tobacco well , and watch the setting of the moon ; and if this should only be over the sea , the result be irresistible . 
 Master Huckaback show no especial sign of joy at my return ; but receive I with a little grunt , which appear to I to mean , ' ah , I think he would hardly be fool enough to go . ' I tell he how sorry I be for have in some way offend he ; and he answer that I do well to grieve for one at least of my offence . to this I make no reply , as behove a man deal with cross and fractious people ; and presently he become well - temper , and send little Ruth for a bottle of wine . she give I a beautiful smile of thank for my forbearance as she pass ; and I know by her manner that she would bring the good bottle in all the cellar . 
 as I have but little time to spare   although the day be long and light   we be force to take our wine with promptitude and rapidity ; and whether this loosen my uncle 's tongue , or whether he mean beforehand to speak , be now almost uncertain . but true it be that he bring his chair very near to mine , after three or four glass , and send Ruth away upon some errand which seem of small importance . at this I be vex , for the room always look so different without she . 
 ' come , Jack , ' he say , ' here be your health , young fellow , and a good and obedient wife to you . not that your wife will ever obey you though ; you be much too easy - temper . even a bitter and stormy woman might live in peace with you , Jack . but never you give she the chance to try . marry some sweet little thing , if you can . if not , do not   marry any . ah , we have the maid to suit you , my lad , in this old town of Dulverton . ' 
 ' have you so , sir ? but perhaps the maid might have no desire to suit I . ' 
 ' that you may take my word she have . the colour of this wine will prove it . the little sly hussy have be to the cobwebbed arch of the cellar , where she have no right to go , for any one under a magistrate . however , I be glad to see it , and we will not spare it , John . after my time , somebody , whoever marry little Ruth , will find some rare wine there , I trow , and perhaps not know the difference . ' 
 think of this the old man sigh , and expect I to sigh after he . but a sigh be not   like a yawn   infectious ; and we be all more prone to be send to sleep than to sorrow by one another . not but what a sigh sometimes may make we think of sigh . 
 ' well , sir , ' cry I , in my sprightly manner , which rouse up most people , ' here be to your health and dear little Ruth 's : and may you live to knock off the cobwebs from every bottle in under the arch . Uncle Reuben , your life and health , sir ? ' 
 with that I take my glass thoughtfully , for it be wondrous good ; and Uncle Ben be pleased to see I dwell pleasantly on the subject with parenthesis , and self - commune , and oral judgment unpronounce , though smacking of fine decision . ' Curia vult advisari , ' as the lawyer say ; which mean , ' let we have another glass , and then we can think about it . ' 
 ' come now , John , ' say Uncle Ben , lay his wrinkle hand on my knee , when he see that none could heed we , ' I know that you have a sneaking fondness for my grandchild Ruth . do not   interrupt I now ; you have ; and to deny it will only provoke I . ' 
 ' I do like Ruth , sir , ' I say boldly , for fear of misunderstanding ; ' but I do not love she . ' 
 ' very well ; that make no difference . like may very soon be love   as some people call it   when the maid have money to help she . ' 
 ' but if there be , as there be in my case ' 
 ' once for all , John , not a word . I do not attempt to lead you into any engagement with little Ruth ; neither will I blame you   though I may be disappoint   if no such engagement should ever be . but whether you will have my grandchild , or whether you will not and such a chance be rarely offer to a fellow of your standing ' Uncle Ben despise all farmer ' in any case I have at least resolve to let you know my secret ; and for two good reason . the first be that it wear I out to dwell upon it , all alone , and the second be that I can trust you to fulfil a promise . moreover , you be my next of kin , except among the womankind ; and you be just the man I want , to help I in my enterprise . ' 
 ' and I will help you , sir , ' I answer , fear some conspiracy , ' in anything that be true , and loyal , and accord to the law of the realm . ' 
 ' ha , ha ! ' cry the old man , laugh until his eye run over , and spread out his skinny hand upon his shin breech , ' thou hast go the same fool ' track as the rest ; even as spy Stickles go , and all his precious trooper . land of arm at Glenthorne , and Lynmouth , wagon escort across the moor , sound of metal and booming noise ! ah , but we manage it cleverly , to cheat even those so near to we . disaffection at Taunton , sign of insurrection at Dulverton , revolutionary tanner at Dunster ! we set it all abroad , right well . and not even you to suspect our work ; though we think at one time that you watch we . now who , do you suppose , be at the bottom of all this Exmoor insurgency , all this western rebellion not that I say there be none , mind but who be at the bottom of it ? ' 
 ' either Mother Melldrum , ' say I , be now a little angry , ' or else old Nick himself . ' 
 ' nay , old Uncle Reuben ! ' say this , Master Huckaback cast back his coat , and stand up , and make the most of himself . 
 ' well ! ' cry I , be now quite come to the limit of my intellect , ' then , after all , Captain Stickles be right in call you a rebel , sir ! ' 
 ' of course he be ; could so keen a man be wrong about an old fool like I ? but come , and see our rebellion , John . I will trust you now with everything . I will take no oath from you ; only your word to keep silence ; and most of all from your mother . ' 
 ' I will give you my word , ' I say , although like not such pledge ; which make a man think before he speak in ordinary company , against his usual practice . however , I be now so curious , that I think of nothing else ; and scarcely could believe at all that Uncle Ben be quite right in his head . 
 ' take another glass of wine , my son , ' he cry with a cheerful countenance , which make he look more than ten year young ; ' you shall come into partnership with I : your strength will save we two horse , and we always fear the horse work . come and see our rebellion , my boy ; you be a make man from to - night . ' 
 ' but where be I to come and see it ? where be I to find it , sir ? ' 
 ' meet I , ' he answer , yet close his hand , and wrinkle with doubt his forehead , ' come alone , of course ; and meet I at the Wizard 's Slough , at ten to - morrow morning . ' 




 CHAPTER LVIII 

 MASTER HUCKABACK 's SECRET 

 Knowing Master Huckaback to be a man of his word , as well as one who would have other so , I be careful to be in good time the next morning , by the side of the Wizard 's Slough . I be free to admit that the name of the place bear a feeling of uneasiness , and a love of distance , in some measure to my heart . but I do my good not to think of this ; only I think it a wise precaution , and due for the sake of my mother and Lorna , to load my gun with a dozen slug make from the lead of the old church - porch , lay by , long since , against witchcraft . 
 I be well aware that some people now begin to doubt about witchcraft ; or at any rate feign to do so ; be desirous to disbelieve whatever they be afraid of . this spirit be grow too common among we , and will end   unless we put a stop to it !   in the destruction of all religion . and as regard witchcraft , a man be bind either to believe in it , or to disbelieve the Bible . for even in the New Testament , discard many thing of the Old , such as sacrifice , and Sabbath , and fast , and other misery , witchcraft be clearly speak of as a thing that must continue ; that the Evil One be not utterly rob of his vested interest . hence let no one tell I that witchcraft be do away with ; for I will meet he with St. Paul , than whom no well man , and few less superstitious , can be find in all the Bible . 
 feel these thing more in those day than I feel they now , I fetch a goodish compass round , by the way of the cloven rock , rather than cross Black Barrow down , in a reckless and unholy manner . there be several spot , upon that down , cursed and smite , and blast , as if thunderbolt have fall there , and Satan sit to keep they warm . at any rate it be good   as every one acknowledge   not to wander there too much ; even with a doctor of divinity on one arm and of medicine upon the other . 
 therefore , I , be all alone , and on foot   as seem the wise , prefer a course of roundabout ; and start about eight o'clock , without mention my business , arrive at the mouth of the deep descent , such as John Fry describe it . now this   though I have not speak of it   be not my first time of be there . for , although I could not bring myself to spy upon Uncle Reuben , as John Fry have do , yet I think it no ill manner , after he have leave our house , to have a look at the famous place , where the malefactor come to life , at least in John 's opinion . at that time , however , I see nothing except the great ugly black morass , with the grisly reed around it ; and I do not care to go very near it , much less to pry on the further side . 
 now , on the other hand , I be bent to get at the very bottom of this mystery   if there be any , have less fear of witch or wizard , with a man of Uncle Reuben 's wealth to take my part , and see I through . so I rattle the ramrod down my gun , just to know if the charge be right , after so much walking ; and find it full six inch deep , as I like to have it , go boldly down the steep gorge of rock , with a firm resolve to shoot any witch unless it be good Mother Melldrum . nevertheless to my surprise , all be quiet , and fair to look at , in the decline of the narrow way , with great stalk fern come forth like tree , yet hang like cobwebs over one . and along one side , a little spring be get rid of its water . any man might stop and think ; or he might go on and think ; and in either case , there be none to say that he be make a fool of himself . 
 when I come to the foot of this ravine , and over against the great black slough , there be no sign of Master Huckaback , nor of any other live man , except myself , in the silence . therefore , I sit in a niche of rock , gaze at the slough , and ponder the old tradition about it . 
 they say that , in the ancient time , a mighty necromancer live in the wilderness of Exmoor . here , by spell and incantation , he build himself a strong high palace , eight - sided like a spider 's web , and stand on a central steep ; so that neither man nor beast could cross the moor without his knowledge . if he wish to rob and slay a traveller , or to have wild ox , or stag for food , he have nothing more to do than sit at one of his eight window , and point his unholy book at he . any move creature , at which that book be point , must obey the call , and come from whatever distance , if sight once by the wizard . 
 this be a bad condition of thing , and all the country groan under it ; and Exmoor   although the most honest place that a man could wish to live in   be begin to get a bad reputation , and all through that vile wizard . no man durst even go to steal a sheep , or a pony , or so much as a deer for dinner , lest he should be bring to book by a far big rogue than he be . and this go on for many year ; though they pray to God to abate it . but at last , when the wizard be get fat and haughty upon his high stomach , a mighty deliverance come to Exmoor , and a warning , and a memory . for one day the sorcerer gaze from his window face the southeast of the compass , and he yawn , have kill so many man that now he be weary of it . 
 ' Ifackins , ' he cry , or some such oath , both profane and uncomely , ' I see a man on the verge of the sky - line , go along laboriously . a pilgrim , I trow , or some such fool , with the nail of his boot inside they . too thin to be worth eat ; but I will have he for the fun of the thing ; and most of those saint have get money . ' 
 with these word he stretch forth his leg on a stool , and point the book of heathenish spell back upwards at the pilgrim . now this good pilgrim be plod along , soberly and religiously , with a pound of flint in either boot , and not an ounce of meat inside he . he feel the spell of the wicked book , but only as a horse might feel a ' gee - wug ! ' address to he . it be in the power of this good man , either to go on , or turn aside , and see out the wizard 's meaning . and for a moment he halt and stand , like one in two mind about a thing . then the wizard clap one cover to , in a jocular and insulting manner ; and the sound of it come to the pilgrim 's ear , about five mile in the distance , like a great gun fire at he . 
 ' by our Lady , ' he cry , ' I must see to this ; although my poor foot have no skin below they . I will teach this heathen miscreant how to scoff at Glastonbury . ' 
 thereupon he turn his course , and plough along through the moor and bog , towards the eight - sided palace . the wizard sit on his chair of comfort , and with the rank contempt observe the holy man plough towards he . ' he have something good in his wallet , I trow , ' say the black thief to himself ; ' these fellow get always the pick of the wine , and the good of a woman 's money . ' then he cry , ' come in , come in , good sir , ' as he always do to every one . 
 ' bad sir , I will not come in , ' say the pilgrim ; ' neither shall you come out again . here be the bone of all you have slay ; and here shall your own bone be . ' 
 ' hurry I not , ' cry the sorcerer ; ' that be a thing to think about . how many mile hast thou travel this day ? ' 
 but the pilgrim be too wide awake , for if he have speak of any number , bear no cross upon it , the necromancer would have have he , like a ball at bando - play . therefore he answer , as truly as need be , ' by the grace of our Lady , nine . ' 
 now nine be the cross of all cross number , and full to the lip of all crochet . so the wizard stagger back , and think , and inquire again with bravery , ' where can you find a man and wife , one go up - hill and one go down , and not a word speak between they ? ' 
 ' in a cucumber plant , ' say the modest saint ; blush even to think of it ; and the wizard know he be do for . 
 ' you have try I with ungodly question , ' continue the honest pilgrim , with one hand still over his eye , as he think of the feminine cucumber ; ' and now I will ask you a pure one . to whom of mankind have you ever do good , since God see fit to make you ? ' 
 the wizard thought , but could quote no one ; and he look at the saint , and the saint at he , and both their heart be tremble . ' can you mention only one ? ' ask the saint , point a piece of the true cross at he , hop he might cling to it ; ' even a little child will do ; try to think of some one . ' 
 the earth be rock beneath their foot , and the palace window darken on they , with a tint of blood , for now the saint be come inside , hop to save the wizard . 
 ' if I must tell the pure truth , ' say the wizard , look up at the arch of his window , ' I can tell of only one to whom I ever have do good . ' 
 ' one will do ; one be quite enough ; be quick before the ground open . the name of one and this cross will save you . lay your thumb on the end of it . ' 
 ' Nay , that I can not do , great saint . the devil have mercy upon I . ' 
 all this while the palace be sink , and blackness come over they . 
 ' Thou hast all but do for thyself , ' say the saint , with a glory burn round his head ; ' by that last invocation . yet give we the name of the one , my friend , if one there be ; it will save thee , with the cross upon thy breast . all be crash round we ; dear brother , who be that one ? ' 
 ' my own self , ' cry the wretched wizard . 
 ' then there be no help for thee . ' and with that the honest saint go upward , and the wizard , and all his palace , and even the crag that bear it , sink to the bowel of the earth ; and over they be nothing leave except a black bog fringe with reed , of the tint of the wizard 's whisker . the saint , however , be all right , after sleep off the excitement ; and he found a chapel , some three mile westward ; and there he lie with his holy relic and thither in after age come   as we all come home at last   both my Lorna 's Aunt Sabina , and her guardian Ensor Doone . 
 while yet I dwell upon this strange story , wonder if it all be true , and why such thing do not happen now , a man on horseback appear as suddenly as if he have rise out of the earth , on the other side of the great black slough . at first I be a little scared , my mind be in the tune for wonder ; but presently the white hair , whiter from the blackness of the bog between we , show I that it be Uncle Reuben come to look for I , that way . then I leave my chair of rock , and wave my hat and shout to he , and the sound of my voice among the crag and lonely corner frighten I . 
 Old Master Huckaback make no answer , but   so far as I could guess   beckon I to come to he . there be just room between the fringe of reed and the belt of rock around it , for a man go very carefully to escape that horrible pit - hole . and so I go round to the other side , and there find open space enough , with stunted bush , and starvele tree , and straggle tuft of rush . 
 ' you fool , you be frightened , ' say Uncle Ben , as he look at my face after shake hand : ' I want a young man of steadfast courage , as well as of strength and silence . and after what I hear of the battle at Glen Doone , I think I might trust you for courage . ' 
 ' so you may , ' say I , ' wherever I see mine enemy ; but not where witch and wizard be . ' 
 ' tush , great fool ! ' cry Master Huckaback ; ' the only witch or wizard here be the one that bewitcheth all man . now fasten up my horse , John Ridd , and not too near the slough , lad . ah , we have choose our entrance wisely . two good horseman , and their horse , come hither to spy we out , be go mine on their own account   and their last account it be   down this good wizard 's bog - hole . ' 
 with these word , Uncle Reuben clutch the mane of his horse and come down , as a man do when his leg be old ; and as I myself begin to do , at this time of writing . I offer a hand , but he be vex , and would have nought to do with it . 
 ' now follow I , step for step , ' he say , when I have tether his horse to a tree ; ' the ground be not death   like the wizard 's hole , but many part be treacherous , I know it well by this time . ' 
 without any more ado , he lead I in and out the marshy place , to a great round hole or shaft , brattice up with timber . I never have see the like before , and wonder how they could want a well , with so much water on every side . around the mouth be a few little heap of stuff unused to the daylight ; and I think at once of the tale I have hear concern mine in Cornwall , and the silver cup at Combe - Martin , send to the Queen Elizabeth . 
 ' we have a tree across it , John , ' say Uncle Reuben , smile grimly at my sudden shrink from it : ' but some rogue come spy here , just as one of our man go up . he be frighten half out of his life , I believe , and never venture to come again . but we put the blame of that upon you . and I see that we be wrong , John . ' here he look at I with keen eye , though weak . 
 ' you be altogether wrong , ' I answer . ' be I mean enough to spy upon any one dwell with we ? and more than that , Uncle Reuben , it be mean of you to suppose it . ' 
 ' all idea be different , ' reply the old man to my heat , like a little wear - out rill run down a smithy ; ' you with your strength and youth , and all that , be inclined to be romantic . I take thing as I have know they , go on for seventy year . now will you come and meet the wizard , or do your courage fail you ? ' 
 ' my courage must be none , ' say I , ' if I would not go where you go , sir . ' 
 he say no more , but sign to I to lift a heavy wooden corb with an iron loop across it , and sink in a little pit of earth , a yard or so from the mouth of the shaft . I raise it , and by his direction drop it into the throat of the shaft , where it hang and shake from a great cross - beam lay at the level of the earth . a very stout thick rope be fasten to the handle of the corb , and run across a pulley hang from the centre of the beam , and thence out of sight in the nether place . 
 ' I will first descend , ' he say ; ' your weight be too great for safety . when the bucket come up again , follow I , if your heart be good . ' 
 then he whistle down , with a quick sharp noise , and a whistle from below reply ; and he clomb into the vehicle , and the rope run through the pulley , and Uncle Ben go merrily down , and be out of sight , before I have time to think of he . 
 now be leave on the bank like that , and in full sight of the goodly heaven , I wrestle hard with my flesh and blood , about go down into the pit - hole . and but for the pale shame of the thing , that a white - headed man should adventure so , and green youth doubt about it , never could I have make up my mind ; for I do love air and heaven . however , at last up come the bucket ; and with a short sad prayer I go into whatever might happen . 
 my tooth would chatter , do all I could ; but the strength of my arm be with I ; and by they I hold on the grimy rope , and so ease the foot of the corb , which threaten to go away fathom under I . of course I should still have be safe enough , be like an egg in an egg - cup , too big to care for the bottom ; still I wish that all should be do , in good order , without excitement . 
 the scooping of the side grow black , and the patch of sky above more blue , as with many thought of Lorna , a long way underground I sink . then I be fetch up at the bottom with a jerk and rattle ; and but for hold by the rope so , must have tumble over . two great torch of bale - resin show I all the darkness , one be hold by Uncle Ben and the other by a short square man with a face which seem well - know to I . 
 ' hail to the world of gold , John Ridd , ' say Master Huckaback , smile in the old dry manner ; ' big coward never come down the shaft , now do he , Carfax ? ' 
 ' they be all alike , ' say the short square man , ' fust time as they doos it . ' 
 ' may I go to heaven , ' I cry , ' which be a thing quite out of sight ' for I always have a vein of humour , too small to be follow by any one ' if ever again of my own accord I go so far away from it ! ' Uncle Ben grin less at this than at the way I knock my shin in get out of the bucket ; and as for Master Carfax , he would not even deign to smile . and he seem to look upon my entrance as an interloping . 
 for my part , I have nought to do , after rub my bruised leg , except to look about I , so far as the dullness of light would help . and herein I seem , like a mouse in a trap , able no more than to run to and fro , and knock himself , and stare at thing . for here be a little channel groove with post on either side of it , and end with a heap of darkness , whence the sight come back again ; and there be a scoop place , like a funnel , but pour only to darkness . so I wait for somebody to speak first , not see my way to anything . ' 
 ' you seem to be disappoint , John , ' say Uncle Reuben , look blue by the light of the flambeaux ; ' do you expect to see the roof of gold , and the side of gold , and the floor of gold , John Ridd ? ' 
 ' ha , ha ! ' cry Master Carfax ; ' I reckon she do ; no doubt she do . ' 
 ' you be wrong , ' I reply ; ' but I do expect to see something well than dirt and darkness . ' 
 ' come on then , my lad ; and we will show you some - thing well . we want your great arm on here , for a job that have beat the whole of we . ' 
 with these word , Uncle Ben lead the way along a narrow passage , roof with rock and floor with slate - colour shale and shingle , and wind in and out , until we stop at a great stone block or boulder , lie across the floor , and as large as my mother 's well oaken wardrobe . beside it be several sledge - hammer , batter , and some with broken helve . 
 ' thou great villain ! ' cry Uncle Ben , give the boulder a little kick ; ' I believe thy time be come at last . now , John , give we a sample of the thing they tell of thee . take the big of they sledge - hammer and crack this rogue in two for we . we have try at he for a fortnight , and he be a nut worth crack . but we have no man who can swing that hammer , though all in the mine have handle it . ' 
 ' I will do my very good , ' say I , pull off my coat and waistcoat , as if I be go to wrestle ; ' but I fear he will prove too tough for I . ' 
 ' ay , that her wull , ' grunt Master Carfax ; ' lack'th a Carnishman , and a beg one too , not a little charp such as I be . there be no man outside Carnwall , as can crack that boolder . ' 
 ' bless my heart , ' I answer ; ' but I know something of you , my friend , or at any rate of your family . well , I have beat most of your cornish man , though not my place to talk of it . but mind , if I crack this rock for you , I must have some of the gold inside it . ' 
 ' dost think to see the gold come tumble out like the kernel of a nut , thou zany ? ' ask Uncle Reuben pettishly ; ' now wilt thou crack it or wilt thou not ? for I believe thou canst do it , though only a lad of Somerset . ' 
 Uncle Reuben show by say this , and by his glance at Carfax , that he be proud of his county , and would be disappoint for it if I fail to crack the boulder . so I beg he to stoop his torch a little , that I might examine my subject . to I there appear to be nothing at all remarkable about it , except that it sparkle here and there , when the flash of the flame fall upon it . a great obstinate , oblong , sullen stone ; how could it be worth the breaking , except for make road with ? 
 nevertheless , I take up the hammer , and swing it far behind my head , fetch it down , with all my power , upon the middle of the rock . the roof above rang mightily , and the echo go down delven gallery , so that all the miner flock to know what might be do . but Master Carfax only smile , although the blow shake he where he stand , for behold the stone be still unbroken , and as firm as ever . then I smote it again , with no well fortune , and Uncle Ben look vexed and angry , but all the miner grin with triumph . 
 ' this little tool be too light , ' I cry ; ' one of you give I a piece of strong cord . ' 
 then I take two more of the weighty hammer , and lash they fast to the back of mine , not so as to strike , but to burden the fall . have make this firm , and with room to grasp the handle of the large one only for the helve of the other be short I smile at Uncle Ben , and whirl the mighty implement round my head , just to try whether I could manage it . upon that the miner give a cheer , be honest man , and desirous of see fair play between this ' shameless stone '   as Dan Homer call it   and I with my hammer hammering . 
 then I swing I on high to the swing of the sledge , as a thresher bend back to the rise of his flail , and with all my power descending deliver the ponderous onset . crash and crush the great stone fall over , and thread of sparkle gold appear in the jagged side of the breakage . 
 ' how now , Simon Carfax ? ' cry Uncle Ben triumphantly ; ' wilt thou find a man in Cornwall can do the like of that ? ' 
 ' ay , and more , ' he answer ; ' however , it be pretty fair for a lad of these outlandish part . get your roller , my lad , and lead it to the crush engine . ' 
 I be glad to have be of some service to they ; for it seem that this great boulder have be too large to be draw along the gallery and too hard to crack . but now they move it very easily , take piece by piece , and carefully pick up the fragment . 
 ' Thou hast do we a good turn , my lad , ' say Uncle Reuben , as the other pass out of sight at the corner ; ' and now I will show thee the bottom of a very wondrous mystery . but we must not do it more than once , for the time of day be the wrong one . ' 
 the whole affair be a mystery to I , and far beyond my understanding , I follow he softly , without a word , yet think very heavily , and long to be above ground again . he lead I through small passage , to a hollow place near the descend shaft , where I see a most extraordinary monster fit up . in form it be like a great coffee - mill , such as I have see in London , only a thousand time large , and with heavy windlass to work it . 
 ' put in a barrow - load of the smoulder , ' say Uncle Ben to Carfax , ' and let they work the crank , for John to understand a thing or two . ' 
 ' at this time of day ! ' cry Simon Carfax ; ' and the watching as have be o ' late ! ' 
 however , he do it without more remonstrance ; pour into the scuttle at the top of the machine about a baskeful of broken rock ; and then a dozen man go to the wheel , and force it round , as sailor do . upon that such a hideous noise arise , as I never should have believe any creature capable of make , and I run to the well of the mine for air , and to ease my ear , if possible . 
 ' enough , enough ! ' shout Uncle Ben by the time I be nearly deafen ; ' we will digest our goodly boulder after the devil be come abroad for his evening work . now , John , not a word about what you have learn ; but henceforth you will not be frighten by the noise we make at dusk . ' 
 I could not deny but what this be very clever management . if they could not keep the echo of the upper air from move , the wise plan be to open their valve during the discouragement of the fall evening ; when folk would rather be drive away , than draw into the wild and quagmire , by a sound so deep and awful , come through the darkness . 




 CHAPTER LIX 

 LORNA go away 

 although there be very ancient tale of gold be find upon Exmoor , in lump and solid hummock , and of man who slay one another for it , this deep digging and great labour seem to I a dangerous and unholy enterprise . and Master Huckaback confess that up to the present time his two partner and himself   for they prove to be three adventurer   have put into the earth more gold than they have take out of it . nevertheless he feel quite sure that it must in a very short time succeed , and pay they back an hundredfold ; and he press I with great earnestness to join they , and work there as much as I could , without move my mother 's suspicion . I ask he how they have manage so long to carry on without discovery ; and he say that this be partly through the wildness of the neighbourhood , and the legend that frighten people of a superstitious turn ; partly through their own great caution , and the manner of fetch both supply and implement by night ; but most of all , they have to thank the trouble of the period , the suspicion of rebellion , and the terror of the Doones , which   like the wizard I be speak of   keep folk from be too inquisitive where they have no business . the slough , moreover , have help they well , both by make their access dark , and yet more by swallow up and conceal all that be cast from the mouth of the pit . once , before the attack on Glen Doone , they have a narrow escape from the King 's Commissioner ; for Captain Stickles have hear no doubt the story of John Fry , go with half a dozen trooper , on purpose to search the neighbourhood . now if he have ride alone , most likely he would have discover everything ; but he fear to venture so , have suspicion of a trap . come as they do in a company , all mount and conspicuous , the watchman   who be post now on the top of the hill , almost every day since John Fry 's appearance   could not help espy they , mile distant , over the moorland . he watch they under the shade of his hand , and presently run down the hill , and raise a great commotion . then Simon Carfax and all his man come up , and make thing natural , remove every sign of work ; and finally , sink underground , draw across the mouth of the pit a hurdle thatch with sedge and heather . only Simon himself be leave behind , ensconce in a hole of the crag , to observe the doing of the enemy . 
 Captain Stickles ride very bravely , with all his man clatter after he , down the rocky pass , and even to the margin of the slough . and there they stop , and hold council ; for it be a perilous thing to risk the passage upon horseback , between the treacherous brink and the cliff , unless one know it thoroughly . Stickles , however , and one follower , carefully feel the way along , have their horse well in hand , and bear a rope to draw they out , in case of be founder . then they spur across the rough boggy land , far away than the shaft be . here the ground lie jagged and shaggy , work up with high tuft of reed , or scrag with stunt brushwood . and between the up and down   which meet anybody anyhow   green - cover place tempt the foot , and black bog - hole discourage it . it be not to be marvel at that amid such place as this , for the first time visit , the horse be a little skeary ; and their rider partook of the feeling , as all good rider do . in and out of the tuft they go , with their eye dilate , wish to be out of harm , if conscience be but satisfied . and of this tufty flaggy ground , pock with bog and boglet , one especial nature be that it will not hold impression . 
 see thus no track of man , nor anything but marsh - work , and stormwork , and of the season , these two honest man ride back , and be glad to do so . for above they hang the mountain , cowl with fog , and seam with storm ; and around they desolation ; and below their foot the grave . hence they go , with all goodwill ; and vow for ever afterwards that fear of a simple place like that be only too ridiculous . so they all ride home with mutual praise , and their courage well - approve ; and the only result of the expedition be to confirm John Fry 's repute as a big liar than ever . 
 now I have enough of that underground work , as before relate , to last I for a year to come ; neither would I , for sake of gold , have ever step into that bucket , of my own goodwill again . but when I tell Lorna whom I could trust in any matter of secrecy , as if she have never be a woman all about my great descent , and the honeycombing of the earth , and the mournful noise at eventide , when the gold be under the crusher and bewail the mischief it must do , then Lorna 's chief desire be to know more about Simon Carfax . 
 ' it must be our Gwenny 's father , ' she cry ; ' the man who disappear underground , and whom she have ever be seek . how grieve the poor little thing will be , if it should turn out , after all , that he leave his child on purpose ! I can hardly believe it ; can you , John ? ' 
 ' well , ' I reply ; ' all man be wicked , more or less , to some extent ; and no man may say otherwise . ' 
 for I do not wish to commit myself to an opinion about Simon , lest I might be wrong , and Lorna think less of my judgment . 
 but be resolve to see this out , and do a good turn , if I could , to Gwenny , who have do I many a good one , I beg my Lorna to say not a word of this matter to the handmaiden , until I have far search it out . and to carry out this resolve , I go again to the place of business where they be grind gold as freely as an apothecary at his pill . 
 have now true right of entrance , and be know to the watchman , and regard   since I crack the boulder   as one who could pay his footing , and perhaps would be the master , when Uncle Ben should be choke with money , I find the corb send up for I rather soon than I wish it . for the smell of the place underground , and the way man 's eye come out of they , with link , and brand , and flambeaux , instead of God 's light to look at , be to I a point of caution , rather than of pleasure . 
 no doubt but what some man enjoy it , be bear , like worm , to dig , and to live in their own scooping . yet even the worm come up sometimes , after a good soft shower of rain , and hold discourse with one another ; whereas these man , and the horse let down , come above ground never . 
 and the change of the sky be half the change our nature call for . earth we have , and all its produce   move from the first appearance , and the hope with infant ' eye , through the bloom of beauty 's promise , to the rich and ripe fulfilment , and the fall back to rest ; sea we have   with all its wonder shed on eye , and ear , and heart ; and the thought of something more   but without the sky to look at , what would earth , and sea , and even our own self , be to we ? 
 do we look at earth with hope ? yes , for victual only . do we look at sea with hope ? yes , that we may escape it . at the sky alone   though question with the doubt of sunshine , or scatter with uncertain star , at the sky alone we look with pure hope and with memory . 
 hence it always hurt my feeling when I get into that bucket , with my small - clothe turn up over , and a kerchief round my hat . but know that my purpose be sound , and my motif pure , I let the sky grow to a little blue hole , and then to nothing over I . at the bottom Master Carfax meet I , be captain of the mine , and desirous to know my business . he wear a loose sack round his shoulder , and his beard be two foot long . 
 ' my business be to speak with you , ' I answer rather sternly ; for this man , who be nothing more than Uncle Reuben 's servant , have carry thing too far with I , show no respect whatever ; and though I do not care for much , I like to receive a little , even in my early day . 
 ' coom into the muck - hole , then , ' be his gracious answer ; and he lead I into a filthy cell , where the miner change their jacket . 
 ' Simon Carfax , I begin , with a manner to discourage he ; ' I fear you be a shallow fellow , and not worth my trouble . ' 
 ' then do not   take it , ' he reply ; ' I want no man 's trouble . ' 
 ' for your sake I would not , ' I answer ; ' but for your daughter 's sake I will ; the daughter whom you leave to starve so pitifully in the wilderness . ' 
 the man star at I with his pale gray eye , whose colour be lose from candle light ; and his voice as well as his body shake , while he cry , 
 ' it be a lie , man . no daughter , and no son have I. nor be ever child of mine leave to starve in the wilderness . you be too big for I to tackle , and that make you a coward for say it . ' his hand be play with a pickaxe helve , as if he long to have I under it . 
 ' perhaps I have wrong you , Simon , ' I answer very softly ; for the sweat upon his forehead shine in the smoky torchlight ; ' if I have , I crave your pardon . but do you not bring up from Cornwall a little maid name " Gwenny , " and suppose to be your daughter ? ' 
 ' ay , and she be my daughter , my last and only child of five ; and for she I would give this mine , and all the gold will ever come from it . ' 
 ' you shall have she , without either mine or gold ; if you only prove to I that you do not abandon she . ' 
 ' abandon she ! I abandon Gwenny ! ' he cry with such a rage of scorn , that I at once believe he . ' they tell I she be dead , and crush , and bury in the drift here ; and half my heart die with she . the Almighty blast their mining - work , if the scoundrel lie to I ! ' 
 ' the scoundrel must have lie to you , ' I answer , with a spirit fire by his heat of fury : ' the maid be live and with we . come up ; and you shall see she . ' 
 ' rig the bucket , ' he shout out along the echo gallery ; and then he fall against the wall , and through the grimy sack I see the heaving of his breast , as I have see my opponent 's chest , in a long hard bout of wrestling . for my part , I could do no more than hold my tongue and look at he . 
 without another word we rise to the level of the moor and mire ; neither would Master Carfax speak , as I lead he across the barrow . in this he be welcome to his own way , for I do love silence ; so little harm can come of it . and though Gwenny be no beauty , her father might be fond of she . 
 so I put he in the cow - house   not to frighten the little maid , and the fold shutter over he , such as we use at the beesting ; and he listen to my voice outside , and hold on , and preserve himself . for now he would have scoop the earth , as cattle do at yearning - time , and as meekly and as patiently , to have his child restore to he . not to make long tale of it for this thing be beyond I , through want of true experience I go and fetch his Gwenny forth from the back kitchen , where she be fight , as usual , with our Betty . 
 ' come along , you little Vick , ' I say , for so we call she ; ' I have a message to you , Gwenny , from the Lord in heaven . ' 
 ' do not   ' ee talk about he , ' she answer ; ' she have long forgatten I . ' 
 ' that he have never do , you stupid . come , and see who be in the cowhouse . ' 
 Gwenny know ; she know in a moment . look into my eye , she know ; and hang back from I to sigh , she know it even well . 
 she have not much elegance of emotion , be flat and square all over ; but none the less for that her heart come quick , and her word come slowly . 
 ' oh , Jan , you be too good to cheat I . be it joke you be put upon I ? ' 
 I answer she with a gaze alone ; and she tuck up her clothe and follow I because the road be dirty . then I open the door just wide enough for the child to to go her father , and leave those two to have it out , as might be most natural . and they take a long time about it . 
 meanwhile I need must go and tell my Lorna all the matter ; and her joy be almost as great as if she herself have find a father . and the wonder of the whole be this , that I get all the credit ; of which not a thousandth part belong by right and reason to I . yet so it almost always be . if I work for good desert , and slave , and lie awake at night , and spend my unborn life in dream , not a blink , nor wink , nor inkle of my labour ever tell . it would have be well to leave unburned , and to keep undevoure , the fuel and the food of life . but if I have labour not , only act by some impulse , whim , caprice , or anything ; or even act not at all , only let thing float by ; pile upon I commendation , bravo , and applause , almost work I up to tempt once again   though sick of it   the ill luck of deserve . 
 without intend any harm , and mean only good indeed , I have now do serious wrong to Uncle Reuben 's prospect . for Captain Carfax be full as angry at the trick play on he as he be happy in discover the falsehood and the fraud of it . nor could I help agree with he , when he tell I all of it , as with tear in his eye he do , and ready to be my slave henceforth ; I could not forbear from own that it be a low and heartless trick , unworthy of man who have family ; and the recoil whereof be well deserve , whatever it might end in . 
 for when this poor man leave his daughter , asleep as he suppose , and have his food , and change of clothe , and Sunday hat to see to , he mean to return in an hour or so , and settle about her sustenance in some house of the neighbourhood . but this be the very thing of all thing which the leader of the enterprise , who have bring he up from Cornwall , for his note skill in metal , be determine , whether by fair mean or foul , to stop at the very outset . Secrecy be their main object , what chance could there be of it , if the miner be allow to keep their child in the neighbourhood ? hence , on the plea of feast Simon , they keep he drunk for three day and three night , assure he   whenever he have gleam enough to ask for she   that his daughter be as well as could be , and enjoy herself with the child . not wish the maid to see he tipsy , he press the matter no far ; but apply himself to the bottle again , and drink her health with pleasure . 
 however , after three day of this , his constitution rise against it , and he become quite sober ; with a certain lowness of heart moreover , and a sense of error . and his first desire to right himself , and easy way to do it , be by exert parental authority upon Gwenny . possess with this intention   for he be not a sweet temper man , and his head be ache sadly   he seek for Gwenny high and low ; first with threat , and then with fear , and then with tear and wail . and so he become to the other man a warning and a great annoyance . therefore they combine to swear what seem a very likely thing , and might be true for all they know , to wit , that Gwenny have come to seek for her father down the shaft - hole , and peer too eagerly into the dark , have topple forward , and go down , and lain at the bottom as dead as a stone .
