 ' and thou be so happy with drink , ' the villain finish up to he , ' and get drunker every day , we think it shame to trouble thee ; and we bury the wench in the low drift ; and no use to think more of she ; but come and have a glass , Sim . ' 
 but Simon Carfax swear that drink have lose he his wife , and now have lose he the last of his five child , and would lose he his own soul , if far he go on with it ; and from that day to his death he never touch strong drink again . nor only this ; but be soon appoint captain of the mine , he allow no man on any pretext to bring cordial thither ; and to this and his stern hard rule and stealthy secret management   as much as to good luck and place   might it be attribute that scarcely any but themselves have dream about this Exmoor mine . 
 as for I , I have no ambition to become a miner ; and the state to which gold - seeking have bring poor Uncle Ben be not at all encouraging . my business be to till the ground , and tend the growth that come of it , and store the fruit in Heaven 's good time , rather than to scoop and burrow like a weasel or a rat for the yellow root of evil . moreover , I be lead from home , between the hay and corn harvest   when we often have a week to spare , by a call there be no resist ; unless I give up all regard for wrestling , and for my county . 
 now here many person may take I amiss , and there always have be some confusion ; which people who ought to have know well have work into subject of quarrelling . by birth it be true , and can not be deny , that I be a man of Somerset ; nevertheless by breed I be , as well as by education , a son of Devon also . and just as both of our two county vow that Glen Doone be none of theirs , but belong to the other one ; so now , each with hot claim and jangle   lead even to blow sometimes , assert and would swear to it   as I become more famous   that John Ridd be of its own producing , breed of its own true blood , and basely steal by the other . 
 now I have not judge it in any way needful or even become and delicate , to enter into my wrestling adventure , or describe my progress . the whole thing be so different from Lorna , and her gentle manner , and her style of walking ; moreover I must seem   even to kind people   to magnify myself so much , or at least attempt to do it , that I have scratch out write page , through my well taste and sense . 
 neither will I , upon this head , make any difference even now ; be simply betray into mention the matter because bare truth require it , in the tale of Lorna 's fortune . 
 for a mighty giant have arise in a part of Cornwall : and his calf be twenty - five inch round , and the breadth of his shoulder two foot and a quarter ; and his stature seven foot and three - quarter . round the chest he be seventy inch , and his hand a foot across , and there be no scale strong enough to judge of his weight in the market - place . now this man or I should say , his backer and his boaster , for the giant himself be modest send I a brave and haughty challenge , to meet he in the ring at bodmin - town , on the first day of August , or else to return my champion 's belt to they by the messenger . 
 it be no use to deny but that I be greatly dash and scar at first . for my part , I be only , when measure without clothe on , sixty inch round the breast , and round the calf scarce twenty - one , only two foot across the shoulder , and in height not six and three - quarter . however , my mother would never believe that this man could beat I ; and Lorna be of the same mind , I resolve to go and try he , as they would pay all expense and a hundred pound , if I conquer he ; so confident be those Cornishmen . 
 now this story be too well know for I to go through it again and again . every child in Devonshire know , and his grandson will know , the song which some clever man make of it , after I have treat he to water , and to lemon , and a little sugar , and a drop of eau - de - vie . enough that I have find the giant quite as big as they have describe he , and enough to terrify any one . but trust in my practice and study of the art , I resolve to try a back with he ; and when my arm be round he once , the giant be but a farthingale put into the vice of a blacksmith . the man have no bone ; his frame sink in , and I be afraid of crush he . he lie on his back , and smile at I ; and I beg his pardon . 
 now this affair make a noise at the time , and redound so much to my credit , that I be deeply grieve at it , because deserve none . for I do like a good strife and struggle ; and the doubt make the joy of victory ; whereas in this case , I might as well have be send for a match with a hay - mow . however , I get my hundred pound , and make up my mind to spend every farthing in present for mother and Lorna . 
 for Annie be marry by this time , and long before I go away ; as need scarcely be say , perhaps ; if any one follow the week and the month . the wedding be quiet enough , except for everybody 's good wish ; and I desire not to dwell upon it , because it grieve I in many way . 
 but now that I have try to hope the very good for dear Annie , a deep blow than could have come , even through she , await I . for after that visit to Cornwall , and with my prize - money about I , I come on foot from Okehampton to Oare , so as to save a little sum towards my time of marry . for Lorna 's fortune I would not have ; small or great I would not have it ; only if there be no deny we would devote the whole of it to charitable us , as Master Peter Blundell have do ; and perhaps the future age would endeavour to be grateful . Lorna and I have settle this question at least twice a day , on the average ; and each time with more satisfaction . 
 now come into the kitchen with all my cash in my breech pocket   golden guineas , with an elephant on they , for the stamp of the Guinea Company , I find dear mother most heartily glad to see I safe and sound again for she have dread that giant , and dream of he and she never ask I about the money . Lizzie also be soft , and more gracious than usual ; especially when she see I pour guineas , like peppercorn , into the pudding - basin . but by the way they hang about , I know that something be go wrong . 
 ' where be Lorna ? ' I ask at length , after try not to ask it ; ' I want she to come , and see my money . she never see so much before . ' 
 ' alas ! ' say mother with a heavy sigh ; ' she will see a great deal more , I fear ; and a deal more than be good for she . whether you ever see she again will depend upon her nature , John . ' 
 ' what do you mean , mother ? have you quarrel ? why do not Lorna come to I ? be I never to know ? ' 
 ' now , John , be not so impatient , ' my mother reply , quite calmly , for in truth she be jealous of Lorna , ' you could wait now , very well , John , if it be till this day week , for the coming of your mother , John . and yet your mother be your good friend . who can ever fill her place ? ' 
 think of her future absence , mother turn away and cry ; and the box - iron sing the blanket . 
 ' now , ' say I , be wild by this time ; ' Lizzie , you have a little sense ; will you tell I where be Lorna ? ' 
 ' the Lady Lorna Dugal , ' say Lizzie , screw up her lip as if the title be too grand , ' be go to London , brother John ; and not likely to come back again . we must try to get on without she . ' 
 ' you little   something ' I cry , which I dare not write down here , as all you be too good for such language ; but Lizzie 's lip provoke I so ' my Lorna go , my Lorna go ! and without good - bye to I even ! it be your spite have sicken she . ' 
 ' you be quite mistaken there , ' she reply ; ' how can folk of low degree have either spite or like towards the people so far above they ? the Lady Lorna Dugal be go , because she could not help herself ; and she weep enough to break ten heart if heart be ever break , John . ' 
 ' Darling Lizzie , how good you be ! ' I cry , without notice her sneer ; ' tell I all about it , dear ; tell I every word she say . ' 
 ' that will not take long , ' say Lizzie , quite as unmove by soft coaxing as by urgent cursing ; ' the lady speak very little to any one , except indeed to mother , and to Gwenny Carfax ; and Gwenny be go with she , so that the benefit of that be lose . but she leave a letter for " poor John , " as in charity she call he . how grand she look , to be sure , with the fine clothe on that be come for she ! ' 
 ' where be the letter , you utter vixen ! oh , may you have a husband ! ' 
 ' who will thresh it out of you , and starve it , and swear it out of you ! ' be the meaning of my imprecation : but Lizzie , not dream as yet of such thing , could not understand I , and be rather thankful ; therefore she answer quietly , 
 ' the letter be in the little cupboard , near the head of Lady Lorna 's bed , where she use to keep the diamond necklace , which we contrive to get steal . ' 
 without another word I rush   so that every board in the house shake   up to my lose Lorna 's room , and tear the little wall - niche open and espy my treasure . it be as simple , and as homely , and loving , as even I could wish . part of it run as follow , the other part it behove I not to open out to stranger : ' my own love , and sometime lord , take it not amiss of I , that even without farewell , I go ; for I can not persuade the man to wait , your return be doubtful . my great - uncle , some grand lord , be await I at Dunster , have fear of venture too near this Exmoor country . I , who have be so lawless always , and the child of outlaws , be now to atone for this , it seem , by live in a court of law , and under special surveillance   as they call it , I believe   of his Majesty 's Court of Chancery . my uncle be appoint my guardian and master ; and I must live beneath his care , until I be twenty - one year old . to I this appear a dreadful thing , and very unjust , and cruel ; for why should I lose my freedom , through heritage of land and gold ? I offer to abandon all if they would only let I go ; I go down on my knee to they , and say I want title not , neither land , nor money ; only to stay where I be , where first I have know happiness . but they only laugh and call I " child , " and say I must talk of that to the King 's High Chancellor . their order they have , and must obey they ; and Master Stickles be order too , to help as the King 's Commissioner . and then , although it pierce my heart not to say one " goodbye , John , " I be glad upon the whole that you be not here to dispute it . for I be almost certain that you would not , without force to yourself , have let your Lorna go to people who never , never can care for she . ' 
 here my darling have weep again , by the token on the paper ; and then there follow some sweet word , too sweet for I to chatter they . but she finish with these noble line , which   be common to all humanity , in a case of steadfast love   I do no harm , but rather help all true love by repeat . ' of one thing rest you well assured and I do hope that it may prove of service to your rest , love , else would my own be break no difference of rank , or fortune , or of life itself , shall ever make I swerve from truth to you . we have pass through many trouble , danger , and dispartment , but never yet be doubt between we ; neither ever shall be . each have trust well the other ; and still each must do so . though they tell you I be false , though your own mind harbour it , from the sense of thing around , and your own undervaluing , yet take counsel of your heart , and cast such thought away from you ; be unworthy of itself they must be unworthy also of the one who dwell there ; and that one be , and ever shall be , your own Lorna Dugal . ' 
 some people can not understand that tear should come from pleasure ; but whether from pleasure or from sorrow   mix as they be in the twisted string of a man 's heart , or a woman 's , great tear fall from my stupid eye , even on the blots of Lorna 's . 
 ' no doubt it be all over , ' my mind say to I bitterly ; ' trust I , all shall yet be right , ' my heart reply very sweetly . 




 CHAPTER LX 

 ANNIE luckier than JOHN 

 some people may look down upon we for our slavish way   as they may choose to call they , but in our part of the country , we do love to mention title , and to roll it on our tongue , with a conscience and a comfort . even if a man know not , through fault of education , who the Duke of this be , or the Earl of that , it will never do for he to say so , lest the room look down on he . therefore he must nod his head , and say , ' ah , to be sure ! I know he as well as ever I know my own good woman 's brother . he marry Lord Flipflap 's second daughter , and a precious life she lead he . ' whereupon the room look up at he . but I , be quite unable to carry all this in my head , as I ought , be speedily put down by people of a noble tendency , apt at Lords , and pat with Dukes , and know more about the King than his Majesty would have request . therefore , I fall back in thought , not dare in word to do so , upon the title of our horse . and all these horse deserve their name , not have merely inherit , but by their own doing earn they . Smiler , for instance , have be so call , not so much from a habit of smile , as from his general geniality , white nose , and white ankle . this worthy horse be now in year , but hale and gay as ever ; and when you let he out of the stable , he could neigh and whinny , and make man and horse know it . on the other hand , Kickums be a horse of morose and surly order ; harbour up revenge , and lead a rider to false confidence . very smoothly he would go , and as gentle as a turtle - dove ; until his rider fully believe that a pack - thread be enough for he , and a pat of approval upon his neck the aim and crown of his worthy life . then suddenly up go his hind foot to heaven , and the rider for the most part fly over his nose ; whereupon good kickum would take advantage of his favourable position to come and bite a piece out of his back . now in my present state of mind , be understand of nobody , have none to bear I company , neither wish to have any , an indefinite kind of attraction draw I into Kickum 's society . a bond of mutual sympathy be soon establish between we ; I would ride no other horse , neither Kickums be ride by any other man . and this good horse become as jealous about I as a dog might be ; and would lash out , or run tooth foremost , at any one who come near he when I be on his back . 
 this season , the reaping of the corn , which have be but a year ago so pleasant and so lightsome , be become a heavy labour , and a thing for grumble rather than for gladness . however , for the sake of all , it must be attend to , and with as fair a show of spirit and alacrity as might be . for otherwise the rest would drag , and drop their hand and idle , be quick to take infection of dullness than of diligence . and the harvest be a heavy one , even heavy than the year before , although of poor quality . therefore be I force to work as hard as any horse could during all the daylight hour , and defer till night the brooding upon my misfortune . but the darkness always find I stiff with work , and weary , and less able to think than to dream , may be , of Lorna . and now the house be so dull and lonesome , want Annie 's pretty presence , and the light of Lorna 's eye , that a man have no temptation after supper - time even to sit and smoke a pipe . 
 for Lizzie , though so learn , and pleasant when it suit she , never have take very kindly to my love for Lorna , and be of a proud and slightly upstart nature , could not bear to be eclipse in bearing , look , and breeding , and even in clothe , by the stranger . for one thing I will say of the Doones , that whether by purchase or plunder , they have always dress my darling well , with her own sweet taste to help they . and though Lizzie 's natural hate of the maid   as a Doone and burden with father 's death   should have be change to remorse when she learn of Lorna 's real parentage , it be only alter to sullenness , and discontent with herself , for frequent rudeness to an innocent person , and one of such high descent . moreover , the child have imbibe strange idea as to our aristocracy , partly perhaps from her own way of thinking , and partly from reading of history . for while , from one point of view she look up at they very demurely , as commission by God for the country 's good ; from another sight she dislike they , as ready to sacrifice their good and follow their bad member . 
 yet why should this wench dare to judge upon a matter so far beyond she , and form opinion which she know well than declare before mother ? but with I she have no such scruple , for I have no authority over she ; and my intellect she look down upon , because I praise her own so . thus she make herself very unpleasant to I ; by little jag and jerk of sneer , speed as though unwittingly ; which I   who now consider myself ally to the aristocracy , and perhaps take air on that account   have not wit enough to parry , yet have wind enough to feel . 
 now any one who do not know exactly how mother feel and think , would have expect my mother   than whom could be no well one   to pet I , and make much of I , under my sad trouble ; to hang with anxiety on my look , and shed her tear with mine   if any , and season every dish of meat put by for her John 's return . and if the whole truth must be tell , I do expect that sort of thing , and think what a plague it would be to I ; yet not get it , be vex , as if by some new injury . for mother be a special creature   as I suppose we all be , be the warm of the warm , when fire at the proper corner ; and yet , if take at the wrong point , you would say she be incombustible . 
 hence it come to pass that I have no one even to speak to , about Lorna and my grievance ; for Captain Stickles be now go southward ; and John Fry , of course , be too low for it , although a married man , and well under his wife 's management . but find myself unable at last to bear this any long , upon the first day when all the wheat be cut , and the stook set up in every field , yet none quite fit for carry , I saddle good kickum at five in the morning , and without a word to mother   for a little anxiety might do she good   off I set for Molland parish , to have the counsel and the comfort of my darling Annie . 
 the horse take I over the ground so fast   there be few well to go when he like , that by nine o'clock Annie be in my arm , and blush to the colour of Winnie 's cheek , with sudden delight and young happiness . 
 ' you precious little soul ! ' I cry : ' how do Tom behave to you ? ' 
 ' hush ! ' say Annie : ' how dare you ask ? he be the kind , and the good , and the noble of all man , John ; not even set yourself aside . now look not jealous , John : so it be . we all have special gift , you know . you be as good as you can be , John ; but my husband 's special gift be nobility of character . ' here she look at I , as one who have discover something quite unknown . 
 ' I be devilish glad to hear it , ' say I , be touch at go down so : ' keep he to that mark , my dear ; and cork the whisky bottle . ' 
 ' yes , darling John , ' she answer quickly , not desire to open that subject , and be too sweet to resent it : ' and how be lovely Lorna ? what an age it be since I have see you ! I suppose we must thank she for that . ' 
 ' you may thank she for see I now , ' say I ; ' or rather , ' see how hurt she look , ' you may thank my knowledge of your kindness , and my desire to speak of she to a soft - hearted dear little soul like you . I think all the woman be go mad . even mother treat I shamefully . and as for Lizzie ' here I stop , know no word strong enough , without shock Annie . 
 ' do you mean to say that Lorna be go ? ' ask Annie , in great amazement ; yet leap at the truth , as woman do , with nothing at all to leap from . 
 ' gone . and I never shall see she again . it serve I right for aspire so . ' 
 be grieve at my manner , she lead I in where none could interrupt we ; and in spite of all my dejection , I could not help notice how very pretty and even elegant all thing be around . for we upon Exmoor have little taste ; all we care for be warm comfort , and plenty to eat and to give away , and a hearty smack in everything . but Squire Faggus have see the world , and keep company with great people ; and the taste he have first display in the shoeing of farmer ' horse   which lead almost to his ruin , by bring he into jealousy , and flattery , and dash way   have now be cultivate in London , and by moonlight , so that none could help admire it . 
 ' well ! ' I cry , for the moment drop care and woe in astonishment : ' we have nothing like this at Plover 's barrow ; nor even Uncle Reuben . I do hope it be honest , Annie ? ' 
 ' would I sit in a chair that be not my own ? ' ask Annie , turn crimson , and drop defiantly , and with a whisk of her dress which I never have see before , into the very grandest one : ' would I lie on a couch , brother John , do you think , unless good money be pay for it ? because other people be clever , John , you need not grudge they their earning . ' 
 ' a couch ! ' I reply : ' why what can you want with a couch in the day - time , Annie ? a couch be a small bed , set up in a room without space for a good four - poster . what can you want with a couch downstairs ? I never hear of such nonsense . and you ought to be in the dairy . ' 
 ' I will not   cry , brother John , I will not ; because you want to make I cry ' and all the time she be cry ' you always be so nasty , John , sometimes . ah , you have no nobility of character , like my husband . and I have not see you for two month , John ; and now you come to scold I ! ' 
 ' you little darling , ' I say , for Annie 's tear always conquer I ; ' if all the rest ill - use I , I will not quarrel with you , dear . you have always be true to I ; and I can forgive your vanity . your thing be very pretty , dear ; and you may couch ten time a day , without my interference . no doubt your husband have pay for all this , with the pony he steal from Exmoor . nobility of character be a thing beyond my understanding ; but when my sister love a man , and he do well and flourish , who be I to find fault with he ? mother ought to see these thing : they would turn her head almost : look at the pimple on the chair ! ' 
 ' they be nothing , ' Annie answer , after kiss I for my kindness : ' they be only put in for the time indeed ; and we be to have much well , with gold all round the binding , and double plush at the corner ; so soon as ever the King repay the debt he owe to my poor Tom . ' 
 I think to myself that our present King have be most unlucky in one thing debt all over the kingdom . not a man who have strike a blow for the King , or for his poor father , or even say a good word for he , in the time of his adversity , but expect at least a baronetcy , and a grant of estate to support it . many have call King Charles ungrateful : and he may have be so . but some indulgence be due to a man , with entry few on the credit side , and a terrible column of debit . 
 ' have no fear for the chair , ' I say , for it creak under I very fearfully , have leg not so large as my finger ; ' if the chair break , Annie , your fear should be , lest the tortoise - shell run into I . why , it be strip like a viper 's loin ! I see some hundred in London ; and very cheap they be . they be make to be sell to the country people , such as you and I , dear ; and carefully keep they will last for almost half a year . now will you come back from your furniture , and listen to my story ? ' 
 Annie be a hearty dear , and she know that half my talk be joke , to make light of my worrying . therefore she take it in good part , as I well know that she would do ; and she lead I to a good honest chair ; and she sit in my lap and kiss I . 
 ' all this be not like you , John . all this be not one bit like you : and your cheek be not as they ought to be . I shall have to come home again , if the woman worry my brother so . we always hold together , John ; and we always will , you know . ' 
 ' you dear , ' I cry , ' there be nobody who understand I as you do . Lorna make too much of I , and the rest they make too little . ' 
 ' not mother ; oh , not mother , John ! ' 
 ' no , mother make too much , no doubt ; but want it all for herself alone ; and reckon it as a part of she . she make I more wroth than any one : as if not only my life , but all my head and heart must seek from her , and have no other thought or care . ' 
 be speed of my grumbling thus , and ease into well temper , I tell Annie all the strange history about Lorna and her departure , and the small chance that now remain to I of ever see my love again . to this Annie would not hearken twice , but judge woman by her faithful self , be quite vex with I for speak so . and then , to my surprise and sorrow , she would deliver no opinion as to what I ought to do until she have consult darling Tom . 
 Dear Tom know much of the world , no doubt , especially the dark side of it . but to I it scarcely seem become that my course of action with regard to the Lady Lorna Dugal should be refer to Tom Faggus , and depend upon his decision . however , I would not grieve Annie again by make light of her husband ; and so when he come in to dinner , the matter be lay before he . 
 now this man never confess himself surprised , under any circumstance ; his knowledge of life be so profound , and his charity universal . and in the present case he vow that he have suspect it all along , and could have throw light upon Lorna 's history , if we have see fit to apply to he . upon further inquiry I find that this light be a very dim one , flow only from the fact that he have stop her mother 's coach , at the village of Bolham , on the Bampton Road , the day before I see they . find only woman therein , and these in a sad condition , Tom with his usual chivalry   as he have no scent of the necklace   allow they to pass ; with nothing more than a pleasant exchange of courtesy , and a testimonial force upon he , in the shape of a bottle of burgundy wine . this the poor countess hand he ; and he twist the cork out with his tooth , and drink her health with his hat off . 
 ' a lady she be , and a true one ; and I be a pretty good judge , ' say Tom : ' ah , I do like a high lady ! ' 
 our Annie look rather queer at this , have no pretension to be one : but she conquer herself , and say , ' yes , Tom ; and many of they like you . ' 
 with this , Tom go on the brag at once , be but a shallow fellow , and not of settle principle , though steady than he use to be ; until I feel myself almost bound to fetch he back a little ; for of all thing I do hate brag the most , as any reader of this tale must by this time know . therefore I say to Squire Faggus , ' come back from your highway day . you have marry the daughter of an honest man ; and such talk be not fit for she . if you be right in rob people , I be right in rob you . I could bind you to your own mantelpiece , as you know thoroughly well , Tom ; and drive away with your own horse , and all your good behind they , but for the sense of honesty . and should I not do as fine a thing as any you do on the highway ? if everything be of public right , how do this chair belong to you ? clever as you be , Tom Faggus , you be nothing but a fool to mix your felony with your farmership . drop the one , or drop the other ; you can not maintain they both . ' 
 as I finish very sternly a speech which have exhaust I more than ten round of wrestling but I be carry away by the truth , as sometimes happen to all of we Tom have not a word to say ; albeit his mind be so much more nimble and rapid than ever mine be . he lean against the mantelpiece   a newly - invent affair in his house   as if I have cord he to it , even as I speak of do . and he lay one hand on his breast in a way which make Annie creep softly to he , and look at I not like a sister . 
 ' you have do I good , John , ' he say at last , and the hand he give I be tremble : ' there be no other man on God 's earth would have dare to speak to I as you have do . from no other would I have take it . nevertheless every word be true ; and I shall dwell on it when you be go . if you never do good in your life before , John , my brother , you have do it now . ' 
 he turn away , in bitter pain , that none might see his trouble ; and Annie , go along with he , look as if I have kill our mother . for my part , I be so upset , for fear of have go too far , that without a word to either of they , but a message on the title - page of King James his Prayer - book , I saddle kickum , and be off , and glad of the moorland air again . 




 CHAPTER LXI 

 THEREFORE he seek COMFORT 

 it be for poor Annie 's sake that I have speak my mind to her husband so freely , and even harshly . for we all know she would break her heart , if Tom take to evil way again . and the right mode of prevent this be , not to coax , and flatter , and make a hero of he   which he do for himself , quite sufficiently , but to set before he the folly of the thing , and the ruin to his own interest . they would both be vex with I , of course , for have leave they so hastily , and especially just before dinner - time ; but that would soon wear off ; and most likely they would come to see mother , and tell she that I be hard to manage , and they could feel for she about it . 
 now with a certain yearning , I know not what , for softness , and for one who could understand I for simple as a child though be , I find few to do that last , at any rate in my love - time I rely upon Kickum 's strength to take I round by Dulverton . it would make the journey some eight mile long , but what be that to a brisk young horse , even with my weight upon he ? 
 and have leave Squire Faggus and Annie much soon than have be intend , I have plenty of time before I , and too much , ere a prospect of dinner . therefore I strike to the right , across the hill , for Dulverton . 
 Pretty Ruth be in the main street of the town , with a basket in her hand , go home from the market . 
 ' why , Cousin Ruth , you be grow , I exclaim ; ' I do believe you be , Ruth . and you be almost too tall , already . ' 
 at this the little thing be so pleased , that she smile through her blush beautifully , and must need come to shake hand with I ; though I sign to she not to do it , because of my horse 's temper . but scarcely be her hand in mine , when kickum turn like an eel upon she , and catch she by the left arm with his tooth , so that she scream with agony . I see the white of his vicious eye , and strike he there with all my force , with my left hand over her right arm , and he never use that eye again ; none the less he keep his hold on she . then I smite he again on the jaw , and catch the little maid up by her right hand , and lay she on the saddle in front of I ; while the horse be giddy and stagger with blow , and foil of his spite , run backward . Ruth 's wit be go ; and she lie before I , in such a helpless and senseless way that I could have kill vile kickum . I strike the spur into he past the rowel , and away he go at full gallop ; while I have enough to do to hold on , with the little girl lie in front of I . but I call to the man who be flock around , to send up a surgeon , as quick as could be , to Master Reuben Huckaback 's . 
 the moment I bring my right arm to bear , the vicious horse have no chance with I ; and if ever a horse be well pay for spite , Kickums have his change that day . the bridle would almost have hold a whale and I draw on it so that his low jaw be well - nigh break from he ; while with both spur I tear his flank , and he learn a little lesson . there be time when a man be more vicious than any horse may vie with . therefore by the time we have reach Uncle Reuben 's house at the top of the hill , the bad horse be only too happy to stop ; every string of his body be tremble , and his head hang down with impotence . I leap from his back at once , and carry the maiden into her own sweet room . 
 now Cousin Ruth be recover softly from her fright and faintness ; and the volley of the wind from gallop so have make her little ear quite pink , and shake her lock all round she . but any one who might wish to see a comely sight and a move one , need only have look at Ruth Huckaback , when she learn   and imagine yet more than it be   the manner of her little ride with I . her hair be of a hazel - brown , and full of wave readiness ; and with no concealment of the trick , she spread it over her eye and face . be so delighted with she , and so glad to see she safe , I kiss she through the thick of it , as a cousin have a right to do ; yea , and ought to do , with gravity . 
 ' darling , ' I say ; ' he have bite you dreadfully : show I your poor arm , dear . ' 
 she pull up her sleeve in the simple manner , rather to look at it herself , than to show I where the wound be . her sleeve be of dark blue Taunton staple ; and her white arm shine , come out of it , as round and plump and velvety , as a stalk of asparagus , newly fetch out of the ground . but above the curved soft elbow , where no room be for one cross word   accord to our proverb ,   three sad gash , edge with crimson , spoil the flow of the pearly flesh . my presence of mind be lose altogether ; and I raise the poor sore arm to my lip , both to stop the bleeding and to take the venom out , have hear how wise it be , and think of my mother . but Ruth , to my great amazement , draw away from I in bitter haste , as if I have be insert instead of extract poison . for the bite of a horse be most venomous ; especially when he shed his tooth ; and far more to be fear than the bite of a dog , or even of a cat . and in my haste I have forget that Ruth might not know a word about this , and might doubt about my meaning , and the warmth of my osculation . but know her danger , I durst not hee her childishness , or her feeling . 
    a maid with an elbow sharp , or knee , 
  Hath cross word two , out of every three . 
 ' do not   be a fool , Cousin Ruth , ' I say , catch she so that she could not move ; ' the poison be soak into you . do you think that I do it for pleasure ? ' 
 the spread of shame on her face be such , when she see her own misunderstanding , that I be ashamed to look at she ; and occupy myself with draw all the risk of glander forth from the white limb , hang helpless now , and leave entirely to my will . before I be quite sure of have wholly exhausted suction , and when I have make the hole in her arm look like the gill of a lamprey , in come the doctor , partly drunk , and in haste to get through his business . 
 ' ha , ha ! I see , ' he cry ; ' bite of a horse , they tell I . very poisonous ; must be burn away . Sally , the iron in the fire . if you have a fire , this weather . ' 
 ' crave your pardon , good sir , ' I say ; for poor little Ruth be faint again at his savage order : ' but my cousin 's arm shall not be burn ; it be a great deal too pretty , and I have suck all the poison out . look , sir , how clean and fresh it be . ' 
 ' bless my heart ! and so it be ! no need at all for cauterising . the epidermis will close over , and the cutis and the pelli . John Ridd , you ought to have study medicine , with your heal power . half my virtue lie in touch . a clean and wholesome body , sir ; I have teach you the latin grammar . I leave you in excellent hand , my dear , and they wait for I at shovel - board . bread and water poultice cold , to be renew , tribus horis . John Ridd , I be at school with you , and you beat I very lamentably , when I try to fight with you . you remember I not ? it be likely enough : I be force to take strong water , John , from infirmity of the liver . attend to my direction ; and I will call again in the morning . ' 
 and in that melancholy plight , care nothing for business , go one of the clever fellow ever know at Tiverton . he could write Latin verses a great deal fast than I could ever write english prose , and nothing seem too great for he . we think that he would go to Oxford and astonish every one , and write in the style of Buchanan ; but he fall all abroad very lamentably ; and now , when I meet he again , be come down to push - pin and shovel - board , with a wager of spirit pende . 
 when Master Huckaback come home , he look at I very sulkily ; not only because of my refusal to become a slave to the gold - digging , but also because he regard I as the cause of a savage broil between Simon Carfax and the man who have cheat he as to his Gwenny . however , when Uncle Ben see Ruth , and know what have befall she , and she with tear in her eye declare that she owe her life to Cousin Ridd , the old man become very gracious to I ; for if he love any one on earth , it be his little granddaughter . 
 I could not stay very long , because , my horse be quite unfit to travel from the injury which his violence and vice have bring upon he , there be nothing for I but to go on foot , as none of Uncle Ben 's horse could take I to Plover 's barrow , without downright cruelty : and though there would be a harvest - moon , Ruth agree with I that I must not keep my mother wait , with no idea where I might be , until a late hour of the night . I tell Ruth all about our Annie , and her noble furniture ; and the little maid be very lively   although her wound be pain she so , that half her laughter come ' on the wrong side of her mouth , ' as we rather coarsely express it ; especially she laugh about Annie 's new - fangle closet for clothe , or standing - press , as she call it . this have frighten I so that I would not come without my stick to look at it ; for the front be inlay with two fiery dragon , and a glass which distort everything , make even Annie look hideous ; and when it be open , a woman 's skeleton , all in white , reveal itself , in the midst of three stand woman . ' it be only to keep my good frock in shape , ' Annie have explain to I ; ' hang they up do ruin they so . but I own that I be afraid of it , John , until I have get all my good clothe there , and then I become very fond of it . but even now it frighten I sometimes in the moonlight . ' 
 have make poor Ruth a little cheerful , with a full account of all Annie 's frock , material , pattern , and fashion   of which I have take a list for my mother , and for Lizzie , lest they should cry out at man 's stupidity about anything of real interest , I proceed to tell she about my own trouble , and the sudden departure of Lorna ; conclude with all the show of indifference which my pride could muster , that now I never should see she again , and must do my good to forget she , as be so far above I . I have not intend to speak of this , but Ruth 's face be so kind and earnest , that I could not stop myself . 
 ' you must not talk like that , Cousin Ridd , ' she say , in a low and gentle tone , and turn away her eye from I ; ' no lady can be above a man , who be pure , and brave , and gentle . and if her heart be worth have , she will never let you give she up , for her grandeur , and her nobility . ' 
 she pronounce those last few word , as I think , with a little bitterness , unperceive by herself perhaps , for it be not in her appearance . but I , attach great importance to a maiden 's opinion about a maiden   because she might judge from experience , would have lead she far into that subject . but she decline to follow , have now no more to say in a matter so removed from she . then I ask her full and straight , and look at she in such a manner that she could not look away , without appear vanquish by feeling of her own which thing be very vile of I ; but all man be so selfish , 
 ' dear cousin , tell I , once for all , what be your advice to I ? ' 
 ' my advice to you , ' she answer bravely , with her dark eye full of pride , and instead of flinch , foil I , ' be to do what every man must do , if he would win fair maiden . since she can not send you token , neither be free to return to you , follow she , pay your court to she ; show that you will not be forget ; and perhaps she will look down I mean , she will relent to you . ' 
 ' she have nothing to relent about . I have never vex nor injure she . my thought have never stray from she . there be no one to compare with she . ' 
 ' then keep she in that same mind about you . see now , I can advise no more . my arm be swell painfully , in spite of all your goodness , and bitter task of surgeonship . I shall have another poultice on , and go to bed , I think , Cousin Ridd , if you will not hold I ungrateful . I be so sorry for your long walk . surely it might be avoid . give my love to dear Lizzie : oh , the room be go round so . ' 
 and she faint into the arm of Sally , who be come just in time to fetch she : no doubt she have be suffer agony all the time she talk to I . leave word that I would come again to inquire for she , and fetch Kickums home , so soon as the harvest permit I , I give direction about the horse , and stride away from the ancient town , be soon upon the moorland . 
 now , through the whole of that long walk the latter part of which be lead by starlight , till the moon arise I dwelt , in my young and foolish way , upon the ordering of our step by a power beyond we . but as I could not bring my mind to any clearness upon this matter , and the star shed no light upon it , but rather confuse I with wonder how their Lord could attend to they all , and yet to a puny fool like I , it come to pass that my thought on the subject be not worth ink , if I know they . 
 but it be perhaps worth ink to relate , so far as I can do so , mother 's delight at my return , when she have almost abandon hope , and conclude that I be go to London , in disgust at her behaviour . and now she be look up the lane , at the rise of the harvest - moon , in despair , as she say afterwards . but if she have despair in truth , what use to look at all ? yet accord to the epigram make by a good Blundellite , 
 despair be never yet so deep in sink as in seem ; despair be hope just drop asleep for well chance of dream . 
 and mother 's dream be a happy one , when she know my step at a furlong distant ; for the night be of those that carry sound thrice as far as day can . she recover herself , when she be sure , and even make up her mind to scold I , and feel as if she could do it . but when she be in my arm , into which she throw herself , and I by the light of the moon descry the silver gleam on one side of her head   now spread since Annie 's departure , bless my heart and yours therewith , no room be leave for scold . she hug I , and she cling to I ; and I look at she , with duty make tenfold , and discharge by love . we say nothing to one another ; but all be right between we . 
 even Lizzie behave very well , so far as her nature admit ; not even say a nasty thing all the time she be get my supper ready , with a weak imitation of Annie . she know that the gift of cooking be not vouchsafe by God to she ; but sometimes she would do she good , by intellect to win it . whereas it be no more to be win by intellect than be divine poetry . an amount of strong quick heart be needful , and the understanding must second it , in the one art as in the other . now my fare be very choice for the next three day or more ; yet not turn out like Annie 's . they could do a thing well enough on the fire ; but they could not put it on table so ; nor even have plate all pip hot . this be Annie 's special gift ; bear in she , and ready to cool with she ; like a plate bear away from the fireplace . I sigh sometimes about Lorna , and they think it be about the plate . and mother would stand and look at I , as much as to say , ' no please he ' ; and Lizzie would jerk up one shoulder , and cry , ' he have well have Lorna to cook for he ' ; while the whole truth be that I want not to be plague about any cookery ; but just to have something good and quiet , and then smoke and think about Lorna . 
 nevertheless the time go on , with one change and another ; and we gather all our harvest in ; and Parson Bowden thank God for it , both in church and out of it ; for his tithe would be very goodly . the unmatched cold of the previous winter , and general fear of scarcity , and our own talk about our ruin , have send price up to a grand high pitch ; and we do our good to keep they there . for nine englishman out of every ten believe that a bitter winter must breed a sour summer , and explain away topmost price . while accord to my experience , more often it would be otherwise , except for the public thinking so . however , I have say too much ; and if any farmer read my book , he will vow that I write it for nothing else except to rob his family . 




 CHAPTER LXII 

 the king must not be prayed for 

 all our neighbourhood be surprised that the Doones have not ere now attack , and probably make an end of we . for we lay almost at their mercy now , have only Sergeant Bloxham , and three man , to protect we , Captain Stickles have be order southward with all his force ; except such as might be needful for collect toll , and watch the import at Lynmouth , and thence to Porlock . the Sergeant , have now imbibe a taste for writing report   though his first great effort have do he no good , and only offend Stickles , report weekly from Plover 's barrow , whenever he could find a messenger . and though we feed not Sergeant Bloxham at our own table , with the good we have   as in the case of Stickles , who represent his Majesty , yet we treat he so well , that he report very highly of we , as loyal and true - hearted liege , and most devoted to our lord the King . and indeed he could scarcely have do less , when Lizzie write great part of his report , and furbish up the rest to such a pitch of lustre , that Lord Clarendon himself need scarce have be ashamed of they . and though this cost a great deal of ale , and even of strong water   for Lizzie would have it the duty of a critic to stand treat to the author , and though it be otherwise a plague , as give the maid such air of patronage , and such pretence to politic ; yet there be no stop it , without the risk of mortal offence to both writer and reviewer . our mother also , while disapprove Lizzie 's long stay in the saddle - room on a Friday night and a Saturday , and insist that Betty should be there , be nevertheless as proud as need be , that the King should read our Eliza ' s writing at least so the innocent soul believe and we all look forward to something great as the fruit of all this history . and something great do come of it , though not as we expect ; for these report , or as many of they as be ever open , stand we in good stead the next year , when we be accuse of harbouring and comfort guilty rebel . 
 now the reason why the Doones do not attack we be that they be prepare to meet another and more powerful assault upon their fortress ; be assure that their repulse of King 's troop could not be look over when bring before the authority . and no doubt they be right ; for although the conflict in the Government during that summer and autumn have delay the matter yet positive order have be issue that these outlaws and malefactor should at any price be bring to justice ; when the sudden death of King Charles the Second throw all thing into confusion , and all mind into a panic . 
 we hear of it first in church , on Sunday , the eighth day of February , 1684 - 5 , from a cousin of John Fry , who have ride over on purpose from Porlock . he come in just before the anthem , splash and heat from his ride , so that every one turn and look at he . he want to create a stir   know how much would be make of he , and he take the good way to do it . for he let the anthem go by very quietly or rather I should say very pleasingly , for our choir be exceed proud of itself , and I sing bass twice as loud as a bull , to beat the clerk with the clarionet and then just as Parson Bowden , with a look of pride at his minstrel , be kneel down to begin the prayer for the King 's Most excellent Majesty   for he never read the litany , except upon Easter Sunday , up jump young Sam Fry , and shout , 
 ' I forbid that there prai - er . ' 
 ' what ! ' cry the parson , rise slowly , and look for some one to shut the door : ' have we a rebel in the congregation ? ' for the parson be grow short - sighted now , and know not Sam Fry at that distance . 
 ' no , ' reply Sam , not a whit abash by the staring of all the parish ; ' no rebel , parson ; but a man who mislaiketh popery and murder . that there prai - er be a prai - er for the dead . ' 
 ' Nay , ' cry the parson , now recognise and know he to be our John 's first cousin , ' you do not mean to say , Sam , that his gracious Majesty be dead ! ' 
 ' dead as a sto - un : poison by they Papishers . ' and Sam rub his hand with enjoyment , at the effect he have produce . 
 ' remember where you be , Sam , ' say Parson Bowden solemnly ; ' when do this most sad thing happen ? the King be the head of the Church , Sam Fry ; when do he leave she ? ' 
 ' day afore yesterday . twelve o'clock . Warn't   we quick to hear of ' un ? ' 
 ' can not   be , ' say the minister : ' the tiding can never have come so soon . anyhow , he will want it all the more . let we pray for his gracious Majesty . ' 
 and with that he proceed as usual ; but nobody cry ' amen , ' for fear of be entangle with Popery . but after give forth his text , our parson say a few word out of book , about the many virtue of his Majesty , and self - denial , and devotion , compare his pious mirth to the dancing of the patriarch David before the ark of the covenant ; and he add , with some severity , that if his flock would not join their pastor   who be much more likely to judge aright   in pray for the King , the least they could do on return home be to pray that the King might not be dead , as his enemy have assert . 
 now when the service be over , we kill the king , and we bring he to life , at least fifty time in the churchyard : and Sam Fry be mount on a high gravestone , to tell every one all he know of it . but he know no more than he have tell we in the church , as before repeat : upon which we be much disappointed with he , and incline to disbelieve he ; until he happily remember that his Majesty have die in great pain , with blue spot on his breast and black spot all across his back , and these in the form of a cross , by reason of Papists have poison he . when Sam call this to his remembrance   or to his imagination   he be overwhelm , at once , with so many invitation to dinner , that he scarce know which of they to accept ; but decide in our favour . 
 grieve much for the loss of the king , however greatly it might be   as the parson have declare it be , while tell we to pray against it   for the royal benefit , I resolve to ride to Porlock myself , directly after dinner , and make sure whether he be dead , or not . for it be not by any mean hard to suppose that Sam Fry , be John 's first cousin , might have inherit either from grandfather or grandmother some of those gift which have make our John so famous for mendacity . at Porlock I find that it be too true ; and the woman of the town be in great distress , for the King have always be popular with they : the man , on the other hand , be forecast what would be likely to ensue . 
 and I myself be of this number , rid sadly home again ; although bind to the King as churchwarden now ; which dignity , next to the parson 's in rank , be with we   as it ought to be in every good parish   hereditary . for who can stick to the church like the man whose father stick to it before he ; and who know all the little in , and great out , which must in these troublous time come across ? 
 but though appoint at last , by virtue of be good farmer in the parish   as well as by vice of mismanagement on the part of my mother , and Nicholas Snowe , who have thoroughly muxe up everything , be too quick - headed ; yet , while I dwell with pride upon the fact that I stand in the King 's shoe , as the manager and promoter of the Church of England , and I know that we must miss his Majesty   whose arm be above the Commandments , as the leader of our thought in church , and handsome upon a guinea ; nevertheless I keep on think how his death would act on I . 
 and here I see it , many way . in the first place , trouble must break out ; and we have eight - and - twenty rick ; count grain , and straw , and hay . moreover , mother be grow weak about riot , and shooting , and burn ; and she gather the bed - clothe around her ear every night , when her foot be tuck up ; and pray not to awake until morning . in the next place , much rebellion   though we would not own it ; in either sense of the verb , to ' own '   be whispering , and pluck skirt , and make sign , among we . and the terror of the Doones help greatly ; as a fruitful tree of lawlessness , and a good excuse for everybody . and after this or rather before it , and first of all indeed   if I must state the true order   arise upon I the thought of Lorna , and how these thing would affect her fate . 
 and indeed I must admit that it have occur to I sometimes , or be suggest by other , that the Lady Lorna have not behave altogether kindly , since her departure from among we . for although in those day the post   as we call the service of letter - carrying , which now come within twenty mile of we   do not extend to our part of the world , yet it might have be possible to procure for hire a man who would ride post , if Lorna fear to trust the pack - horse , or the trooper , who go to and fro . yet no message whatever have reach we ; neither any token even of her safety in London . as to this last , however , we have no misgiving , have learn from the orderly , more than once , that the wealth , and beauty , and adventure of young Lady Lorna Dugal be greatly talk of , both at court and among the common people . 
 now rid sadly homeward , in the sunset of the early spring , I be more than ever touch with sorrow , and a sense of be , as it be , abandon . and the weather grow quite beautiful , and so mild that the tree be bud , and the cattle full of happiness , I could not but think of the difference between the world of to - day and the world of this day twelvemonth . then all be howl desolation , all the earth block up with snow , and all the air with barb of ice as small as splintered needle , yet glitter , in and out , like star , and gather so upon a man   if long he stay among they   that they begin to weigh he down to sleepiness and frozen death . not a sign of life be move , nor be any change of view ; unless the wild wind strike the crest of some cold drift , and bow it . 
 now , on the other hand , all be good . the open palm of spring be lay upon the yielding of the hill ; and each particular valley seem to be the glove for a finger . and although the sun be low , and dip in the western cloud , the gray light of the sea come up , and take , and taking , tell the special tone of everything . all this lie upon my heart , without a word of thinking , spread light and shadow there , and the soft delight of sadness . nevertheless , I would it be the savage snow around I , and the piping of the restless wind , and the death of everything . for in those day I have Lorna . 
 then I think of promise fair ; such as glow around I , where the red rock hold the sun , when he be depart ; and the distant crag endeavour to retain his memory . but as evening spread across they , shade with a silent fold , all the colour steal away ; all remembrance wan and die . 
 ' so it have be with love , ' I think , ' and with simple truth and warmth . the maid have choose the glitter star , instead of the plain daylight . ' 
 nevertheless I would not give in , although in deep despondency   especially when I pass the place where my dear father have fight in vain , and I try to see thing right and then judge aright about they . this , however , be more easy to attempt than to achieve ; and by the time I come down the hill , I be none the wiser . only I could tell my mother that the King be dead for sure ; and she would have try to cry , but for thought of her mourning . 
 there be not a moment for lament . all the mourning must be ready   if we care to beat the Snowes   in eight - and - forty hour : and , although it be Sunday night , mother now feel sure of the thing , sit up with Lizzie , cut pattern , and stitch thing on brown paper , and snipping , and lay the fashion down , and request all opinion , yet when give , scorn they ; insomuch that I grow weary even of tobacco   which have comfort I since Lorna , and pray she to go on until the King should be alive again . 
 the thought of that so flurry she for she never yet could see a joke that she lay her scissor on the table and say , ' the Lord forbid , John ! after what I have cut up ! ' 
 ' it would be just like he , ' I answer , with a know smile : ' mother , you have well stop . pattern may do very well ; but do not   cut up any more good stuff . ' 
 ' well , good lack , I be a fool ! three table peg with needle ! the Lord in his mercy keep his Majesty , if ever he hath get he ! ' 
 by this device we go to bed ; and not another stitch be strike until the trooper have office - tiding that the King be truly dead . hence the snowe beat we by a day ; and both old Betty and Lizzie lay the blame upon I , as usual . 
 almost before we have put off the mourning , which as loyal subject we keep for the King three month and a week ; rumour of disturbance , of plotting , and of outbreak begin to stir among we . we hear of fighting in Scotland , and buying of ship on the continent , and of arm in Dorset and Somerset ; and we keep our beacon in readiness to give signal of a landing ; or rather the soldier do . for we , have trustworthy report that the King have be to high mass himself in the Abbey of Westminster , make all the bishop go with he , and all the guard in London , and then torture all the Protestants who dare to wait outside , moreover have receive from the Pope a flower grow in the Virgin Mary 's garden , and warrant to last for ever , we of the moderate party , hear all this and ten time as much , and have no love for this sour James , such as we have for the lively Charles , be ready to wait for what might happen , rather than care about stop it . therefore we listen to rumour gladly , and shake our head with gravity , and predict , every man something , but scarce any two the same . nevertheless , in our part , thing go on as usual , until the middle of June be nigh . we plough the ground , and sow the corn , and tend the cattle , and heed every one his neighbour 's business , as carefully as heretofore ; and the only thing that move we much be that Annie have a baby . this be a very fine child with blue eye , and christen ' John ' in compliment to I , and with I for his godfather , it be natural to suppose that I think a good deal about he ; and when mother or Lizzie would ask I , all of a sudden , and treacherously , when the fire flare up at supper - time   for we always keep a little wood just alight in summer - time , and enough to make the pot boil , then when they would say to I , ' John , what be you think of ? at a word , speak ! ' I would always answer , ' little John Faggus ' ; and so they make no more of I . 
 but when I be down , on Saturday the thirteenth of June , at the blacksmith 's forge by Brendon town , where the Lynn - stream run so close that he dip his horseshoe in it , and where the news be apt to come first of all to our neighbourhood   except upon a Sunday , while we be talk of the hay - crop , and of a great sheep - stealer , round the corner come a man upon a piebald horse look flagged and weary . but see half a dozen of we , young , and brisk , and hearty , he make a flourish with his horse , and wave a blue flag vehemently , shout with great glory , 
 ' Monmouth and the protestant faith ! Monmouth and no Popery ! Monmouth , the good King 's eld son ! down with the poisoning murderer ! down with the black usurper , and to the devil with all papist ! ' 
 ' why so , thou little varlet ? ' I ask very quietly ; for the man be too small to quarrel with : yet know Lorna to be a ' papist , ' as we choose to call they though they might as well call we ' kingist , ' after the head of our Church I think that this scurvy scampish knave might show they the way to the place he mention , unless his courage fail he . 
 ' Papist yourself , be you ? ' say the fellow , not dare to answer much : ' then take this , and read it . ' 
 and he hand I a long rigmarole , which he call a ' Declaration ' : I see that it be but a heap of lie , and thrust it into the blacksmith 's fire , and blow the bellow thrice at it . no one dare attempt to stop I , for my mood have not be sweet of late ; and of course they know my strength . 
 the man ride on with a mutter noise , have win no recruit from we , by force of my example : and he stop at the ale - house far down , where the road go away from the Lynn - stream . some of we go thither after a time , when our horse be shodden and rasp , for although we might not like the man , we might be glad of his tiding , which seem to be something wonderful . he have set up his blue flag in the tap - room , and be teach every one . 
 ' here coom'th Maister Jan Ridd , ' say the landlady , be well pleased with the call for beer and cider : ' her hath be to lunnon - town , and live within a maile of I . arl the news coom from they nowadays , instead of from here , as she ought to do . if Jan Ridd say it be true , I will try almost to belave it . hath the good Duke land , sir ? ' and she look at I over a foaming cup , and blow the froth off , and put more in . 
 ' I have no doubt it be true enough , ' I answer , before drinking ; ' and too true , Mistress Pugsley . many a poor man will die ; but none shall die from our parish , nor from Brendon , if I can help it . ' 
 and I know that I could help it ; for every one in those little place would abide by my advice ; not only from the fame of my schooling and long sojourn in London , but also because I have earn repute for be very ' slow and sure ' : and with nine people out of ten this be the very good recommendation . for they think themselves much before you in wit , and under no obligation , but rather confer a favour , by do the thing that you do . hence , if I care for influence which mean , for the most part , make people do one 's will , without know it my first step toward it would be to be call , in common parlance , ' slow but sure . ' 
 for the next fortnight we be daily trouble with conflict rumour , each man relate what he desire , rather than what he have right , to believe . we be tell that the Duke have be proclaim King of England in every town of Dorset and of Somerset ; that he have win a great battle at Axminster , and another at Bridport , and another somewhere else ; that all the western county have rise as one man for he , and all the militia have join his rank ; that Taunton , and Bridgwater , and Bristowe , be all mad with delight , the two former be in his hand , and the latter craving to be so . and then , on the other hand , we hear that the Duke have be vanquish , and put to flight , and upon be apprehend , have confess himself an impostor and a papist as bad as the King be . 
 we long for Colonel Stickles   as he always become in time of war , though he fall back to Captain , and even Lieutenant , directly the fight be over , for then we should have win trusty news , as well as good consideration . but even Sergeant Bloxham , much against his will , be go , have leave his heart with our Lizzie , and a collection of all his writing . all the soldier have be order away at full speed for Exeter , to join the Duke of Albemarle , or if he be go , to follow he . as for we , who have feed they so long   although not quite for nothing , we must take our chance of Doones , or any other enemy . 
 now all these tiding move I a little ; not enough to spoil appetite , but enough to make thing lively , and to teach I that look of wisdom which be breed of practice only , and the hearing of many lie . therefore I withhold my judgment , fear to be triumph over , if it should happen to miss the mark . but mother and Lizzie , ten time in a day , predict all they could imagine ; and their prophecy increase in strength accord to contradiction . yet this be not in the proper style for a house like our , which know the news , or at least have know it ; and still be famous , all around , for the last advice . even from Lynmouth , people send up to Plover 's barrow to ask how thing be go on : and it be very grievous to answer that in truth we know not , neither have hear for day and day ; and our reputation be so great , especially since the death of the King have go abroad from Oare parish , that many inquirer would only wink , and lie a finger on the lip , as if to say , ' you know well enough , but see not fit to tell I . ' and before the end arrive , those people believe that they have be right all along , and that we have conceal the truth from they . 
 for I myself become involved   God know how much against my will and my proper judgment   in the trouble , and the conflict , and the cruel work come afterwards . if ever I have make up my mind to anything in all my life , it be at this particular time , and as stern and strong as could be . I have resolve to let thing pass , to hear about they gladly , to encourage all my friend to talk , and myself to express opinion upon each particular point , when in the fullness of time no further doubt could be . but all my policy go for nothing , through a few touch of feeling . 
 one day at the beginning of July , I come home from mow about noon , or a little later , to fetch some cider for all of we , and to eat a morsel of bacon . for mow be no joke that year , the summer be wonderfully wet   even for our wet country , and the swathe fall heavy over the scythe than ever I could remember it . we be drench with rain almost every day ; but the mowing must be do somehow ; and we must trust to God for the haymaking . 
 in the courtyard I see a little cart , with iron brake underneath it , such as fastidious people use to deaden the jolting of the road ; but few man under a lord or baronet would be so particular . therefore I wonder who our noble visitor could be . but when I enter the kitchen - place , brush up my hair for somebody , behold it be no one great than our Annie , with my godson in her arm , and look pale and tear - begone . and at first she could not speak to I . but presently have sit down a little , and receive much praise for her baby , she smile and blush , and find her tongue as if she have never go from we . 
 ' how natural it all look again ! oh , I love this old kitchen so ! baby dear , only look at it wid he pitty , pitty eye , and he tongue out of his mousy ! but who put the flour - riddle up there . and look at the pestle and mortar , and rust I declare in the patty pan ! and a book , positively a dirty book , where the clean skewer ought to hang ! oh , Lizzie , Lizzie , Lizzie ! ' 
 ' you may just as well cease lamenting , ' I say , ' for you can not   alter Lizzie 's nature , and you will only make mother uncomfortable , and perhaps have a quarrel with Lizzie , who be proud as Punch of her housekeeping . ' 
 ' she , ' cry Annie , with all the contempt that could be compress in a syllable . ' well , John , no doubt you be right about it . I will try not to notice thing . but it be a hard thing , after all my care , to see everything go to ruin . but what can be expect of a girl who know all the king of carthage ? ' 
 ' there be no king of Carthage , Annie . they be call , why let I see they be call oh , something else . ' 
 ' never mind what they be call , ' say Annie ; ' will they cook our dinner for we ? but now , John , I be in such trouble . all this talk be make - believe . ' 
 ' do not   you cry , my dear : do not   cry , my darling sister , ' I answer , as she drop into the worn place of the settle , and bent above her infant , rock as if both their heart be one : ' do not   you know , Annie , I can not tell , but I know , or at least I mean , I have hear the man of experience say , it be so bad for the baby . ' 
 ' perhaps I know that as well as you do , John , ' say Annie , look up at I with a gleam of her old laughing : ' but how can I help cry ; I be in such trouble . ' 
 ' tell I what it be , my dear . any grief of yours will vex I greatly ; but I will try to bear it . ' 
 ' then , John , it be just this . Tom have go off with the rebel ; and you must , oh , you must go after he . ' 




 CHAPTER LXIII 

 JOHN be worst by the WOMEN 

 move as I be by Annie 's tear , and gentle style of coax , and most of all by my love for she , I yet declare that I could not go , and leave our house and homestead , far less my dear mother and Lizzie , at the mercy of the merciless Doones . 
 ' be that all your objection , John ? ' ask Annie , in her quick pant way : ' would you go but for that , John ? ' 
 ' now , ' I say , ' be in no such hurry ' for while I be gradually yield , I like to pass it through my finger , as if my finger shape it : ' there be many thing to be think about , and many way of view it . ' 
 ' oh , you never can have love Lorna ! no wonder you give she up so ! John , you can love nobody , but your oat - rick , and your hay - rick . ' 
 ' sister mine , because I rant not , neither rave of what I feel , can you be so shallow as to dream that I feel nothing ? what be your love for Tom Faggus ? what be your love for your baby   pretty darling as he be   to compare with such a love as for ever dwell with I ? because I do not prate of it ; because it be beyond I , not only to express , but even form to my own heart in thought ; because I do not shape my face , and would scorn to play to it , as a thing of acting , and lie it out before you , be you fool enough to think ' but here I stop , have say more than be usual with I . 
 ' I be very sorry , John . Dear John , I be so sorry . what a shallow fool I be ! ' 
 ' I will go seek your husband , ' I say , to change the subject , for even to Annie I would not lay open all my heart about Lorna : ' but only upon condition that you ensure this house and people from the Doones meanwhile . even for the sake of Tom , I can not leave all helpless . the oat - rick and the hay - rick , which be my only love , they be welcome to make cinder of . but I will not have mother treat so ; nor even little Lizzie , although you scorn your sister so . ' 
 ' oh , John , I do think you be the hard , as well as the softest of all the man I know . not even a woman 's bitter word but what you pay she out for . will you never understand that we be not like you , John ? we say all sort of spiteful thing , without a bit of meaning . John , for God 's sake fetch Tom home ; and then revile I as you please , and I will kneel and thank you . ' 
 ' I will not promise to fetch he home , ' I answer , be ashamed of myself for have lose command so : ' but I will promise to do my good , if we can only hit on a plan for leave mother harmless . ' 
 Annie think for a little while , try to gather her smooth clear brow into maternal wrinkle , and then she look at her child , and say , ' I will risk it , for daddy 's sake , darling ; you precious soul , for daddy 's sake . ' I ask she what she be go to risk . she would not tell I ; but take upper hand , and see to my cider - can and bacon , and go from corner to cupboard , exactly as if she have never be marry ; only without an apron on . and then she say , ' now to your mower , John ; and make the most of this fine afternoon ; kiss your godson before you go . ' and I , be use to obey she , in little thing of that sort , kiss the baby , and take my can , and go back to my scythe again . 
 by the time I come home it be dark night , and pour again with a foggy rain , such as we have in July , even more than in January . be soak all through , and through , and with water quelche in my boot , like a pump with a bad bucket , I be only too glad to find Annie 's bright face , and quick figure , flit in and out the firelight , instead of Lizzie sit grandly , with a feast of literature , and not a drop of gravy . mother be in the corner also , with her cheery - colour ribbon glisten very nice by candle - light , look at Annie now and then , with memory of her babyhood ; and then at she have a baby : yet half afraid of praise she much , for fear of that young Lizzie . but Lizzie show no jealousy : she truly love our Annie   now that she be go from we , and she want to know all sort of thing , and she adore the baby . therefore Annie be allow to attend to I , as she use to do . 
 ' now , John , you must start the first thing in the morning , ' she say , when the other have leave the room , but somehow she stick to the baby , ' to fetch I back my rebel , accord to your promise . ' 
 ' not so , ' I reply , mislike the job , ' all I promise be to go , if this house be assure against any onslaught of the Doones . ' 
 ' just so ; and here be that assurance . ' with these word she draw forth a paper , and lay it on my knee with triumph , enjoy my amazement . this , as you may suppose be great ; not only at the document , but also at her possession of it . for in truth it be no less than a formal undertaking , on the part of the Doones , not to attack Plover 's Barrows farm , or mol any of the inmate , or carry off any chattel , during the absence of John Ridd upon a special errand . this document be sign not only by the Counsellor , but by many other Doones : whether Carver 's name be there , I could not say for certain ; as of course he would not sign it under his name of ' Carver , ' and I have never hear Lorna say to what   if any   he have be baptize . 
 in the face of such a deed as this , I could no long refuse to go ; and have receive my promise , Annie tell I   as be only fair   how she have procure that paper . it be both a clever and courageous act ; and would have seem to I , at first sight , far beyond Annie 's power . but none may gauge a woman 's power , when her love and faith be move . 
 the first thing Annie have do be this : she make herself look ugly . this be not an easy thing ; but she have learn a great deal from her husband , upon the subject of disguise . it hurt her feeling not a little to make so sad a fright of herself ; but what could it matter ? if she lose Tom , she must be a far great fright in earnest , than now she be in seem . and then she leave her child asleep , under Betty Muxworthy 's tendance for Betty take to that child , as if there never have be a child before and away she go in her own ' spring - cart '   as the name of that engine prove to be , without a word to any one , except the old man who have drive she from Molland parish that morning , and who coolly take one of our good horse , without ' by your leave ' to any one . 
 Annie make the old man drive she within easy reach of the Doone - gate , whose position she know well enough , from all our talk about it . and there she bid the old man stay , until she should return to he . then with she comely figure hide by a dirty old woman 's cloak , and her fair young face deface by patch and by liniment , so that none might covet she , she address the young man at the gate in a cracked and tremble voice ; and they be scarcely civil to the ' old hag , ' as they call she . she say that she bear important tiding for Sir Counsellor himself , and must be conduct to he . to he accordingly she be lead , without even any hoodwinking , for she have spectacle over her eye , and make believe not to see ten yard . 
 she find Sir Counsellor at home , and when the rest be out of sight , throw off all disguise to he , flash forth as a lovely young woman , from all her wrap and disfigurement . she fling her patch on the floor , amid the old man 's laughter , and let she tuck - up hair come down ; and then go up and kiss he . 
 ' worthy and reverend Counsellor , I have a favour to ask , ' she begin . 
 ' so I should think from your proceeding , ' the old man interrupt ' ah , if I be half my age ' 
 ' if you be , I would not sue so . but most excellent Counsellor , you owe I some amend , you know , for the way in which you rob I . ' 
 ' beyond a doubt I do , my dear . you have put it rather strongly ; and it might offend some people . nevertheless I own my debt , have so fair a creditor . ' 
 ' and do you remember how you sleep , and how much we make of you , and would have see you home , sir ; only you do not wish it ? ' 
 ' and for excellent reason , child . my good escort be in my cloak , after we make the cream to rise . ha , ha ! the unholy spell . my pretty child , have it injure you ? ' 
 ' yes , I fear it have , say Annie ; ' or whence can all my ill luck come ? ' and here she show some sign of cry , know that Counsellor hat it . 
 ' you shall not have ill luck , my dear . I have hear all about your marriage to a very noble highwayman . ah , you make a mistake in that ; you be worthy of a Doone , my child ; your frying be a blessing mean for those who can appreciate . ' 
 ' my husband can appreciate , ' she answer very proudly ; ' but what I wish to know be this , will you try to help I ? ' 
 the Counsellor answer that he would do so , if her need be moderate ; whereupon she open her meaning to he , and tell of all her anxiety . consider that Lorna be go , and her necklace in his possession , and that I   against whom alone of we the Doones could bear any malice   would be out of the way all the while , the old man readily undertake that our house should not be assault , nor our property molest , until my return . and to the promptitude of his pledge , two thing perhaps contribute , namely , that he know not how we be strip of all defender , and that some of his own force be away in the rebel camp . for   as I learn thereafter   the Doones be now in direct feud with the present Government , and sure to be crush if that prevail , have resolve to drop all religious question , and cast in their lot with Monmouth . and the turbulent youth , be long restrain from their wonte outlet for vehemence , by the trooper in the neighbourhood , be only too glad to rush forth upon any promise of blow and excitement . 
 however , Annie know little of this , but take the Counsellor 's pledge as a mark of especial favour in her behalf   which it may have be to some extent , and thank he for it most heartily , and feel that he have earn the necklace ; while he , like an ancient gentleman , disclaim all obligation , and send she under an escort safe to her own cart again . but Annie , repasse the sentinel , with her youth restore and bloom with the flush of triumph , go up to they very gravely , and say , ' the old hag wish you good - evening , gentleman ' ; and so make her good curtsey . 
 now , look at it as I would , there be no excuse leave for I , after the promise give . Dear Annie have not only cheat the Doones , but also have get the good of I , by a pledge to a thing impossible . and I bitterly say , ' I be not like Lorna : a pledge once give , I keep it . '
