 ' I will not have a word against Lorna , ' cry Annie ; ' I will answer for her truth as surely as I would for my own or your , John . ' and with that she vanquish I . 
 but when my poor mother hear that I be commit , by word of honour , to a wild - goose chase , among the rebel , after that runagate Tom Faggus , she simply star , and would not believe it . for lately I have joke with she , in a little style of jerk , as people do when out of sort ; and she , not understand this , and know joke to be out of my power , would only look , and sigh , and toss , and hope that I mean nothing . at last , however , we convince she that I be in earnest , and must be off in the early morning , and leave John Fry with the hay crop . 
 then mother be ready to fall upon Annie , as not content with disgrace we , by wed a man of new honesty   if indeed of any , but lay trap to catch her brother , and entangle he perhaps to his death , for the sake of a worthless fellow ; and ' felon ' she be go to say , as by the shape of her lip I know . but I lay my hand upon dear mother 's lip ; because what must be , must be ; and if mother and daughter stay at home , well in love than in quarrel . 
 right early in the morning , I be off , without word to any one ; know that mother and sister mine have cry each her good self to sleep ; relent when the light be out , and sorry for hard word and thought ; and yet too much alike in nature to understand each other . therefore I take good kickum , who   although with one eye spoil   be worth ten sweet - temper horse , to a man who know how to manage he ; and be well charge both with bacon and powder , forth I set on my wild - goose chase . 
 for this I claim no bravery . I care but little what come of it ; save for mother 's sake , and Annie 's , and the keeping of the farm , and discomfiture of the Snowes , and lament of Lorna at my death , if die I must in a lonesome manner , not find out till afterwards , and bleach bone leave to weep over . however , I have a little kettle , and a pound and a half of tobacco , and two dirty pipe and a clean one ; also a bit of clothe for change , also a brisket of hung venison , and four loaf of farmhouse bread , and of the upper side of bacon a stone and a half it might be not to mention diver small thing for campaigning , which may come in handily , when no one else have get they . 
 we go away in merry style ; my horse be ready for anything , and I only glad of a bit of change , after month of working and brooding ; with no content to crown the work ; no hope to hatch the brooding ; or without hatch to reckon it . who could tell but what Lorna might be discover , or at any rate hear of , before the end of this campaign ; if campaign it could be call of a man who go to fight nobody , only to redeem a runagate ? and vex as I be about the hay , and the hunch - back rick John be sure to make   which spoil the look of a farm - yard , still even this be well than to have the mow and house fire , as I have nightly expect , and be wear out with the worry of it . 
 yet there be one thing rather unfavourable to my present enterprise , namely , that I know nothing of the country I be bind to , nor even in what part of it my business might be suppose to lie . for beside the uncertainty cause by the conflict of report , it be likely that King Monmouth 's army would be move from place to place , accord to the prospect of supply and of reinforcement . however , there would arise more chance of get news as I go on : and my road be towards the east and south , Dulverton would not lie so very far aside of it , but what it might be worth a visit , both to collect the late tiding , and to consult the map and plan in Uncle Reuben 's parlour . therefore I draw the off - hand rein , at the cross - road on the hill , and make for the town ; expect perhaps to have breakfast with Master Huckaback , and Ruth , to help and encourage we . this little maiden be now become a very great favourite with I , have long outgrow , no doubt , her childish fancy and folly , such as my mother and Annie have plant under her soft brown hair . it have be my duty , as well as my true interest   for Uncle Ben be more and more testy , as he go on gold - digging , to ride thither , now and again , to inquire what the doctor think of she . not that her wound be long in healing , but that people can scarcely be too careful and too inquisitive , after a great horse - bite . and she always let I look at the arm , as I have be first doctor ; and she hold it up in a graceful manner , curve at the elbow , and with a sweep of white roundness go to a wrist the size of my thumb or so , and without any thimble - top standing forth , such as even our Annie have . but gradually all I could see , above the elbow , where the bite have be , be very clear , transparent skin , with very firm sweet flesh below , and three little blue mark as far asunder as the prong of a toasting - fork , and no deep than where a twig have chafe the peel of a waxen apple . and then I use to say in fun , as the child do , ' shall I kiss it , to make it well , dear ? ' 
 now Ruth look very grave indeed , upon hearing of this my enterprise ; and cry , say she could almost cry , for the sake of my dear mother . do I know the risk and chance , not of the battlefield alone , but of the havoc afterwards ; the swearing away of innocent life , and the hurdle , and the hanging ? and if I would please not to laugh   which be so unkind of I , have I never hear of imprisonment , and torture with the cruel boot , and sell into slavery , where the sun and the lash outvie one another in cut a man to piece ? I reply that of all these thing I have hear , and would take especial care to steer I free of all of they . my duty be all that I wish to do ; and none could harm I for do that . and I beg my cousin to give I good - speed , instead of talk dolefully . upon this she change her manner wholly , become so lively and cheerful that I be convince of her indifference , and surprise even more than gratify . 
 ' go and earn your spur , Cousin Ridd , ' she say : ' you be strong enough for anything . which side be to have the benefit of your doughty arm ? ' 
 ' have I not tell you , Ruth , ' I answer , not be fond of this kind of talk , more suitable for Lizzie , ' that I do not mean to join either side , that be to say , until ' 
 ' until , as the common proverb go , you know which way the cat will jump . oh , John Ridd ! oh , John Ridd ! ' 
 ' nothing of the sort , ' say I : ' what a hurry you be in ! I be for the King of course . ' 
 ' but not enough to fight for he . only enough to vote , I suppose , or drink his health , or shout for he . ' 
 ' I can not   make you out to - day , Cousin Ruth ; you be nearly as bad as Lizzie . you do not say any bitter thing , but you seem to mean they . ' 
 ' no , cousin , think not so of I . it be far more likely that I say they , without mean they . ' 
 ' anyhow , it be not like you . and I know not what I can have do in any way , to vex you . ' 
 ' dear I , nothing , Cousin Ridd ; you never do anything to vex I . ' 
 ' then I hope I shall do something now , Ruth , when I say good - bye . God know if we ever shall meet again , Ruth : but I hope we may . ' 
 ' to be sure we shall , ' she answer in her bright manner . ' try not to look wretched , John : you be as happy as a Maypole . ' 
 ' and you as a rose in May , ' I say ; ' and pretty nearly as pretty . give my love to Uncle Ben ; and I trust he to keep on the win side . ' 
 ' of that you need have no misgiving . never yet have he fail of it . now , Cousin Ridd , why go you not ? you hurry I so at breakfast time ? ' 
 ' my only reason for waiting , Ruth , be that you have not kiss I , as you be almost bind to do , for the last time perhaps of see I . ' 
 ' oh , if that be all , just fetch the stool ; and I will do my good , cousin . ' 
 ' I pray you be not so vexatious ; you always use to do it nicely , without any stool , Ruth . ' 
 ' ah , but you be grow since then , and become a famous man , John Ridd , and a member of the nobility . go your way , and win your spur . I want no lip - service . ' 
 be at the end of my wit , I do even as she order I . at least I have no spur to win , because there be big one on my boot , pay for in the Easter bill , and make by a famous saddler , so as never to clog with marsh - weed , but prick as hard as any horse , in reason , could desire . and Kickums never want spur ; but always go tail - foremost , if anybody offer they for his consideration . 




 CHAPTER LXIV 

 slaughter in the MARSHES 

 we rattle away at a merry pace , out of the town of Dulverton ; my horse be gaily feed , and myself quite fit again for go . of course I be puzzle about Cousin Ruth ; for her behaviour be not at all such as I have expect ; and indeed I have hop for a far more loving and move farewell than I get from she . but I say to myself , ' it be useless ever to count upon what a woman will do ; and I think that I must have vex she , almost as much as she vex I . and now to see what come of it . ' so I put my horse across the moorland ; and he throw his chest out bravely . 
 now if I try to set down at length all the thing that happen to I , upon this adventure , every in and out , and up and down , and to and fro , that occupy I , together with the thing I see , and the thing I hear of , however much the wise people might applaud my narrative , it be likely enough that idle reader might exclaim , ' what ail this man ? know he not that man of part and of real understanding , have tell we all we care to hear of that miserable business . let he keep to his farm , and his bacon , and his wrestling , and constant feeding . ' 
 fear to meet with such rebuff   which after my death would vex I , I will try to set down only what be needful for my story , and the clearing of my character , and the good name of our parish . but the manner in which I be bandy about , by false information , from pillar to post , or at other time drive quite out of my way by the presence of the King 's soldier , may be know by the name of the follow town , to which I be send in succession , Bath , Frome , Wells , Wincanton , Glastonbury , Shepton , Bradford , Axbridge , Somerton , and Bridgwater . 
 this last place I reach on a Sunday night , the fourth or fifth of July , I think or it might be the sixth , for that matter ; inasmuch as I have be too much worried to get the day of the month at church . only I know that my horse and myself be glad to come to a decent place , where meat and corn could be have for money ; and be quite weary of wander about , we hop to rest there a little . 
 of this , however , we find no chance , for the town be full of the good Duke 's soldier ; if man may be call so , the half of whom have never be drill , nor have fire a gun . and it be rumour among they , that the ' popish army , ' as they call it , be to be attack that very night , and with God 's assistance beat . however , by this time I have be teach to pay little attention to rumour ; and have seek vainly for Tom Faggus among these poor rustic warrior , I take to my hostel ; and go to bed , be as weary as weary can be . 
 fall asleep immediately , I take heed of nothing ; although the town be all alive , and light have come glance , as I lie down , and shout make echo all round my room . but all I do be to bolt the door ; not an inch would I budge , unless the house , and even my bed , be on fire . and so for several hour I lay , in the depth of the deep slumber , without even a dream on its surface ; until I be rouse and awaken at last by a pushing , and pull , and pinch , and a plucking of hair out by the root . and at length , be able to open mine eye , I see the old landlady , with a candle , heavily wonder at I . 
 ' can not   you let I alone ? ' I grumble . ' I have pay for my bed , mistress ; and I will not   get up for any one . ' 
 ' would to God , young man , ' she answer , shake I as hard as ever , ' that the popish soldier may sleep this night , only half as strong as thou dost ! fie on thee , fie on thee ! get up , and go fight ; we can hear the battle already ; and a man of thy size mought stop a cannon . ' 
 ' I would rather stop a - bed , ' say I ; ' what have I to do with fighting ? I be for King James , if any . ' 
 ' then thou mayest even stop a - bed , ' the old woman mutter sulkily . ' a would never have labour half an hour to awake a Papisher . but hearken you one thing , young man ; Zummerzett thou art , by thy brogue ; or at least by thy understanding of it ; no Zummerzett maid will look at thee , in spite of thy size and stature , unless thou strik a blow this night . ' 
 ' I lack no Zummerzett maid , mistress : I have a fair than your brown thing ; and for she alone would I strike a blow . ' 
 at this the old woman give I up , as be beyond correction : and it vex I a little that my great fame have not reach so far as Bridgwater , when I think that it go to Bristowe . but those people in East Somerset know nothing about wrestling . Devon be the headquarters of the art ; and Devon be the county of my chief love . Howbeit , my vanity be move , by this slur upon it for I have tell she my name be John Ridd , when I have a gallon of ale with she , ere ever I come upstairs ; and she have nod , in such a manner , that I think she know both name and fame and here be I , not only shake , pinch , and with many hair pull out , in the midst of my first good sleep for a week , but also abuse , and take amiss , and   which vex I most of all   unknown . 
 now there be nothing like vanity to keep a man awake at night , however he be weary ; and most of all , when he believe that he be do something great this time , if never do before yet other people will not see , except what they may laugh at ; and so be far above he , and sleep themselves the happy . therefore their sleep rob his own ; for all thing play so , in and out   with the godly and ungodly ever move in a balance , as they have do in my time , almost every year or two , all thing have such nice reply of produce to the call for it , and such a spread across the world , give here and take there , yet on the whole pretty even , that haply sleep itself have but a certain stock , and keep in hand , and sell to flatter   which can pay   that which flatten vanity can not pay , and will not sue for . 
 be that as it may , I be by this time wide awake , though much aggrieve at feel so , and through the open window hear the distant roll of musketry , and the beating of drum , with a quick rub - a - dub , and the ' come round the corner ' of trumpet - call . and perhaps Tom Faggus might be there , and shoot at any moment , and my dear Annie leave a poor widow , and my godson Jack an orphan , without a tooth to help he . 
 therefore I revile myself for all my heavy laziness ; and partly through good honest will , and partly through the sting of pride , and yet a little perhaps by virtue of a young man 's love of riot , up I arise , and dress myself , and wake Kickums   who be snore , and set out to see the bad of it . the sleepy hostler scratch his poll , and could not tell I which way to take ; what odd to he who be King , or Pope , so long as he pay his way , and get a bit of bacon on Sunday ? and would I please to remember that I have rouse he up at night , and the quality always make a point of pay four time over for a man 's loss of his beauty - sleep . I reply that his loss of beauty - sleep be rather improve to a man of so high complexion ; and that I , be none of the quality , must pay half - quality price : and so I give he double fee , as become a good farmer ; and he be glad to be quit of Kickums ; as I see by the turn of his eye , while go out at the archway . 
 all this be do by lanthorn light , although the moon be high and bold ; and in the northern heaven , flag and ribbon of a jostling pattern ; such as we often have in autumn , but in July very rarely . of these Master Dryden have speak somewhere , in his courtly manner ; but of he I think so little because by fashion prefer to Shakespeare that I can not remember the passage ; neither be it a credit to he . 
 therefore I be guide mainly by the sound of gun and trumpet , in rid out of the narrow way , and into the open marsh . and thus I might have find my road , in spite of all the spread of water , and the glaze of moonshine ; but that , as I follow sound   far from hedge or causeway , fog   like a chestnut - tree in blossom , touch with moonlight   meet I . now fog be a thing that I understand , and can do with well enough , where I know the country ; but here I have never be before . it be nothing to our Exmoor fog ; not to be compare with they ; and all the time one could see the moon ; which we can not do in our fog ; nor even the sun , for a week together . yet the gleam of water always make the fog more difficult : like a curtain on a mirror ; none can tell the boundary . 
 and here we have broad - water patch , in and out , inlay on land , like mother - of - pearl in brown Shittim wood . to a wild duck , bear and breed there , it would almost be a puzzle to find her own nest amongst we ; what chance then have I and kickum , both unused to marsh and mere ? each time when we think that we must be right , now at last , by track or passage , and approach the conflict , with the sound of it wax near , suddenly a break of water would be lay before we , with the moon look mildly over it , and the northern light behind we , dance down the line of fog . 
 it be an awful thing , I say   and to this day I remember it , to hear the sound of rag fight , and the yell of rave slayer , and the howl of poor man stricken hard , and shatter from wrath to wail ; then suddenly the dead low hush , as of a soul depart , and spirit kneel over it . through the vapour of the earth , and white breath of the water , and beneath the pale round moon   bow as the drift go by , all this rush and pause of fear pass or linger on my path . 
 at last , when I almost despair of escape from this tangle of spongy bank , and of hazy creek , and reed - fringe , my horse hear the neigh of a fellow - horse , and be only too glad to answer it ; upon which the other , have lose its rider , come up and prick his ear at we , and gaze through the fog very steadfastly . therefore I encourage he with a soft and genial whistle , and Kickums do his good to tempt he with a snort of inquiry . however , nothing would suit that nag , except to enjoy his new freedom ; and he caper away with his tail set on high , and the stirrup - iron clash under he . therefore , as he might know the way , and appear to have be in the battle , we follow he very carefully ; and he lead we to a little hamlet , call   as I find afterwards   West Zuyland , or Zealand , so name perhaps from its situation amid this inland sea . 
 here the King 's troop have be quite lately , and their fire be still burn ; but the man themselves have be summon away by the night attack of the rebel . hence I procure for my guide a young man who know the district thoroughly , and who lead I by many intricate way to the rear of the rebel army . we come upon a broad open moor strip with sullen water course , shag with sedge , and yellow iris , and in the dry part with bilberry . for by this time it be four o'clock , and the summer sun , rise wanly , show we all the ghastly scene . 
 would that I have never be there ! often in the lonely hour , even now it haunt I : would , far more , that the piteous thing have never be do in England ! fly man , fling back from dream of victory and honour , only glad to have the luck of life and limb to fly with , mud - bedraggle , foul with slime , reek both with sweat and blood , which they could not stop to wipe , cursing , with their pump - out lung , every stick that hinder they , or gory puddle that slip the step , scarcely able to leap over the corse that have drag to die . and to see how the corse lay ; some , as fair as death in sleep ; with the smile of placid valour , and of noble manhood , hover yet on the silent lip . these have bloodless hand put upwards , white as wax , and firm as death , clasp   as on a monument   in prayer for dear one leave behind , or in high thanksgiving . and of these man there be nothing in their broad blue eye to fear . but other be of different sort ; simple fellow unused to pain , accustom to the bill - hook , perhaps , or rasp of the knuckle in a quick - set hedge , or make some to - do at breakfast , over a thumb cut in sharpen a scythe , and expect their wife to make more to - do . yet here lie these poor chap , dead ; dead , after a deal of pain , with little mind to bear it , and a soul they have never think of ; gone , their God alone know whither ; but to mercy we may trust . upon these thing I can not dwell ; and none I trow would ask I : only if a plain man see what I see that morning , he   if God have bless he with the heart that be in most of we   must have sicken of all desire to be great among mankind . 
 see I rid to the front   where the work of death go on among the man of true english pluck ; which , when move , no farth move , the fugitive call out to I , in half a dozen dialect , to make no utter fool of myself ; for the great gun be come , and the fight be over ; all the rest be slaughter . 
 ' arl oop wi Moonmo ' , ' shout one big fellow , a miner of the Mendip hill , whose weapon be a pickaxe : ' na oose to vaight na moor . wend thee hame , yoong mon agin . ' 
 upon this I stop my horse , desire not to be shoot for nothing ; and eager to aid some poor sick people , who try to lift their arm to I . and this I do to the good of my power , though void of skill in the business ; and more inclined to weep with they than to check their weeping . while I be give a drop of cordial from my flask to one poor fellow , who sit up , while his life be ebb , and with slow insistence urge I , when his broken voice would come , to tell his wife   whose name I know not   something about an apple - tree , and a golden guinea store in it , to divide among six child in the midst of this I feel warm lip lay against my cheek quite softly , and then a little push ; and behold it be a horse lean over I ! I arise in haste , and there stand Winnie , look at I with beseech eye , enough to melt a heart of stone . then see my attention fix she turn her head , and glance back sadly toward the place of battle , and give a little wistful neigh : and then look I full in the face again , as much as to say , ' do you understand ? ' while she scrap with one hoof impatiently . if ever a horse try hard to speak , it be Winnie at that moment . I go to her side and pat she ; but that be not what she want . then I offer to leap into the empty saddle ; but neither do that seem good to she : for she run away toward the part of the field at which she have be glance back , and then turn round , and shake her mane , entreat I to follow she . 
 upon this I learn from the die man where to find his apple - tree , and promise to add another guinea to the one in store for his child ; and so , commend he to God , I mount my own horse again , and to Winnie 's great delight , profess myself at her service . with her ring silvery neigh , such as no other horse of all I ever know could equal , she at once proclaim her triumph , and tell her master   or mean to tell , if death should not have close his ear   that she be come to his aid , and bring one who might be trust , of the high race that kill . 
 a cannon - bullet   fire low , and plough the marsh slowly   meet poor Winnie front to front ; and she , be as quick as thought , lower her nose to sniff at it . it might be a message from her master ; for it make a mournful noise . but luckily for Winnie 's life , a rise of wet ground take the ball , even under her very nose ; and there it cut a splashy groove , miss she off hindfoot by an inch , and scatter black mud over she . it frighten I much more than Winnie ; of that I be quite certain : because though I be firm enough , when it come to a real tussle , and the heart of a fellow warm up and tell he that he must go through with it ; yet I never do approve of make a cold pie of death . 
 therefore , with those reckless cannon , brazen - mouthed , and bellowing , two furlong off , or it might be more   and the more the merry , I would have give that year 's hay - crop for a bit of a hill , or a thicket of oak , or almost even a badger 's earth . People will call I a coward for this   especially when I have make up my mind , that life be not worth have without any sign of Lorna ; nevertheless , I can not help it : those be my feeling ; and I set they down , because they make a mark on I . at Glen Doone I have fight , even against cannon , with some spirit and fury : but now I see nothing to fight about ; but rather in every poor double corpse , a good reason for not fight . so , in cold blood rid on , and yet ashamed that a man should shrink where a horse go bravely , I cast a bitter blame upon the reckless way of Winnie . 
 nearly all be scatter now . of the noble countryman   arm with scythe or pickaxe , blacksmith 's hammer , or fold - pitcher , who have stand their ground for hour against blaze musketry   from man whom they could not get at , by reason of the water - dyke , and then against the deadly cannon , drag by the Bishop 's horse to slaughter his own sheep ; of these sturdy englishman , noble in their want of sense , scarce one out of four remain for the coward to shoot down . ' cross the rhaine , ' they shout out , ' cross the rhaine , and coom within rache : ' but the other mongrel Britons , with a mongrel at their head , find it pleasanter to shoot man who could not shoot in answer , than to meet the chance of mischief from strong arm , and strong heart . 
 the last scene of this piteous play be act , just as I ride up . broad daylight , and upstande sun , winnow fog from the eastern hill , and spread the moor with freshness ; all along the dyke they shine , glisten on the willow - trunk , and touch the bank with a hoary gray . but alas ! those bank be touch more deeply with a gory red , and strew with fall trunk , more woeful than the wreck of tree ; while howl , cursing , yelling , and the loathsome reek of carnage , drown the scent of the new - mow hay , and the carol of the lark . 
 then the cavalry of the King , with their horse at full speed , dash from either side upon the helpless mob of countryman . a few pike feebly level meet they ; but they shoot the pikemen , draw sword , and helter - skelter leap into the shattered and scatter mass . right and leave they hack and hew ; I could hear the snapping of scythe beneath they , and see the flash of their sweeping sword . how it must end be plain enough , even to one like myself , who have never behold such a battle before . but Winnie lead I away to the left ; and as I could not help the people , neither stop the slaughter , but find the cannon - bullet come very rudely nigh I , I be only too glad to follow she . 




 CHAPTER LXV 

 fall among LAMBS 

 that faithful creature , whom I begin to admire as if she be my own   which be no little thing for a man to say of another man 's horse , stop in front of a low black shed , such as we call a ' linhay . ' and here she utter a little greeting , in a subdued and soften voice , hop to obtain an answer , such as her master be will not to give in a cheery manner . receive no reply , she enter ; and I   who could scarce keep up with she , poor Kickums be weary   leap from his back , and follow . there I find she sniff gently , but with great emotion , at the body of Tom Faggus . a corpse poor Tom appear to be , if ever there be one in this world ; and I turn away , and feel unable to keep altogether from weeping . but the mare either could not understand , or else would not believe it . she reach her long neck forth , and feel he with she under lip , pass it over his skin as softly as a mother would do to an infant ; and then she look up at I again ; as much as to say , ' he be all right . ' 
 upon this I take courage , and handle poor Tom , which be young I have fear at first to do . he groan very feebly , as I raise he up ; and there be the wound , a great savage one   whether from pike - thrust or musket - ball , gap and well in his right side , from which a piece seem to be tear away . I bind it up with some of my linen , so far as I know how ; just to stanch the flow of blood , until we could get a doctor . then I give he a little weak brandy and water , which he drink with the great eagerness , and make sign to I for more of it . but not know how far it be right to give cordial under the circumstance , I hand he unmixe water that time ; think that he be too far go to perceive the difference . but herein I wrong Tom Faggus ; for he shake his head and frown at I . even at the door of death , he would not drink what Adam drink , by whom come death into the world . so I give he a little more eau - de - vie , and he take it most submissively . 
 after that he seem well , and a little colour come into his cheek ; and he look at Winnie and know she ; and would have her nose in his clammy hand , though I think it not good for either of they . with the stay of my arm he sit upright , and faintly look about he ; as if at the end of a violent dream , too much for his power of mind . then he manage to whisper , ' be Winnie hurt ? ' 
 ' as sound as a roach , ' I answer . ' then so be I , ' say he : ' put I upon she back , John ; she and I die together . ' 
 surprise as I be at this fatalism   for so it appear to I , of which he have often show symptom before   but I take they for mere levity , now I know not what to do ; for it seem to I a murderous thing to set such a man on horseback ; where he must surely bleed to death , even if he could keep the saddle . but he tell I , with many break and pause , that unless I obey his order , he would tear off all my bandage , and accept no further aid from I . 
 while I be yet hesitate , a storm of horse at full gallop go by , tearing , swearing , bear away all the country before they . only a little pollard hedge keep we from their blood - shoot eye . ' now be the time , ' say my cousin Tom , so far as I could make out his word ; on their heel , I be safe , John , if I have only Winnie under I . Winnie and I die together . ' 
 see this strong bent of his mind , strong than any pain of death , I even do what his feeble eye sometimes implore , and sometimes command . with a strong sash , from his own hot neck , bind and twisted , tight as wax , around his damaged waist , I set he upon Winnie 's back , and place his trembling foot in stirrup , with a band from one to another , under the good mare 's body ; so that no swerve could throw he out : and then I say , ' lean forward , Tom ; it will stop your hurt from bleed . ' he lean almost on the neck of the mare , which , as I know , must close the wound ; and the light of his eye be quite different , and the pain of his forehead unstrung itself , as if he feel the undulous readiness of her volatile pace under he . 
 ' God bless you , John ; I be safe , ' he whisper , fear to open his lung much : ' who can come near my Winnie mare ? a mile of her gallop be ten year of life . look out for yourself , John Ridd . ' he suck his lip , and the mare go off , as easy and swift as a swallow . 
 ' well , ' think I , as I look at kickum , ignobly crop up a bit of grass , ' I have do a very good thing , no doubt , and ought to be thankful to God for the chance . but as for get away unharmed , with all these scoundrel about I , and only a foundered horse to trust in good and spiteful as he be upon the whole , I begin to think that I have make a fool of myself , accord to my habit . no wonder Tom say , " look out for yourself ! " I shall look out from a prison window , or perhaps even out of a halter . and then , what will Lorna think of I ? ' 
 be in this wistful mood , I resolve to abide awhile , even where fate have throw I ; for my horse require good rest no doubt , and be take it even while he crop , with his hind leg far away stretch out , and his foreleg gather under he , and his muzzle on the mole - hill ; so that he have five supporting from his mother earth . moreover , the linhay itself be full of very ancient cow dung ; than which there be no balmy and more maiden soporific . hence I resolve , upon the whole , though grieve about breakfast , to light a pipe , and go to sleep ; or at least until the hot sun should arouse the fly . 
 I may have sleep three hour , or four , or it might be even five for I never count time , while sleep when a shake more rude than the old landlady 's , bring I back to the world again . I look up , with a mighty yawn ; and see twenty , or so , of foot - soldier . 
 ' this linhay be not your , ' I say , when they have quite arouse I , with tongue , and hand , and even sword - prick : ' what business have you here , good fellow ? ' 
 ' business bad for you , ' say one , ' and will lead you to the gallow . ' 
 ' do you wish to know the way out again ? ' I ask , very quietly , as be no braggadocio . 
 ' we will show thee the way out , ' say one , ' and the way out of the world , ' say another : ' but not the way to heaven , ' say one chap , most unlikely to know it : and thereupon they all fall wag , like a bed of clover leaf in the morning , at their own choice humour . 
 ' will you pile your arm outside , ' I say , ' and try a bit of fair play with I ? ' 
 for I dislike these man sincerely , and be fain to teach they a lesson ; they be so unchristian in appearance , have face of a coffee colour , and dirty beard half over they . moreover their dress be outrageous , and their address still bad . however , I have wiser let they alone , as will appear afterwards . these savage - look fellow laugh at the idea of my have any chance against some twenty of they : but I know that the place be in my favour ; for my part of it have be fence off   for wean a calf most likely , so that only two could come at I at once ; and I must be very much out of training , if I could not manage two of they . therefore I lay aside my carbine , and the two horse - pistol ; and they with many coarse joke at I go a little way outside , and set their weapon against the wall , and turn up their coat sleeve jauntily ; and then begin to hesitate . 
 ' go you first , Bob , ' I hear they say : ' you be the big man of we ; and Dick the wrestler along of you . we will back you up , boy . ' 
 ' I will warrant I will draw the badger , ' say Bob ; ' and not a tooth will I leave he . but mind , for the honour of Kirke 's lamb , every man stand I a glass of gin . ' then he , and another man , make a rush , and the other come double - quick - march on their heel . but as Bob run at I most stupidly , not even know how to place his hand , I catch he with my knuckle at the back of his neck , and with all the sway of my right arm send he over the head of his comrade . meanwhile Dick the wrestler have grapple I , expect to show off his art , of which indeed he have some small knowledge ; but be quite of the light - weight , in a second he be fly after his companion Bob . 
 now these two man be hurt so badly , the light one have knock his head against the lintel of the outer gate , that the rest have no desire to encounter the like misfortune . so they hang back whispering ; and before they have make up their mind , I rush into the midst of they . the suddenness and the weight of my onset take they wholly by surprise ; and for once in their life , perhaps , Kirke 's lamb be worthy of their name . like a flock of sheep at a dog 's attack they fall away , hustle one another , and my only difficulty be not to tumble over they . 
 I have take my carbine out with I , have a fondness for it ; but the two horse - pistol I leave behind ; and therefore feel good title to take two from the magazine of the lamb . and with these , and my carbine , I leap upon kickum , who be now quite glad of a gallop again ; and I bade adieu to that mongrel lot ; yet they have the meanness to shoot at I . thank God for my deliverance   inasmuch as those man would have string I up , from a pollard - ash without trial , as I hear they tell one another , and see the tree they have settle upon , I venture to go rather fast on my way , with doubt and uneasiness urge I . and now my way be home again . nobody could say but what I have do my duty , and rescue Tom   if he could be rescue   from the mischief into which his own perverseness and love of change   rather than deep religious conviction , to which our Annie ascribe his outbreak   have lead , or seem likely to lead he . and how proud would my mother be ; and ah well , there be nobody else to be proud of I now . 
 but while think these thing , and desire my breakfast , beyond any power of describing , and even beyond my remembrance , I fall into another fold of lamb , from which there be no exit . these , like true crusader , meet I , swagger very heartily , and with their barrel of cider set , like so many cannon , across the road , over against a small hostel . 
 ' we have win the victory , my lord King , and we mean to enjoy it . down from thy horse , and have a stoup of cider , thou big rebel . ' 
 ' no rebel be i. my name be John Ridd . I belong to the side of the king : and I want some breakfast . ' 
 these fellow be truly hospitable ; that much will I say for they . be accustom to arab way , they could toss a grill , or fritter , or the inner meaning of an egg , into any form they please , comely and very good to eat ; and it lead I to think of Annie . so I make the rare breakfast any man might hope for , after all his trouble ; and get on with these brown fellow well than could be expect , I crave permission to light a pipe , if not disagreeable . hear this , they roar at I , with a superior laughter , and ask I , whether or not , I know the tobacco - leaf from the chick - weed ; and when I be force to answer no , not have go into the subject , but be content with anything brown , they clap I on the back and swear they have never see any one like I . upon the whole this please I much ; for I do not wish to be take always as of the common pattern : and so we smoke admirable tobacco for they would not have any of mine , though very courteous concern it and I be begin to understand a little of what they tell I ; when up come those confound lamb , who have show more tail than head to I , in the linhay , as I mention . 
 now these man upset everything . have be among wrestler so much as my duty compel I to be , and have learn the necessity of the rest which follow the conflict , and the right of discussion which all people have to pay their sixpence to enter ; and how they obtrude this right , and their wisdom , upon the man who have labour , until he forget all the work he do , and begin to think that they do it ; have some knowledge of this sort of thing , and the flux of mind swim in liquor , I foresaw a brawl , as plainly as if it be Bear Street in Barnstaple . 
 and a brawl there be , without any error , except of the man who hit their friend , and those who defend their enemy . my partner in breakfast and beer - can swore that I be no prisoner , but the good and most loyal subject , and the fine - hearted fellow they have ever the luck to meet with . whereas the man from the linhay swear that I be a rebel miscreant ; and have I they would , with a rope's - end ready , in spite of every   violent language   who have get drunk at my expense , and be mislead by my   strong word   lie . 
 while this fight be go on   and its mere occurrence show , perhaps , that my conversation in those day be not entirely despicable else why should my new friend fight for I , when I have pay for the ale , and therefore win the wrong tense of gratitude ?   it be in my power at any moment to take horse and go . and this would have be my wise plan , and a very great saving of money ; but somehow I feel as if it would be a mean thing to slip off so . even while I be hesitate , and the man be break each other 's head , a superior officer ride up , with his sword draw , and his face on fire . 
 ' what , my lamb , my lamb ! ' he cry , smite with the flat of his sword ; ' be this how you waste my time and my purse , when you ought to be catch a hundred prisoner , worth ten pound apiece to I ? who be this young fellow we have here ? speak up , sirrah ; what art thou , and how much will thy good mother pay for thee ? ' 
 ' my mother will pay naught for I , ' I answer ; while the lamb fall back , and glower at one another : ' so please your worship , I be no rebel ; but an honest farmer , and well - prove of loyalty . ' 
 ' ha , ha ; a farmer art thou ? those fellow always pay the good . good farmer , come to yon barren tree ; thou shalt make it fruitful . ' 
 Colonel Kirke make a sign to his man , and before I could think of resistance , stout new rope be fling around I ; and with three man on either side I be lead along very painfully . and now I see , and repent deeply of my careless folly , in stop with those boon - companion , instead of be far away . but the newness of their manner to I , and their mode of regard the world   differ so much from mine own , as well as the flavour of their tobacco , have make I quite forget my duty to the farm and to myself . yet methought they would be tender to I , after all our speech : how then be I disappointed , when the man who have drink my beer , draw on those grievous rope , twice as hard as the man I have be at strife with ! yet this may have be from no ill will ; but simply that have fall under suspicion of laxity , they be compel , in self - defence , now to be over - zealous . 
 nevertheless , however pure and godly might be their motif , I behold myself in a grievous case , and likely to get the bad of it . for the face of the Colonel be hard and stern as a block of bogwood oak ; and though the man might pity I and think I unjustly execute , yet they must obey their order , or themselves be put to death . therefore I address myself to the Colonel , in a most ingratiating manner ; beg he not to sully the glory of his victory , and dwell upon my pure innocence , and even good service to our lord the King . but Colonel Kirke only give command that I should be smite in the mouth ; which office Bob , whom I have fling so hard out of the linhay , perform with great zeal and efficiency . but be aware of the come smack , I thrust forth a pair of tooth ; upon which the knuckle of my good friend make a melancholy shipwreck . 
 it be not in my power to tell half the thought that move I , when we come to the fatal tree , and see two man hang there already , as innocent perhaps as I be , and henceforth entirely harmless . though order by the Colonel to look steadfastly upon they , I could not bear to do so ; upon which he call I a paltry coward , and promise my breech to any man who would spit upon my countenance . this vile thing Bob , be anger perhaps by the smart wound of his knuckle , bravely step forward to do for I , trust no doubt to the rope I be lead with . but , unluckily as it prove for he , my right arm be free for a moment ; and therewith I deal he such a blow , that he never spake again . for this thing I have often grieve ; but the provocation be very sore to the pride of a young man ; and I trust that God have forgive I . at the sound and sight of that bitter stroke , the other man draw back ; and Colonel Kirke , now black in the face with fury and vexation , give order for to shoot I , and cast I into the ditch hard by . the man raise their piece , and point at I , wait for the word to fire ; and I , be quite overcome by the hurry of these event , and quite unprepared to die yet , could only think all upside down about Lorna , and my mother , and wonder what each would say to it . I spread my hand before my eye , not be so brave as some man ; and hop , in some foolish way , to cover my heart with my elbow . I hear the breath of all around , as if my skull be a sound - board ; and know even how the different man be finger their trigger . and a cold sweat break all over I , as the Colonel , prolong his enjoyment , begin slowly to say , ' fire . ' 
 but while he be yet dwell on the ' f , ' the hoof of a horse dash out on the road , and horse and horseman fling themselves betwixt I and the gun muzzle . so narrowly be I save that one man could not check his trigger : his musket go off , and the ball strike the horse on the wither , and scar he exceedingly . he begin to lash out with his heel all around , and the Colonel be glad to keep clear of he ; and the man make excuse to lower their gun , not really wish to shoot I . 
 ' how now , Captain Stickles ? ' cry Kirke , the more angry because he have show his cowardice ; ' dare you , sir , to come betwixt I and my lawful prisoner ? ' 
 ' Nay , hearken one moment , Colonel , ' reply my old friend Jeremy ; and his damage voice be the sweetest sound I have hear for many a day ; ' for your own sake , hearken . ' he look so full of momentous tiding , that Colonel Kirke make a sign to his man not to shoot I till further order ; and then he go aside with Stickles , so that in spite of all my anxiety I could not catch what pass between they . but I fancy that the name of the Lord Chief - Justice Jeffreys be speak more than once , and with emphasis and deference . 
 ' then I leave he in your hand , Captain Stickles , ' say Kirke at last , so that all might hear he ; and though the news be good for I , the smile of baffle malice make his dark face look most hideous ; ' and I shall hold you answerable for the custody of this prisoner . ' 
 ' Colonel Kirke , I will answer for he , ' Master Stickles reply , with a grave bow , and one hand on his breast : ' John Ridd , you be my prisoner . follow I , John Ridd . ' 
 upon that , those precious lamb flock away , leave the rope still around I ; and some be glad , and some be sorry , not to see I swing . be free of my arm again , I touch my hat to Colonel Kirke , as become his rank and experience ; but he do not condescend to return my short salutation , have espy in the distance a prisoner , out of whom he might make money . 
 I wring the hand of Jeremy Stickles , for his truth and goodness ; and he almost weep   for since his wound he have be a weakened man   as he answer , ' turn for turn , John . you save my life from the Doones ; and by the mercy of God , I have save you from a far bad company . let your sister Annie know it . ' 




 CHAPTER lxvi 

 suitable DEVOTION 

 now Kickums be not like Winnie , any more than a man be like a woman ; and so he have not follow my fortune , except at his own distance . no doubt but what he feel a certain interest in I ; but his interest be not devotion ; and man might go his way and be hang , rather than horse would meet hardship . therefore , see thing to be bad , and his master involve in trouble , what do this horse do but start for the ease and comfort of Plover 's barrow , and the plentiful ration of oat abide in his own manger . for this I do not blame he . it be the manner of mankind . 
 but I could not help be very uneasy at the thought of my mother 's discomfort and worry , when she should spy this good horse come home , without any master , or rider , and I almost hop that he might be catch   although he be worth at least twenty pound   by some of the King 's trooper , rather than find his way home , and spread distress among our people . yet , know his nature , I doubt if any could catch , or catch would keep he . 
 Jeremy Stickles assure I , as we take the road to Bridgwater , that the only chance for my life   if I still refuse to fly   be to obtain an order forthwith , for my despatch to London , as a suspect person indeed , but not find in open rebellion , and believe to be under the patronage of the great Lord Jeffreys . ' for , ' say he , ' in a few hour time you would fall into the hand of Lord Feversham , who have win this fight , without see it , and who have return to bed again , to have his breakfast more comfortably . now he may not be quite so savage perhaps as Colonel Kirke , nor find so much sport in gibbeting ; but he be equally pitiless , and his price no doubt would be high . ' 
 ' I will pay no price whatever , ' I answer , ' neither will I fly . an hour agone I would have flee for the sake of my mother , and the farm . but now that I have be take prisoner , and my name be know , if I fly , the farm be forfeit ; and my mother and sister must starve . moreover , I have do no harm ; I have bear no weapon against the king , nor desire the success of his enemy . I like not that the son of a bona - roba should be King of England ; neither do I count the Papists any bad than we be . if they have aught to try I for , I will stand my trial . ' 
 ' then to London thou must go , my son . there be no such thing as trial here : we hang the good folk without it , which save they much anxiety . but quicken thy step , good John ; I have influence with Lord Churchill , and we must contrive to see he , ere the foreigner fall to work again . Lord Churchill be a man of sense , and imprison nothing but his money . ' 
 we be lucky enough to find this nobleman , who have since become so famous by his foreign victory . he receive we with great civility ; and look at I with much interest , be a tall and fine young man himself , but not to compare with I in size , although far well favour . I like his face well enough , but think there be something false about it . he put I a few keen question , such as a man not assure of honesty might have find hard to answer ; and he stand in a very upright attitude , make the most of his figure . 
 I see nothing to be proud of , at the moment , in this interview ; but since the great Duke of Marlborough rise to the top of glory , I have try to remember more about he than my conscience quite back up . how should I know that this man would be foremost of our kingdom in five - and - twenty year or so ; and not know , why should I heed he , except for my own pocket ? nevertheless , I have be so cross - question far bad than by young Lord Churchill about his grace the Duke of Marlborough , and what he say to I , and what I say then , and how his grace reply to that , and whether he smile like another man , or screw up his lip like a button   as our parish tailor say of he , and whether I know from the turn of his nose that no frenchman could stand before he : all these inquiry have worry I so , ever since the Battle of Blenheim , that if tailor would only print upon waistcoat , I would give double price for a vest bearing this inscription , ' no information can be give about the Duke of Marlborough . ' 
 now this good Lord Churchill for one might call he good , by comparison with the very bad people around he grant without any long hesitation the order for my safe deliverance to the Court of King 's Bench at Westminster ; and Stickles , who have to report in London , be empower to convey I , and make answerable for produce I . this arrangement would have be entirely to my liking , although the time of year be bad for leave Plover 's barrow so ; but no man may quite choose his time , and on the while I would have be quite content to visit London , if my mother could be warn that nothing be amiss with I , only a mild , and as one might say , nominal captivity . and to prevent her anxiety , I do my good to send a letter through good Sergeant Bloxham , of whom I hear as quarter with Dumbarton 's regiment at Chedzuy . but that regiment be away in pursuit ; and I be force to entrust my letter to a man who say that he know he , and accept a shilling to see to it . 
 for fear of any unpleasant change , we set forth at once for London ; and truly thankful may I be that God in his mercy spar I the sight of the cruel and bloody work with which the whole country reek and howl during the next fortnight . I have hear thing that set my hair on end , and make I loathe good meat for day ; but I make a point of set down only the thing which I see do ; and in this particular case , not many will quarrel with my decision . enough , therefore , that we ride on   for Stickles have find I a horse at last   as far as Wells , where we sleep that night ; and be join in the morning by several trooper and orderly , we make a slow but safe journey to London , by way of Bath and Reading . 
 the sight of London warm my heart with various emotion , such as a cordial man must draw from the heart of all humanity . here there be quick way and manner , and the rapid sense of knowledge , and the power of understanding , ere a word be speak . whereas at Oare , you must say a thing three time , very slowly , before it get inside the skull of the good man you be address . and yet we be far more clever there than in any parish for fifteen mile . 
 but what move I most , when I see again the noble oil and tallow of the London light , and the dripping torch at almost every corner , and the handsome signboard , be the thought that here my Lorna live , and walk , and take the air , and perhaps think now and then of the old day in the good farm - house . although I would make no approach to she , any more than she have do to I   upon which grief I have not dwelt , for fear of seem selfish , yet there must be some large chance , or the little chance might be enlarge , of fall in with the maiden somehow , and learn how her mind be set . if against I , all should be over . I be not the man to sigh and cry for love , like a Romeo : none should even guess my grief , except my sister Annie . 
 but if Lorna love I still as in my heart of heart I hop then would I for no one care , except her own delicious self . rank and title , wealth and grandeur , all should go to the wind , before they scar I from my own true love . 
 think thus , I go to bed in the centre of London town , and be bite so grievously by creature whose name be ' legion , ' mad with the delight of get a wholesome farmer among they , that verily I be ashamed to walk in the courtly part of the town next day , have lump upon my face of the size of a pickle walnut . the landlord say that this be nothing ; and that he expect , in two day at the utmost , a very fresh young Irishman , for whom they would all forsake I . nevertheless , I decline to wait , unless he could find I a hayrick to sleep in ; for the insect of grass only tickle . he assure I that no hayrick could now be find in London ; upon which I be force to leave he , and with mutual esteem we part . 
 the next night I have well luck , be introduce to a decent widow , of very high Scotch origin . that house be sweep and garnish so , that not a bit be leave to eat , for either man or insect . the change of air have make I hungry , I want something after supper ; be quite ready to pay for it , and show my purse as a symptom . but the face of Widow MacAlister , when I propose to have some more food , be a thing to be draw   if it could be draw far   by our new caricaturist . 
 therefore I leave she also ; for liefer would I be eat myself than have nothing to eat ; and so I come back to my old furrier ; the which be a thoroughly hearty man , and welcome I to my room again , with two shilling add to the rent , in the joy of his heart at see I . be under parole to Master Stickles , I only go out betwixt certain hour ; because I be account as liable to be call upon ; for what purpose I know not , but hop it might be a good one . I feel it a loss , and a hindrance to I , that I be so bound to remain at home during the session of the court of law ; for thereby the chance of ever behold Lorna be very greatly contract , if not altogether annihilate . for these be the very hour in which the people of fashion , and the high world , be will not to appear to the rest of mankind , so as to encourage they . and of course by this time , the Lady Lorna be high among people of fashion , and be not likely to be see out of fashionable hour . it be true that there be some place of expensive entertainment , at which the well sort of mankind might be see and study , in their hour of relaxation , by those of the low order , who could pay sufficiently . but alas , my money be get low ; and the privilege of see my better be more and more deny to I , as my cash draw short . for a man must have a good coat at least , and the pocket not wholly empty , before he can look at those whom God have create for his ensample . 
 hence , and from many other cause part of which be my own pride it happen that I abode in London betwixt a month and five week ' time , ere ever I see Lorna . it seem unfit that I should go , and waylay she , and spy on she , and say   or mean to say , ' Lo , here be your poor faithful farmer , a man who be unworthy of you , by mean of his common birth ; and yet who dare to crawl across your path , that you may pity he . for God 's sake show a little pity , though you may not feel it . ' such behaviour might be comely in a love - lorn boy , a page to some grand princess ; but I , John Ridd , would never stoop to the lowering of love so . 
 nevertheless I hear of Lorna , from my worthy furrier , almost every day , and with a fine exaggeration . this honest man be one of those who in virtue of their trade , and nicety of behaviour , be admit into noble life , to take measurement , and show pattern . and while so do , they contrive to acquire what be to the english mind at once the most important and most interesting of all knowledge , the science of be able to talk about the title people . so my furrier   whose name be Ramsack , have to make robe for peer , and cloak for their wife and otherwise , know the great folk , sham or real , as well as he know a fox or skunk from a wolverine skin . 
 and when , with some fencing and foil of inquiry , I hint about Lady Lorna Dugal , the old man 's face become so pleasant that I know her birth must be wondrous high . at this my own countenance fall , I suppose , for the well she be bear , the hard she would be to marry and mistake my object , he take I up : 
 ' perhaps you think , Master Ridd , that because her ladyship , Lady Lorna Dugal , be of scottish origin , therefore her birth be not as high as of our english nobility . if you think so you be wrong , sir . she come not of the sandy Scotch race , with high cheek - bone , and raw shoulder - blade , who set up pillar in their courtyard . but she come of the very good Scotch blood , descend from the norseman . her mother be of the very noble race , the Lords of Lorne ; higher even than the great Argyle , who have lately make a sad mistake , and pay for it most sadly . and her father be descend from the King Dugal , who fight against Alexander the Great . no , no , Master Ridd ; none of your promiscuous blood , such as run in the vein of half our modern peerage . ' 
 ' why should you trouble yourself about it , Master Ramsack ? ' I reply : ' let they all go their own way : and let we all look up to they , whether they come by hook or crook . ' 
 ' not at all , not at all , my lad . that be not the way to regard it . we look up at the well - bear man , and side - way at the base - bear . ' 
 ' then we be all base - bear ourselves . I will look up to no man , except for what himself have do . ' 
 ' come , Master Ridd , you might be lash from New - gate to Tyburn and back again , once a week , for a twelvemonth , if some people hear you . keep your tongue more close , young man ; or here you lodge no long ; albeit I love your company , which smell to I of the hayfield . ah , I have not see a hayfield for nine - and - twenty year , John Ridd . the cursed moth keep I at home , every day of the summer . ' 
 ' spread your fur on the haycock , ' I answer very boldly : ' the indoor moth can not abide the presence of the outdoor one . ' 
 ' be it so ? ' he answer : ' I never think of that before . and yet I have know such strange thing happen in the way of fur , that I can well believe it . if you only know , John , the way in which they lay their egg , and how they work tail - foremost ' 
 ' tell I nothing of the kind , ' I reply , with equal confidence : ' they can not work tail - foremost ; and they have no tail to work with . ' for I know a little about grub , and the ignorance concern they , which we have no right to put up with . however , not to go into that   for the argument last a fortnight ; and then be only come so far as to begin again , Master Ramsack soon convince I of the thing I know already ; the excellence of Lorna 's birth , as well as her lofty place at Court , and beauty , and wealth , and elegance . but all these only make I sigh , and wish that I be bear to they . 
 from Master Ramsack I discover that the nobleman to whose charge Lady Lorna have be commit , by the Court of Chancery , be Earl Brandir of Lochawe , her poor mother 's uncle . for the Countess of Dugal be daughter , and only child , of the last Lord Lorne , whose sister have marry Sir Ensor Doone ; while he himself have marry the sister of Earl Brandir . this nobleman have a country house near the village of Kensington ; and here his niece dwell with he , when she be not in attendance on her Majesty the Queen , who have take a liking to she . now since the King have begin to attend the celebration of mass , in the chapel at Whitehall and not at Westminster Abbey , as our gossip have aver he have give order that the door should be throw open , so that all who could make interest to get into the antechamber , might see this form of worship . Master Ramsack tell I that Lorna be there almost every Sunday ; their Majesties be most anxious to have the presence of all the nobility of the catholic persuasion , so as to make a goodly show . and the worthy furrier , have influence with the door - keeper , kindly obtain admittance for I , one Sunday , into the antechamber . 
 here I take care to be in wait , before the Royal procession enter ; but be unknown , and of no high rank , I be not allow to stand forward among the well people , but order back into a corner very dark and dismal ; the verger remarking , with a grin , that I could see over all other head , and must not set my own so high . be frightened to find myself among so many people of great rank and gorgeous apparel , I blush at the notice draw upon I by this uncourteous fellow ; and silently fall back into the corner by the hanging . 
 you may suppose that my heart beat high , when the King and Queen appear , and enter , follow by the Duke of Norfolk , bear the sword of state , and by several other nobleman , and people of repute . then the door of the chapel be throw wide open ; and though I could only see a little , be in the corner so , I think that it be beautiful . bower of rich silk be there , and plenty of metal shin , and polished wood with lovely carving ; flower too of the noble kind , and candle make by somebody who have learn how to clarify tallow . this last thing amaze I more than all , for our dip never will come clear , melt the mutton - fat how you will . and methought that this hanging of flower about be a pretty thing ; for if a man can worship God good of all beneath a tree , as the natural instinct be , surely when by fault of climate the tree would be too apt to drip , the very good make - believe be to have enough and to spare of flower ; which to the dweller in London seem to have grow on the tree deny they . 
 be that as it may , when the King and Queen cross the threshold , a mighty flourish of trumpet arise , and a wave of banner . the Knights of the Garter   whoever they be   be to attend that day in state ; and some go in , and some stay out , and it make I think of the difference betwixt the ewe and the wether . for the ewe will go wherever you lead they ; but the wether will not , have strong opinion , and mean to abide by they . and one man I notice be of the wether , to wit the Duke of Norfolk ; who stop outside with the sword of state , like a beadle with a rapping - rod . this have take more to tell than the time it happen in . for after all the man be go , some to this side , some to that , accord to their feeling , a number of lady , beautifully dress , be of the Queen 's retinue , begin to enter , and be star at three time as much as the man have be . and indeed they be worth look at   which man never be to my idea , when they trick themselves with gewgaw , but none be so well worth eye - service as my own beloved Lorna . she enter modestly and shyly , with her eye upon the ground , know the rudeness of the gallant , and the large sum she be price at . her dress be of the pure white , very sweet and simple , without a line of ornament , for she herself adorn it . the way she walk , and touch her skirt   rather than seem to hold it up   with a white hand beam one red rose , this and her stately supple neck , and the flowing of her hair would show , at a distance of a hundred yard , that she could be none but Lorna Doone . Lorna Doone of my early love ; in the day when she blush for her name before I by reason of dishonesty ; but now the Lady Lorna Dugal as far beyond reproach as above my poor affection . all my heart , and all my mind , gather themselves upon she . would she see I , or would she pass ? be there instinct in our love ? 
 by some strange chance she see I . or be it through our destiny ? while with eye keep sedulously on the marble floor , to shun the weight of admiration thrust too boldly on they , while with shy quick step she pass , some one   perhaps with purpose   trod on the skirt of her clear white dress , with the quickness teach she by many a scene of danger , she look up , and her eye meet mine . 
 as I gaze upon she , steadfastly , yearningly , yet with some reproach , and more of pride than humility , she make I one of the courtly bow which I do so much det ; yet even that be sweet and graceful , when my Lorna do it . but the colour of her pure clear cheek be nearly as deep as that of my own , when she go on for the religious work . and the shining of her eye be owe to an unpaid debt of tear . 
 upon the whole I be satisfied . Lorna have see I , and have not   accord to the phrase of the high world then   even try to ' cut ' I . whether this low phrase be bear of their own stupid meanness , or whether it come of necessity exercise on a man without money , I know not , and I care not . but one thing I know right well ; any man who ' cut ' a man   except for vice or meanness   should be quarter without quarter . 
 all these proud thought rise within I as the lovely form of Lorna go inside , and be no more see . and then I feel how coarse I be ; how apt to think strong thought , and so on ; without brain to bear I out : even as a hen 's egg , lay without enough of lime , and look only a poor jelly . 
 nevertheless , I wait on ; as my usual manner be . for to be beat , while run away , be ten time bad than to face it out , and take it , and have do with it . so at least I have always find , because of reproach of conscience : and all the thing those clever people carry on inside , at large , make I long for our Parson Bowden that he might know how to act . 
 while I store up , in my memory , enough to keep our parson go through six pipe on a Saturday night to have it as right as could be next day a lean man with a yellow beard , too thin for a good Catholic   which religion always fatten , come up to I , work sideways , in the manner of a female crab . 
 ' this be not to my liking , ' I say : ' if aught thou hast , speak plainly ; while they make that horrible noise inside . ' 
 nothing have this man to say ; but with many sigh , because I be not of the proper faith , he take my reprobate hand to save I : and with several religious tear , look up at I , and wink with one eye . although the skin of my palm be thick , I feel a little suggestion there , as of a gentle leaf in spring , fear to seem too forward . I pay the man , and he go happy ; for the standard of heretical silver be pure than that of the Catholics . 
 then I lift up my little billet ; and in that dark corner read it , with a strong rainbow of colour come from the angle light . and in mine eye there be enough to make rainbow of strong sun , as my anger cloud off . 
 not that it begin so well ; but that in my heart I know   ere three line be through I   that I be with all heart love and beyond that , who may need ? the darling of my life go on , as if I be of her own rank , or even well than she be ; and she dot she ' I be , ' and cross she ' t 's , ' as if I be at least a schoolmaster . all of it be do in pencil ; but as plain as plain could be . in my coffin it shall lie , with my ring and something else . therefore will I not expose it to every man who buy this book , and haply think that he have buy I to the bottom of my heart . enough for man of gentle birth   who never be inquisitive   that my love tell I , in her letter , just to come and see she . 
 I run away , and could not stop . to behold even she , at the moment , would have dash my fancy 's joy . yet my brain be so amiss , that I must do something . therefore to the river Thames , with all speed , I hurry ; and keep all my good clothe on   indue for sake of Lorna , into the quiet stream I leap , and swam as far as London Bridge , and eat nobler dinner afterwards . 




 CHAPTER LXVII 

 LORNA STILL be lorna 

 although a man may be as simple as the flower of the field ; know when , but scarcely why , he close to the bitter wind ; and feel why , but scarcely when , he open to the genial sun ; yet without his quest much into the capsule of himself to do which be a misery he may have a general notion how he happen to be get on . 
 I feel myself to be get on well than at any time since the last wheat - harvest , as I take the lane to Kensington upon the Monday evening . for although no time be give in my Lorna 's letter , I be not inclined to wait more than decency require . and though I go and watch the house , decency would not allow I to knock on the Sunday evening , especially when I find at the corner that his lordship be at home . 
 the lane and field between Charing Cross and the village of Kensington , be , or be at that time , more than reasonably infest with footpad and with highwayman . however , my stature and holly club keep these fellow from do more than cast sheep 's eye at I . for it be still broad daylight , and the view of the distant village , Chelsea , Battersea , Tyburn , and other , as well as a few large house , among the ham and towards the river , make it seem less lonely . therefore I sing a song in the broad Exmoor dialect , which cause no little amazement in the mind of all who meet I . 
 when I come to Earl Brandir 's house , my natural modesty forbid I to appear at the door for guest ; therefore I go to the entrance for servant and retainer . here , to my great surprise , who should come and let I in but little Gwenny Carfax , whose very existence have almost escape my recollection . her mistress , no doubt , have see I come , and send she to save trouble . but when I offer to kiss Gwenny , in my joy and comfort to see a farm - house face again , she look ashamed , and turn away , and would hardly speak to I . 
 I follow she to a little room , furnish very daintily ; and there she order I to wait , in a most ungracious manner . ' well , ' think I , ' if the mistress and the maid be alike in temper , well it have be for I to abide at Master Ramsack 's . ' but almost ere my thought be do , I hear the light quick step which I know as well as ' watch , ' my dog , know mine ; and my breast begin to tremble , like the trembling of an arch ere the keystone be put in . 
 almost ere I hop for fear and hope be so entangled that they hinder one another the velvet hanging of the doorway part , with a little doubt , and then a good face put on it . Lorna , in her perfect beauty , stand before the crimson fold , and her dress be all pure white , and her cheek be rosy pink , and her lip be scarlet . 
 like a maiden , with skill and sense check violent impulse , she stay there for one moment only , just to be admire ; and then like a woman , she come to I , see how alarmed I be . the hand she offer I I take , and raise it to my lip with fear , as a thing too good for I . ' be that all ? ' she whisper ; and then her eye gleam up at I ; and in another instant , she be weep on my breast . 
 ' Darling Lorna , Lady Lorna , ' I cry , in astonishment , yet unable but to keep she close to I , and close ; ' surely , though I love you so , this be not as it should be . ' 
 ' yes , it be , John . yes , it be . nothing else should ever be . oh , why have you behave so ? ' 
 ' I be behave . ' I reply , ' to the very good of my ability . there be no other man in the world could hold you so , without kiss you . ' 
 ' then why do not   you do it , John ? ' ask Lorna , look up at I , with a flash of her old fun . 
 now this matter , proverbially , be not for discussion , and repetition . enough that we say nothing more than , ' oh , John , how glad I be ! ' and ' Lorna , Lorna Lorna ! ' for about five minute . then my darling draw back proudly , with blushing cheek , and tear - bright eye , she begin to cross - examine I . 
 ' Master John Ridd , you shall tell the truth , the whole truth , and nothing but the truth . I have be in Chancery , sir ; and can detect a story . now why have you never , for more than a twelvemonth , take the small notice of your old friend , Mistress Lorna Doone ? ' although she speak in this lightsome manner , as if it make no difference , I see that her quick heart be move , and the flash of her eye control . 
 ' simply for this cause , I answer , ' that my old friend and true love , take not the small heed of I . nor know I where to find she . ' 
 ' what ! ' cry Lorna ; and nothing more ; be overcome with wondering ; and much inclined to fall away , but for my assistance . I tell she , over and over again , that not a single syllable of any message from she , or tiding of her welfare , have reach I , or any one of we , since the letter she leave behind ; except by soldier 's gossip . 
 ' oh , you poor dear John ! ' say Lorna , sigh at thought of my misery : ' how wonderfully good of you , think of I as you must have do , not to marry that little plain thing   or perhaps I should say that lovely creature , for I have never see she , Mistress Ruth I forget her name ; but something like a towel . ' 
 ' Ruth Huckaback be a worthy maid , ' I answer with some dignity ; ' and she alone of all our world , except indeed poor Annie , have keep her confidence in you , and tell I not to dread your rank , but trust your heart , Lady Lorna . ' 
 ' then Ruth be my good friend , ' she answer , ' and be worthy of you , dear John . and now remember one thing , dear ; if God should part we , as may be by nothing short of death , try to marry that little Ruth , when you cease to remember I . and now for the head - traitor . I have often suspect it : but she look I in the face , and wish fearful thing , which I can not repeat . ' 
 with these word , she move an implement such as I have not see before , and which make a ringing noise at a serious distance . and before I have cease wonder for if such thing go on , we might ring the church bell , while sit in our back - kitchen little Gwenny Carfax come , with a grave and sullen face . 
 ' Gwenny , ' begin my Lorna , in a tone of high rank and dignity , ' go and fetch the letter which I give you at various time for despatch to Mistress Ridd . ' 
 ' how can I fetch they , when they be go ? it be no use for he to tell no lie ' 
 ' now , Gwenny , can you look at I ? ' I ask , very sternly ; for the matter be no joke to I , after a year 's unhappiness . 
 ' I do not   want to look at ' ee . what should I look at a young man for , although he do offer to kiss I ? ' 
 I see the spite and impudence of this last remark , and so do Lorna , although she could not quite refrain from smile . 
 ' now , Gwenny , not to speak of that , ' say Lorna , very demurely , ' if you think it honest to keep the letter , be it honest to keep the money ? ' 
 at this the cornish maiden break into a rage of honesty : ' a putt the money by for ' ee . ' Ee shall have every farden of it . ' and so she fling out of the room . 
 ' and , Gwenny , ' say Lorna very softly , follow under the door - hanging ; ' if it be not honest to keep the money , it be not honest to keep the letter , which would have be worth more than any gold to those who be so kind to you . your father shall know the whole , Gwenny , unless you tell the truth . ' 
 ' now , a will tell all the truth , ' this strange maiden answer , talk to herself at least as much as to her mistress , while she go out of sight and hearing . and then I be so glad at have my own Lorna once again , clear of all contempt for we , and true to I through all of it , that I would have forgive Gwenny for treason , or even forgery . 
 ' I trust she so much , ' say Lorna , in her old ill - fortuned way ; ' and look how she have deceive I ! that be why I love you , John   set other thing aside , because you never tell I falsehood ; and you never could , you know . ' 
 ' well , I be not so sure of that . I think I could tell any lie , to have you , darling , all my own . ' 
 ' yes . and perhaps it might be right . to other people besides we two . but you could not do it to I , John . you never could do it to I , you know . ' 
 before I quite perceive my way to the bottom of the distinction although beyond doubt a valid one Gwenny come back with a leathern bag , and toss it upon the table . not a word do she vouchsafe to we ; but stand there , look injure . 
 ' go , and get your letter , John , ' say Lorna very gravely ; ' or at least your mother 's letter , make of message to you . as for Gwenny , she shall go before Lord Justice Jeffreys . ' I know that Lorna mean it not ; but think that the girl deserve a frightening ; as indeed she do . but we both mistook the courage of this child of Cornwall . she step upon a little round thing , in the nature of a stool , such as I never have see before , and thus deliver her sentiment . 
 ' and you may take I , if you please , before the great Lord Jeffreys . I have do no more than duty , though I do it crookedly , and tell a heap of lie , for your sake . and pretty gratitude I get . ' 
 ' much gratitude you have show , ' reply Lorna , ' to Master Ridd , for all his kindness and his goodness to you . who be it that go down , at the peril of his life , and bring your father to you , when you have lose he for month and month ? who be it ? answer I , Gwenny ? ' 
 ' Girt Jan Ridd , ' say the handmaid , very sulkily . 
 ' what make you treat I so , little Gwenny ? ' I ask , for Lorna would not ask lest the reply should vex I . 
 ' because ' ee be'est below she so . her shanna ' have a poor farmering chap , not even if she be a Carnishman . all her land , and all her birth and who be you , I would like to know ? ' 
 ' Gwenny , you may go , ' say Lorna , redden with quiet anger ; ' and remember that you come not near I for the next three day . it be the only way to punish she , ' she continue to I , when the maid be go , in a storm of sob and weeping . ' now , for the next three day , she will scarcely touch a morsel of food , and scarcely do a thing but cry . make up your mind to one thing , John ; if you mean to take I , for well for bad , you will have to take Gwenny with I .
