 ' I would take you with fifty gwennie , ' say I , ' although every one of they hat I , which I do not believe this little maid do , in the bottom of her heart . ' 
 ' no one can possibly hate you , John , ' she answer very softly ; and I be well pleased with this , than if she have call I the most noble and glorious man in the kingdom . 
 after this , we speak of ourselves and the way people would regard we , suppose that when Lorna come to be her own free mistress   as she must do in the course of time   she be to throw her rank aside , and refuse her title , and care not a fig for folk who care less than a fig - stalk for she , should shape her mind to its native bent , and to my perfect happiness . it be not my place to say much , lest I should appear to use an improper and selfish influence . and of course to all man of common sense , and to everybody of middle age   who must know well what be good for youth , the thought which my Lorna entertain would be enough to prove her madness . 
 not that we could not keep she well , comfortably , and with nice clothe , and plenty of flower , and fruit , and landscape , and the knowledge of our neighbour ' affair , and their kind interest in our own . still this would not be as if she be the owner of a county , and a haughty title ; and able to lead the first man of the age , by her mind , and face , and money . 
 therefore be I quite resolve not to have a word to say , while this young queen of wealth and beauty , and of nobleman 's desire , make her mind up how to act for her purest happiness . but to do her justice , this be not the first thing she be think of : the test of her judgment be only this , ' how will my love be happiest ? ' 
 ' now , John , ' she cry ; for she be so quick that she always have my thought beforehand ; ' why will you be backward , as if you care not for I ? do you dream that I be doubt ? my mind have be make up , good John , that you must be my husband , for well , I will not say how long , lest you should laugh at my folly . but I believe it be ever since you come , with your stocking off , and the loach . right early for I to make up my mind ; but you know that you make up your , John ; and , of course , I know it ; and that have a great effect on I . now , after all this age of loving , shall a trifle sever we ? ' 
 I tell she that it be no trifle , but a most important thing , to abandon wealth , and honour , and the brilliance of high life , and be despise by every one for such abundant folly . moreover , that I should appear a knave for take advantage of her youth , and boundless generosity , and ruin   as man would say   a noble maid by my selfishness . and I tell she outright , have work myself up by my own conversation , that she be bind to consult her guardian , and that without his knowledge , I would come no more to see she . her flash of pride at these last word make she look like an empress ; and I be about to explain myself well , but she put forth her hand and stop I . 
 ' I think that condition should rather have proceed from I . you be mistaken , Master Ridd , in suppose that I would think of receive you in secret . it be a different thing in Glen Doone , where all except yourself be thief , and when I be but a simple child , and oppress with constant fear . you be quite right in threaten to visit I thus no more ; but I think you might have wait for an invitation , sir . ' 
 ' and you be quite right , Lady Lorna , in point out my presumption . it be a fault that must ever be find in any speech of mine to you . ' 
 this I say so humbly , and not with any bitterness for I know that I have go too far and make she so polite a bow , that she forgive I in a moment , and we beg each other 's pardon . 
 ' now , will you allow I just to explain my own view of this matter , John ? ' say she , once more my darling . ' it may be a very foolish view , but I shall never change it . please not to interrupt I , dear , until you have hear I to the end . in the first place , it be quite certain that neither you nor I can be happy without the other . then what stand between we ? worldly position , and nothing else . I have no more education than you have , John Ridd ; nay , and not so much . my birth and ancestry be not one whit more pure than your , although they may be well know . your descent from ancient freeholder , for five - and - twenty generation of good , honest man , although you bear no coat of arm , be well than the lineage of nine proud english nobleman out of every ten I meet with . in manner , though your mighty strength , and hatred of any meanness , sometimes break out in violence of which I must try to cure you , dear in manner , if kindness , and gentleness , and modesty be the true thing want , you be immeasurably above any of our court - gallant ; who indeed have very little . as for difference of religion , we allow for one another , neither have be bring up in a bitterly pious manner . ' 
 here , though the tear be in my eye , at the love thing love say of I , I could not help a little laugh at the notion of any bitter piety be find among the Doones , or even in mother , for that matter . Lorna smile , in her sly manner , and go on again : 
 ' now , you see , I have prove my point ; there be nothing between we but worldly position if you can defend I against the Doones , for which , I trow , I may trust you . and worldly position mean wealth , and title , and the right to be in great house , and the pleasure of be envy . I have not be here for a year , John , without learn something . oh , I hate it ; how I hate it ! of all the people I know , there be but two , besides my uncle , who do not either covet , or det I . and who be those two , think you ? ' 
 ' Gwenny , for one , ' I answer . 
 ' yes , Gwenny , for one . and the queen , for the other . the one be too far below I   I mean , in her own opinion , and the other too high above . as for the woman who dislike I , without have even hear my voice , I simply have nothing to do with they . as for the man who covet I , for my land and money , I merely compare they with you , John Ridd ; and all thought of they be over . oh , John , you must never forsake I , however cross I be to you . I think you would have go , just now ; and though I would not move to stop you , my heart would have break . ' 
 ' you do not   catch I go in a hurry , ' I answer very sensibly , ' when the lovely maiden in all the world , and the good , and the dearest , love I . all my fear of you be go , darle Lorna , all my fear ' 
 ' be it possible you could fear I , John , after all we have be through together ? now you promise not to interrupt I ; be this fair behaviour ? well , let I see where I leave off oh , that my heart would have break . upon that point , I will say no more , lest you should grow conceited , John ; if anything could make you so . but I do assure you that half London however , upon that point also I will check my power of speech , lest you think I conceite . and now to put aside all nonsense ; though I have talk none for a year , John , have be so unhappy ; and now it be such a relief to I ' 
 ' then talk it for an hour , ' say I ; ' and let I sit and watch you . to I it be the very sweetest of all sweetest wisdom . ' 
 ' Nay , there be no time , ' she answer , glance at a jewelled timepiece , scarcely large than an oyster , which she draw from her waist - band ; and then she push it away , in confusion , lest its wealth should startle I . ' my uncle will come home in less than half an hour , dear : and you be not the one to take a side - passage , and avoid he . I shall tell he that you have be here ; and that I mean you to come again . ' 
 as Lorna say this , with a manner as confident as need be , I see that she have learn in town the power of her beauty , and know that she could do with most man aught she set her mind upon . and as she stand there , flush with pride and faith in her own loveliness , and radiant with the love itself , I feel that she must do exactly as she please with every one . for now , in turn , and elegance , and richness , and variety , there be nothing to compare with her face , unless it be her figure . therefore I give in , and say , 
 ' darling , do just what you please . only make no rogue of I . ' 
 for that she give I the simple , kind , and sweetest of all kiss ; and I go down the great stair grandly , think of nothing else but that . 




 CHAPTER LXVIII 

 JOHN be JOHN no long 

 it would be hard for I to tell the state of mind in which I live for a long time after this . I put away from I all torment , and the thought of future care , and the sight of difficulty ; and to myself appear , which mean that I become the lucky of lucky fellow , since the world itself begin . I think not of the harvest even , nor of the man who would get their wage without have earn they , nor of my mother 's anxiety and worry about John Fry 's great fatness   which be grow upon he , and how she would cry fifty time in a day , ' ah , if our John would only come home , how different everything would look ! ' 
 although there be no soldier now quarter at Plover 's barrow , all be busy in harass the country , and hang the people where the rebellion have thriven most , my mother , have receive from I a message contain my place of abode , contrive to send I , by the pack - horse , as fine a maund as need be of provision , and money , and other comfort . therein I find address to Colonel Jeremiah Stickles , in Lizzie 's good handwriting , half a side of the dried deer 's flesh , in which he rejoice so greatly . also , for Lorna , a fine green goose , with a little salt towards the tail , and new - lay egg inside it , as well as a bottle of brandied cherry , and seven , or it may have be eight pound of fresh homemade butter . moreover , to myself there be a letter full of good advice , excellently well express , and would have be of the great value , if I have care to read it . but I read all about the farm affair , and the man who have offer himself to our Betty for the five pound in her stocking ; as well as the antic of Sally Snowe , and how she have almost throw herself at Parson Bowden 's head   old enough to be her grandfather , because on the Sunday after the hanging of a Countisbury man , he have preach a beautiful sermon about christian love ; which Lizzie , with her sharp eye , find to be the work of good Bishop Ken . also I read that the Doones be quiet ; the parish round about have unite to feed they well through the harvest time , so that after the day 's hard work , the farmer might go to bed at night . and this plan have be find to answer well , and to save much trouble on both side , so that everybody wonder it have not be do before . but Lizzie think that the Doones could hardly be expect much long to put up with it , and probably would not have do so now , but for a little adversity ; to wit , that the famous Colonel Kirke have , in the most outrageous manner , hang no less than six of they , who be capture among the rebel ; for he say that man of their rank and breeding , and above all of their religion , should have know well than to join plough - boy , and carter , and pickaxeman , against our Lord the king , and his Holiness the Pope . this hang of so many Doones cause some indignation among people who be use to they ; and it seem for a while to check the rest from any spirit of enterprise . 
 moreover , I find from this same letter   which be pin upon the knuckle of a leg of mutton , for fear of be lose in straw   that good Tom Faggus be at home again , and nearly cure of his dreadful wound ; but intend to go to war no more , only to mind his family . and it grieve he more than anything he ever could have imagine , that his duty to his family , and the strong power of his conscience , so totally forbid he to come up and see after I . for now his design be to lead a new life , and be in charity with all man . many well man than he have be hang , he see no cause to doubt ; but by the grace of God he hop himself to cheat the gallow . 
 there be no further news of moment in this very clever letter , except that the price of horse ' shoe be go up again , though already twopence - farthe each ; and that Betty have break her lover 's head with the stocking full of money ; and then in the corner it be write that the distinguished man of war , and worshipful scholar , Master Bloxham , be now promote to take the toll , and catch all the rebel around our part . 
 Lorna be greatly pleased with the goose , and the butter , and the brandied cherry ; and the Earl Brandir himself declare that he never taste well than those last , and would beg the young man from the country to procure he instruction for make they . this nobleman , be as deaf as a post , and of a very solid mind , could never be bring to understand the nature of my thought towards Lorna . he look upon I as an excellent youth , who have rescue the maiden from the Doones , whom he cordially detest ; and learn that I have throw two of they out of window   as the story be tell he , he pat I on the back , and declare that his door would ever be open to I , and that I could not come too often . 
 I think this very kind of his lordship , especially as it enable I to see my darling Lorna , not indeed as often as I wish , but at any rate very frequently , and as many time as modesty   ever my lead principle   would in common conscience approve of . and I make up my mind that if ever I could help Earl Brandir , it would be as we say , when with brandy and water the ' proud moment of my life , ' when I could fulfil the pledge . 
 and I soon be able to help Lord Brandir , as I think , in two different way ; first of all as regard his mind , and then as concern his body : and the latter perhaps be the great service , at his time of life . but not to be too nice about that ; let I tell how these thing be . 
 Lorna say to I one day , be in a state of excitement whereto she be over prone , when reft of my slowness to steady she , 
 ' I will tell he , John ; I must tell he , John . it be mean of I to conceal it . ' 
 I think that she mean all about our love , which we have endeavour thrice to drill into his fine old ear ; but could not make he comprehend , without risk of bring the house down : and so I say , ' by all mean ; darling ; have another try at it . ' 
 Lorna , however , look at I for her eye tell more than tongue as much as to say , ' well , you be a stupid . we agree to let that subject rest . ' and then she see that I be vex at my own want of quickness ; and so she speak very kindly , 
 ' I mean about his poor son , dearest ; the son of his old age almost ; whose loss throw he into that dreadful cold for he go , without hat , to look for he which end in his lose the use of his dear old ear . I believe if we could only get he to Plover 's barrow for a month , he would be able to hear again . and look at his age ! he be not much over seventy , John , you know ; and I hope that you will be able to hear I , long after you be seventy , John . ' 
 ' well , ' say I , ' God settle that . or at any rate , he leave we time to think about those question , when we be over fifty . now let I know what you want , Lorna . the idea of my be seventy ! but you would still be beautiful . ' 
 ' to the one who love I , ' she answer , try to make wrinkle in her pure bright forehead : ' but if you will have common sense , as you always will , John , whether I wish it or otherwise I want to know whether I be bind , in honour , and in conscience , to tell my dear and good old uncle what I know about his son ? ' 
 ' first let I understand quite clearly , ' say I , never be in a hurry , except when passion move I , ' what his lordship think at present ; and how far his mind be urge with sorrow and anxiety . ' this be not the first time we have speak of the matter . 
 ' why , you know , John , well enough , ' she answer , wonder at my coolness , ' that my poor uncle still believe that his one beloved son will come to light and live again . he have make all arrangement accordingly : all his property be settle on that supposition . he know that young Alan always be what he call a " feckless ne'er - do - weel ; " but he love he all the more for that . he can not believe that he will die , without his son come back to he ; and he always have a bedroom ready , and a bottle of Alan 's favourite wine cool from out the cellar ; he have make I work he a pair of slipper from the size of a mouldy boot ; and if he hear of a new tobacco much as he hat the smell of it he will go to the other end of London to get some for Alan . now you know how deaf he be ; but if any one say , " Alan , " even in the place outside the door , he will make his courteous bow to the very high visitor , and be out there in a moment , and search the entire passage , and yet let no one know it . ' 
 ' it be a piteous thing , ' I say ; for Lorna 's eye be full of tear . 
 ' and he mean I to marry he . it be the pet scheme of his life . I be to grow more beautiful , and more highly teach , and graceful ; until it please Alan to come back , and demand I . can you understand this matter , John ? or do you think my uncle mad ? ' 
 ' Lorna , I should be mad myself , to call any other man mad , for hop . ' 
 ' then will you tell I what to do ? it make I very sorrowful . for I know that Alan Brandir lie below the sod in Doone - valley . ' 
 ' and if you tell his father , ' I answer softly , but clearly , ' in a few week he will lie below the sod in London ; at least if there be any . ' 
 ' perhaps you be right , John , ' she reply : ' to lose hope must be a dreadful thing , when one be turn of seventy . therefore I will never tell he . ' 
 the other way in which I manage to help the good Earl Brandir be of less true moment to he ; but as he could not know of the first , this be the one which move he . and it happen pretty much as follow though I hardly like to tell , because it advance I to such a height as I myself be giddy at ; and which all my friend resent greatly   save those of my own family , and even now be sometimes bitter , in spite of all my humility . now this be a matter of history , because the King be concern in it ; and be so strongly misunderstood ,   especially in my own neighbourhood , I will overcome so far as I can   my diffidence in tell it . 
 the good Earl Brandir be a man of the noble charity . true charity begin at home , and so do his ; and be afraid of lose the way , if it go abroad . so this good nobleman keep his money in a handsome pewter box , with his coat of arm upon it , and a double lid and lock . moreover , there be a heavy chain , fix to a staple in the wall , so that none might carry off the pewter with the gold inside of it . Lorna tell I the box be full , for she have see he go to it , and she often think that it would be nice for we to begin the world with . I tell she that she must not allow her mind to dwell upon thing of this sort ; be wholly against the last commandment set up in our church at Oare . 
 now one evening towards September , when the day be draw in , look back at the house to see whether Lorna be look after I , I espy   by a little glimpse , as it be   a pair of villainous fellow   about whom there could be no mistake   watch from the thicket - corner , some hundred yard or so behind the good Earl 's dwelling . ' there be mischief afoot , ' think I to myself , be thoroughly conversant with theft , from my knowledge of the Doones ; ' how will be the moon to - night , and when may we expect the watch ? ' 
 I find that neither moon nor watch could be look for until the morning ; the moon , of course , before the watch , and more likely to be punctual . therefore I resolve to wait , and see what those two villain do , and save   if it be possible   the Earl of Brandir 's pewter box . but inasmuch as those bad man be almost sure to have see I leave the house and look back , and strike out on the London road , I march along at a merry pace , until they could not discern I ; and then I fetch a compass round , and refresh myself at a certain inn , entitle the cross - bone and Buttons . 
 here I remain until it be very nearly as dark as pitch ; and the house be full of footpad and cutthroat , I think it right to leave they . one or two come after I , in the hope of design a stratagem ; but I drop they in the darkness ; and know all the neighbourhood well , I take up my position , two hour before midnight , among the shrub at the eastern end of Lord Brandir 's mansion . hence , although I might not see , I could scarcely fail to hear , if any unlawful entrance either at back or front be make . 
 from my own observation , I think it likely that the attack would be in the rear ; and so indeed it come to pass . for when all the light be quench , and all the house be quiet , I hear a low and wily whistle from a clump of tree close by ; and then three figure pass between I and a whitewashed wall , and come to a window which open into a part of the servant ' basement . this window be carefully raise by some one inside the house ; and after a little whispering , and something which sound like a kiss , all the three man enter . 
 ' oh , you villain ! ' I say to myself , ' this be bad than any Doone job ; because there be treachery in it . ' but without wait to consider the subject from a moral point of view , I creep along the wall , and enter very quietly after they ; be rather uneasy about my life , because I bear no fire - arm , and have nothing more than my holly staff , for even a violent combat . 
 to I this be matter of deep regret , as I follow these vile man inward . nevertheless I be resolve that my Lorna should not be rob again . through we   or at least through our Annie   she have lose that brilliant necklace ; which then be her only birthright : therefore it behove I doubly , to preserve the pewter box ; which must belong to she in the end , unless the thief get hold of it . 
 I go along very delicately   as a man who have learn to wrestle can do , although he may weigh twenty stone , follow carefully the light , bring by the traitorous maid , and shake in her loose dishonest hand . I see she lead the man into a little place call a pantry ; and there she give they cordial , and I could hear they boast . 
 not to be too long over it which they be much inclined to be I follow they from this drinking - bout , by the aid of the light they bear , as far as Earl Brandir 's bedroom , which I know , because Lorna have show it to I that I might admire the tapestry . but I have say that no horse could ever be shod as the horse be shod therein , unless he have the foot of a frog , as well as a frog to his foot . and Lorna have be vex at this   as taste and high art always be , at any small accurate knowledge , and so she have bring I out again , before I have time to admire thing . 
 now , keep well away in the dark , yet near than be necessary to my own dear Lorna 's room , I see these fellow try the door of the good Earl Brandir , know from the maid , of course , that his lordship could hear nothing , except the name of Alan . they try the lock , and push at it , and even set their knee upright ; but a scottish nobleman may be trust to secure his door at night . so they be force to break it open ; and at this the guilty maid , or woman , run away . these three rogue for rogue they be , and no charity may deny it burst into Earl Brandir 's room , with a light , and a crowbar , and fire - arm . I think to myself that this be hard upon an honest nobleman ; and if further mischief could be save , I would try to save it . 
 when I come to the door of the room , be myself in shadow , I behold two bad man try vainly to break open the pewter box , and the third with a pistol - muzzle lay to the night - cap of his lordship . with foul face and yet fouler word , this man be demand the key of the box , which the other man could by no mean open , neither drag it from the chain . 
 ' I tell you , ' say this age Earl , begin to understand at last what these rogue be up for ; ' I will give no key to you . it all belong to my boy , Alan . no one else shall have a farthing . ' 
 ' then you may count your moment , lord . the key be in your old cramped hand . one , two , and at three , I shoot you . ' 
 I see that the old man be abroad ; not with fear , but with great wonder , and the regret of deafness . and I see that rather would he be shoot than let these man go rob his son , bury now , or lay to bleach in the tangle of the wood , three , or it might be four year agone , but still alive to his father . Hereupon my heart be move ; and I resolve to interfere . the thief with the pistol begin to count , as I cross the floor very quietly , while the old Earl fearfully gaze at the muzzle , but clench still tight his wrinkle hand . the villain , with hair all over his eye , and the great horse - pistol level , cry ' three , ' and pull the trigger ; but luckily , at that very moment , I strike up the barrel with my staff , so that the shot pierce the tester , and then with a spin and a thwack I bring the good holly down upon the rascal 's head , in a manner which stretch he upon the floor . 
 meanwhile the other two robber have take the alarm , and rush at I , one with a pistol and one with a hanger ; which force I to be very lively . fear the pistol most , I fling the heavy velvet curtain of the bed across , that he might not see where to aim at I , and then stoop very quickly I catch up the senseless robber , and set he up for a shield and target ; whereupon he be shoot immediately , without have the pain of know it ; and a happy thing it be for he . now the other two be at my mercy , be man below the average strength ; and no hanger , except in most skilful hand , as well as firm and strong one , have any chance to a powerful man arm with a stout cudgel , and thoroughly practise in single - stick . 
 so I take these two rogue , and bind they together ; and leave they under charge of the butler   a worthy and shrewd Scotchman , I myself go in search of the constable , whom , after some few hour , I find ; neither be they so drunk but what they could take roped man to prison . in the morning , these two man be bring before the Justices of the Peace : and now my wonderful luck appear ; for the merit of have defeat , and catch they , would never have raise I one step in the State , or in public consideration , if they have only be common robber , or even notorious murderer . but when these fellow be recognise , by some one in the court , as protestant witness out of employment , companion and understrapper to Oates , and Bedloe , and carstair , and hand in glove with Dangerfield , Turberville ; and Dugdale in a word , the very man against whom his Majesty the King bear the bitter rancour , but whom he have hitherto fail to catch when this be lay before the public   with emphasis and admiration , at least a dozen man come up , whom I have never see before , and pray I to accept their congratulation , and to be sure to remember they ; for all be of neglect merit , and require no more than a piece of luck . 
 I answer they very modestly , and each accord to his worth , as state by himself , who of course could judge the good . the magistrate make I many compliment , ten time more than I deserve , and take good care to have they copy , that his Majesty might see they . and ere the case be thoroughly hear , and those poor fellow be commit , more than a score of generous man have offer to lend I a hundred pound , wherewith to buy a new Court suit , when call before his Majesty . 
 now this may seem very strange to we who live in a well and purer age or say at least that we do so and yet who be we to condemn our father for teach we well manner , and at their own expense ? with these point any virtuous man be bind to deal quite tenderly , make allowance for corruption , and not be too sure of himself . and to tell the truth , although I have see so little of the world as yet , that which astonish I in the matter , be not so much that they pay I court , as that they find out so soon the expediency of do it . 
 in the course of that same afternoon I be send for by his Majesty . he have summon first the good Earl Brandir , and receive the tale from he , not without exaggeration , although my lord be a Scotchman . but the chief thing his Majesty care to know be that , beyond all possible doubt , these be the very precious fellow from perjury turn to robbery . 
 be fully assure at last of this , his Majesty have rub his hand , and order the boot of a strict pattern   which he himself have invent   to be bring at once , that he might have they in the good possible order . and he oil they himself , and express his fear that there be no man in London quite competent to work they . nevertheless he would try one or two , rather than wait for his pleasure , till the torturer come from Edinburgh . 
 the next thing he do be to send for I ; and in great alarm and flurry I put on my good clothe , and hire a fashionable hairdresser , and drink half a gallon of ale , because both my hand be shake . then forth I set , with my holly staff , wish myself well out of it . I be show at once , and before I desire it , into his Majesty 's presence , and there I stand most humbly , and make the good bow I could think of . 
 as I could not advance any far for I see that the Queen be present , which frighten I tenfold his Majesty , in the most gracious manner , come down the room to encourage I . and as I remain with my head bend down , he tell I to stand up , and look at he . 
 ' I have see thee before , young man , he say ; ' thy form be not one to be forget . where be it ? thou art most likely to know . ' 
 ' may it please your Most gracious Majesty the king , ' I answer , find my voice in a manner which surprise myself ; ' it be in the Royal Chapel . ' 
 now I mean no harm whatever by this . I ought to have say the ' Ante - chapel , ' but I could not remember the word , and fear to keep the King look at I . 
 ' I be well - pleased , ' say his Majesty , with a smile which almost make his dark and stubborn face look pleasant , ' to find that our great subject , great I mean in the bodily form , be also a good Catholic . Thou needest not say otherwise . the time shall be , and that right soon , when man shall be proud of the one true faith . ' here he stop , have go rather far ! but the gleam of his heavy eye be such that I durst not contradict . 
 ' this be that great Johann Reed , ' say her Majesty , come forward , because the King be in meditation ; ' for whom I have so much hear , from the dear , dear Lorna . ah , she be not of this black countree , she be of the breet Italie . ' 
 I have try to write it , as she say it : but it want a well scholar to express her mode of speech . 
 ' now , John Ridd , ' say the King , recover from his thought about the true Church , and think that his wife be not to take the lead upon I ; ' thou hast do great service to the realm , and to religion . it be good to save Earl Brandir , a loyal and catholic nobleman ; but it be great service to catch two of the vile bloodhound ever lay on by heretic . and to make they shoot one another : it be rare ; it be rare , my lad . now ask we anything in reason ; thou canst carry any honour , on thy club , like Hercules . what be thy chief ambition , lad ? ' 
 ' well , ' say I , after think a little , and mean to make the most of it , for so the Queen 's eye convey to I ; ' my mother always use to think that have be school at Tiverton , with thirty mark a year to pay , I be worthy of a coat of arm . and that be what she long for . ' 
 ' a good lad ! a very good lad , ' say the King , and he look at the Queen , as if almost in joke ; ' but what be thy condition in life ? ' 
 ' I be a freeholder , ' I answer , in my confusion , ' ever since the time of King Alfred . a Ridd be with he in the isle of Athelney , and we hold our farm by gift from he ; or at least people say so . we have have three very good harvest run , and might support a coat of arm ; but for myself I want it not . ' 
 ' thou shalt have a coat , my lad , ' say the King , smile at his own humour ; ' but it must be a large one to fit thee . and more than that shalt thou have , John Ridd , be of such loyal breed , and have do such service . ' 
 and while I wonder what he mean , he call to some of the people in wait at the farth end of the room , and they bring he a little sword , such as Annie would skewer a turkey with . then he signify to I to kneel , which I do   after dust the board , for the sake of my good breech , and then he give I a little tap very nicely upon my shoulder , before I know what he be up to ; and say , ' Arise , Sir John Ridd ! ' 
 this astonish and amaze I to such extent of loss of mind , that when I get up I look about , and think what the snowe would think of it . and I say to the King , without form of speech , 
 ' Sir , I be very much oblige . but what be I to do with it ? ' 




 CHAPTER LXIX 

 not to be PUT up with 

 the coat of arm , devise for I by the Royal herald , be of great size , and rich colour , and full of bright imagining . they do I the honour to consult I first , and to take no notice of my advice . for I beg that there might be a good - sized cow on it , so as to stamp our pat of butter before they go to market : also a horse on the other side , and a flock snow up at the bottom . but the gentleman would not hear of this ; and to find something more appropriate , they inquire strictly into the annal of our family . I tell they , of course , all about King Alfred ; upon which they settle that one quarter should be , three cake on a bar , with a lion regardant , do upon a field of gold . also I tell they that very likely there have be a Ridd in the battle fight , not very far from Plover 's barrow , by the Earl of Devon against the Danes , when Hubba their chief be kill , and the sacred standard take . as some of the Danes be say to be bury , even upon land of ours , and we call their graf   if such they be   even to this day ' barrow , ' the herald quite agree with I that a Ridd might have be there , or thereabout ; and if he be there , he be almost certain to have do his good , be in sight of hearth and home ; and it be plain that he must have have good leg to be at the same time both there and in Athelney ; and good leg be an argument for good arm ; and suppose a man of this sort to have do his utmost   as the manner of the Ridds be , it be next to certain that he himself must have capture the standard . moreover , the name of our farm be pure proof ; a plover be a wild bird , just the same as a raven be . upon this chain of reasoning , and without any weak misgiving , they charge my grow escutcheon with a black raven on a ground of red . and the next thing which I mention possess absolute certainty , to wit , that a pig with two head have be bear upon our farm , not more than two hundred year agone   although he die within a week , my third quarter be make at once , by a two - head boar with noble tusk , sable upon silver . all this be very fierce and fine ; and so I press for a peaceful corner in the low dexter , and obtain a wheat - sheaf set upright , gold upon a field of green . 
 here I be inclined to pause , and admire the effect ; for even De Whichehalse could not show a bearing so magnificent . but the herald say that it look a mere sign - board , without a good motto under it ; and the motto must have my name in it . they offer I first , ' ridd non ridendu ' ; but I say , ' for God 's sake , gentleman , let I forget my Latin . ' then they propose , ' ridd readeth riddle ' : but I beg they not to set down such a lie ; for no Ridd ever have make , or make out , such a thing as a riddle , since Exmoor itself begin . thirdly , they give I , ' ridd never be ride , ' and fear to make any further objection , I let they inscribe it in bronze upon blue . the herald think that the King would pay for this noble achievement ; but his Majesty , although graciously pleased with their ingenuity , decline in the most decide manner to pay a farthing towards it ; and as I have now no money leave , the herald become as blue as azure , and as red as gule ; until her Majesty the Queen come forward very kindly , and say that if his Majesty give I a coat of arm , I be not to pay for it ; therefore she herself do so quite handsomely , and feel goodwill towards I in consequence . 
 now be in a hurry so far at least as it be in my nature to hurry to get to the end of this narrative , be it likely that I would have dwell so long upon my coat of arm , but for some good reason ? and this good reason be that Lorna take the great pride in it , and think   or at any rate say   that it quite throw into the shade , and eclipse , all her own ancient glory . and half in fun , and half in earnest , she call I ' Sir John ' so continually , that at last I be almost angry with she ; until her eye be bedew with tear ; and then I be angry with myself . 
 begin to be short of money , and grow anxious about the farm , long also to show myself and my noble escutcheon to mother , I take advantage of Lady Lorna 's interest with the Queen , to obtain my acquittance and full discharge from even nominal custody . it have be intend to keep I in waiting , until the return of Lord Jeffreys , from that awful circuit of shamble , through which his name be still use by mother to frighten their child into bed . and right glad be I for even London shrink with horror at the news to escape a man so bloodthirsty , savage , and even to his friend   among whom I be reckon   malignant . 
 Earl Brandir be greatly pleased with I , not only for have save his life , but for save that which he value more , the wealth lay by for Lord Alan . and he introduce I to many great people , who quite kindly encourage I , and promise to help I in every way when they hear how the King have speak . as for the furrier , he could never have enough of my society ; and this worthy man , pray my commendation , demand of I one thing only to speak of he as I find he . as I have find he many a Sunday , furbish up old fur for new , with a glaze to conceal the moth ' ravage , I beg he to reconsider the point , and not to demand such accuracy . he say , ' well , well ; all trade have trick , especially the trick of business ; and I must take he if I be his true friend accord to his own description . ' this I be glad enough to do ; because it save so much trouble , and I have no money to spend with he . but still he request the use of my name ; and I beg he to do the good with it , as I never have keep a banker . and the ' John Ridd cuff , ' and the ' Sir John mantles , ' and the ' Holly - staff cape , ' he put into his window , as the winter be come on , ay and sell   for everybody be burn with gossip about I , must have make this good man 's fortune ; since the excess of price over value be the true test of success in life . 
 to come away from all this stuff , which grieve a man in London when the brisk air of the autumn clear its way to Ludgate Hill , and clever ' prentice run out , and sniff at it , and feed upon it   have little else to eat ; and when the horse from the country be a goodly sight to see , with the rasp of winter bristle rise through and among the soft summer - coat ; and when the new straw begin to come in , golden with the harvest gloss , and smell most divinely at those strange livery - stable , where the nag be put quite tail to tail ; and when all the London folk themselves be ask about white frost   from recollection of childhood ; then , I say , such a yearning seize I for moory crag , and for dewy blade , and even the grunting of our sheep   when the sun go down , that nothing but the new wisps of Samson could have hold I in London town . 
 Lorna be move with equal longing towards the country and country way ; and she speak quite as much of the glistening dew as she do of the smell of our oven . and here let I mention although the two be quite distinct and different that both the dew and the bread of Exmoor may be seek , whether high or low , but never find elsewhere . the dew be so crisp , and pure , and pearly , and in such abundance ; and the bread be so sweet , so kind , and homely , you can eat a loaf , and then another . 
 now while I be walk daily in and out great crowd of man   few of whom have any freedom from the care of money , and many of whom be even morbid with a bad pest call ' politic ' , I could not be quit of think how we jostle one another . God have make the earth quite large , with a spread of land large enough for all to live on , without fight . also a mighty spread of water , lay hand on sand and cliff with a solemn voice in storm - time ; and in the gentle weather move man to thought of equity . this , as well , be full of food ; be two - third of the world , and reserve for devouring knowledge ; by the time the son of man have feed away the dry land . yet before the land itself have acknowledge touch of man , upon one in a hundred acre ; and before one mile in ten thousand of the exhaustless ocean have ever feel the plunge of hook , or comb of the haul - net ; lo , we crawl , in flock together upon the hot ground that sting we , even as the black grub crowd upon the harry nettle ! surely we be too much give to follow the track of each other . 
 however , for a moralist , I never set up , and never shall , while common sense abide with I . such a man must be very wretched in this pure dearth of morality ; like a fisherman where no fish be ; and most of we have enough to do to attend to our own moral . enough that I resolve to go ; and as Lorna could not come with I , it be even bad than stop . nearly everybody vow that I be a great fool indeed , to neglect so rudely which be the proper word , they say the pushing of my fortune . but I answer that to push be rude , and I leave it to people who have no room ; and think that my fortune must be heavy , if it would not move without push . 
 Lorna cry when I come away   which give I great satisfaction , and she send a whole trunkful of thing for mother and Annie , and even Lizzie . and she seem to think , though she say it not , that I make my own occasion for go , and might have stay on till the winter . whereas I know well that my mother would think   and every one on the farm the same   that here I have be in London , lag , and take my pleasure , and look at shop , upon pretence of King 's business , and leave the harvest to reap itself , not to mention the spending of money ; while all the time there be nothing whatever , except my own love of adventure and sport , to keep I from come home again . but I know that my coat of arm , and title , would turn every bit of this grumble into fine admiration . 
 and so it fall out , to a great extent than even I desire ; for all the parish round about united in a sumptuous dinner , at the Mother Melldrum inn for now that good lady be dead , and her name and face set on a sign - post to which I be invite , so that it be as good as a summon . and if my health be no well next day , it be not from want of good wish , any more than from stint of the liquor . 
 it be needless to say that the real gentry for a long time treat my new honour with contempt and ridicule ; but gradually as they find that I be not such a fool as to claim any equality with they , but go about my farm - work , and throw another man at wrestling , and touch my hat to the magistrate , just the same as ever ; some gentleman of the high blood of which we think a great deal more than of gold , around our neighbourhood actually express a desire to make my acquaintance . and when , in a manner quite straightforward , and wholly free from bitterness , I thank they for this   which appear to I the high honour yet offer I , but decline to go into their company because it would make I uncomfortable , and themselves as well , in a different way , they do what nearly all englishman do , when a thing be right and sensible . they shake hand with I ; and say that they could not deny but that there be reason in my view of the matter . and although they themselves must be the loser which be a handsome thing to say they would wait until I be a little old and more aware of my own value . 
 now this remind I how it be that an english gentleman be so far in front of foreign nobleman and prince . I have see at time , a little , both of one and of the other , and make more than due allowance for the difficulty of language , and the difference of training , upon the whole , the balance be in favour of our people . and this , because we have two weight , solid and   even in scale of manner   outweigh all light complaisance ; to wit , the inborn love of justice , and the power of abide . 
 yet some people may be surprised that man with any love of justice , whether inborn or otherwise , could continue to abide the arrogance , and rapacity , and tyranny of the Doones . 
 for now as the winter pass , the Doones be not keep themselves at home , as in honour they be bind to do . twenty sheep a week , and one fat ox , and two stout red deer   for wholesome change of diet , as well as threescore bushel of flour , and two hogshead and a half of cider , and a hundredweight of candle , not to mention other thing of almost every variety which they get by insist upon it surely these might have suffice to keep the people in their place , with no outburst of wantonness . nevertheless , it be not so ; they have make complaint about something too much ewe - mutton , I think it be and in spite of all the pledge give , they have ride forth , and carry away two maiden of our neighbourhood . 
 now these two maiden be know , because they have serve the beer at an ale - house ; and many man who have look at they , over a pint or quart vessel   especially as they be comely girl , think that it be very hard for they to go in that way , and perhaps themselves unwilling . and their mother   although she have take some money , which the Doones be always full of   declare that it be a robbery ; and though it increase for a while the custom , that must soon fall off again . and who would have her two girl now , clever as they be and good ? 
 before we have finish meditate upon this loose outrage for so I at least would call it , though people accustomed to the law may take a different view of it we have news of a thing far bad , which turn the heart of our woman sick . this I will tell in most careful language , so as to give offence to none , if skill of word may help it .  
   the follow story be strictly true ; and true it be that 
  the country - people rise , to a man , at this dastard cruelty , 
  and do what the Government fail to do . Ed . 
 Mistress Margery Badcock , a healthy and upright young woman , with a good rich colour , and one of the fine hen - roost anywhere round our neighbourhood , be nurse her child about six of the clock , and look out for her husband . now this child be too old to be nurse , as everybody tell she ; for he could run , say two yard alone , and perhaps four or five , by hold to handle . and he have a way of look round , and spread his leg , and laugh , with his brave little body well fetch up , after a desperate journey to the end of the table , which his mother say nothing could equal . nevertheless , he would come to be nurse , as regular as a clock , almost ; and , inasmuch as he be the first , both father and mother make much of he ; for God only know whether they could ever compass such another one . 
 Christopher Badcock be a tenant farmer , in the parish of Martinhoe , rent some fifty acre of land , with a right of common attach to they ; and at this particular time , be now the month of February , and fine open weather , he be hard at work plough and prepare for spring corn . therefore his wife be not surprised although the dusk be fall , that farmer Christopher should be at work in ' blind man 's holiday , ' as we call it . 
 but she be surprised , nay astonish , when by the light of the kitchen fire   brighten up for her husband , she see six or seven great armed man burst into the room upon she ; and she scream so that the maid in the back kitchen hear she , but be afraid to come to help . two of the strong and fierce man at once seize poor young Margery ; and though she fight for her child and home , she be but an infant herself in their hand . in spite of tear , and shriek , and struggle , they tear the babe from the mother 's arm , and cast it on the lime ash floor ; then they bear she away to their horse   for by this time she be senseless , and tell the other to sack the house , ride off with their prize to the valley . and from the description of one of those two , who carry off the poor woman , I know beyond all doubt that it be Carver Doone himself . 
 the other Doones be leave behind , and grieve perhaps in some respect , set to with a will to scour the house , and to bring away all that be good to eat . and be a little vex herein   for the Badcocks be not a rich couple   and find no more than bacon , and egg , and cheese , and little item , and nothing to drink but water ; in a word , their taste be offend , they come back , to the kitchen , and stamp ; and there be the baby lie . 
 by evil luck , this child begin to squeal about his mother , have be pet hitherto , and will not to get all he want , by raise his voice but a little . now the mark of the floor be upon his head , as the maid   who have steal to look at he , when the rough man be swear upstairs   give evidence . and she put a dish - cloth under his head , and kiss he , and run away again . her name be Honour Jose , and she mean what be right by her master and mistress ; but could not help be frightened . and many woman have blame she , as I think unduly , for her mode of forsake baby so . if it have be her own baby , instinct rather than reason might have have the day with she ; but the child be bear of her mistress , she wish he good luck , and leave he , as the fierce man come downstairs . and be alarm by their power of language   because they have find no silver , she creep away in a breathless hurry , and afraid how her breath might come back to she . for oftentime she have hiccough . 
 while this good maid be in the oven , by side of back - kitchen fireplace , with a faggot of wood draw over she , and lie so that her own heart beat worse than if she be bake ; the man   as I say before   come downstairs , and stamp around the baby . 
 ' Rowland , be the bacon good ? ' one of they ask with an oath or two ; ' it be too bad of Carver to go off with the only prize , and leave we in a starve cottage ; and not enough to eat for two of we . fetch down the stave of the rack , my boy . what be farmer to have for supper ? ' 
 ' naught but an onion or two , and a loaf and a rasher of rusty bacon . these poor devil live so badly , they be not worth rob . ' 
 ' no game ! then let we have a game of loriot with the baby ! it will be the good thing that could befall a lusty infant heretic . ride a cock - horse to Banbury Cross . Bye , bye , baby bunting ; toss he up , and let I see if my wrist be steady . ' 
 the cruelty of this man be a thing it make I sick to speak of ; enough that when the poor baby fall   without attempt at cry or scream , think it part of his usual play , when they toss he up , to come down again , the maid in the oven of the back - kitchen , not be any door between , hear they say as follow , 
  ' if any man asketh who kill thee , 
  say ' twa the Doones of Bagworthy . '  

    always pronounce ' Badgery . ' 
 now I think that when we hear this story , and poor Kit Badcock come all around , in a sort of half - crazy manner , not look up at any one , but drop his eye , and ask whether we think he have be well - treat , and seem void of regard for life , if this be all the style of it ; then have know he a lusty man , and a fine singer in an ale - house , and much incline to lay down the law , as show a high hand about woman , I really think that it move we more than if he have go about rant , and raving , and vow revenge upon every one . 




 CHAPTER LXX 

 compel to volunteer 

 there have be some trouble in our own home during the previous autumn , while yet I be in London . for certain note fugitive from the army of King Monmouth   which he himself have desert , in a low and currish manner , have fail to obtain free shipment from the coast near Watersmouth , have return into the wild of Exmoor , trust to lurk , and be comfort among the common people . neither be they disappointed , for a certain length of time ; nor in the end be their disappointment cause by fault on our part . Major Wade be one of they ; an active and well - mean man ; but prone to fail in courage , upon last trial ; although in a moment ready . Squire John Whichehalse   not the baron   and Parson Powell   catch he   two or three month before my return   in Farley farmhouse , near Brendon . he have be up at our house several time ; and Lizzie think a great deal of he . and well I know that if at that time I have be in the neighbourhood , he should not have be take so easily . 
    not our parson Bowden , nor any more a friend of his . our 
  Parson Bowden never have naught whatever to do with it ; and 
  never smoke a pipe with Parson Powell after it . J.R. 
 John Birch , the farmer who have shelter he , be so fearful of punishment , that he hang himself , in a few day ' time , and even before he be apprehend . but nothing be do to Grace Howe , of Bridgeball , who have be Wade 's great comforter ; neither be anything do to we ; although Eliza have add greatly to mother 's alarm and danger by fall upon Rector Powell , and most soundly rat he for his meanness , and his cruelty , and cowardice , as she call it , in set man with firearm upon a poor helpless fugitive , and rob all our neighbourhood of its fame for hospitality . however , by mean of Sergeant Bloxham , and his good report of we , as well as by virtue of Wade 's confession   which prove of use to the Government   my mother escape all penalty . 
 it be likely enough that good folk will think it hard upon our neighbourhood to be threaten , and sometimes heavily punish , for kindness and humanity ; and yet to be leave to help ourselves against tyranny , and base rapine . and now at last our gorge be risen , and our heart in tumult . we have bear our trouble long , as a wise and wholesome chastisement ; quite content to have some few thing of our own unmeddle with . but what could a man dare to call his own , or what right could he have to wish for it , while he leave his wife and child at the pleasure of any stranger ? 
 the people come flock all around I , at the blacksmith 's forge , and the Brendon alehouse ; and I could scarce come out of church , but they get I among the tombstone . they all agree that I be bind to take command and management . I bid they go to the magistrate , but they say they have be too often . then I tell they that I have no wit for ordering of an armament , although I could find fault enough with the one which have not succeed . but they would hearken to none of this . 
 all they say be ' try to lead we ; and we will try not to run away . ' 
 this seem to I to be common sense , and good stuff , instead of mere bragging ; moreover , I myself be move by the bitter wrong of Margery , have know she at the Sunday - school , ere ever I go to Tiverton ; and have in those day , serious thought of make she my sweetheart ; although she be three year my elder . but now I feel this difficulty the Doones have behave very well to our farm , and to mother , and all of we , while I be away in London . therefore , would it not be shabby , and mean , for I to attack they now ? 
 yet be press still hard and hard , as day by day the excitement grow   with more and more talk over it , and no one else come forward to undertake the business , I agree at last to this ; that if the Doones , upon fair challenge , would not endeavour to make amend by give up Mistress Margery , as well as the man who have slay the babe , then I would lead the expedition , and do my good to subdue they . all our man be content with this , be thoroughly well assure from experience , that the haughty robber would only shoot any man who durst approach they with such proposal . 
 and then arise a difficult question who be to take the risk of make overture so unpleasant ? I wait for the rest to offer ; and as none be ready , the burden fall on I , and seem to be of my own inviting . hence I undertake the task , soon than reason about it ; for to give the cause of everything be bad than to go through with it . 
 it may have be three of the afternoon , when leave my witness behind   for they prefer the background   I appear with our Lizzie 's white handkerchief upon a kidney - bean stick , at the entrance to the robber ' dwelling . scarce know what might come of it , I have take the wise precaution of fasten a Bible over my heart , and another across my spinal column , in case of have to run away , with rude man shoot after I . for my mother say that the Word of God would stop a two - inch bullet , with three ounce of powder behind it . now I take no weapon , save those of the Spirit , for fear of be misunderstood . but I could not bring myself to think that any of honourable birth would take advantage of an unarmed man come in guise of peace to they . 
 and this conclusion of mine hold good , at least for a certain length of time ; inasmuch as two decent Doones appear , and hearing of my purpose , offer , without violence , to go and fetch the captain ; if I would stop where I be , and not begin to spy about anything . to this , of course , I agree at once ; for I want no more spying , because I have thorough knowledge of all in and out already . therefore , I stand wait steadily , with one hand in my pocket feel a sample of corn for market ; and the other against the rock , while I wonder to see it so brown already . 
 those man come back in a little while , with a sharp short message that Captain Carver would come out and speak to I by - and - by , when his pipe be finish . accordingly , I wait long , and we talk about the sign of bloom for the come apple season , and the rain that have fall last Wednesday night , and the principal dearth of Devonshire , that it will not grow many cowslip which we quite agree to be the prettiest of spring flower ; and all the time I be wonder how many black and deadly deed these two innocent youth have commit , even since last Christmas . 
 at length , a heavy and haughty step sound along the stone roof of the way ; and then the great Carver Doone draw up , and look at I rather scornfully . not with any speak scorn , nor flash of strong contumely ; but with that air of think little , and pray not to be trouble , which always vex a man who feel that he ought not to be despise so , and yet know not how to help it . 
 ' what be it you want , young man ? ' he ask , as if he have never see I before . 
 in spite of that strong loathing which I always feel at sight of he , I command my temper moderately , and tell he that I be come for his good , and that of his worshipful company , far more than for my own . that a general feeling of indignation have arise among we at the recent behaviour of certain young man , for which he might not be answerable , and for which we would not condemn he , without know the right of the question . but I beg he clearly to understand that a vile and inhuman wrong have be do , and such as we could not put up with ; but that if he would make what amend he could by restore the poor woman , and give up that odious brute who have slay the harmless infant , we would take no further motion ; and thing should go on as usual . as I put this in the few word that would meet my purpose , I be grieve to see a disdainful smile spread on his sallow countenance . then he make I a bow of mock courtesy , and reply as follow , 
 ' Sir John , your new honour have turn your poor head , as might have be expect . we be not in the habit of desert anything that belong to we ; far less our sacred relative . the insolence of your demand well - nigh outdo the ingratitude . if there be a man upon Exmoor who have grossly ill - use we , kidnap our young woman , and slay half a dozen of our young man , you be that outrageous rogue , Sir John . and after all this , how have we behave ? we have lay no hand upon your farm , we have not carry off your woman , we have even allow you to take our queen , by creep and crawl treachery ; and we have give you leave of absence to help your cousin the highwayman , and to come home with a title . and now , how do you requite we ? by inflame the boorish indignation at a little frolic of our young man ; and by come with insolent demand , to yield to which would ruin we . ah , you ungrateful viper ! ' 
 as he turn away in sorrow from I , shake his head at my badness , I become so overcome   never have be quite assure , even by people 's praise , about my own goodness ; moreover , the light which he throw upon thing differ so greatly from my own , that , in a word not to be too long I fear that I be a villain . and with many bitter pang for I have bad thing to repent of I begin at my leisure to ask myself whether or not this bill of indictment against John Ridd be true . some of it I know to be   however much I condemn myself   altogether out of reason ; for instance , about my go away with Lorna very quietly , over the snow , and to save my love from be starve away from I . in this there be no creep neither crawl treachery ; for all be do with slide ; and yet I be so out of training for be charge by other people beyond mine own conscience , that Carver Doone 's harsh word come on I , like prickly spinach sow with rake . therefore I reply , and say , 
 ' it be true that I owe you gratitude , sir , for a certain time of forbearance ; and it be to prove my gratitude that I be come here now . I do not think that my evil deed can be set against your own ; although I can not speak flowingly upon my good deed as you can . I take your queen because you starve she , have steal she long before , and kill her mother and brother . this be not for I to dwell upon now ; any more than I would say much about your murdering of my father . but how the balance hang between we , God know well than thou or I , thou low miscreant , Carver Doone . ' 
 I have work myself up , as I always do , in the manner of heavy man ; grow hot like an ill - washered wheel revolving , though I start with a cool axle ; and I feel ashamed of myself for heat , and ready to ask pardon . but Carver Doone regard I with a noble and fearless grandeur . 
 ' I have give thee thy choice , John Ridd , ' he say in a lofty manner , which make I drop away under he ; ' I always wish to do my good with the bad people who come near I . and of all I have ever meet with thou art the very bad , Sir John , and the most dishonest . ' 
 now after all my labour to pay every man to a penny , and to allow the woman over , when among the couch - grass   which be a sad thing for their gown , to be charge like this , I say , so amazed I that I stand , with my leg quite open , and ready for an earthquake . and the scornful way in which he say ' Sir John , ' go to my very heart , remind I of my littleness . but see no use in bandy word , nay , rather the chance of mischief , I do my good to look calmly at he , and to say with a quiet voice , ' Farewell , Carver Doone , this time , our day of reckoning be nigh . ' 
 ' Thou fool , it be come , ' he cry , leap aside into the niche of rock by the doorway ; ' fire ! ' 
 save for the quickness of spring , and readiness , learn in many a wrestling bout , that knavish trick must have end I ; but scarce be the word ' fire ! ' out of his mouth ere I be out of fire , by a single bind behind the rocky pillar of the opening . in this jump I be so brisk , at impulse of the love of life   for I see the muzzle set upon I from the darkness of the cavern , that the man who have train their gun upon I with goodwill and daintiness , could not check their finger crook upon the heavy trigger ; and the volley sing with a roar behind it , down the avenue of crag . 
 with one thing and another , and most of all the treachery of this dastard scheme , I be so amazed that I turn and run , at the very top of my speed , away from these vile fellow ; and luckily for I , they have not another charge to send after I . and thus by good fortune , I escape ; but with a bitter heart , and mind at their treacherous usage . 
 without any further hesitation ; I agree to take command of the honest man who be burn to punish , ay and destroy , those outlaw , as now beyond all bearing . one condition , however , I make , namely , that the Counsellor should be spar if possible ; not because he be less a villain than any of the other , but that he seem less violent ; and above all , have be good to Annie . and I find hard work to make they listen to my wish upon this point ; for of all the Doones , Sir Counsellor have make himself most hat , by his love of law and reason . 
 we arrange that all our man should come and fall into order with pike and musket , over against our dung - hill , and we settle early in the day , that their wife might come and look at they . for most of these man have good wife ; quite different from sweetheart , such as the militia have ; woman indeed who could hold to a man , and see to he , and bury he if his luck be evil and perhaps have no one afterwards . and all these woman press their right upon their precious husband , and bring so many child with they , and make such a fuss , and hugging , and race after little leg , that our farm - yard might be take for an out - door school for baby rather than a review ground . 
 I myself be to and fro among the child continually ; for if I love anything in the world , foremost I love child . they warm , and yet they cool our heart , as we think of what we be , and what in young clothe we hop to be ; and how many thing have come across . and to see our motif move in the little thing that know not what their aim or object be , must almost or ought at least , to lead we home , and soften we . for either end of life be home ; both source and issue be God . 
 nevertheless , I must confess that the child be a plague sometimes . they never could have enough of I be a hundred to one , you might say but I have more than enough of they ; and yet be not content . for they have so many way of talk , and of tug at my hair , and of sit upon my neck   not even two with their leg alike , and they force I to jump so vehemently , seem to court the peril of my come down neck and crop with they , and urge I still to go fast , however fast I might go with they ; I assure you that they be sometimes so hard and tyrannical over I , that I might almost as well have be among the very Doones themselves . 
 nevertheless , the way in which the child make I useful prove also of some use to I ; for their mother be so pleased by the exertion of the ' great Gee - gee ' as all the small one entitle I that they give I unlimited power and authority over their husband ; moreover , they do their utmost among their relative round about , to fetch recruit for our little band . and by such mean , several of the yeomanry from Barnstaple , and from Tiverton , be add to our number ; and inasmuch as these be arm with heavy sword , and short carabine , their appearance be truly formidable . 
 Tom Faggus also join we heartily , be now quite heal of his wound , except at time when the wind be easterly . he be make second in command to I ; and I would gladly have have he first , as more fertile in expedient ; but he decline such rank on the plea that I know most of the seat of war ; besides that I might be hold in some measure to draw authority from the king . also Uncle Ben come over to help we with his advice and presence , as well as with a band of stout warehouseman , whom he bring from Dulverton . for he have never forgive the old outrage put upon he ; and though it have be to his interest to keep quiet during the last attack , under Commander Stickles for the sake of his secret gold mine yet now he be in a position to give full vent to his feeling . for he and his partner when fully - assured of the value of their digging , have obtain from the Crown a licence to adventure in search of mineral , by payment of a heavy fine and a yearly royalty . therefore they have now no long any cause for secrecy , neither for dread of the outlaws ; have so add to their force as to be a match for they . and although Uncle Ben be not the man to keep his miner idle an hour more than might be help , he promise that when we have fix the moment for an assault on the valley , a score of they should come to aid we , head by Simon Carfax , and arm with the gun which they always keep for the protection of their gold . 
 now whether it be Uncle Ben , or whether it be Tom Faggus or even my own self for all three of we claim the sole honour be more than I think fair to settle without allow they a voice . but at any rate , a clever thing be devise among we ; and perhaps it would be the fair thing to say that this bright stratagem   worthy of the great Duke himself   be contribute , little by little , among the entire three of we , all have pipe , and schnapps - and - water , in the chimney - corner . however , the world , which always judge accord to reputation , vow that so fine a stroke of war could only come from a highwayman ; and so Tom Faggus get all the honour , at less perhaps than a third of the cost . 
 not to attempt to rob he of it for robber , more than any other , contend for right of property let I try to describe this grand artifice . it be know that the Doones be fond of money , as well as strong drink , and other thing ; and more especially fond of gold , when they could get it pure and fine . therefore it be agree that in this way we should tempt they ; for we know that they look with ridicule upon our rustic preparation ; after repulse King 's trooper , and the militia of two county , be it likely that they should yield their fortress to a set of ploughboy ? we , for our part , feel of course , the power of this reasoning , and that where regular troop have fail , half - arm countryman must fail , except by superior judgment and harmony of action . though perhaps the militia would have suffice , if they have only fight against the foe , instead of against each other . from these thing we take warning ; have fail through over - confidence , be it not possible now to make the enemy fail through the selfsame cause ? 
 hence , what we devise be this ; to delude from home a part of the robber , and fall by surprise on the other part . we cause it to be spread abroad that a large heap of gold be now collect at the mine of the Wizard 's Slough . and when this rumour must have reach they , through woman who come to and fro , as some entirely faithful to they be allow to do , we send Captain Simon Carfax , the father of little Gwenny , to demand an interview with the Counsellor , by night , and as it be secretly . then he be to set forth a list of imaginary grievance against the owner of the mine ; and to offer partly through resentment , partly through the hope of gain , to betray into their hand , upon the Friday night , by far the great weight of gold as yet send up for refining . he be to have one quarter part , and they to take the residue . but inasmuch as the convoy across the moor , under his command , would be strong , and strongly arm , the Doones must be sure to send not less than a score of man , if possible . he himself , at a place agree upon , and fit for an ambuscade , would call a halt , and contrive in the darkness to pour a little water into the priming of his company 's gun . 
 it cost we some trouble and a great deal of money to bring the sturdy Cornishman into this deceitful part ; and perhaps he never would have consent but for his obligation to I , and the wrong   as he say   of his daughter . however , as he be the man for the task , both from his coolness and courage , and be know to have charge of the mine , I press he , until he undertake to tell all the lie we require . and right well he do it too , have once make up his mind to it ; and perceive that his own interest call for the total destruction of the robber . 




 CHAPTER LXXI 

 a long account settled 

 have resolve on a night - assault   as our undisciplined man , three - fourth of whom have never be shoot at , could not fairly be expect to march up to visible musket - mouth , we care not much about drill our force , only to teach they to hold a musket , so far as we could supply that weapon to those with the clever eye ; and to give they familiarity with the noise it make in explode . and we fix upon Friday night for our venture , because the moon would be at the full ; and our powder be come from Dulverton on the Friday afternoon . 
 Uncle Reuben do not mean to expose himself to shoot , his time of life for risk of life be now well over and the residue too valuable . but his counsel , and his influence , and above all his warehouseman , well practise in beat carpet , be of true service to we . his miner also do great wonder , have a grudge against the Doones ; as indeed who have not for thirty mile round their valley ? 
 it be settle that the yeoman , have good horse under they , should give account   with the miner ' help   of as many Doones as might be despatch to plunder the pretended gold . and as soon as we know that this party of robber , be it more or less , be out of hear from the valley , we be to fall to , ostensibly at the Doone - gate   which be impregnable now , but in reality upon their rear , by mean of my old water - slide . for I have choose twenty young fellow , partly miner , and partly warehouseman , and sheep farmer , and some of other vocation , but all to be rely upon for spirit and power of climbing . and with proper tool to aid we , and myself to lead the way , I feel no doubt whatever but that we could all attain the crest where first I have meet with Lorna . 
 upon the whole , I rejoice that Lorna be not present now . it must have be irksome to her feeling to have all her kindre and old associate   much as she keep aloof from they   put to death without ceremony , or else put all of we to death . for all of we be resolve this time to have no more shilly - shallying ; but to go through with a nasty business , in the style of honest Englishmen , when the question come to ' your life or mine . ' 
 there be hardly a man among we who have not suffer bitterly from the miscreant now before we . one have lose his wife perhaps , another have lose a daughter accord to their age , another have lose his favourite cow ; in a word , there be scarcely any one who have not to complain of a hayrick ; and what surprise I then , not now , be that the man least injure make the great push concern it . but be the wrong too great to speak of , or too small to swear about , from poor Kit Badcock to rich Master Huckaback , there be not one but go heart and soul for stamp out these firebrand . 
 the moon be lift well above the shoulder of the upland , when we , the choose band , set forth , have the short cut along the valley to foot of the Bagworthy water ; and therefore have allow the rest an hour , to fetch round the moor and hill ; we be not to begin our climb until we hear a musket fire from the height on the left - hand side , where John Fry himself be station , upon his own and his wife 's request ; so as to keep out of action . and that be the place where I have be use to sit , and to watch for Lorna . and John Fry be to fire his gun , with a ball of wool inside it , so soon as he hear the hurly - burly at the Doone - gate beginning ; which we , by reason of waterfall , could not hear , down in the meadow there . 
 we wait a very long time , with the moon march up heaven steadfastly , and the white fog tremble in chord and column , like a silver harp of the meadow . and then the moon draw up the fog , and scarf herself in white with they ; and so be proud , gleam upon the water , like a bride at her look - glass ; and yet there be no sound of either John Fry , or his blunderbuss . 
 I begin to think that the worthy John , be out of all danger , and have bring a counterpane   accord to his wife 's direction , because one of the child have a cold , must veritably have go to sleep ; leave other people to kill , or be kill , as might be the will of God ; so that he be comfortable . but herein I do wrong to John , and be ready to acknowledge it ; for suddenly the most awful noise that anything short of thunder could make , come down among the rock , and go and hang upon the corner . 
 ' the signal , my lad , ' I cry , leap up and rub my eye ; for even now , while condemn John unjustly , I be give he right to be hard upon I . ' now hold on by the rope , and lie your quarter - staff across , my lad ; and keep your gun point to heaven , lest haply we shoot one another . ' 
 ' we shall not   never shutt one anoother , wi ' our goon at that mark , I reckon , ' say an oldish chap , but as tough as leather , and esteem a wit for his dryness . 
 ' you come next to I , old Ike ; you be enough to dry up the water ; now , remember , all lean well forward . if any man throw his weight back , down he go ; and perhaps he may never get up again ; and most likely he will shoot himself . ' 
 I be still more afraid of their shoot I ; for my chief alarm in this steep ascent be neither of the water nor of the rock , but of the loaded gun we bore . if any man slip , off might go his gun , and however good his meaning , I be first be most likely to take far more than I fain would apprehend . 
 for this cause , I have debate with Uncle Ben and with Cousin Tom as to the expediency of our climbing with gun unload . but they , not be in the way themselves , assure I that there be nothing to fear , except through uncommon clumsiness ; and that as for charge our gun at the top , even veteran troop could scarcely be trust to perform it properly in the hurry , and the darkness , and the noise of fighting before they . 
 however , thank God , though a gun go off , no one be any the bad for it , neither do the Doones notice it , in the thick of the firing in front of they . for the order to those of the sham attack , conduct by Tom Faggus , be to make the great possible noise , without exposure of themselves ; until we , in the rear , have fall to ; which John Fry be again to give the signal of . 
 therefore we , of the choose band , steal up the meadow quietly , keep in the blots of shade , and hollow of the watercourse . and the early notice the Counsellor have , or any one else , of our presence , be the blazing of the log - wood house , where live that villain Carver . it be my especial privilege to set this house on fire ; upon which I have insist , exclusively and conclusively . no other hand but mine should lay a brand , or strike steel on flint for it ; I have make all preparation carefully for a goodly blaze . and I must confess that I rub my hand , with a strong delight and comfort , when I see the home of that man , who have fire so many house , have its turn of smoke , and blaze , and of crackle fury .
