 we take good care , however , to burn no innocent woman or child in that most righteous destruction . for we bring they all out beforehand ; some be glad , and some be sorry ; accord to their disposition . for Carver have ten or a dozen wife ; and perhaps that have something to do with his take the loss of Lorna so easily . one child I notice , as I save he ; a fair and handsome little fellow , whom   if Carver Doone could love anything on earth beside his wretched self   he do love . the boy climb on my back and ride ; and much as I hat his father , it be not in my heart to say or do a thing to vex he . 
 leave these poor injure people to behold their burn home , we draw aside , by my direction , into the covert beneath the cliff . but not before we have lay our brand to three other house , after call the woman forth , and bid they go for their husband , and to come and fight a hundred of we . in the smoke and rush , and fire , they believe that we be a hundred ; and away they run , in consternation , to the battle at the Doone - gate . 
 ' all Doone - town be on fire , on fire ! ' we hear they shriek as they go ; ' a hundred soldier be burn it , with a dreadful great man at the head of they ! ' 
 presently , just as I expect , back come the warrior of the Doones ; leave but two or three at the gate , and burn with wrath to crush under foot the presumptuous clown in their valley . just then the waxing fire leap above the red crest of the cliff , and dance on the pillar of the forest , and lap like a tide on the stone of the slope . all the valley flow with light , and the limpid water redden , and the fair young woman shine , and the naked child glisten . 
 but the fine sight of all be to see those haughty man stride down the causeway darkly , reckless of their end , but resolute to have two life for every one . a fine dozen of young man could not have be find in the world perhaps , nor a braver , nor a viler one . 
 see how few there be of they , I be very loath to fire , although I cover the leader , who appear to be dash Charley ; for they be at easy distance now , brightly shine by the fire - light , yet ignorant where to look for we . I think that we might take they prisoner though what good that could be God know , as they must have be hang thereafter anyhow I be loath to shoot , or to give the word to my follower . 
 but my follower wait for no word ; they see a fair shot at the man they abhor , the man who have rob they of home or of love , and the chance be too much for their charity . at a signal from old Ikey , who level his own gun first , a dozen musket be discharge , and half of the Doones drop lifeless , like so many log of firewood , or chop - block roll over . 
 although I have see a great battle before , and a hundred time the carnage , this appear to I to be horrible ; and I be at first incline to fall upon our man for behave so . but one instant show I that they be right ; for while the valley be fill with howling , and with shriek of woman , and the beam of the blaze house fall , and hiss in the bubble river ; all the rest of the Doones leap at we , like so many demon . they fire wildly , not see we well among the hazel bush ; and then they club their musket , or draw their sword , as might be ; and furiously drive at we . 
 for a moment , although we be twice their number , we fall back before their valorous fame , and the power of their onset . for my part , admire their courage greatly , and count it slur upon manliness that two should be down upon one so , I withhold my hand awhile ; for I care to meet none but Carver ; and he be not among they . the whirl and hurry of this fight , and the hard blow rain down for now all gun be empty take away my power of seeing , or reasoning upon anything . yet one thing I see , which dwell long with I ; and that be Christopher Badcock spend his life to get Charley 's . 
 how he have find out , none may tell ; both be dead so long ago ; but , at any rate , he have find out that Charley be the man who have rob he of his wife and honour . it be Carver Doone who take she away , but Charleworth Doone be beside he ; and , accord to cast of dice , she fall to Charley 's share . all this Kit Badcock   who be mad , accord to our measure   have discover , and treasure up ; and now be his revenge - time . 
 he have come into the conflict without a weapon of any kind ; only beg I to let he be in the very thick of it . for he , he say , life be no matter , after the loss of his wife and child ; but death be matter to he , and he mean to make the most of it . such a face I never see , and never hope to see again , as when poor Kit Badcock spy Charley come towards we . 
 we have think this man a patient fool , a philosopher of a little sort , or one who could feel nothing . and his quiet manner of go about , and the gentleness of his answer   when some brute ask he where his wife be , and whether his baby have be well - truss , these have mislead we to think that the man would turn the mild cheek to everything . but I , in the loneliness of our barn , have listen , and have weep with he . 
 therefore be I not surprised , so much as all the rest of we , when , in the foremost of red light , Kit go up to Charleworth Doone , as if to some inheritance ; and take his seisin of right upon he , be himself a powerful man ; and beg a word aside with he . what they say aside , I know not ; all I know be that without weapon , each man kill the other . and Margery Badcock come , and weep , and hang upon her poor husband ; and die , that summer , of heart - disease . 
 now for these and other thing   whereof I could tell a thousand   be the reckoning come that night ; and not a line we miss of it ; soon as our bad blood be up . I like not to tell of slaughter , though it might be of wolf and tiger ; and that be a night of fire and slaughter , and of very long - harbour revenge . enough that ere the daylight break upon that wan March morning , the only Doones still leave alive be the Counsellor and Carver . and of all the dwelling of the Doones   inhabit with luxury , and luscious taste , and licentiousness   not even one be leave , but all make potash in the river . 
 this may seem a violent and unholy revenge upon they . and I   who lead the heart of it   have in these my latter year doubt how I shall be judge , not of man for God only know the error of man 's judgment but by that great God Himself , the front of whose forehead be mercy . 




 CHAPTER LXXII 

 the COUNSELLOR and the carver 

 from that great confusion for nothing can be break up , whether lawful or unlawful , without a vast amount of dust , and many people grumble , and mourn for the good old time , when all the world be happiness , and every man a gentleman , and the sun himself far brighter than since the brassy idol upon which he shine be break from all this loss of ancient landmark   as unrobbed man begin to call our clearance of those murderer   we return on the follow day , almost as full of anxiety as we be of triumph . in the first place , what could we possibly do with all these woman and child , throw on our hand as one might say , with none to protect and care for they ? again how should we answer to the justice of the peace , or perhaps even to Lord Jeffreys , for have , without even a warrant , take the law into our own hand , and abate our nuisance so forcibly ? and then , what be to be do with the spoil , which be of great value ; though the diamond necklace come not to public light ? for we see a mighty host of claimant already leap up for booty . every man who have ever be rob , expect usury on his loss ; the lord of the manor demand the whole ; and so do the King 's Commissioner of revenue at Porlock ; and so do the man who have fight our battle ; while even the parson , both Bowden and Powell , and another who have no parish in it , threaten we with the just wrath of the Church , unless each have tithe of the whole of it . 
 now this be not as it ought to be ; and it seem as if by burn the nest of robber , we have but hatch their egg ; until be make sole guardian of the capture treasure   by reason of my know honesty   I hit upon a plan , which give very little satisfaction ; yet carry this advantage , that the grumbler argue against one another and for the most part come to blow ; which renew their goodwill to I , as be abuse by the adversary . 
 and my plan be no more than this not to pay a farthing to lord of manor , parson , or even King 's Commissioner , but after make good some of the recent and prove loss where the man could not afford to lose to pay the residue   which might be worth some fifty thousand pound   into the Exchequer at Westminster ; and then let all the claimant file what will they please in Chancery . 
 now this be a very noble device , for the mere name of Chancery , and the high repute of the fee therein , and low repute of the lawyer , and the comfortable knowledge that the woolsack itself be the golden fleece , absorb gold for ever , if the standard be but pure ; consideration of these thing stave off at once the lord of the manor , and all the little farmer , and even those whom most I fear ; videlicet , the parson . and the King 's Commissioner be compel to profess himself content , although of all he be most aggrieve ; for his picking would have be goodly . 
 moreover , by this plan I make although I never think of that a mighty friend worth all the enemy , whom the loss of money move . the first man now in the kingdom   by virtue perhaps of energy , rather than of excellence   be the great Lord Jeffreys , appoint the head of the Equity , as well as the law of the realm , for his kindness in hang five hundred people , without the mere brief of trial . nine out of ten of these people be innocent , it be true ; but that prove the merit of the Lord Chief Justice so much the great for hang they , as show what might be expect of he , when he truly get hold of a guilty man . now the King have see the force of this argument ; and not be without gratitude for a high - season dish of cruelty , have promote the only man in England , combine the gift of both butcher and cook . 
 nevertheless , I do beg you all to believe of I and I think that , after follow I so long , you must believe it that I do not even know at the time of Lord Jeffreys 's high promotion . not that my knowledge of this would have lead I to act otherwise in the matter ; for my object be to pay into an office , and not to any official ; neither if I have know the fact , could I have see its bearing upon the receipt of my money . for the King 's Exchequer be , meseemeth , of the Common Law ; while Chancery be of Equity , and well name for its many chance . but the true result of the thing be this Lord Jeffreys be now head of the law , and almost head of the kingdom , get possession of that money , and be kindly pleased with it . 
 and this meet our second difficulty ; for the law have win and laugh over the spoil , must have injure its own title by impugn our legality . 
 next , with regard to the woman and child , we be long in a state of perplexity . we do our very good at the farm , and so do many other to provide for they , until they should manage about their own subsistence . and after a while this trouble go , as nearly all trouble go with time . some of the woman be take back by their parent , or their husband , or it may be their sweetheart ; and those who fail of this , go forth , some upon their own account to the New World plantation , where the fair sex be valuable ; and some to english city ; and the plainer one to field work . and most of the child go with their mother , or be bind apprentice ; only Carver Doone 's handsome child have lose his mother and stay with I . 
 this boy go about with I everywhere . he have take as much of like to I first show in his eye by the firelight as his father have of hatred ; and I , perceive his noble courage , scorn of lie , and high spirit , become almost as fond of Ensie as he be of I . he tell we that his name be ' Ensie , ' mean for ' Ensor , ' I suppose , from his father 's grandfather , the old Sir Ensor Doone . and this boy appear to be Carver 's heir , have be bear in wedlock , contrary to the general manner and custom of the Doones . 
 however , although I love the poor child , I could not help feel very uneasy about the escape of his father , the savage and brutal Carver . this man be leave to roam the country , homeless , foodless , and desperate , with his giant strength , and great skill in arm , and the whole world to be revenge upon . for his escape the miner , as I shall show , be answerable ; but of the Counsellor 's safe departure the burden lie on myself alone . and inasmuch as there be people who consider themselves ill - use , unless one tell they everything , straiten though I be for space , I will glance at this transaction . 
 after the desperate charge of young Doones have be meet by we , and break , and just as Poor Kit Badcock die in the arm of the dead Charley , I happen to descry a patch of white on the grass of the meadow , like the head of a sheep after washing - day . observe with some curiosity how carefully this white thing move along the bar of darkness betwixt the panel of firelight , I run up to intercept it , before it reach the little postern which we use to call Gwenny 's door . perceive I , the white thing stop , and be for make back again ; but I run up at full speed ; and lo , it be the flow silvery hair of that sage the Counsellor , who be scuttle away upon all four ; but now rise and confront I . 
 ' John , ' he say , ' Sir John , you will not play falsely with your ancient friend , among these violent fellow , I look to you to protect I , John . ' 
 ' honoured sir , you be right , ' I reply ; ' but surely that posture be unworthy of yourself , and your many resource . it be my intention to let you go free . ' 
 ' I know it . I could have swear to it . you be a noble fellow , John . I say so , from the very first ; you be a noble fellow , and an ornament to any rank . ' 
 ' but upon two condition , ' I add , gently take he by the arm ; for instead of display any desire to commune with my nobility , he be edge away toward the postern ; ' the first be that you tell I truly   for now it can matter to none of you   who it be that slew my father . ' 
 ' I will tell you truly and frankly , John ; however painful to I to confess it . it be my son , Carver . ' 
 ' I think as much , or I feel as much all along , ' I answer ; ' but the fault be none of your , sir ; for you be not even present . ' 
 ' if I have be there , it would not have happen . I be always opposed to violence . therefore , let I haste away ; this scene be against my nature . ' 
 ' you shall go directly , Sir Counsellor , after meet my other condition ; which be , that you place in my hand Lady Lorna 's diamond necklace . ' 
 ' ah , how often I have wish , ' say the old man with a heavy sigh , ' that it might yet be in my power to ease my mind in that respect , and to do a thoroughly good deed by lawful restitution . ' 
 ' then try to have it in your power , sir . surely , with my encouragement , you might summon resolution . ' 
 ' alas , John , the resolution have be ready long ago . but the thing be not in my possession . Carver , my son , who slay your father , upon he you will find the necklace . what be jewel to I , young man , at my time of life ? bauble and trash , I detest they , from the sin they have lead I to answer for . when you come to my age , good Sir John , you will scorn all jewel , and care only for a pure and bright conscience . ah ! ah ! let I go . I have make my peace with God . ' 
 he look so hoary , and so silvery , and serene in the moonlight , that verily I must have believe he , if he have not draw in his breast . but I happen to have notice that when an honest man give vent to noble and great sentiment , he spread his breast , and throw it out , as if his heart be swell ; whereas I have see this old gentleman draw in his breast more than once , as if it happen to contain well good than sentiment . 
 ' will you applaud I , kind sir , ' I say , keep he very tight , all the while , ' if I place it in your power to ratify your peace with God ? the pledge be upon your heart , no doubt , for there it lie at this moment . ' 
 with these word , and some apology for have recourse to strong measure , I thrust my hand inside his waistcoat , and draw forth Lorna 's necklace , purely sparkle in the moonlight , like the dancing of new star . the old man make a stab at I , with a knife which I have not espy ; but the vicious onset fail ; and then he knelt , and clasp his hand . 
 ' oh , for God 's sake , John , my son , rob I not in that manner . they belong to I ; and I love they so ; I would give almost my life for they . there be one jewel I can look at for hour , and see all the light of heaven in it ; which I never shall see elsewhere . all my wretched , wicked life oh , John , I be a sad hypocrite but give I back my jewel . or else kill I here ; I be a babe in your hand ; but I must have back my jewel . ' 
 as his beautiful white hair fall away from his noble forehead , like a silver wreath of glory , and his powerful face , for once , be move with real emotion , I be so amazed and overcome by the grand contradiction of nature , that verily I be on the point of give he back the necklace . but honesty , which be say to be the first instinct of all the Ridds   though I myself never find it so , happen here to occur to I , and so I say , without more haste than might be expect , 
 ' Sir Counsellor , I can not give you what do not belong to I . but if you will show I that particular diamond which be heaven to you , I will take upon myself the risk and the folly of cut it out for you . and with that you must go content ; and I beseech you not to starve with that jewel upon your lip . ' 
 see no hope of well term , he show I his pet love of a jewel ; and I think of what Lorna be to I , as I cut it out   with the hinge of my knife sever the snake of gold   and place it in his careful hand . another moment , and he be go , and away through Gwenny 's postern ; and God know what become of he . 
 now as to Carver , the thing be this so far as I could ascertain from the valiant miner , no two of whom tell the same story , any more than one of they tell it twice . the band of Doones which sally forth for the robbery of the pretend convoy be meet by Simon Carfax , accord to arrangement , at the ruin house call the Warren , in that part of Bagworthy Forest where the river Exe   as yet a very small stream   run through it . the Warren , as all our people know , have belong to a fine old gentleman , whom every one call ' the squire , ' who have retreat from active life to pass the rest of his day in fishing , and shooting , and help his neighbour . for he be a man of some substance ; and no poor man ever leave the Warren without a bag of good victual , and a few shilling put in his pocket . however , this poor squire never make a great mistake , than in hop to end his life peacefully upon the bank of a trout - stream , and in the green forest of Bagworthy . for as he come home from the brook at dusk , with his fly - rod over his shoulder , the Doones fall upon he , and murder he , and then sack his house , and burn it . 
 now this have make honest people timid about go past the Warren at night ; for , of course , it be say that the old Squire ' walk , ' upon certain night of the moon , in and out of the trunk of tree , on the green path from the river . on his shoulder he bear a fishing - rod , and his book of trout - fly , in one hand , and on his back a wicker - creel ; and now and then he would burst out laugh to think of his come so near the Doones . 
 and now that one turn to consider it , this seem a strangely righteous thing , that the scene of one of the great crime even by Doones commit should , after twenty year , become the scene of vengeance fall   like hail from heaven   upon they . for although the Warren lie well away to the westward of the mine ; and the gold , under escort to Bristowe , or London , would have go in the other direction ; Captain Carfax , find this place well suit for work of his design , have persuade the Doones , that for reason of Government , the ore must go first to Barnstaple for inspection , or something of that sort . and as every one know that our Government send all thing westward when eastward bind , this have win the more faith for Simon , as be accord to nature . 
 now Simon , have meet these flower of the flock of villainy , where the rise moonlight flow through the weir - work of the wood , beg they to dismount ; and lead they with an air of mystery into the Squire 's ruin hall , black with fire , and green with weed . 
 ' captain , I have find a thing , ' he say to Carver Doone , himself , ' which may help to pass the hour , ere the lump of gold come by . the smuggler be a noble race ; but a miner 's eye be a match for they . there lie a puncheon of rare spirit , with the Dutchman 's brand upon it , hide behind the break hearth . set a man to watch outside ; and let we see what this be like . ' 
 with one accord they agree to this , and Carver pledge Master Carfax , and all the Doones grow merry . but Simon be bind , as he say , to see to their strict sobriety , draw a bucket of water from the well into which they have throw the dead owner , and beg they to mingle it with their drink ; which some of they do , and some refuse . 
 but the water from that well be pour , while they be carouse , into the priming - pan of every gun of theirs ; even as Simon have promise to do with the gun of the man they be come to kill . then just as the giant Carver arise , with a glass of pure holland in his hand , and by the light of the torch they have strike , propose the good health of the Squire 's ghost in the broken doorway stand a press of man , with pointed musket , cover every drunken Doone . how it fare upon that I know not , have none to tell I ; for each man work , neither thought of telling , nor whether he might be alive to tell . the Doones rush to their gun at once , and point they , and pull at they ; but the Squire 's well have drown their fire ; and then they know that they be betray , but resolve to fight like man for it . upon fight I can never dwell ; it breed such savage delight in I ; of which I would fain have less . enough that all the Doones fight bravely ; and like man   though bad one   die in the hall of the man they have murder . and with they die poor young De Whichehalse , who , in spite of his good father 's prayer , have cast in his lot with the robber . Carver Doone alone escape . partly through his fearful strength , and his yet more fearful face ; but mainly perhaps through his perfect coolness , and his mode of take thing . 
 I be happy to say that no more than eight of the gallant miner be kill in that combat , or die of their wound afterwards ; and add to these the eight we have lose in our assault on the valley   and two of they excellent warehouseman , it cost no more than sixteen life to be rid of nearly forty Doones , each of whom would most likely have kill three man in the course of a year or two . therefore , as I say at the time , a great work be do very reasonably ; here be nigh upon forty Doones destroy   in the valley , and up at the Warrens   despite their extraordinary strength and high skill in gunnery ; whereas of we ignorant rustic there be only sixteen to be count dead though other might be lamed , or so , and of those sixteen only two have leave wife , and their wife do not happen to care for they . 
 yet , for Lorna ' s sake , I be vex at the bold escape of Carver . not that I seek for Carver 's life , any more than I do for the Counsellor 's ; but that for we it be no light thing , to have a man of such power , and resource , and desperation , leave at large and furious , like a famished wolf round the sheepfold . yet greatly as I blame the yeoman , who be post on their horse , just out of shot from the Doone - gate , for the very purpose of intercept those who escape the miner , I could not get they to admit that any blame attach to they . 
 but lo , he have dash through the whole of they , with his horse at full gallop ; and be nearly out of shot before they begin to think of shoot he . then it appear from what a boy say for boy manage to be everywhere that Captain Carver ride through the Doone - gate , and so to the head of the valley . there , of course , he behold all the house , and his own among the number , flame with a handsome blaze , and throw a fine light around such as he often have revel in , when of other people 's property . but he swear the deadly of all oath , and see himself to be vanquish   so far as the luck of the moment go , spur his great black horse away , and pass into the darkness . 




 CHAPTER LXXIII 

 how to get out of chancery 

 thing at this time so befell I , that I can not tell one half ; but be like a boy who have leave his lesson   to the master 's very footfall   unready , except with false excuse . and as this make no good work , so I lament upon my lingering , in the time when I might have get through a good page , but go astray after trifle . however , every man must do accord to his intellect ; and look at the easy manner of my constitution , I think that most man will regard I with pity and goodwill for try , more than with contempt and wrath for have try unworthily . even as in the wrestling ring , whatever man do his good , and make an honest conflict , I always lay he down with softness , ease off his dusty fall . 
 but the thing which next betide I be not a fall of any sort ; but rather a most glorious rise to the summit of all fortune . for in good truth it be no less than the return of Lorna my Lorna , my own darling ; in wonderful health and spirit , and as glad as a bird to get back again . it would have do any one good for a twelve - month to behold her face and doing , and her beam eye and smile   not to mention blush also at my salutation , when this queen of every heart run about our room again . she do love this , and she must see that , and where be our old friend the cat ? all the house be full of brightness , as if the sun have come over the hill , and Lorna be his mirror . 
 my mother sit in an ancient chair , and wipe her cheek , and look at she ; and even Lizzie 's eye must dance to the freshness and joy of her beauty . as for I , you might call I mad ; for I run out and fling my good hat on the barn , and kiss mother Fry , till she make at I with the sugar - nipper . 
 what a quantity of thing Lorna have to tell we ! and yet how often we stop her mouth at least mother , I mean , and Lizzie and she quite as often would stop she own , run up in her joy to some one of we ! and then there arise the eat business which people now call ' refreshment , ' in these dandyfie day of our language for how be it possible that our Lorna could have come all that way , and to her own Exmoor , without be terribly hungry ? 
 ' oh , I do love it all so much , ' say Lorna , now for the fiftieth time , and not mean only the victual : ' the scent of the gorse on the moor drive I wild , and the primrose under the hedge . I be sure I be mean for a farmer 's I mean for a farm - house life , dear Lizzie ' for Lizzie be look saucily ' just as you be mean for a soldier 's bride , and for write despatch of victory . and now , since you will not ask I , dear mother , in the excellence of your manner , and even John have not the impudence , in spite of all his coat of arm I must tell you a thing , which I vow to keep until tomorrow morning ; but my resolution fail I . I be my own mistress what think you of that , mother ? I be my own mistress ! ' 
 ' then you shall not be so long , ' cry I ; for mother seem not to understand she , and seek about for her glass : ' darling , you shall be mistress of I ; and I will be your master . ' 
 ' a frank announcement of your intent , and beyond doubt a true one ; but surely unusual at this stage , and a little premature , John . however , what must be , must be . ' and with tear spring out of smile , she fall on my breast , and cry a bit . 
 when I come to smoke a pipe over it   after the rest be go to bed , I could hardly believe in my good luck . for here be I , without any merit , except of bodily power , and the absence of any falsehood   which surely be no commendation , so place that the noble man in England might envy I , and be vex with I . for the noble lady in all the land , and the pure , and the sweetest hang upon my heart , as if there be none to equal it . 
 I dwell upon this matter , long and very severely , while I smoke a new tobacco , bring by my own Lorna for I , and next to herself most delicious ; and as the smoke curl away , I think , ' surely this be too fine to last , for a man who never deserve it . ' 
 see no way out of this , I resolve to place my faith in God ; and so go to bed and dream of it . and have no presence of mind to pray for anything , under the circumstance , I think it well to fall asleep , and trust myself to the future . yet ere I fall asleep the roof above I swarm with angel , have Lorna under it . 
 in the morning Lorna be ready to tell her story , and we to hearken ; and she wear a dress of most simple stuff ; and yet perfectly wonderful , by mean of the shape and her figure . Lizzie be wild with jealousy , as might be expect   though never would Annie have be so , but have praise it , and crave for the pattern , and mother not understand it , look forth , to be teach about it . for it be strange to note that lately my dear mother have lose her quickness , and be never quite brisk , unless the question be about myself . she have see a great deal of trouble ; and grief begin to close on people , as their power of life decline . we say that she be hard of hearing ; but my opinion be , that see I inclined for marriage make her think of my father , and so perhaps a little too much , to dwell on the courting of thirty year agone . anyhow , she be the very good of mother ; and would smile and command herself ; and be   or try to believe herself   as happy as could be , in the doing of the young folk , and her own skill in detect they . yet , with the wisdom of age , renounce any opinion upon the matter ; since none could see the end of it . 
 but Lorna in her bright young beauty , and her knowledge of my heart , be not to be check by any thought of haply come evil . in the morning she be up , even soon than I be , and through all the corner of the hen , remember every one of they . I catch she and salute she with such warmth   be now none to look at we , that she vow she would never come out again ; and yet she come the next morning . 
 these thing ought not to be chronicle . yet I be of such nature , that find many part of life adverse to our wish , I must now and then draw pleasure from the blessed portion . and what portion can be more blessed than with youth , and health , and strength , to be love by a virtuous maid , and to love she with all one 's heart ? neither be my pride diminish , when I find what she have do , only from her love of I . 
 Earl Brandir 's ancient steward , in whose charge she have travel , with a proper escort , look upon she as a lovely maniac ; and the mixture of pity and admiration wherewith he regard she , be a strange thing to observe ; especially after he have see our simple house and manner . on the other hand , Lorna consider he a worthy but foolish old gentleman ; to whom true happiness mean no more than money and high position . 
 these two last she have be ready to abandon wholly , and have in part escape from they , as the enemy of her happiness . and she take advantage of the time , in a truly clever manner . for that happen to be a time as indeed all time hitherto   so far as my knowledge extend , have , somehow , or other , happen to be when everybody be only too glad to take money for do anything . and the great money - taker in the kingdom   next to the King and Queen , of course , who have due pre - eminence , and have teach the maid of honour   be generally acknowledge to be the Lord Chief Justice Jeffreys . 
 upon his return from the bloody assize , with triumph and great glory , after hang every man who be too poor to help it , he please his gracious Majesty so purely with the description of their delightful agony , that the King exclaim , ' this man alone be worthy to be at the head of the law . ' accordingly in his hand be place the Great Seal of England . 
 so it come to pass that Lorna 's destiny hang upon Lord Jeffreys ; for at this time Earl Brandir die , be take with gout in the heart , soon after I leave London . Lorna be very sorry for he ; but as he have never be able to hear one tone of her sweet silvery voice , it be not to be suppose that she weep without consolation . she grieve for he as we ought to grieve for any good man go ; and yet with a comforting sense of the benefit which the bless exchange must bring to he . 
 now the Lady Lorna Dugal appear to Lord Chancellor Jeffreys so exceed wealthy a ward that the lock would pay for turn . therefore he come , of his own accord , to visit she , and to treat with she ; have hear   for the man be as big a gossip as never care for anybody , yet love to know all about everybody   that this wealthy and beautiful maiden would not listen to any young lord , have pledge her faith to the plain John Ridd . 
 thereupon , our Lorna manage so to hold out golden hope to the Lord High Chancellor , that he , be not more than three part drunk , see his way to a heap of money . and there and then   for he be not the man to daily long about anything   upon surety of a certain round sum the amount of which I will not mention , because of his kindness towards I he give to his fair ward permission , under sign and seal , to marry that loyal knight , John Ridd ; upon condition only that the King 's consent should be obtain . 
 his Majesty , well - dispose towards I for my previous service , and regard I as a good Catholic , be move moreover by the Queen , who desire to please Lorna , consent , without much hesitation , upon the understanding that Lorna , when she become of full age , and the mistress of her property   which be still under guardianship , should pay a heavy fine to the Crown , and devote a fix portion of her estate to the promotion of the holy Catholic faith , in a manner to be dictate by the King himself . inasmuch , however , as King James be drive out of his kingdom before this arrangement could take effect , and another king succeed , who desire not the promotion of the catholic religion , neither hanker after subsidy ,   whether French or English , that agreement be pronounce invalid , improper , and contemptible . however , there be no get back the money once pay to Lord Chancellor Jeffreys . 
 but what think we of money at this present moment ; or of position , or anything else , except indeed one another ? Lorna tell I , with the sweetest smile , that if I be minded to take she at all , I must take she without anything ; inasmuch as she mean , upon come of age , to make over the residue of her estate to the next - of - kin , as be unfit for a farmer 's wife . and I reply with the great warmth and a readiness to worship she , that this be exactly what I long for , but have never dare to propose it . but dear mother look most exceeding grave ; and say that to be sure her opinion could not be expect to count for much , but she really hop that in three year ' time we should both he a little wise , and have more regard for our interest , and perhaps those of other by that time ; and Master Snowe have daughter only , and nobody come to marry they , if anything happen to the good old man and who could tell in three year ' time what might happen to all or any of we ? why perhaps his farm would be for sale , and perhaps Lady Lorna 's estate in Scotland would fetch enough money to buy it , and so throw the two farm into one , and save all the trouble about the brook , as my poor father have long to do many and many a time , but not have a title could not do all quite as he want . and then if we young people grow tired of the old mother , as seem only too likely , and be accord to nature , why we could send she over there , and Lizzie to keep her company . 
 when mother have finish , and wipe her eye , Lorna , who have be blush rosily at some portion of this great speech , fling her fair arm around mother 's neck , and kiss she very heartily , and scold she   as she well deserve   for her want of confidence in we . my mother reply that if anybody could deserve her John , it be Lorna ; but that she could not hold with the rashness of give up money so easily ; while her next - of - kin would be John himself , and who could tell what other , by the time she be one - and - twenty ? 
 Hereupon , I feel that after all my mother have common sense on her side ; for if Master Snowe 's farm should be for sale , it would be far more to the purpose than my coat of arm , to get it ; for there be a different pasture there , just suit for change of diet to our sheep as well as large cattle . and beside this , even with all Annie 's skill   and of course yet more now she be go , their butter would always command in the market from one to three farthing a pound more than we could get for our . and few thing vex we more than this . whereas , if we get possession of the farm , we might , without breach of the market - law , or any harm do to any one   the price being but a prejudice , sell all our butter as Snowe butter , and do good to all our customer . 
 think thus , yet remember that Farmer Nicholas might hold out for another score of year as I heartily hop he might or that one , if not all , of his comely daughter might marry a good young farmer   or farmer , if the case be so   or that , even without that , the farm might never be put up for sale ; I beg my Lorna to do as she like ; or rather to wait and think of it ; for as yet she could do nothing . 




 CHAPTER LXXIV 

 DRIVEN beyond ENDURANCE 

  also know as blood upon the altar in other edition 
 everything be settle smoothly , and without any fear or fuss , that Lorna might find end of trouble , and myself of eager waiting , with the help of Parson Bowden , and the good wish of two county . I could scarce believe my fortune , when I look upon her beauty , gentleness , and sweetness , mingle with enough of humour and warm woman 's feeling , never to be dull or tiring ; never themselves to be weary . 
 for she might be call a woman now ; although a very young one , and as full of playful way , or perhaps I may say ten time as full , as if she have know no trouble . to wit , the spirit of bright childhood , have be so curb and straiten , ere its time be over , now break forth , enrich and vary with the garb of conscious maidenhood . and the sense of steadfast love , and eager love enfold she , colour with so many tinge all her look , and word , and thought , that to I it be the noble vision even to think about she . 
 but this be far too bright to last , without bitter break , and the plunging of happiness in horror , and of passionate joy in agony . my darling in her soft moment , when she be alone with I , when the spark of defiant eye be veil beneath dark lash , and the challenge of gay beauty pass into sweetest invitation ; at such time of her pure love and warm faith in I , a deep abide fear would flutter in her bound heart , as of deadly fate 's approach . she would cling to I , and nestle to I , be scared of coyishness , and lie one arm around my neck , and ask if I could do without she . 
 hence , as all emotion haply , of those who be more to we than ourselves , find within we strong echo , and more perfect answer , so I could not be regardless of some hide evil ; and my dark misgiving deepen as the time draw nearer . I keep a steadfast watch on Lorna , neglect a field of bean entirely , as well as a litter of young pig , and a cow somewhat give to jaundice . and I let Jem Slocombe go to sleep in the tallat , all one afternoon , and Bill Dadds draw off a bucket of cider , without so much as a ' by your leave . ' for these man know that my knighthood , and my coat of arm , and   most of all   my love , be greatly against good farming ; the sense of our country be and perhaps it may be sensible that a man who stick up to be anything , must allow himself to be cheat . 
 but I never do stick up , nor would , though all the parish bid I ; and I whistle the same tune to my horse , and hold my plough - tree , just the same as if no king , nor Queen , have ever come to spoil my tune or hand . for this thing , nearly all the man around our part upbraid I ; but the woman praise I : and for the most part these be right , when themselves be not concern . 
 however humble I might be , no one know anything of our part of the country , would for a moment doubt that now here be a great to do and talk of John Ridd and his wedding . the fierce fight with the Doones so lately , and my leading of the combat   though I fight not more than need be , and the vanishing of Sir Counsellor , and the gallop madness of Carver , and the religious fear of the woman that this last be go to hell for he himself have declare that his aim , while he cut through the yeomanry also their remorse , that he should have be make to go thither with all his child leave behind these thing , I say   if ever I can again contrive to say anything , have lead to the broad excitement about my wedding of Lorna . we hear that people mean to come from more than thirty mile around , upon excuse of see my stature and Lorna 's beauty ; but in good truth out of sheer curiosity , and the love of meddle . 
 our clerk have give notice , that not a man should come inside the door of his church without shilling - fee ; and woman   as sure to see twice as much   must every one pay two shilling . I think this wrong ; and as church - warden , beg that the money might be pay into mine own hand , when take . but the clerk say that be against all law ; and he have order from the parson to pay it to he without any delay . so as I always obey the parson , when I care not much about a thing , I let they have it their own way ; though feel inclined to believe , sometimes , that I ought to have some of the money . 
 dear mother arrange all the in and out of the way in which it be to be do ; and Annie and Lizzie , and all the Snowes , and even Ruth Huckaback   who be there , after great persuasion , make such a sweeping of dress that I scarcely know where to place my foot , and long for a staff , to put by their gown . then Lorna come out of a pew half - way , in a manner which quite astonish I , and take my left hand in her right , and I pray God that it be do with . 
 my darling look so glorious , that I be afraid of glance at she , yet take in all her beauty . she be in a fright , no doubt ; but nobody should see it ; whereas I say   to myself at least , ' I will go through it like a grave - digger . ' 
 Lorna 's dress be of pure white , cloud with faint lavender   for the sake of the old Earl Brandir , and as simple as need be , except for perfect loveliness . I be afraid to look at she , as I say before , except when each of we say , ' I will , ' and then each dwell upon the other . 
 it be impossible for any who have not love as I have to conceive my joy and pride , when after ring and all be do , and the parson have bless we , Lorna turn to look at I with her glance of subtle fun subdue by this great act . 
 her eye , which none on earth may ever equal , or compare with , tell I such a depth of comfort , yet await further commune , that I be almost amazed , thoroughly as I know they . darle eye , the sweet eye , the lovely , the most love eye the sound of a shot rang through the church , and those eye be fill with death . 
 Lorna fall across my knee when I be go to kiss she , as the bridegroom be allow to do , and encourage , if he need it ; a flood of blood come out upon the yellow wood of the altar step , and at my foot lie Lorna , try to tell I some last message out of her faithful eye . I lift she up , and pet she , and coax she , but it be no good ; the only sign of life remain be a spirt of bright red blood . 
 some man know what thing befall they in the supreme time of their life far above the time of death but to I come back as a hazy dream , without any knowledge in it , what I do , or feel , or think , with my wife 's arm flagging , flagging , around my neck , as I raise she up , and softly put they there . she sigh a long sigh on my breast , for her last farewell to life , and then she grow so cold , and cold , that I ask the time of year . 
 it be Whit - Tuesday , and the lilac all in blossom ; and why I think of the time of year , with the young death in my arm , God or his angel , may decide , have so strangely give we . enough that so I do , and look ; and our white lilac be beautiful . then I lay my wife in my mother 's arm , and beg that no one would make a noise , go forth for my revenge . 
 of course , I know who have do it . there be but one man in the world , or at any rate , in our part of it , who could have do such a thing such a thing . I use no harsh word about it , while I leap upon our good horse , with bridle but no saddle , and set the head of Kickums towards the course now point out to I . who show I the course , I can not tell . I only know that I take it . and the man fall back before I . 
 weapon of no sort have I. Unarmed , and wonder at my strange attire   with a bridal vest , work by our Annie , and red with the blood of the bride , I go forth just to find out this ; whether in this world there be or be not God of justice . 
 with my vicious horse at a furious speed , I come upon Black Barrow down , direct by some shout of man , which seem to I but a whisper . and there , about a furlong before I , ride a man on a great black horse , and I know that the man be Carver Doone . 
 ' your life or mine , ' I say to myself ; ' as the will of God may be . but we two live not upon this earth , one more hour together . ' 
 I know the strength of this great man ; and I know that he be arm with a gun if he have time to load again , after shoot my Lorna or at any rate with pistol , and a horseman 's sword as well . nevertheless , I have no more doubt of kill the man before I than a cook have of spit a headless fowl . 
 sometimes see no ground beneath I , and sometimes heed every leaf , and the crossing of the grass - blade , I follow over the long moor , reckless whether see or not . but only once the other man turn round and look back again , and then I be beside a rock , with a reedy swamp behind I . 
 although he be so far before I , and rid as hard as ride he might , I see that he have something on the horse in front of he ; something which need care , and stop he from look backward . in the whirling of my wit , I fancy first that this be Lorna ; until the scene I have be through fall across hot brain and heart , like the drop at the close of a tragedy . rush there through crag and quag , at utmost speed of a maddened horse , I see , as of another 's fate , calmly   as on canvas lay , the brutal deed , the piteous anguish , and the cold despair . 
 the man turn up the gully lead from the moor to Cloven Rocks , through which John Fry have track Uncle Ben , as of old relate . but as Carver enter it , he turn round , and behold I not a hundred yard behind ; and I see that he be bear his child , little Ensie , before he . Ensie also descry I , and stretch his hand and cry to I ; for the face of his father frighten he . 
 Carver Doone , with a vile oath , thrust spur into his flagging horse , and lay one hand on a pistol - stock ; whence I know that his slung carbine have receive no bullet since the one that have pierce Lorna . and a cry of triumph rise from the black depth of my heart . what care I for pistol ? I have no spur , neither be my horse one to need the rowel ; I rather hold he in than urge he , for he be fresh as ever ; and I know that the black steed in front , if he breast the steep ascent , where the track divide , must be in our reach at once . 
 his rider know this ; and , have no room in the rocky channel to turn and fire , draw rein at the crossway sharply , and plunge into the black ravine lead to the Wizard 's Slough . ' be it so ? ' I say to myself with a brain and head cold as iron ; ' though the foul fiend come from the slough , to save thee ; thou shalt carve it , Carver . ' 
 I follow my enemy carefully , steadily , even leisurely ; for I have he , as in a pitfall , whence no escape might be . he think that I fear to approach he , for he know not where he be : and his low disdainful laugh come back . ' laugh he who win , ' think I. 
 a gnarled and half - starve oak , as stubborn as my own resolve , and smite by some storm of old , hang from the crag above I . rise from my horse 's back , although I have no stirrup , I catch a limb , and tear it   like a mere wheat - awn   from the socket . man show the rent even now , with wonder ; none with more wonder than myself . 
 Carver Doone turn the corner suddenly on the black and bottomless bog ; with a start of fear he rein back his horse , and I think he would have turn upon I . but instead of that , he again ride on ; hop to find a way round the side . 
 now there be a way between cliff and slough for those who know the ground thoroughly , or have time enough to search it ; but for he there be no road , and he lose some time in seek it . upon this he make up his mind ; and wheeling , fire , and then ride at I . 
 his bullet strike I somewhere , but I take no heed of that . fear only his escape , I lay my horse across the way , and with the limb of the oak strike full on the forehead his charge steed . Ere the slash of the sword come nigh I , man and horse roll over , and wellnigh bear my own horse down , with the power of their onset . 
 Carver Doone be somewhat stunned , and could not arise for a moment . meanwhile I leap on the ground and await , smooth my hair back , and bar my arm , as though in the ring for wrestling . then the little boy run to I , clasp my leg , and look up at I , and the terror in his eye make I almost fear myself . 
 ' Ensie , dear , ' I say quite gently , grieve that he should see his wicked father kill , ' run up yonder round the corner and try to find a pretty bunch of bluebell for the lady . ' the child obey I , hang back , and look back , and then laugh , while I prepare for business . there and then I might have kill mine enemy , with a single blow , while he lie unconscious ; but it would have be foul play . 
 with a sullen and black scowl , the Carver gather his mighty limb , and arise , and look round for his weapon ; but I have put they well away . then he come to I and gaze ; be will not to frighten thus young man . 
 ' I would not harm you , lad , ' he say , with a lofty style of sneer : ' I have punish you enough , for most of your impertinence . for the rest I forgive you ; because you have be good and gracious to my little son . go , and be content . ' 
 for answer , I smite he on the cheek , lightly , and not to hurt he : but to make his blood leap up . I would not sully my tongue by speak to a man like this . 
 there be a level space of sward between we and the slough . with the courtesy derive from London , and the procession I have see , to this place I lead he . and that he might breathe himself , and have every fibre cool , and every muscle ready , my hold upon his coat I loose , and leave he to begin with I , whenever he think proper . 
 I think that he feel that his time be come . I think he know from my knit muscle , and the firm arch of my breast , and the way in which I stand ; but most of all from my stern blue eye ; that he have find his master . at any rate a paleness come , an ashy paleness on his cheek , and the vast calf of his leg bow in , as if he be out of training . 
 see this , villain as he be , I offer he first chance . I stretch forth my left hand , as I do to a weak antagonist , and I let he have the hug of I . but in this I be too generous ; have forget my pistol - wound , and the cracking of one of my short low rib . Carver Doone catch I round the waist , with such a grip as never yet have be lay upon I . 
 I hear my rib go ; I grasp his arm , and tear the muscle out of it    as the string come out of an orange ; then I take he by the throat , which be not allow in wrestling ; but he have snatch at mine ; and now be no time of dalliance . in vain he tug , and strain , and writhe , dash his bleed fist into my face , and fling himself on I with gnash jaw . beneath the iron of my strength for God that day be with I I have he helpless in two minute , and his fiery eye loll out . 
    a far more terrible clutch than this be hand down , to 
  weak age , of the great John Ridd . Ed . 
 ' I will not harm thee any more , ' I cry , so far as I could for panting , the work be very furious : ' Carver Doone , thou art beat : own it , and thank God for it ; and go thy way , and repent thyself . ' 
 it be all too late . even if he have yield in his ravening frenzy for his beard be like a mad dog 's jowl even if he would have own that , for the first time in his life , he have find his master ; it be all too late . 
 the black bog have he by the foot ; the sucking of the ground draw on he , like the thirsty lip of death . in our fury , we have heed neither wet nor dry ; nor thought of earth beneath we . I myself might scarcely leap , with the last spring of o'er - labour leg , from the engulf grave of slime . he fall back , with his swarthy breast   from which my gripe have rent all clothing , like a hummock of bog - oak , stand out the quagmire ; and then he toss his arm to heaven , and they be black to the elbow , and the glare of his eye be ghastly . I could only gaze and pant ; for my strength be no more than an infant 's , from the fury and the horror . scarcely could I turn away , while , joint by joint , he sink from sight . 




 CHAPTER LXXV 

 LIFE and lorna COME again 

 when the little boy come back with the bluebell , which he have manage to find as child always do find flower , when old eye see none the only sign of his father leave be a dark brown bubble , upon a newly form patch of blackness . but to the center of its pulpy gorge the greedy slough be heave , and sullenly grind its weltering jaw among the flag and the sedge . 
 with pain , and ache , both of mind and body , and shame at my own fury , I heavily mount my horse again , and , look down at the innocent Ensie . would this playful , love child grow up like his cruel father , and end a godless life of hatred with a death of violence ? he lift his noble forehead towards I , as if to answer , " Nay , I will not " : but the word he speak be these : 
 ' Don , ' for he could never say ' John ' ' oh , Don , I be so glad that nasty naughty man be go away . take I home , Don . take I home . ' 
 it have be say of the wicked , ' not even their own child love they . ' and I could easily believe that Carver Doone 's cold - hearted way have scar from he even his favorite child . no man would I call truly wicked , unless his heart be cold . 
 it hurt I , more than I can tell , even through all other grief , to take into my arm the child of the man just slay by I . the feeling be a foolish one , and a wrong one , as the thing have be for I would fain have save that man , after he be conquer nevertheless my arm go coldly round that little fellow ; neither would they have go at all , if there have be any help for it . but I could not leave he there , till some one else might fetch he ; on account of the cruel slough , and the raven which have come hover over the dead horse ; neither could I , with my wound , tie he on my horse and walk . 
 for now I have spend a great deal of blood , and be rather faint and weary . and it be lucky for I that Kickums have lose spirit , like his master , and go home as mildly as a lamb . for , when we come towards the farm , I seem to be rid in a dream almost ; and the voice both of man and woman   who have hurry forth upon my track , as they meet I , seem to wander from a distant muffling cloud . only the thought of Lorna 's death , like a heavy knell , be toll in the belfry of my brain . 
 when we come to the stable door , I rather fall from my horse than get off ; and John Fry , with a look of wonder take Kickum 's head , and lead he in . into the old farmhouse I totter , like a weanle child , with mother in her common clothe , help I along , yet fear , except by stealth , to look at I . 
 ' I have kill he , ' be all I say ; ' even as he kill Lorna . now let I see my wife , mother . she belong to I none the less , though dead . ' 
 ' you can not see she now , dear John , ' say Ruth Huckaback , come forward ; since no one else have the courage . ' Annie be with she now , John . ' 
 ' what have that to do with it ? let I see my dead one ; and pray myself to die . ' 
 all the woman fall away , and whisper , and look at I , with side glance , and some sobbing ; for my face be hard as flint . Ruth alone stand by I , and drop her eye , and tremble . then one little hand of hers steal into my great shake palm , and the other be lay on my tattered coat : yet with her clothe she shun my blood , while she whisper gently , 
 ' John , she be not your dead one . she may even be your live one yet , your wife , your home , and your happiness . but you must not see she now . ' 
 ' be there any chance for she ? for I , I mean ; for I , I mean ? ' 
 ' God in heaven know , dear John . but the sight of you , and in this sad plight , would be certain death to she . now come first , and be heal yourself . ' 
 I obey she , like a child , whisper only as I go , for none but myself know her goodness ' Almighty God will bless you , darling , for the good you be do now . ' 
 tenfold , ay and a thousandfold , I pray and I believe it , when I come to know the truth . if it have not be for this little maid , Lorna must have die at once , as in my arm she lie for dead , from the dastard and murderous cruelty . but the moment I leave her Ruth come forward and take the command of every one , in right of her firmness and readiness . 
 she make they bear her home at once upon the door of the pulpit , with the cushion under the droop head . with her own little hand she cut off , as tenderly as a pear be peel , the bridal - dress , so steeped and stain , and then with her dainty transparent finger   no large than a pencil   she probe the vile wound in the side , and fetch the reek bullet forth ; and then with the cold water stanch the flowing of the life - blood . all this while my darling lie insensible , and white as death ; and need nothing but her maiden shroud . 
 but Ruth still sponge the poor side and forehead , and watch the long eyelash flat upon the marble cheek ; and lay her pure face on the faint heart , and bid they fetch her spanish wine . then she part the pearly tooth   feebly clench on the hover breath , and pour in wine from a christen spoon , and raise the graceful neck and breast , and stroke the delicate throat , and wait ; and then pour in a little more . 
 Annie all the while look on with horror and amazement , count herself no second - rate nurse , and this as against all theory . but the quiet lifting of Ruth 's hand , and one glance from her dark bright eye , tell Annie just to stand away , and not intercept the air so . and at the very moment when all the rest have settle that Ruth be a simple idiot , but could not harm the dead much , a little flutter in the throat , follow by a short low sigh , make they pause , and look and hope . 
 for hour , however , and day , she lie at the very verge of death , keep alive by nothing but the care , the skill , the tenderness , and the perpetual watchfulness of Ruth . Luckily Annie be not there very often , so as to meddle ; for kind and clever nurse as she be , she must have do more harm than good . but my broken rib , which be set by a doctor , who chance to be at the wedding , be allot to Annie 's care ; and great inflammation ensue , it be quite enough to content she . this doctor have pronounce poor Lorna dead ; wherefore Ruth refuse most firmly to have aught to do with he . she take the whole case on herself ; and with God 's help she bear it through . 
 now whether it be the light and brightness of my Lorna 's nature ; or the freedom from anxiety for she know not of my hurt ; or , as some people say , her birthright among wound and violence , or her manner of not drink beer I leave that doctor to determine who pronounce she dead . but anyhow , one thing be certain ; sure as star of hope above we ; Lorna recover , long ere I do . 
 for the grief be on I still of have lose my love and lover at the moment she be mine . with the power of fate upon I , and the black cauldron of the wizard 's death boil in my heated brain , I have no faith in the tale they tell . I believe that Lorna be in the churchyard , while these rogue be lie to I . for with strength of blood like mine , and power of heart behind it , a broken bone must burn itself . 
 mine go hard with fire of pain , be of such size and thickness ; and I be ashamed of he for break by reason of a pistol - ball , and the mere hug of a man . and it fetch I down in conceit of strength ; so that I be careful afterwards . 
 all this be a lesson to I . all this make I very humble ; illness be a thing , as yet , altogether unknown to I . not that I cry small , or skulk , or fear the death which some foretold ; shake their head about mortification , and a green appearance . only that I seem quite fit to go to heaven , and Lorna . for in my sick distracted mind   stir with many tossing , like the bead in the spread of frog - spawn carry by the current , hang the black and central essence of my future life . a life without Lorna ; a tadpole life . all stupid head ; and no body . 
 many man may like such life ; anchorite , fakir , high - priest , and so on ; but to my mind , it be not the native thing God mean for we . my dear mother be a show , with cry and with fret . the Doones , as she think , be bear to destroy we . Scarce have she come to some liveliness   though sprinkle with tear , every now and then   after her great bereavement , and ten year ' time to dwell on it when lo , here be her husband 's son , the pet child of her own good John , murder like his father ! well , the way of God be wonderful ! 
 so they be , and so they be ; and so they ever will be . let we debate they as we will , our way be his , and much the same ; only second - hand from he . and I expect something from he , even in my bad of time , know that I have do my good . 
 this be not edify talk as our Nonconformist parson say , when he can get no more to drink therefore let I only tell what become of Lorna . one day , I be sit in my bedroom , for I could not get downstair , and there be no one strong enough to carry I , even if I would have allow it . 
 though it cost I sore trouble and weariness , I have put on all my Sunday clothe , out of respect for the doctor , who be come to bleed I again   as he always do twice a week ; and it strike I that he have seem hurt in his mind , because I wear my bad clothe to be bleed in for lie in bed I would not , after six o'clock ; and even that be great laziness . 
 I look at my right hand , whose grasp have be like that of a blacksmith 's vice ; and it seem to myself impossible that this could be John Ridd 's . the great frame of the hand be there , as well as the muscle , stand forth like the guttering of a candle , and the broad blue vein , go up the back , and cross every finger . but as for colour , even Lorna 's could scarcely have be whiter ; and as for strength , little Ensie Doone might have come and hold it fast . I laugh as I try in vain to lift the basin set for bleed I . 
 then I think of all the lovely thing go on out - of - door just now , concern which the drowsy song of the bee come to I . these must be among the thyme , by the sound of their great content . therefore the rose must be in blossom , and the woodbine , and clove - gilly - flower ; the cherry on the wall must be turn red , the yellow Sally must be on the brook , wheat must be callow with quaver bloom , and the early meadow swathe with hay . 
 yet here be I , a helpless creature quite unfit to stir among they , gift with no sight , no scent of all the change that move our love , and lead our heart , from month to month , along the quiet path of life . and what be bad , I have no hope of care ever for they more . 
 presently a little knock sound through my gloomy room , and suppose it to be the doctor , I try to rise and make my bow . but to my surprise it be little Ruth , who have never once come to visit I , since I be place under the doctor 's hand . Ruth be dress so gaily , with rosette , and flower , and what not , that I be sorry for her bad manner ; and think she be come to conquer I , now that Lorna be do with . 
 Ruth run towards I with sparkle eye , be rather short of sight ; then suddenly she stop , and I see entire amazement in her face . 
 ' can you receive visitor , Cousin Ridd ? why , they never tell I of this ! ' she cry : ' I know that you be weak , dear John ; but not that you be die . whatever be that basin for ? ' 
 ' I have no intention of dying , Ruth ; and I like not to talk about it . but that basin , if you must know , be for the doctor 's purpose . ' 
 ' what , do you mean bleed you ? you poor weak cousin ! be it possible that he do that still ? ' 
 ' twice a week for the last six week , dear . nothing else have keep I alive . ' 
 ' nothing else have kill you , nearly . there ! ' and she set her little boot across the basin , and crush it . ' not another drop shall they have from you . be Annie such a fool as that ? and Lizzie , like a zany , at her book ! and kill her brother , between they ! ' 
 I be surprised to see Ruth excited ; her character be so calm and quiet . and I try to soothe she with my feeble hand , as now she kneel before I . 
 ' dear cousin , the doctor must know well . Annie say so , every day . what have he be bring up for ? ' 
 ' bring up for slay and murder . twenty doctor kill King Charles , in spite of all the woman . will you leave it to I , John ? I have a little will of my own ; and I be not afraid of doctor . will you leave it to I , dear John ? I have save your Lorna 's life . and now I will save your ; which be a far , far easy business . ' 
 ' you have save my Lorna 's life ! what do you mean by talk so ? ' 
 ' only what I say , Cousin John . though perhaps I overprize my work . but at any rate she say so . ' 
 ' I do not understand , ' I say , fall back with bewilderment ; ' all woman be such liar . ' 
 ' have you ever know I tell a lie ? ' Ruth in great indignation more feigned , I doubt , than real ' your mother may tell a story , now and then when she feel it right ; and so may both your sister . but so you can not do , John Ridd ; and no more than you can I do it . ' 
 if ever there be virtuous truth in the eye of any woman , it be now in Ruth Huckaback 's : and my brain begin very slowly to move , the heart be almost torpid from perpetual loss of blood . 
 ' I do not understand , ' be all I could say for a very long time . 
 ' will you understand , if I show you Lorna ? I have fear to do it , for the sake of you both . but now Lorna be well enough , if you think that you be , Cousin John . surely you will understand , when you see your wife . ' 
 follow she , to the very utmost of my mind and heart , I feel that all she say be truth ; and yet I could not make it out . and in her last few word there be such a power of sadness rise through the cover of gaiety , that I say to myself , half in a dream , ' Ruth be very beautiful . ' 
 before I have time to listen much for the approach of footstep , Ruth come back , and behind her Lorna ; coy as if of her bridegroom ; and hang back with her beauty . Ruth bang the door , and run away ; and Lorna stand before I . 
 but she do not stand for an instant , when she see what I be like . at the risk of all thick bandage , and upset a dozen medicine bottle , and scatter leech right and leave , she manage to get into my arm , although they could not hold she . she lay her pant warm young breast on the place where they mean to bleed I , and she set my pale face up ; and she would not look at I , have great faith in kiss . 
 I feel my life come back , and warm ; I feel my trust in woman flow ; I feel the joy of live now , and the power of do it . it be not a moment to describe ; who feel can never tell of it . but the rush of Lorna 's tear , and the challenge of my bride 's lip , and the throbbing of my wife 's heart   now at last at home on mine , make I feel that the world be good , and not a thing to be weary of . 
 little more have I to tell . the doctor be turn out at once ; and slowly come back my former strength , with a darling wife , and good victual . as for Lorna , she never tired of sit and watch I eat and eat . and such be her heart that she never tire of be with I here and there , among the beautiful place , and talk with her arm around I so far at least as it can go , though half of mine may go round she of the many fear and trouble , danger and discouragement , and bad of all the bitter parting , which we use to have , somehow . 
 there be no need for my farming hard than become a man of weight . Lorna have great store of money , though we never draw it out , except for some poor neighbor ; unless I find she a sumptuous dress , out of her own perquisite . and this she always look upon as a wondrous gift from I ; and kiss I much when she put it on , and walk like the noble woman she be . and yet I may never behold it again ; for she get back to her simple clothe , and I love she the well in they . I believe that she give half the grandeur away , and keep the other half for the child . 
 as for poor Tom Faggus , every one know his bitter adventure , when his pardon be recall , because of his journey to Sedgemoor . not a child in the country , I doubt , but know far more than I do of Tom 's most desperate doing . the law have ruin he once , he say ; and then he have be too much for the law : and now that a quiet life be his object , here the base thing come after he . and such be his dread of this evil spirit , that be catch upon Barnstaple Bridge , with soldier at either end of it   yet doubtful about approach he , he set his strawberry mare , sweet Winnie , at the left - hand parapet , with a whisper into her dove - colour ear . without a moment 's doubt she leap it , into the foaming tide , and swam , and land accord to order . also his flight from a public - house   where a trap be set for he , but Winnie come and break down the door , and put two man under , and trod on they ,   be as well know as any ballad . it be report for awhile that poor Tom have be catch at last , by mean of his fondness for liquor , and be hang before Taunton Jail ; but luckily we know well . with a good wife , and a wonderful horse , and all the country attach to he , he keep the law at a wholesome distance , until it become too much for its master ; and a new king arise . upon this , Tom sue his pardon afresh ; and Jeremy Stickles , who suit the time , be glad to help he in get it , as well as a compensation . thereafter the good and respectable Tom live a godly   though not always sober   life ; and bring up his child to honesty , as the first of all qualification . 
 my dear mother be as happy as possibly need be with we ; have no cause for jealousy , as other arise around she . and everybody be well pleased , when Lizzy come in one day and toss she bookshelf over , and declare that she would have Captain Bloxham , and nobody should prevent she . for that he alone , of all the man she have ever meet with , know good writing when he see it , and could spell a word when tell . as he have now succeed to Captain Stickle 's position   Stickles go up the tree , and have the power of collect , and of keep , what he like , there be nothing to be say against it ; and we hop that he would pay she out . 
 I send little Ensie to Blundell 's school , at my own cost and charge , have change his name , for fear of what anyone might do to he . I call he Ensie Jones ; and we get he a commission , and after many scrape of spirit , he do great thing in the Low Countries . he look upon I as his father ; and without my leave will not lay claim to the heritage and title of the Doones , which clearly belong to he . 
 Ruth Huckaback be not married yet ; although upon Uncle Reuben 's death she come into all his property ; except , indeed , 2000 pound , which Uncle Ben , in his dry manner , bequeath ' to Sir John Ridd , the worshipful knight , for grease of the testator 's boot . ' and he leave almost a mint of money , not from the mine , but from the shop , and the good use of usury . for the mine have bring in just what it cost , when the vein of gold end suddenly ; leave all concerned much old , and some , I fear , much poor ; but no one utterly ruin , as be the case with most of they . Ruth herself be his true mine , as upon death - bed he find . I know a man even worthy of she : and though she be not very young , he love she , as I love Lorna . it be my firm conviction , that in the end he will win she ; and I do not mean to dance again , except at dear Ruth 's wedding ; if the floor be strong enough . 
 of Lorna , of my lifelong darling , of my more and more loved wife , I will not talk ; for it be not seemly that a man should exalt his pride . year by year her beauty grow , with the growth of goodness , kindness , and true happiness above all with loving . for change , she make a joke of this , and play with it , and laugh at it ; and then , when my slow nature marvel , back she come to the earnest thing . and if I wish to pay she out for something very dreadful as may happen once or twice , when we become too gladsome I bring she to forget sadness , and to I for cure of it , by the two word ' Lorna Doone . '
